topics.title: SRS for College Library Management Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SRS for College Library Management Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a comprehensive, formal, and complete description of the *intended behavior*, *functionalities*, *constraints*, and *performance attributes* of a software system, written after the requirements elicitation and analysis phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IEEE 830 Standard):** The SRS establishes the *baseline* against which the software is validated and verified. It serves as the **contract** between the developer, the client, and the testing team. For a College Library Management Software (CLMS), the SRS defines *what* the system must do (searching a book, issuing, returning, fine calculation) — *not how* it must be implemented.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an SRS like the **architectural blueprint and the rulebook** for constructing a new college library building:

| Real-World Analogy | Equivalent in Software Engineering |
|---|---|
| **Architectural Drawing** (rooms, doors, windows) | **Functional Requirements** (login, search, issue book) |
| **Fire Safety Rules & Material Specs** | **Non-Functional Requirements** (security, performance, reliability) |
| **Customer's Wishlist ("I want a reading lounge")** | **User Requirements** (Student, Faculty, Librarian needs) |
| **Construction Code (GOVT standards)** | **Constraints** (platform, budget, technology) |
| **Final Building Handover Document** | **Approved SRS Document (Baseline)** |

> [!NOTE]
> If a student orders a "red book" and the SRS only says "book", they cannot complain later. The SRS is the **single source of truth** for what the software will and will *not* do. KTU examiners frequently test this *contractual* nature of the SRS.

## 1.3 Why SRS for a College Library Management Software (CLMS)?

A College Library has multiple **stakeholders** — students, faculty, librarians, and the HOD/Principal — each with overlapping yet distinct needs. Without an SRS:

- The developer might build a "return book" feature that doesn't calculate overdue fines.
- The librarian might assume RFID tag reading, while the developer built a barcode-only system.
- The student might expect an Android app, but the developer delivers a desktop application.

The SRS removes **ambiguity**, **incompleteness**, and **inconsistency**.

## 1.4 Characteristics of a Good SRS (KTU Mnemonic: **"SCRCUC"**)

1. **S**pecification-Correct – reflects actual user needs.
2. **C**onsistent – no conflicting requirements.
3. **R**ealistic – implementable within budget/technology.
4. **C**omplete – covers all functional and non-functional needs.
5. **U**nambiguous – only one interpretation per requirement.
6. **C**heckable / Verifiable – testable by QA team.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 830 Standard SRS Structure (Mandatory for KTU 2024)

The IEEE 830 standard prescribes the following hierarchy. For the CLMS, each section is filled as follows:

### Section 1: Introduction
- **1.1 Purpose** — Define the goal of the CLMS (automate book management, reduce manual errors).
- **1.2 Scope** — The CLMS will manage book cataloging, member registration, issue/return, fine calculation, and report generation. It will *not* include digital e-book hosting or library infrastructure (HVAC/lighting).
- **1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations** — e.g., CLMS, ISBN, RFID, OPAC, RBAC.
- **1.4 References** — IEEE 830-1998, KTU syllabus, college regulations.
- **1.5 Overview** — A roadmap of the rest of the document.

### Section 2: Overall Description
- **2.1 Product Perspective** — The CLMS is a *new, self-contained* web-based application replacing the existing manual register system.
- **2.2 Product Functions** — High-level summary: Catalog Management, Member Management, Circulation (Issue/Return), Fine & Reservation Management, Report Generation.
- **2.3 User Characteristics** —
  - *Librarian:* Trained, full-access, computer-literate.
  - *Faculty:* Moderate use, search and reserve.
  - *Student:* Basic user, search and view status.
- **2.4 Constraints** — Must run on Windows/Linux, use MySQL DB, browser-based (Chrome/Firefox).
- **2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies** — Assumes stable internet and that all books are barcoded.

### Section 3: Specific Requirements (The Core)

This is the **most heavily tested** section in KTU exams.

#### 3.1 Functional Requirements (FR)
Functional requirements describe *what the system shall do*. Each FR must be uniquely numbered.

| FR ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| FR-01 | The system shall allow the librarian to add a new book to the catalog by entering ISBN, title, author, publisher, and category. | High |
| FR-02 | The system shall allow a member to search for a book by title, author, or ISBN. | High |
| FR-03 | The system shall allow the librarian to issue a book to a registered member, subject to a maximum limit of 3 books per student and 5 per faculty. | High |
| FR-04 | The system shall automatically calculate overdue fines at the rate of ₹2 per day for students and ₹1 per day for faculty. | High |
| FR-05 | The system shall generate a daily circulation report listing all issued and returned books. | Medium |
| FR-06 | The system shall allow members to reserve a book that is currently issued. | Medium |
| FR-07 | The system shall send an email/SMS notification to the member 2 days before the due date. | Low |
| FR-08 | The system shall allow the admin to manage user roles (Librarian, Faculty, Student). | High |

#### 3.2 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)
NFRs describe *how well* the system performs.

| NFR ID | Category | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| NFR-01 | Performance | The system shall support 500 concurrent users with response time ≤ 2 seconds. |
| NFR-02 | Security | Passwords shall be encrypted using SHA-256; failed login attempts > 3 shall lock the account for 15 minutes. |
| NFR-03 | Availability | The system shall have 99.5% uptime during college working hours (8 AM – 8 PM). |
| NFR-04 | Usability | A new user shall be able to perform a book search within 1 minute without training. |
| NFR-05 | Maintainability | New modules (e.g., e-journals) shall be addable without modifying existing modules. |
| NFR-06 | Portability | The system shall run on Windows and Linux servers. |
| NFR-07 | Reliability | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) ≥ 720 hours. |

#### 3.3 External Interface Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks to *list* interface requirements. Memorize the four sub-types:

- **3.3.1 User Interfaces** — GUI mockups, color schemes, responsive design.
- **3.3.2 Hardware Interfaces** — Barcode scanner, receipt printer, RFID reader (optional).
- **3.3.3 Software Interfaces** — MySQL 8.0, Apache Tomcat 9, JDK 11, SMTP for email.
- **3.3.4 Communication Interfaces** — HTTPS (port 443), TCP/IP protocol.

#### 3.4 Performance Requirements
- Transaction throughput: minimum 100 transactions/minute.
- Database query response: < 500 ms for search operations.
- Backup: Daily incremental + weekly full backup.

#### 3.5 Design Constraints
- Must use **3-tier architecture** (Presentation, Business Logic, Database).
- Programming language: Java / Python / PHP (instructor-defined).
- Compliance with IEEE 830 documentation standard.

#### 3.6 Software System Attributes
- Maintainability index ≥ 80.
- Code coverage by unit tests ≥ 75%.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Acronym | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **SRS** | Software Requirements Specification | The deliverable of requirements phase |
| **IEEE 830** | IEEE Standard for SRS documentation | KTU syllabus explicitly references this |
| **FR** | Functional Requirement | Describes *what* the system does |
| **NFR** | Non-Functional Requirement | Describes *how well* the system does it |
| **DFD** | Data Flow Diagram | Models functional flow of CLMS |
| **ER** | Entity-Relationship | Models data structure (Book, Member, Issue) |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Access matrix for Librarian/Student/Faculty |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | NFR reliability metric |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | NFR maintainability metric |
| **ISBN** | International Standard Book Number | Used in FR-01 (book entry) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fine Calculation Formula** (Frequently asked 7-mark question):
>
> Let $D_d$ = Due Date, $D_r$ = Return Date, $R$ = Rate per day.
>
> $$\text{Fine} = \max\left(0, \; (D_r - D_d)\right) \times R$$
>
> For students, $R = \text{₹}2$ per day. For faculty, $R = \text{₹}1$ per day.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Overdue Fine Calculation

The CLMS must calculate overdue fine when a book is returned. Below is the step-by-step analytical derivation used in the SRS:

### Step 1: Define Input Variables

Let:
- $B_{\text{id}}$ = unique Book Identifier (e.g., ISBN-9780123456789)
- $M_{\text{id}}$ = unique Member Identifier (e.g., College Roll No. / Employee ID)
- $D_i$ = Date of Issue (in ISO 8601 format: YYYY-MM-DD)
- $D_d$ = Due Date (calculated as $D_i + L$, where $L$ = Loan period)
- $D_r$ = Date of Return (recorded at time of return)
- $R_s$ = Fine rate per day for **student** = ₹2
- $R_f$ = Fine rate per day for **faculty** = ₹1
- $T$ = Member Type (Student / Faculty)

### Step 2: Determine Loan Period $L$

For a typical college library:

$$
L = \begin{cases} 14 \text{ days} & \text{if } T = \text{Student} \\ 30 \text{ days} & \text{if } T = \text{Faculty} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Due Date

$$
D_d = D_i + L
$$

### Step 4: Compute Days Overdue

$$
\Delta D = \max(0, \; D_r - D_d)
$$

The $\max$ function ensures that no negative fines are charged if the book is returned *on or before* the due date.

### Step 5: Compute the Fine

The fine amount is therefore:

$$
\text{Fine} = \Delta D \times R(t)
$$

where the rate function is:

$$
R(t) = \begin{cases} R_s = 2 & \text{if } T = \text{Student} \\ R_f = 1 & \text{if } T = \text{Faculty} \end{cases}
$$

Combining the above into a single closed-form expression:

$$
\text{Fine} = \max\left(0, \; (D_r - D_d)\right) \times \begin{cases} 2 & \text{if } T = \text{Student} \\ 1 & \text{if } T = \text{Faculty} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A student borrows a book on **2025-01-10** and returns it on **2025-01-30**. Compute the fine.

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $D_i = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}10$
- $L = 14$ days (since student)
- $D_r = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}30$

**Step 2:** Compute $D_d$.

$$
D_d = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}10 + 14 = 2025\text{-}01\text{-}24
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\Delta D$.

$$
\Delta D = \max(0, \; 2025\text{-}01\text{-}30 - 2025\text{-}01\text{-}24) = 6 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply rate $R_s = 2$.

$$
\text{Fine} = 6 \times 2 = \text{₹}12
$$

**Final Answer:** The student owes a fine of **₹12**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Concurrent User Capacity (NFR-01)

The system must support 500 concurrent users. The NFR specifies that response time must be $\le 2$ seconds. A simplified Little's Law estimation:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Concurrent Users}}{\text{Average Response Time}} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ requests/second}
$$

This sets a baseline server requirement. A production server with 8 vCPUs and 16 GB RAM typically handles 300–500 RPS, satisfying the NFR.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: SRS Validation Checklist

For each requirement in the CLMS SRS, KTU examiners use the **SMART** validation:

| Acronym | Test Question | Applied to FR-01 (Add Book) |
|---|---|---|
| **S**pecific | Is the requirement clearly stated? | Yes – "librarian adds book using ISBN, title, author, publisher, category." |
| **M**easurable | Can it be measured/tested? | Yes – test that a book with valid ISBN is added. |
| **A**chievable | Can it be implemented? | Yes – basic CRUD operation. |
| **R**elevant | Is it aligned with library goals? | Yes – cataloging is core. |
| **T**ime-bound | Is there a deadline/SLA? | Yes – add operation must complete in ≤ 2 seconds (NFR-01). |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Use Case Diagram for CLMS

The following Mermaid diagram captures the primary actors and use cases of the College Library Management Software.

```mermaid
graph TD
    actor1[Student]
    actor2[Faculty]
    actor3[Librarian]
    actor4[Admin]

    uc1[Search Book]
    uc2[View Book Details]
    uc3[Reserve Book]
    uc4[Check Issue History]
    uc5[Issue Book]
    uc6[Return Book]
    uc7[Calculate Fine]
    uc8[Add Book]
    uc9[Update Book]
    uc10[Delete Book]
    uc11[Register Member]
    uc12[Generate Report]
    uc13[Manage User Roles]
    uc14[Send Notification]

    actor1 --> uc1
    actor1 --> uc2
    actor1 --> uc3
    actor1 --> uc4

    actor2 --> uc1
    actor2 --> uc2
    actor2 --> uc3
    actor2 --> uc4

    actor3 --> uc5
    actor3 --> uc6
    actor3 --> uc7
    actor3 --> uc8
    actor3 --> uc9
    actor3 --> uc10
    actor3 --> uc12

    actor4 --> uc11
    actor4 --> uc13
    actor4 --> uc14

    uc5 -.extends.-> uc7
    uc6 -.extends.-> uc7
    uc12 -.includes.-> uc5
    uc12 -.includes.-> uc6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The `.extends.>` and `.includes.>` relationships in Mermaid are expressed using dashed arrows with labels. In the SRS, you would document these as supplementary use-case descriptions under Section 3.1.

## 4.2 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) - Level 0 (Context Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    student[Student]
    faculty[Faculty]
    librarian[Librarian]
    admin[Admin]
    clms[CLMS System]
    db[(Database)]

    student -->|Search Request| clms
    clms -->|Search Results| student

    faculty -->|Search Request| clms
    clms -->|Search Results| faculty

    librarian -->|Issue/Return Request| clms
    clms -->|Confirmation| librarian

    admin -->|User Management| clms
    clms -->|Status| admin

    clms <-->|Read/Write| db
```

## 4.3 Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram for CLMS

```mermaid
graph TD
    book1[Book]
    member1[Member]
    issue1[Issue]
    category1[Category]
    publisher1[Publisher]

    book1 -->|belongs to| category1
    book1 -->|published by| publisher1
    issue1 -->|refers to| book1
    issue1 -->|issued to| member1
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    ui[Presentation Layer - Web Browser]
    biz[Business Logic Layer - Java Spring Boot]
    db[Data Layer - MySQL Database]
    ext[External Interfaces - Email and Barcode]

    ui -->|HTTP Request| biz
    biz -->|SQL Queries| db
    db -->|Result Set| biz
    biz -->|JSON Response| ui
    biz -->|SMTP and USB| ext
    ext -->|Status| biz
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to *"draw a DFD or use case diagram for the SRS"*, always label **actors**, **processes**, **data stores**, and **data flows** clearly. Examiners award full marks only when all four elements are present in the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]
**Define the term Software Requirements Specification (SRS). List any four characteristics of a good SRS.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** is a formal document that describes *what* the system must do, *how well* it must perform, and under what *constraints* it must operate. It serves as the contract between the developer and the client, and forms the baseline for verification and validation.

Four characteristics of a good SRS:
1. **Correct** – reflects actual user needs.
2. **Unambiguous** – only one interpretation possible.
3. **Complete** – covers all functional and non-functional aspects.
4. **Verifiable / Testable** – can be validated through tests.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four characteristics)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar]
**Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements with one example each in the context of a College Library Management Software.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirement | Non-Functional Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Describes *what* the system shall do. | Describes *how well* the system performs. |
| Testability | Tested via functional/black-box tests. | Tested via load/stress/security tests. |
| Example (CLMS) | "The system shall allow a librarian to issue a book to a registered student." | "The system shall respond to a book search query in ≤ 2 seconds." |
| Verifiability | Verified by feature presence. | Verified by metric measurement. |

*(1.5 marks for difference + 1.5 marks for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks [KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** *Explain the IEEE 830 standard structure of an SRS. Describe each section briefly.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Prepare a detailed SRS for a College Library Management Software. The SRS must include: purpose, scope, at least 6 functional requirements, at least 4 non-functional requirements, and external interface requirements.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**(a) IEEE 830 SRS Structure (7 marks)**

The IEEE 830 standard prescribes the following structure:

1. **Introduction** (1.5 marks)
   - *Purpose:* Why the SRS is being written.
   - *Scope:* Boundaries of the system.
   - *Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations.*
   - *References.*
   - *Overview of the document.*

2. **Overall Description** (2 marks)
   - *Product Perspective:* How the system fits in the existing environment (new/replacement/subsystem).
   - *Product Functions:* High-level summary of capabilities.
   - *User Characteristics:* Skills and expertise of each user class.
   - *Constraints:* Regulatory, hardware, software, time limits.
   - *Assumptions and Dependencies.*

3. **Specific Requirements** (3 marks)
   - *Functional Requirements.*
   - *Non-Functional Requirements (Performance, Security, Maintainability, etc.).*
   - *External Interface Requirements (User, Hardware, Software, Communication).*
   - *Design Constraints.*
   - *Software System Attributes.*

*(Remaining 0.5 mark for clean diagram or example)*

---

**(b) Detailed SRS for CLMS (7 marks)**

**1. Purpose (0.5 mark)**
The purpose of this SRS is to define the functional and non-functional requirements of the College Library Management Software (CLMS) that automates book management, member registration, issue/return, and report generation for XYZ College.

**2. Scope (0.5 mark)**
The CLMS will handle cataloging, circulation, fine calculation, and reporting. It will *not* include digital e-book hosting, library infrastructure management, or inter-library loan systems.

**3. Functional Requirements (3 marks)** — sample entries:

- **FR-01:** The system shall allow the librarian to add a new book with fields: ISBN, title, author, publisher, category, and total copies.
- **FR-02:** The system shall allow members (student/faculty) to search for a book by title, author, ISBN, or category.
- **FR-03:** The system shall allow the librarian to issue a book to a registered member, enforcing limits of 3 books (student) and 5 books (faculty).
- **FR-04:** The system shall compute overdue fine using $\text{Fine} = \max(0, D_r - D_d) \times R(t)$.
- **FR-05:** The system shall generate a daily report of issued, returned, and reserved books.
- **FR-06:** The system shall allow members to reserve a currently issued book and notify them upon availability.

**4. Non-Functional Requirements (2 marks):**

- **NFR-01 (Performance):** Response time ≤ 2 seconds for 500 concurrent users.
- **NFR-02 (Security):** Passwords hashed using SHA-256; account locks after 3 failed login attempts for 15 minutes.
- **NFR-03 (Availability):** 99.5% uptime during 8 AM – 8 PM college hours.
- **NFR-04 (Usability):** A first-time user shall complete a book search within 1 minute without training.

**5. External Interface Requirements (1 mark):**
- *User Interface:* Web-based GUI with responsive design for desktop and mobile browsers.
- *Hardware Interface:* Barcode scanner (USB), receipt printer.
- *Software Interface:* MySQL 8.0, Apache Tomcat 9, JDK 11.
- *Communication Interface:* HTTPS on port 443.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024]

**(a)** *Identify the actors and draw a Use Case Diagram for a College Library Management Software. List at least 6 use cases with brief descriptions.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the difference between Functional and Non-Functional Requirements. For the CLMS, write 5 functional and 5 non-functional requirements with unique IDs.* **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution Outline:

**(a) Use Case Diagram (7 marks):**
- **Actors (1 mark):** Student, Faculty, Librarian, Admin.
- **Use Cases (4 marks):** Search Book, View Details, Reserve Book, Issue Book, Return Book, Add Book, Calculate Fine, Generate Report, Manage Members.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** Use the diagram from Section 4.1. Include `extends`/`includes` relationships for **Calculate Fine** (extends both Issue and Return).

**(b) Functional vs Non-Functional (7 marks):**
- *Difference (1 mark)* — as in Q2 of Part A.
- *5 Functional Requirements (3 marks)*: FR-01 to FR-05 from the table in Section 2.1.
- *5 Non-Functional Requirements (3 marks)*: NFR-01 to NFR-05 from the table in Section 2.1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing "What" and "How":** Do not write *"The system shall use a hash table for searching"* — that is a *design*, not a *requirement*. Requirements describe behavior, not implementation.
> 2. **Vague Quantifiers:** Avoid *"fast"*, *"secure"*, *"easy to use"*. Always quantify: *"response time ≤ 2 seconds"*, *"3 failed logins lock the account"*.
> 3. **Missing IDs:** Every requirement must have a unique identifier (FR-01, NFR-02). Examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark per missing ID.
> 4. **Forgetting Interfaces:** Many students skip the External Interface Requirements sub-section. This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss in KTU 2024 papers.
> 5. **No Priority:** KTU 2024 expects priorities (High/Medium/Low) for functional requirements. Omitting them loses marks.
> 6. **Confusing SRS with SDD:** The SRS says *what*; the Software Design Description (SDD) says *how*. Do not describe database schema in detail in the SRS.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ **SRS = contract** between client and developer, follows **IEEE 830** standard.
- ✅ IEEE 830 has 3 main sections: **Introduction**, **Overall Description**, **Specific Requirements**.
- ✅ **Functional Requirements (FR)** = *what* the system does (issue book, calculate fine).
- ✅ **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** = *how well* (performance, security, usability, availability, reliability, maintainability, portability).
- ✅ Every requirement must be **SMART** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
- ✅ Every requirement needs a **unique ID** (FR-01, NFR-01).
- ✅ **External Interfaces** has 4 sub-types: User, Hardware, Software, Communication.
- ✅ **Fine Formula:** $\text{Fine} = \max(0, (D_r - D_d)) \times R(t)$.
- ✅ **Student loan period = 14 days**, **Faculty = 30 days**, **Fine = ₹2/day (student), ₹1/day (faculty)**.
- ✅ **Use Case Diagram** must show actors (Student, Faculty, Librarian, Admin), use cases, and `extends`/`includes` relationships.
- ✅ **DFD** must show processes, data stores, data flows, and external entities.
- ✅ **Avoid "How" in SRS** — leave design decisions to the SDD.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** SMART requirements, IEEE 830 structure, FR vs NFR difference, fine calculation, use case diagram, external interfaces, validation checklist.
- ✅ Memorize at least **6 functional** and **4 non-functional** requirements with IDs for instant recall in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component and Connector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component and Connector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component-and-Connector (C&C) View:** A view of software architecture that describes the **runtime entities** (components) of a system and the **interactions** (connectors) among them. It focuses on how the system *behaves and communicates at runtime*, in contrast to the Module view which focuses on static code organization.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (mapped to the **SEI/Clements architectural viewtype model**), the C&C view is one of the **three primary architectural views** documented for any non-trivial software system:

1. **Module View** – Static decomposition into units of implementation.
2. **Component-and-Connector (C&C) View** – Runtime structure & interaction.
3. **Allocation View** – Mapping of software elements to the environment (hardware, file systems, teams).

### Primary Vocabulary (KTU Terminology)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Component** | A principal unit of **computation** and state holding at runtime (e.g., process, service, object). |
| **Connector** | A principal unit of **interaction** among components (e.g., pipe, RPC, event bus). |
| **Port** | The interaction point on a component through which it communicates. |
| **Role** | The interaction point on a connector. |
| **Interface** | The specification of valid interactions at a port. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why can't a single architectural view capture everything?"* — Answer: because each view answers a different question. The C&C view specifically answers **"How do runtime elements talk to each other?"**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **Component-and-Connector view** like the **kitchen of a busy restaurant at dinner service**:

- **Components** are the *cooks (chefs), waiters, and the cashier*. Each has a specific job and a "state" (e.g., Chef A is currently grilling).
- **Connectors** are the *order tickets, the service window pass, and the bell that dings when an order is ready*. They are not "people" — they are the *mechanisms* through which cooks and waiters coordinate.
- **Ports** are the *windows in the kitchen wall* — the only place a cook can hand out food or accept a new order.
- **Roles** are the *labels on the window* — "Hot food out," "New orders in," "Dessert pickup."

So a C&C diagram is essentially the **"live service map"** of your software, while a Module view is the **"blueprint of the building"**.

---

## 1.3 Why the C&C View Matters (Syllabus Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 Software Engineering syllabus (Module 2)** explicitly introduces architectural views to teach students that architecture is *multi-dimensional*. C&C is critical because:
> - It captures **runtime qualities**: performance, availability, security, concurrency.
> - It enables **performance analysis** (latency, throughput), **reliability analysis** (single points of failure), and **security analysis** (trust boundaries).
> - It is the foundation for reasoning about **distributed systems**, **microservices**, and **cloud-native architectures** — top KTU interview topics.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Element Types of the C&C View

### 2.1.1 Components (Computational Entities)

A component is any **runtime entity that:**
- Has an **identity** (is distinguishable from other components).
- Encapsulates **state** and **processing**.
- Interacts **only through well-defined ports**.
- Is **substitutable** (can be replaced by another component of the same interface type).

**KTU Recognized Component Subtypes:**

| Subtype | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Process / Service** | Long-lived, independently schedulable runtime unit. | Web server process, microservice. |
| **Object** | Encapsulated state + behavior, typically in OO systems. | EJB, POJO within a JVM. |
| **Filter** | Transforms a stream of data (Pipe-Filter style). | `gzip`, `sort`, `grep` in Unix. |
| **Repository** | Central data store interacted with by multiple clients. | Database, blackboard. |
| **Data Abstraction (ADT)** | Encapsulated data structure with operations. | A custom stack object. |
| **Concurrent Task** | An executable unit that runs independently. | A thread, a coroutine. |

### 2.1.2 Connectors (Interaction Mechanisms)

A connector is an **architectural element that mediates and coordinates interaction** between components. It is **not** a passive data pipe — it may implement complex protocol logic.

**KTU Recognized Connector Subtypes:**

| Subtype | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Procedure Call / RPC** | Synchronous remote call. | gRPC, CORBA, REST GET. |
| **Event / Message Bus** | Asynchronous publish-subscribe backbone. | Kafka, RabbitMQ. |
| **Pipe** | Unidirectional byte/data stream between filters. | Unix pipe, OS pipeline. |
| **Shared Variable / Data Repository** | Direct read/write to a shared memory region. | Shared database, file. |
| **Link / TCP Socket** | Low-level network connection. | HTTP over TCP. |
| **Broker** | Coordinates request forwarding to multiple services. | Service registry, ESB. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Connectors are **first-class architectural elements**, not merely "lines on a diagram." They have their own behavior, performance characteristics, and failure modes. Drawing an arrow and labeling it "REST" is insufficient — you must specify the *connector type*.

### 2.1.3 Ports, Roles, and Interfaces

- **Port:** A point of *interaction on a component*. A component may have multiple ports (e.g., an HTTP port and an admin port).
- **Role:** A point of *interaction on a connector*. Connectors have typed roles (e.g., *client-role* and *server-role*).
- **Interface:** The **specification** of allowed interactions — what messages, operations, or signals may cross the boundary.

A *port of a component attaches to a role of a connector* — this is the **attachment relation** in C&C.

---

## 2.2 Properties & Constraints (KTU 2024 Terminology)

Each element in a C&C view carries **properties** (tagged values) and is governed by **constraints** (rules). These are examinable.

### Component Properties
- **Computational:** CPU usage, memory footprint, threads.
- **State-related:** Stateless vs. stateful, persistence.
- **Identity:** Unique process ID, instance count.

### Connector Properties
- **Protocol:** Synchronous / asynchronous, request-reply / one-way.
- **Performance:** Latency, throughput, jitter.
- **Reliability:** Reconnection, retry, message ordering guarantees.
- **Security:** Encryption, authentication, authorization rules.

### System-level Constraints
- **Allowed Connectors:** "Components may only communicate via the official message bus."
- **Trust Boundaries:** "External components must not have direct access to the internal database connector."

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While C&C is mostly qualitative, several **quantitative quality-attribute formulas** are examinable. They are derived from connector/component latency, throughput, and availability.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{end-to-end} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{comp_i} + T_{conn_i})$ | Total end-to-end latency along a call chain of n components and n-1 connectors. | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $A_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | System availability = product of all serial-component availabilities. | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | $\lambda_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Failure rate of series components is the sum of individual rates. | failures/hour |
| 4 | $MTBF_{system} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{system}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures for series components. | hours |
| 5 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks completed}}{\text{Time interval}}$ | Number of tasks a pipeline can process per unit time (Bottleneck filter limits it). | tasks/s |
| 6 | $\text{Pipeline Throughput} = \min_{f \in F} (\text{rate}(f))$ | Throughput of a Pipe-Filter system = rate of the slowest filter. | tasks/s |
| 7 | $Q_{pipes} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{\max(T_i)}$ | Quick estimate of parallel speedup (Amdahl-style). | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R = 1 - (1 - r)^n$ | Probability of at least one success in n independent redundant replicas (r is replica reliability). | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe `|` in formulas must NEVER be typed as `|` in markdown tables** because the parser will break. Use `\mid` or `\vert`. The table above follows this rule.

---

## 2.4 C&C Architectural Styles (KTU Frequently Tested)

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists several **C&C styles** under the umbrella of architectural patterns. These are the most-asked categories.

### 2.4.1 Client–Server Style

- **Components:** Clients, Server(s).
- **Connectors:** RPC, HTTP/HTTPS, SQL.
- **Topology:** 1-to-N, N-to-1, or N-to-N.
- **Use case:** Web apps, database clients.

### 2.4.2 Pipe-and-Filter Style

- **Components:** Filters.
- **Connectors:** Pipes.
- **Data flow:** Unidirectional streams.
- **Use case:** Unix shell, signal processing, ETL.

### 2.4.3 Publish–Subscribe Style

- **Components:** Publishers, Subscribers.
- **Connectors:** Event bus / broker.
- **Coupling:** Loose, asynchronous.
- **Use case:** Event-driven systems, Kafka-based microservices.

### 2.4.4 Shared-Data Style (Repository / Blackboard)

- **Components:** Computations.
- **Connectors:** Shared data store (DB or blackboard).
- **Use case:** IDEs, AI blackboard systems, banking core.

### 2.4.5 Service-Oriented / Microservices Style

- **Components:** Loosely coupled services.
- **Connectors:** ESB, REST, gRPC, message queue.
- **Use case:** Cloud-native enterprise apps.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Through a C&C Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most commonly asked quantitative question** on C&C: "A system has 3 components connected in a sequence. Given the latencies, find the total response time."

Let a request travel through components $C_1, C_2, C_3$ connected by connectors $K_1, K_2$ in series (the typical client → app → DB → app → client pattern). Let:

- $T_{C_i}$ = computation time spent in component $i$.
- $T_{K_i}$ = transit/communication time through connector $i$.

**Step 1 — Identify the call path.**

The request visits $C_1 \to K_1 \to C_2 \to K_2 \to C_3$.

**Step 2 — Sum the serial delays.**

$$
T_{total} = T_{C_1} + T_{K_1} + T_{C_2} + T_{K_2} + T_{C_3}
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ components and $n-1$ connectors (asynchronous round-trip considered).**

For a full client–server round trip with $n$ hops:
$$
T_{round} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{C_i} + T_{K_i})
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**

Given: $T_{C_1} = 5\text{ ms},\ T_{K_1} = 2\text{ ms},\ T_{C_2} = 10\text{ ms},\ T_{K_2} = 3\text{ ms},\ T_{C_3} = 7\text{ ms}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= 5 + 2 + 10 + 3 + 7 \\
&= 28 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- The **dominant delay** is in $C_2$ (10 ms) — the KTU examiner will accept answers like *"Optimization priority is component C₂"*.
- The **sum of connector delays** (5 ms) is non-trivial — emphasizes that connectors are not free.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Availability in a Series C&C Chain

> [!NOTE]
> Availability questions test whether students understand that a chain is **only as strong as its weakest serial link**.

**Step 1 — Define per-element availability.**

Let $A_i$ be the probability that element $i$ (component or connector) is operational.

**Step 2 — Assume statistical independence of failures (a standard KTU assumption).**

For series:
$$
A_{sys} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \cdots \times A_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example.**

Given: $A_{comp1} = 0.99,\ A_{conn1} = 0.995,\ A_{comp2} = 0.98,\ A_{conn2} = 0.999,\ A_{comp3} = 0.97$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sys} &= 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.98 \times 0.999 \times 0.97 \\
&= 0.935 \quad (\text{approx. } 93.5\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Even with each individual element at 97–99.9% availability, the **system-wide** availability drops noticeably. This is the architectural motivation for **redundancy** (see formula 8 above).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pipe-Filter Throughput and Bottleneck

**Step 1 — Define per-filter processing rate.**

Let $r_f$ = number of data units processed per second by filter $f$.

**Step 2 — Pipeline constraint.**

In a steady-state pipe-filter chain, the slowest filter determines the throughput:
$$
\text{Throughput}_{pipe} = \min_{f \in F} r_f
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**

Filter A: 100 req/s, Filter B: 60 req/s, Filter C: 90 req/s.
$$
\text{Throughput} = \min(100, 60, 90) = 60 \text{ req/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the bottleneck.**

Filter B is the bottleneck. Optimization should target B (e.g., add caching, parallelize internally, or split into two filters).

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: Module View vs. C&C View

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**.

| Aspect | Module View | C&C View |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Question** | How is the code *organized*? | How do runtime entities *interact*? |
| **Time Dimension** | Static (compile time) | Dynamic (runtime) |
| **Principal Elements** | Modules, packages, classes, subroutines. | Components, connectors, ports, roles. |
| **Relations** | "Is part of", "depends on", "inherits from". | "Attaches to", "interacts via", "communicates with". |
| **Quality Attributes Addressed** | Modifiability, reusability, maintainability. | Performance, availability, security, concurrency. |
| **Tooling** | UML class diagrams, package diagrams. | UML deployment + sequence/communication diagrams, AADL. |
| **Audience** | Developers, maintainers. | Performance engineers, SREs, integrators. |

---

## 3.5 Worked KTU 14-Mark Style Scenario

> **Scenario:** A payment processing system has 3 components — *WebServer, OrderService, PaymentService* — connected by 2 connectors — *HTTPSBus* and *InternalRPC*.

**(a) Draw the C&C view** (see Section 4 for the Mermaid diagram).

**(b) Identify the elements, their types, and the interactions.**

- **Components:** WebServer (process/service), OrderService (process/service), PaymentService (process/service).
- **Connectors:** HTTPSBus (network/RPC connector), InternalRPC (RPC connector).
- **Ports:** WebServer has port `p_https`; OrderService has `p_in` and `p_pay`; PaymentService has `p_pay_in`.
- **Roles:** HTTPSBus has `clientRole` (attached to WebServer) and `serverRole` (attached to OrderService). InternalRPC has `callerRole` (OrderService) and `calleeRole` (PaymentService).

**(c) Discuss performance and availability implications.**

- **Latency** is the sum of all $T_{comp}$ and $T_{conn}$.
- **Availability** of the system is the product of the 3 component availabilities and 2 connector availabilities.
- **Bottleneck** analysis: if the *InternalRPC* has high latency, the entire checkout flow suffers — optimization candidate.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical C&C View — Generic Element Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Component A]:::comp -->|via port p1| R1((Role r1))
    R1 --- B[Connector K]
    R2((Role r2)) --- B
    B -->|via port p2| C[Component C]:::comp
    classDef comp fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

> **Interpretation:** The connector is **not** a passive line — it is a distinct architectural element that holds roles. Components communicate only via their **ports** attached to the connector's **roles**.

---

## 4.2 Detailed C&C View — Online Payment System (Worked Example)

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef component fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef connector fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef port fill:#facc15,stroke:#a16207,color:#000000

    WS[WebServer]:::component
    OS[OrderService]:::component
    PS[PaymentService]:::component
    DB[(OrderDatabase)]:::component

    P_WS[HTTPSPort]:::port
    P_OS_in[OrderInPort]:::port
    P_OS_pay[PaymentOutPort]:::port
    P_PS_in[PaymentInPort]:::port
    P_OS_db[DBReadPort]:::port
    P_DB[DBPort]:::port

    R_BUS_srv((ServerRole)):::port
    R_BUS_cli((ClientRole)):::port
    R_RPC_cal((CalleeRole)):::port
    R_RPC_calr((CallerRole)):::port
    R_DB_read((ReadRole)):::port
    R_DB_serv((ServeRole)):::port

    BUS[HTTPSBus]:::connector
    RPC[InternalRPC]:::connector
    DBCONN[JDBCConnector]:::connector

    WS --- P_WS
    P_WS --- R_BUS_cli
    R_BUS_cli --- BUS
    BUS --- R_BUS_srv
    R_BUS_srv --- P_OS_in
    P_OS_in --- OS

    OS --- P_OS_pay
    P_OS_pay --- R_RPC_calr
    R_RPC_calr --- RPC
    RPC --- R_RPC_cal
    R_RPC_cal --- P_PS_in
    P_PS_in --- PS

    OS --- P_OS_db
    P_OS_db --- R_DB_read
    R_DB_read --- DBCONN
    DBCONN --- R_DB_serv
    R_DB_serv --- P_DB
    P_DB --- DB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Key:**
> - **Teal boxes** = Components.
> - **Red boxes** = Connectors.
> - **Yellow nodes** = Ports (on components) and Roles (on connectors).
> - Each connection is from a **port → role** or **role → port**, never directly component-to-component.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Differentiate between the Module View and the Component-and-Connector View of software architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Module View** describes the static, *implementation-time* decomposition of the system into units such as modules, packages, classes, and subroutines. It addresses questions of *modifiability, reusability,* and *code organization*, and is used primarily by developers.

The **Component-and-Connector (C&C) View** describes the *runtime* structure — components (processes, services, objects) and connectors (RPC, message bus, pipe). It addresses questions of *performance, availability, security,* and *concurrency*, and is used by integrators and system engineers.

**Key distinction:** Module view = static & source-level; C&C view = dynamic & runtime-level. The two views **complement** each other; neither is sufficient alone.

*(3 marks: 1 for Module, 1 for C&C, 1 for distinction.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**What is a connector in the C&C view? Why is it treated as a first-class architectural element?**

**Model Answer:**

A **connector** is an architectural element that **mediates and coordinates interaction** between components. It encapsulates the *mechanism* of communication (protocol, transport, security).

It is treated as a first-class element because:
1. Connectors have **non-trivial behavior** (e.g., encryption, retries, ordering).
2. Connectors have **measurable quality attributes** (latency, throughput, failure rate).
3. Connectors are **independent of any single component** and can be reused across the system.
4. They define **architectural style** — e.g., message bus, pipe, RPC.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B for each module features an **internal choice**: the student must answer **one** of two questions. Below are two complete 14-mark alternatives covering Component and Connector.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **Component-and-Connector (C&C) view** of software architecture. Discuss the **elements, relations, properties, and constraints** of this view with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Client–Server** and **Publish–Subscribe** C&C styles. For each, give the **components, connectors, typical use cases,** and **at least one advantage and one limitation.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The C&C view describes the *runtime* architecture of a system as a set of **components** (computational units) and **connectors** (interaction mechanisms), with **ports, roles, and interfaces** governing how they attach and communicate.

**2. Elements (2 marks):**
- **Components** — processes, services, threads, objects, filters, repositories.
- **Connectors** — RPC, message bus, pipe, shared data, event bus, broker.
- **Ports** — typed interaction endpoints on a component.
- **Roles** — typed endpoints on a connector.
- **Interfaces** — contracts defining what messages/operations are valid.

**3. Relations (1 mark):**
- **Attachment:** a port of a component is *attached* to a role of a connector.
- **Interfacing:** the interface specification on a port must match the role specification.

**4. Properties (2 marks):**
- *Component properties:* state, CPU/memory usage, identity, lifetime.
- *Connector properties:* protocol (sync/async), latency, throughput, security, reliability.
- *System properties (derived):* end-to-end latency, system availability, throughput.

**5. Constraints (1 mark):**
Examples: *"All inter-service communication must use the message bus"*; *"External clients cannot directly invoke internal components."* Constraints govern *which attachments are allowed* and *which connectors may be used.*

**Example:** In a web app, the WebServer component (port `http`) attaches to the HTTPConnector (role `client`) which links to the ApplicationServer (port `http_in`).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**1. Client–Server Style (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Components | Clients, one or more Servers. |
| Connectors | RPC, HTTP, SQL. |
| Use cases | Web apps, banking, email. |
| Advantage | Centralized control, easy data consistency. |
| Limitation | Server is a single point of failure; scalability bottleneck. |

**2. Publish–Subscribe Style (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Components | Publishers, Subscribers. |
| Connectors | Event bus / message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ). |
| Use cases | Event-driven microservices, IoT, real-time analytics. |
| Advantage | Loose coupling; publishers and subscribers don't know each other. |
| Limitation | Eventual consistency, message-ordering challenges, debugging complexity. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** A software system has **four components** connected in **series** by **three connectors**. The component processing times are 4 ms, 7 ms, 6 ms, and 5 ms. The connector transit times are 1 ms, 2 ms, and 1 ms. Compute the **total end-to-end latency**. Identify the **bottleneck** and suggest **two optimization strategies.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Pipe-and-Filter** C&C style. In a pipeline with three filters having rates of 80 req/s, 50 req/s, and 90 req/s respectively, calculate the **steady-state throughput** and the **filter that must be optimized.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Identify the chain (1 mark):**

$$
C_1 \xrightarrow{K_1} C_2 \xrightarrow{K_2} C_3 \xrightarrow{K_3} C_4
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the latencies (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= (4 + 7 + 6 + 5) + (1 + 2 + 1) \\
&= 22 + 4 \\
&= 26 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bottleneck identification (1 mark):**

The **bottleneck component** is $C_2$ at 7 ms (highest individual component latency). Connector $K_2$ at 2 ms is also the slowest connector.

**Step 4 — Two optimization strategies (2 marks):**

1. **Optimize $C_2$:** introduce caching, pre-computation, or move heavy processing to a background worker.
2. **Parallelize or split $C_2$** into two sub-components, or add a **circuit breaker** to avoid timeouts cascading.
3. *(Alternative, acceptable)* **Optimize $K_2$** by upgrading to a faster protocol (e.g., gRPC instead of REST) or moving components geographically closer (reduce network RTT).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Pipe-and-Filter explanation (3 marks):**

In the **Pipe-and-Filter** style:
- **Components** are **filters** that incrementally transform a data stream.
- **Connectors** are **pipes** that carry unidirectional data between filters.
- Filters are **independent** and **combinable**.
- **Use cases:** Unix shell pipelines, compiler phases (lex → parse → semantic), signal processing, ETL.

**Key property:** the slowest filter dictates overall throughput.

**Step 2 — Throughput calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{pipe} &= \min(80, 50, 90) \\
&= 50 \text{ req/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the filter to optimize (1 mark):**

**Filter 2** (50 req/s) is the bottleneck. Optimization candidates:
- Add caching inside Filter 2.
- Increase its allocated CPU/memory.
- Split its workload across two parallel filters (with a load-balancer connector upstream).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Drawing arrows instead of connectors** — In a C&C diagram, an arrow is **not** a connector. You must draw the connector as a *named box* (e.g., "HTTPSBus") with attached roles. Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for treating connectors as mere lines.
> 2. **Confusing modules and components** — A Java *class* is a **module** element; an *instance of that class running as a service* is a **component**. Mixing the two loses conceptual marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify the connector type** — Writing "communicates via network" is vague. Say "communicates via an **asynchronous message-bus connector using AMQP**".
> 4. **No mention of ports/roles** — KTU 2024 specifically tests these. A diagram missing ports/roles is incomplete.
> 5. **Skipping availability math in long answers** — A 7-mark C&C question often includes an availability or latency sub-part. A common error is to *add* availabilities instead of *multiplying* them.
> 6. **Forgetting to label the diagram** — Always label axes (e.g., "Component Type: Filter"), include a legend, and use distinct shapes for components vs. connectors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Component and Connector View**
> - **C&C view = runtime view**; it answers *"how do parts interact at runtime?"*
> - **Five core elements:** Component, Connector, Port, Role, Interface.
> - **Two core relations:** *Attachment* (port ↔ role) and *Interfacing* (interface compatibility).
> - **Connectors are first-class elements** — they have behavior, performance, and failure modes.
> - **Latency formula:** $T_{total} = \sum (T_{comp} + T_{conn})$.
> - **Availability formula (series):** $A_{sys} = \prod A_i$ — multiplies, not adds.
> - **Throughput (Pipe-Filter):** $\min(r_f)$ — bottleneck filter wins.
> - **C&C Styles** (must-know): Client-Server, Pipe-Filter, Publish-Subscribe, Shared-Data/Repository, Service-Oriented.
> - **C&C addresses** performance, availability, security, concurrency — *not* modifiability.
> - **Differences from Module view:** static vs. dynamic, code-level vs. process-level, developers vs. integrators.
> - **Diagrams must show:** components as boxes, connectors as distinct named elements, ports as small circles/squares, roles on connectors.
> - **Common KTU trap:** confusing *connectors* with *links*; remember, a *link* is just a relationship, while a *connector* is an element with its own behavior.
> - **Exam-favorite words** to use: *port, role, interface, attachment, interaction, runtime, latency, throughput, availability, bottleneck, redundancy, trust boundary.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview (Section 2)

## 1.1 Formal Definition Restated

The **Component-and-Connector (C&C) view** is an architectural view that captures the *runtime structure* of a software system. It identifies the **principal computational elements (components)** and the **principal interaction elements (connectors)** that exist once the system is *deployed and executing*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Canonical Statement (memorize for 2-mark definition questions):**
> *"The Component-and-Connector view describes a system's runtime structure as a graph of components and connectors interacting through ports and roles, focusing on behavioral and runtime quality attributes such as performance, availability, concurrency, and security."*

## 1.2 Intuition in Plain English

Imagine a **broadcasting studio** during a live news show:

- **The Anchor, the Reporter in the field, and the Producer in the control room** are *components*. Each is a *person doing a job in real time*.
- **The satellite link, the IFB (interruptible foldback) channel, and the telephone line** are *connectors*. They are not people — they are the *mediums* of communication.
- **The microphone jack, the earpiece, the video feed port** are *ports* on the components.
- **The "anchor-end" of the satellite link** is a *role* of the connector.

You would never say "the anchor talks to the producer through thin air." You would specify the *channel* (connector) and the *endpoint* (port/role). That is exactly what a C&C view does for software.

## 1.3 Distinguishing Features (C&C vs. Other Views)

| Feature | C&C View | Module View | Allocation View |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time | Runtime | Compile/Source time | Build/Deploy time |
| Element | Component / Connector | Module / Package | Software ↔ Hardware / File system / Team |
| Question | "How do runtime parts interact?" | "How is code organized?" | "Where does each part run / who builds it?" |
| QA focus | Performance, availability, security | Modifiability, reusability | Build cost, deploy feasibility |

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU numerical parameters** that may appear in problems:
> - **Latency unit:** milliseconds (**ms**) — typical component delays range from **1 ms to 100 ms**.
> - **Throughput unit:** requests per second (**req/s**) — common systems target **100–10 000 req/s**.
> - **Availability target:** expressed as "nines" — e.g., **99.9% (three nines)** = 8.77 hours downtime/year; **99.99%** = 52.6 minutes/year.
> - **Standard failure rate ($\lambda$):** typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ failures/hour** for well-engineered components.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Section 3)

## 2.1 Component Decomposition — The C&C Anatomy

A C&C view is essentially a **typed, directed graph** where:
- **Nodes** = components and connectors.
- **Edges** = attachment relations between ports and roles.
- **Constraints** = rules restricting valid attachments.

### 2.1.1 Components — Theory Breakdown

A *component* in C&C is:
1. A **runtime entity** (it exists only when the system is running).
2. Has an **identity** and **state**.
3. **Encapsulates** computation.
4. **Communicates only via ports** — internal structure is hidden.
5. Is **independently deployable / schedulable** (in most cases).

**Why "independently schedulable" matters:**
- It enables **parallel execution** — multiple components can run concurrently.
- It enables **distribution** — components can live on different machines.
- It enables **independent failure analysis** — one component can fail without taking others down (in principle).

### 2.1.2 Connectors — Theory Breakdown

A *connector* is:
1. A **first-class element** of the architecture (not a mere line).
2. Defines a **protocol** of interaction.
3. Mediates **all communication** between the components attached to it.
4. May implement **non-trivial logic** (buffering, routing, security, transformation).

**Why treat connectors as first-class?**
- A "line" on a UML diagram says nothing about *how* components interact.
- A connector element forces the architect to specify *which protocol* (REST, gRPC, Kafka), *which semantics* (sync, async, fire-and-forget, request-reply), and *which qualities* (latency, throughput, security).

### 2.1.3 Ports, Roles, Interfaces — Theory Breakdown

- **Port** (component-side): "How the component wishes to be contacted."
- **Role** (connector-side): "What the connector expects from a participant."
- **Interface**: "The contract of valid messages/operations at a port or role."

A component's port *attaches* to a connector's role **only if** the interfaces are **compatible**. This is the **interfacing relation**.

## 2.2 Properties and Constraints — Detailed

### 2.2.1 Component-Level Properties
- *Computational:* CPU load, memory footprint, threads.
- *State-related:* persistent vs. transient, size of state.
- *Identity-related:* process ID, instance number, naming scheme.
- *Lifecycle:* creation time, termination conditions.

### 2.2.2 Connector-Level Properties
- *Protocol:* synchronous / asynchronous; request-reply / one-way / streaming.
- *Performance:* latency, throughput, jitter.
- *Reliability:* retry policy, message ordering, durability, exactly-once semantics.
- *Security:* encryption, authentication, authorization rules.

### 2.2.3 System-Level Constraints (KTU High-Yield)
- **Architectural Style constraints:** "All inter-service communication must be via the message bus."
- **Trust boundary constraints:** "External clients must terminate at the API gateway; they cannot directly invoke internal services."
- **Performance constraints:** "End-to-end latency must be ≤ 200 ms for 95% of requests."
- **Availability constraints:** "System availability must be ≥ 99.9%."

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value and conditional operators use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown parsing. **Never** use the raw `|` character inside table cells.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{C_i} + T_{K_i} \right)$ | End-to-end latency across n components and n-1 connectors. | ms |
| 2 | $A_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | System availability in series. | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)$ | System availability in parallel (redundancy). | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Series failure rate. | failures/hr |
| 5 | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures. | hr |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput}_{pipe} = \min_{f \in F} r_f$ | Pipe-filter throughput = slowest filter. | req/s |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{T_{serial}}{T_{parallel}}$ | Speedup from parallelization (Amdahl-style). | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_{at-least-one} = 1 - (1 - r)^{n}$ | Probability ≥1 of n redundant replicas succeeding. | dimensionless |
| 9 | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu (\mu - \lambda)}$ | Mean response time in M/M/1 queue (Little's result). | s |
| 10 | $U = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Server utilization in M/M/1. | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The C&C view is **not academic** — it is used daily in industry:

- **Performance Engineering:** SREs use C&C diagrams to identify latency hotspots by tracing components along the call graph.
- **Capacity Planning:** Cloud architects use connector throughput limits to size infrastructure.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Identifying the **critical path** in a C&C diagram tells you what to back up first.
- **Security Auditing:** Connectors crossing **trust boundaries** (e.g., the DMZ) are the focal points of security reviews.
- **Microservices Migration:** When moving from a monolith to microservices, the C&C view defines the **target** architecture.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Section 4)

## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Latency (General Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A user request flows through several components and connectors. The total response time is the sum of all delays along the path.

**Given:** $n$ components $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ connected in series by $n-1$ connectors $K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_{n-1}$. Each component $C_i$ has processing time $T_{C_i}$; each connector $K_i$ has transit time $T_{K_i}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the call path.**

The request enters $C_1$, then traverses $K_1$ to $C_2$, then $K_2$ to $C_3$, and so on until $C_n$.

**Step 2 — Write the latency equation.**

$$
T_{e2e} = T_{C_1} + T_{K_1} + T_{C_2} + T_{K_2} + \cdots + T_{C_{n-1}} + T_{K_{n-1}} + T_{C_n}
$$

**Step 3 — Compact summation form.**

$$
T_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{C_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} T_{K_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example (numerical).**

Given: $T_{C_1} = 3,\ T_{C_2} = 5,\ T_{C_3} = 4,\ T_{K_1} = 2,\ T_{K_2} = 3$ (all in ms).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{e2e} &= 3 + 2 + 5 + 3 + 4 \\
&= 17 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret bottleneck.**

The largest single contributor is $C_2$ at 5 ms — the optimization target. The connector overhead (5 ms) is 29% of total time, showing that **connector optimization is often as impactful as component optimization**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series Availability (Multiplicative)

**Step 1 — Define availability.**

For a single element with up-time fraction $A$, the down-time fraction is $(1 - A)$.

**Step 2 — Series dependency.**

For a series chain, *all* elements must be up. Assuming independence:
$$
A_{sys} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \cdots \times A_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sys} &= 0.995 \times 0.99 \times 0.999 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \\
&= 0.935 \quad (\text{i.e., } 93.5\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Five 97–99.9% reliable elements give only 93.5% system availability. This is the **engineering motivation for redundancy** (parallel availability, see formula 3).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parallel Availability (Redundancy)

**Step 1 — Define parallel configuration.**

A system is **up** if *at least one* of $n$ redundant replicas is up.

**Step 2 — Compute.**

Probability all are down:
$$
P(\text{all down}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)
$$

Probability at least one is up:
$$
A_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example (2 redundant replicas, each 90%).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{sys} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9) \times (1 - 0.9) \\
&= 1 - 0.01 \\
&= 0.99 \quad (\text{i.e., } 99\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Two 90% replicas in parallel give 99% — a 10× improvement. This is why KTU examiners ask this: **redundancy is the architectural lever for availability.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pipe-Filter Throughput Bottleneck

**Step 1 — Define per-filter rate.**

Filter $f$ processes at $r_f$ data units per second.

**Step 2 — Pipeline steady-state.**

In steady state, the entire pipeline runs at the rate of its slowest filter (otherwise the slowest filter would accumulate an infinite queue):
$$
\text{Throughput} = \min_{f \in F} r_f
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example.**

Three filters: $r_{A} = 100$, $r_{B} = 60$, $r_{C} = 90$ req/s.

$$
\text{Throughput} = \min(100, 60, 90) = 60 \text{ req/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify optimization target.**

Filter B (60 req/s) is the bottleneck. Strategies:
1. **Cache:** add an LRU cache inside B to skip repeated work.
2. **Parallelize internally:** split B's workload across 2 threads (if work is divisible).
3. **Replace:** swap B for a more efficient implementation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three C&C Styles

| Criterion | Client-Server | Pipe-Filter | Publish-Subscribe |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Components** | Clients, Server(s) | Filters | Publishers, Subscribers |
| **Connectors** | RPC, HTTP, SQL | Pipes (unidirectional streams) | Event bus / broker |
| **Coupling** | Tight to server contract | Loose (filters independent) | Very loose (no mutual knowledge) |
| **Data Flow** | Request-reply | Stream | Event-driven, async |
| **State** | Server typically stateful | Stateless filters | Bus may buffer; components often stateless |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale server) | Horizontal (add filters) | Horizontal (add subscribers) |
| **Failure Impact** | Server failure = total outage | Slowest filter = bottleneck | Bus failure = total outage |
| **Best Use Case** | Web apps, OLTP | Data pipelines, compilers | Real-time, event-driven systems |
| **Drawback** | Server bottleneck | Cannot easily request random access | Hard to debug, eventual consistency |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Section 4 diagrams continued)

## 4.1 Detailed C&C Diagram — E-Commerce Checkout System

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef componentStyle fill:#0d9488,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef connectorStyle fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef portStyle fill:#fde047,stroke:#a16207,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef interfaceStyle fill:#f9a8d4,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px

    subgraph External["External World"]
        USER((EndUser)):::componentStyle
    end

    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"]
        WEB[WebServer]:::componentStyle
        CART[CartService]:::componentStyle
        ORD[OrderService]:::componentStyle
    end

    subgraph BackendLayer["Backend Layer"]
        PAY[PaymentService]:::componentStyle
        INV[InventoryService]:::componentStyle
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["Data Layer"]
        ORDDB[(OrderDB)]:::componentStyle
        INVDB[(InventoryDB)]:::componentStyle
        PAYDB[(PaymentDB)]:::componentStyle
    end

    WEB_PORT((HTTPSPort)):::portStyle
    CART_IN((CartInPort)):::portStyle
    CART_OUT((CartOutPort)):::portStyle
    ORD_IN((OrderInPort)):::portStyle
    ORD_PAY((OrderPayPort)):::portStyle
    ORD_INV((OrderInvPort)):::portStyle
    PAY_IN((PayInPort)):::portStyle
    INV_IN((InvInPort)):::portStyle

    HTTPS_CONN[HTTPSBus]:::connectorStyle
    RPC_CONN[InternalRPC]:::connectorStyle
    DB_CONN[JDBCConnector]:::connectorStyle

    HTTPS_CLIENT((ClientRole)):::portStyle
    HTTPS_SERVER((ServerRole)):::portStyle
    RPC_CALLER((CallerRole)):::portStyle
    RPC_CALLEE((CalleeRole)):::portStyle
    DB_READER((ReadRole)):::portStyle
    DB_WRITER((WriterRole)):::portStyle

    USER --- WEB_PORT
    WEB_PORT --- HTTPS_CLIENT
    HTTPS_CLIENT === HTTPS_CONN
    HTTPS_CONN === HTTPS_SERVER
    HTTPS_SERVER --- CART_IN
    CART_IN --- CART

    CART --- CART_OUT
    CART_OUT --- RPC_CALLER
    RPC_CALLER === RPC_CONN
    RPC_CONN === RPC_CALLEE
    RPC_CALLEE --- ORD_IN
    ORD_IN --- ORD

    ORD --- ORD_PAY
    ORD_PAY --- RPC_CALLER
    ORD --- ORD_INV
    ORD_INV --- RPC_CALLER

    ORD --- ORD_IN
    ORD_IN --- DB_READER
    DB_READER === DB_CONN
    DB_CONN === DB_WRITER
    DB_WRITER --- ORDDB

    PAY --- PAY_IN
    INV --- INV_IN
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Teal boxes** = Components (computational entities).
> - **Red boxes** = Connectors (interaction mechanisms).
> - **Yellow circles** = Ports (on components) and Roles (on connectors).
> - **Double-line `===`** = an attachment between a role and a port through a connector.
> - Components are **layered**: External → Application → Backend → Data.
