# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap (Section 5)

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Regular]
**Define Component-and-Connector (C&C) view. List any FOUR elements of this view.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Component-and-Connector (C&C) view** is a view of software architecture that describes a system's **runtime structure** as a graph of **components** (units of computation) and **connectors** (units of interaction), connected through typed **ports** and **roles** governed by **interfaces**.

The four elements are:
1. **Component** — e.g., process, service, thread.
2. **Connector** — e.g., RPC, message bus, pipe.
3. **Port** — interaction endpoint on a component.
4. **Role** — interaction endpoint on a connector.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing with brief clarity.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]
**Mention any THREE differences between a Module view and a Component-and-Connector view.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Module View | C&C View |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static, source-level decomposition. | Dynamic, runtime-level decomposition. |
| 2 | Elements: modules, packages, classes. | Elements: components, connectors, ports, roles. |
| 3 | QA focus: modifiability, reusability. | QA focus: performance, availability, security, concurrency. |
| 4 | Audience: developers, maintainers. | Audience: integrators, SREs, performance engineers. |

*(Any three valid differences fetch 3 marks: 1 per difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** For each module, there is **internal choice** — students answer **one** of two 14-mark questions. Two full-length alternatives follow.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **elements, relations, properties, and constraints** of the Component-and-Connector view. Give an example C&C diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Client-Server** and **Publish-Subscribe** C&C styles. For each, list the components, connectors, two advantages, and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**1. Elements (2 marks):**
- **Component:** A principal unit of computation/state at runtime. E.g., process, service, thread, object.
- **Connector:** A principal unit of interaction. E.g., RPC, pipe, message bus.
- **Port:** A component-side interaction point.
- **Role:** A connector-side interaction point.
- **Interface:** The contract on a port or role.

**2. Relations (1.5 marks):**
- **Attachment:** a port of a component is attached to a role of a connector.
- **Interfacing:** the port's interface must be compatible with the role's interface.

**3. Properties (1.5 marks):**
- *Component:* state, CPU/memory, identity.
- *Connector:* protocol, latency, throughput, reliability, security.
- *System:* end-to-end latency, system availability, scalability, throughput.

**4. Constraints (1 mark):**
- Architectural style constraints: e.g., "All inter-service communication must use the message bus."
- Trust boundary constraints: e.g., "External clients must not directly access the internal DB."
- Performance constraints: e.g., "End-to-end latency ≤ 200 ms."

**5. Example (1 mark):**
A web app with `WebServer` (component) attached via its `httpPort` to the `HTTPSBus` connector (role `clientRole` and `serverRole`), the latter attached to the `AppServer` (port `httpIn`).

*(Refer to Section 4 Diagram 4.1 for a full schematic.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Client-Server (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Components | Clients (UI), one or more Servers (data/business logic). |
| Connectors | RPC, HTTP, SQL. |
| Use case | Banking app, e-commerce site. |
| Advantage 1 | Centralized data and business rules. |
| Advantage 2 | Easy to enforce security at the server. |
| Limitation | Server is a single point of failure and scalability bottleneck. |

**Publish-Subscribe (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Components | Publishers, Subscribers. |
| Connectors | Event bus / message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ). |
| Use case | Real-time analytics, IoT, microservices. |
| Advantage 1 | Loose coupling; publishers and subscribers are unaware of each other. |
| Advantage 2 | Easy to add new subscribers without modifying publishers. |
| Limitation | Harder to debug, eventual consistency, ordering challenges. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** A 3-tier web application consists of a **Web Server**, **Application Server**, and **Database**, connected by an **HTTP connector** and a **JDBC connector**. Given the following data:
- Web Server processing time: **3 ms**; HTTP transit: **2 ms**
- App Server processing time: **8 ms**; JDBC transit: **1 ms**
- Database processing time: **12 ms**
- Compute the **total end-to-end latency**. Identify the **bottleneck** and recommend **two optimizations.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Pipe-and-Filter** C&C style with a diagram. A pipeline has three filters with processing rates of **120 req/s, 75 req/s,** and **100 req/s** respectively. Calculate the **pipeline throughput** and the **filter to be optimized.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Identify the chain (1 mark):**

$$
\text{WebServer} \xrightarrow{HTTP} \text{AppServer} \xrightarrow{JDBC} \text{Database}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the latencies (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{e2e} &= T_{Web} + T_{HTTP} + T_{App} + T_{JDBC} + T_{DB} \\
&= 3 + 2 + 8 + 1 + 12 \\
&= 26 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bottleneck identification (1 mark):**

The **Database** at **12 ms** is the largest contributor — it is the bottleneck.

**Step 4 — Two optimizations (2 marks):**

1. **Database optimization:** add indexes, denormalize hot tables, introduce a read-replica, or move heavy queries to a cache (Redis) to avoid hitting the DB.
2. **Asynchronous processing:** move non-critical DB writes to a background queue (event-driven approach) so the request returns faster.
3. *(Alternative)* **Query optimization:** rewrite slow queries, batch operations, or use prepared statements.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Pipe-and-Filter explanation (3 marks):**

In the **Pipe-and-Filter** style:
- **Filters** are components that **transform** a stream of data.
- **Pipes** are unidirectional connectors that carry data from one filter to the next.
- Each filter is **independent** and **composable**.
- **Use cases:** Unix shell pipelines, compiler phases (lex → parse → semantic → codegen), signal processing, ETL pipelines.

**Step 2 — Throughput calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput}_{pipe} &= \min(120, 75, 100) \\
&= 75 \text{ req/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Filter to optimize (1 mark):**

**Filter 2 (75 req/s)** is the bottleneck. Optimization candidates:
1. **Cache:** add a memoization layer inside Filter 2 for repeated inputs.
2. **Parallelize internally:** split Filter 2's work across multiple worker threads.
3. **Replace:** swap Filter 2 for a more efficient algorithm/implementation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks — and How to Avoid Them**
> 1. **Treating connectors as mere arrows.** A C&C diagram **must** show connectors as named architectural elements (boxes) with attached roles. Drawing a single arrow between two components and labeling it "HTTP" is **incomplete** and loses up to **3 marks** in a 14-mark answer.
> 2. **Confusing class/component.** A *class definition* is a **module** element; an *instance of that class running as a process* is a **component**. Examiners look for this distinction.
> 3. **Adding availabilities instead of multiplying.** In a series chain, $A_{sys} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \cdots \times A_n$. A common error is to write $A_1 + A_2$ — this is **wrong** and loses the full 2 marks allocated.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify connector type.** Saying "components communicate via network" is vague. Always state: *"asynchronous message bus (Kafka)"* or *"synchronous RPC (gRPC)"*.
> 5. **No ports/roles in the diagram.** KTU 2024 specifically tests these. A diagram missing ports and roles is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Using `|` inside markdown tables.** A LaTeX expression like $\lvert x \rvert$ typed as `|x|` in a markdown table will break the table. **Always** use `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert` in tables.
> 7. **Not labelling axes/legend in diagrams.** A Mermaid diagram must clearly distinguish components, connectors, ports, and roles — either via color, shape, or a legend.
> 8. **Skipping the optimization recommendation.** Quantitative questions on latency/throughput *always* carry a 1–2 mark "suggest optimization" sub-part. A number without a recommendation loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Component-and-Connector View (Module 2)**
> - **C&C view = runtime view.** It answers *"how do runtime parts interact?"*
> - **Five core elements:** Component, Connector, Port, Role, Interface.
> - **Two core relations:** *Attachment* (port ↔ role) and *Interfacing* (interface compatibility).
> - **Connectors are first-class elements** — they have behavior, performance, and failure modes.
> - **Latency formula:** $T_{e2e} = \sum (T_{comp} + T_{conn})$.
> - **Series availability:** $A_{sys} = \prod A_i$ — multiplies, never adds.
> - **Parallel availability (redundancy):** $A_{sys} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$.
> - **Pipe-Filter throughput:** $\min(r_f)$ — slowest filter wins.
> - **M/M/1 queue response time:** $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ (advanced).
> - **Five major C&C styles:** Client-Server, Pipe-Filter, Publish-Subscribe, Shared-Data/Repository, Service-Oriented.
> - **Quality attributes addressed by C&C:** performance, availability, security, concurrency, scalability.
> - **Differences from Module view:** static vs. dynamic; code-level vs. process-level; developers vs. integrators.
> - **Diagrams must show:** components as boxes, connectors as *distinct named elements*, ports as small nodes, roles on connectors.
> - **Common KTU trap:** confusing *connectors* (architectural elements) with *links* (just relationships). A connector has its own behavior; a link does not.
> - **Exam-favorite vocabulary to use in answers:** *port, role, interface, attachment, interaction, runtime, latency, throughput, availability, bottleneck, redundancy, trust boundary, decoupling, asynchrony.*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layered
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Layered

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Layered Architecture** is a structural software architectural pattern that organizes the system into a hierarchy of distinct, horizontally stacked **layers** (also called *tiers* or *levels*), where each layer provides a specific, cohesive set of services to the layer immediately above it and consumes services from the layer immediately below it through well-defined, controlled interfaces.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, layered architecture falls under the broader category of **Architectural Styles / Architectural Patterns** used during the *Software Design* phase of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). The pattern enforces a strict **separation of concerns**, ensuring that the system is decomposed into cohesive, loosely-coupled modules arranged in a logical stack.

Mathematically, a layered system with $n$ layers can be represented as an ordered set:
$$L = \{L_1, L_2, L_3, \dots, L_n\}$$

where $L_1$ is typically the **topmost** (Presentation) layer and $L_n$ is the **bottommost** (Data) layer. Communication is governed by the **adjacency rule**: a layer $L_i$ may only invoke services of $L_{i+1}$, never $L_{i+2}$ or lower.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Multi-Story Office Building**

Imagine a corporate office tower:

- **Ground Floor (Reception / Lobby):** This is where visitors (the *user*) first interact. The receptionist only handles greetings, queries, and directing people. The receptionist does **not** do accounting.
- **Middle Floors (Departments like HR, Finance, Engineering):** Each floor has a specific job. HR handles people issues, Finance handles money. The HR floor never directly goes to the Finance floor's internal lockers — they pass requests through proper channels.
- **Basement (Archives / Storage / Servers):** The physical files and data vaults. Nobody outside the building even knows what's down there. The top floor doesn't care *how* the file is retrieved, only *that* it is.

Each floor is a **layer**. A floor can call upon the floor directly below it, but never skips floors. This is **exactly** how layered software architecture works:

| Building Concept | Software Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Reception Desk | Presentation Layer (UI) |
| Department Floors | Business Logic Layer |
| Basement Vaults | Data Access / Database Layer |

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Layer:** A logical grouping of related functionalities (e.g., UI, Business Rules, Data).
- **Tier:** A *physical* deployment of layers on separate machines (often confused with "layer" — *tier* = hardware, *layer* = logical).
- **Closed Layer:** A layer that strictly requests services only from the layer directly below it.
- **Open Layer:** A layer that can bypass and request services from any lower layer (breaks the strict adjacency rule).
- **N-Tier Architecture:** A specific instance where **N** layers are physically distributed across N machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The KTU examiner often tests the difference between *Layer* (logical) and *Tier* (physical). Marking is awarded for explicitly clarifying this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Standard Layers (Classical 4-Layer Model)

The most widely adopted layered model in industry and academia consists of the following layers, ordered from top to bottom:

### 2.1.1 Presentation Layer (Layer 1 — Top)
- **Responsibility:** Handles all user interaction. Renders the User Interface (UI), captures user input, and displays results.
- **Technologies:** HTML/CSS/JS, React, Angular, JSP, Swing, mobile UI frameworks.
- **Communicates with:** Business Logic Layer.
- **Key Principle:** Contains *no* business rules — only display logic and input validation.

### 2.1.2 Business Logic Layer / Application Layer (Layer 2)
- **Responsibility:** Core domain logic. Validates business rules, processes data, orchestrates workflows, performs calculations, and applies policies.
- **Technologies:** Java, Python, C#, Node.js server-side code.
- **Communicates with:** Presentation Layer (above) and Persistence Layer (below).
- **Key Principle:** *Heart of the system.* Must be technology-agnostic with respect to UI and DB.

### 2.1.3 Persistence Layer / Data Access Layer (Layer 3)
- **Responsibility:** Abstracts the storage mechanism. Translates business objects into database operations (CRUD) and vice versa. Implements the **Repository / DAO pattern**.
- **Technologies:** Hibernate, JPA, JDBC, SQLAlchemy, Entity Framework.
- **Communicates with:** Business Logic Layer (above) and Database Layer (below).
- **Key Principle:** Encapsulates *all* SQL/data-store-specific code.

### 2.1.4 Database Layer (Layer 4 — Bottom)
- **Responsibility:** Actual data storage, retrieval, and management. The physical persistence engine.
- **Technologies:** MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, MongoDB, file systems.
- **Communicates with:** Persistence Layer only.
- **Key Principle:** Should never be directly accessed by upper layers.

## 2.2 Architectural Rules and Design Constraints

1. **Adjacency Rule:** $L_i \to L_{i+1}$ only (Closed architecture). Violations lead to *spaghetti architecture*.
2. **No Upward Calls:** Lower layers MUST NOT call upper layers. This creates cyclic dependencies.
3. **Layer Encapsulation:** Each layer's internal implementation is hidden; only its public interface is exposed.
4. **Request Flow (Top-Down):** A request originates at $L_1$ and propagates downward: $L_1 \to L_2 \to L_3 \to L_4$.
5. **Response Flow (Bottom-Up):** The result travels upward: $L_4 \to L_3 \to L_2 \to L_1$.

## 2.3 Real-World Examples (KTU Favorites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examples the KTU Examiner Loves:**

| System / Domain | Layer Mapping |
|---|---|
| **OSI 7-Layer Model** (Networking) | Application → Presentation → Session → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical |
| **Java EE / Spring Boot** | Controller → Service → Repository → Database |
| **MVC Pattern** (Strict view) | View (Presentation) → Controller (Application) → Model (Data) |
| **TCP/IP Protocol Suite** | Application → Transport → Internet → Network Access |
| **Online Banking Portal** | Web UI → Transaction Engine → Account DB → DBMS |

## 2.4 Quality Attributes Affected

- **High Cohesion:** Each layer groups functionally similar responsibilities. **Cohesion metric** $\approx$ **High** (target value: **7/7** on cohesion scale).
- **Low Coupling:** Layers interact only through defined interfaces. **Coupling metric** $\approx$ **Low** (target value: **Loose**).
- **Modifiability:** A change to the database (e.g., MySQL → MongoDB) only impacts the Persistence layer, not the UI.
- **Performance:** Generally **lower** than monolithic or peer-to-peer architectures due to request/response traversal overhead.
- **Testability:** **High** — each layer can be unit-tested independently using mock objects for adjacent layers.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| **Concept** | **Description** | **KTU Expected Answer** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of classical layers | Standard count | **4 layers** |
| Layer vs Tier | Logical vs Physical | **Layer = logical, Tier = physical** |
| Request direction | Originates at | **Top layer (UI)** |
| Response direction | Returns to | **Top layer (UI)** |
| Closed layer rule | Can call only | **Layer directly below** |
| Open layer rule | Can call | **Any lower layer** |
| Adjacent layer violation | Skipping layers | **Anti-pattern** |
| Best-suited application | Type | **Enterprise / business apps with clear separation** |
| Worst-suited application | Type | **High-performance real-time systems / streaming** |
| Coupling achieved | Inter-layer | **Loose coupling** |
| Cohesion achieved | Intra-layer | **High cohesion** |
| OSI layers (analog) | Networking reference | **7 layers** |
| Spring Boot mapping | Industry standard | **Controller $\to$ Service $\to$ Repository $\to$ DB** |

> **Engineering Utility:** Layered architecture is the **de facto** industry standard for enterprise software (banking, e-commerce, ERP systems). It enables **independent deployability**, **team parallelization** (UI team, backend team, DB team work simultaneously), and **technology substitution** without full rewrite.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: Request Flow & Dependency Resolution

## 3.1 Formal Request Flow Derivation

Let us model a user-initiated request as a directed function call sequence through a 4-layer system.

### Step 1: Define the layer set
$$L = \{L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4\}$$
where $L_1$ = Presentation, $L_2$ = Business Logic, $L_3$ = Persistence, $L_4$ = Database.

### Step 2: Define the dependency relation
The adjacency rule states that a layer $L_i$ depends on $L_{i+1}$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$. Formally:
$$D = \{(L_1, L_2), (L_2, L_3), (L_3, L_4)\}$$

### Step 3: Request initialization
A user action $A$ enters at $L_1$. We represent this as a tuple:
$$R_0 = (A, L_1, t_0)$$
where $t_0$ is the entry timestamp.

### Step 4: Layer-by-layer delegation
At each layer $L_i$, the request $R$ is transformed and passed downward:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } L_1: \quad & R_1 = f_1(R_0) \quad \text{(UI captures input, validates format)} \\
\text{At } L_2: \quad & R_2 = f_2(R_1) \quad \text{(Business rules applied, logic executed)} \\
\text{At } L_3: \quad & R_3 = f_3(R_2) \quad \text{(Query constructed, DB call prepared)} \\
\text{At } L_4: \quad & R_4 = f_4(R_3) \quad \text{(Data persisted / retrieved from DBMS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Response propagation (reverse flow)
The response travels upward through the *same* layers:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } L_4: \quad & P_4 = R_4 \quad \text{(Raw data)} \\
\text{At } L_3: \quad & P_3 = g_3(P_4) \quad \text{(Mapped to domain objects)} \\
\text{At } L_2: \quad & P_2 = g_2(P_3) \quad \text{(Processed per business rules)} \\
\text{At } L_1: \quad & P_1 = g_1(P_2) \quad \text{(Formatted for UI display)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Total traversal cost
If each layer crossing incurs a latency cost $c$, the total round-trip latency for a single request is:
$$T_{\text{total}} = 2(n-1) \cdot c + T_{\text{DB}} = 2(4-1) \cdot c + T_{\text{DB}} = 6c + T_{\text{DB}}$$

This is precisely why **performance optimization** in layered systems often involves *caching* at $L_2$ to avoid round-trips to $L_4$.

## 3.2 Closed vs Open Layer Mathematical Comparison

### Closed Layer Architecture
A closed layer $L_i$ invokes only $L_{i+1}$. The number of allowed edges in the call graph is:
$$E_{\text{closed}} = (n-1) \quad \text{for } n \text{ layers}$$

For $n = 4$: $E_{\text{closed}} = 3$ edges.

### Open Layer Architecture
An open layer $L_i$ may invoke any lower layer. The number of allowed edges is:
$$E_{\text{open}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

For $n = 4$: $E_{\text{open}} = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ edges.

**Interpretation:** Open architecture is more *flexible* but sacrifices the **information hiding** and **portability** benefits of the closed approach.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1([End User])
    pres1["L1 PRESENTATION LAYER UI FORMS AND VIEWS"]
    biz1["L2 BUSINESS LOGIC LAYER RULES AND WORKFLOWS"]
    pers1["L3 PERSISTENCE LAYER DAO AND REPOSITORY"]
    db1["L4 DATABASE LAYER MYSQL OR MONGODB"]

    user1 --> pres1
    pres1 -->|"Request  A"| biz1
    biz1 -->|"Query B"| pers1
    pers1 -->|"SQL C"| db1
    db1 -->|"Result D"| pers1
    pers1 -->|"Domain Object E"| biz1
    biz1 -->|"Processed Output F"| pres1
    pres1 -->|"Rendered Response G"| user1
```

## 4.2 Closed vs Open Layer Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Closed Architecture
        c1["L1"] -->|"Allowed"| c2["L2"]
        c2 -->|"Allowed"| c3["L3"]
        c3 -->|"Allowed"| c4["L4"]
    end

    subgraph Open Architecture
        o1["L1"] --> o2["L2"]
        o1 -.->|"Bypass Allowed"| o3["L3"]
        o1 -.->|"Bypass Allowed"| o4["L4"]
        o2 --> o3
        o2 -.->|"Bypass Allowed"| o4
        o3 --> o4
    end
```

## 4.3 Real-World Mapping — Spring Boot Application

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    browserA["Web Browser Client"]
    controllerA["CONTROLLER LAYER REST API ENDPOINTS"]
    serviceA["SERVICE LAYER BUSINESS LOGIC"]
    repoA["REPOSITORY LAYER SPRING DATA JPA"]
    dbA["DATABASE LAYER POSTGRESQL SERVER"]

    browserA -->|"HTTP GET POST"| controllerA
    controllerA -->|"Method Call"| serviceA
    serviceA -->|"CRUD Operation"| repoA
    repoA -->|"JDBC Query"| dbA
    dbA -->|"Result Set"| repoA
    repoA -->|"Entity Object"| serviceA
    serviceA -->|"DTO"| controllerA
    controllerA -->|"JSON Response"| browserA
```

## 4.4 Anti-Pattern — Skipping Layers (Spaghetti)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bad1["L1 Presentation"]
    bad2["L2 Business"]
    bad3["L3 Persistence"]
    bad4["L4 Database"]

    bad1 -->|"Allowed"| bad2
    bad2 -->|"Allowed"| bad3
    bad3 -->|"Allowed"| bad4
    bad1 -.->|"VIOLATION Skip"| bad3
    bad2 -.->|"VIOLATION Skip"| bad4
    bad1 -.->|"VIOLATION Skip"| bad4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The dashed lines marked **VIOLATION** represent the *anti-pattern* of skipping layers. This is one of the most common deductions in KTU valuation — always justify why a particular layer bypass is **not** used in a well-designed closed layered system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Layered Architectural Style. List any four standard layers used in it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Layered Architecture is a software architectural style that organizes a system into a hierarchy of layers, where each layer performs a specific role and provides services to the layer above while consuming services from the layer below.

The four standard layers are:
1. **Presentation Layer** — handles user interface and input/output.
2. **Business Logic Layer** — implements core domain rules and workflows.
3. **Persistence Layer** — manages data access and storage operations.
4. **Database Layer** — actual data store (DBMS).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Layer and a Tier in software architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Layer | Tier |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | **Logical** decomposition | **Physical** deployment |
| Location | Exists conceptually in code | Exists on separate hardware/servers |
| Example | Presentation, Business, Data layers | 3-tier = 3 separate machines |
| Coupling | Logical via interfaces | Physical via network |

A 4-layer system can be deployed as a 1-tier (all on one machine) or 4-tier system (each on a separate machine).

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **conflate layer with tier** in the opening sentence of their answer. This alone can cost **1–2 marks**. Always explicitly state *"layer = logical, tier = physical"* in your first 2 lines.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a) Explain the Layered Architectural Style in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the responsibilities of each of the four standard layers. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Layered Architectural Style decomposes a system into a stack of layers, each fulfilling a distinct role. Communication is restricted to adjacent layers (closed architecture), enforcing a clean separation of concerns.

**Layer Responsibilities:**

1. **Presentation Layer (L1):** Manages all user-facing components. Validates input formats, displays output, and forwards processed requests to L2. Contains *no* business logic. *Example:* HTML forms, React components.

2. **Business Logic Layer (L2):** The core engine. Implements business rules, performs calculations, orchestrates workflows, and applies domain policies. *Example:* Order processing, tax computation.

3. **Persistence Layer (L3):** Translates business objects into database queries (INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE) using the DAO/Repository pattern. Hides DB-specific syntax from L2. *Example:* Hibernate, JDBC code.

4. **Database Layer (L4):** The actual data store. Executes queries, manages transactions, and returns result sets. *Example:* MySQL, Oracle.

**Diagram:**

```
[L1 Presentation]  <-- User
        |
        v
[L2 Business Logic]
        |
        v
[L3 Persistence]
        |
        v
[L4 Database]
```

**Advantages:** high cohesion, loose coupling, easy testability, parallel development, technology substitution.

**(b) Discuss the merits and demerits of the Layered Architecture. Justify when it is most appropriate to use. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Merits:**
- **Modifiability:** Changes in one layer (e.g., switching from MySQL to MongoDB) require minimal changes elsewhere.
- **Testability:** Each layer can be unit-tested with mocks/stubs.
- **Reusability:** Lower layers can serve multiple upper layers.
- **Team Productivity:** UI team, backend team, and DB team can work in parallel.
- **Standardization:** Industry-standard, well-understood, easy onboarding.

**Demerits:**
- **Performance Overhead:** Request must traverse all layers; can be slow for high-throughput systems.
- **Cascading Changes:** A poorly designed layer boundary can force changes to ripple upward.
- **Not Suitable for All Domains:** Real-time, embedded, or stream-processing systems suffer from latency.
- **Tight Coupling Risk (Open Layers):** If layer boundaries are violated, the architecture degrades into "spaghetti."

**When to Use:**
- Enterprise business applications (banking, ERP, CRM).
- Systems with clear separation between UI, logic, and data.
- Applications where maintainability and testability are prioritized over raw performance.

**When *Not* to Use:**
- High-frequency trading platforms.
- Real-time signal processing.
- Tiny, single-purpose scripts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a) Compare and contrast Closed Layered Architecture and Open Layered Architecture. Use diagrams to support your answer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Closed Layered | Open Layered |
|---|---|---|
| Layer Access | Each layer accesses **only** the layer directly below | Each layer can access **any** lower layer |
| Coupling | **Loose** (strict interfaces) | **Tighter** (direct deep access) |
| Portability | **High** (can replace any layer) | **Low** (upper layers depend on deep internals) |
| Performance | Slower (must traverse all layers) | **Faster** (can bypass) |
| Information Hiding | **Strong** | **Weakened** |
| Use Case | Large enterprise systems | Performance-critical internal tools |

**Closed Diagram:** Arrows only between consecutive layers ($L_1 \leftrightarrow L_2 \leftrightarrow L_3 \leftrightarrow L_4$).

**Open Diagram:** Arrows from $L_1$ directly to $L_3$ and $L_4$ are permitted.

**Conclusion:** Closed architecture is preferred in practice because the performance overhead is negligible in most business applications, and the benefits of encapsulation far outweigh the speed gain of open architecture.

**(b) With a real-world example (such as an Online Banking System), explain how the Layered Architecture is applied. Map each layer to a concrete component. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Scenario:** A customer transfers ₹5,000 from Account A to Account B via a web portal.

**Layer Mapping for Online Banking System:**

| Layer | Concrete Component | Role in Transfer Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 Presentation** | Web Portal (HTML + React) | Captures "From Account," "To Account," and "Amount" inputs |
| **L2 Business Logic** | Transaction Service Module | Validates sufficient balance, applies daily transfer limits, debits/credits accounts atomically |
| **L3 Persistence** | AccountRepository (Spring Data JPA) | Executes `UPDATE accounts SET balance=...` SQL statements |
| **L4 Database** | MySQL / Oracle DB | Stores and updates account balances in `accounts` table |

**Request Flow:**
1. User fills form in **L1**.
2. **L1** calls `transferFunds()` in **L2**.
3. **L2** validates business rules and calls `updateBalance()` in **L3**.
4. **L3** issues SQL `UPDATE` to **L4**.
5. **L4** confirms and result propagates back up to **L1** as a success/failure message displayed to the user.

This strict layering ensures the bank can swap MySQL for Oracle, or the web portal for a mobile app, with minimal impact on the business logic — a hallmark of **modifiability** and **portability**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

- [x] **Layered Architecture** = stack of layers with strict separation of concerns.
- [x] **4 Standard Layers:** Presentation $\to$ Business Logic $\to$ Persistence $\to$ Database.
- [x] **Layer = Logical**, **Tier = Physical** (favourite 3-mark question).
- [x] **Closed Architecture:** Only adjacent layer calls allowed.
- [x] **Open Architecture:** Any lower layer can be called directly.
- [x] **Request flows top-down**, **Response flows bottom-up**.
- [x] **Total layers traversed** $= 2(n-1)$ round-trips.
- [x] **Best for:** Enterprise apps, banking, ERP, CRM.
- [x] **Worst for:** Real-time, high-frequency, embedded, streaming systems.
- [x] **Real-world analogies:** OSI model (7 layers), TCP/IP (4 layers), Spring Boot (Controller $\to$ Service $\to$ Repository $\to$ DB).
- [x] **Quality attributes achieved:** High cohesion, loose coupling, high testability, good modifiability.
- [x] **Quality attributes sacrificed:** Performance (due to traversal overhead).
- [x] **Common anti-pattern:** Skipping layers (creating "spaghetti architecture").
- [x] **MVC $\neq$ Layered** strictly, but MVC is often *implemented using* layered style.
- [x] **Mention diagrams** in Part B answers — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for neat labeled diagrams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** In 14-mark answers, always structure your response as: (1) Definition $\to$ (2) Diagram $\to$ (3) Layer-wise explanation $\to$ (4) Advantages/Disadvantages $\to$ (5) Real-world example. This 5-part structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and maximizes marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repository
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Repository # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Software Configuration Management (SCM)**, a **Repository** is formally defined as a **centralized, shared, persistent, and managed data store** that contains all software configuration items (CIs) and related artifacts produced throughout the software development lifecycle. It serves as the **single source of truth** for the project, holding source code, design documents, test cases, build scripts, models, defect reports, and version history. According to KTU 2024 Scheme SCM terminology, a repository provides controlled access, auditability, version control, and integrity assurance for all software engineering artifacts. It is the technical backbone of the SCM process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > A Repository is a *logical and physical data model* that stores software artifacts along with their **versioning metadata**, **change history**, and **inter-relationship information**. It enables the four pillars of SCM: Configuration Identification, Version Control, Change Control, and Auditing. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy: A Library with a Smart Catalog System** Imagine a huge university library: - **The Library Building** = The Repository (the physical/digital storehouse) - **The Books** = Software Configuration Items (CIs) such as source code files, design documents - **The Card Catalog / Database Index** = Metadata of each item (author, edition, last modified, current location) - **The Librarian** = Repository Access Control & Check-in/Check-out mechanism - **Multiple Copies of the Same Book (different editions)** = Versions and Branches - **The Borrowing Register** = Audit log of who took what, when, and when returned Just as the library ensures: 1. Every book is properly cataloged (Identification) 2. Only one student borrows a book at a time (Check-out) 3. Editions are tracked (Versioning) 4. A record exists of all borrowings (Audit) ...a **Repository** ensures that all software artifacts are properly cataloged, controlled, versioned, and traceable across the development team. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A Repository is NOT just a folder. It is a *system* (database + version control engine + access policies + audit logs) that manages the entire lifecycle of software artifacts. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties of a Repository The following **standard metrics** characterize a well-engineered repository: - **Atomicity** of transactions - **ACID compliance** for state changes - **Storage Scalability** measured in GB/TB - **Concurrent User Support** (number of parallel checkouts) - **Lock Granularity** (file-level vs. package-level vs. project-level) - **Retention Period** for historical versions - **Mean Time To Recover (MTTR)** for corrupt states > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Principle:** A repository must be **durable**, **consistent**, **isolated**, and **transactional** to support professional software engineering practices. --- ## 1.4 Repository in the Broader SCM Context The repository is one of the **four primary SCM components** in the KTU 2024 Scheme: | SCM Component | Function | Repository's Role | |---|---|---| | Configuration Identification | Identify CIs | Stores CI identifiers | | Version Control | Track changes | Stores all versions | | Change Control | Manage changes | Records change requests & approvals | | **Repository** | **Central data store** | **Backs all three above** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Repository A repository is composed of the following structural layers (from KTU 2024 Module 2 perspective): ### Layer 1: Storage Layer - Persistent storage of artifacts (file system, database, or hybrid). - Stores both **content** (the actual file) and **metadata** (attributes). ### Layer 2: Versioning Layer - Maintains a **version graph** of every artifact. - Supports branching, merging, tagging, and baselining. - Typical data model: **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. ### Layer 3: Access Control Layer - Authentication, authorization, role-based permissions. - Implements **Check-in / Check-out** semantics. - Prevents concurrent overwrites using **lock mechanisms**. ### Layer 4: Audit & History Layer - Logs every transaction (read/write/lock). - Provides traceability for regulatory and process compliance. --- ## 2.2 Types of Repositories (KTU High-Yield Classification) ### 2.2.1 Based on Topology | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Centralized Repository** | Single server hosts all artifacts | SVN (Apache Subversion), CVS | | **Distributed Repository** | Each user has a full local clone | Git (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) | | **Hybrid Repository** | Combines centralized control with distributed workflow | Gerrit, Perforce | ### 2.2.2 Based on Content Type | Type | Stores | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Source Code Repository** | Code files, build scripts | Git, SVN | | **Document Repository** | Requirements, design docs | SharePoint, Confluence | | **Binary Artifact Repository** | Compiled binaries, libraries | JFrog Artifactory, Nexus | | **Model Repository** | UML models, diagrams | Enterprise Architect, MagicDraw | | **Test Repository** | Test cases, test data | TestRail, Zephyr | --- ## 2.3 Repository Operations (Core SCM Functions) The repository supports these **fundamental operations** (mandatory for KTU board exam): 1. **Check-in (Commit)**: Adding a new or modified CI to the repository. 2. **Check-out (Update)**: Extracting a CI for editing/viewing. 3. **Branch**: Creating a parallel line of development. 4. **Merge**: Combining branches. 5. **Tag / Label**: Marking a specific version (e.g., v1.0-release). 6. **Baseline**: A frozen, approved snapshot of a set of CIs. 7. **Diff / Compare**: Showing differences between versions. 8. **Rollback**: Reverting to a previous version. 9. **Lock / Unlock**: Exclusive access control. 10. **Audit Query**: Searching history of changes. --- ## 2.4 Logical Architecture of a Repository A modern repository has a **3-tier logical architecture**: ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TIER 1: CLIENT INTERFACE │ │ (CLI tools, GUI clients, IDE plugins) │ └──────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘ │ (API calls: HTTP/SSH/Git protocol) ┌──────────────────▼─────────────────────────┐ │ TIER 2: SERVICE / TRANSACTION LAYER │ │ (Auth, Access Control, Lock Manager, │ │ Version Engine, Diff Engine, Merge Tool) │ └──────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘ │ (SQL / File I/O) ┌──────────────────▼─────────────────────────┐ │ TIER 3: PERSISTENT STORAGE LAYER │ │ (Object Database + Metadata DB + │ │ Audit Logs + Object Store / Blob Store) │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Total Storage Used | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times V_i)$ | Sum of (content size × number of versions) | MB / GB | | Compression Ratio | $R_c = \dfrac{S_{uncompressed}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Effectiveness of delta compression | dimensionless | | Retrieval Time | $T_r = T_{lookup} + T_{transfer}$ | Total time to fetch a version | seconds (s) | | Concurrency Limit | $L_c = \dfrac{U_{max}}{M_{lock}}$ | Max users = Max users / Min lock duration | users | | Mean Time to Recovery | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{recovery}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Avg recovery time from corruption | hours (h) | | Repository Availability | $A = \dfrac{UPTIME}{UPTIME + DOWNTIME} \times 100\%$ | System uptime percentage | % | | Delta Storage Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \Delta V_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Savings via delta encoding | dimensionless | | Branching Factor | $B = \dfrac{N_{branches}}{N_{releases}}$ | Avg branches per release | dimensionless | | Audit Completeness | $A_c = \dfrac{N_{logged\_events}}{N_{total\_events}} \times 100\%$ | % of events recorded | % | | Check-out Latency | $T_{co} = T_{auth} + T_{lock\_acquire} + T_{fetch}$ | End-to-end checkout time | seconds (s) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The $MTTR$ and **Availability** formulas are the most commonly asked derivations in KTU Module 2 numerical/analytical questions. Always show the substitution step. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility A repository is **foundational** in: - **DevOps pipelines** (CI/CD source of truth) - **Aerospace & Defense** (DO-178C compliance — every artifact must be traceable) - **Banking & FinTech** (regulatory audit, SOX compliance) - **Medical Devices** (FDA 21 CFR Part 11 — electronic records & signatures) - **Open Source Collaboration** (GitHub enables global distributed teams) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The repository is the *only* component of SCM that physically persists. All SCM processes (identification, control, status accounting, auditing) are meaningless without a well-designed repository backing them. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Repository Storage Using Delta Compression In a repository that uses **delta encoding**, instead of storing every full version of a file, we store only the **differences (deltas)** between successive versions. This dramatically reduces storage. ### Given: - $n$ = total number of versions of a file - $V_i$ = size of version $i$ (full size) - $\Delta V_i$ = size of the delta between version $i-1$ and version $i$ - The first version must be stored in full (no prior baseline) ### Derivation: **Step 1: Storage without delta encoding (baseline approach)** $$S_{full} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$ **Reasoning:** Each version is stored as a complete, independent copy. This is the naive approach used by early SCM tools (e.g., RCS storing full copies). **Step 2: Storage with delta encoding** The first version is stored in full. Every subsequent version is stored as a delta from the previous one. $$S_{delta} = V_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \Delta V_i$$ **Reasoning:** The repository reconstructs any version $i$ by applying the chain of deltas: $$\text{Reconstruct}(V_i) = V_1 \oplus \Delta V_2 \oplus \Delta V_3 \oplus \dots \oplus \Delta V_i$$ where $\oplus$ denotes the **delta application operation**. **Step 3: Compute the storage savings** $$\Delta S = S_{full} - S_{delta} = \sum_{i=2}^{n} (V_i - \Delta V_i)$$ **Step 4: Compute the storage efficiency ratio** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{delta}}{S_{full}} = 1 - \frac{V_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \Delta V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}$$ **Step 5: Boundary condition check** - If $\Delta V_i = V_i$ (i.e., every version is completely different) → $\eta = 1 - \frac{n V_i}{n V_i} = 0$ (no savings, as expected). - If $\Delta V_i = 0$ (no change) → $\eta = 1 - \frac{V_1}{n V_1} = 1 - \frac{1}{n}$ (massive savings, approaching 100% for large $n$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Repository Availability **Given:** - $U$ = Total uptime in a measurement period - $D$ = Total downtime in the same period - $T$ = Total period length **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Total period is the sum of uptime and downtime. $$T = U + D$$ **Step 2:** Availability $A$ is defined as the fraction of time the repository is operational. $$A = \frac{U}{T}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $T = U + D$: $$A = \frac{U}{U + D}$$ **Step 4:** Express as a percentage (KTU board convention): $$A_{\%} = \frac{U}{U + D} \times 100\%$$ **Step 5:** Reliability equivalence — if we know the Mean Time Between Failures ($MTBF$) and Mean Time To Repair ($MTTR$): $$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$ This is the **standard reliability engineering formula** applied to repository infrastructure. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Repository Topologies | Criterion | Centralized (SVN) | Distributed (Git) | Hybrid (Perforce/Gerrit) | |---|---|---|---| | Single Point of Failure | Yes (high) | No (low) | Partial (medium) | | Offline Work | Not possible | Fully supported | Partial | | Storage per User | Low | High (full clone) | Medium | | Network Required | Always | Only for sync | Always for central ops | | Branching Cost | Expensive | Cheap & fast | Moderate | | Audit Trail | Strong (centralized log) | Distributed (each clone has log) | Strong (centralized) | | Scalability (users) | Limited (server bottleneck) | High | High | | Suitable for | Small teams, regulated industries | Open source, large teams | Enterprise, mixed workflows | --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A software project repository contains 10 versions of a source code file. The first version is 500 KB. Each subsequent version is on average 80% similar to its predecessor (i.e., only 20% changes). Calculate the storage saved using delta encoding. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Full storage (no delta) Each version is 500 KB. So: $$S_{full} = 10 \times 500 \text{ KB} = 5000 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 2:** Delta storage First version: 500 KB Each subsequent delta = $500 \times 0.20 = 100$ KB $$S_{delta} = 500 + 9 \times 100 = 500 + 900 = 1400 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 3:** Storage saved $$\Delta S = 5000 - 1400 = 3600 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 4:** Efficiency $$\eta = 1 - \frac{1400}{5000} = 1 - 0.28 = 0.72 = 72\%$$ **Answer:** The repository saves **3600 KB (72% storage efficiency)** by using delta encoding. > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** Always include the units (KB, MB, GB) in your final answer. KTU examiners explicitly award marks for unit correctness. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Repository Architecture & Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB devA[Developer A]:::userNode devB[Developer B]:::userNode devC[Developer C]:::userNode clientLayer[Client Interface Layer
CLI - GUI - IDE Plugin]:::clientNode authMod[Authentication Module]:::svcNode accCtrl[Access Control Module]:::svcNode lockMgr[Lock Manager]:::svcNode verEng[Version Engine
DAG Tracking]:::svcNode diffEng[Diff and Merge Engine]:::svcNode tagMgr[Tag and Baseline Manager]:::svcNode metaDB[(Metadata Database
CI Attributes and Versions)]:::dataNode blobStore[(Object Store
File Blobs and Deltas)]:::dataNode auditLog[(Audit Log
Transaction History)]:::dataNode devA --> clientLayer devB --> clientLayer devC --> clientLayer clientLayer --> authMod authMod --> accCtrl accCtrl --> lockMgr lockMgr --> verEng verEng --> diffEng diffEng --> tagMgr tagMgr --> metaDB tagMgr --> blobStore tagMgr --> auditLog classDef userNode fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef clientNode fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef svcNode fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dataNode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Check-in / Check-out Lifecycle (State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Available Available --> CheckedOut: User performs checkout
and acquires lock CheckedOut --> Modified: User edits CI locally Modified --> CheckedIn: User commits changes
and releases lock CheckedIn --> Available: Repository updates
version graph and audit log CheckedOut --> Available: User cancels checkout
lock released CheckedIn --> [*]: CI baselined
and frozen ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Repository Version Graph (DAG Structure) ```mermaid flowchart LR v1[Version 1.0
Baseline]:::verNode v2[Version 1.1]:::verNode v3[Version 1.2
Release Tag]:::relNode v4[Version 2.0
Branch A]:::branchNode v5[Version 2.1
Branch A]:::branchNode v6[Version 1.2.1
Hotfix]:::hotfixNode v7[Version 2.2
Merged]:::mergeNode v1 --> v2 v2 --> v3 v3 --> v4 v4 --> v5 v3 --> v6 v5 --> v7 v6 --> v7 classDef verNode fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef relNode fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef branchNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef hotfixNode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mergeNode fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Repository Interaction with SCM Processes ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RepositoryCore[Repository Core System] ident[Configuration Identification]:::procNode verctrl[Version Control]:::procNode chgctrl[Change Control]:::procNode statacc[Status Accounting]:::procNode audit[Auditing]:::procNode stor[Persistent Storage]:::dataNode end ident <--> stor verctrl <--> stor chgctrl <--> stor statacc <--> stor audit <--> stor classDef procNode fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dataNode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a Repository in the context of Software Configuration Management. List any four operations supported by a repository.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A *Repository* in SCM is a **centralized, shared, and managed data store** that persistently holds all software configuration items (CIs) — such as source code, documents, models, and build artifacts — along with their **version metadata**, **change history**, and **inter-relationship information**. It acts as the single source of truth for the entire software project, supporting identification, version control, change management, and audit. **Four operations (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Check-in (Commit)** — Adding a new or modified CI to the repository. 2. **Check-out (Update)** — Extracting a CI for editing or viewing. 3. **Branch / Merge** — Creating parallel development lines and combining them. 4. **Baseline / Tag** — Marking a frozen, approved snapshot of CIs as a release point. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Centralized and Distributed Repositories. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Centralized Repository | Distributed Repository | |---|---|---| | Architecture | Single central server holds all artifacts | Every user has a full local clone | | Network | Required for every operation | Required only for sync (push/pull) | | Failure tolerance | Single point of failure | Highly fault-tolerant | | Example | **SVN (Apache Subversion)** | **Git (GitHub / GitLab)** | (2 marks for the table + 1 mark for the examples) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **logical architecture of a Repository** in detail. Describe its three-tier structure with the function of each tier. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, derive the **storage savings achieved by delta encoding** in a version control repository. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Three-Tier Logical Architecture of a Repository (7 marks)** A modern repository is structured into three distinct tiers: **Tier 1 — Client Interface Layer (2 marks):** This is the topmost layer with which users directly interact. It includes: - **Command-Line Interface (CLI)** tools (e.g., `git`, `svn`). - **Graphical User Interface (GUI)** tools (e.g., TortoiseGit, SourceTree). - **IDE plugins** (e.g., Eclipse, IntelliJ, VS Code SCM extensions). - Communicates with the service layer using protocols such as HTTP/HTTPS, SSH, or the Git Smart HTTP protocol. **Tier 2 — Service / Transaction Layer (3 marks):** This is the brain of the repository. It contains: - **Authentication Module** — verifies user identity. - **Access Control Module** — enforces read/write permissions based on roles. - **Lock Manager** — implements check-in / check-out semantics to prevent concurrent overwrites. - **Version Engine** — tracks the version DAG, supports branching, merging, and tagging. - **Diff and Merge Engine** — computes textual or binary differences and assists in conflict resolution. - **Tag / Baseline Manager** — creates immutable snapshots of CIs at logical milestones (releases). **Tier 3 — Persistent Storage Layer (2 marks):** The bottom tier that physically stores everything: - **Metadata Database** — relational/NoSQL DB storing CI attributes (name, version, author, timestamps). - **Object / Blob Store** — stores actual file content (often using delta encoding for efficiency). - **Audit Log** — append-only log of every transaction (who, what, when, why). - May be implemented using PostgreSQL, MySQL, file systems, or object stores like Amazon S3. --- **(b) Derivation of Storage Savings via Delta Encoding (7 marks)** **Given (1 mark):** - $n$ = total number of versions - $V_i$ = size of version $i$ - $\Delta V_i$ = size of delta from version $i-1$ to $i$ **Full Storage (2 marks):** $$S_{full} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$ **Delta Storage (2 marks):** $$S_{delta} = V_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \Delta V_i$$ **Efficiency Calculation (1 mark):** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{delta}}{S_{full}} = 1 - \frac{V_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \Delta V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}$$ **Numerical Example (1 mark):** For $n = 5$ versions, each $V_i = 400$ KB, with each delta $\Delta V_i = 80$ KB (20% change): - $S_{full} = 5 \times 400 = 2000$ KB - $S_{delta} = 400 + 4 \times 80 = 720$ KB - $\eta = 1 - \frac{720}{2000} = 64\%$ savings. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Discuss the **different types of repositories** based on topology and content. Compare centralized vs. distributed repositories using a parameter matrix. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **check-in / check-out lifecycle** of a Configuration Item in a repository. Why is locking important? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Types of Repositories (7 marks)** **Based on Topology (3 marks):** 1. **Centralized Repository:** A single central server holds all artifacts. All clients connect to it for every operation. *Example: SVN.* 2. **Distributed Repository:** Every user has a complete local copy of the entire history. Operations are local; syncing is peer-to-peer. *Example: Git.* 3. **Hybrid Repository:** Combines centralized control with distributed workflow. *Example: Perforce, Gerrit.* **Based on Content (2 marks):** - Source Code Repository (Git, SVN) - Document Repository (Confluence, SharePoint) - Binary Artifact Repository (Artifactory, Nexus) - Model Repository (Enterprise Architect) - Test Repository (TestRail) **Comparison Matrix (2 marks):** | Parameter | Centralized | Distributed | |---|---|---| | Offline Access | No | Yes | | Fault Tolerance | Low | High | | Storage per User | Low | High | | Network Dependency | Continuous | Periodic | | Branching Cost | High | Low | --- **(b) Check-in / Check-out Lifecycle (7 marks)** **Lifecycle States (4 marks):** 1. **Available:** CI resides in the repository, accessible to authorized users. 2. **Checked-Out:** A user acquires the CI and an exclusive lock. Other users can only read (or are blocked, depending on lock mode). 3. **Modified:** The user edits the CI in their local working copy. 4. **Checked-In:** The user commits the modified CI; the lock is released; a new version is created in the version graph; the audit log is updated. 5. **Baselined:** A specific version is frozen as a baseline (e.g., release v2.0); no further modifications allowed without a formal change request. **Importance of Locking (3 marks):** - **Prevents Lost Update Problem:** Without locking, two users editing simultaneously would overwrite each other's changes silently. - **Ensures Data Integrity:** Only one writer at a time guarantees a consistent version is stored. - **Supports Auditability:** Lock acquire/release events are recorded, providing a precise timeline of who held which CI. - **Alternative — Optimistic Concurrency Control:** Some modern distributed repositories (e.g., Git) avoid hard locks and use **merge** to resolve conflicts. This trades stronger guarantees for higher parallelism. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse Repository with Version Control System (VCS).** A VCS is *one component* of a repository. The repository is the *entire backing store* that the VCS manages. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you use these terms interchangeably. > 2. **Always state the unit** (KB, MB, GB, seconds) in numerical answers. Marks are explicitly reserved for units in KTU paper valuation. > 3. **Do not skip the formula derivation steps.** KTU 2024 Scheme requires step-by-step evaluation. A single final answer without intermediate steps will be marked zero. > 4. **Misclassifying repositories:** Students often call SVN "distributed" — it is NOT. SVN is **centralized**. Git is the canonical example of distributed. > 5. **In state-machine / lifecycle questions**, always mention the **triggering event** for each state transition (e.g., "on commit", "on lock release"). Examiners award 0.5 marks for transitions alone. > 6. **Repository ≠ Database.** A repository includes versioning, locking, and audit; a plain database does not. This distinction is a favorite KTU trap question. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Repository** = centralized shared data store for all software CIs (single source of truth). - ✅ Holds **content + metadata + version history + audit logs**. - ✅ Four pillars of SCM (**Identification, Version Control, Change Control, Auditing**) all rely on the repository. - ✅ **Three-tier architecture:** Client Interface → Service/Transaction Layer → Persistent Storage. - ✅ **Types by topology:** Centralized (SVN), Distributed (Git), Hybrid (Perforce). - ✅ **Types by content:** Source, Document, Binary, Model, Test repositories. - ✅ **Core operations:** Check-in, Check-out, Branch, Merge, Tag, Baseline, Diff, Rollback, Lock, Audit Query. - ✅ **Delta encoding** formula: $S_{delta} = V_1 + \sum_{i=2}^{n} \Delta V_i$. - ✅ **Storage efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{delta}}{S_{full}}$. - ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$. - ✅ **Locking** prevents the Lost Update Problem; alternative is **optimistic concurrency via merge**. - ✅ **Repository ≠ Database ≠ VCS** — it is a *system* encompassing all three. - ✅ **ACID compliance** is mandatory for transactional integrity. - ✅ Repository is **foundational** in regulated industries (aerospace, medical, banking). - ✅ **Exam formula favorites:** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ and $\eta = 1 - \frac{S_{delta}}{S_{full}}$. - ✅ **Always include units** in numerical answers.

topics.title: Client-Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Client-Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Client-Server Architecture** is a distributed application structure that partitions tasks and workloads between **providers of a resource or service**, called **servers**, and **service requesters**, called **clients**. In this model, the client initiates a request, and the server responds by providing the requested resource, data, or service over a network using a well-defined protocol.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the Client-Server model is the foundational **distributed system pattern** where:

- **Client**: A process (program) that requests a service by sending a message to the server.
- **Server**: A process (program) that provides a service by waiting passively for incoming requests, then acting upon them and returning the result.
- **Network**: The communication medium (LAN, WAN, Internet) that carries the request/response messages.
- **Protocol**: A formal set of rules (e.g., **HTTP**, **TCP/IP**, **FTP**, **RPC**) governing the exchange.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OECST723 Module 2):** The Client-Server topic in Module 2 emphasizes *architectural styles*, *tiered structures*, *middleware roles*, and *distributed interaction patterns* — directly mapping to **CO2: Apply architectural patterns in software design**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Model 🍽️

To make the Client-Server model instantly intuitive, consider a **restaurant**:

| Real-World Element | Client-Server Counterpart | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Customer (diner) | Client (e.g., Web Browser) | Initiates a request ("I want food") |
| Waiter | Network + Protocol (HTTP) | Carries the request/response |
| Chef / Kitchen | Server (e.g., Web Server, DB) | Processes the request, prepares result |
| Hot Food on a tray | Response (HTML page, JSON data) | Delivered back to the requester |
| Restaurant Manager | Middleware | Manages auth, sessions, queues |

**The Flow:**
1. The customer (client) browses the menu (UI) and places an order (request).
2. The waiter (network) carries the order slip to the kitchen (server).
3. The chef (server logic) prepares the meal (executes business logic).
4. The waiter returns the dish (response) to the customer's table.

Just as a restaurant cannot serve a customer without a waiter and a kitchen, a **Client-Server system** cannot function without a **client, a server, and a communication protocol** linking them.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Building Blocks

> [!NOTE]
> Every Client-Server system, regardless of complexity, consists of exactly **three** logical components. This is a non-negotiable KTU 2024 fundamental.

1. **Presentation Layer (Client Side):** Handles the **User Interface (UI)** and input validation. Examples: Browser (Chrome, Firefox), Mobile app, Desktop GUI.
2. **Application / Processing Layer (Server Side):** Executes the **business logic**, enforces rules, and processes data.
3. **Data Management Layer (Database Server):** Stores, retrieves, and manages persistent data.

**Standard Metrics used in Client-Server evaluation (KTU 2024):**
- **Response Time (R):** Total time from request initiation to response receipt.
- **Throughput (X):** Number of requests served per unit time, typically measured in **requests/second**.
- **Latency (L):** Time taken for a single message to traverse the network one-way.
- **Bandwidth (B):** Maximum data transmission capacity, typically in **Mbps (Megabits per second)**.
- **Concurrency (C):** Maximum number of simultaneous clients a server can handle.

---

## 1.4 Why Client-Server? The Historical Motivation

Before client-server, we had **mainframe computing** (1960s-70s) — all processing happened on a single powerful machine accessed via "dumb terminals" (no local processing). This had two major problems:

- **Scalability Bottleneck:** Only one machine did everything; upgrading meant replacing the entire mainframe.
- **High Cost:** Mainframes were extremely expensive.

Client-Server emerged in the **1980s** with the rise of affordable personal computers (PCs) and **Local Area Networks (LANs)**. It distributed work: **fat clients** (powerful PCs) handled presentation, while servers centralized data and shared resources (e.g., file servers, print servers). The modern web (1990s onward) popularized the **thin-client** browser-based model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Model — How a Request Travels

Let us decompose a single client-server interaction into **6 explicit steps** (a common KTU long-answer requirement):

**Step 1 — Request Initiation**
The client application (e.g., a browser) constructs a request message. This involves **marshalling** the request parameters into a protocol-defined format (e.g., an HTTP GET request line: `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`).

**Step 2 — Protocol Encoding**
The request is serialized into a byte stream according to the transport protocol (typically **TCP** for reliability, **UDP** for speed).

**Step 3 — Network Transmission**
The byte stream is broken into **packets**, each carrying source IP, destination IP, source port, and destination port. Packets traverse routers and switches via **IP routing**.

**Step 4 — Server Demultiplexing**
The server's operating system receives the packets on a known port (e.g., **port 80** for HTTP, **port 443** for HTTPS). The **server application** (e.g., Apache, Nginx) is listening on this port and accepts the connection.

**Step 5 — Service Execution**
The server processes the request. This may involve querying a **database server** (a separate machine), executing business logic, or fetching a file. The result is prepared.

**Step 6 — Response Transmission**
The server **marshals** the response (e.g., HTML, JSON, XML), sends it back over the same connection (or a new one), and the client **unmarshals** and renders it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Architecture:** Centralizing data on servers ensures **data consistency**, **security**, and a **single source of truth**. Distributing processing to clients leverages their unused CPU cycles and provides **responsive UIs**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 numerical and derivations. Memorize the variable definitions and the units.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Response Time | $T_{R} = T_{N} + T_{S} + T_{C}$ | $T_{N}$ = Network time (s), $T_{S}$ = Server processing time (s), $T_{C}$ = Client rendering time (s) | Estimating end-to-end user-perceived latency |
| Network Transit Time (one-way) | $T_{N} = L + \frac{D}{B}$ | $L$ = Latency (s), $D$ = Data size (bits), $B$ = Bandwidth (bits/s) | Predicting WAN response times |
| Throughput (Pipeline) | $X = \frac{1}{\max_{i}(T_{i})}$ | $T_{i}$ = Stage time in pipeline (s) | Finding bottleneck in multi-stage processing |
| M/M/1 Mean Response Time | $T_{R} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\mu$ = Service rate (req/s), $\lambda$ = Arrival rate (req/s) | Queueing analysis for server load |
| M/M/1 Utilization | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq \rho < 1$ | Server capacity planning |
| Amdahl's Speedup Law | $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = Parallelizable fraction, $N$ = Number of servers | Predicting scalability of distributed servers |
| Connection Capacity | $C = \frac{B \times 10^{6}}{8 \times P_{avg}}$ | $B$ = Bandwidth (Mbps), $P_{avg}$ = Avg page size (bytes) | Estimating max concurrent web users |
| Round Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \times L + \frac{D_{req} + D_{res}}{B}$ | $L$ = One-way latency, $D$ = Data sizes | Network protocol design |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Client-Server Architectures (KTU 2024 Focus)

### A) By Tier Count (Most Frequently Examined)

**1-Tier Architecture:**
- Client, server, and database all reside in the **same process/machine**.
- Example: A standalone MS Access application.
- Pros: Simple, no network needed. Cons: No distribution, no scalability.

**2-Tier Architecture:**
- **Client** (presentation + some logic) ↔ **Server** (logic + data).
- The client directly talks to the database server using APIs like **ODBC** or **JDBC**.
- Example: Traditional desktop apps connecting to a centralized database.
- Pros: Fast for small user bases. Cons: "Fat client" deployment headaches, scalability limits.

**3-Tier Architecture:** ⭐ *Most important for KTU*
- **Tier 1: Presentation Layer** (Client / Browser) — UI only, no business logic.
- **Tier 2: Application Layer** (Application Server, e.g., Node.js, Spring Boot) — Business logic.
- **Tier 3: Data Layer** (Database Server, e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) — Data storage.
- Example: Any modern e-commerce website (Amazon, Flipkart).
- Pros: **Scalability** (each tier scales independently), **maintainability**, **security** (DB hidden behind app server).

**N-Tier / Multi-Tier Architecture:**
- Extends 3-tier by adding layers like **load balancers**, **caching servers** (Redis), **message queues** (RabbitMQ), and **CDNs**.
- Used in **enterprise and cloud-native** systems (microservices).
- Pros: Ultimate flexibility. Cons: Operational complexity (DevOps overhead).

### B) By Client Type

| Client Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin Client** | Minimal local processing; mainly displays server-rendered content. | Web browser, Chromebook |
| **Thick (Fat) Client** | Substantial local processing; runs heavy business logic locally. | Desktop IDE (Eclipse), MS Office |
| **Hybrid Client** | Mix of both; some logic local, some remote. | Mobile banking app |

### C) By Server Type

- **File Server:** Stores and shares files (e.g., NFS, SMB).
- **Database Server:** Hosts DBMS (e.g., Oracle, PostgreSQL).
- **Application Server:** Hosts business logic and middleware (e.g., JBoss, Tomcat).
- **Web Server:** Serves HTTP requests (e.g., Apache, Nginx).
- **Proxy Server:** Intermediary for client requests (e.g., Squid, HAProxy).
- **Mail Server:** Handles email (e.g., Sendmail, Exchange).

---

## 2.4 Middleware — The Hidden Hero

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Middleware** is software that sits between the client and the server (or between multiple servers) to **hide the complexity of distribution**. It handles concerns like **location transparency**, **heterogeneity**, **security**, and **failure handling**.

**Categories of Middleware (KTU expects knowledge of these):**

1. **RPC (Remote Procedure Call):** Calls a procedure on a remote machine as if it were local (e.g., gRPC, Java RMI).
2. **Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM):** Asynchronous communication via queues (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka, ActiveMQ).
3. **Object Request Broker (ORB):** Distributed object invocations (e.g., CORBA, .NET Remoting).
4. **Database Middleware:** ODBC, JDBC drivers that abstract database access.
5. **Transaction Processing Monitors (TP Monitors):** Coordinate distributed transactions (e.g., CICS, Tuxedo).

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **E-commerce:** Middleware handles payment processing, inventory checks, and order routing between microservices.
- **Banking:** TP monitors ensure **ACID** properties across distributed accounts.
- **IoT Systems:** MOM (Kafka) handles millions of sensor events per second asynchronously.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Response Time Decomposition

**Problem:** A client sends a 5 KB HTTP request to a server. The one-way network latency is **20 ms**, the available bandwidth is **100 Mbps**, the server processing time is **80 ms**, and the client takes **15 ms** to render the response. Compute the **Total Response Time (RTT-based)** assuming the response payload is 50 KB.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**
Request size: $D_{req} = 5 \text{ KB} = 5 \times 8 \times 1024 = 40{,}960 \text{ bits}$
Response size: $D_{res} = 50 \text{ KB} = 50 \times 8 \times 1024 = 409{,}600 \text{ bits}$
Latency: $L = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.020 \text{ s}$
Bandwidth: $B = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$
Server processing time: $T_{S} = 80 \text{ ms} = 0.080 \text{ s}$
Client render time: $T_{C} = 15 \text{ ms} = 0.015 \text{ s}$

**Step 2 — Compute Request Transmission Time**
$$T_{req} = \frac{D_{req}}{B} = \frac{40{,}960}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.4096 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Response Transmission Time**
$$T_{res} = \frac{D_{res}}{B} = \frac{409{,}600}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute One-Way Network Transit (one direction)**
$$T_{N,req} = L + T_{req} = 20 + 0.4096 = 20.4096 \text{ ms}$$
$$T_{N,res} = L + T_{res} = 20 + 4.096 = 24.096 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Two-Way Network Time**
$$T_{N,total} = T_{N,req} + T_{N,res} = 20.4096 + 24.096 = 44.5056 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Response Time**
The total response time is the sum of two-way network transit, server processing, and client render:
$$T_{R} = T_{N,total} + T_{S} + T_{C}$$
$$T_{R} = 44.5056 + 80 + 15 = 139.5056 \text{ ms}$$

**Interpretation:** Out of the total **139.5 ms**, **server processing (80 ms) is the dominant bottleneck (~57%)**, followed by network latency. To improve performance, the **server** should be optimized first (caching, faster DB queries), not the network.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law Applied to a Server Cluster

**Problem:** A web application spends **70%** of its request time in **parallelizable** operations (e.g., serving static assets from multiple nodes) and **30%** in **sequential** operations (e.g., writing to a single primary database). The system scales from 1 to 8 server nodes. Find the **speedup** at $N = 8$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters**
Parallelizable fraction: $P = 0.70$
Sequential fraction: $1 - P = 0.30$
Number of nodes: $N = 8$

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law**
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$$
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \frac{0.70}{8}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominator**
$$\frac{0.70}{8} = 0.0875$$
$$0.30 + 0.0875 = 0.3875$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Speedup**
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.3875} \approx 2.58$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Maximum Theoretical Speedup (as $N \to \infty$)**
$$S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}} = \frac{1}{1-P} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33$$

**Interpretation:** Even with **8 servers**, the speedup is only **2.58×** (not 8×), because the **30% sequential bottleneck** (the database write) caps scalability. This is the **fundamental lesson of Amdahl's Law** in client-server design: **identify and eliminate sequential bottlenecks** before adding more servers. The **maximum possible speedup is 3.33×**, no matter how many servers you add.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — M/M/1 Queueing Model for Server Capacity Planning

**Problem:** A web server's request service rate is $\mu = 500$ requests/second, and the average arrival rate is $\lambda = 400$ requests/second. Compute the **server utilization** $\rho$, the **mean response time** $T_{R}$, and the **mean number of requests in the system** $N$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Utilization**
$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{400}{500} = 0.80$$
Utilization is **80%**, which is near the danger zone (most engineers warn above 80%).

**Step 2 — Compute Mean Response Time using M/M/1 formula**
$$T_{R} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{500 - 400} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Mean Number of Requests in System using Little's Law**
$$N = \lambda \times T_{R} = 400 \times 0.01 = 4 \text{ requests}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Mean Number in Queue (separately)**
$$N_{Q} = \rho \times N = 0.80 \times 4 = 3.2 \text{ requests}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, **4 requests** are either being processed or waiting, and **3.2 of them are in the queue**. As $\lambda$ approaches $\mu$, both $T_{R}$ and $N$ **diverge to infinity** — the system becomes unstable. This is why capacity planners keep $\rho < 0.7$ for safety.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — 1-Tier vs 2-Tier vs 3-Tier vs N-Tier

| Criterion | 1-Tier | 2-Tier | 3-Tier | N-Tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Processes** | 1 | 2 | 3 | $\geq 4$ |
| **Network Hops** | 0 | 1 | 2 | $\geq 2$ |
| **Scalability** | None | Limited | High | Very High |
| **Security** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Deployment Complexity** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Fault Tolerance** | None | Low | Medium | High |
| **Development Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Example** | Standalone desktop app | Client-Server DB app | Web application | Microservices cloud app |
| **KTU Frequency** | Rarely asked | Often asked | **Most asked** | Increasingly asked |

## 4.5 Failure and Recovery Flow in Client-Server

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CONNECTING
    CONNECTING --> CONNECTED : TCP Handshake OK
    CONNECTING --> FAILED : Timeout
    CONNECTED --> SENDING : Client Sends Request
    SENDING --> WAITING : Await Server Response
    WAITING --> RECEIVED : Response Arrives
    WAITING --> TIMEOUT : No Response
    TIMEOUT --> RETRY : Reconnect
    RETRY --> CONNECTING
    RECEIVED --> PROCESSING
    PROCESSING --> [*]
    FAILED --> RETRY
```

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Thin Client and a Thick Client. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Thin Client | Thick Client |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A client that performs minimal local processing; relies heavily on the server for business logic. | A client that performs substantial local processing and runs business logic locally. |
| **Resource Usage** | Low CPU, low memory, low disk requirements. | High CPU, high memory, high disk requirements. |
| **Network Dependency** | Always requires network connectivity to function. | Can function partially offline. |
| **Deployment** | Centralized; no per-machine installation. | Distributed; per-machine installation and updates. |
| **Example** | A web browser accessing Gmail. | Microsoft Word with installed desktop spell-check. |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definitions + 1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for examples)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**What is Middleware? List any two types of middleware with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Middleware** is a software layer that sits between the client and server (or between distributed components) to abstract the complexity of heterogeneous networks, protocols, and operating systems, providing services like communication, security, and transaction management.

**Two Types:**

1. **Remote Procedure Call (RPC) Middleware:** Allows a program to invoke a procedure on a remote machine as if it were a local call. *Example: Java RMI, gRPC.*

2. **Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM):** Enables asynchronous communication between distributed systems via message queues. *Example: RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka.*
