The value overflowed because the Ariane 5 trajectory produced a horizontal velocity greater than **$32\,767$** in the SRI's internal scaled units (where the scale factor is $100$ per m/s, i.e., units of $0.01$ m/s).

**Step 2 — Ada language features that failed (2 marks):**
- **Strong typing failed** because Ada's `INTEGER_16` accepts the assignment and performs an implicit narrowing conversion with a runtime range check. When the check fails, the language **does raise an exception** — but the SRI code did not include a corresponding `exception` block to handle `CONSTRAINT_ERROR` (or `OPERAND_ERROR` for the 1750A target).
- **The `pragma SUPPRESS (RANGE_CHECK)`** was *not* used; the check was active. However, suppression would have been the *wrong* mitigation — it would have caused silent wrap-around. The correct mitigation was an explicit handler.
- **Default runtime behavior on unhandled exception** was to terminate the protected partition and emit diagnostics — which is what propagated the failure.

**Step 3 — Threshold calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Overflow Threshold} = \frac{\text{INT16}_{\max}}{k_{\text{scale}}} = \frac{32\,767}{100} = 327.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

Therefore, any horizontal velocity above **327.67 m/s** would cause the conversion to fail. The Ariane 5 trajectory reached this value at approximately **T+27.3 seconds** (using the empirical lateral acceleration $\alpha \approx 12$ m/s², computed as $t = 327.67/12 \approx 27.3$ s). The actual recorded exception occurred at **T+36.7 seconds**, slightly later due to filter dynamics and pre-launch calibration offsets.

**Part (b) — Cascade Analysis & Preventive Architecture \[7 Marks\]**

**Cascade description (5 marks):**
1. The SRI processor raised the exception when `BH` exceeded the INT16 limit.
2. The default Ada exception handler (no custom one was linked) caused the SRI processor to enter **safe-mode shutdown**.
3. As a final act before shutdown, the SRI **dumped its working memory and diagnostic data** onto the shared **MIL-STD-1553B bus** for post-flight analysis.
4. The On-Board Computer (OBC), which subscribes to the same bus channel for **flight attitude data**, interpreted the diagnostic dump as a legitimate quaternion and Euler-angle packet.
5. The OBC's nozzle command generator, receiving a phantom attitude with implied $\alpha > 20^\circ$, commanded the thrust-vector actuators to the **mechanical hard-stop at $\pm 16^\circ$**.
6. The aerodynamic side-load induced by this nozzle deflection exceeded structural limits, and the Flight Termination System (FTS) issued the **self-destruct command at T+39 seconds**.

**Single architectural change to prevent the failure (2 marks):**
**Decouple the diagnostic data bus from the flight-control data bus.** The 1553B bus should have been logically partitioned so that **diagnostic dumps could never be addressed to the OBC's flight-attitude channel**. Alternatively, the OBC should have implemented a **plausibility / range check** on incoming attitude data — any value exceeding the dynamic envelope of the vehicle (e.g., angular rates above $50^\circ$/s) should have been rejected as a sensor fault, and the OBC should have reverted to a **redundant second SRI** (which existed but was not consulted due to the bus-routing failure).

---

### 🅱️ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Requirements Engineering** and **Software Reuse** in the Ariane 5 failure. Why was the alignment function running during flight when it was originally designed for pre-launch use only? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compare the V-Model verification activities that were *performed* versus those that *should have been performed* to catch the Ariane 5 defect. Which V&V activity, if added, would have detected the overflow before launch? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Requirements & Reuse \[7 Marks\]**

**Requirements Engineering failure (3 marks):**
The SRI's alignment function was originally specified for **pre-launch initialization only** — to compute the inertial reference frame while the rocket was stationary on the launch pad. The Ariane 4 requirements explicitly stated that the function would be **disabled before engine ignition**. However, the requirements specification for Ariane 5 was derived by **inheriting the Ariane 4 spec verbatim**, and no one formally re-specified what should happen to the alignment function **after the launcher-stage transition**. The implicit assumption was that "the SRI will continue running the function because it has always run the function," which is a classic **requirements assumption defect**.

**Software Reuse failure (2 marks):**
The Ariane 4 SRI was a **heritage system** — ESA had invested 10+ years in its development. Reusing it on Ariane 5 was justified on grounds of cost, schedule, and risk reduction (paradoxically, the reuse *introduced* the risk). The reuse was **not preceded by a formal delta-analysis** comparing Ariane 4 and Ariane 5 operational envelopes. A proper Software Reuse Procedure (per IEEE 1517 or ESA PSS-05) would have mandated:
- Identification of **changed operational parameters** between the two missions
- Re-validation of all safety-critical computations against the new envelope
- Independent re-integration testing

**Why the function was running in flight (2 marks):**
The SRI computer was designed to be **single-mode** during the first ~50 seconds of flight. The alignment function continued to execute (every 50 ms) because:
- The SRI's startup code was identical between ground and flight modes.
- The "disable alignment post-launch" command was scheduled to be sent by the OBC at a specific mission time, but the OBC did not issue this command because the SRI was **already in shutdown by the time** the OBC attempted to do so.
- The SRI's task scheduler had no **phase-awareness** (no mode parameter distinguishing "ground" from "flight").

**Part (b) — V-Model V&V Comparison \[7 Marks\]**

**V-Model activities performed (3 marks):**
| V-Model Element | Performed? | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Testing | Yes | Passed (zero velocity case only) |
| Software Integration | Yes | Passed (SRI standalone) |
| Hardware/Software Integration | Partial | SRI tested with OBC software simulator, not actual OBC bus semantics |
| System Validation | No | Deferred — full trajectory simulation not yet ready |
| Acceptance / Qualification | No | Incomplete at time of launch |

**V-Model activities that should have been performed (3 marks):**
1. **Range / Boundary-Value Testing** of the alignment function with realistic Ariane 5 trajectory velocity profiles.
2. **Exception Handler Coverage Analysis** — formal proof that every Ada `exception` raised in safety-critical code paths has a corresponding `exception` clause.
3. **Bus-Level Integration Testing** — verification that diagnostic data emitted on shutdown cannot be misinterpreted as flight-control data by the OBC.
4. **Independent Safety Re-Assessment** (e.g., per ECSS-Q-ST-80) of the heritage SRI for the new operational envelope.
5. **Software FMEA** identifying "operand overflow" as a single-point failure mode.

**The single V&V activity that would have detected the bug (1 mark):**
**Range / Boundary-Value Testing** of the alignment function with input data from the **predicted Ariane 5 trajectory** (not Ariane 4's). Had this been performed during the SRI Software Integration Test, the test would have failed at approximately $v_{\text{horizontal}} = 327.67$ m/s, and the bug would have been caught pre-flight.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Naming the wrong language or processor.** The SRI used **Ada 83** on a **MIL-STD-1750A** processor. Writing "C++" or "Java" loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Confusing the BH variable with roll/pitch/yaw.** `BH` is the **horizontal bias velocity** in SRI internal units, not a raw velocity or an attitude angle.
> 3. **Stating that "the bug was a single line of code."** The Inquiry Board explicitly rejected this view. The defect was a **process failure spanning requirements, design, coding, and testing**. Marks are awarded for a systemic analysis.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the 1553B bus coupling.** The OBC's misinterpretation of SRI diagnostics is the **bridge** between the SRI failure and the vehicle destruction. Omitting the bus means you only get ~50% of the cascade marks.
> 5. **Writing "integer overflow" without specifying 16-bit signed.** Always qualify: *16-bit signed integer (`INTEGER_16`), max value **32 767**.*
> 6. **Skipping the calculation.** KTU 14-mark questions on case studies expect a **quantitative demonstration** (e.g., the $327.67$ m/s threshold). Pure qualitative answers cap at 8–9 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Things You MUST Remember for Ariane 5 (Flash-Recall Format)**

1. **Date:** 4 June 1996, Kourou, **Flight 501** (V-501). Loss = **~370 M USD**.
2. **Time-to-failure:** **T+39 seconds** at **~3700 m** altitude.
3. **Root cause:** Ada `OPERAND_ERROR` (overflow) during `INTEGER_16` conversion of 64-bit FLOAT `BH` (horizontal velocity) in the SRI alignment function.
4. **The threshold:** $\text{INT16}_{\max} = 2^{15} - 1 = 32\,767$ (in SRI units of $0.01$ m/s, equivalent to **327.67 m/s**).
5. **The bug location:** The SRI software, specifically the alignment function (originally Ariane 4 heritage code).
6. **The SRI processor:** **MA31750** (MIL-STD-1750A, 16-bit architecture).
7. **The bus:** **MIL-STD-1553B**, shared between SRI and OBC.
8. **The cascade:** Exception → CPU shutdown → Diagnostic dump to bus → OBC misinterprets as attitude → Nozzle hard-stop (+/- 16°) → FTS self-destruct.
9. **The missing exception handler:** An Ada `exception` block for `OPERAND_ERROR` was written but **not linked** in the protected software build.
10. **The redundant SRI:** A second SRI existed but was never consulted because the bus-routing fault caused OBC to use the primary's (corrupted) output.
11. **The investigation:** **Lions Inquiry Board Report (1996)** — publicly available ESA document.
12. **The V-Model lesson:** Each software requirement must have a **traceable, executable test** — and heritage requirements must be **re-justified** for the new envelope.
13. **The reuse lesson:** **Software Reuse Risk = f(Envelope Change, Code Path Coverage, Test Coverage on New Trajectory)**. All three were negative for Ariane 5.
14. **The SE phases violated:** Requirements, Architecture, Detailed Design, Coding, Unit Test, Integration Test, System Test, FMEA, Risk Management — essentially **the full lifecycle**.
15. **The takeaway phrase for exams:** *"Ariane 5 was not lost to a software bug — it was lost to a process failure that a single missing exception handler could not have caused alone."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Oriented Software Design - UML Diagrams and Relationships - Static and Dynamic Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Software Design - UML Diagrams and Relationships - Static and Dynamic Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Object Oriented Software Design (OOSD)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
Object Oriented Software Design (OOSD) is a software design methodology that revolves around identifying and organizing a system around **data objects** rather than functions or procedures, where each object is an instance of a class that encapsulates both state (attributes) and behavior (operations), and these objects interact through well-defined interfaces to fulfill system requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** OOSD bridges the gap between analysis and implementation. It transforms the *what* (analysis model) into the *how* (design model) using object-oriented principles — **Abstraction, Encapsulation, Modularity, Hierarchy**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Think of a software system as a **professional restaurant kitchen**:
- The **Menu** = the system requirements (what the customer wants).
- The **Chefs** = objects. Each chef (Grill Chef, Pastry Chef, Sous Chef) is an *object* of the *class* `Chef`. They know their own recipes (methods) and carry their own tools (private attributes).
- The **Kitchen Pass** = the interface. The Head Chef calls out orders, and the relevant chef responds — they don't need to know *how* the other chef cooks.
- The **Kitchen Layout** = the static design (class diagram). The **flow of orders during service** = the dynamic design (sequence, activity diagrams).

This separation — *structure (static) vs. behavior (dynamic)* — is the heart of UML.

---

## 1.2 Unified Modeling Language (UML) — The Universal Blueprint

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** UML (Unified Modeling Language) is a **standardized, general-purpose visual modeling language** standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It is not a programming language, but a **visual specification language** used to **visualize, specify, construct, and document** the artifacts of a software-intensive system.

### The 14 Official UML 2.5 Diagrams — Master Overview Table

UML diagrams are broadly classified into **two categories**: **Static (Structural)** and **Dynamic (Behavioral)**.

| Category | Diagram | Purpose (One-liner) |
|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Class Diagram | Shows classes, attributes, operations, and relationships |
| **Static** | Object Diagram | Snapshot of objects and their links at a point in time |
| **Static** | Component Diagram | Shows organization of physical software components |
| **Static** | Deployment Diagram | Shows physical hardware nodes and software artifacts |
| **Static** | Package Diagram | Groups related UML elements into logical packages |
| **Static** | Composite Structure Diagram | Internal structure of a class (parts, ports) |
| **Dynamic** | Use Case Diagram | Captures functional requirements and actors |
| **Dynamic** | Activity Diagram | Workflow / procedural flow of activities |
| **Dynamic** | State Machine Diagram | Lifecycle states of an object and transitions |
| **Dynamic** | Sequence Diagram | Time-ordered message exchange between objects |
| **Dynamic** | Communication Diagram | Object interactions emphasizing links (was Collaboration) |
| **Dynamic** | Interaction Overview | Mixes activity and sequence diagrams |
| **Dynamic** | Timing Diagram | State changes vs. time along a linear axis |
| **Dynamic** | Profile Diagram | Extends UML for domain-specific customizations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** Module 2 demands a deep focus on **Class, Object, Sequence, Use Case, Activity, and State Machine** diagrams. These six appear in 80%+ of past KTU question papers.

---

## 1.3 The "Static vs. Dynamic" Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of OOSD:**
> - **Static Models** answer: *"What is the structure of the system?"* — They are **frozen in time**, showing classes, relationships, and components. They do not change as the system runs.
> - **Dynamic Models** answer: *"How does the system behave over time?"* — They capture **temporal evolution**, message passing, state changes, and workflows.

### Real-World Analogy
- **Static (Class Diagram)** = The **architectural blueprint** of a building. Shows rooms (classes), walls (boundaries), and doors (associations). Frozen.
- **Dynamic (Sequence Diagram)** = The **CCTV footage** of people moving through the building, opening doors, and using the elevator. Time-based.

| Aspect | Static Model | Dynamic Model |
|---|---|---|
| Time Dimension | **No time** involved | **Time-ordered** |
| Captures | Structure, relationships | Behavior, control flow |
| Primary Diagrams | Class, Object, Component | Sequence, State, Activity |
| Exam Question Style | "Draw class diagram for ATM" | "Draw sequence diagram for login" |
| KTU Weightage | **High (≈60%)** | **High (≈40%)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 UML Relationships — The Six Cardinal Connectors

Every element in a UML diagram is connected via **six fundamental relationship types**. Mastering these is non-negotiable for KTU.

### Relationship Cheat Sheet

| # | Relationship | Symbol | Strength | Description | Lifetime Coupling |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Association** | Solid line | Weak structural | "Uses-a" / "Knows-a" general link | Independent |
| 2 | **Directed Association** | Solid line + arrow | Weak | Navigation from A to B | Independent |
| 3 | **Aggregation** | Hollow diamond (◇) | Medium | "Has-a" whole-part, **parts can survive whole** | Independent |
| 4 | **Composition** | Filled diamond (◆) | Strong | "Contains-a" whole-part, **parts die with whole** | Dependent (co-existent) |
| 5 | **Dependency** | Dashed arrow (⇢) | Weakest | "Depends-on" — temporary usage | Transient |
| 6 | **Generalization** | Solid line + hollow triangle (△) | Strong | "Is-a" — inheritance | Strongly coupled |
| 7 | **Realization** | Dashed line + hollow triangle (△) | Contractual | "Implements" — class implements interface | Strongly coupled |

### Why These Distinctions Matter — The "Why" and "How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Aggregation vs. Composition — The Memory Trick:**
> - **Aggregation** = **Library and Books.** A library ceases to exist, but the books still survive (they move to another library). → **Hollow diamond**.
> - **Composition** = **Human and Heart.** A human ceases to exist, the heart cannot exist independently. → **Filled diamond**.

### Multiplicity Notation (Universities Love This!)

| Notation | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `1` | Exactly one | A `Person` has `1` `Heart` |
| `0..1` | Zero or one | A `Customer` has `0..1` `LoyaltyCard` |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many | A `Library` contains `*` `Books` |
| `1..*` | One or many | A `Team` has `1..*` `Players` |
| `n..m` | Specific range | An exam paper is answered by `30..60` `Students` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Examiners check if you've placed multiplicity on **both ends** of the association. Omitting one end = **−2 marks**.

### Visibility Modifiers (Inside Class Boxes)

| Symbol | Visibility | Java/C++ Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | Public | `public` |
| `-` | Private | `private` |
| `#` | Protected | `protected` |
| `~` | Package | default (package-private) |

---

## 2.2 Static Models (Structural) — The "What Exists"

### A. Class Diagram — The King of UML

A **class diagram** is the backbone of OOSD. It represents:
- **Classes** (rectangles with 3 compartments: name, attributes, operations)
- **Relationships** (the six types above)
- **Constraints, notes, stereotypes**

#### Class Notation Anatomy
```
┌──────────────────────────────┐
│       ClassName              │  ← Stereotype in « »
├──────────────────────────────┤
│ - attributeName : Type = init│  ← Attributes
│ + const : Type {readOnly}    │
├──────────────────────────────┤
│ + methodName() : ReturnType  │  ← Operations
│ - privateHelper() : void     │
└──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Stereotypes (UML 2.5 standard):**
- `«interface»` — purely abstract contract
- `«abstract»` — cannot be instantiated
- `«utility»` — static methods only
- `«enumeration»` — fixed set of literals
- `«datatype»` — pure value type
- `«actor»`, `«exception»`, `«metaclass»`

### B. Object Diagram — "A Snapshot in Time"

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An object diagram is an **instance of a class diagram**. Where classes are blueprints, object diagrams show the **concrete objects** (with actual values) that exist at a particular moment.

**Convention:** Object names are **underlined**, formatted as `objectName : ClassName`.

```
┌────────────────────────┐
│   rahul : Student      │
├────────────────────────┤
│ studentId = "S101"     │
│ gpa      = 8.7         │
│ year     = 3           │
├────────────────────────┤
│ calculateFee()         │
└────────────────────────┘
```

### C. Component & Deployment Diagrams
- **Component Diagram:** Shows software modules (`.jar`, `.dll`, `.exe`) and their **provided/required interfaces** (ball-and-socket notation: ◯ provides, ◁ requires).
- **Deployment Diagram:** Shows physical nodes (servers, devices) and the **artifacts** deployed on them. Includes communication paths.

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Models (Behavioral) — The "What Happens"

### A. Use Case Diagram — "Who Does What"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Components:**
> - **Actor** (stick figure or `«actor»` for system actors) — external entity.
> - **Use Case** (ellipse) — a unit of functionality providing value.
> - **System Boundary** (rectangle) — separates inside from outside.
> - **Relationships:** `<<include>>` (mandatory reuse), `<<extend>>` (optional extension with extension points), **Generalization** (specific use case inherits base use case).

### B. Sequence Diagram — "The Time Strip"

The **most asked KTU dynamic diagram**. Key elements:

| Element | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifeline** | Dashed vertical line | Object's existence over time |
| **Activation Bar** | Thin rectangle on lifeline | Object is executing |
| **Message** | Solid arrow `→` | Synchronous call |
| **Reply (return)** | Dashed arrow `⇢` | Return value |
| **Self-call** | Loop back to same lifeline | Internal recursion |
| **alt / opt / loop / par** | Combined fragments | Control logic |
| **Creation message** | Arrow pointing to new lifeline | Object creation |
| **Destruction message** | `X` at lifeline end | Object deletion |

### C. State Machine Diagram — "The Lifecycle"

Models **states of a single object** and the **events** that trigger transitions.

**Core elements:**
- **State:** Rounded rectangle (initial state = filled black circle, final state = bullseye ◎).
- **Transition:** Arrow with `event[guard]/action` syntax.
- **Guard:** Boolean condition in `[ ]`.
- **Action:** Activity executed on transition in `/ action`.

### D. Activity Diagram — "The Workflow"

Essentially a **flowchart** with enhanced features:
- **Initial node** ●, **Final node** ◎, **Decision** ◇, **Merge** ◇, **Fork** ┳ (parallel split), **Join** ┻ (synchronization bar).
- **Swimlanes:** Partition responsibility across actors/classes (BPMN-inspired).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Static vs. Dynamic Comparison

| Static Model | Dynamic Model |
|---|---|
| Class, Object, Component, Deployment, Package, Composite Structure | Use Case, Activity, State Machine, Sequence, Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview |
| Captures **structure** | Captures **behavior** |
| Time-independent | Time-dependent |
| Snapshot view | Evolutionary view |
| Question stem: "Design the class structure for…" | Question stem: "Show the interaction/order of events when…" |
| Mark allocation in KTU: **7–10 marks** typical | Mark allocation in KTU: **7–14 marks** typical |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common trap is asking students to "draw a class diagram AND a sequence diagram for the same scenario." You must draw **both** correctly. Static alone or dynamic alone = partial marks only.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Method to Draw a Class Diagram (KTU's Favorite 7-Marker)

When a KTU question says: *"Draw the class diagram for an Online Shopping System,"* follow this **6-step ritual**:

### Step 1 — Identify Nouns (Classes)
Read the problem text. Underline all **nouns** that represent business entities.
> "An **Online Shopping System** has **Customers** who place **Orders** containing **Products**. **Payments** are processed through **PaymentGateway**. The **Admin** manages **Inventory**."
> Classes: `Customer`, `Order`, `Product`, `Payment`, `PaymentGateway`, `Admin`, `Inventory`, `OnlineShoppingSystem`.

### Step 2 — Identify Attributes (Member Variables)
For each class, ask: *"What data does this entity store?"*

$$
\text{Attributes}(Customer) = \{\text{customerId}, \text{name}, \text{email}, \text{phone}, \text{address}\}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify Operations (Methods)
For each class, ask: *"What actions can this entity perform?"*

$$
\text{Operations}(Customer) = \{\text{register()}, \text{login()}, \text{placeOrder()}, \text{viewOrderHistory()}\}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Relationships
- `Customer` **places** `Order` → one-to-many association.
- `Order` **contains** `Product` → **composition** (products exist in order context).
- `Payment` **uses** `PaymentGateway` → dependency.
- `Admin` **is a** `User` → generalization (if `User` is a base class).

### Step 5 — Apply Multiplicity
- A `Customer` places **0..\*** `Orders`.
- An `Order` belongs to **1** `Customer`.
- An `Order` contains **1..\*** `Products`.
- A `Product` appears in **0..\*** `Orders`.

### Step 6 — Apply Visibility & Stereotypes
- Mark attributes as `+` (public getters) or `-` (private fields).
- Mark `PaymentGateway` as `«interface»` if it's an abstract contract.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Class Diagram for ATM System

**Problem (KTU Typical):** *"Design the class diagram for an ATM system that allows customers to withdraw, deposit, check balance, and transfer money. The bank has multiple ATMs and accounts."*

### Extracted Classes

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| `ATM` | `atmId`, `location`, `cashAvailable` | `authenticateCard()`, `dispenseCash()`, `printReceipt()` |
| `Bank` | `bankName`, `branchCode` | `addATM()`, `addCustomer()` |
| `Customer` | `customerId`, `name`, `pin` | `withdraw()`, `deposit()`, `checkBalance()`, `transfer()` |
| `Account` (abstract) | `accountNumber`, `balance` | `debit(amount)`, `credit(amount)` |
| `SavingsAccount` | `interestRate` | `calculateInterest()` |
| `CurrentAccount` | `overdraftLimit` | `checkOverdraft()` |
| `Transaction` | `txnId`, `timestamp`, `amount` | `execute()`, `rollback()` |

### Derived Relationships

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATM} &\xrightarrow{\text{serves}} \text{Customer} \quad (\text{Association, } 1..*) \\
\text{Customer} &\xrightarrow{\text{owns}} \text{Account} \quad (\text{Composition, } 1..*) \\
\text{SavingsAccount} &\xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{Account} \quad (\text{Generalization}) \\
\text{CurrentAccount} &\xrightarrow{\text{extends}} \text{Account} \quad (\text{Generalization}) \\
\text{ATM} &\xrightarrow{\text{creates}} \text{Transaction} \quad (\text{Composition, } 1..*) \\
\text{Transaction} &\dashrightarrow \text{Account} \quad (\text{Dependency})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Justification of Relationship Choices
- **ATM–Customer: Association**, not composition, because a customer exists independent of the ATM.
- **Customer–Account: Composition**, because closing the customer should close accounts (in this simplified model).
- **Account (abstract) – SavingsAccount/CurrentAccount: Generalization** (Is-A).
- **Transaction – Account: Dependency**, because transaction merely *uses* account temporarily.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Sequence Diagram for ATM Withdrawal

**Scenario:** Customer inserts card, enters PIN, requests ₹5000 withdrawal.

### Step-by-Step Time Sequence

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &: \text{Customer} \xrightarrow{\text{insertCard()}} \text{ATM} \\
t_2 &: \text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\text{readCard()}} \text{CardReader} \\
t_3 &: \text{ATM} \rightarrow \text{Customer} \text{ [prompt: Enter PIN]} \\
t_4 &: \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{ATM} \text{ [PIN: ****]} \\
t_5 &: \text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\text{validatePIN()}} \text{BankServer} \\
t_6 &: \text{BankServer} \rightarrow \text{ATM} \text{ [valid]} \\
t_7 &: \text{ATM} \rightarrow \text{Customer} \text{ [select transaction]} \\
t_8 &: \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{ATM} \text{ [Withdrawal ₹5000]} \\
t_9 &: \text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\text{checkBalance()}} \text{Account} \\
t_{10} &: \text{Account} \rightarrow \text{ATM} \text{ [balance: ₹12000]} \\
t_{11} &: \text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\text{dispenseCash()}} \text{CashDispenser} \\
t_{12} &: \text{ATM} \rightarrow \text{Customer} \text{ [Take cash]} \\
t_{13} &: \text{ATM} \xrightarrow{\text{printReceipt()}} \text{ReceiptPrinter}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Combined Fragments Used in KTU Answers
- **`opt` (Optional):** "if PIN invalid, eject card" — wraps conditional steps.
- **`alt` (Alternative):** "if balance ≥ amount, dispense; else, show insufficient funds" — mutually exclusive branches.
- **`loop`:** "while cash not dispensed, retry" — bounded iteration.
- **`par` (Parallel):** "dispense cash AND print receipt simultaneously" — concurrency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Marking Insight:** Always include the **return arrows (dashed)** for synchronous messages. Skipping them = **−1 to −2 marks** depending on examiner strictness.

---

## 3.4 Decision Matrix — Which Diagram Should I Draw?

| Question Stem Contains… | Draw… |
|---|---|
| "Draw the static structure / design the classes" | **Class Diagram** |
| "Show the static view / current state of objects" | **Object Diagram** |
| "Show the interaction in time order / messages exchanged" | **Sequence Diagram** |
| "Show all possible states of the object" | **State Machine Diagram** |
| "Show the workflow / business process" | **Activity Diagram** |
| "Identify the actors and functionality" | **Use Case Diagram** |
| "Show the deployment on hardware" | **Deployment Diagram** |
| "Show the software modules and their interfaces" | **Component Diagram** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** **"CLASS is STATIC, SEQUENCE is TIME, STATE is LIFECYCLE, ACTIVITY is WORKFLOW, USE CASE is REQUIREMENT."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram — Bank Account Hierarchy

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Account {
        <<abstract>>
        -accountNumber : String
        -balance : double
        +debit(amount : double) boolean
        +credit(amount : double) void
    }
    class SavingsAccount {
        -interestRate : double
        +calculateInterest() double
    }
    class CurrentAccount {
        -overdraftLimit : double
        +checkOverdraft() boolean
    }
    class Customer {
        -customerId : String
        -name : String
        -pin : int
        +withdraw(amount : double) void
        +deposit(amount : double) void
    }
    class Bank {
        -bankName : String
        -branchCode : String
        +addCustomer(c : Customer) void
    }
    class Transaction {
        -txnId : String
        -timestamp : Date
        -amount : double
        +execute() void
    }
    Account <|-- SavingsAccount : extends
    Account <|-- CurrentAccount : extends
    Customer "1" *-- "1..*" Account : owns
    Customer "1" --> "0..*" Transaction : initiates
    Bank "1" o-- "0..*" Customer : serves
    Transaction ..> Account : updates
```

## 4.2 Use Case Diagram — Online Shopping System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Customer((Customer))
    Admin((Admin))
    PaymentGateway((Payment Gateway))

    subgraph System [Online Shopping System]
        UC1([Browse Products])
        UC2([Add to Cart])
        UC3([Checkout])
        UC4([Make Payment])
        UC5([View Order History])
        UC6([Manage Inventory])
        UC7([Generate Reports])
    end

    Customer --> UC1
    Customer --> UC2
    Customer --> UC3
    UC3 -.includes.-> UC4
    UC4 -.extends.-> PaymentGateway
    Customer --> UC5
    Admin --> UC6
    Admin --> UC7

    style System fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style UC3 fill:#fffacd
    style UC4 fill:#fffacd
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — ATM Withdrawal Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor C as Customer
    participant A as ATM
    participant R as CardReader
    participant B as BankServer
    participant Acc as Account
    participant D as CashDispenser
    participant P as ReceiptPrinter

    C->>A: insertCard()
    A->>R: readCard()
    R-->>A: cardData
    A-->>C: promptEnterPIN()
    C->>A: enterPIN(1234)
    A->>B: validatePIN(card, pin)
    alt PIN Valid
        B-->>A: authenticated
        A-->>C: selectTransaction()
        C->>A: withdraw(5000)
        A->>Acc: checkBalance()
        Acc-->>A: balance=12000
        alt Balance Sufficient
            A->>Acc: debit(5000)
            A->>D: dispenseCash(5000)
            D-->>A: cashDispensed
            A->>P: printReceipt()
            P-->>A: receiptPrinted
            A-->>C: takeCashAndReceipt()
        else Insufficient Funds
            A-->>C: displayInsufficientBalance()
        end
    else PIN Invalid
        A-->>C: ejectCard()
    end
```

## 4.4 State Machine Diagram — Order Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending : orderPlaced
    Pending --> Confirmed : paymentReceived
    Pending --> Cancelled : userCancels
    Confirmed --> Packed : warehousePacks
    Packed --> Shipped : courierPicksUp
    Shipped --> Delivered : customerSigns
    Delivered --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]

    state Confirmed {
        [*] --> AwaitingStock
        AwaitingStock --> ReadyToPack : stockAvailable
    }
```

## 4.5 Activity Diagram — Login Workflow with Swimlanes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> A1[/Enter Username/]
    A1 --> A2[/Enter Password/]
    A2 --> D1{Valid?}
    D1 -- No --> A3[Show Error]
    A3 --> A1
    D1 -- Yes --> A4[Create Session]
    A4 --> A5[Redirect to Dashboard]
    A5 --> End([End])
```

## 4.6 Component Diagram — Three-Tier Web Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ClientTier [Client Tier]
        Browser[Web Browser]
    end
    subgraph AppTier [Application Tier]
        AuthComp[<<component>>\nAuth Service]
        OrderComp[<<component>>\nOrder Service]
        PayComp[<<component>>\nPayment Service]
    end
    subgraph DataTier [Data Tier]
        UserDB[(User DB)]
        OrderDB[(Order DB)]
    end

    Browser -- HTTP --> AuthComp
    AuthComp --> OrderComp
    OrderComp --> PayComp
    AuthComp --> UserDB
    OrderComp --> OrderDB
    PayComp --> OrderDB

    style AuthComp fill:#ffe4b5
    style OrderComp fill:#ffe4b5
    style PayComp fill:#ffe4b5
    style UserDB fill:#e6e6fa
    style OrderDB fill:#e6e6fa
```

## 4.7 Deployment Diagram — E-Commerce System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientNode [Client Devices]
        Laptop[Laptop]
        Mobile[Mobile Phone]
    end
    subgraph WebServer [Web Server Node]
        WebApp[<<artifact>>\nwebapp.war]
    end
    subgraph AppServer [Application Server Node]
        AppLogic[<<artifact>>\norderlogic.jar]
        AuthLogic[<<artifact>>\nauthservice.jar]
    end
    subgraph DBServer [Database Server Node]
        OracleDB[(Oracle DB)]
    end

    Laptop -- HTTPS --> WebApp
    Mobile -- HTTPS --> WebApp
    WebApp --> AppLogic
    WebApp --> AuthLogic
    AppLogic -- TCP --> OracleDB
    AuthLogic -- TCP --> OracleDB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between static and dynamic models in UML. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Static models** represent the *structure* of a system and are *time-independent*; they describe what exists in the system.
> - **Examples:** Class Diagram, Object Diagram, Component Diagram, Deployment Diagram, Package Diagram.
>
> **Dynamic models** represent the *behavior* of a system and are *time-dependent*; they describe how the system evolves and interacts.
> - **Examples:** Sequence Diagram, State Machine Diagram, Activity Diagram, Use Case Diagram.
>
> **Key difference:** Static = "what is there"; Dynamic = "what happens over time." *(1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for two examples of each type.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the difference between aggregation and composition with a suitable example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Aggregation (◇) | Composition (◆) |
|---|---|---|
| Strength | Weak whole-part | Strong whole-part |
| Lifetime | Part can outlive whole | Part dies with whole |
| Example | **Team — Player** (a team dissolves, players still exist) | **House — Room** (a house demolished, rooms cease to exist) |

> Aggregation is a *"shared aggregation"* where the container does not own the contents exclusively, while composition is an *"exclusive ownership"* relationship with co-dependent lifetimes. *(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for example.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** *"Draw the UML class diagram for a Library Management System. The system should manage Books, Members, Librarians, and Loans. A member can borrow multiple books, a book can be loaned to only one member at a time, and a librarian issues loans."*

#### Model Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Identify Classes** — `Book`, `Member`, `Librarian`, `Loan`, `Library` *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Identify Attributes** *(1 mark)*
- `Book`: `bookId`, `title`, `author`, `ISBN`, `status` (available/loaned)
- `Member`: `memberId`, `name`, `contactNo`, `membershipDate`
- `Librarian`: `staffId`, `name`, `shift`
- `Loan`: `loanId`, `issueDate`, `dueDate`, `returnDate`

**Step 3: Identify Operations** *(1 mark)*
- `Book`: `checkAvailability()`, `markLoaned()`, `markReturned()`
- `Member`: `requestLoan()`, `returnBook()`, `viewHistory()`
- `Librarian`: `issueLoan()`, `processReturn()`, `addBook()`
- `Loan`: `calculateFine()`, `extendDueDate()`

**Step 4: Relationships & Multiplicity** *(2 marks)*
- `Member` "borrows" `Book` via `Loan` (associative class pattern).
- `Librarian` *issues* `Loan` → Association 1..* to 1..* with `Loan`.
- `Library` *employs* `Librarian` → Aggregation 1 to 1..* (librarian survives library closure in aggregation, but in this scenario, let's model as **composition** if we treat librarian as internal staff).
- `Loan` *references* `Book` and `Member` (composition with Loan as the whole).

**Step 5: Class Diagram Drawing** *(2 marks — awarded for correctness, notation, and visibility)*

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Book {
        -bookId : String
        -title : String
        -author : String
        -ISBN : String
        -status : String
        +checkAvailability() boolean
        +markLoaned() void
        +markReturned() void
    }
    class Member {
        -memberId : String
        -name : String
        -contactNo : String
        -membershipDate : Date
        +requestLoan() Loan
        +returnBook(loanId) void
        +viewHistory() void
    }
    class Librarian {
        -staffId : String
        -name : String
        -shift : String
        +issueLoan(m : Member, b : Book) Loan
        +processReturn(loanId) void
        +addBook(b : Book) void
    }
    class Loan {
        -loanId : String
        -issueDate : Date
        -dueDate : Date
        -returnDate : Date
        +calculateFine() double
        +extendDueDate() void
    }
    Member "1" --> "0..*" Loan : requests
    Librarian "1" --> "0..*" Loan : issues
    Loan "1" --> "1" Book : lends
    Loan "1" --> "1" Member : borrows
    Librarian "1..*" -- "1" Library : works in
```

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** *"Draw the sequence diagram for a member borrowing a book from the library, showing authentication, book search, availability check, and loan issuance."*

#### Model Solution — Step-by-Step

**Time-ordered message exchange:**

1. `Member → LibraryUI : login(memberId, password)`
2. `LibraryUI → AuthService : validateCredentials()`
3. `AuthService → MemberDB : fetchMember()`
4. `MemberDB → AuthService : memberData`
5. `AuthService → LibraryUI : authToken`
6. `Member → LibraryUI : searchBook(title)`
7. `LibraryUI → BookCatalog : searchByTitle(title)`
8. `BookCatalog → BookDB : queryBooks()`
9. `BookDB → BookCatalog : listOfBooks`
10. `BookCatalog → LibraryUI : displayResults()`
11. `Member → LibraryUI : selectBook(bookId)`
12. `LibraryUI → Book : checkAvailability()`
13. `Book → LibraryUI : available=true`
14. `Member → LibraryUI : requestLoan(bookId)`
15. `LibraryUI → Librarian : issueLoan(member, book)`
16. `Librarian → Loan : createLoan()`
17. `Loan → Book : markLoaned()`
18. `Librarian → LibraryUI : loanConfirmation`
19. `LibraryUI → Member : displayLoanReceipt()`

**Combined fragments to include:** `alt` (if book unavailable, show error), `opt` (if member not authenticated, redirect to login).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor M as Member
    participant UI as LibraryUI
    participant AS as AuthService
    participant DB as MemberDB
    participant BC as BookCatalog
    participant BDB as BookDB
    participant L as Librarian
    participant Ln as Loan
    participant Bk as Book

    M->>UI: login(id, pass)
    UI->>AS: validateCredentials()
    AS->>DB: fetchMember()
    DB-->>AS: memberData
    alt Authenticated
        AS-->>UI: token
        M->>UI: searchBook(title)
        UI->>BC: searchByTitle(title)
        BC->>BDB: query()
        BDB-->>BC: booksList
        BC-->>UI: displayResults
        M->>UI: selectBook(bookId)
        UI->>Bk: checkAvailability()
        alt Available
            Bk-->>UI: true
            UI->>L: issueLoan(member, book)
            L->>Ln: createLoan()
            Ln->>Bk: markLoaned()
            L-->>UI: loanReceipt
            UI-->>M: displayReceipt
        else Not Available
            UI-->>M: showErrorBookUnavailable
        end
    else Not Authenticated
        AS-->>UI: invalidCredentials
        UI-->>M: showLoginError
    end
```

---

### Question B (Option 2): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** *"Explain the various UML relationships with proper notation. Illustrate each with an example from a University Course Registration System."*

#### Model Solution — All Six Relationships

**1. Association (—):** `Student` "registers for" `Course` — a student takes many courses, a course has many students. Solid line, no arrow (or with navigation arrow).

**2. Aggregation (◇—):** `Department` ◇— `Faculty` — a department has faculty, but if the department closes, faculty members can be reassigned. Hollow diamond on the "whole" side.

**3. Composition (◆—):** `University` ◆— `Department` — a department cannot exist without the university. Filled diamond.

**4. Generalization (—△):** `Person` △ `Student` and `Person` △ `Professor` — inheritance hierarchy. Solid line with hollow triangle pointing to parent.

**5. Dependency (- - >):** `Registration` - - > `Course` — the registration object temporarily references the course object. Dashed arrow.

**6. Realization (- - △):** `StudentServiceImpl` realizes `«interface» StudentService` — implementation of contract. Dashed line with hollow triangle.

**Class diagram (excerpt):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Person {
        -name : String
        -dob : Date
        +getName() String
    }
    class Student {
        -studentId : String
        -cgpa : double
        +register(course : Course) void
    }
    class Professor {
        -empId : String
        -specialization : String
        +teach(course : Course) void
    }
    class Course {
        -courseCode : String
        -title : String
        -credits : int
        +getRoster() List
    }
    class Department {
        -deptId : String
        -deptName : String
        +addFaculty() void
    }
    class University {
        -uniName : String
        -location : String
    }
    class Registration {
        -regId : String
        -regDate : Date
        +confirm() void
    }
    class StudentService {
        <<interface>>
        +enroll() void
    }
    class StudentServiceImpl {
        +enroll() void
    }

    Person <|-- Student
    Person <|-- Professor
    Student "1" --> "0..*" Course : registers
    Professor "1" --> "1..*" Course : teaches
    University "1" *-- "1..*" Department : contains
    Department "1" o-- "1..*" Professor : employs
    Registration ..> Course : references
    Registration ..> Student : references
    StudentServiceImpl ..|> StudentService : realizes
```

**Mark allocation:** 6 relationships × 1 mark each (notation + example) = **6 marks**, plus 1 mark for cohesive class diagram = **7 marks**.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** *"Draw the state machine diagram and activity diagram for the Course Registration process, including validation, prerequisite check, and fee payment."*

#### Model Solution

**State Machine Diagram for a `Registration` object:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initiated : studentSubmits
    Initiated --> Validating : formReceived
    Validating --> ValidationFailed : invalidData / notifyStudent
    ValidationFailed --> [*]
    Validating --> CheckingPrereq : dataValid
    CheckingPrereq --> PrereqMissing : missingPrereq
    PrereqMissing --> [*]
    CheckingPrereq --> AwaitingPayment : prereqsMet
    AwaitingPayment --> PaymentFailed : paymentTimeout / sendReminder
    PaymentFailed --> [*]
    AwaitingPayment --> Confirmed : paymentSuccess / enrollStudent
    Confirmed --> Completed : semesterEnds
    Completed --> [*]
```

**Activity Diagram with swimlanes for Course Registration:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Student
        S1[Start Registration] --> S2[Fill Form]
        S2 --> S3[Submit Form]
    end
    subgraph System
        S3 --> V1{Validate Data}
        V1 -- Invalid --> S4[Show Errors]
        S4 --> S2
        V1 -- Valid --> V2{Check Prerequisites}
        V2 -- Missing --> S5[Block Registration]
        S5 --> End1([End])
        V2 -- Met --> V3[Generate Fee Slip]
        V3 --> S6[Display Payment Link]
    end
    subgraph PaymentGateway
        S6 --> P1[Student Pays]
        P1 --> P2{Payment OK?}
        P2 -- Yes --> P3[Confirm Transaction]
        P2 -- No --> P4[Notify Failure]
    end
    P3 --> C1[Enroll in Course]
    P4 --> S5
    C1 --> End2([End])
```

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Mistakes on UML Diagrams:**
>
> 1. **Missing multiplicity** on at least one end of an association line: **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing aggregation (◇) with composition (◆)** — examiners look specifically for the diamond fill style: **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting return arrows** (dashed) in sequence diagrams after synchronous calls: **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 4. **No visibility symbols** (+, −, #) in class diagrams: **−1 mark** for whole diagram.
> 5. **No `«stereotype»` on interfaces** like `«interface» PaymentGateway`: **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Drawing a flowchart instead of an activity diagram** — flowcharts lack fork/join/swimlanes: **−2 marks** for activity questions.
> 7. **Mixing static and dynamic elements** — e.g., putting methods in object diagrams, or attributes in sequence diagrams: **−2 marks**.
> 8. **No initial state (●) or final state (◎)** in state diagrams: **−1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Eve Memorization):**
>
> - **UML 2.5 has 14 diagrams** → 7 structural (static) + 7 behavioral (dynamic).
> - **Class diagram** is the most important static diagram. Three compartments: name, attributes, operations.
> - **Six relationship types:** Association, Aggregation, Composition, Dependency, Generalization, Realization. Know symbols cold.
> - **Aggregation vs Composition:** hollow vs filled diamond. Lifetime test = whole deletion.
> - **Multiplicity ranges:** `*` = many, `1..*` = one or more, `0..1` = optional one. Always place on **both** ends.
> - **Visibility:** `+` public, `−` private, `#` protected.
> - **Sequence diagrams** = lifelines + time-ordered messages + activation bars + combined fragments (`alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`).
> - **State machine diagrams** = states + transitions (event[guard]/action) + initial (●) + final (◎) states.
> - **Activity diagrams** = workflows with decisions, forks, joins, and swimlanes.
> - **Use case diagrams** = actors + use cases + system boundary + `<<include>>` (mandatory) / `<<extend>>` (optional) / generalization.
> - **Components use ball-and-socket notation** (◯ provides, ◁ requires).
> - **Deployment diagrams** show physical nodes (cubes) and artifacts (documents).
> - **Object diagrams** are instances — names **underlined** as `objName : ClassName`.
> - **Interface** in UML = `«interface»` stereotype or lollipop notation (◯).
> - **KTU loves asking both static and dynamic for the same scenario** — practice drawing class + sequence for ATM, Library, Hospital, E-Commerce.
> - **Always include at least 2–3 attributes and 2–3 operations per class** in exam answers.
> - **Use real-world multiplicities** — e.g., 1 person has 1 heart (composition, `1` to `1`), 1 team has 1..* players (aggregation, `1` to `1..*`).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Class Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Class Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Class Diagram** is a static structural UML (Unified Modeling Language) diagram that represents the system's **objects, their attributes, operations, and the static relationships** that exist between them. It is one of the most important artifacts produced during the **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** phase and is considered the backbone of any object-oriented system model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A *Class Diagram* depicts the static view of an application. It is a collection of classes, interfaces, associations, collaborations, and constraints. It is used both for **conceptual modeling** (domain modeling) and for **detailed software design** (class modeling).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**. Before construction, you draw a **blueprint** that shows the walls, doors, windows, and how rooms connect to each other. A Class Diagram is exactly this — but for software. Each **class** is like a *room template* (it describes what furniture — *attributes* — it has and what activities — *operations* — happen inside it). The **lines connecting rooms** represent how they interact, such as a *living room connecting to a kitchen*. Just as a blueprint lets builders understand the structure without seeing the actual house, a class diagram lets developers understand the system without seeing the actual code.

### 1.3 Purpose of a Class Diagram (KTU High-Yield Points)

- To **visualize** the static structure of an object-oriented system.
- To **document** classes, their attributes, methods, and relationships.
- To act as a **bridge** between requirement specification and code implementation.
- To enable **forward and reverse engineering** in modern CASE tools.
- To support **reuse** by clearly exposing inheritance and interface structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU 2024 OECST723 syllabus (Module 2 — Modelling with UML), the class diagram is the *primary* diagram used in the analysis and design phase and is examinable for both short and long answer questions.

### 1.4 Core Building Block — The Class

A class in UML is represented as a **rectangle divided into three compartments**:

| Compartment | Purpose | Example |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| **Name Compartment** (top) | Holds the class name in bold, centered | `Student` |
| **Attribute Compartment** (middle) | Lists the fields/properties the class holds | `- name : String` |
| **Operation Compartment** (bottom) | Lists the methods/operations the class can perform | `+ calculateGPA() : float` |

### 1.5 Visibility Notations (Mandatory for KTU Exam)

Visibility is denoted by a single character prefix in front of attribute/operation names:

$$ \text{Visibility Symbols in UML} = \{\,+\text{ (public)},\ -\text{ (private)},\ \#\text{ (protected)},\ \sim\text{ (package)}\,\} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this symbol set** — KTU examiners award partial marks specifically for correct visibility notation. Confusing `#` with `+` is a very common mistake listed in the official valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Class Diagram — Structured Logic

A complete class diagram is composed of **five primary elements**. Let us break each one down.

#### 2.1.1 Classes
- Represented by a **rectangle**.
- May be **concrete** (can be instantiated, e.g., `Car`) or **abstract** (cannot be instantiated, name shown in *italics*, e.g., `Vehicle`).
- A class with no operations and no attributes is called a **marker class** (e.g., `Serializable`).

#### 2.1.2 Interfaces
- Represented as a **circle (lollipop)** OR as a rectangle with the stereotype `<<interface>>` above the name.
- Contain **only operation signatures** (no implementation).
- A class *realizes* an interface using a **dashed line with a hollow triangle** arrow.

#### 2.1.3 Relationships (The Heart of the Diagram)
There are **six** fundamental relationship types in a class diagram:

1. **Association** — *has-a* relationship (general)
2. **Aggregation** — *whole–part* (shared, weak ownership) — hollow diamond
3. **Composition** — *whole–part* (strong ownership, lifecycle dependent) — filled diamond
4. **Generalization** — *is-a* (inheritance) — hollow triangle
5. **Dependency** — *uses-a* (transient) — dashed arrow
6. **Realization** — implements an interface — dashed hollow triangle

#### 2.1.4 Multiplicity
- Specifies **how many objects** participate in a relationship.
- Written at the **ends** of the association line.

| Notation | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `1` | Exactly one |
| `0..1` | Zero or one |
| `*` or `0..*` | Zero or many |
| `1..*` | One or many |
| `n..m` | Between n and m |

#### 2.1.5 Roles
- The **purpose** an object plays in a relationship.
- Written at the end of the association line, often near the multiplicity.
- Example: A `Person` *works for* a `Company` — "employee" and "employer" are roles.

### 2.2 KTU Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet

| # | Element | UML Notation | KTU Keyword |
|---|---------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | Public visibility | `+` | accessible to all |
| 2 | Private visibility | `-` | accessible within class only |
| 3 | Protected visibility | `#` | accessible in subclasses |
| 4 | Package visibility | `~` | accessible within package |
| 5 | Aggregation | hollow diamond ◇ | weak "has-a" |
| 6 | Composition | filled diamond ◆ | strong "has-a", part-of |
| 7 | Generalization | hollow triangle △ | "is-a" (inheritance) |
| 8 | Dependency | dashed arrow ⇢ | "uses-a" |
| 9 | Realization | dashed hollow triangle | implements interface |
| 10 | Abstract class | *italic* name | cannot instantiate |
| 11 | Static member | *underlined* | class-level (not instance) |
| 12 | Multiplicity 1 | `1` | exactly one |
| 13 | Multiplicity many | `*` | zero or many |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Class diagrams are the **foundation for code generation** in tools like *Rational Rose*, *StarUML*, *Enterprise Architect*, and *Visual Paradigm*. The notations above map **one-to-one** with object-oriented language constructs (Java, C++, Python).

### 2.3 Analytical Notes for Each Relationship

- **Association vs Aggregation:** If the *whole* object does not control the lifecycle of the *part*, it is aggregation. Example: `Department` ◇→ `Professor` (a professor can exist without a department).
- **Aggregation vs Composition:** If deleting the *whole* deletes the *part*, it is composition. Example: `House` ◆→ `Room` (a room of a demolished house ceases to exist).
- **Generalization vs Realization:** Generalization connects **two classes** (parent and child). Realization connects **a class to an interface**.
- **Dependency vs Association:** Dependency is *weaker* — it is a "uses" relationship that is **temporary**. Association implies a *structural link* of longer duration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure (How to Draw / Analyse a Class Diagram)

Because class diagrams are *graphical*, the "derivation" in this topic is the **analytical procedure** a designer follows. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Draw the class diagram for X system"* or *"Identify the relationships in the given scenario."* The step-by-step methodology below is what earns full marks.

### 3.1 The Six-Step Derivation Procedure

**Step 1 — Identify the Nouns (Candidate Classes)**

Read the problem statement (use case / scenario). Underline every **noun**. These become your *candidate classes*. Example: *"A university has students, courses, and instructors. Students enroll in courses. Instructors teach courses."*
- Candidate classes: `University`, `Student`, `Course`, `Instructor`.

**Step 2 — Identify the Verbs (Candidate Operations / Relationships)**

Underline every **verb**. These become either methods of a class or relationships between classes.
- "enroll in" → relationship between `Student` and `Course`.
- "teach" → relationship between `Instructor` and `Course`.

**Step 3 — Filter Candidates (Remove Irrelevant Classes)**

Discard classes that are:
- **Redundant** (e.g., two nouns mean the same thing).
- **Vague** (e.g., "system", "process").
- **Outside the system boundary** (e.g., "real world" entities that don't matter).
- **Attributes masquerading as classes** (e.g., "colour" is an attribute, not a class).

**Step 4 — Add Attributes and Operations**

For each surviving class, add:
- *Attributes* — the data fields it stores.
- *Operations* — the methods/behaviours it performs.

Example for `Student`:
- Attributes: `studentID`, `name`, `email`, `cgpa`.
- Operations: `register()`, `viewGrades()`, `calculateGPA()`.

**Step 5 — Establish Relationships with Multiplicity**

For each pair of related classes, decide:

$$
\text{Relationship Type} = \begin{cases} \text{Association} & \text{general structural link} \\ \text{Aggregation} & \text{weak has-a, no lifecycle} \\ \text{Composition} & \text{strong has-a, lifecycle tied} \\ \text{Generalization} & \text{is-a, common features} \\ \text{Realization} & \text{class implements interface} \\ \text{Dependency} & \text{temporary use} \end{cases}
$$

Then place multiplicity at both ends. Example: One `Instructor` teaches many `Course`s, but one `Course` is taught by one `Instructor`:

$$
\text{Instructor} \; \overset{1}{\rightarrow} \;\; \text{teaches} \;\; \overset{*}{\leftarrow} \; \text{Course}
$$

**Step 6 — Refine with Visibility, Stereotypes, and Constraints**

- Apply `+`, `-`, `#`, `~` to all attributes and operations.
- Mark abstract classes in *italics*.
- Use `<<interface>>`, `<<enumeration>>`, `<<utility>>` stereotypes.
- Add constraints in `{braces}` where applicable (e.g., `{age >= 18}`).

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example

**Scenario:** *"An online shopping system has Customers who place Orders. Each Order contains one or more Products. Customers make Payments for their Orders."*

**Step-by-step extraction:**

1. **Nouns (Classes):** `Customer`, `Order`, `Product`, `Payment`, `System`.
2. **Verbs (Relationships):** "places", "contains", "makes".
3. **Filter:** Drop `System` (vague, architectural).
4. **Add attributes/operations:**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|-------|------------|------------|
| Customer | - customerID, - name, - email | + placeOrder(), + makePayment() |
| Order | - orderID, - date, - total | + addProduct(), + calculateTotal() |
| Product | - productID, - name, - price | + getPrice() |
| Payment | - paymentID, - amount, - mode | + processPayment() |

5. **Relationships with multiplicity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Customer} &\xleftrightarrow{\text{places}} \text{Order} \quad \text{(1 to 0..*)} \\
\text{Order} &\xleftrightarrow{\text{contains}} \text{Product} \quad \text{(1..* to 1..*)} \\
\text{Customer} &\xleftrightarrow{\text{makes}} \text{Payment} \quad \text{(1 to 0..*)} \\
\text{Order} &\xleftrightarrow{\text{requires}} \text{Payment} \quad \text{(1 to 1)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The `Order`–`Product` link is typically an **aggregation** (products exist independently of any single order). The `Order`–`Payment` link is often a **composition** (an order is meaningless without its payment in many business rules).

### 3.3 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Correct Relationship

| If the statement says... | Use this relationship | UML symbol |
|--------------------------|----------------------|------------|
| "A is a B" / "A is a kind of B" | Generalization | Hollow triangle |
| "A is part of B (A can exist alone)" | Aggregation | Hollow diamond |
| "A is part of B (A dies with B)" | Composition | Filled diamond |
| "A uses B temporarily" | Dependency | Dashed arrow |
| "A implements interface B" | Realization | Dashed hollow triangle |
| "A is associated with B (no ownership)" | Association | Plain line |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Class Diagram Anatomy (Block-Level View)

The diagram below is a **Mermaid block diagram** showing the *meta-structure* of any class diagram — the compartments, notation, and the typical layout of a class rectangle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASS["CLASS RECTANGLE STRUCTURE"]
        direction TB
        nodeA["CLASS NAME COMPARTMENT\nBold Centered Italic if Abstract"]
        nodeB["ATTRIBUTE COMPARTMENT\nvisibility name colon type\nequals default value"]
        nodeC["OPERATION COMPARTMENT\nvisibility name parentheses arg colon return"]
        nodeA --- nodeB
        nodeB --- nodeC
    end

    subgraph VIS["VISIBILITY LEGEND"]
        v1["PLUS symbol PUBLIC"]
        v2["MINUS symbol PRIVATE"]
        v3["HASH symbol PROTECTED"]
        v4["TILDE symbol PACKAGE"]
    end

    subgraph REL["RELATIONSHIP LEGEND"]
        r1["ASSOCIATION plain line"]
        r2["AGGREGATION hollow diamond"]
        r3["COMPOSITION filled diamond"]
        r4["GENERALIZATION hollow triangle"]
        r5["DEPENDENCY dashed arrow"]
        r6["REALIZATION dashed hollow triangle"]
    end
```

### 4.2 Real Worked Class Diagram — University System (Mermaid Block View)

The figure below renders the class diagram of the *university scenario* analyzed in Section 3. It maps the classes, attributes, methods, and the **six** relationship types. Because Mermaid does not natively support UML class diagrams, we use a functional block representation that preserves the structural meaning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOM["UNIVERSITY SYSTEM CLASS DIAGRAM"]
        direction TB

        subgraph STU["CLASS Student"]
            sa1["ATTRIBUTES\nMINUS studentID int\nMINUS name String\nMINUS cgpa float"]
            so1["OPERATIONS\nPLUS register Course\nPLUS calculateGPA float"]
            sa1 --- so1
        end

        subgraph CRS["CLASS Course"]
            ca1["ATTRIBUTES\nMINUS courseID String\nMINUS title String\nMINUS credits int"]
            co1["OPERATIONS\nPLUS addStudent Student\nPLUS getEnrolledList"]
            ca1 --- co1
        end

        subgraph INS["CLASS Instructor"]
            ia1["ATTRIBUTES\nMINUS empID int\nMINUS name String\nMINUS department String"]
            io1["OPERATIONS\nPLUS assignCourse Course\nPLUS viewSchedule"]
            ia1 --- io1
        end

        subgraph PER["ABSTRACT CLASS Person italic"]
            pa1["ATTRIBUTES\nHASH name String\nHASH dob Date"]
            po1["OPERATIONS\nPLUS getDetails String"]
            pa1 --- po1
        end

        STU -.->|GENERALIZATION is a| PER
        INS -.->|GENERALIZATION is a| PER

        STU -->|ASSOCIATION 0 dot dot STAR enrolls| CRS
        INS -->|ASSOCIATION 1 teaches| CRS
    end
```

### 4.3 Reading the Diagram (Architecture Map)

- The **center block** is `Course` — it is the most *coupled* class, which is typical in real-world models.
- `Student` and `Instructor` both **inherit from `Person`** — this is **generalization**.
- The **multiplicity symbols** `0..*` and `1` are placed at the line ends; in the diagram above they are written inline as `0 dot dot STAR` and `1` for visual clarity in the block representation.
- `Person` is shown as an **abstract class** (italicized), and its `name` and `dob` attributes use `#` (protected) so that subclasses can access them directly.
- The `enrolls` link between `Student` and `Course` is a **bidirectional association** (no diamond, plain line) because a student knows its courses and a course knows its students — neither owns the other.

### 4.4 Multiplicity Mapping Reference (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["END A CLASS"] -->|1 EXACTLY ONE| B["END B CLASS"]
    C["END C CLASS"] -->|STAR ZERO OR MANY| D["END D CLASS"]
    E["END E CLASS"] -->|0 dot dot 1 OPTIONAL| F["END F CLASS"]
    G["END G CLASS"] -->|1 dot dot STAR AT LEAST ONE| H["END H CLASS"]
    I["END I CLASS"] -->|n dot dot m RANGE| J["END J CLASS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** When drawing by hand in the KTU answer sheet, always use *plain lines* for association, *hollow diamond* for aggregation, *filled diamond* for composition, and *hollow triangle* for generalization. The block diagrams above are merely a *teaching aid*; the *actual UML notation* is what gets marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the various components of a UML class diagram with a short note on each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A class diagram consists of the following components:
1. **Classes** — Rectangles representing objects with shared structure and behaviour (1 mark).
2. **Relationships** — Lines connecting classes: association, aggregation, composition, generalization, dependency, realization (1 mark).
3. **Multiplicity, roles, and constraints** — Numeric and contextual annotations at the ends of relationships (1 mark).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between aggregation and composition in a UML class diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Aggregation (Hollow Diamond) | Composition (Filled Diamond) |
|---------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Ownership | Weak / shared | Strong / exclusive |
| Lifecycle | Part can exist without whole | Part is destroyed with whole |
| Example | Department ◇ Professor | House ◆ Room |

(1 mark per row + 1 mark for any one valid example).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU ESE part-B questions in this module are 14 marks each, with *internal choice*. You must answer either **Q(A)** or **Q(B)**. Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the different notations used to represent **visibility** of class members in a UML class diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the following relationships with suitable examples: (i) Association, (ii) Aggregation, (iii) Composition, (iv) Generalization.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Visibility Notations:**
The four visibility symbols in UML are: `+` (public — accessible to all classes), `-` (private — accessible only within the same class), `#` (protected — accessible within the class and its subclasses), and `~` (package — accessible only within the same package). They are placed as a prefix to the attribute or operation name inside the class rectangle.

Example: A `BankAccount` class may be written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BankAccount} & \\
-\ &\text{balance} : \text{double} \\
+\ &\text{getBalance()} : \text{double} \\
\#\ &\text{auditLog} : \text{String[]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks for listing symbols, 2 marks for example, 2 marks for diagram — total 7)

**(b) Relationships (with examples):**
- **Association:** A *plain line* between classes representing a structural link. Example: `Teacher` — `Student` (a teacher teaches students).
- **Aggregation:** A *hollow diamond* on the "whole" side, representing weak ownership. Example: `Department` ◇— `Faculty` (a faculty member can exist without a department).
- **Composition:** A *filled diamond* on the "whole" side, representing strong ownership with shared lifecycle. Example: `Library` ◆— `Book` (deleting the library deletes its books).
- **Generalization:** A *hollow triangle* on the parent side, representing inheritance. Example: `Car` △— `Vehicle`.

(Approx 1.5 to 2 marks per relationship; 7 marks total)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the **UML class diagram** for the following scenario: *"A library issues books to members. Each book has a title, ISBN, and author. A member can borrow at most 5 books. Each borrowing is recorded with a date."*

**(b) [7 marks]** Identify and justify the **relationship type** between:
(i) Library and Book, (ii) Book and Author, (iii) Member and BorrowRecord.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Class Diagram Construction:**

| Class | Attributes | Operations |
|-------|------------|------------|
| Library | - name : String, - address : String | + addBook(), + registerMember() |
| Book | - ISBN : String, - title : String, - author : String | + getDetails() |
| Member | - memberID : int, - name : String | + borrowBook(), + returnBook() |
| BorrowRecord | - recordID : int, - borrowDate : Date | + calculateDueDate() |

**Relationships with multiplicity:**

- Library ◆— *1 to ** — Book (composition; books belong to a library).
- Member — * to 1..5 — Book (association with multiplicity constraint).
- Member 1 — 0..* BorrowRecord (association).

(4 marks for classes + 3 marks for diagram with relationships)

**(b) Relationship Justification:**
- **(i) Library and Book → Composition.** Books of a library are *owned* by the library; if the library is closed, its books cease to exist as library items (2 marks).
- **(ii) Book and Author → Association** (sometimes aggregation in author-centric systems). A book has an author, but the author does not "own" the book (2 marks).
- **(iii) Member and BorrowRecord → Composition.** A borrow record is meaningless without its member; it has no independent existence (3 marks).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong diamond placement** — Aggregation/Composition diamond is placed on the *whole* side, not the *part* side. Reversing this costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `#` (protected) with `+` (public)** — These two are mixed up in nearly 40% of answer sheets.
> 3. **Missing multiplicity** — Drawing the relationship line but *forgetting* the multiplicity numbers costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 4. **Using association where inheritance is required** — If the problem says *"A is a kind of B"*, it is **generalization**, not association.
> 5. **Forgetting to italicize abstract class names** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the abstractness is not visually indicated.
> 6. **Not labelling roles** — Long answer questions often require role names; skipping them costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Class Diagram** is the *static structural view* of a system in UML.
- A class is drawn as a **rectangle with three compartments**: name, attributes, operations.
- **Visibility symbols:** `+` public, `-` private, `#` protected, `~` package.
- **Six relationships:** association, aggregation, composition, generalization, dependency, realization.
- **Aggregation vs Composition:** hollow diamond (weak) vs filled diamond (strong, lifecycle-dependent).
- **Generalization** is the *"is-a"* relationship (inheritance); **Realization** is *"implements interface"*.
- **Multiplicity** is mandatory at both ends of every relationship line.
- **Abstract classes** are written in *italics*; **static members** are *underlined*.
- **Marker / interface** classes have no attributes — they simply tag classes with a capability.
- **Reading procedure:** Identify nouns (classes) → identify verbs (relationships) → filter → add attributes/operations → set multiplicity → add visibility and constraints.
- **Common KTU keywords to memorize:** "has-a" (association/aggregation/composition), "is-a" (generalization), "uses-a" (dependency), "implements" (realization).
- **Tools for practice:** StarUML, Lucidchart, draw.io, PlantUML, Visual Paradigm — hands-on drawing helps in the 14-mark diagram questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: State Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** State Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **State Diagram** (also called a *State Machine Diagram* or *Statechart Diagram*) is one of the **five behavioural diagrams** defined in the **Unified Modeling Language (UML) 2.5** specification. It models the **dynamic, time-dependent behaviour** of a single object (or system) by capturing the **sequence of states** it passes through during its lifetime in response to **events**, along with the **actions** performed and the **guard conditions** that must be satisfied to trigger a transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition (Verbatim from syllabus context):**
> *"A state diagram is used to model the dynamic behaviour of a class, use case, or the entire system. It shows the various states an object can be in and the events that cause transitions between these states."*

The diagram belongs to the *Dynamic/Behavioural view* of the **4+1 Architectural View Model** and is most commonly used in **Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD)** under the *Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson* (Three Amigos) framework adopted by KTU.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather State Machine"

Imagine the **Weather of Kerala as a state machine**:

- The weather can be in one of a few **states** at any moment: `SUNNY`, `RAINING`, `WINDY`, `STORMY`.
- An **event** occurs (e.g., *temperature drops*, *humidity rises*).
- Based on this event and a **guard condition** (e.g., *humidity > 80%*), the weather **transitions** to a new state.
- During the transition, an **action** occurs (e.g., *start cloud formation*).

Just as the weather does not exist in two states at once, an object in a state diagram is assumed to occupy **exactly one state at a time**. This single-state-at-a-time assumption is the **Markov Property** that underpins all state-based formalisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why State Diagrams are High-Yield in KTU Exams:**
> 1. They carry a full **14-mark Part B** weightage almost every semester.
> 2. They test **mapping a real problem** to UML syntax — a skill examiners reward.
> 3. They integrate *OOAD concepts* (objects, events, classes) with *formal methods* (FSMs, Mealy/Moore machines).

### 1.3 Scope of the Topic in KTU 2024

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECST723, Module 2)**, the State Diagram topic is taught under the umbrella of *Modelling with UML*. The expected cognitive levels mapped to **Bloom's Revised Taxonomy (Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001)** are:

| Bloom Level | Verb Used in Syllabus | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| L2 — Understand | *Explain, Describe* | Identify components of a state diagram |
| L3 — Apply | *Draw, Construct* | Build a state diagram for a given problem |
| L4 — Analyse | *Compare, Differentiate* | Distinguish Mealy vs Moore representations |
| L5 — Evaluate | *Justify, Assess* | Validate completeness of state model |

The standard **KTU mark split** for this topic in ESE is **14 marks** (Part B Module Choice, with a typical sub-division of 7 + 7 marks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Core Components of a State Diagram

A UML 2.5 state diagram is constructed from the following **five mandatory constructs**:

1. **States** — The conditions or situations of an object during its life.
2. **Transitions** — The flow from one state to another.
3. **Events** — The stimulus that triggers a transition.
4. **Actions** — The atomic computation executed as a result of a transition.
5. **Guard Conditions** — A Boolean expression that must be true for a transition to be taken.

Additionally, **auxiliary constructs** include:
- **Initial Pseudostate** (filled black circle ●)
- **Final State** (filled black circle inside a ring ◉)
- **Decision Node** (diamond ◇)
- **Fork / Join Bars** (thick black/white bars) for concurrent substates
- **History Pseudostate** (H in a circle) for remembering the last active substate

### 2.2 Anatomy of a State Node

A state in UML is drawn as a **rounded rectangle** with an optional **compartment structure**:

$$
\text{State Name} \quad \big/ \quad \text{entry} \rightarrow \text{action} \quad \big/ \quad \text{do} \rightarrow \text{activity} \quad \big/ \quad \text{exit} \rightarrow \text{action}
$$

The notation convention is:

| Compartment | Keyword | Trigger | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top | (state name) | — | `Idle` |
| Middle | `entry/` | State entry | `entry / start_timer` |
| Middle | `do/` | Ongoing activity | `do / display_clock` |
| Middle | `exit/` | State exit | `exit / stop_timer` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State vs Mode vs Status — A common KTU confusion point:**
> A *State* implies a *change in behaviour*. A *Mode* is a coarse-grained region (e.g., "Power ON" mode). A *Status* is a passive data attribute (e.g., "status = busy"). Only **States** are valid nodes in a state diagram.

### 2.3 The Transition Formula

A transition in UML is a **5-tuple** of the form:

$$
T = \langle S_{src},\ E,\ G,\ A,\ S_{dst} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $S_{src}$ = Source state
- $E$ = Event trigger
- $G$ = Guard condition (Boolean)
- $A$ = Action specification
- $S_{dst}$ = Destination state

The **transition firing rule** is the logical conjunction:

$$
\text{Transition Fires} \iff (E \text{ occurs}) \;\land\; (G \text{ evaluates to } \text{true})
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — it covers ~80% of all Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transition tuple | $T = \langle S_{src}, E, G, A, S_{dst} \rangle$ | Logical structure |
| 2 | Firing condition | $F \iff E \land G$ | Boolean |
| 3 | Number of transitions (worst case) | $T_{max} = n(n-1)$ for $n$ states | Combinatorial |
| 4 | Number of internal actions per state | $A_{int} = 3$ (entry, do, exit) | Cardinality |
| 5 | Mealy output function | $O = \lambda(S, I)$ | Function of state + input |
| 6 | Moore output function | $O = \lambda(S)$ | Function of state only |
| 7 | Number of distinct Mealy outputs | $O_{mealy} = \vert S \vert \times \vert I \vert$ | Cardinality |
| 8 | Number of distinct Moore outputs | $O_{moore} = \vert S \vert$ | Cardinality |
| 9 | State count (compound events) | $\vert S \vert = 2^n$ for $n$ binary events | Exponentiation |
| 10 | Reachability | $S_i \leadsto S_j$ exists in graph | Graph reachability |

> **Notation clarification:** $\vert S \vert$ denotes *cardinality* of the state set $S$, NOT a vertical pipe separator.

### 2.5 Mealy vs Moore Machines — A Critical KTU Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This distinction is a **favourite KTU Part A (3-mark) question**.
