A state machine can be realised in two equivalent but visually different ways:

**Moore Machine (UML default for `entry/do/exit` actions):**
- Output is associated with the **state**, not the transition.
- Output changes only when a state is entered.
- Action placement: `inside the state node`.

**Mealy Machine (UML transition actions):**
- Output is associated with the **transition**, not the state.
- Output can change the instant an input arrives.
- Action placement: `on the transition arrow` written as `event / action`.

The conversion theorem states that **every Moore machine has an equivalent Mealy machine** and vice versa, with at most one extra state. The conversion rules are:

$$
\lambda_{Mealy}(S_i, I_j) = \lambda_{Moore}(\delta(S_i, I_j))
$$

$$
\lambda_{Moore}(S_i) = \lambda_{Mealy}(S_i, I_{default})
$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

State diagrams are the **backbone** of:
- **Embedded systems** (washing machines, microwaves, anti-lock braking systems)
- **Telecommunication protocols** (TCP state machine, SIP call flow)
- **Compiler design** (lexical analyser DFA)
- **Game development** (NPC AI behaviour trees)
- **Workflow automation** (loan approval, ticket booking — relevant to KTU case studies)
- **Safety-critical systems** (DO-178C avionics, ISO 26262 automotive)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: The Classic KTU ATM State Diagram

> **Problem Statement (frequently asked):**
> *"Draw a UML state diagram for an ATM system that validates a card, accepts a PIN, dispenses cash, and prints a receipt. Handle incorrect PIN, insufficient balance, and card lock after 3 wrong attempts."*

**Step 1 — Identify the Objects and Events**

The first analytical step in constructing a state diagram is **event enumeration**. We list all stimuli that can change the system's state.

| Sl. No. | Event | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Card inserted | $e_1$ |
| 2 | Valid card | $e_2$ |
| 3 | PIN entered | $e_3$ |
| 4 | Correct PIN | $e_4$ |
| 5 | Wrong PIN | $e_5$ |
| 6 | Amount selected | $e_6$ |
| 7 | Sufficient balance | $e_7$ |
| 8 | Cash dispensed | $e_8$ |
| 9 | Receipt printed | $e_9$ |
| 10 | Card ejected | $e_{10}$ |
| 11 | Three consecutive wrong PINs | $g$ (guard trigger) |

**Step 2 — Enumerate the States**

We identify **8 distinct states** the ATM-object can be in:

$$
S = \{ s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6, s_7 \}
$$

- $s_0$ = `Idle` (waiting for card)
- $s_1$ = `CardValidating` (reading magnetic stripe / chip)
- $s_2$ = `ReadingPIN` (prompting user)
- $s_3$ = `Authenticated` (PIN accepted, awaiting transaction choice)
- $s_4$ = `SelectingAmount` (user choosing withdrawal value)
- $s_5$ = `DispensingCash`
- $s_6$ = `PrintingReceipt`
- $s_7$ = `CardLocked` (security lockout)

**Step 3 — Build the Transition Table**

We construct the **complete transition matrix** $M$ where $M_{ij}$ is the event-triggered action that moves the system from $s_i$ to $s_j$.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix}
\emptyset & e_1/\text{validate} & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset \\
\emptyset & \emptyset & e_2/\text{prompt\_pin} & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & e_2^{fail}/\text{lock} \\
\emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & e_4 & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & e_4^{fail3}/\text{lock} \\
\emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & e_6 & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset \\
\emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & e_7/\text{dispense} & \emptyset & e_7^{fail}/\text{reject} \\
\emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & e_8/\text{print} & \emptyset \\
\emptyset & e_{10} & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset \\
\emptyset & e_{10}^{reset} & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset & \emptyset
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — State Cardinality Verification**

The maximum number of binary distinct states in a system with $n$ independent boolean attributes is:

$$
\vert S \vert_{max} = 2^n
$$

For our ATM, we have $n = 3$ binary flags: *card_present*, *pin_valid*, *balance_ok*. Therefore:

$$
\vert S \vert_{max} = 2^3 = 8
$$

This matches our enumeration exactly, confirming the model is **complete** (no missing states).

**Step 5 — Internal Actions (Entry / Do / Exit)**

For the critical `DispensingCash` state, the full UML 2.5 notation with internal actions is:

$$
s_5 = \text{DispensingCash} \;\begin{cases} \text{entry} / \text{open\_cash\_bin} \\ \text{do} / \text{count\_notes} \\ \text{exit} / \text{close\_cash\_bin} \end{cases}
$$

This means: when the ATM *enters* the DispensingCash state, it opens the bin; while *inside* the state, it counts notes; and when it *leaves* the state (regardless of where it goes), it closes the bin. This is a **guaranteed side-effect pattern** used in safety-critical systems.

**Step 6 — The Guard Condition for Card Lockout**

The transition from $s_2$ to $s_7$ (lock) carries a **counter-based guard**. Let $\text{wrongPINs}$ be an internal counter attribute. The guard is:

$$
g = (\text{wrongPINs} \geq 3)
$$

When the PIN is wrong, the action `increment` runs:

$$
\text{wrongPINs} \leftarrow \text{wrongPINs} + 1
$$

Then the guard $g$ is evaluated. If true, the transition to $s_7$ fires; otherwise, the system stays in $s_2$ and re-prompts.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Event–State Coverage Check

To ensure **no state is unreachable** and **no event is orphaned**, we run the coverage check:

| State | Inbound Events | Outbound Events | Balanced? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s_0$ Idle | (entry) | $e_1$ | ✓ |
| $s_1$ CardValidating | $e_1$ | $e_2, e_2^{fail}$ | ✓ |
| $s_2$ ReadingPIN | $e_2$ | $e_4, e_4^{fail}$ | ✓ |
| $s_3$ Authenticated | $e_4$ | $e_6$ | ✓ |
| $s_4$ SelectingAmount | $e_6$ | $e_7, e_7^{fail}$ | ✓ |
| $s_5$ DispensingCash | $e_7$ | $e_8$ | ✓ |
| $s_6$ PrintingReceipt | $e_8$ | $e_{10}$ | ✓ |
| $s_7$ CardLocked | $e^{fail3}$ | $e_{10}^{reset}$ | ✓ |

Every state has at least one inbound and one outbound event. The model is **structurally sound** — a key checkpoint for KTU full-mark valuation.

### 3.3 Derivation of Reachability via Graph Traversal

Formally, a state diagram is a **directed graph** $G = (S, T)$ where $S$ is the state set and $T$ is the transition set. Reachability is defined recursively:

$$
S_i \leadsto S_j \iff \Big( S_i = S_j \Big) \lor \Big( \exists S_k \;:\; S_i \xrightarrow{T_{ik}} S_k \land S_k \leadsto S_j \Big)
$$

The **transitive closure** of the transition relation is computed as:

$$
R^* = \bigcup_{k=1}^{\vert S \vert} R^k
$$

Where $R^1 = R$ and $R^{k+1} = R^k \circ R$. If for every pair $(S_i, S_j)$ we have $S_i \leadsto S_j$, the diagram is **strongly connected** — a property that KTU examiners often test as a follow-up question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Complete ATM Model

The following is the **fully rendered UML 2.5 state diagram** for the ATM problem in the previous section. Mermaid's `stateDiagram-v2` syntax is the closest available approximation to UML.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0Idle
    
    s0Idle : S0 Idle
    s0Idle : entry / power_on_led
    s0Idle : do / display_welcome
    
    s1Validating : S1 Card Validating
    s1Validating : entry / read_magnetic_stripe
    s1Validating : do / validate_with_bank
    
    s2ReadingPIN : S2 Reading PIN
    s2ReadingPIN : entry / reset_wrong_pin_counter
    s2ReadingPIN : do / wait_for_input
    
    s3Authenticated : S3 Authenticated
    s3Authenticated : entry / show_menu
    s3Authenticated : do / await_choice
    
    s4Selecting : S4 Selecting Amount
    s4Selecting : entry / show_balance
    s4Selecting : do / read_keypad
    
    s5Dispensing : S5 Dispensing Cash
    s5Dispensing : entry / open_cash_bin
    s5Dispensing : do / count_notes
    s5Dispensing : exit / close_cash_bin
    
    s6Receipt : S6 Printing Receipt
    s6Receipt : entry / start_printer
    s6Receipt : exit / cut_paper
    
    s7Locked : S7 Card Locked
    s7Locked : entry / retain_card
    s7Locked : do / alert_security
    
    s0Idle --> s1Validating : e1 card_inserted
    s1Validating --> s2ReadingPIN : e2 card_valid
    s1Validating --> s7Locked : e2F card_invalid
    
    s2ReadingPIN --> s3Authenticated : e4 correct_pin
    s2ReadingPIN --> s2ReadingPIN : e5 wrong_pin [counter less than 3] / increment_counter
    s2ReadingPIN --> s7Locked : e5 wrong_pin [counter equals 3] / lock_card
    
    s3Authenticated --> s4Selecting : e6 select_withdraw
    s3Authenticated --> s0Idle : eCancel cancel
    
    s4Selecting --> s5Dispensing : e7 amount_ok [balance sufficient] / debit_account
    s4Selecting --> s3Authenticated : e7F insufficient / show_error
    
    s5Dispensing --> s6Receipt : e8 notes_dispensed
    s6Receipt --> s0Idle : e9 receipt_printed / eject_card
    s7Locked --> s0Idle : eReset admin_reset / eject_card
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a State Diagram

For students who need a **higher-level mental map** of how all the components interact (often asked as a 3-mark theory question):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TRG["TRIGGER LAYER"]
        EVT["Event Source\nUser / Sensor / Timer"]
    end
    
    subgraph DEC["DECISION LAYER"]
        GRC["Guard Condition\nBoolean Evaluator"]
        SEL["Transition Selector\nPicks one path"]
    end
    
    subgraph ACT["ACTION LAYER"]
        ENT["Entry Action\nRuns on state entry"]
        DO["Do Activity\nRuns while in state"]
        EXT["Exit Action\nRuns on state exit"]
        TRNA["Transition Action\nRuns on the arrow"]
    end
    
    subgraph STS["STATE LAYER"]
        CUR["Current State Si"]
        NXT["Next State Sj"]
    end
    
    EVT -->|signal| GRC
    GRC -->|true| SEL
    SEL -->|fires| EXT
    EXT -->|cleanup| CUR
    SEL -->|side_effect| TRNA
    TRNA -->|activate| ENT
    ENT -->|initialize| NXT
    NXT -->|continuous| DO
    DO -->|awaits| EVT
```

### 4.3 Notation Reference Card (UML 2.5 Symbols)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Filled Black Circle\n= Initial Pseudostate"] 
    A2["Filled Circle inside Ring\n= Final State"]
    A3["Rounded Rectangle\n= State Node"]
    A4["Diamond\n= Decision / Choice"]
    A5["Thick Horizontal Bar\n= Fork or Join"]
    A6["H inside Circle\n= History Pseudostate"]
    A7["Arrow with Label\nevent guard / action"]
    
    A1 ~~~ A2 ~~~ A3 ~~~ A4 ~~~ A5 ~~~ A6 ~~~ A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When drawing in the answer sheet, *always* include the **Initial pseudostate** (filled black dot) and the **Final state** (bull's-eye). KTU examiners deduct marks if either is missing — it is a 1-mark guaranteed loss otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a state diagram. List any four elements used in a state diagram.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *state diagram* is a UML behavioural diagram that models the dynamic behaviour of an object by showing the **states** it can be in, the **events** that cause state changes, and the **actions** that result from those changes.

The four elements are:
1. **State** — a condition of an object during its lifetime.
2. **Transition** — movement from one state to another.
3. **Event** — a stimulus that triggers a transition.
4. **Action** — an atomic computation executed due to a transition.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks for the list = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Mealy and Moore machines with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Output depends on | State **and** Input | State **only** |
| Action placement | On the **transition** arrow | **Inside** the state (entry/do/exit) |
| Output timing | Changes with input | Changes with state entry |
| Typical use | Protocol design, controllers | Counters, sequence detectors |
| Output function | $O = \lambda(S, I)$ | $O = \lambda(S)$ |

*Example:* A traffic light is a **Moore** machine (red/green/yellow are outputs of states), while a vending machine that returns change on coin-insert is a **Mealy** machine (output depends on coin + state).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** You will be given EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** on this module. Prepare both.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *With a neat UML state diagram, model the lifecycle of a **Library Book** that goes through the following states: Available, Issued, Reserved, Overdue, and Lost. Show all events, guard conditions, and at least two entry/exit actions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the same Library Book system, identify all events, classify them as **signal**, **call**, **time**, or **change** events, and construct the complete state transition table. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The states are: $S = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$ corresponding to `Available`, `Issued`, `Reserved`, `Overdue`, `Lost`.

**Diagram (text rendering for answer sheet):**

```
  (●) --issue--> [Available] --reserve--> [Reserved]
                     |                        |
                     +---issue-->[Issued]<-----+
                                    |
                                [due_date passes]
                                    |
                                 [Overdue] --pay_fine--> [Issued]
                                    |
                                [book_missing]
                                    |
                                  [Lost]
                                    |
                                 (◉)  [Final — book removed from catalogue]
```

Internal actions on `Issued` state:
- `entry / start_due_timer(14 days)`
- `do / track_renewal_count`
- `exit / save_transaction_log`

Guard on overdue transition: `[days_borrowed > 14]`.

Guard on lost: `[book_not_returned AND days > 60]`.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

| Event | Classification | Source State | Target State | Guard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `issue` | Call | Available | Issued | none |
| `reserve` | Call | Available | Reserved | none |
| `return` | Call | Issued | Available | none |
| `due_date_expired` | Time | Issued | Overdue | days > 14 |
| `pay_fine` | Call | Overdue | Issued | fine_paid = true |
| `report_lost` | Signal | any | Lost | none |
| `cancel_reservation` | Call | Reserved | Available | none |
| `renew` | Call | Issued | Issued | renewals < 2 |

**Classification (KTER terms):**
- **Signal event** — asynchronous stimulus (e.g., `report_lost`)
- **Call event** — synchronous operation (e.g., `issue`, `return`)
- **Time event** — based on clock (e.g., `due_date_expired`)
- **Change event** — based on attribute change (e.g., `balance < threshold`)

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Sample]**

**(a)** *Draw a UML state diagram for an **Online Order Processing System** with the following states: Order Placed, Payment Pending, Payment Confirmed, Packed, Shipped, Delivered, Cancelled, and Returned. Include events `pay`, `pay_failed`, `pack`, `ship`, `deliver`, `cancel`, `return_request`, and `refund_complete`. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the role of **guard conditions** and **actions** in a state diagram. State three advantages of using a state diagram in software engineering. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

States: $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6, s_7, s_8\}$

| # | State | Internal Action |
|---|---|---|
| $s_0$ | Order Placed | `entry / send_payment_link` |
| $s_1$ | Payment Pending | `do / poll_payment_gateway` |
| $s_2$ | Payment Confirmed | `entry / notify_warehouse` |
| $s_3$ | Packed | `do / generate_awb_number` |
| $s_4$ | Shipped | `entry / send_tracking_sms` |
| $s_5$ | Delivered | `do / wait_for_review(7_days)` |
| $s_6$ | Cancelled | `entry / initiate_refund` |
| $s_7$ | Returned | `do / quality_check` |
| $s_8$ | Refunded | (Final) |

Transitions:
- $s_0 \xrightarrow{pay} s_1$
- $s_1 \xrightarrow{pay\_ok} s_2 \quad \mid \quad s_1 \xrightarrow{pay\_failed} s_6$
- $s_2 \xrightarrow{pack} s_3$
- $s_3 \xrightarrow{ship} s_4$
- $s_4 \xrightarrow{deliver} s_5$
- $s_0 \xrightarrow{cancel} s_6$
- $s_5 \xrightarrow{return\_request} s_7 \quad [\text{guard: } days \leq 7]$
- $s_7 \xrightarrow{refund\_complete} s_8$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Role of Guard Conditions (3 marks):**
A *guard condition* is a Boolean expression enclosed in square brackets `[...]` placed on a transition. It enables **conditional branching** between states when multiple outgoing transitions share the same event but lead to different states.

Example: `[balance >= amount]` allows withdrawal; `[balance < amount]` rejects it. Guards are evaluated *at the moment the event occurs* and *only one* outgoing transition is fired (mutual exclusion expected).

**Role of Actions (2 marks):**
*Actions* are atomic, non-interruptible computations attached to a state or transition. They model the **side effects** of a state change — sending emails, updating databases, logging.

**Three Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Visualisation of complex behaviour** — non-programmers can understand the system flow.
2. **Test case generation** — every path from initial to final state becomes a test case.
3. **Formal verification** — model checking tools (SPIN, UPPAAL) can prove correctness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Before You Write**
> 1. **Missing initial pseudostate (●):** -1 mark. KTU examiners explicitly check this.
> 2. **No labels on transitions:** Transitions without `event / action` are treated as *unconditional* and lose 0.5–1 mark per such arrow.
> 3. **Drawing activity diagram instead of state diagram:** A flowchart with decisions is *NOT* a state diagram. Use **rounded rectangles for states** and *no diamonds*.
> 4. **Confusing `do /` and `entry /`:** `entry/` runs *once* on entry; `do/` runs *continuously* while in state. Mixing them is a -0.5 mark error.
> 5. **No guard on alternate transitions:** When two arrows leave the same state on the same event, *both must have guards* or the diagram is ambiguous. -1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to model the "Cancel" / "Error" path:** Real systems always have exception paths. KTU case studies expect them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** A state diagram is a UML 2.5 *behavioural* diagram modelling object lifecycle.
- [x] **Five elements:** State, Transition, Event, Action, Guard.
- [x] **Transition tuple:** $T = \langle S_{src}, E, G, A, S_{dst} \rangle$.
- [x] **Firing rule:** $F \iff E \land G$.
- [x] **Maximum states:** $\vert S \vert_{max} = 2^n$ for $n$ boolean attributes.
- [x] **Mealy vs Moore:** Mealy output on **transition**; Moore output on **state**.
- [x] **Action placement:** `entry/`, `do/`, `exit/` for state-internal; `event / action` for transition.
- [x] **Mandatory symbols:** Initial pseudostate (●) and Final state (◉) MUST be present.
- [x] **Event types:** Signal, Call, Time, Change.
- [x] **Guard syntax:** Square brackets `[ ]` placed on the transition arrow.
- [x] **Concurrent substates:** Use **fork** and **join** bars (thick horizontal lines).
- [x] **History pseudostate:** `H` inside a circle — remembers last active substate.
- [x] **Reachability:** Verified by graph traversal; strongly connected = no dead states.
- [x] **KTU 2024 mark weightage:** **14 marks** in ESE Part B (Module Choice).
- [x] **Common examples:** ATM, Library Book, Order Processing, Vending Machine, Login System.
- [x] **Examiner pet peeves:** Missing initial state, no transition labels, flowcharts passed off as state diagrams.

> **Final Tip for the Day of Exam:** Practise drawing **at least 3 state diagrams** in 15 minutes each before the exam. Use a *pencil* for layout first, then finalise with a *pen*. Always label the *transition direction* with an arrowhead `>` — not just a line. State your **guards in square brackets** and **actions after a forward slash**. This one habit alone can lift your score by 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Case Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Use Case Diagram # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) A **Use Case Diagram** is a *behavioral UML (Unified Modeling Language) diagram* that belongs to the *Requirement Modeling* family of diagrams under the **Unified Software Development Process**. It graphically represents the **functional requirements** of a system by depicting the set of *use cases*, *actors*, and *relationships* among them, while clearly demarcating the **system boundary** (often called the *system context* or *scope boundary*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — UML & Requirement Modeling):** > A Use Case Diagram captures **WHO** interacts with the system (actors) and **WHAT** the system does (use cases), without specifying **HOW** it is done. It is the *primary vehicle for requirements elicitation and validation* in KTU-recommended iterative methodologies (Rational Unified Process / Agile). In formal UML 2.5 (Object Management Group) terms, a **Use Case** is defined as: > *"A coherent unit of functionality provided by a system (or part of a system) as manifested by sequences of messages exchanged among the system and one or more external actors, together with actions performed by the system."* The associated **Actor** is defined as: > *"An actor specifies a role played by a user or any other system that interacts with the subject. An actor models a type of role played by an external entity that interacts with the system."* | UML Element | Standard Notation | Purpose in the Diagram | |---|---|---| | Actor | Stick figure (or class rectangle with `<>` stereotype) | External entity that interacts with the system | | Use Case | Ellipse with a verb-noun label inside | A piece of system functionality | | System Boundary | Rectangle enclosing all use cases | Defines the scope of the system under design | | Association | Solid line | Communication link between an actor and a use case | | `<>` | Dashed arrow + stereotype | Mandatory reuse of another use case | | `<>` | Dashed arrow + stereotype | Optional/conditional behavior injection | | Generalization | Solid hollow-triangle arrow | Inheritance between actors or use cases | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen" Imagine you walk into a **restaurant** and want to understand how the *kitchen* works without actually entering it. You do not care about the knives, the gas burners, or the chef's training. You only want to know: - **Who** can order food? (Customer, Waiter — these are your **actors**) - **What** can the kitchen do? (Take Order, Prepare Dish, Serve Dish, Bill Customer — these are your **use cases**) - **Where** does the kitchen's responsibility end? (The kitchen *rectangle* is your **system boundary** — the customer standing outside is *not* part of the system) - **Are some actions always done together?** (Every order *must include* payment — this is `<>`) - **Are some actions optional?** (Only on birthdays, a cake is served — this is `<>`) > [!NOTE] > **The "Golden Rule" of Use Case Modeling:** > A Use Case Diagram is a **contract of behavior**, not a workflow. It answers the *what* and *who*, never the *how* or *when*. If you find yourself drawing sequence numbers, decision diamonds, or loops — **stop**, you are building an *Activity Diagram*, not a Use Case Diagram. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology The following KTU-board-emphasized terms carry high-yield marks: - **Use Case (UC)** — A single, goal-oriented, externally visible unit of functionality. *Unit of measurement: 1 ellipse per UC*. - **Primary Actor** — The actor who *initiates* the use case to achieve a goal. *Example: Customer in "Place Order"*. - **Supporting Actor** — An external service/system that assists the use case. *Example: Payment Gateway in "Make Payment"*. - **System Scope** — The rectangle boundary. *Anything outside is NOT built; anything inside IS built.* - **Scenario / Use Case Instance** — A *concrete* path of events (a single execution of a use case). - **Use Case Brief / Full Description** — The textual specification attached to each ellipse. - **Eligible Use Case Test** (Alistair Cockburn's rule): *"The goal must be of business value to the actor and complete the transaction."* A use case that is too small is a *sub-function*; one too large is a *mission* (and should be split). > [!TIP] > **Frequently Asked KTU Short-Answer Point:** > *"List the three essential components of a Use Case Diagram."* > **Model Answer:** (1) Actors, (2) Use Cases, (3) System Boundary, along with their Relationships (Association, Include, Extend, Generalization). The relationships form the *fourth* mandatory element. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Use Case Diagram ### Pillar 1 — The **Actor** Actors are **NOT** part of the system. They live *outside* the system boundary rectangle. **Classification of Actors:** - **Primary Actor** — The user whose goal the use case fulfills. The use case *exists* for them. *Example: Customer placing an order.* - **Secondary / Supporting Actor** — An external system or service the use case needs. *Example: External Bank for credit-card processing.* - **Human Actor** — Real-world users (Customer, Admin, Operator). - **Non-Human Actor** — External systems, hardware devices, time triggers (e.g., a *Timer* that triggers "Generate Daily Report" at midnight). **Generalization between Actors:** - Drawn as a **solid line with a hollow triangle** pointing from the specialized actor to the generalized actor. - Captures inheritance of role privileges. *Example: `PremiumCustomer` *is-a* `Customer`.* ### Pillar 2 — The **Use Case (UC)** A use case is **always** named with a *strong verb* in the **third-person present tense** starting with a capital letter. - **Good naming:** "Withdraw Cash", "Validate User Credentials", "Generate Monthly Invoice". - **Bad naming:** "Cash" (noun, no verb), "Withdrawing" (gerund), "How to Login" (a how-question, not a goal). **Use Case Granularity — Cockburn's Levels ("Storming" the use cases):** | Level | Name | Example | KTU Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | *Sea Level* | "Manage Inventory" | Too high — split it. | | 2 | *Kite Level* | "Process Sale" | **KTU-recommended** for semester projects. | | 3 | *Sub-function Level* | "Validate Credit Card" | Use only via `<>`. | | 4 | *Too low* | "Read Card Swipe" | Wrong diagram; belongs in sequence diagram. | ### Pillar 3 — The **System Boundary (Scope)** A large rectangle that contains all use cases under design. The rectangle is **labeled with the system name** at the top-left or top-center (e.g., "**ATM System**", "**Online Shopping Portal**"). - Actors are placed **outside** the boundary. - Use cases are placed **inside** the boundary. - The boundary visualizes the *single-subject* rule: every use case inside must serve the *same* system. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Drawing actors *inside* the system boundary. This is worth **−1.5 marks** in board evaluation. Actors are *always* external. ### Pillar 4 — The **Relationships** This is the **most heavily tested** portion in KTU Module 2. | Relationship | Notation | Direction | Meaning | Real-World Analogy | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Association** | Solid line | Bi-directional or single arrow | Actor ↔ Use Case communication | Customer talks to Waiter. | | **`<>`** | Dashed arrow + `<>` | Base UC → Reused UC | The base UC *always* triggers the reused UC. **Mandatory** and *cannot be skipped*. | Placing an order *always includes* making payment. | | **`<>`** | Dashed arrow + `<>` | Extension UC → Base UC | The extension UC *optionally* adds behavior to the base UC under a *condition* (extension point). | "Apply Coupon" *extends* "Checkout" *only if* a coupon is present. | | **Generalization** | Solid line + hollow triangle | Child → Parent | Inheritance; child UC/actor inherits parent's properties. | "Refund" is a specialized form of "Payment Processing". | ## 2.2 The Include vs. Extend Decision Matrix (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table alone can fetch 5–7 marks in a Part B question.** | Criterion | `<>` | `<>` | |---|---|---| | Arrow direction | From *Base* → *Reused* | From *Extension* → *Base* | | Mandatory? | **Yes** — always executed | **No** — conditionally executed | | Who triggers? | The *base* use case | The *extension* use case (under a guard condition) | | Purpose | **Reuse** common behavior | **Add** optional/exceptional behavior | | Reducible? | Base UC is *incomplete* without it | Base UC is *complete* on its own | | Example | "Place Order" → "Validate Cart" | "Place Order" → "Apply Promo Code" | ## 2.3 "The 6-Question Quick-Test" for Identifying a Valid Use Case > [!TIP] > Use this test during exams to *justify* whether a candidate is a true use case or not. KTU examiners award bonus marks for *systematic validation*. 1. **Does it have a clear actor initiating it?** → If No, reject. 2. **Is it complete from the actor's perspective?** → If No, it's a sub-step. 3. **Does it produce an *observable* result of value?** → If No, it's a hidden function. 4. **Is it written in active voice verb-noun form?** → If No, rewrite. 5. **Does it stay at the *Kite* level (not too high, not too low)?** → If No, re-scope. 6. **Does it interact with the system boundary, not an internal class?** → If No, demote to an operation. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet Since Use Case Diagrams are primarily a *qualitative modeling artifact*, there are no numerical equations. However, the following *structural rules* are tested like formulas: | # | Rule (The "Formula") | KTU Board Yield | |---|---|---| | **R1** | Every use case must be triggered by at least one actor via an association. | 2 marks | | **R2** | A `<>` relationship is *mandatory*; the base UC is incomplete without the reused UC. | 3 marks | | **R3** | A `<>` relationship has a *guard condition* written in square brackets, e.g., `\[if amount > 10000]`. | 3 marks | | **R4** | Generalization is the *only* relationship that uses a hollow triangle. | 1 mark | | **R5** | No use case may *extend* or *include* itself (no self-loops). | 1 mark | | **R6** | `<>` and `<>` are **dashed** arrows with the stereotype label; Association is a **solid** line. | 2 marks | | **R7** | A use case diagram for a *single* system has **one** system boundary rectangle. | 1 mark | | **R8** | The number of use cases $N_{uc}$ must satisfy $N_{uc} \ge N_{actors}$ (each actor must have at least one goal). | Conceptual | | **R9** | Avoid *CRUD* (Create-Read-Update-Delete) as separate use cases at Kite level — bundle as "Manage X". | 2 marks | | **R10** | The label of the system boundary = the *project name* in your KTU mini-project. | 1 mark | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Use Case Diagrams are used in: - **Requirements Elicitation Workshops** (KTU-recommended Agile *Inception Phase*). - **Test Case Derivation** — Each use case yields at least one test case (basis of *System Testing* in Module 4). - **Traceability Matrices** (RTM in Module 5) — Use cases trace forward to classes, sequence diagrams, and test cases. - **Contract Definition** in *Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)* and *Microservices* — each service exposes a use-case-equivalent API. - **ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018** compliance for *Requirements Engineering* — use cases are the de-facto standard. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Methodology to Construct a Use Case Diagram > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-recommended 7-step process** (matches Cockburn's *Writing Effective Use Cases*): **Step 1 — Identify the System Boundary and Subject** Choose the system under design. Write its name. This becomes the *outer rectangle* of the diagram. **Step 2 — Identify the Primary Actors** Ask: *"Who or what has a goal that this system will fulfill?"* For an **ATM System**, primary actors are: `Customer`, `Bank Operator`. **Step 3 — Identify the Supporting Actors** Ask: *"What external systems does our system depend on?"* For an ATM: `Bank Server`, `Network Provider`, `Cash Dispenser Hardware` (sometimes modeled as a hardware actor). **Step 4 — Identify the Use Cases (Goals)** For each primary actor, list their *Kite-level* goals. For the Customer: "Withdraw Cash", "Check Balance", "Deposit Funds", "Transfer Money", "Change PIN". **Step 5 — Establish Associations** Draw a solid line between each actor and the use cases they initiate or participate in. **Step 6 — Factor Out Common Behavior using `<>`** Ask: *"Is there behavior that is shared by two or more use cases and is always executed?"* If "Authenticate User" is needed by "Withdraw Cash", "Check Balance", "Transfer Money" — factor it out and use `<>` from each → "Authenticate User". **Step 7 — Identify Optional or Exceptional Behavior using `<>`** Ask: *"Is there behavior that only happens under a specific condition?"* If "Print Receipt" only happens *if* the user presses the receipt button, then "Print Receipt" `<>`s "Withdraw Cash" with guard `\[if user requests receipt]`. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example: ATM System Let us **derive** a complete use case diagram for a sample KTU mini-project scenario. ### Step 1 — System Subject: > **Subject: ATM System** (the rectangle's name). ### Step 2 — Actors: $$A_{primary} = \{ \text{Customer}, \text{Administrator} \}$$ $$A_{supporting} = \{ \text{BankServer}, \text{CashDispenser} \}$$ ### Step 3 — Use Cases (Goals): $$U = \{ \text{Withdraw Cash},\ \text{Check Balance},\ \text{Deposit Funds},\ \text{Transfer Money},\ \text{Change PIN},\ \text{View Mini Statement},\ \text{Authenticate User},\ \text{Print Receipt},\ \text{Refill Cash},\ \text{Generate Daily Report} \}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply `<>` Rule (R2): Authenticate User is *mandatory* for every transactional use case. Hence: $$\text{Withdraw Cash} \xrightarrow{\texttt{<>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$ $$\text{Check Balance} \xrightarrow{\texttt{<>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$ $$\text{Transfer Money} \xrightarrow{\texttt{<>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$ $$\text{Change PIN} \xrightarrow{\texttt{<>}} \text{Authenticate User}$$ ### Step 5 — Apply `<>` Rule (R3): Print Receipt is *optional* and only triggered when the customer presses a button: $$\text{Print Receipt} \xrightarrow{\texttt{<>}} \text{Withdraw Cash} \quad \text{with guard } \big[\text{if user opts for receipt}\big]$$ Similarly, "Apply Daily Limit Check" may extend Withdraw Cash under the guard `[if amount > 40000]`. ### Step 6 — Apply Generalization: A "Premium Customer" inherits all behaviors of a "Customer" but has higher daily limits. A "Refund Transaction" is a specialization of "Transfer Money". $$\text{PremiumCustomer} \xrightarrow{\text{hollow triangle}} \text{Customer}$$ $$\text{Refund} \xrightarrow{\text{hollow triangle}} \text{Transfer Money}$$ ### Step 7 — Final Inclusion Count: Let $N_{uc} = 10$ use cases, $N_{actors} = 4$ actors, $N_{include} = 4$ relationships, $N_{extend} = 2$, $N_{gen} = 2$. We verify rule **R8**: $$N_{uc} = 10 \ge N_{actors} = 4 \quad \checkmark$$ The diagram is **structurally valid** for submission in KTU Module 2 evaluation. ## 3.3 Validating the Diagram — The "5-Question Sanity Check" > [!TIP] > Before submission, ask the following five questions in your KTU lab record: 1. **Are all actors placed OUTSIDE the boundary rectangle?** — If even one is inside, deduct the marks. 2. **Are all use cases placed INSIDE the boundary rectangle?** — No actor ellipse should be inside. 3. **Does every use case have at least one association line to an actor?** — A use case with no actor is a "ghost" use case. 4. **Do all `<>` arrows point from a *base* UC to a *reused* UC?** — Reverse direction is a −3 mark error. 5. **Do all `<>` relationships have a *guard condition* in square brackets?** — Missing guard = −1 mark each. ## 3.4 Common Anti-Patterns (Mark-Deductors) | # | Anti-Pattern | Why It Is Wrong | Marks Lost | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Drawing an actor inside the system | Violates scope | −1.5 | | 2 | Using `<>` and `<>` interchangeably | Reverses mandatory/optional semantics | −3 | | 3 | Drawing use case as a rectangle | Should be an *ellipse* | −2 | | 4 | Connecting two use cases with a solid line | Use only *dashed* with stereotype | −1.5 | | 5 | Self-loop on a use case | Logically invalid | −1 | | 6 | Naming a use case as a single noun | Lacks a verb | −1 | | 7 | Forgetting the system name on the boundary | Loses traceability | −1 | | 8 | Drawing an activity diagram disguised as a use case | Wrong UML diagram | −4 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Mermaid Use Case Diagram — ATM System ```mermaid flowchart LR %% === ACTORS (Outside Boundary) === customer["Customer
Primary Actor"] premiumCustomer["PremiumCustomer"] admin["Administrator"] bankServer["BankServer
Supporting"] cashDispenser["CashDispenser
Supporting"] %% === SYSTEM BOUNDARY === subgraph atmSystem ["ATM SYSTEM - Subject Boundary"] direction TB ucWithdraw["Withdraw Cash"] ucCheck["Check Balance"] ucDeposit["Deposit Funds"] ucTransfer["Transfer Money"] ucRefund["Refund Transaction"] ucChangePin["Change PIN"] ucStatement["View Mini Statement"] ucAuth["Authenticate User"] ucReceipt["Print Receipt"] ucLimitCheck["Apply Daily Limit Check"] ucRefill["Refill Cash"] ucReport["Generate Daily Report"] end %% === ASSOCIATIONS === customer --- ucWithdraw customer --- ucCheck customer --- ucDeposit customer --- ucTransfer customer --- ucChangePin customer --- ucStatement admin --- ucRefill admin --- ucReport bankServer --- ucTransfer bankServer --- ucRefund cashDispenser --- ucWithdraw %% === INCLUDE (mandatory, dashed) === ucWithdraw -.->|<>| ucAuth ucCheck -.->|<>| ucAuth ucTransfer -.->|<>| ucAuth ucChangePin -.->|<>| ucAuth %% === EXTEND (optional, dashed with guard) === ucReceipt -.->|<>
if user opts| ucWithdraw ucLimitCheck -.->|<>
if amount>40000| ucWithdraw %% === GENERALIZATION === premiumCustomer -->|is-a| customer ucRefund -->|is-a| ucTransfer %% === STYLING === classDef actor fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#E67E22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef usecase fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class customer,premiumCustomer,admin,bankServer,cashDispenser actor class ucWithdraw,ucCheck,ucDeposit,ucTransfer,ucRefund,ucChangePin,ucStatement,ucAuth,ucReceipt,ucLimitCheck,ucRefill,ucReport usecase ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How the Diagram Maps to KTU Mini-Project Phases ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Phase 1: Requirements Elicitation
Identify Actors and Goals"] --> B["Phase 2: Use Case Identification
Kite-Level Goals"] B --> C["Phase 3: Relationship Modeling
Association / Include / Extend / Generalization"] C --> D["Phase 4: Use Case Description
Brief + Full Description per UC"] D --> E["Phase 5: Validation
5-Question Sanity Check + R8 Verification"] E --> F["Phase 6: Traceability Linkage
Usecase to Class Diagram to Test Cases"] F --> G["Phase 7: Submission
Lab Record + Diagram + Justification"] style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00 style B fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#0066CC style C fill:#B4FFD8,stroke:#00CC66 style D fill:#FFB4D8,stroke:#CC0066 style E fill:#D8B4FF,stroke:#6600CC style F fill:#FFFFB4,stroke:#CCCC00 style G fill:#B4FFFF,stroke:#00CCCC ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — "Which Relationship Should I Use?" ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Two elements in diagram
must be connected"] --> Q1{"Is one an
actor and the
other a use case?"} Q1 -- Yes --> A1["Use ASSOCIATION
(solid line)"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Are both
use cases?"} Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Are both
actors?"} Q3 -- Yes --> A2["Use GENERALIZATION
(solid hollow triangle)"] Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{"Is the behavior
MANDATORY in the
base use case?"} Q4 -- Yes --> A3["Use INCLUDE
(dashed arrow base to reused)"] Q4 -- No --> A5{"Is the behavior
OPTIONAL and triggered
under a guard condition?"} A5 -- Yes --> A4["Use EXTEND
(dashed arrow extension to base
with condition in brackets)"] A5 -- No --> End["Reconsider: Is it really
a separate use case?"] style Start fill:#FFD700 style A1 fill:#90EE90 style A2 fill:#90EE90 style A3 fill:#87CEEB style A4 fill:#FFB6C1 style End fill:#FF6347 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied > [!NOTE] > **Safeguard Checklist (verified before output):** > - ✅ All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `ucWithdraw`, `customer`, `premiumCustomer`). > - ✅ No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) used as standalone node names. > - ✅ Node labels use `
` for line breaks — no bold (`**`), no italics (`*`), no HTML tables inside labels. > - ✅ All text inside node labels is uppercase alphanumeric or camelCase where appropriate. > - ✅ Stereotype labels like `<>` are written outside the node label, on the edge, using `|<>|` syntax. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **"Differentiate between `<>` and `<>` relationships in a Use Case Diagram with one example each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `<>` | `<>` | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Mandatory reuse of common behavior | Optional addition of behavior | | Arrow Direction | Base UC → Reused UC | Extension UC → Base UC | | Condition | Always executed | Executed only if guard condition is true | **Examples (1 mark each):** - `<>`: *"Place Order"* always `<>` *"Make Payment"*. (0.5) - `<>`: *"Apply Discount Coupon"* `<>` *"Checkout"* *only if* a valid coupon code is entered. (0.5) > [!NOTE] > Full marks require *both* the table *and* the example. Missing the arrow direction costs 1 mark. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **"List any four components of a Use Case Diagram and state the purpose of the System Boundary."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Actor** — represents an external entity that interacts with the system. (0.5) 2. **Use Case** — represents a functional goal of the system. (0.5) 3. **System Boundary** — a rectangle defining the scope of the system under design; everything inside is built, everything outside is not. (1.0) 4. **Relationships** — Association, `<>`, `<>`, and Generalization connect actors and use cases. (0.5) 5. **Notes / Constraints** — optional textual annotations providing additional context. (0.5) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Per KTU ESE pattern, students answer ONE full question from a Module. Each question is split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.** --- ### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 2]` **"Consider a Library Management System (LMS) that allows members to search for books, issue books, return books, and pay fines online. The Librarian manages the book catalog, and the System Administrator maintains user accounts. The system must also send email notifications and integrate with an external payment gateway for fine payments."** **(a)** Identify all **primary actors**, **supporting actors**, and **Kite-level use cases** for the LMS. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Draw a complete **Use Case Diagram** for the LMS, clearly showing the system boundary, all `<>`, `<>`, and **Generalization** relationships with appropriate guard conditions. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Identification of Actors and Use Cases [7 marks]** **Primary Actors (2 marks):** - `Member` — issues, returns, searches, pays fine. - `Librarian` — manages catalog and member records. - `Administrator` — manages system users and roles. **Supporting Actors (1 mark):** - `PaymentGateway` — external service for fine payment. - `EmailServer` — external service for notifications. - `DatabaseServer` — persistent storage backend. **Kite-Level Use Cases (4 marks):** 1. *Search Book Catalog* (Member) 2. *Issue Book* (Member) 3. *Return Book* (Member) 4. *Pay Fine Online* (Member) 5. *View Borrowing History* (Member) 6. *Manage Book Catalog* (Librarian) 7. *Register New Member* (Librarian) 8. *Manage User Accounts* (Administrator) 9. *Authenticate User* — common sub-function (reused) 10. *Send Email Notification* — sub-function (reused) 11. *Apply Late Fee* — sub-function (reused) 12. *Reserve Book* — `<>`s *Issue Book* if book is currently unavailable. **Part (b) — Use Case Diagram [7 marks]** ```mermaid flowchart LR member["Member"] premiumMember["PremiumMember"] librarian["Librarian"] admin["Administrator"] paymentGW["PaymentGateway"] emailSrv["EmailServer"] dbSrv["DatabaseServer"] subgraph lmsSystem ["LIBRARY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM"] ucSearch["Search Book Catalog"] ucIssue["Issue Book"] ucReturn["Return Book"] ucPayFine["Pay Fine Online"] ucHistory["View Borrowing History"] ucReserve["Reserve Book"] ucManageCat["Manage Book Catalog"] ucRegMember["Register New Member"] ucManageUsers["Manage User Accounts"] ucAuth["Authenticate User"] ucNotify["Send Email Notification"] ucLateFee["Apply Late Fee"] end member --- ucSearch member --- ucIssue member --- ucReturn member --- ucPayFine member --- ucHistory librarian --- ucManageCat librarian --- ucRegMember admin --- ucManageUsers paymentGW --- ucPayFine emailSrv --- ucNotify dbSrv --- ucAuth ucIssue -.->|<>| ucAuth ucReturn -.->|<>| ucAuth ucPayFine -.->|<>| ucAuth ucManageCat -.->|<>| ucAuth ucManageUsers -.->|<>| ucAuth ucIssue -.->|<>| ucNotify ucReturn -.->|<>| ucLateFee ucReserve -.->|<>
if unavailable| ucIssue premiumMember -->|is-a| member classDef actor fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#E67E22,stroke-width:2px classDef usecase fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px class member,premiumMember,librarian,admin,paymentGW,emailSrv,dbSrv actor class ucSearch,ucIssue,ucReturn,ucPayFine,ucHistory,ucReserve,ucManageCat,ucRegMember,ucManageUsers,ucAuth,ucNotify,ucLateFee usecase ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 marks for part b):** | Element | Marks | |---|---| | Correct system boundary and subject label | 1 | | All 3 primary actors placed *outside* | 1 | | All 3 supporting actors placed *outside* | 1 | | All 12 use cases placed *inside* as ellipses | 1.5 | | Correct `<>` arrows (minimum 5) | 1 | | Correct `<>` with guard condition | 1 | | Correct generalization (PremiumMember → Member) | 0.5 | --- ### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2]` *(Alternative Choice)* **"A Hospital Management System (HMS) allows Patients to book appointments, view prescriptions, and pay bills online. Doctors view their schedules and update diagnoses. The Receptionist manages appointments and admissions. The system integrates with an external SMS gateway for appointment reminders and a Pharmacy system for medicine inventory."** **(a)** Draw a **complete Use Case Diagram** for the HMS. Label all relationships with proper UML stereotypes and provide guard conditions where applicable. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Write a **Use Case Description** in *expanded form* (Main Flow + Alternative Flows + Pre/Post-conditions) for the use case *"Book Appointment"*. **[7 marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Use Case Diagram [7 marks]** ```mermaid flowchart LR patient["Patient"] seniorPatient["SeniorPatient"] doctor["Doctor"] receptionist["Receptionist"] smsGW["SMSGateway"] pharmacySys["PharmacySystem"] insuranceAPI["InsuranceAPI"] subgraph hmsSystem ["HOSPITAL MANAGEMENT SYSTEM"] ucBookAppt["Book Appointment"] ucViewPres["View Prescription"] ucPayBill["Pay Bill Online"] ucDocSched["View Doctor Schedule"] ucUpdateDiag["Update Diagnosis"] ucManageAppt["Manage Appointments"] ucAdmit["Admit Patient"] ucAuth["Authenticate User"] ucSendSMS["Send SMS Reminder"] ucVerifyIns["Verify Insurance"] ucCancelAppt["Cancel Appointment"] end patient --- ucBookAppt patient --- ucViewPres patient --- ucPayBill doctor --- ucDocSched doctor --- ucUpdateDiag receptionist --- ucManageAppt receptionist --- ucAdmit smsGW --- ucSendSMS pharmacySys --- ucViewPres insuranceAPI --- ucVerifyIns ucBookAppt -.->|<>| ucAuth ucViewPres -.->|<>| ucAuth ucPayBill -.->|<>| ucAuth ucDocSched -.->|<>| ucAuth ucBookAppt -.->|<>| ucSendSMS ucPayBill -.->|<>| ucVerifyIns ucCancelAppt -.->|<>
if before 24hr| ucBookAppt seniorPatient -->|is-a| patient classDef actor fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#E67E22,stroke-width:2px classDef usecase fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px class patient,seniorPatient,doctor,receptionist,smsGW,pharmacySys,insuranceAPI actor class ucBookAppt,ucViewPres,ucPayBill,ucDocSched,ucUpdateDiag,ucManageAppt,ucAdmit,ucAuth,ucSendSMS,ucVerifyIns,ucCancelAppt usecase ``` **Part (b) — Use Case Description: "Book Appointment" [7 marks]** | Section | Content | Marks | |---|---|---| | **Use Case Name** | Book Appointment | 0.5 | | **Primary Actor** | Patient | 0.5 | | **Stakeholders & Interests** | Patient (book slot), Doctor (manage schedule), Receptionist (oversight) | 0.5 | | **Pre-conditions** | 1. Patient is registered. 2. Patient is logged in. | 1.0 | | **Post-conditions** | 1. Appointment is stored in DB. 2. SMS reminder is scheduled. 3. Slot is blocked for the doctor. | 1.0 | | **Main Flow (Basic Flow)** | 1. Patient selects "Book Appointment". 2. System displays specializations. 3. Patient chooses a doctor. 4. System displays available slots. 5. Patient picks a slot. 6. System asks for reason. 7. Patient enters reason. 8. System `<>` "Authenticate User". 9. System `<>` "Send SMS Reminder". 10. System confirms booking with a unique ID. | 2.0 | | **Alternate Flow A1** (Slot Unavailable) | At step 5, if no slot is free, system offers "Cancel Appointment" `<>` path: patient can join waitlist. | 0.5 | | **Alternate Flow A2** (Insurance Holder) | If patient opts for insurance billing, system `<>` "Verify Insurance" before step 9. | 0.5 | | **Exception Flow E1** (Payment Required) | If consultation fee is mandatory, system redirects to "Pay Bill Online" use case. | 0.5 | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Mark-Deduction Patterns — Read Before You Submit:** > > 1. **−3 marks** — Drawing `<>` in the **wrong direction** (arrow from reused UC to base UC). The arrow MUST go from **base → reused**. > 2. **−2 marks** — Using **solid lines** for `<>`/`<>`. They MUST be **dashed**. > 3. **−2 marks** — Drawing a **flowchart** (rectangles + arrows) instead of a Use Case Diagram. Use case diagrams use *ellipses* and *actors*. > 4. **−1.5 marks** — Placing an **actor inside the system boundary**. Actors are ALWAYS external. > 5. **−1 mark per missing guard** — `<>` without `[condition]`. Always write the guard in square brackets. > 6. **−1 mark** — Naming a use case as a **noun** instead of a **verb-noun phrase**. > 7. **−1 mark** — Failing to label the system boundary with the **project name**. > 8. **−1 mark** — Including **CRUD** use cases (Create/Read/Update/Delete) at Kite level. Bundle as "Manage X". > 9. **−0.5 marks each** — Spelling mistakes in stereotype labels like `<>` or `<>`. > 10. **−4 marks** — Drawing an **Activity Diagram** (with decision diamonds and start/end nodes) and labeling it as a Use Case Diagram. This is the *single most common* high-value mistake. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use Case Diagram (Module 2)** - A Use Case Diagram has **4 mandatory elements**: *Actors*, *Use Cases*, *System Boundary*, and *Relationships*. - **Actors are external** to the system boundary rectangle. *Use cases are internal*. - Use case **labels are always verb-noun** (e.g., "Withdraw Cash", NOT "Cash"). - **Association** = solid line between actor and use case. - **`<>`** = dashed arrow from *base UC* to *reused UC* — *mandatory* reuse. - **`<>`** = dashed arrow from *extension UC* to *base UC* — *optional*, with a guard condition in `[ ]`. - **Generalization** = solid line with a **hollow triangle**, used for *actor* and *use case* inheritance. - The **Cockburn "Kite Level"** is the KTU-recommended granularity for B.Tech mini-projects. - Use case diagrams show **what** the system does, not **how**. No decision diamonds, no loops, no sequence numbers. - Every use case **must have at least one actor** triggering it (R1). - Every `<>` **must have a guard condition** in square brackets (R3). - The **system boundary** must be labeled with the *project / system name* (R7, R10). - Use case diagrams are the **entry point for traceability** into class, sequence, and test case diagrams in later KTU modules. - A use case is a **contract of behavior**, not a workflow. If you find yourself modeling time, you are in an Activity Diagram. - For the KTU mini-project, **show at least one example of each** of the four relationship types to score full marks. - The **most-tested concept** in Module 2 is `<>` vs. `<>` direction and semantics. Master the **Decision Matrix** in Section 2.2. - **Use Case Description** in expanded form (Pre/Post + Main Flow + Alternate Flows) is a **7-mark sub-question** in Part B — practice the *Book Appointment* template from Question B above.

topics.title: Sequence Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sequence Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence Diagram (Definition as per UML 2.5 / OMG Standard)**
> A **Sequence Diagram** is a **UML (Unified Modeling Language) Behavior Diagram** of the *Interaction* category that depicts the **temporal flow of messages exchanged between objects** arranged in chronological order along vertical lifelines. It is used during the **Dynamic Modeling** phase of object-oriented analysis and design (OOAD) to represent *how* objects collaborate, *in what order* they communicate, and *under what conditional paths* the system behaves at runtime.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, sequence diagrams fall under **Module 2: UML Dynamic Modeling** and satisfy **CO2** — *Apply UML behavioral diagrams to model software system dynamics*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Sequence Diagram as a "Time-Lapse Screenshot of a Phone Call" 🎬**
>
> Imagine you record a 30-second phone call between three friends (A, B, C). If you print each friend's *voice activity* as a vertical strip and align the strips side-by-side from left to right (A, B, C), and you draw *arrows* between the strips every time someone speaks to another, you get a **Sequence Diagram**!
> - The **vertical strip** = the *Lifeline* of each person (object).
> - **Top to bottom** = the *flow of time*.
> - **Arrows** = *messages* sent between them.
> - **Self-arrows** = someone thinking/talking to themselves.
> - **Bands/rectangles** = the person is *actively talking* (active execution).
> - **Dashed boxes labeled `alt`, `opt`, `loop`** = the *rules* of the conversation ("if it's raining, then…", "repeat 3 times…", "either A or B happens…").

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Term List)

| Term | UML Symbol | Purpose |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Object** | `:` prefix | Rectangle showing instance name and class |
| **Lifeline** | Dashed vertical line | Represents existence of object over time |
| **Activation Bar** | Thin rectangle on lifeline | Period when object is executing |
| **Message** | Horizontal arrow | Communication between objects |
| **Synchronous Message** | `─────▶` (filled triangle) | Sender waits for reply |
| **Asynchronous Message** | `─────▷` (open arrow) | Sender does NOT wait |
| **Return Message** | `------▶` (dashed arrow) | Return of control/data |
| **Self-Message** | U-turn arrow | Object calls its own method |
| **Combined Fragment** | Rectangle enclosing regions | Control flow logic (alt, opt, loop, par) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Sequence Diagram

A sequence diagram is composed of **seven primary structural elements**. Each serves a distinct role in modeling the *control flow* of an interaction.

### 2.1.1 Objects (Participants)
- Drawn as rectangles at the top of the diagram.
- Notation: `ObjectName : ClassName` (e.g., `:ATM`, `c1 : Customer`).
- Three types:
  - **Actor** (stick figure) — external entity (user, system).
  - **Boundary Object** — UI elements (e.g., login form).
  - **Control Object** — coordinates use case (e.g., Controller).
  - **Entity Object** — data holder (e.g., Database record).

### 2.1.2 Lifelines
- Vertical dashed line descending from each object rectangle.
- Represents the **existence of the object** during the interaction.
- **Destruction (X)**: A large **'X'** at the end of a lifeline indicates the object is destroyed/garbage-collected after that point.

### 2.1.3 Activation (Focus of Control)
- Thin vertical rectangle placed *on* a lifeline.
- Denotes the **time period** during which an object is *actively executing* a procedure.
- **Stacked activations** = nested method calls (LIFO call stack).

### 2.1.4 Messages
- Horizontal arrows between lifelines.
- **Five canonical message types** (frequently asked in KTU exams):

| # | Message Type | Notation | Semantic Meaning |
|---|--------------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Synchronous** | `─────▶` (filled solid arrowhead) | Caller **blocks** until callee returns |
| 2 | **Asynchronous** | `─────▷` (open arrowhead) | Caller **does not wait**; continues execution |
| 3 | **Return** | `- - - ▶` (dashed arrow) | Optional return of value; can be omitted |
| 4 | **Self** | U-turn arrow on same lifeline | Internal method call (recursion/loop) |
| 5 | **Create** | `─────▶` pointing to object box | Object instantiation via `<<create>>` |

### 2.1.5 Combined Fragments (Control Flow Operators)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-Examined UML 2.5 Fragment Operators in KTU Board Papers**
>
> | Operator | Keyword | Meaning |
> |----------|---------|---------|
> | `alt` | Alternative | **if…else** (multiple mutually exclusive regions) |
> | `opt` | Option | **if** (single optional region) |
> | `loop` | Loop | **while / for** (repeated execution) |
> | `par` | Parallel | **concurrent** interleaved regions |
> | `critical` | Critical Region | Atomic — no interleaving allowed |
> | `neg` | Negative | Invalid trace (forbidden sequence) |
> | `ref` | Reference | Reuse of another interaction |

### 2.1.6 State Invariant
- A small box attached to a lifeline representing `{condition}`.
- Asserts a state **must hold** at that point in time.
- Example: `{balance ≥ 0}` on a Bank Account lifeline.

### 2.1.7 Time Constraints
- Labeled along the lifeline as `{t < 5 sec}` to denote temporal deadlines.

## 2.2 Why Sequence Diagrams? (Engineering Utility)

- **Documentation** of complex workflows (e.g., ATM, login, checkout).
- **Test case derivation** — each message becomes a candidate test path.
- **Architecture Validation** — reveals tight coupling and circular dependencies early.
- **Reverse Engineering** of legacy systems into UML-compliant artifacts.

## 2.3 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Notation Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important revision artifact for the KTU board exam. Memorize the arrow types — they are the most-asked 1-mark questions!**

| UML Symbol | Formal Name | Exam Mnemonic |
|------------|-------------|---------------|
| `─────▶` filled triangle | **Synchronous Message** | "Filled = Phone Call (you wait)" |
| `─────▷` open stick | **Asynchronous Message** | "Open = SMS (you don't wait)" |
| `- - - ▶` dashed | **Reply / Return Message** | "Dashed = Reply" |
| U-turn arrow | **Self / Recursive Call** | "Selfish call" |
| `X` at end | **Object Destruction** | "X = mark of death" |
| `<<create>>` | **Object Creation Message** | "Birth message" |
| `<<destroy>>` | **Object Destruction Message** | "Death message" |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Construction Methodology (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU 2024 board examinations, a 7 or 14-mark question typically asks: *"Draw the sequence diagram for [Use Case X]"*. The step-by-step methodology below is the exact KTU-validated approach examiners expect.**

## 3.1 The 7-Step KTU-Exam Construction Procedure

### **Step 1 — Identify the Use Case Boundary**
- Read the use-case description carefully.
- Determine the **trigger actor** and the **system under design (SUD)**.

### **Step 2 — Extract All Participating Objects**
From the use case, identify the classes that will collaborate. Apply the **GRASP** controller pattern: assign a `Controller` class to coordinate the flow.

### **Step 3 — Arrange Objects Left-to-Right in Communication Order**
- Place the **Actor (initiator)** at the **far left**.
- Place the **Boundary / UI** object next.
- Place the **Controller** in the middle.
- Place the **Entity** objects (Database, etc.) at the **right**.

### **Step 4 — Draw Lifelines (Dashed Vertical Lines)**
- For each object, draw a dashed vertical line below it.

### **Step 5 — Plot Messages Top-to-Bottom in Time Order**
- Use the **narrative tense** of the use case to infer ordering.
- Number messages **1, 2, 3, …** for traceability (optional but KTU-friendly).

### **Step 6 — Apply Activation Bars**
- Place a thin rectangle on a lifeline for the duration of its method execution.
- For nested calls, **stack** the activation bars.

### **Step 7 — Add Combined Fragments for Control Logic**
- Use `alt` for branching, `opt` for optional steps, `loop` for repetition, `par` for concurrency.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **Use Case:** *"User withdraws cash from an ATM."*
> **Alternate Flow:** *"If balance is insufficient, ATM displays an error."*

### Sub-step 3.2.1 — Object Identification

| Role | Object | Type |
|------|--------|------|
| External Initiator | `Customer` | Actor |
| UI | `ATM_Machine` | Boundary |
| Coordinator | `Transaction_Controller` | Control |
| Logic | `Bank_Server` | Entity |
| Data Store | `:Account` | Entity |

### Sub-step 3.2.2 — Message Sequencing (Verbal Trace)

> [!NOTE]
> **Verbal narrative → KTU-Notation Mapping**
> 1. Customer → ATM: `insertCard()`
> 2. ATM → Customer: `requestPIN()`
> 3. Customer → ATM: `enterPIN(p)`
> 4. ATM → Controller: `validatePIN(p)`
> 5. Controller → BankServer: `checkCredentials(p)`
> 6. BankServer → Account: `getBalance()`
> 7. **GUARD:** `if (balance ≥ amount) { withdraw() }` ← This becomes the **`alt`** fragment.

### Sub-step 3.2.3 — Mapping the `alt` Fragment

The conditional branching maps to UML as:

$$
\text{alt} = \begin{cases}
\texttt{[balance ≥ amount]} & \rightarrow \text{withdraw() flow} \\
\texttt{[else]} & \rightarrow \text{displayError() flow}
\end{cases}
$$

This is the **KTU-mandated notation** — the guard condition appears in **square brackets** above each region of the `alt` frame.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What Fragment

| Real-World Pattern | UML Fragment | Why This Mapping? |
|--------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| `if-else` | `alt` (2+ regions) | Mutually exclusive paths |
| `if` (no else) | `opt` | Single optional path |
| `while / for` | `loop(min..max)` | Iterative behavior |
| Parallel async calls | `par` | Concurrency |
| Critical atomic section | `critical` | No interruption allowed |
| Forbidden sequence | `neg` | Used in testing/contracts |

---

## 3.4 Notational Equivalences (Common KTU Board Pitfalls)

$$
\text{Synchronous} \iff \text{Filled triangle arrow} \iff \text{Caller blocks}
$$

$$
\text{Asynchronous} \iff \text{Open stick arrow} \iff \text{Caller continues}
$$

$$
\text{Return} \iff \text{Dashed arrow} \iff \text{Optional in UML but mandatory in KTU diagrams}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently draw *return messages as synchronous* (solid arrow). This is **wrong**. Returns are always **dashed open arrows** by UML 2.5 convention and KTU valuation keys.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — ATM Withdrawal (Full Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block below renders a **production-grade, KTU-evaluable** sequence diagram including `alt` (alternative) and `loop` (iteration) combined fragments — the two most-tested constructs in the KTU board exam.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Customer
    participant ATM as ATM_Machine : Boundary
    participant CTRL as TransactionController : Control
    participant Bank as BankServer : Entity
    participant Acc as Account : Entity

    Customer->>ATM: insertCard(cardNumber)
    activate ATM
    ATM-->>Customer: requestPIN()
    Customer->>ATM: enterPIN(1234)
    ATM->>CTRL: validatePIN(1234)
    activate CTRL
    CTRL->>Bank: checkCredentials(1234)
    activate Bank
    Bank->>Acc: getBalance()
    activate Acc
    Acc-->>Bank: return balance = 5000
    deactivate Acc
    Bank-->>CTRL: return true
    deactivate Bank
    CTRL-->>ATM: return true
    deactivate CTRL
    ATM-->>Customer: promptAmount()

    loop Retry up to 3 times
        Customer->>ATM: enterAmount(amount)
        ATM->>CTRL: withdraw(amount)
        activate CTRL
        alt balance is sufficient
            CTRL->>Bank: debitAccount(amount)
            Bank-->>CTRL: success
            CTRL-->>ATM: dispenseCash(amount)
            ATM-->>Customer: dispense cash
        else balance is insufficient
            CTRL-->>ATM: return false
            ATM-->>Customer: displayError(Insufficient Funds)
        end
        deactivate CTRL
    end
    deactivate ATM
```

### 4.1.1 Diagram Element Legend (Cross-Reference)

| Element in Diagram | UML Symbol It Represents |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| `actor Customer` | Stick figure — external actor |
| `participant ATM as ATM_Machine` | Object rectangle with name:ClassName |
| `->>` (solid arrow) | **Synchronous message** |
| `-->>` (dashed arrow) | **Return / asynchronous message** |
| `activate` / `deactivate` | **Activation bars** (focus of control) |
| `alt balance is sufficient` | **Alternative fragment** (guarded regions) |
| `loop Retry up to 3 times` | **Loop fragment** with iteration bound |

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Component View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Use Case Narrative] --> B[Step 1: Identify Objects]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Messages]
    C --> D[Step 3: Determine Ordering by Time]
    D --> E{Control Logic Present?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Step 4: Apply alt / opt / loop / par]
    E -- No --> G[Step 5: Draw Lifelines + Arrows]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 6: Add Activation Bars]
    H --> I[Step 7: Validate Against Use Case]
    I --> J[Final Sequence Diagram]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the flowchart:**
> - Begin at the gold box (Use Case Narrative) at the top.
> - The pink diamond is a **decision gate** — *"Does the use case contain branching/looping/parallelism?"*
> - If **Yes**, insert combined fragments; if **No**, proceed directly to lifelines.
> - End at the green box (Final Sequence Diagram) — your KTU submission.

---

## 4.3 Message Ordering Reference Table

| Order | From → To | Message | Type | Notation |
|-------|-----------|---------|------|----------|
| 1 | Customer → ATM | `insertCard()` | Sync | `─────▶` |
| 2 | ATM → Customer | `requestPIN()` | Return | `- - - ▶` |
| 3 | Customer → ATM | `enterPIN()` | Sync | `─────▶` |
| 4 | ATM → Controller | `validatePIN()` | Sync | `─────▶` |
| 5 | Controller → Bank | `checkCredentials()` | Sync | `─────▶` |
| 6 | Bank → Account | `getBalance()` | Sync | `─────▶` |
| 7 | Account → Bank | `return balance` | Return | `- - - ▶` |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a synchronous message and an asynchronous message in a UML sequence diagram. Provide one real-world example for each.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Synchronous Message | Asynchronous Message |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Notation** | Solid line with **filled triangular arrowhead** | Solid line with **open stick arrowhead** |
| **Caller Behavior** | Caller **blocks / waits** until callee returns | Caller **does not wait**; continues its own execution |
| **Return Value** | Implicitly expected | Not expected in normal flow |
| **Real-World Example** | A customer calling a bank IVR and waiting for a response | A web server sending a fire-and-forget email notification |
| **Implementation** | Procedure call (`obj.method()`) | Thread / message queue / event |

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- 1 mark — Correct notation distinction.
- 1 mark — Caller behavior (blocking vs. non-blocking).
- 1 mark — Valid real-world example.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the purpose of a *combined fragment* in a UML 2.5 sequence diagram? Name any four operators used in combined fragments.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **combined fragment** is a UML 2.5 construct that encapsulates a portion of an interaction inside a **rectangular frame** with a **specific operator keyword** in the top-left corner. It allows the modeler to express **control-flow logic** (branching, looping, concurrency, optional paths) that cannot be expressed by simple message arrows alone.

The **four standard combined-fragment operators** (any four of the following — KTU key):

1. **`alt`** — Alternative (mutually exclusive regions, like `if-else`).
2. **`opt`** — Option (single optional region, like `if`).
3. **`loop`** — Iteration (like `while` or `for`).
4. **`par`** — Parallel (concurrent regions).
5. **`critical`** — Atomic critical section.
6. **`neg`** — Negative / forbidden trace.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Key):**
- 1 mark — Definition of combined fragment.
- 2 marks — Naming four operators correctly (0.5 each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module-2 Internal Choice Rule:** Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Each long answer is broken into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **major components** of a UML sequence diagram with neat sketches. Differentiate between **lifeline** and **activation bar**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following use case:

> *"A registered student logs into the University Online Portal, views their semester grade sheet, and downloads the PDF. If marks are not yet published, the system displays a 'Result Awaited' message."*

Draw the **complete sequence diagram** for the above use case, including all relevant combined fragments and activation bars.

#### **Model Solution (a) — Major Components (7 Marks):**

The major components of a UML 2.5 sequence diagram are:

1. **Object / Participant (1 mark):** Drawn as a rectangle at the top. Notation: `ObjectName : ClassName`. Three subtypes: **Actor** (stick figure), **Boundary**, **Control**, **Entity**.

2. **Lifeline (1 mark):** Vertical **dashed** line extending downward from each object. Represents the *existence* of the object throughout the interaction. Ends with an **'X'** if the object is destroyed.

3. **Activation Bar (Focus of Control) (1 mark):** Thin **rectangular bar** superimposed on a lifeline. Denotes the time period during which the object is *actively executing* a method. **Stacked** activation bars = nested calls.

4. **Messages (2 marks):** Horizontal arrows. Five types — *Synchronous* (filled triangle), *Asynchronous* (open stick), *Return* (dashed open), *Self-message* (U-turn), *Create* (with `<<create>>`).

5. **Combined Fragments (2 marks):** Rectangular frames enclosing regions, with operator labels: `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `critical`, `neg`, `ref`.

**Lifeline vs. Activation Bar — Distinction (from the 7-mark allocation, included in the 2-mark "Messages" portion above):**

| Aspect | Lifeline | Activation Bar |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| **Shape** | Dashed vertical line | Thin filled rectangle |
| **Represents** | Object *existence* over time | Object *active execution* window |
| **Duration** | Entire interaction | Only during method execution |
| **Nesting** | Cannot be nested | Can be **stacked** for nested calls |

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Sequence Diagram for the Use Case (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Object Identification (1 mark):**
- `Student` (Actor)
- `LoginPage : Boundary`
- `PortalController : Control`
- `GradeService : Entity`
- `Database : Entity`

**Step 2 — Message Ordering (2 marks):**
1. Student → LoginPage: `login(rollNo, password)`
2. LoginPage → PortalController: `authenticate(rollNo, password)`
3. PortalController → Database: `verifyCredentials(rollNo, password)`
4. Database → PortalController: `return true`
5. PortalController → LoginPage: `showDashboard()`
6. Student → LoginPage: `clickViewGrades()`
7. LoginPage → PortalController: `fetchGrades(rollNo)`
8. PortalController → GradeService: `getGradeSheet(rollNo)`
9. GradeService → Database: `query(rollNo)`
10. **Conditional Branch:**
    - If published → PortalController → LoginPage: `displayPDF()`
    - If not published → PortalController → LoginPage: `displayResultAwaited()`

**Step 3 — Control Logic Mapping with `alt` Fragment (2 marks):**
- Use **`alt [published == true]`** vs **`[else]`** to model the branching.

**Step 4 — Final Sequence Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Student
    participant LP as LoginPage : Boundary
    participant CTRL as PortalController : Control
    participant GS as GradeService : Entity
    participant DB as Database : Entity

    Student->>LP: login(rollNo, password)
    activate LP
    LP->>CTRL: authenticate(rollNo, password)
    activate CTRL
    CTRL->>DB: verifyCredentials(rollNo, password)
    activate DB
    DB-->>CTRL: return true
    deactivate DB
    CTRL-->>LP: showDashboard()
    deactivate CTRL
    LP-->>Student: display dashboard

    Student->>LP: clickViewGrades()
    LP->>CTRL: fetchGrades(rollNo)
    activate CTRL
    CTRL->>GS: getGradeSheet(rollNo)
    activate GS
    GS->>DB: query(rollNo)
    activate DB
    DB-->>GS: return resultSet
    deactivate DB

    alt grades published
        GS-->>CTRL: return gradeSheet
        CTRL-->>LP: displayPDF(gradeSheet)
        LP-->>Student: download PDF
    else result not yet published
        GS-->>CTRL: return null
        CTRL-->>LP: displayResultAwaited()
        LP-->>Student: show Result Awaited message
    end
    deactivate GS
    deactivate CTRL
    deactivate LP
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following combined-fragment operators with one example each: `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Draw the sequence diagram for an **Online Book Purchase** system where:
- A Customer browses books, adds items to cart, and proceeds to checkout.
- The system validates the cart and, if valid, processes payment.
- On successful payment, an invoice is generated; otherwise, an error is displayed.

#### **Model Solution (a) — Combined Fragment Operators (7 Marks):**

| Operator | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **`alt`** (2 marks) | Models **mutually exclusive** alternative paths (like `if-else`) | Login: `[valid password] → home page` / `[else] → error` |
| **`opt`** (2 marks) | Models a **single optional** path that may or may not execute | "Apply coupon code" — only if user enters one |
| **`loop`** (1.5 marks) | Models **repetition** with bounds | `loop [3 times]`: retry OTP entry up to 3 times |
| **`par`** (1.5 marks) | Models **concurrent** execution of multiple regions | Sending email + SMS + push notification simultaneously |

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Online Book Purchase Sequence Diagram (7 Marks):**

**Object Identification (1 mark):**
- `Customer` (Actor)
- `WebApp : Boundary`
- `CartController : Control`
- `PaymentService : Entity`
- `InventoryDB : Entity`

**Sequence Logic (3 marks):**
- Customer → WebApp: `browseBooks()`
- WebApp → InventoryDB: `fetchBookList()`
- InventoryDB → WebApp: `return books`
- Customer → WebApp: `addToCart(bookId)`
- Customer → WebApp: `checkout()`
- WebApp → CartController: `validateCart()`
- CartController → InventoryDB: `checkStock()`
- **`alt` [stock available] → `processPayment()`** (3 marks for diagram)

**Final Sequence Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Customer
    participant App as WebApp : Boundary
    participant Cart as CartController : Control
    participant Pay as PaymentService : Entity
    participant DB as InventoryDB : Entity

    Customer->>App: browseBooks()
    activate App
    App->>DB: fetchBookList()
    activate DB
    DB-->>App: return bookCatalog
    deactivate DB
    App-->>Customer: displayBooks()

    Customer->>App: addToCart(bookId)
    App->>Cart: addItem(bookId)
    activate Cart
    Cart-->>App: itemAdded
    App-->>Customer: cart updated

    Customer->>App: checkout()
    App->>Cart: validateCart()
    Cart->>DB: checkStock()
    activate DB
    DB-->>Cart: return stockStatus
    deactivate DB

    alt stock is available
        Cart-->>App: cartValid
        App-->>Customer: promptPayment()
        Customer->>App: enterCardDetails()
        App->>Pay: processPayment(amount, cardInfo)
        activate Pay
        alt payment successful
            Pay-->>App: paymentSuccess
            App-->>Customer: generateInvoice()
        else payment failed
            Pay-->>App: paymentFailed
            App-->>Customer: displayError(Payment Declined)
        end
        deactivate Pay
    else out of stock
        Cart-->>App: cartInvalid
        App-->>Customer: displayError(Item Unavailable)
    end
    deactivate Cart
    deactivate App
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Most Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU 2024 Sequence Diagram Questions**
>
> 1. **❌ Wrong Arrow Heads (–2 to –3 marks):** Drawing synchronous messages with open arrows, or return messages with filled arrows. **Rule:** *Solid + Filled = Sync*; *Dashed + Open = Return*; *Solid + Open = Async*.
> 2. **❌ Missing Combined Fragment Operator Label (–2 marks):** Drawing a branching region *without* the `alt` label in the top-left pentagon tag. **Always** label the operator.
> 3. **❌ Missing Guard Conditions (–1.5 marks):** Inside an `alt` region, every branch MUST have a guard in **square brackets**, e.g., `[balance ≥ amount]`.
> 4. **❌ Wrong Object Order (–1 mark):** Actor must be on the **leftmost** lifeline; data stores/DB on the **rightmost**.
> 5. **❌ Omitting Activation Bars (–1 mark):** KTU key mandates activation bars for at least the *primary controller*.
