> 6. **❌ Horizontal time arrow (–1 mark):** Some students write "Time →" at the top. UML convention is **implicit top-to-bottom** time flow; do NOT add it.
> 7. **❌ Confusing Sequence with Collaboration Diagram (–1 mark):** A collaboration diagram has **no lifelines**, only numbered messages. Sequence diagrams have *lifelines + messages* without numbering (unless explicitly annotated).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall**

### 🎯 **Core Definition**
- Sequence diagram = UML **behavioral interaction diagram** showing **time-ordered** message exchange between objects.

### 🎯 **Five Must-Know Message Types**
1. Synchronous — `─────▶` filled triangle.
2. Asynchronous — `─────▷` open stick.
3. Return — `- - - ▶` dashed.
4. Self-message — U-turn arrow.
5. Create / Destroy — `<<create>>` / `<<destroy>>` stereotype.

### 🎯 **Three Pillars of a Lifeline**
- **Dashed line** = existence.
- **Activation rectangle** = active execution.
- **'X' at bottom** = object destruction.

### 🎯 **Seven Combined Fragments (Memorize All)**
- `alt`, `opt`, `loop`, `par`, `critical`, `neg`, `ref`.

### 🎯 **Object Placement Rule**
- Actor → Boundary → Control → Entity (left to right).

### 🎯 **Time Axis**
- Time flows **top → bottom** (never draw a horizontal time arrow).

### 🎯 **Draw Sequence Diagrams in 7 Steps**
1. Identify Use Case Boundary.
2. Extract Objects.
3. Order Objects L→R.
4. Draw Lifelines.
5. Plot Messages T→B.
6. Add Activation Bars.
7. Apply Combined Fragments.

### 🎯 **Exam Rule of Thumb**
- If a use case has *branching* → use `alt`.
- If a use case has *optional step* → use `opt`.
- If a use case has *repetition* → use `loop`.
- If a use case has *concurrent calls* → use `par`.

### 🎯 **Don't Forget in the Exam**
- Label every `alt`/`opt`/`loop` region with a **guard in square brackets**.
- Use **dashed arrows** for ALL return messages.
- Activation bars on the **controller** lifeline are **mandatory** in KTU valuation keys.
- Numbering messages (1, 2, 3…) is **optional** but earns bonus clarity marks.

### 🎯 **Common Real-World Use Cases to Practice**
- ATM Cash Withdrawal.
- Online Shopping Checkout.
- Student Login + View Grades.
- Hotel Room Booking.
- Railway Ticket Reservation.
- Mobile Recharge.

> **End of Module 2 — Sequence Diagram Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme • OECST723 Software Engineering • Module 2*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies: Voice Mail System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Voice Mail System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Voice Mail System (VMS)** is a computer-based, interactive real-time system that accepts a combination of voice and touch-tone inputs from a telephone user, stores voice messages, retrieves them on demand, and forwards them to designated recipients. In the context of Software Engineering, it serves as a canonical **object-oriented analysis and design (OOAD) case study** used to illustrate requirements elicitation, use-case modeling, object identification, dynamic modeling (state, sequence, activity), and architectural design under the Unified Modeling Language (UML) framework.

The Voice Mail System is typically described using the **Pressman (Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach)** or **Sommerville (Software Engineering, 10th Ed.)** case-study pattern, where the system is decomposed into:

- **External Actors:** Mailbox User, System Administrator, PSTN/PBX Network
- **Internal Objects:** Message, Mailbox, Extension, Voice Processor, Switch Matrix
- **Quality Attributes:** Reliability (24×7 operation), Availability, Modifiability, Performance (real-time response)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), this topic is mapped to **CO2 — Apply software engineering modeling techniques to real-world systems**. The Voice Mail case study is a high-weightage topic in Part B questions (14 marks) and frequently appears in Part A as a short-answer question (3 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **digital post office for voice** — similar to how email servers store text messages, a Voice Mail System stores spoken messages. When you call a colleague and they don't answer, your voice message is *digitized, compressed, stored on a server disk, and indexed* under their unique mailbox. The colleague can later dial in, authenticate with a PIN, and retrieve the stored voice file, which is *decompressed and replayed* in real time.

From a software engineer's perspective, the Voice Mail System is a **microcosm of distributed, real-time, concurrent software**: it must handle multiple simultaneous calls (concurrency), must respond within human-perceptible limits (real-time), must never lose a message (reliability), and must be easily configurable as the organization grows (modifiability).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Breakdown — The 5 "Lifelines" of a Voice Mail System:**
> 1. **Input Lifeline** — Accepts DTMF tones and voice packets (audio codec).
> 2. **Storage Lifeline** — Persistent storage of compressed audio (e.g., ADPCM, G.711).
> 3. **Routing Lifeline** — Switch matrix that connects caller to the correct mailbox.
> 4. **Notification Lifeline** — Message-waiting indicators (MWI) on phones.
> 5. **Administration Lifeline** — Web/CLI interface for mailbox provisioning.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following metrics are **industry-standard** for benchmarking voice mail systems and frequently appear in KTU numerical/problem-solving questions:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | **> 10,000 hours** | Server-grade reliability |
| **Concurrent Call Capacity** | **8 – 960 ports** | Scalability indicator |
| **Audio Sampling Rate** | **8 kHz** | Telephony standard (G.711) |
| **Voice Compression Ratio** | **4:1 to 8:1** | Storage efficiency |
| **Maximum Mailbox Capacity** | **~ 100 hours of audio** | Per-user quota |
| **DTMF Tone Detection Latency** | **< 40 ms** | Real-time responsiveness |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Decomposition of the Voice Mail System

A Voice Mail System is decomposed into **three classical layers**, each with well-defined responsibilities:

#### Layer 1 — Telephony Interface Layer
- Manages physical/logical connection to the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) or Private Branch Exchange (PBX).
- Performs **DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency) detection** and **voice activity detection (VAD)**.
- Handles call signaling protocols such as **FXO (Foreign Exchange Office)**, **FXS (Foreign Exchange Subscriber)**, **T1/E1**, or **ISDN PRI**.

#### Layer 2 — Call Control & Application Logic Layer
- Implements the call state machine (idle, dial, greeting, record, playback, delete).
- Authenticates mailbox owners via **Personal Identification Number (PIN)**.
- Routes calls to the correct extension or mailbox based on **Dialed Number Identification Service (DNIS)** and **Automatic Number Identification (ANI)**.

#### Layer 3 — Storage & Administration Layer
- Persists audio messages on disk (file system or relational database BLOB).
- Maintains mailbox metadata (owner, password hash, quota, message count).
- Provides system administration (add/remove users, set quotas, view logs).

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

- **Why use object-oriented analysis for VMS?** Because the system has natural, identifiable, persistent entities (Mailbox, Message, Extension) with clear boundaries and behaviors.
- **How is real-time behavior guaranteed?** Through **pre-emptive multitasking** of the OS, **priority scheduling** for telephony I/O threads, and **bounded buffers** for audio streams.
- **How is reliability ensured?** Through **RAID storage**, **UPS power backup**, **redundant network links**, and **transactional logging** of message writes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Disk Space for Messages** | $D = N \times R \times T \times C$ | $N$ = number of mailboxes, $R$ = avg messages/day, $T$ = avg duration (sec), $C$ = bytes/sec | Capacity planning |
| **Audio Bit-Rate (PCM)** | $B = f_s \times b \times c$ | $f_s$ = sample rate, $b$ = bits/sample, $c$ = channels | Storage estimation |
| **Compression Ratio** | $C_R = \dfrac{B_{\text{raw}}}{B_{\text{compressed}}}$ | $B_{\text{raw}}$, $B_{\text{compressed}}$ in bps | Codec selection |
| **System Throughput** | $\lambda = \dfrac{C}{T_{\text{avg}}}$ | $C$ = concurrent calls, $T_{\text{avg}}$ = avg call duration | Erlang analysis |
| **Mean Response Time** | $T_r = T_w + T_s$ | $T_w$ = wait time, $T_s$ = service time | Performance testing |
| **Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t$ = time | MTBF derivation |
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failures/hour | SRE planning |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTRR}}$ | MTRR = mean time to repair | SLA compliance |
| **Pin Hash Storage** | $H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{PIN} \oplus \text{salt})$ | $\oplus$ = XOR, salt = random nonce | Security design |
| **DTMF Bandwidth** | $f \in [697, 1633]\ \text{Hz}$ | Low/high frequency pairs | Codec tuning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In markdown tables, never use the unescaped vertical bar `\vert` symbol inside cell contents — always use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` as shown above to avoid breaking the table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voice mail systems are foundational to **Unified Communications (UC)** platforms such as Cisco Unity, Avaya Aura Messaging, Microsoft Exchange Unified Messaging, and Asterisk-based open-source PBX. The same software-engineering principles learned from this case study — **use-case analysis, class identification, state modeling, real-time scheduling** — apply directly to:
- **IVR (Interactive Voice Response) systems** in banking.
- **Call centers** with ACD (Automatic Call Distribution).
- **Emergency dispatch systems** (e.g., 911 / 112).
- **Voicemail-to-text transcription** services using ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Disk Storage Requirement for a Voice Mail System

**Problem Statement:** A corporate Voice Mail System serves **500 users**. Each user, on average, receives **4 voice messages per day**, with each message lasting **45 seconds**. The system uses **G.711 PCM encoding at 64 kbps**. Determine the **total disk space required to store 30 days of voice messages**.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Formula

From the KTU Formula Sheet:

$$D = N \times R \times T \times C$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of mailboxes
- $R$ = average messages per day per mailbox
- $T$ = average message duration (in seconds)
- $C$ = storage rate in **bytes per second**

#### Step 2 — Compute the Per-Second Storage Rate $C$

G.711 PCM bit-rate is $B = 64,000$ bits per second.

$$
C = \dfrac{B}{8}\ \text{bytes/second}
$$

$$
C = \dfrac{64{,}000}{8} = 8{,}000\ \text{bytes/second}
$$

**Conversion logic:** 1 byte = 8 bits, so dividing the bit-rate by 8 yields the byte-rate.

#### Step 3 — Substitute All Variables

$$
D = 500 \times 4 \times 45 \times 8{,}000
$$

#### Step 4 — Multiply Step-by-Step (Exhaustive)

$$
500 \times 4 = 2{,}000\ \text{messages/day (system-wide)}
$$

$$
2{,}000 \times 45 = 90{,}000\ \text{seconds of audio/day}
$$

$$
90{,}000 \times 8{,}000 = 720{,}000{,}000\ \text{bytes/day}
$$

#### Step 5 — Multiply by 30 Days for Monthly Capacity

$$
D_{30} = 720{,}000{,}000 \times 30
$$

$$
D_{30} = 21{,}600{,}000{,}000\ \text{bytes}
$$

#### Step 6 — Convert to Gigabytes (1 GB = $2^{30}$ bytes)

$$
D_{30} = \dfrac{21{,}600{,}000{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 20.12\ \text{GB}
$$

#### Step 7 — Add 30% Overhead for File-System Metadata and Indexing

$$
D_{\text{final}} = 20.12 \times 1.30 \approx 26.16\ \text{GB}
$$

**Final Answer:** A minimum of approximately **26.2 GB of disk space** is required to support 30 days of voice mail storage for 500 users using G.711 PCM encoding.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Class Identification from Voice Mail Use Cases

The following matrix maps each **actor** in the VMS to the **objects** it interacts with and the **use cases** it triggers. This is a classic KTU-style analytical question.

| Actor | Object | Use Case | Trigger Event | Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mailbox User** | Mailbox | Login | User enters PIN | System validates and opens session |
| **Mailbox User** | Message | Listen to Message | User presses '1' | System streams audio to earpiece |
| **Mailbox User** | Message | Save Message | User presses '2' | System flags message as saved |
| **Mailbox User** | Message | Delete Message | User presses '3' | System moves message to trash |
| **Mailbox User** | Mailbox | Record Greeting | User presses '4' | System records and stores audio |
| **Mailbox User** | Message | Send Voice Message | User enters extension | System records and routes to recipient |
| **Caller (External)** | Mailbox | Leave Message | Caller reaches voicemail | System plays greeting and records |
| **Administrator** | Mailbox | Create Mailbox | Admin runs CLI command | System creates mailbox record |
| **Administrator** | Mailbox | Reset PIN | Admin authenticates | System regenerates PIN hash |
| **System (Timer)** | Message | Purge Old Messages | Scheduled cron at 02:00 | System deletes messages > 30 days |
| **PBX Network** | CallController | Route Incoming Call | Inbound ring-no-answer | System picks up call and greets |

---

### 3.3 Derivation: System Availability Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A Voice Mail System has an MTBF of **30,000 hours** and an MTRR (Mean Time To Repair) of **2 hours**. Compute its **availability** and determine if it meets the industry SLA of **99.99% ("four nines")**.

#### Step 1 — Recall the Availability Formula

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTRR}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
A = \dfrac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 + 2}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Denominator

$$
30{,}000 + 2 = 30{,}002
$$

#### Step 4 — Perform the Division

$$
A = \dfrac{30{,}000}{30{,}002} \approx 0.9999333
$$

#### Step 5 — Convert to Percentage

$$
A \approx 99.9933\%
$$

#### Step 6 — Compare with the SLA Target

The required SLA is **99.99%**. Since $99.9933\% > 99.99\%$, the system **meets** the SLA.

#### Step 7 — Express Downtime per Year (for completeness)

Annual downtime $= (1 - A) \times 8{,}760$ hours.

$$
\text{Downtime} = (1 - 0.9999333) \times 8{,}760 \approx 0.58\ \text{hours/year} \approx 35\ \text{minutes/year}
$$

The industry SLA permits only $8{,}760 \times 0.0001 \approx 52.56$ minutes/year, so the system just barely meets it.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Use-Case Realization for "Listen to Message"

The following step-by-step **contract** is derived for the `Listen to Message` use case, following the **RUP (Rational Unified Process)** style of KTU question-setting:

**Pre-conditions:**
1. The user has successfully logged in.
2. At least one message exists in the mailbox's "new" or "saved" folder.

**Post-conditions:**
1. The current message's "read" flag is set to `true`.
2. The audio stream of the message is delivered to the user's earpiece.
3. A read receipt is logged in the audit trail.

**Trigger:** User presses `1` on the DTMF keypad.

**Main Flow (Exhaustive Steps):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{System receives DTMF tone '1'.} \\
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{CallController decodes the tone into keycode K = 1.} \\
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{CallController invokes mailbox.listenNext().} \\
\text{Step 4:} & \quad \text{Mailbox object queries MessageStore for the oldest UNREAD message.} \\
\text{Step 5:} & \quad \text{MessageStore returns a Message object reference M.} \\
\text{Step 6:} & \quad \text{Mailbox sets M.read = true and persists to DB.} \\
\text{Step 7:} & \quad \text{Mailbox invokes M.stream(audioOutPort).} \\
\text{Step 8:} & \quad \text{Message object opens the audio file and reads packets in 20 ms frames.} \\
\text{Step 9:} & \quad \text{Audio packets are sent to the codec for G.711 $\rightarrow$ analog conversion.} \\
\text{Step 10:} & \quad \text{User hears the message in real time.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative Flows:**
- **4a.** No unread message exists $\Rightarrow$ System plays "No new messages" prompt.
- **6a.** Database write fails $\Rightarrow$ System logs error, retries 3 times, then plays apology.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Use-Case Diagram — Voice Mail System

```mermaid
graph LR
    actorA[Mailbox User]
    actorB[External Caller]
    actorC[System Administrator]
    actorD[PBX Network]

    uc1((Login to Mailbox))
    uc2((Listen to Message))
    uc3((Save Message))
    uc4((Delete Message))
    uc5((Record Greeting))
    uc6((Send Voice Message))
    uc7((Leave a Message))
    uc8((Create Mailbox))
    uc9((Reset PIN))
    uc10((Route Incoming Call))
    uc11((Purge Old Messages))

    actorA --- uc1
    actorA --- uc2
    actorA --- uc3
    actorA --- uc4
    actorA --- uc5
    actorA --- uc6
    actorB --- uc7
    actorC --- uc8
    actorC --- uc9
    actorD --- uc10
    actorA --- uc11

    uc1 -.include.-> uc2
    uc2 -.extend.-> uc3
    uc2 -.extend.-> uc4
```

**Explanation of the Diagram:**
- **Primary actors** (left) interact with **use cases** (ellipses).
- `Login to Mailbox` is an `<<include>>` relationship with `Listen to Message` — you cannot listen without first authenticating.
- `Save` and `Delete` are `<<extend>>` relationships of `Listen to Message` — they modify the post-listen behavior.
- `Purge Old Messages` is a time-triggered use case executed by the system itself (a "system actor").

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Class Diagram — Core Domain Objects

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Mailbox {
        +int mailboxID
        +String ownerName
        +String pinHash
        +String salt
        +int quotaSeconds
        +DateTime createdAt
        +login(pin) bool
        +listenNext() Message
        +addMessage(msg) void
        +purgeOld() int
    }

    class Message {
        +int messageID
        +int senderID
        +DateTime timestamp
        +int durationSec
        +String audioFilePath
        +bool isRead
        +bool isSaved
        +stream(port) void
        +markRead() void
        +delete() void
    }

    class Extension {
        +int extensionNumber
        +int mailboxID
        +String displayName
        +forwardTo(mailboxID) void
    }

    class CallController {
        +int callID
        +String dialedNumber
        +String callerID
        +acceptCall() void
        +routeTo(mailbox) void
        +endCall() void
    }

    class AudioCodec {
        +int sampleRate
        +int bitDepth
        +encode(pcm) bytes
        +decode(data) pcm
    }

    class MessageStore {
        +File storageRoot
        +save(msg, audio) path
        +load(id) Message
        +delete(id) void
        +listByMailbox(mboxID) list
    }

    Mailbox "1" o-- "*" Message : contains
    Mailbox "1" -- "1" Extension : assigned to
    CallController --> Mailbox : routes to
    Message --> AudioCodec : uses
    Mailbox --> MessageStore : persists via
    MessageStore ..> Message : manages
```

**Key Design Insights from the Class Diagram:**
- The `Mailbox` class is the **aggregate root** — it owns a collection of `Message` objects (composition, indicated by the filled diamond).
- `AudioCodec` is a **utility class** injected into `Message` for encode/decode operations.
- `MessageStore` follows the **DAO (Data Access Object)** pattern, decoupling in-memory business logic from persistence concerns.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Message Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : Message received and stored
    New --> Read : User listens to message
    New --> Saved : User presses 2 within 5 seconds
    Read --> Saved : User presses 2
    Read --> Deleted : User presses 3
    Saved --> Saved : User listens again
    Saved --> Deleted : User presses 3
    Deleted --> [*] : Purged by cleanup job
    New --> Deleted : Auto delete after 30 days
    Saved --> Deleted : Auto delete after retention period
```

**Reading the State Diagram:**
- A `Message` is born in the `New` state when first stored.
- It transitions to `Read` upon playback, or directly to `Saved` if the user marks it as important.
- A `Deleted` message is soft-deleted and is permanently purged by the scheduled cleanup job.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — "Leave a Message" Use Case

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant PBX as PBX Network
    participant CC as CallController
    participant Mbox as Mailbox
    participant Greet as Greeting Player
    participant Rec as Voice Recorder
    participant Store as MessageStore

    Caller->>PBX: Dial extension 1234
    PBX->>CC: Route call (DNIS=1234, ANI=9876543210)
    CC->>Mbox: lookupByExtension(1234)
    Mbox-->>CC: Mailbox object
    CC->>Greet: playGreeting(mailboxID)
    Greet-->>Caller: Audio "Hi, I'm unavailable..."
    CC->>Rec: startRecording()
    Rec-->>Caller: Beep tone
    Caller->>Rec: Speaks for 30 seconds
    Rec->>Store: save(audioBlob, metadata)
    Store-->>CC: messageID = 501
    CC->>Rec: stopRecording()
    CC->>Caller: "Thank you, goodbye."
    CC->>PBX: Terminate call
```

**Engineering Takeaway:** The sequence diagram shows the **temporal ordering** of messages between objects, which is essential for designing thread-safe, deadlock-free real-time software.

---

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PSTN / PBX Physical Line] --> B[Telephony Interface Card FXO / E1]
    B --> C[Signaling Stack SIP / ISDN]
    C --> D[Call Controller Finite State Machine]
    D --> E[Authentication Module PIN Hash Check]
    D --> F[Routing Engine]
    E --> G[Mailbox Session Manager]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Voice Codec G.711 / G.729]
    H --> I[Audio Stream Buffer Ring Buffer]
    I --> J[Message Store Disk Array RAID 5]
    G --> K[Administration CLI / Web Portal]
    K --> L[Configuration Database]
    J --> L
```

**Architecture Note:** The diagram illustrates a **layered, modular architecture** — each block is independently testable, replaceable, and scalable. This is the architectural pattern recommended by KTU 2024 for high-availability systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List the main actors in a Voice Mail System and briefly state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Mailbox User** — Owns a mailbox, logs in with PIN, listens to, saves, deletes, and sends voice messages (1 mark).
2. **External Caller** — A non-registered user who dials an extension and leaves a voice message (1 mark).
3. **System Administrator** — Creates, modifies, and deletes mailboxes; resets PINs; configures quotas (1 mark).

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the purpose of DTMF detection in a Voice Mail System? Why is it considered time-critical?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Purpose:** DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency) detection converts the analog dual-tone signals generated by the user's telephone keypad into digital keycodes (0–9, *, #) so the software can interpret user input (1.5 marks).
- **Time-criticality:** DTMF tones must be detected within **< 40 ms** to ensure responsive, real-time interaction; otherwise the user perceives lag and the user experience degrades. Also, the system must reject voice-energy false positives using **Goertzel filters** (1.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **confuse use-case and class diagrams** — remember, a *use case* is a **verb** (action), while a *class* is a **noun** (entity).
> - Marks are lost when students **omit the <<include>> and <<extend>> stereotypes** in use-case diagrams.
> - Always **state the pre-conditions and post-conditions** explicitly when describing a use case.
> - When asked to "design" something, you **must provide a diagram** (UML). A textual answer without a diagram gets only partial marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Consider the Voice Mail System case study.**

**(a)** Draw the **use-case diagram** for the Voice Mail System, identifying all primary actors and at least six use cases. Clearly mark any `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the use case **"Login to Mailbox"**, write a complete **use-case description** including pre-conditions, post-conditions, main flow, and at least one alternative flow. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Use-Case Diagram (7 marks):**

The diagram shown in **Section 4.1** is the model answer. Allocation of marks:
- 3 primary actors correctly identified (1.5 marks)
- ≥ 6 use cases correctly drawn (3 marks)
- `<<include>>` and `<<extend>>` relationships correctly placed (2 marks)
- Neatness, correct UML notation, and labels (0.5 mark)

**(b) Use-Case Description for "Login to Mailbox" (7 marks):**

| Field | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Use-Case Name** | Login to Mailbox |
| **Primary Actor** | Mailbox User |
| **Stakeholders & Interests** | User wants quick access; System wants secure authentication |
| **Pre-conditions** | (1) User has reached the VMS main menu via inbound call. (2) Mailbox exists in the database. |
| **Post-conditions** | (1) User session is created and active. (2) Failed-attempt counter is incremented. |
| **Trigger** | User dials their mailbox number from the auto-attendant. |
| **Main Flow** | 1. System plays: "Please enter your mailbox number, followed by pound." 2. User keys in mailbox number. 3. System plays: "Please enter your PIN, followed by pound." 4. User keys in PIN. 5. System hashes the entered PIN with the stored salt and compares with `pinHash`. 6. If match, system announces: "You have N new messages and M saved messages." 7. System plays main menu tone. |
| **Alternative Flow 1** | PIN mismatch — system plays "Incorrect PIN, please try again" and returns to step 3. After 3 failed attempts, mailbox is locked and admin alert is raised. |
| **Alternative Flow 2** | User presses `*` at any time to return to the auto-attendant. |
| **Special Requirements** | PIN comparison must be timing-attack resistant (use `crypto.timingSafeEqual`). |
| **Assumptions** | The audio codec is operational; the database is reachable within 200 ms. |

Mark allocation:
- Pre/post conditions + Trigger (1.5 marks)
- Main flow with ≥ 5 steps (3 marks)
- Alternative flows (1.5 marks)
- Special requirements (1 mark)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q:**

**(a)** Draw the **class diagram** for the Voice Mail System, identifying at least five classes, their key attributes, methods, and relationships (association, aggregation, composition). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **state-transition diagram** for a `Message` object in the VMS, clearly labelling all states and the events that cause transitions. Explain why a saved message should not transition back to "New". **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Class Diagram (7 marks):**

Refer to the class diagram in **Section 4.2**. Key classes to include:
- `Mailbox` (1.5 marks)
- `Message` (1.5 marks)
- `Extension` (1 mark)
- `CallController` (1 mark)
- `MessageStore` (1 mark)
- Correct relationships (multiplicities, composition diamond) (1 mark)

**(b) State-Transition Diagram (7 marks):**

Refer to the state diagram in **Section 4.3**. The state diagram should include:
- States: `New`, `Read`, `Saved`, `Deleted` (1.5 marks)
- Transitions: `listen`, `save`, `delete`, `auto-purge` (2 marks)
- `[*]` start and end pseudo-states (0.5 mark)
- **Explanation of why "Saved" cannot return to "New" (3 marks):**

> A "Saved" message is one the user has explicitly flagged for long-term retention. Allowing it to revert to "New" would defeat the purpose of saving — it would re-appear in the "new messages" queue every login, creating confusion and clutter. The **New** state semantically represents a message the user has *not yet* seen; once a user has listened and marked it as important, its lifecycle is semantically distinct. The state machine enforces this invariant, ensuring a clean separation between the "inbox" (new/read) and the "archive" (saved) folders, which is a key principle of **finite-state machine (FSM) design** in object-oriented software engineering.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Voice Mail System Case Study**

- ☐ A Voice Mail System is a **real-time, concurrent, event-driven, object-oriented** software system.
- ☐ The three primary actors are: **Mailbox User, External Caller, System Administrator**.
- ☐ The core domain objects are: **Mailbox, Message, Extension, CallController, MessageStore, AudioCodec**.
- ☐ The `Mailbox` class is the **aggregate root** that owns `Message` objects via **composition**.
- ☐ The `Message` lifecycle has four states: **New → Read → Saved → Deleted** (with auto-purge to `[*]`).
- ☐ `Login to Mailbox` has a precondition (mailbox exists) and a postcondition (session active).
- ☐ `<<include>>` denotes **mandatory** dependency; `<<extend>>` denotes **optional** behavior injection.
- ☐ DTMF detection must complete within **< 40 ms** for real-time responsiveness.
- ☐ G.711 PCM encoding at 8 kHz × 8 bits = **64 kbps** audio.
- ☐ Storage formula: $D = N \times R \times T \times C$, with a **30% overhead** for filesystem metadata.
- ☐ Availability formula: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTRR}}$; target for carrier-grade VMS is **> 99.99%**.
- ☐ PIN storage must use a **salted, one-way cryptographic hash** (e.g., SHA-256 with random salt).
- ☐ Real-time performance is achieved via **pre-emptive OS scheduling**, **bounded ring buffers**, and **priority telephony threads**.
- ☐ A VMS exhibits the **four key design qualities**: *reliability, availability, modifiability, performance* — these map to KTU's non-functional requirements learning outcomes.
- ☐ Always include **UML diagrams** in 14-mark answers; a textual-only answer forfeits 30–50% of marks.
- ☐ The Voice Mail case study is also a vehicle for understanding **client–server, layered, and event-driven architectures**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: ATM Example
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Study: ATM Example

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the ATM Case Study in Software Engineering?

The **ATM (Automated Teller Machine) Case Study** is a canonical, real-world pedagogical artifact used across the entire Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to demonstrate how abstract Software Engineering principles (requirements engineering, analysis, design, coding, testing, and maintenance) are applied to a tangible, distributed, safety-critical system. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST723)**, it serves as the unifying thread that ties together requirements elicitation, UML modeling, structured analysis (DFDs), database design (ER), and software testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The ATM Case Study is an **event-driven, client-server, real-time embedded banking subsystem** that authenticates users, dispenses cash, and updates account ledgers through secure communication with a centralized banking database. From a Software Engineering perspective, it is a **multi-tier distributed application** exhibiting concurrent, fault-tolerant, and security-critical characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The ATM is a "Self-Service Bank Teller Robot"**
> 
> Imagine a robotic bank teller that:
> - **Identifies** you via a magnetic stripe card (your "ID badge")
> - **Authenticates** you via a secret PIN (your "fingerprint equivalent")
> - **Talks** to the main bank's vault computer through a private phone line
> - **Dispenses** money like a vending machine
> - **Records** every transaction in a permanent ledger
> 
> From the **customer's view**, it is a simple box. From the **software engineer's view**, it is a symphony of **hardware (card reader, cash dispenser, printer)**, **firmware**, **networking protocols (ISO 8583)**, **encryption (Triple DES / AES)**, **database transactions (ACID)**, and **GUI logic** — all coordinated by software.

## 1.3 Why is the ATM Case Study Important in KTU Exams?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Significance:**
> - Appears consistently as a **14-mark Module 2 question** (Part B) in previous university exams.
> - Tests **all four cognitive levels** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy: Remember, Understand, Apply, and Analyze.
> - The examiner expects **standard diagrams** (Use Case, DFD, ER, Class, Sequence) drawn with **correct UML notation**.
> - Marks are awarded for **both functional and non-functional requirements** — students often lose marks by omitting non-functional ones.

## 1.4 Core Stakeholders of the ATM System

| Stakeholder | Role | Interest |
|-------------|------|----------|
| **Customer** | Primary user | Withdraw cash, check balance, transfer funds |
| **Bank** | System owner | Transaction accuracy, security, audit trails |
| **Maintenance Engineer** | Technical user | Diagnostics, cash replenishment, jam clearance |
| **Network Administrator** | Operations | Uptime, connectivity, encryption keys |
| **Auditor / Regulator** | Compliance | Traceability, non-repudiation, daily reports |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the ATM System — Structured Analytical Steps

A software engineer must dissect the ATM into **four logical tiers** to model it correctly:

### Tier 1 — Hardware / Embedded Layer
- Card reader (magnetic stripe + EMV chip)
- Keypad (EPP — Encrypting Pin Pad)
- Cash dispenser (precision mechanical + sensors)
- Receipt printer, display screen, speakers

### Tier 2 — Application / GUI Layer
- The **ATM client software** running on a hardened OS (e.g., Windows Embedded).
- Handles user interaction, session state, and I/O orchestration.

### Tier 3 — Network / Middleware Layer
- Communication with the bank's **host processor** (switch / router).
- **ISO 8583** messaging standard for financial transactions.
- **Triple DES / AES-256** encryption for PIN blocks.

### Tier 4 — Banking Backend / Database Layer
- Maintains **customer accounts**, **balances**, and **daily transaction logs**.
- Enforces **ACID properties** for transaction integrity.

## 2.2 Functional Requirements (FR) — What the System Does

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Functional Requirements specify the **specific behaviors or functions** the system must perform.

| FR ID | Functional Requirement | Priority |
|-------|------------------------|----------|
| **FR1** | The system shall authenticate a customer using card + 4-digit PIN. | High |
| **FR2** | The system shall allow cash withdrawal in multiples of ₹100, ₹500, ₹2000. | High |
| **FR3** | The system shall display account balance on request. | High |
| **FR4** | The system shall print a transaction receipt. | Medium |
| **FR5** | The system shall support fund transfer between linked accounts. | High |
| **FR6** | The system shall retain the card for **3 incorrect PIN attempts**. | High |
| **FR7** | The system shall support mini-statement (last 5–10 transactions). | Medium |
| **FR8** | The system shall perform daily cash reconciliation. | High |
| **FR9** | The system shall log every transaction with timestamp and ATM ID. | High |
| **FR10** | The system shall support PIN change. | Medium |

## 2.3 Non-Functional Requirements (NFR) — How the System Performs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Non-Functional Requirements specify the **quality attributes** of the system — performance, security, availability, etc. **KTU examiners award dedicated marks for these.**

| NFR ID | Non-Functional Requirement | Metric |
|--------|----------------------------|--------|
| **NFR1** | Transaction response time | **≤ 4 seconds** |
| **NFR2** | Availability / Uptime | **99.9%** (≤ 8.76 hours downtime/year) |
| **NFR3** | Security — PIN encryption | **Triple DES / AES-256** |
| **NFR4** | Concurrency | ≥ **1000 simultaneous sessions** per host |
| **NFR5** | Reliability | MTBF ≥ **10,000 hours** |
| **NFR6** | Auditability | 100% of transactions logged immutably |
| **NFR7** | Usability | Transaction completion ≤ **30 seconds** for trained user |
| **NFR8** | Maintainability | Modular design, Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) ≤ **1 hour** |
| **NFR9** | Compliance | **PCI-DSS, RBI guidelines, ISO 8583** |
| **NFR10** | Localization | Supports English, Hindi, Malayalam, Tamil |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since the ATM is not a numerical-physics system, the "formulas" here are **software engineering metrics** and **quality formulas** that KTU examiners expect.

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Units / Threshold |
|---------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Decimal / Percentage (e.g., 0.999) |
| **Reliability (Series)** | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Failure probability |
| **Reliability (Parallel)** | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$ | Redundancy gain |
| **MTTF** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| **MTBF** | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Hours |
| **Failure Intensity (Basic Musa Model)** | $\lambda(\mu) = \lambda_0 \cdot e^{-\theta \mu}$ | Failures / hour |
| **COCOMO II — Effort** | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^b \cdot EAF$ | Person-months |
| **Function Point (Unadjusted)** | $UFP = 4 \cdot Inputs + 5 \cdot Outputs + \dots$ | Function points |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Number |
| **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defects / KLOC |
| **Test Coverage (Statement)** | $SC = \dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | Percentage |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ATM case study is **not theoretical** — the same engineering principles are used to design:
> - **Self-checkout kiosks** in retail (Walmart, Tesco)
> - **Point-of-Sale (POS)** terminals
> - **Vending machines** with cashless payment
> - **Airline self-service check-in kiosks**
> - **Smart fuel dispensers**
> 
> In every case, the pattern is identical: **secure user authentication → request → remote validation → action → receipt → audit log**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Use Case Modeling for ATM

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A **Use Case** describes a sequence of actions the system performs that yield an observable result of value to a particular actor.

### Master Use Case: **"Customer Withdraws Cash"**

**Actors:** Customer (Primary), Bank Host System (Secondary)

**Preconditions:**
1. The ATM is online and operational.
2. The customer possesses a valid bank card.
3. The customer has a valid 4–6 digit PIN.

**Main Flow (Happy Path):**

| Step | Actor Action | System Response |
|------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Customer inserts card. | System reads card, validates magnetic stripe/chip. |
| 2 | — | System prompts for PIN. |
| 3 | Customer enters PIN. | System encrypts PIN block and sends to host. |
| 4 | — | System awaits host authentication response. |
| 5 | — | Host returns **APPROVED**. |
| 6 | — | System displays main menu (Withdraw, Balance, Transfer, Mini-Statement, PIN Change). |
| 7 | Customer selects **Withdraw**. | System displays account type menu (Savings / Current). |
| 8 | Customer selects account. | System displays amount options (Fast Cash ₹500/₹1000/₹2000/₹5000/₹10000/Other). |
| 9 | Customer selects amount. | System sends debit request to host. |
| 10 | — | Host validates balance, debits account, returns APPROVED with new balance. |
| 11 | — | System dispenses cash from cassette. |
| 12 | — | System prints receipt. |
| 13 | — | System ejects card. |
| 14 | Customer retrieves card and cash. | — |

**Alternative Flows:**

| ID | Alternative Flow | Trigger |
|----|------------------|---------|
| **AF1** | Invalid PIN | PIN does not match → retry counter increments. |
| **AF2** | Card retained | After **3** invalid PINs, system retains card. |
| **AF3** | Insufficient funds | Host returns DECLINED → system displays "Insufficient Balance". |
| **AF4** | Cash dispenser jam | System displays "Transaction cannot be completed, please contact branch". |
| **AF5** | Network timeout (>30 s) | System cancels session, returns card. |
| **AF6** | Customer cancels | At any step, customer presses CANCEL → card returned, session terminated. |
| **AF7** | Daily limit exceeded | Host returns DECLINED-LIMIT. |

**Postconditions:**
- On success: Account debited, cash dispensed, audit log updated.
- On failure: Account state unchanged, error logged.

### Secondary Use Cases

| Use Case ID | Use Case Name | Primary Actor |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| **UC-01** | Authenticate Customer | Customer |
| **UC-02** | Withdraw Cash | Customer |
| **UC-03** | Check Balance | Customer |
| **UC-04** | Mini Statement | Customer |
| **UC-05** | Fund Transfer | Customer |
| **UC-06** | Change PIN | Customer |
| **UC-07** | Refill Cash Cassette | Maintenance Engineer |
| **UC-08** | View Daily Audit Log | Auditor |
| **UC-09** | Download End-of-Day Report | Bank Admin |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step DFD Construction for ATM

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** is a graphical tool that depicts the flow of data through a system, its processes, external entities, and data stores. KTU requires all **4 levels** (Context, Level 0, Level 1, optionally Level 2).

### Level 0 — Context Diagram

The entire ATM system is a **single process** with external entities (Customer, Bank Host).

**Entities:**
- **Customer** — initiates transactions
- **Bank Host System** — validates and processes

**Single Process:** `ATM System (Process 0)`

**Data Flows:**
- `Card + PIN` (Customer → ATM)
- `Transaction Request` (ATM → Bank Host)
- `Authorization Response` (Bank Host → ATM)
- `Cash + Receipt` (ATM → Customer)

### Level 1 — Decomposed DFD

**Processes:**

| Process ID | Process Name | Description |
|------------|--------------|-------------|
| **1.0** | Verify Customer | Reads card, validates PIN via host. |
| **2.0** | Process Transaction | Handles Withdraw / Balance / Transfer / Mini-Statement. |
| **3.0** | Dispense Cash | Activates dispenser mechanism. |
| **4.0** | Print Receipt | Generates transaction receipt. |
| **5.0** | Update Transaction Log | Writes to local audit log. |

**Data Stores:**

| Store ID | Data Store Name |
|----------|-----------------|
| **D1** | Customer Account DB (at Bank Host) |
| **D2** | ATM Transaction Log (local) |
| **D3** | Session State (in-memory) |

### Level 2 — Detailed Sub-Process Example: Process 1.0 (Verify Customer)

| Sub-Process | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| **1.1** | Read Card (magnetic + chip) |
| **1.2** | Prompt for PIN |
| **1.3** | Encrypt PIN using hardware EPP |
| **1.4** | Send authentication request to Bank Host |
| **1.5** | Receive response (APPROVED / DECLINED) |
| **1.6** | Track failure count; retain card after 3 fails |

## 3.3 ER Diagram for ATM Database

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram** models the persistent data of the system. KTU expects entities, attributes, primary keys (underlined), and relationships with cardinalities.

### Core Entities

| Entity | Attributes |
|--------|-----------|
| **CUSTOMER** | **customer_id** (PK), name, address, phone, email, dob |
| **ACCOUNT** | **account_no** (PK), customer_id (FK), account_type, balance, open_date, status |
| **CARD** | **card_no** (PK), account_no (FK), issue_date, expiry_date, pin_hash, status |
| **ATM** | **atm_id** (PK), location, bank_branch_id, cash_limit, status |
| **TRANSACTION** | **txn_id** (PK), account_no (FK), atm_id (FK), txn_type, amount, timestamp, status |
| **CASH_CASSETTE** | **cassette_id** (PK), atm_id (FK), denomination, count, refill_date |
| **BANK_BRANCH** | **branch_id** (PK), name, address, ifsc_code |

### Relationships

| Relationship | Cardinality | Description |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| CUSTOMER **owns** ACCOUNT | 1 : N | One customer can have multiple accounts. |
| ACCOUNT **linked_to** CARD | 1 : N | One account can have multiple cards. |
| ACCOUNT **has** TRANSACTION | 1 : N | Every transaction belongs to one account. |
| ATM **executes** TRANSACTION | 1 : N | One ATM handles many transactions. |
| ATM **contains** CASH_CASSETTE | 1 : N | An ATM has multiple cassettes. |
| BANK_BRANCH **manages** ATM | 1 : N | A branch manages several ATMs. |
| CUSTOMER **initiates** TRANSACTION (via CARD) | M : N | Resolved through CARD. |

## 3.4 Detailed SDLC Mapping for ATM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 2 directly maps the ATM to **SDLC phases**. Examiners ask: *"Apply the Waterfall / Iterative model to the ATM case study."*

| SDLC Phase | Deliverable for ATM |
|------------|---------------------|
| **1. Feasibility Study** | ROI analysis, cost of ATM ≈ ₹3–5 Lakh, lifecycle 7 years, payback period. |
| **2. Requirement Analysis** | SRS document containing all FRs and NFRs (Section 2.2 & 2.3). |
| **3. System Design** | DFDs, ER diagram, Use Case diagram, Class diagram, Sequence diagram, State diagram. |
| **4. Implementation / Coding** | Java / C++ / C# on embedded platform; SQL for backend. |
| **5. Testing** | Unit, Integration, System, UAT, Security (Penetration), Performance (Load/Stress). |
| **6. Deployment** | Installation at branch, network provisioning, key loading. |
| **7. Maintenance** | Cash refill, firmware updates, security patches, daily reconciliation. |

## 3.5 Test Case Design Matrix for ATM

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Test Case** is a set of conditions under which a tester determines whether the system meets requirements.

| Test ID | Test Case | Input | Expected Output | Result |
|---------|-----------|-------|-----------------|--------|
| **TC-01** | Valid Card + Valid PIN | Card: 1234567890, PIN: 1234 | Main menu displayed | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-02** | Invalid PIN (1st attempt) | PIN: 0000 | "Invalid PIN, 2 attempts remaining" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-03** | Invalid PIN (3rd attempt) | PIN: 0000 | Card retained, "Card blocked" message | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-04** | Withdraw ₹2000 (balance ₹5000) | Amount: 2000 | ₹2000 dispensed, balance ₹3000 | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-05** | Withdraw ₹5000 (balance ₹2000) | Amount: 5000 | "Insufficient Funds" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-06** | Withdraw exceeding daily limit | Amount: 75000 | "Daily limit exceeded" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-07** | Network timeout | Server offline > 30 s | "Service unavailable, please try later" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-08** | Cash dispenser jam | Forced jam | "Transaction failed, please contact branch" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-09** | Card jam during insertion | Forced jam | "Card reader error, contact branch" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-10** | Receipt printer out of paper | No paper | "Receipt unavailable" but txn succeeds | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-11** | PIN change | Old PIN: 1234, New PIN: 5678 | "PIN changed successfully" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-12** | Mini-statement request | — | Last 10 transactions listed | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-13** | Fund transfer to non-existent account | Account: 999999 | "Invalid account number" | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-14** | Cancel mid-transaction | Press CANCEL | Card returned, no debit | Pass / Fail |
| **TC-15** | Concurrent users (Load test) | 500 users / 5 min | Response time ≤ 4 s | Pass / Fail |

## 3.6 Cyclomatic Complexity for ATM Withdraw Module (Sample Code Path)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (McCabe):** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ where E = edges, N = nodes, P = connected components (usually 1).

For the **Withdraw** decision tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
V(G) &= (\text{12 edges}) - (\text{10 nodes}) + 2(1) \\
V(G) &= 12 - 10 + 2 \\
V(G) &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A cyclomatic complexity of **4** indicates **low-to-moderate** complexity — ideal for unit testing. KTU accepts values **≤ 10** as maintainable.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any six functional requirements of an ATM system.**

**Model Answer:**

> Functional requirements describe the specific functions the ATM must perform:
> 1. **FR1:** The system shall authenticate the customer using card and 4-digit PIN.
> 2. **FR2:** The system shall allow cash withdrawal in denominations of ₹100, ₹500, and ₹2000.
> 3. **FR3:** The system shall display the current account balance on customer request.
> 4. **FR4:** The system shall support fund transfer between the customer's own and third-party accounts.
> 5. **FR5:** The system shall print a transaction receipt containing date, time, amount, and balance.
> 6. **FR6:** The system shall retain the customer's card after **3 consecutive invalid PIN attempts**.

**Marks Distribution:** 6 points × 0.5 mark = 3 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between functional and non-functional requirements with ATM examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Specify **what** the system does. | Specify **how well** the system does it. |
| **Focus** | Specific behavior or features. | Quality attributes, constraints. |
| **ATM Example** | "ATM shall dispense cash after authentication." | "ATM shall complete transaction in **≤ 4 seconds**." |
| **ATM Example 2** | "ATM shall support mini-statement." | "ATM shall be available **99.9%** of the time." |
| **ATM Example 3** | "ATM shall encrypt PIN using Triple DES." | "PIN block shall never be transmitted in plain text (security constraint)." |
| **Verifiability** | Verified by functional tests. | Verified by performance, load, security tests. |

**Marks Distribution:** Difference explanation = 2 marks, ATM examples = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 2

> **Common Question (ESE Pattern):** *"Apply the Software Engineering principles covered in Module 2 to the ATM Case Study. Draw relevant diagrams and list requirements."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Enumerate **10 functional requirements** and **8 non-functional requirements** for an ATM system with measurable metrics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **Use Case Diagram** for the ATM system covering authentication, withdrawal, balance inquiry, fund transfer, and PIN change. Write a detailed use case description for the *"Customer Withdraws Cash"* use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**Functional Requirements (5 marks for 10 points × 0.5):**

1. The ATM shall authenticate users via card and PIN.
2. The ATM shall dispense cash in multiples of ₹100, ₹500, and ₹2000.
3. The ATM shall display the account balance.
4. The ATM shall allow fund transfer to other accounts.
5. The ATM shall print a transaction receipt.
6. The ATM shall provide a mini-statement of the last 10 transactions.
7. The ATM shall allow PIN change.
8. The ATM shall retain the card after 3 invalid PIN entries.
9. The ATM shall update the local transaction log for every transaction.
10. The ATM shall support cancellation at any stage of the transaction.

**Non-Functional Requirements (2 marks for 4 points × 0.5):**

1. **Performance:** Transaction response time ≤ **4 seconds**.
2. **Availability:** System uptime ≥ **99.9%**.
3. **Security:** PIN encrypted using **Triple DES / AES-256**.
4. **Compliance:** Adheres to **PCI-DSS, RBI, ISO 8583** standards.
5. **Reliability:** MTBF ≥ **10,000 hours**.
6. **Usability:** A new user must complete withdrawal within **30 seconds** after first training.
7. **Maintainability:** MTTR ≤ **1 hour**; modular software architecture.
8. **Concurrency:** Support ≥ **1000 simultaneous** sessions per host.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Use Case Diagram (4 marks):**

The Use Case Diagram should include:
- **Actors:** Customer (primary), Bank Host (secondary), Maintenance Engineer.
- **Use Cases:** UC01-Authenticate Customer, UC02-Withdraw Cash, UC03-Check Balance, UC04-Mini Statement, UC05-Fund Transfer, UC06-Change PIN.
- **Relationships:** `<<include>>` from UC02-06 into UC01-Authenticate; `<<extend>>` for invalid PIN → retain card.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the full diagram.)*

**Use Case Description for "Customer Withdraws Cash" (3 marks):**

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Use Case ID** | UC-02 |
| **Name** | Customer Withdraws Cash |
| **Primary Actor** | Customer |
| **Secondary Actor** | Bank Host System |
| **Precondition** | ATM is online; valid card inserted; valid PIN entered. |
| **Main Flow** | 1. Customer inserts card. 2. ATM reads card. 3. Customer enters PIN. 4. ATM sends auth request to host. 5. Host approves. 6. ATM shows menu. 7. Customer selects Withdraw. 8. Customer selects amount. 9. ATM sends debit request. 10. Host approves. 11. ATM dispenses cash. 12. ATM prints receipt. 13. ATM ejects card. |
| **Postcondition** | Customer's account debited; cash dispensed; transaction logged. |
| **Alternative Flows** | A1: Invalid PIN → retry or retain card. A2: Insufficient funds → display error. A3: Network failure → cancel and return card. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **Context Diagram (Level 0 DFD)** and **Level 1 DFD** for the ATM system. Identify all processes, data stores, entities, and data flows. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an **ER Diagram** for the ATM database system. Identify at least **5 entities**, their attributes with primary keys underlined, and specify relationships with cardinality. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

**Context Diagram (3 marks):**
- **External Entities:** Customer, Bank Host System.
- **Single Process:** ATM System (Process 0).
- **Data Flows:** Card+PIN, Transaction Request, Auth Request, Auth Response, Cash+Receipt, Debit Update.
- *(Refer to Section 4.2 for diagram.)*

**Level 1 DFD (4 marks):**
- **Process 1.0 — Verify Customer:** Reads card, validates PIN, manages session state.
- **Process 2.0 — Process Transaction:** Handles withdraw, balance, transfer, mini-statement.
- **Process 3.0 — Dispense Cash:** Activates dispenser hardware.
- **Process 4.0 — Print Receipt:** Sends data to printer.
- **Process 5.0 — Update Log:** Writes to local audit log.
- **Data Stores:** D1 (Customer Account DB), D2 (ATM Transaction Log), D3 (Session State).
- *(Refer to Section 4.3 for diagram.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**ER Diagram Components (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**

**Entities (1 mark each, 5 entities):**
- **CUSTOMER** (**customer_id**, name, phone, email)
- **ACCOUNT** (**account_no**, account_type, balance, status)
- **CARD** (**card_no**, pin_hash, expiry_date, status)
- **ATM** (**atm_id**, location, cash_limit, status)
- **TRANSACTION** (**txn_id**, txn_type, amount, timestamp, status)

**Relationships with Cardinality (2 marks):**
- CUSTOMER **owns** ACCOUNT → **1 : N**
- ACCOUNT **has** CARD → **1 : N**
- ACCOUNT **records** TRANSACTION → **1 : N**
- ATM **executes** TRANSACTION → **1 : N**
- CARD **authorizes** TRANSACTION → **1 : N**

*(Refer to Section 3.3 for the full attribute list.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Critical Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DFDs with Flowcharts:** DFDs show **data movement**, not control flow. Never use decision diamonds in DFDs.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Non-Functional Requirements:** Examiners **specifically allocate 2–3 marks** for NFRs. Omitting them = guaranteed loss.
> 
> 3. **Unlabelled Diagrams:** A Use Case Diagram without actor names, use case names, or relationship labels scores **0–1 marks maximum**, even if the shape is correct.
> 
> 4. **ER Diagram — Missing Primary Key Marks:** Forgetting to **underline the primary key** of each entity results in deduction of **0.5 mark per entity** (up to 2.5 marks total).
> 
> 5. **Mixing UML Notations:** Do not put rectangles around use cases (that's old UML 1.x); use ovals. Do not use arrows pointing from use case to actor for `<<include>>` (incorrect direction).
> 
> 6. **No Cardinality on Relationships:** Every relationship in an ER diagram must have **1:1, 1:N, or M:N** explicitly written.
> 
> 7. **Missing Alternate Flows:** Use case descriptions without alternate flows (error handling) are considered **incomplete** — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 
> 8. **SDLC Mapping Without Phase-wise Deliverable:** If asked to "apply SDLC to ATM", list the **specific deliverable** for each phase, not generic SDLC definitions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ATM Case Study**
> 
> ✅ **The ATM is a 4-tier system:** Hardware → Application → Network → Backend.
> 
> ✅ **Always distinguish Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements** with at least 5 examples each.
> 
> ✅ **SDLC applied to ATM** must mention **specific deliverables** (SRS, DFDs, ER, code, test plan, deployment).
> 
> ✅ **Use Case Diagram must include:** Actors (stick figures), Use Cases (ovals), System Boundary (rectangle), and `<<include>>` / `<<extend>>` arrows.
> 
> ✅ **DFD Levels to remember:** Context (Level 0) → single process; Level 1 → decomposed processes; Level 2 → further decomposition if needed.
> 
> ✅ **DFD Components:** External Entities (rectangles), Processes (circles/rounded rectangles), Data Stores (open-ended rectangles), Data Flows (labeled arrows).
> 
> ✅ **ER Diagram must have:** Entities, Attributes, Primary Keys (underlined), Relationships, Cardinality.
> 
> ✅ **Common ATM actors:** Customer, Bank Host, Maintenance Engineer, Auditor.
> 
> ✅ **Security constraints:** PIN encrypted with Triple DES / AES; card retained after 3 wrong PINs; session timeout 30 seconds.
> 
> ✅ **Non-Functional metrics to memorize:** Response time **≤ 4 s**, availability **99.9%**, MTBF **≥ 10,000 hours**.
> 
> ✅ **Test cases must cover:** Valid path, invalid PIN, insufficient funds, network failure, hardware jam, cancellation, daily limit.
> 
> ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity formula:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — remember for white-box testing questions.
> 
> ✅ **ATM follows ISO 8583** standard for financial message exchange.
> 
> ✅ **ACID properties** must be maintained for all transactions (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).
> 
> ✅ **The ATM is a classic example of an event-driven, real-time, safety-critical, distributed system.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Pattern - Model View Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Software Pattern - Model View Controller ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern is a foundational **architectural design pattern** used in software engineering to separate an application into three interconnected logical components: the **Model**, the **View**, and the **Controller**. This separation is intended to decouple the internal representation of information (the Model) from the way it is presented to the user (the View) and accepted from the user (the Controller). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (OECST723 Module 2):** > "Model-View-Controller (MVC) is a software architectural pattern that isolates business logic (Model) from user interface (View) and input processing (Controller), enabling modular development, parallel work streams, and ease of maintenance." The pattern was originally conceived by **Trygve Reenskaug** in 1979 at Xerox PARC while working on the Smalltalk programming language. It is formally classified under the **"Gang of Four" (GoF)** category as an **architectural-level pattern** that promotes the principle of **Separation of Concerns (SoC)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant System** > > Imagine a **restaurant**: > - **Model** = The **Kitchen**. It holds the recipes, ingredients, and cooking logic. It doesn't know who is eating or how the food is plated. It just knows *how to prepare data (food)*. > - **View** = The **Dining Table & Plate Presentation**. It is what the customer (user) *sees*. The plate can be changed, decorated, or served differently, but the food itself remains the same. > - **Controller** = The **Waiter**. The waiter takes the order (user input), passes it to the kitchen (Model), and then brings the prepared dish back to the customer (View). > > If the chef changes the recipe, the waiter doesn't need to be retrained. If the plates are redesigned, the kitchen's cooking logic is untouched. **Each role is independent, but they collaborate.** ### 1.3 The Three Core Components | Component | Responsibility | Knows About | |---|---|---| | **Model** | Business logic, data, rules, state | The application's data domain | | **View** | UI rendering, display logic, output formatting | How to present the Model | | **Controller** | Input handling, event flow, coordination | User input and routing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU High-Yield Point:** > The **Model NEVER knows about the View**. The **View NEVER directly updates the Model**. The **Controller is the only mediator** that coordinates between them. This is the essence of MVC. --- ### 1.4 Why MVC Matters — Engineering Motivation In software engineering, the **3 most expensive activities** are maintenance, extension, and debugging. Without separation: - A change in the UI (e.g., new button color) would force recompilation of business logic. - A change in business rules (e.g., new tax calculation) would force UI rewrites. - Multiple developers cannot work in parallel without merge conflicts. MVC solves this by enforcing a **strict modular boundary** through the **Observer Pattern** (Model notifies View) and **Strategy Pattern** (View delegates to Controller). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics (Gang of Four Classification):** > - **Pattern Category:** Architectural / Presentation Layer Pattern > - **Classification Scope:** Object-Scoped, Component-Level > - **Coupling Reduction:** Aims for **Low Coupling** and **High Cohesion** > - **Solves:** Spaghetti Code, Duplicate Business Logic, Tight UI-Business Coupling ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the MVC Pattern MVC operates as a **triangular data flow** with controlled communication channels: **Step 1 — User Interaction Initiation** The user performs an action (e.g., clicks a button, types text). This event is captured by the **View layer** (e.g., a web page, a desktop form). The View is "dumb" — it only knows *something happened*, not *what to do*. **Step 2 — Delegation to Controller** The View forwards the raw user event to the **Controller**. The Controller is the *decision-maker*. It interprets the input (e.g., "User pressed Login button") and decides which business operation to trigger. **Step 3 — Model State Mutation** The Controller invokes the appropriate method on the **Model**. The Model executes business rules, performs validation, queries databases, and updates its internal state. It does **not** notify the View directly — instead, it fires **change notifications** to registered observers. **Step 4 — View Refresh via Notification** The View, which is registered as an **observer** of the Model, receives the change event. It then **pulls** (or receives pushed) the updated data from the Model and re-renders itself. The user sees the updated state. ### 2.2 The Three Components — Deep Dive #### **A. The Model (Data + Business Logic Layer)** The Model is the **heart** of the pattern. It represents: - The **domain entities** (e.g., `Student`, `Order`, `Account`). - The **business rules** (e.g., "discount applies only if cart total > 5000"). - The **data access logic** (database queries, API calls, file I/O). - The **application state**. **Key Principle:** The Model is **framework-agnostic**. It does not import UI libraries. It exposes a public API (methods like `calculateTotal()`, `getUserById()`) and a notification mechanism (e.g., events/listeners). #### **B. The View (Presentation Layer)** The View handles **everything the user sees**: - HTML, CSS, XML, GUI widgets, JSON output, etc. - It is a **passive observer** that renders the Model. - A Model can have **multiple Views** (e.g., one web view, one mobile view, one PDF report view all reading the same Model). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Design Rule:** The View contains **zero business logic**. It only has presentation logic (e.g., "if user.age < 18, hide alcohol section"). Anything involving calculations, validations, or data fetching belongs in the Model. #### **C. The Controller (Input Coordination Layer)** The Controller is the **traffic cop**: - Receives user input from the View. - Translates UI events into Model method calls. - Selects the next View (in web MVC, this means choosing which page to render). - Handles error/success flow. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — MVC Properties | Property | Description | KTU Exam Weightage | |---|---|---| | **Pattern Type** | Architectural / Presentation Tier | High | | **Origin** | Trygve Reenskaug, Xerox PARC, 1979 | Medium | | **Core Principle** | Separation of Concerns (SoC) | Very High | | **Model Responsibilities** | Data + Business Rules + State | Very High | | **View Responsibilities** | UI Rendering + Display Logic Only | Very High | | **Controller Responsibilities** | Input Routing + Mediation | Very High | | **Communication Model→View** | Observer Pattern (Publish/Subscribe) | High | | **Communication View→Controller** | Strategy Pattern (Delegate) | High | | **Communication Controller→Model** | Direct Method Invocation | Medium | | **Number of Models per View** | One View can map to many Models (M:N) | Medium | | **Number of Views per Model** | One Model can have many Views (1:N) | High | | **Testability** | Model is independently unit-testable | Medium | | **Used In** | Java Swing, .NET ASP.NET MVC, Spring MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django (MTV variant) | High | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Communication & Coupling Equations Although MVC is qualitative, software engineering exams often quantify its benefits using these metrics: **Coupling Reduction Metric:** $$C_{\text{before}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{coupling}(M_i, V_j)$$ $$C_{\text{after}} = \text{coupling}(M, V) + \text{coupling}(V, C) + \text{coupling}(C, M)$$ Where coupling is measured as the number of inter-component dependencies. **Cohesion Metric (per component):** $$\text{Cohesion}(X) = \frac{\text{Number of related responsibilities in } X}{\text{Total responsibilities in } X}$$ **Line of Code (LoC) Savings from Reuse (KTU Favorite):** $$\text{LoC}_{\text{saved}} = N_{\text{views}} \times \text{LoC}_{\text{shared\_logic}}$$ Where $N_{\text{views}}$ is the number of views reusing the same Model. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > MVC is the de-facto standard for **web application frameworks** (Spring, ASP.NET, Rails, Django, Express). Modern variants include **MVP (Model-View-Presenter)**, **MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)**, and **HMVC (Hierarchical MVC)**, all derived from Reenskaug's original MVC. ### 2.5 Variations of MVC (KTU Frequently Tested) | Variant | Difference from Pure MVC | Used In | |---|---|---| | **MVC (Classic)** | View polls Model actively | Smalltalk | | **MVC (Web/Modern)** | Controller selects View, Model passed as data | Spring MVC, ASP.NET MVC | | **MVP** | Presenter has reference to View (active) | Windows Forms, GWT | | **MVVM** | ViewModel exposes data streams (reactive) | WPF, Angular, Vue | | **HMVC** | Triad components form a hierarchy | Modular web apps | | **MVA (Model-View-Adapter)** | Adapter decouples Model from View | Mobile apps | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — MVC Operational Walkthrough ### 3.1 Worked Analytical Example: Online Shopping Cart (KTU Board Favorite) Let us trace the **complete MVC lifecycle** for a user action: *"User clicks 'Add to Cart' for a product."* #### **Step 1: Setup — System Initialization** The application boots. A **CartModel** object is created holding products, quantities, and totals. A **CartView** GUI window subscribes to the CartModel as an observer. A **CartController** is wired to the CartView's button-click event listener. **Initial State Representation:** $$S_0 = \{ \text{Model}_{cart} = \emptyset, \text{View}_{active} = \text{true}, \text{Controller}_{ready} = \text{true} \}$$ > This represents the resting state before any user interaction. #### **Step 2: User Action — Click Event** The user clicks the **"Add to Cart"** button. The OS captures the hardware event, the framework (e.g., Swing's Event Dispatch Thread or a browser's JS event loop) dispatches it to the **CartView**. $$\text{Event} = \langle \text{type: click, target: btnAddToCart, payload: productId=42 \rangle}$$ The View's event handler fires. The View **does not** process the business meaning — it simply routes the raw event to its registered **Controller**. > **Logic Explanation:** The View's role here is purely a *messenger*. It does not know what "Add to Cart" means in business terms. It only knows the button was clicked. #### **Step 3: Controller Interpretation** The **CartController.onAddToCart(productId)** method is invoked. The Controller interprets the event: "User wants to add product with ID 42 to their cart." It performs input validation (e.g., is the product in stock? is the user logged in?). **Validation Function:** $$V(\text{productId}) = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } \text{productId} \in \text{Catalog} \land \text{stock} > 0 \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ If validation fails, the Controller triggers an error path (e.g., show error View). If valid, it calls the Model. > **Logic Explanation:** Validation is a *Controller responsibility* because it pertains to *input correctness*, not to *domain rules*. Domain rules (e.g., "max 5 items per cart") belong in the Model. #### **Step 4: Model Business Logic Execution** The Controller calls **CartModel.addItem(productId)**. The Model executes: - Looks up the product in the catalog. - Checks business rule: `if (cart.items.length >= 5) throw "Max cart size"`. - Adds the product to its internal `items` list. - Recalculates the total price. - Marks its state as **dirty** and fires the **change notification** event. **State Transition Equation:** $$S_{\text{Model}, n+1} = f(S_{\text{Model}, n}, \text{productId}) = S_{\text{Model}, n} \cup \{\text{productId}\}$$ **Total Recalculation:** $$T_{n+1} = T_n + P(\text{productId}) \cdot Q(\text{productId})$$ Where $P$ is unit price and $Q$ is quantity. > **Logic Explanation:** Notice that the Model performs *arithmetic* and *rule-checking* (business logic), while the Controller only passed the request. This division is the core of MVC's power. #### **Step 5: Notification Broadcast** The Model emits a **"cartChanged"** event to all registered observers (the CartView and any other subscribed listeners like a mini-cart widget). **Notification Function:** $$N(\text{event}) = \{ \text{View}_1.\text{update}(), \text{View}_2.\text{update}(), \ldots, \text{View}_k.\text{update}() \}$$ This is a **one-to-many broadcast** — the Model has no knowledge of *which* Views exist or *how many* there are. > **Logic Explanation:** The Model is **decoupled** from the View. It just shouts "I changed!" and any interested party listens. This is the **Observer Pattern** in action. #### **Step 6: View Re-render** Each View's **update()** method is called. The View calls `Model.getItems()` and `Model.getTotal()` to **pull** the latest data, then re-renders its visual components (e.g., updates the cart icon badge, refreshes the items list, updates the total label). **View Render Function:** $$R_{\text{View}} = g(\text{Model.items}, \text{Model.total}, \text{Model.metadata})$$ Where $g$ is a presentation function (HTML generation, DOM update, etc.). > **Logic Explanation:** The View's only logic is *display formatting*. It does not know *why* the cart changed — only that it did. #### **Step 7: Cycle Complete — Final State** $$\boxed{S_{\text{final}} = \{ \text{Model.items} = \{\text{product42}\}, \text{View shows updated cart}, \text{Controller idle} \}}$$ The system is now in a new steady state, ready for the next user action. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Responsibility vs. Forbidden Actions | Component | MUST Do | MUST NOT Do | KTU Pitfall | |---|---|---|---| | **Model** | Hold data, execute rules, notify observers | Know about View, render UI, handle input | Calling `System.out.println` (UI leak) | | **View** | Render Model data, forward input to Controller | Modify Model directly, contain business logic | Performing `price * quantity` (business logic) | | **Controller** | Validate input, route to Model, select View | Implement business rules, render UI | Hardcoding domain calculations | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *Controller* with *Business Logic*. Remember: **Controllers handle input flow; Models handle domain rules**. A Controller computing discounts is a code smell called *"Fat Controller" / "Smart UI Anti-Pattern"*. ### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: MVC vs. Other Patterns (KTU Frequently Asked) | Aspect | MVC | MVP | MVVM | Layered Architecture | |---|---|---|---|---| | **View knows Model?** | Yes (passive) | No (via Presenter) | No (via ViewModel) | Sometimes | | **View has reference to Controller?** | Yes | No (Presenter holds View) | No (ViewModel does) | No | | **Data Binding** | Manual | Manual | Automatic (reactive) | N/A | | **Testability** | Medium | High | Very High | Medium | | **Best For** | Web apps, Smalltalk | Desktop, GWT | WPF, Angular, Vue | Enterprise systems | | **Communication Pattern** | Observer + Strategy | Presenter pulls | Two-way binding | Top-down calls | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Classic MVC Interaction Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph userLayer["USER LAYER"] u1((USER)) end subgraph viewLayer["VIEW LAYER (Presentation)"] v1["CartView
HTML Form
JSP Page
React Component"] end subgraph controllerLayer["CONTROLLER LAYER (Input Coordination)"] c1["CartController
onAddToCart
onCheckout
onRemoveItem"] end subgraph modelLayer["MODEL LAYER (Business + Data)"] m1["CartModel
addItem
removeItem
calculateTotal
items list
total field"] end subgraph observerChannel["OBSERVER CHANNEL"] obs1["changeNotification
cartChanged event"] end u1 -->|"clicks button"| v1 v1 -->|"delegates event"| c1 c1 -->|"invokes method"| m1 m1 -->|"fires event"| obs1 obs1 -.->|"notifies update"| v1 v1 -.->|"pulls data"| m1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent **direct method calls**. Dashed arrows represent **Observer notifications** (loose coupling). The Model never directly calls the View. ### 4.2 MVC Request-Response Lifecycle (Web MVC Variant) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant V as View (JSP) participant C as Controller (Servlet) participant M as Model (POJO/Service) participant DB as Database U->>V: GET /addToCart?pid=42 V->>C: dispatch request activate C C->>C: validate input C->>M: cartModel.addItem(42) activate M M->>DB: SELECT product WHERE id=42 DB-->>M: product data M->>M: apply business rules M->>M: update internal state M-->>C: return success deactivate M C->>C: select next View (cart.jsp) C->>V: forward Model to View deactivate C V->>M: model.getItems() V-->>U: render HTML response ``` ### 4.3 Component Dependency Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph level1["Top Level: User Interface"] A1["View Component
Renders Data"] end subgraph level2["Middle Level: Coordination"] B1["Controller Component
Routes Input"] end subgraph level3["Bottom Level: Domain"] C1["Model Component
Holds State
Enforces Rules"] end A1 -->|"depends on"| B1 B1 -->|"depends on"| C1 C1 -.->|"notifies (weak)"| A1 style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: MVC Layered System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph clientSide["CLIENT SIDE (Presentation Tier)"] V1["VIEW LAYER
HTML / CSS / JS
JSP / Thymeleaf
Mobile UI / Desktop GUI
RESPONSIBILITY:
Display data to user
Capture user input"] end subgraph appServer["APPLICATION SERVER (Logic Tier)"] C1["CONTROLLER LAYER
Servlet / Spring Controller
Route Handler
RESPONSIBILITY:
Receive HTTP/UI requests
Validate input format
Dispatch to Model
Choose response View"] M1["MODEL LAYER
POJOs / Entities
Service Classes
Business Rules
RESPONSIBILITY:
Domain logic
Data integrity
Persist to storage
Notify observers of state change"] end subgraph dataSide["DATA STORAGE (Persistence Tier)"] D1["DATABASE / FILE SYSTEM
MySQL / PostgreSQL
MongoDB / Redis
Flat files / S3
RESPONSIBILITY:
Long-term data storage"] end V1 <-->|"HTTP Request / Response"| C1 C1 -->|"method call"| M1 M1 <-->|"JDBC / ORM / API"| D1 M1 -.->|"change event (Observer)"| V1 style V1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Notice the **two-way coupling** between View↔Controller (the user always interacts) and the **one-way weak coupling** from Model→View (only via notification). The Model↔Database coupling is for persistence, not user interaction. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Define the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern. List its three components and state the primary responsibility of each.
