(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern is a software architectural pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components to achieve **Separation of Concerns (SoC)**. **The three components are:** 1. **Model:** Manages the application's **data, business logic, and rules**. It is the domain layer that holds the state and notifies observers when state changes. It is independent of the UI. 2. **View:** Handles the **presentation layer** — what the user sees. It renders data from the Model and forwards user input to the Controller. It contains no business logic. 3. **Controller:** Acts as the **intermediary** between Model and View. It receives user input from the View, validates it, invokes appropriate Model methods, and selects the next View to display. **Key Principle:** The Model never directly knows about the View; communication is via the **Observer Pattern**, ensuring loose coupling and independent testability. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample QP]** **Explain how the Observer Pattern is used to implement communication between the Model and View in MVC. Why is this design choice important? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** In MVC, the **Model** is the **Subject (Publisher)** and the **View(s)** are the **Observers (Subscribers)**. The implementation works as follows: **Working Mechanism:** 1. When a View is created, it **registers itself** with the Model as a listener (e.g., `model.addObserver(view)`). 2. Whenever the Model's state changes (e.g., after `addItem()`), the Model fires a **change notification event** to all registered observers. 3. Each View's `update()` method is invoked, which **pulls the latest data** from the Model and re-renders itself. **Why This Design Is Important:** - **Loose Coupling:** The Model has no compile-time dependency on specific View classes. It only knows "there are listeners." - **Multiple Views:** A single Model can drive many Views (1:N relationship) without modification — e.g., the same cart data shown in a webpage, mobile app, and PDF receipt simultaneously. - **Extensibility:** New Views can be added by simply registering them; no Model changes are needed. - **Testability:** The Model can be unit-tested without instantiating any UI components. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often say *"Model sends data to View"* — this is incorrect. The Model sends a **notification/event**, and the View **pulls the data** when it receives the notification. Be precise in wording. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Describe the **MVC architectural pattern** in detail. Draw a neat diagram showing the **interaction between the three components** and explain the role of each in achieving **Separation of Concerns**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A banking application needs to display an account balance. Using MVC, **design the components** (Model, View, Controller) for this scenario. Explain the **flow of control** when a user logs in and views their balance. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — MVC Architectural Pattern (7 Marks) **Definition (1 Mark):** MVC is a presentation-tier architectural pattern that divides an application into three logical components — **Model**, **View**, and **Controller** — to separate business logic, user interface, and input handling respectively. **The Three Components (3 Marks):** | Component | Role | Key Trait | |---|---|---| | **Model** | Encapsulates application data, business rules, validation, and persistence. Notifies observers on state change. | Framework-agnostic, reusable | | **View** | Renders the Model's data visually. Captures user events and forwards them to the Controller. | Passive, no business logic | | **Controller** | Receives input from View, validates it, invokes Model operations, and selects the appropriate next View. | Mediator role | **Interaction Flow Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4. The flow is: User → View → Controller → Model → [Notify] → View → User)* **Step-by-step flow:** 1. User interacts with the **View**. 2. View forwards the event to the **Controller**. 3. Controller validates input and calls the **Model**. 4. Model updates its state and fires a change notification. 5. **View** (observer) is notified, pulls new data, and re-renders. 6. User sees the updated screen. **Separation of Concerns (1 Mark):** Each component has a single, well-defined responsibility. A change in the UI design does not affect business logic. A change in business rules does not affect UI. This enables **parallel development, easier maintenance, and high cohesion with low coupling.** --- #### Part (b) — Banking Application MVC Design (7 Marks) **Component Design (3 Marks):** **Model — `Account.java`:** ``` class Account { private String accountNumber; private double balance; private List observers; public void deposit(double amount) { this.balance += amount; notifyObservers(); } public double getBalance() { return balance; } } ``` **View — `BalanceView.java`:** Renders balance on screen; registers itself as observer of Account. Methods: `displayBalance()`, `update()`. **Controller — `AccountController.java`:** Handles login button click, calls `accountModel.authenticate(userId, password)`, then calls `balanceView.displayBalance()`. **Flow of Control — User Login & View Balance (4 Marks):** 1. **User opens the app** → The `LoginView` is shown. The `LoginController` is wired to it. 2. **User enters credentials and clicks "Login"** → The View forwards the event to the LoginController. 3. **Controller validates input format** (empty fields, numeric ID) → calls `AccountModel.authenticate(userId, pwd)`. 4. **Model checks business rules** (e.g., account active, password matches) → updates state to "logged in" → fires `accountStateChanged` event. 5. **View receives notification** → LoginController selects the next View: `BalanceView`. 6. **BalanceView calls `accountModel.getBalance()`** → renders the balance on screen. 7. **User sees the balance.** **Key Insight:** The `Account` Model is the **same** whether the user views it on a web page, mobile app, ATM screen, or PDF statement — the Model doesn't change, only the View does. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **MVC, MVP, and MVVM** architectural patterns. Use a comparative table and explain **at least two key differences** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of using the MVC pattern. Explain how MVC supports the **"Single Responsibility Principle" (SRP)** from SOLID principles. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — Comparison of MVC, MVP, MVVM (7 Marks) **Comparative Table (4 Marks):** | Aspect | MVC | MVP | MVVM | |---|---|---|---| | **Full Form** | Model-View-Controller | Model-View-Presenter | Model-View-ViewModel | | **Invented By** | Trygve Reenskaug (1979) | Mike Potel (1996) | Microsoft (2005, WPF) | | **View Knows Model?** | Yes (passive view) | No (via Presenter) | No (via ViewModel) | | **Communication Pattern** | Observer (Model→View) | Direct call (View↔Presenter) | Data Binding (View↔ViewModel) | | **Controller/Presenter Role** | Selects View, routes input | Holds reference to View, calls View directly | Exposes observable data streams | | **Testability** | Medium | High | Very High | | **Best Suited For** | Web apps (Spring, Rails) | Desktop, GWT, legacy | WPF, Angular, Vue, React | | **Coupling** | View-Controller tight, View-Model loose | View-Presenter tight, View-Model none | View-ViewModel two-way binding | **Two Key Differences (3 Marks):** 1. **View-Model Coupling:** In **MVC**, the View directly observes the Model — they are coupled. In **MVVM**, the ViewModel is a *projection* of the Model designed specifically for the View, and the View binds to it via declarative data binding (e.g., Angular's `[(ngModel)]`). This makes MVVM ideal for reactive UIs. 2. **Testing Strategy:** In **MVP**, the Presenter holds a reference to a View **interface**, so unit tests can supply a mock View. In **MVC**, the View is often a concrete class, making mocking harder. **MVVM** tests the ViewModel without any UI. 3. **Example:** In **MVC**, a `UserController` processes a login request and forwards to `UserView.jsp`. In **MVVM (Angular)**, a `UserComponent` binds to a `user$` Observable exposed by `UserViewModel` — the View auto-updates when the stream emits. --- #### Part (b) — Advantages, Disadvantages, and SRP Support (7 Marks) **Advantages (3 Marks):** 1. **Separation of Concerns:** UI designers work on Views, backend developers work on Models, and integration is in Controllers — enabling **parallel development**. 2. **Reusability:** The same Model can power multiple Views (web, mobile, reports). 3. **Testability:** The Model can be unit-tested without any UI. 4. **Loose Coupling:** Changes in one layer do not ripple to others. 5. **Maintainability:** Bugs are localized to one component. 6. **Multiple Developers:** Teams can work independently on different components. **Disadvantages (2 Marks):** 1. **Increased Complexity:** Three files instead of one for simple features; not suitable for small scripts. 2. **Learning Curve:** Developers must understand the separation rules. 3. **Over-engineering Risk:** For a 100-line script, MVC adds unnecessary boilerplate. 4. **Controller Bloat:** Poorly designed systems end up with "Fat Controllers" containing business logic. 5. **Indirection:** Debugging requires tracing through three layers. **MVC and Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) (2 Marks):** The **Single Responsibility Principle** states: *"A class should have only one reason to change."* MVC inherently supports SRP: - **Model** has **one reason to change**: business rules or data schema changes. - **View** has **one reason to change**: UI/UX redesign. - **Controller** has **one reason to change**: input flow or routing logic changes. Without MVC, a single monolithic class might have **three reasons to change** (UI tweak, new business rule, new input format) — violating SRP. By separating these, each class has a **single, well-defined responsibility**, making the system more robust to change. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Controller with Business Logic:** The Controller does *not* compute discounts, taxes, or domain rules. It only routes. Putting business logic in the Controller is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU evaluations. > 2. **Forgetting Observer Pattern:** Students often describe MVC as "Model calls View" — but the Model only *notifies*; it does not *call*. This wording mistake loses **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Not Drawing the Diagram:** In 14-mark questions, a **mandatory diagram** is expected. Omitting it results in a **2-mark deduction**. > 4. **Mixing MVP and MVC:** Confusing the Presenter (MVP) with the Controller (MVC) is a **common error** that loses marks in comparison questions. > 5. **Forgetting Real-world Examples:** Citing Spring MVC, ASP.NET, or Ruby on Rails in answer sheets demonstrates applied knowledge and earns **bonus 0.5–1 mark** per the KTU 2024 scheme. --- ### 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST ✅ **MVC = Model + View + Controller** — architectural pattern for separating concerns. ✅ **Model** = Data + Business Logic + State. Does **not** know View. Notifies via Observer. ✅ **View** = UI rendering + input forwarding. **Zero** business logic. ✅ **Controller** = Input validation + Model invocation + View selection. **No** business rules. ✅ **Inventor:** Trygve Reenskaug, Xerox PARC, **1979**, Smalltalk. ✅ **Two key GoF patterns inside MVC:** Observer (Model→View) and Strategy (View→Controller). ✅ **Benefits:** Parallel dev, testability, reusability, low coupling, high cohesion. ✅ **Drawbacks:** Complexity, learning curve, controller bloat risk. ✅ **Variants:** MVP, MVVM, HMVC, MVA — all derivatives of Reenskaug's MVC. ✅ **Modern usage:** Spring MVC, ASP.NET MVC, Ruby on Rails, Django (MTV), Express. ✅ **Communication direction:** View→Controller→Model (request) and Model→View (notification only). ✅ **SOLID alignment:** MVC supports **SRP** (single reason to change per component). ✅ **Exam keyword triggers:** "separation of concerns," "loose coupling," "observer pattern," "Reenskaug," "1979," "presentation tier."

topics.title: Creational Design Pattern Types - Factory Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Creational Design Pattern Types - Factory Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Factory Method Pattern (GoF Creational Pattern)**
> A creational design pattern that **defines an interface for creating an object, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate**. Factory Method lets a class defer instantiation to subclasses, thereby promoting **loose coupling**, **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** compliance, and **dependency inversion** between client code and concrete product classes.

The **Factory Method** is formally catalogued in the *Gang of Four (GoF)* catalogue under **Creational Patterns** and is also referred to as the *Virtual Constructor* pattern. It is one of the most frequently tested design patterns in KTU university examinations, particularly because it directly addresses the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** and the **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)** from Robert C. Martin's SOLID framework.

The four essential participants defined by the GoF are:

| Participant | Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Product** | Abstract interface | Declares the type of objects the factory method creates |
| **ConcreteProduct** | Concrete class | Implements the Product interface |
| **Creator** | Abstract class | Declares the factory method returning a Product object |
| **ConcreteCreator** | Subclass | Overrides the factory method to return a ConcreteProduct |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Factory Method pattern is listed as a *core* creational pattern under **Module 2: Design Patterns**. Examiners frequently test (a) its UML class diagram, (b) the difference between *Simple Factory*, *Factory Method*, and *Abstract Factory*, and (c) scenarios where the pattern should and should not be applied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **logistics management company** that ships goods internationally. The company doesn't know in advance whether the shipment will go by **road (Truck)**, **sea (Ship)**, or **air (Airplane)** — that decision depends on the type of `Logistics` subsidiary (RoadLogistics, SeaLogistics, AirLogistics). The client code only says: *"Give me a transport, plan the route"*. The subsidiary decides *which* concrete transport to build.

**Mapping to the pattern:**
- The `Transport` interface → **Product**
- `Truck`, `Ship`, `Airplane` → **ConcreteProducts**
- The abstract `Logistics` class with `createTransport()` → **Creator**
- `RoadLogistics`, `SeaLogistics` → **ConcreteCreators**

The client (`main()`) never uses `new Truck()` directly — it asks the factory method. This is the essence of Factory Method: **the construction logic is encapsulated and delegated, not inlined in the client.**

## 1.3 The Simple Factory vs. Factory Method Distinction (KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A very common KTU 2-mark/3-mark question asks: *"Differentiate between Simple Factory and Factory Method."*

The **Simple Factory** is *not* a true GoF pattern — it is a single class with a static or instance method that returns different concrete products based on parameters. It is **not open for extension** without modification. The **Factory Method** uses **inheritance** and **polymorphism** to delegate object creation to subclasses, fully satisfying the Open/Closed Principle.

| Aspect | Simple Factory | Factory Method |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern Category | Idiomatic, not a GoF pattern | Official GoF Creational Pattern |
| Extension Mechanism | Modify the factory class | Add a new subclass (no modification) |
| OCP Compliance | Violated | **Fully Compliant** |
| Creation Logic | Centralized in one class | Distributed among subclasses |
| Use Case | Small, stable product families | Evolving product hierarchies |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The Factory Method pattern operates on **five sequential logical steps**. These are the exact steps a KTU examiner expects when asking *"Explain the Factory Method pattern in detail"*.

1. **Define a Product Interface** — Declare an abstract `Product` interface that lists the operations all concrete products must implement (e.g., `void deliver()`, `double calculateCost()`).
2. **Create Concrete Product Classes** — Implement `ConcreteProductA`, `ConcreteProductB`, etc., each providing its own specialized behaviour.
3. **Declare the Creator Abstract Class** — Define an abstract `Creator` that:
   - Optionally contains core business logic that depends on `Product` objects.
   - Declares the **abstract factory method** `factoryMethod()`, which must return objects of type `Product`.
4. **Implement Concrete Creator Subclasses** — Each `ConcreteCreator` overrides `factoryMethod()` to instantiate and return a specific `ConcreteProduct`.
5. **Client Interaction** — The client holds a reference to a `Creator` (not a `ConcreteCreator`) and invokes the factory method. The actual object type is resolved at **runtime** through **dynamic polymorphism**.

## 2.2 Intent, Motivation, and Applicability

### 2.2.1 Intent
The pattern's intent (in KTU/GoF textbook phrasing) is to:
- Encapsulate object creation logic.
- Decouple client code from concrete classes.
- Provide a hook for subclasses to control the type of objects created.

### 2.2.2 Motivation
In a document management framework, suppose the base `Document` class needs to create pages. The base class cannot anticipate which type of page (ResumePage, ReportPage, LetterPage) will be needed — that decision belongs to the subclass (`Resume`, `Report`, `Letter`). The Factory Method solves this by letting each subclass override `createPage()`.

### 2.2.3 Applicability (When to Use)
Use the Factory Method when:
- A class **cannot anticipate** the type of objects it must create.
- A class wants its **subclasses to specify** the objects it creates.
- You want to **delegate the responsibility** of instantiation to helper subclasses.
- You need a **flexible, extensible** creation mechanism that does not break existing client code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering use:** Factory Method is used heavily in **Java Swing** (e.g., `getContentPane()`), **Spring Framework's** `BeanFactory`, **JDBC's** `Connection.createStatement()`, and **C++ STL allocators**. It is foundational for plugin architectures and dependency injection containers.

## 2.3 Participants and Collaborations

The pattern has four key participants operating under these rules:
- **Product** — the abstract interface or abstract class defining the object's contract.
- **ConcreteProduct** — implements the `Product` interface.
- **Creator** — declares the `factoryMethod()` (returning a `Product`) and may also define a default implementation that calls the factory method.
- **ConcreteCreator** — overrides `factoryMethod()` to instantiate and return a specific `ConcreteProduct`.

**Collaborations:** The `Creator` relies on its subclasses to define the factory method so that it returns the appropriate `ConcreteProduct`. The client code interacts with `Product` and `Creator` abstractions only — never with concrete types.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" / Quick Reference Table

| Concept | Notation / Symbol | KTU Definition | Compliance Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product Interface | $P$ | Abstract type of objects created | Must be polymorphic |
| ConcreteProduct | $P_i$ | Subclass implementing $P$ | Extends $P$ |
| Creator (Abstract) | $C$ | Declares `factoryMethod()` | Returns $P$ |
| ConcreteCreator | $C_j$ | Overrides `factoryMethod()` | Returns $P_j$ |
| Factory Method Signature | $f: C \rightarrow P$ | Maps Creator subclass to Product | Pure virtual in C++ |
| Coupling Metric | $L_c$ | Loose Coupling index | $L_c \to 0$ (ideal) |
| OCP Compliance | $O_{cp}$ | Boolean | $O_{cp} = \text{True}$ |
| LSP Compliance | $L_{sp}$ | Liskov Substitution Principle | $L_{sp} = \text{True}$ |

> **Note for the student:** The metrics $L_c$, $O_{cp}$, $L_{sp}$ are conceptual indicators used in software-engineering analysis. Lower coupling ($L_c$) and higher OCP/LSP compliance are design goals the Factory Method helps achieve.

## 2.5 Consequences (Pros and Cons)

**Positive Consequences:**
- **Eliminates tight coupling** between creator and concrete products.
- **Single Responsibility Principle** — creation code in one place.
- **Open/Closed Principle** — new products can be added without modifying existing code.
- Supports **parallel class hierarchies** (e.g., Document ↔ Page).

**Negative Consequences:**
- May lead to **proliferation of subclasses** (each product needs a creator).
- Slightly increases **code complexity** for simple use cases.
- Can complicate **debugging** due to indirection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Java Implementation (Complete, Exam-Ready)

The following is a **complete, compilable** implementation of the Factory Method pattern using the Logistics analogy. Every line is annotated for KTU evaluation.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Product Interface

```java
// File: Transport.java
// PRODUCT interface — the abstract type
public interface Transport {
    void deliver();           // core business method
    double estimateCost();    // another abstract operation
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Create Concrete Products

```java
// File: Truck.java
public class Truck implements Transport {
    @Override
    public void deliver() {
        System.out.println("Delivering cargo by road in a TRUCK.");
    }
    @Override
    public double estimateCost() {
        return 1500.50;   // per-shipment base cost in INR
    }
}
```

```java
// File: Ship.java
public class Ship implements Transport {
    @Override
    public void deliver() {
        System.out.println("Delivering cargo by sea in a SHIP.");
    }
    @Override
    public double estimateCost() {
        return 8500.75;
    }
}
```

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Declare the Abstract Creator

```java
// File: Logistics.java
public abstract class Logistics {
    // Core business logic that depends on Product abstractions
    public void planDelivery() {
        Transport t = createTransport();   // call to factory method
        t.deliver();
        System.out.println("Estimated cost: INR " + t.estimateCost());
    }
    // ABSTRACT FACTORY METHOD — must be overridden by subclasses
    public abstract Transport createTransport();
}
```

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Implement Concrete Creators

```java
public class RoadLogistics extends Logistics {
    @Override
    public Transport createTransport() {
        return new Truck();      // decision made HERE
    }
}
```

```java
public class SeaLogistics extends Logistics {
    @Override
    public Transport createTransport() {
        return new Ship();
    }
}
```

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Client Driver Code

```java
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Logistics logistics = new RoadLogistics();
        logistics.planDelivery();   // outputs Truck behaviour

        logistics = new SeaLogistics();
        logistics.planDelivery();   // outputs Ship behaviour
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Step Maps to KTU COs

| Step | Implementation Element | KTU Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define `Transport` interface | Abstraction | CO2: Apply design patterns | Apply (L3) |
| Concrete `Truck`, `Ship` | Inheritance & Polymorphism | CO2: Apply design patterns | Apply (L3) |
| Abstract `Logistics` with `createTransport()` | Factory Method declaration | CO3: Analyze pattern suitability | Analyze (L4) |
| `RoadLogistics`, `SeaLogistics` subclasses | Overriding factory method | CO2 / CO3 | Apply / Analyze |
| Client uses `Logistics` reference | Decoupling | CO4: Evaluate design trade-offs | Evaluate (L5) |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Derivation

To mathematically express the *decoupling effect*, define:

$$
\text{Coupling}(C, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{DirectRefs}(C \rightarrow P_i)
$$

Where:
- $C$ is the client class,
- $P_i$ is the $i$-th concrete product,
- $\text{DirectRefs}$ counts compile-time references from $C$ to $P_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Without Factory Method: } & \text{Coupling}(C, P) = n \\
\text{With Factory Method: } & \text{Coupling}(C, P) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The client class is reduced from $n$ direct references (one per concrete product) to a single reference (the `Creator` abstract class). This $O(n) \rightarrow O(1)$ reduction in coupling is precisely the *engineering value* the Factory Method delivers.

## 3.4 Extensibility Demonstration

To prove OCP compliance, consider the **additive change** required when a new product `Airplane` is introduced:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New Files Added: } & \{ \text{Airplane.java}, \; \text{AirLogistics.java} \} \\
\text{Existing Files Modified: } & \varnothing
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Zero modifications to existing code — OCP is fully satisfied. KTU examiners often award full marks only if you state this explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid UML Class Diagram (KTU Board Standard)

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical GoF class diagram for the Factory Method pattern. **All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid safeguard.**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class ProductIface {
        <<interface>>
        +deliver() void
        +estimateCost() double
    }
    class Truck {
        +deliver() void
        +estimateCost() double
    }
    class Ship {
        +deliver() void
        +estimateCost() double
    }
    class Airplane {
        +deliver() void
        +estimateCost() double
    }
    class LogisticsAbs {
        <<abstract>>
        +planDelivery() void
        +createTransport() Transport
    }
    class RoadLogistics {
        +createTransport() Transport
    }
    class SeaLogistics {
        +createTransport() Transport
    }
    class AirLogistics {
        +createTransport() Transport
    }
    ProductIface <|.. Truck : implements
    ProductIface <|.. Ship : implements
    ProductIface <|.. Airplane : implements
    LogisticsAbs <|-- RoadLogistics : extends
    LogisticsAbs <|-- SeaLogistics : extends
    LogisticsAbs <|-- AirLogistics : extends
    LogisticsAbs ..> ProductIface : creates
    RoadLogistics ..> Truck : creates
    SeaLogistics ..> Ship : creates
    AirLogistics ..> Airplane : creates
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram (Runtime Interaction)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant LogisticsAbs
    participant RoadLogistics
    participant Truck
    Client->>LogisticsAbs: planDelivery()
    LogisticsAbs->>RoadLogistics: createTransport()
    RoadLogistics->>Truck: new Truck()
    RoadLogistics-->>LogisticsAbs: return truckRef
    LogisticsAbs->>Truck: deliver()
    Truck-->>LogisticsAbs: delivered
    LogisticsAbs->>Truck: estimateCost()
    Truck-->>LogisticsAbs: 1500.50
    LogisticsAbs-->>Client: plan complete
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Pattern Roles)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Code] --> B[Creator Abstract Class]
    B -->|factoryMethod call| C[Concrete Creator Subclass]
    C -->|instantiation| D[Concrete Product Object]
    D -->|returned as| E[Product Interface Reference]
    E -->|polymorphic call| F[Business Logic Execution]
    subgraph "Factory Method Boundary"
        B
        C
    end
    subgraph "Product Hierarchy"
        D
        E
    end
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#d4edda
    style B fill:#fff3cd
    style C fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation Table

| Diagram | Purpose | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Class Diagram | Static structure | Shows 4 participants and their relations |
| Sequence Diagram | Runtime behaviour | Shows delegation from Creator to ConcreteCreator |
| Flowchart | Role boundaries | Visualizes decoupling between client and product |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q:** Define the Factory Method design pattern. List its four main participants.

**Model Answer:**
The Factory Method is a **creational design pattern** that defines an interface for creating an object, but allows subclasses to decide which class to instantiate. It defers instantiation to subclasses, supporting loose coupling and the Open/Closed Principle.

**Four main participants:**
1. **Product** — the abstract interface or class for objects the factory creates.
2. **ConcreteProduct** — implements the Product interface.
3. **Creator** — abstract class declaring the factory method.
4. **ConcreteCreator** — subclass that overrides the factory method to return a ConcreteProduct.

*(Full marks: clear definition + complete 4-participant list.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Q:** Differentiate between Simple Factory and Factory Method pattern.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Simple Factory | Factory Method |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern Category | Idiomatic (not a GoF pattern) | Official GoF Pattern |
| Object Creation | One class decides which object to create | Subclass decides via overriding |
| OCP Compliance | Violated (modification required to extend) | Fully Compliant |
| Mechanism | Conditional logic / switch-case | Inheritance & Polymorphism |

*(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for the comparison, 0.5 mark for correct conclusion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks because they (a) confuse Factory Method with Abstract Factory, (b) draw UML class diagrams with the wrong arrow direction, or (c) fail to mention that the pattern **defers instantiation to subclasses** — this phrase alone is worth 2 marks. Always include a UML class diagram and a real-world analogy.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the Factory Method design pattern with a real-world example. Draw the corresponding UML class diagram and discuss its participants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that implements the Factory Method pattern for a document management system that supports `Resume`, `Report`, and `Letter` documents, each producing its own page type. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Real-world example:** Consider a furniture manufacturing company (`FurnitureShop`) that sells chairs, sofas, and tables. The shop's `createFurniture()` method is abstract; the `ModernFurnitureShop` creates `ModernChair`, `ModernSofa` while the `VictorianFurnitureShop` creates `VictorianChair`, `VictorianSofa`.

**UML Class Diagram (text representation for paper exam):**

```
            <<interface>>                  <<abstract>>
            Furniture                       FurnitureShop
            +assemble()                     +orderFurniture()
            +describe()                     +createFurniture() {abstract}
                  ^                                  ^
                  |                                  |
          +-------+------+                  +---------+----------+
          |              |                  |                    |
    ModernChair  VictorianChair      ModernFurnitureShop  VictorianFurnitureShop
                                    +createFurniture()    +createFurniture()
                                    returns ModernChair   returns VictorianChair
```

**Participants (1.5 marks each, total ~5 marks for participant list):**
- **Product (Furniture):** The abstract interface defining `assemble()` and `describe()`.
- **ConcreteProduct (ModernChair, VictorianChair):** Implement Furniture with distinct style.
- **Creator (FurnitureShop):** Abstract class with `orderFurniture()` (template method) and abstract `createFurniture()`.
- **ConcreteCreator (ModernFurnitureShop, VictorianFurnitureShop):** Override `createFurniture()` to produce style-specific furniture.

**Why Factory Method fits (1 mark):** Furniture style is decided at runtime by the shop subclass; client code interacts only with `Furniture` interface and `FurnitureShop` abstraction.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

```java
// Step 1: Product interface
interface Page {
    void render();
    String getPageType();
}

// Step 2: Concrete Products
class ResumePage implements Page {
    @Override
    public void render() {
        System.out.println("Rendering RESUME page layout.");
    }
    @Override
    public String getPageType() {
        return "Resume";
    }
}

class ReportPage implements Page {
    @Override
    public void render() {
        System.out.println("Rendering REPORT page layout.");
    }
    @Override
    public String getPageType() {
        return "Report";
    }
}

class LetterPage implements Page {
    @Override
    public void render() {
        System.out.println("Rendering LETTER page layout.");
    }
    @Override
    public String getPageType() {
        return "Letter";
    }
}

// Step 3: Abstract Creator
abstract class Document {
    public void openDocument() {
        Page p = createPage();
        p.render();
        System.out.println("Opened: " + p.getPageType());
    }
    public abstract Page createPage();
}

// Step 4: Concrete Creators
class Resume extends Document {
    @Override
    public Page createPage() {
        return new ResumePage();
    }
}

class Report extends Document {
    @Override
    public Page createPage() {
        return new ReportPage();
    }
}

class Letter extends Document {
    @Override
    public Page createPage() {
        return new LetterPage();
    }
}

// Step 5: Client
public class DocumentApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Document doc = new Resume();
        doc.openDocument();    // Outputs Resume behaviour
        doc = new Report();
        doc.openDocument();    // Outputs Report behaviour
        doc = new Letter();
        doc.openDocument();    // Outputs Letter behaviour
    }
}
```

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Product interface & 3 concrete products: 1.5 marks
- Abstract Document creator: 1 mark
- 3 concrete creators: 1.5 marks
- Client driver & output: 1 mark
- Correctness, naming conventions, comments: 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the applicability of the Factory Method pattern. In which situations is it most useful, and what consequences (pros/cons) does it introduce? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Factory Method** pattern with the **Abstract Factory** pattern. Use a suitable example to illustrate the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Applicability (when to use) — 4 marks:**
1. When a class **cannot anticipate** the class of objects it must create.
2. When a class wants its **subclasses to specify** the objects it creates.
3. When classes delegate responsibility to one of several helper subclasses, and you want to **localize the knowledge of which helper subclass is the delegate**.
4. When the system needs to be **open for extension** (new product types) but **closed for modification** (existing code untouched).

**Consequences — 3 marks:**

| Type | Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Positive** | + Loose coupling between client and concrete products |
| | + Promotes OCP, SRP, DIP compliance |
| | + Simplifies adding new product types |
| **Negative** | − Requires subclassing — may lead to class explosion |
| | − Indirection makes debugging harder |
| | − Overkill for simple object creation |

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Factory Method | Abstract Factory |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Create **one** product; subclass decides | Create **families of related** products |
| **Structure** | One factory method, returns one Product | Multiple factory methods, returns a family |
| **Hierarchy Used** | Inheritance | Composition |
| **Extending** | Subclass the Creator | Add a new Concrete Factory |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Use Case** | Single product dimension | Cross-product consistency (e.g., UI themes) |

**Example Illustration (3.5 marks):**

**Factory Method Example:** `RoadLogistics.createTransport()` returns either `Truck` or `Van` — *one* product dimension (mode of transport).

**Abstract Factory Example:** A `UIFactory` with methods `createButton()`, `createCheckbox()`, `createScrollbar()` returning matching Light-theme or Dark-theme components — *family* of related UI elements.

If the exam asks for *one* code/diagram example, the UI theme example is more illustrative and is the KTU-preferred canonical example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Factory Method Pattern)**

- [x] **Definition**: Defines an interface for object creation, but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.
- [x] **Category**: Creational Design Pattern (GoF).
- [x] **4 Participants**: Product, ConcreteProduct, Creator, ConcreteCreator.
- [x] **Key Phrase**: "Defers instantiation to subclasses" — must appear in any 7-mark answer.
- [x] **SOLID Principles Satisfied**: OCP ✓, SRP ✓, DIP ✓, LSP ✓.
- [x] **UML Class Diagram**: Abstract `Creator` → abstract `factoryMethod()` → returns `Product`. `ConcreteCreator` overrides it.
- [x] **Simple Factory vs Factory Method**: Simple Factory is **not** a GoF pattern; centralizes creation; violates OCP.
- [x] **Factory Method vs Abstract Factory**: Factory = one product; Abstract Factory = family of products.
- [x] **Real-world Examples**: Java `Calendar.getInstance()`, JDBC `Connection.createStatement()`, Spring `BeanFactory`, Java Swing `JFrame.getContentPane()`.
- [x] **When NOT to use**: If the product hierarchy is stable and unlikely to grow, the added indirection is unnecessary overhead.
- [x] **Coupling Reduction**: $O(n) \rightarrow O(1)$ direct references from client to concrete products.
- [x] **OCP Proof**: Adding `Airplane` requires **zero** modifications to existing classes — only new files.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a UML class diagram — KTU examiners award **1–2 bonus marks** for a correctly drawn diagram with proper `<>` and `<<interface>>` stereotypes. Use the phrase *"Object creation is deferred to subclasses"* in your introduction — it is the single most valued sentence in any Factory Method answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Factory Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Abstract Factory Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Abstract Factory Pattern** is a **creational design pattern** (one of the original 23 Gang-of-Four / GoF patterns) that provides an interface for creating **families of related or dependent objects** without specifying their **concrete classes**. It is often called a *"factory of factories"* because it encapsulates a group of individual factories that share a common theme, thereby enforcing **consistency (product family integrity)** among the created products.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this pattern is studied under **Software Design Patterns** in *Module 2 (Design & Architecture)*, where students are expected to articulate intent, structure, participants, and apply the pattern via UML class diagrams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GoF Classification:** Creational Pattern
> **Intent:** "Provide an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes."
> **Alternative Names:** *Kit Pattern*, *Factory of Factories*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Furniture Showroom"

Imagine a luxury furniture showroom that manufactures two distinct *families* of furniture:

| Family | Chair | Sofa | Coffee Table |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modern (ArtDeco)** | ArtDeco Chair | ArtDeco Sofa | ArtDeco Table |
| **Victorian (Classic)** | Victorian Chair | Victorian Sofa | Victorian Table |

A customer never buys a *Modern Chair* with a *Victorian Sofa* — the look would clash. The **showroom** acts as the **Abstract Factory**: it guarantees that when you ask for "Modern furniture", you get a Modern Chair **and** a Modern Sofa **and** a Modern Table — all consistent. The customer (Client) only interacts with the showroom's interface, never with the individual factory machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Abstract Factory does NOT create *one* product — it creates a *coherent family* of products. This is what fundamentally distinguishes it from the **Factory Method** pattern, which creates only a *single* product.

## 1.3 When to Use — Applicability Heuristics

- A system must be **configured with one of multiple families of products**.
- A system needs to enforce **product family consistency** (objects from one family must be used together).
- You want to provide a **class library of products**, exposing only their interfaces, hiding implementations.
- The system is designed to be **independent of how its products are created, composed, and represented**.
]<]minimax[>[</section1>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Participants (GoF Roles)

The Abstract Factory pattern involves **five mandatory participants**. Mastering these is essential for KTU 14-mark derivations.

| # | Participant | Role | KTU Exam Terminology |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AbstractFactory** | Declares the interface for operations that create abstract product objects. | Pure-virtual / abstract creator |
| 2 | **ConcreteFactory** | Implements the operations declared in `AbstractFactory` to produce concrete products. | Subclass / concrete creator |
| 3 | **AbstractProduct** | Declares an interface for a type of product object (e.g., Chair, Sofa). | Pure-virtual product type |
| 4 | **ConcreteProduct** | Defines a product object that is created by the corresponding `ConcreteFactory`. | Real-world implementation |
| 5 | **Client** | Uses only the `AbstractFactory` and `AbstractProduct` interfaces — never `new ConcreteProduct()`. | Decoupled consumer |

## 2.2 Why Abstract Factory Works — The "Why/How" Engine

- **Why decouple client from concrete classes?** → Supports the *Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)* from SOLID. The client depends on **abstractions**, not on **concretions**.
- **Why a "factory of factories"?** → Object creation is **centralized** per family. Swapping the entire family is a one-line change: `client.factory = new ModernFactory();`
- **How is consistency enforced?** → A single `ConcreteFactory` instance manufactures *all* members of one family. You cannot accidentally mix Modern-Chair with Victorian-Sofa.
- **How does it support Open/Closed Principle (OCP)?** → New product families are added by **creating a new `ConcreteFactory`** subclass — *existing code is not modified*.

## 2.3 Collaborations (Object Collaboration Flow)

1. The `Client` is configured with one `ConcreteFactory` (e.g., `ModernFurnitureFactory`).
2. The `Client` calls factory methods such as `createChair()` and `createSofa()`.
3. The `ConcreteFactory` instantiates the appropriate `ConcreteProduct` for its family.
4. The `Client` manipulates the products **only through the `AbstractProduct` interface**, so the family can be switched at runtime by replacing the `ConcreteFactory`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic (Valuation Tip):** If a question says *"create a single product"*, the answer is **Factory Method**. If it says *"create a family of related/dependent products"*, the answer is **Abstract Factory**. Mixing these two is the most common 2-mark deduction in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The "formula" of any GoF pattern is its **intent + participants + consequence triad**. The table below is the rapid-revision artifact you should memorize for the 3-mark Part A questions.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Pattern Category | Creational |
| GoF Book Section | Chapter 3 (Creational Patterns) |
| Type Parameter (UML notation) | `<<abstract>>` on factory & product classes |
| Number of Mandatory Participants | 5 |
| Principle Enforced | DIP, OCP, SRP (per factory) |
| Anti-Pattern to Avoid | Direct `new ConcreteClass()` in Client |
| Typical Java/C++ keyword | `abstract class` / `virtual` methods |
| Known Synonym | **Kit Pattern** |
| Distinguishing Feature from Factory Method | Multiple factory methods (one per product) |
]<]minimax[>[</section2>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison: Abstract Factory vs. Factory Method

The KTU 2024 Scheme often asks students to **derive the differences** between these two creational patterns. Below is an exhaustive comparative derivation table you can reproduce in the exam.

| Comparative Axis | Abstract Factory | Factory Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Creates *families* of related objects. | Creates *one* product. |
| **Number of factory methods** | Multiple (one per product in family). | Exactly one. |
| **Inheritance vs. Composition** | Uses **composition** (Client holds a `Factory` reference). | Uses **inheritance** (subclass overrides the factory method). |
| **Level of abstraction** | Higher — abstraction over a set of factories. | Lower — abstraction over a single constructor. |
| **Product consistency** | Enforced (family invariant). | Not enforced. |
| **Extensibility cost** | Add a *new family* — easy. Add a *new product type* — modify all factories. | Add a *new product type* — easy. Add a *new family* — add subclass. |
| **Real-world mapping** | Furniture showroom, UI Toolkit (Win/Mac/Linux look-and-feel). | Document framework's `createDocument()` overridden to `createPDF()`. |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Designing an Abstract Factory

The following derivation shows **how** to arrive at an Abstract Factory from a software-design problem statement. KTU often presents a scenario (e.g., *"design a cross-platform UI library"*) and awards marks for each logical step.

**Step 1 — Identify the product family.**
> The system must support *multiple product variants* that must be used together (e.g., Windows Button + Windows Checkbox + Windows Scrollbar).

**Step 2 — Declare the AbstractProduct interfaces.**
> One interface per distinct product type in the family.

```
Button  → render(): void
Checkbox → render(): void
```

**Step 3 — Implement ConcreteProduct classes for every product type, per family.**

$$
\text{ConcreteProducts} = \{\, \text{WindowsButton},\ \text{MacButton},\ \text{WindowsCheckbox},\ \text{MacCheckbox} \,\}
$$

**Step 4 — Declare the AbstractFactory interface** with one factory method per `AbstractProduct`.

$$
\text{AbstractFactory} = \{\, \text{createButton}() \rightarrow \text{Button},\ \text{createCheckbox}() \rightarrow \text{Checkbox} \,\}
$$

**Step 5 — Implement one ConcreteFactory subclass per family.**

$$
\text{ConcreteFactories} = \{\, \text{WindowsFactory},\ \text{MacFactory} \,\}
$$

**Step 6 — Configure the Client** with a single `ConcreteFactory` instance.

$$
\text{client} \leftarrow \text{new WindowsFactory()}
$$

**Step 7 — Client invokes factory methods** and operates on products via the `AbstractProduct` interface only.

> **Result:** The Client code is identical across families. Switching families = swap the single factory instance. **OCP satisfied.**

## 3.3 Advantages & Disadvantages (Mandatory for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | Products from a family are guaranteed to work together. | Constrains use — cannot mix products across families. |
| **Decoupling** | Client is isolated from concrete classes. | Increases the number of classes (class-explosion). |
| **Extensibility** | Easy to add a new family (just one new `ConcreteFactory`). | Adding a new *product type* requires modifying all existing `ConcreteFactory` subclasses (violates OCP). |
| **Testability** | Easy to inject mock factories for unit testing. | Initial learning curve is steeper than Factory Method. |

## 3.4 Worked Scenario — Cross-Platform GUI Toolkit

Suppose a software company must build a **Cross-Platform UI Toolkit** supporting Windows, macOS, and Linux. Without Abstract Factory, code would be filled with `if (OS == "Win") ... else if (OS == "Mac") ...` — a maintenance nightmare.

Applying Abstract Factory:

$$
\text{UI Toolkit} = \bigcup_{f \in \text{Families}} \text{ConcreteFactory}_f \times \text{AbstractProductTypes}
$$

$$
= \{\text{WinFactory}, \text{MacFactory}, \text{LinuxFactory}\} \times \{\text{Button}, \text{Checkbox}, \text{Scrollbar}\}
$$

Each `ConcreteFactory` knows how to manufacture the *correct native widget* for its OS. The Client receives a factory at startup; **runtime family switching** becomes a one-line operation.
]<]minimax[>[</section3>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram — Canonical Abstract Factory Structure

The diagram below represents the canonical GoF Abstract Factory structure. Memorize the **diamond shape** — two inheritance hierarchies (factories and products) joined by the `Client` dependency.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        -factory AbstractFactory
        +operation()
    }

    class AbstractFactory {
        <<interface>>
        +createProductA() AbstractProductA
        +createProductB() AbstractProductB
    }

    class ConcreteFactory1 {
        +createProductA() AbstractProductA
        +createProductB() AbstractProductB
    }

    class ConcreteFactory2 {
        +createProductA() AbstractProductA
        +createProductB() AbstractProductB
    }

    class AbstractProductA {
        <<interface>>
        +operationA() void
    }

    class AbstractProductB {
        <<interface>>
        +operationB() void
    }

    class ProductA1 {
        +operationA() void
    }

    class ProductA2 {
        +operationA() void
    }

    class ProductB1 {
        +operationB() void
    }

    class ProductB2 {
        +operationB() void
    }

    Client --> AbstractFactory : uses
    Client --> AbstractProductA : uses
    Client --> AbstractProductB : uses
    AbstractFactory <|.. ConcreteFactory1 : implements
    AbstractFactory <|.. ConcreteFactory2 : implements
    ConcreteFactory1 ..> ProductA1 : creates
    ConcreteFactory1 ..> ProductB1 : creates
    ConcreteFactory2 ..> ProductA2 : creates
    ConcreteFactory2 ..> ProductB2 : creates
    AbstractProductA <|.. ProductA1 : implements
    AbstractProductA <|.. ProductA2 : implements
    AbstractProductB <|.. ProductB1 : implements
    AbstractProductB <|.. ProductB2 : implements
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - Solid triangle (`<|..`) = **Inheritance / Realization**
> - Dashed arrow (`..>`) = **Dependency (creates)**
> - Solid arrow (`-->`) = **Association (uses)**
> - The `Client` only knows about `AbstractFactory` and the two `AbstractProduct` interfaces — never the concrete classes.

## 4.2 Object-Collaboration Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Cli as Client
    participant AF as AbstractFactory (interface)
    participant CF1 as ConcreteFactory1
    participant PA1 as ProductA1
    participant PB1 as ProductB1

    Cli->>CF1: new ConcreteFactory1()
    Cli->>AF: createProductA()
    CF1-->>Cli: returns ProductA1
    Cli->>AF: createProductB()
    CF1-->>Cli: returns ProductB1
    Cli->>PA1: operationA()
    PA1-->>Cli: result
    Cli->>PB1: operationB()
    PB1-->>Cli: result
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Runtime View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Client Application] --> B{Family Selection}
    B -->|Family 1| C1[ConcreteFactory1]
    B -->|Family 2| C2[ConcreteFactory2]
    C1 --> P1A[ProductA1]
    C1 --> P1B[ProductB1]
    C2 --> P2A[ProductA2]
    C2 --> P2B[ProductB2]
    P1A --> Out1[AbstractProductA Interface]
    P1B --> Out2[AbstractProductB Interface]
    P2A --> Out1
    P2B --> Out2
    Out1 --> A
    Out2 --> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (UML Section):** When drawing the Abstract Factory UML in the KTU answer sheet, ensure the **Client → AbstractFactory** arrow is a **plain association** and the **AbstractFactory → ConcreteFactory** arrow is a **hollow-triangle generalization**. Mixing the two arrow types is the most common diagram-mark deduction (–1 to –2 marks).
]<]minimax[>[</section4>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Abstract Factory design pattern. Mention any two situations where it is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Abstract Factory** pattern provides an interface for creating *families of related or dependent objects* without specifying their concrete classes. It is also called a **"factory of factories"** because a single concrete factory manufactures an entire consistent product family.

**Applicability (any two):**

1. The system must be **independent of how its products are created, composed, and represented** — e.g., a cross-platform UI toolkit.
2. The system must be **configured with one of multiple families of products** — e.g., Modern vs. Victorian furniture.
3. A family of related product objects is designed to be used together, and this constraint must be **enforced** for consistency.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List the participants of the Abstract Factory pattern and state the role of any one participant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five participants are:

1. **AbstractFactory** — declares the interface for product-creation operations.
2. **ConcreteFactory** — implements the operations to create concrete product objects.
3. **AbstractProduct** — declares the interface for a type of product.
4. **ConcreteProduct** — defines the product object created by the corresponding factory.
5. **Client** — uses only the `AbstractFactory` and `AbstractProduct` interfaces.

**Role of `Client` (sample):** The `Client` is configured with a single `ConcreteFactory` instance and manipulates products only through their `AbstractProduct` interfaces. It never references a `ConcreteProduct` class, ensuring the system is **decoupled** from product implementations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse Abstract Factory with Factory Method — they are frequently mixed up.
> - Always **draw the UML class diagram** in long answers; missing diagrams cost 2–3 marks.
> - State **consequences / trade-offs** (advantages & limitations), not just the structure.
> - Mention the SOLID principles satisfied — examiners award bonus marks for design-principle awareness.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a suitable example, explain the **Abstract Factory design pattern**. Draw the corresponding UML class diagram and identify its participants.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the **Abstract Factory** pattern with the **Factory Method** pattern. Provide a real-world scenario where Abstract Factory is preferred.

**Model Solution (a):**

The Abstract Factory pattern provides an interface for creating **families of related or dependent objects** without specifying their concrete classes. It is a creational design pattern classified under the Gang-of-Four (GoF) catalogue.

**Example:** A *Cross-Platform GUI Toolkit* that must produce native Button, Checkbox, and Scrollbar widgets for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Each OS represents a *product family*; each widget type represents a *product*.

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class GUIFactory {
        <<interface>>
        +createButton() Button
        +createCheckbox() Checkbox
    }
    class WinFactory {
        +createButton() Button
        +createCheckbox() Checkbox
    }
    class MacFactory {
        +createButton() Button
        +createCheckbox() Checkbox
    }
    class Button {
        <<interface>>
        +paint() void
    }
    class Checkbox {
        <<interface>>
        +paint() void
    }
    class WinButton { +paint() void }
    class MacButton { +paint() void }
    class WinCheckbox { +paint() void }
    class MacCheckbox { +paint() void }

    GUIFactory <|.. WinFactory
    GUIFactory <|.. MacFactory
    Button <|.. WinButton
    Button <|.. MacButton
    Checkbox <|.. WinCheckbox
    Checkbox <|.. MacCheckbox
    WinFactory ..> WinButton
    WinFactory ..> WinCheckbox
    MacFactory ..> MacButton
    MacFactory ..> MacCheckbox
```

**Participants Identified:**

| Participant | Role in this Example |
|---|---|
| AbstractFactory | `GUIFactory` interface |
| ConcreteFactory | `WinFactory`, `MacFactory` |
| AbstractProduct | `Button`, `Checkbox` |
| ConcreteProduct | `WinButton`, `MacButton`, `WinCheckbox`, `MacCheckbox` |
| Client | The application class using `GUIFactory` |

**Model Solution (b):**

| Criterion | Abstract Factory | Factory Method |
|---|---|---|
| Intent | Create *families* of related objects. | Create *one* product. |
| Number of factory methods | Multiple (one per product in family). | Single method. |
| Mechanism | Composition — Client holds a `Factory` reference. | Inheritance — subclass overrides a creator method. |
| Consistency | Enforced within a family. | Not enforced. |
| Adding a new family | Easy: add a new `ConcreteFactory`. | Requires a new creator subclass. |
| Adding a new product type | Hard: modify all factories. | Easy: add a new creator subclass. |

**Scenario where Abstract Factory is preferred:** A company ships a *multi-OS design suite* (Photoshop-like). Using Abstract Factory, the entire rendering pipeline (Toolbar, Canvas, ColorPicker) can be **swapped as a single family** when the user switches OS themes — guaranteeing visual and behavioural consistency. With Factory Method alone, mixing widgets across themes would be possible but tedious to control.

**SOLID Principles Supported:**
- **OCP** — new families extend the system without modifying existing client code.
- **DIP** — Client depends on abstractions, not concretions.
- **SRP** — each factory is solely responsible for its family.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **consequences (advantages and limitations)** of using the Abstract Factory pattern. When would you *avoid* using it?

**(b) [7 marks]** Design an Abstract Factory for a **Vehicle Manufacturing System** that supports Electric and Petrol vehicle families, each producing a Car and a Bike. Sketch the UML class diagram and list the participants.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Advantages:**

1. **Product consistency** — all products in a family are guaranteed compatible because one factory produces them all.
2. **Decoupling** — the Client is isolated from concrete product classes (DIP satisfied).
3. **Easy family swapping** — replacing a single `ConcreteFactory` instance re-configures the entire application.
4. **Extensibility** — adding a new family is easy: just add a new `ConcreteFactory` subclass (OCP satisfied).
5. **Supports testability** — mock factories can be injected for unit testing.

**Limitations:**

1. **Class explosion** — the number of classes grows rapidly (2 × N × M classes for N families and M product types).
2. **Adding a new product type is costly** — every `ConcreteFactory` must be modified, breaking OCP at the product level.
3. **Initial complexity** — steeper learning curve for teams unfamiliar with the pattern.
4. **Indirection overhead** — extra layers of abstraction may slightly impact performance and debuggability.

**When to Avoid:**

- The system supports only **one product family** — direct instantiation is simpler.
- The product family structure changes **frequently** — every change touches every factory.
- The project is small/short-lived and the overhead of multiple abstract classes is unjustified.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Use case:** A vehicle manufacturer produces two families — *Electric* and *Petrol*. Each family must produce a `Car` and a `Bike`. Mixing an Electric Car engine with a Petrol Bike engine is invalid, so family integrity must be enforced.

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class VehicleFactory {
        <<interface>>
        +createCar() Car
        +createBike() Bike
    }
    class ElectricFactory {
        +createCar() Car
        +createBike() Bike
    }
    class PetrolFactory {
        +createCar() Car
        +createBike() Bike
    }
    class Car {
        <<interface>>
        +drive() void
    }
    class Bike {
        <<interface>>
        +ride() void
    }
    class ElectricCar { +drive() void }
    class PetrolCar { +drive() void }
    class ElectricBike { +ride() void }
    class PetrolBike { +ride() void }

    VehicleFactory <|.. ElectricFactory
    VehicleFactory <|.. PetrolFactory
    Car <|.. ElectricCar
    Car <|.. PetrolCar
    Bike <|.. ElectricBike
    Bike <|.. PetrolBike
    ElectricFactory ..> ElectricCar
    ElectricFactory ..> ElectricBike
    PetrolFactory ..> PetrolCar
    PetrolFactory ..> PetrolBike
```

**Participant Identification:**

| Participant | Vehicle-Industry Mapping |
|---|---|
| AbstractFactory | `VehicleFactory` |
| ConcreteFactory | `ElectricFactory`, `PetrolFactory` |
| AbstractProduct | `Car`, `Bike` |
| ConcreteProduct | `ElectricCar`, `PetrolCar`, `ElectricBike`, `PetrolBike` |
| Client | The showroom / dealership system |

**Key Insight:** A `Client` configured with `ElectricFactory` will *always* receive an `ElectricCar` **and** an `ElectricBike` — it is *impossible* (by design) to mix families.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Abstract Factory Method**

- **Pattern Type:** Creational (GoF Chapter 3).
- **Intent (verbatim — memorize):** *"Provide an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes."*
- **Synonym:** *Kit Pattern* / *Factory of Factories*.
- **Number of Participants:** Exactly **5** — `AbstractFactory`, `ConcreteFactory`, `AbstractProduct`, `ConcreteProduct`, `Client`.
- **Multiple factory methods per factory** — distinguishes it from Factory Method (which has one).
- **Composition over inheritance** — Client **holds** a factory reference, not subclass.
- **Enforces product-family consistency** — single `ConcreteFactory` produces the entire family.
- **Satisfies SOLID:** OCP (add families), DIP (depend on abstractions), SRP (one factory per family).
- **Trade-off:** Easy to add a new *family*, but hard to add a new *product type* (must edit all factories).
- **Real-world analogies:** Cross-platform UI toolkits (Win/Mac/Linux), furniture showrooms (Modern/Victorian), vehicle manufacturers (Electric/Petrol/Diesel), database connection kits (MySQL/PostgreSQL/Oracle).
- **UML notation:** `<<interface>>` on factory & product top-of-hierarchy classes; **hollow triangle** for inheritance; **dashed arrow** for creation/dependency.
- **Avoid when:** Only one product family exists, product structure changes frequently, or the project is too small to justify the abstraction overhead.
- **Most common KTU mistakes:** (1) Confusing it with Factory Method; (2) Forgetting to draw the UML class diagram; (3) Using inheritance instead of composition for the Client; (4) Failing to list consequences/advantages.
]<]minimax[>[</section5>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singleton Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singleton Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Singleton Method** (more commonly known as the **Singleton Design Pattern**) is a **creational design pattern** from the Gang of Four (GoF) catalog that ensures a class has **exactly one instance** throughout the lifetime of the application, while providing a **global point of access** to that single instance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GoF Catalog Reference:** Singleton is one of the 23 classic design patterns documented by *Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, and John Vlissides* in their seminal 1994 book *"Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software."* It belongs to the **Creational Patterns** family, which deal with object creation mechanisms.

The formal contract of the Singleton pattern mandates three structural rules:

1. **Private Constructor:** The class must declare its constructor as `private` (or `protected`) to prevent external instantiation using the `new` keyword.
2. **Static Private Instance:** The class must hold a `private static` reference to the single instance of itself.
3. **Public Static Accessor:** The class must expose a `public static` method (conventionally named `getInstance()`) that acts as the sole gateway to retrieve (and lazily create, if needed) the instance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Government of a Country

Imagine a country. No matter how many citizens (objects) exist, there is **only one President / Prime Minister** at any given time representing the executive authority. You cannot "create" a new President by walking into a building and demanding one; the position already exists. Every department in the government that needs executive approval must go through a single, well-defined channel — the President's office.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping the analogy to the pattern:**
>
> | Real-World Analogy | Singleton Component |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | President / Prime Minister | The Singleton Instance |
> | Constitution (prevents multiple presidents) | Private Constructor |
> | "Office of the President" | Static `getInstance()` Method |
> | Citizens / Departments | Client Classes Requesting Access |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

The Singleton pattern is evaluated using the following standard software-engineering metrics:

- **Instance Count Guarantee:** Exactly **1** per ClassLoader / JVM lifetime.
- **Global Access Latency:** $O(1)$ (constant time) — a single static method call.
- **Memory Footprint:** Exactly **1** heap object reference.
- **Lazy Initialization Support:** Configurable (Eager vs. Lazy variants).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Singleton pattern enforces a controlled object-creation lifecycle. Below is the structural logic broken into discrete engineering steps:

- **Step 1 — Constructor Privatization**
  * The class declares its constructor as `private`. This means no external class can write `new MyClass()` and bypass the controlled creation mechanism. The `Why`: it forces all instantiation requests to flow through a single, auditable channel.

- **Step 2 — Static Instance Declaration**
  * A `private static` field of the same class type is declared. This field is the *container* that will eventually hold the single instance. The `Why`: the `static` keyword binds the reference to the class itself rather than any object, ensuring it survives the lifetime of all instances (since there will be only one).

- **Step 3 — Public Accessor Definition**
  * A `public static` method (typically `getInstance()`) is defined. This is the *only* public entry point. Inside this method, the logic that decides **whether** to create the instance and **how** to return it resides.

- **Step 4 — Instance Provisioning (Eager vs. Lazy)**
  * **Eager Initialization:** The instance is created at class-loading time, before any client ever calls `getInstance()`. Pros: thread-safe by default. Cons: wastes memory if the instance is never used.
  * **Lazy Initialization:** The instance is created the *first time* `getInstance()` is called. Pros: memory-efficient. Cons: requires explicit thread-safety mechanisms.

- **Step 5 — Thread-Safety Enforcement (for Lazy Variants)**
  * In multi-threaded environments, two threads could simultaneously observe `instance == null` and create two instances. Solutions include: **Synchronized Method**, **Double-Checked Locking (DCL)**, or the **Bill Pugh Solution** using a static inner helper class.

## 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Singleton Variants Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table** — Frequently tested in KTU ESE. Remember: never use the pipe `|` symbol inside table cells; use $\vert$ instead.

| Variant | Initialization Timing | Thread-Safe by Default | Memory Efficiency | KTU Exam Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Eager Initialization** | Class loading (eager) | **Yes** ($\checkmark$) | Low (always allocated) | High |
| **Lazy Initialization (Naive)** | First call (lazy) | No ($\times$) | High | Medium |
| **Synchronized Method** | First call (lazy) | **Yes** ($\checkmark$) | High | High |
| **Double-Checked Locking** | First call (lazy) | **Yes** ($\checkmark$) with `volatile` | High | High |
| **Bill Pugh (Static Helper)** | First `getInstance()` call | **Yes** ($\checkmark$) | High | Highest |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

The Singleton pattern is the de-facto standard for the following cross-cutting engineering concerns:

- **Logging Frameworks:** `Log4j`, `SLF4J` — a single logger writes to one file/output stream.
- **Database Connection Pools:** `HikariCP`, `Apache DBCP` — one pool manager governs all DB connections.
- **Configuration Managers:** `java.util.Properties`, `ConfigParser` — one canonical source of app settings.
- **Caches:** `EhCache`, `Caffeine` — single in-memory cache.
- **Device Drivers:** Printer spoolers, graphics card drivers (one driver instance per hardware).
- **Runtime Environments:** `java.lang.Runtime.getRuntime()` is a classic Singleton in the JDK itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The JDK class `Runtime` (obtained via `Runtime.getRuntime()`) is a textbook example of an **Eager Initialization Singleton** maintained by the JVM. Mentioning this in ESE answers earns quick impression marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Evolution & Implementation Matrix

This section walks through the **logical evolution** of the Singleton pattern — from a naive, unsafe design to production-grade, thread-safe implementations. Each variant is treated as a "derivation" step, showing *why* the next improvement is needed.

## 3.1 Variant 1 — Naive Lazy Singleton (NOT Thread-Safe)

The simplest possible lazy singleton. The instance is created only when first requested.

```
public class NaiveSingleton {
    // Step A: Static field starts as null
    private static NaiveSingleton instance;

    // Step B: Private constructor prevents 'new NaiveSingleton()'
    private NaiveSingleton() {
        System.out.println("Singleton instance created.");
    }

    // Step C: Public accessor
    public static NaiveSingleton getInstance() {
        // Step D: Lazy creation on first call
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new NaiveSingleton();
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**Logical Analysis (The 'Why' Behind the Flaw):**
- In a single-threaded environment, this works perfectly.
- In a multi-threaded environment, consider two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ both calling `getInstance()` simultaneously. Both check `if (instance == null)`, both find it `true`, and both execute `new NaiveSingleton()`. Two instances are created — **the Singleton invariant is broken.**

$$
\text{Broken Invariant:} \quad \text{instance count} \neq 1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Writing the naive version as the *final* answer in a 14-mark question guarantees heavy mark deduction. Always mention the thread-safety limitation explicitly.

## 3.2 Variant 2 — Synchronized Method Singleton (Thread-Safe but Slow)

The simplest fix to the race condition: add the `synchronized` keyword to `getInstance()`.

```
public class ThreadSafeSingleton {
    private static ThreadSafeSingleton instance;

    private ThreadSafeSingleton() {}

    // Step E: synchronized keyword enforces mutual exclusion
    public static synchronized ThreadSafeSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new ThreadSafeSingleton();
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**Logical Analysis:**
- The JVM acquires a class-level monitor lock on `ThreadSafeSingleton.class`. Only one thread can execute `getInstance()` at a time. **Thread-safety is guaranteed.**
- **Performance Cost:** After the instance is created (which happens exactly once), every subsequent call to `getInstance()` still acquires and releases the lock. This is an unnecessary overhead in the 99.9% case where the instance already exists.

$$
\text{Time per call (post-creation)} = T_{lock-acquire} + T_{method-execution} + T_{lock-release}
$$

In high-throughput systems with millions of `getInstance()` calls per second, this overhead is non-trivial.

## 3.3 Variant 3 — Double-Checked Locking (DCL) Singleton

The optimization: check the field *twice* — once without the lock, once with. Synchronize only when the field is actually null.

```
public class DCLSingleton {
    // Step F: 'volatile' is CRITICAL — prevents instruction reordering
    private static volatile DCLSingleton instance;

    private DCLSingleton() {}

    public static DCLSingleton getInstance() {
        // First check (no lock) — fast path for already-created instances
        if (instance == null) {
            // Acquire lock only when needed
            synchronized (DCLSingleton.class) {
                // Second check (with lock) — prevents double creation
                if (instance == null) {
                    instance = new DCLSingleton();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**Logical Analysis (Why `volatile` is non-negotiable):**

The line `instance = new DCLSingleton()` is **not atomic** at the bytecode level. The JVM performs three sub-steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step } \alpha &: \text{ Allocate memory on the heap} \\
\text{Step } \beta &: \text{ Initialize the object fields (constructor body)} \\
\text{Step } \gamma &: \text{ Assign the reference to the static field}
\end{aligned}
$$

Without `volatile`, the JVM is free to **reorder** $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (i.e., assign the reference *before* initialization completes). A second thread could see a non-null `instance` reference pointing to a *partially constructed* object — leading to subtle, hard-to-debug field-level corruption.

The `volatile` keyword inserts a **memory barrier** that prevents this reordering.

## 3.4 Variant 4 — Bill Pugh Singleton (Recommended Gold Standard)

The most elegant solution. Uses Java's class-loader guarantees instead of explicit synchronization.

```
public class BillPughSingleton {
    // Step G: Private constructor
    private BillPughSingleton() {}

    // Step H: Static inner class — NOT loaded until getInstance() is called
    private static class SingletonHelper {
        // Step I: Instance created exactly when SingletonHelper is class-loaded
        private static final BillPughSingleton INSTANCE = new BillPughSingleton();
    }

    // Step J: Public accessor triggers inner-class loading
    public static BillPughSingleton getInstance() {
        return SingletonHelper.INSTANCE;
    }
}
```

**Logical Analysis (The Three Pillars of Bill Pugh):**

1. **Lazy Loading:** The inner class `SingletonHelper` is loaded by the JVM only when `SingletonHelper.INSTANCE` is first referenced — which happens inside `getInstance()`. Until then, the outer class is loaded but no instance exists.

2. **Thread-Safety via Class-Loader:** The JVM specification guarantees that class initialization is **thread-safe**. When the JVM loads `SingletonHelper`, it implicitly acquires a lock on `SingletonHelper.class` and releases it after `INSTANCE` is fully initialized. No explicit `synchronized` keyword is needed.

3. **No `volatile` Required:** Since there is no explicit double-checked locking, there is no risk of instruction reordering visibility issues.

$$
\boxed{\text{Bill Pugh Singleton: Lazy} \cap \text{Thread-Safe} \cap \text{Lock-Free} \cap \text{No-volatile}}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Criterion | Naive Lazy | Synchronized | DCL | Bill Pugh |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lazy | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Thread-Safe | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Lock-Free Hot Path | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ |
| No `volatile` Needed | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Recommended for Production | $\times$ | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ | $\checkmark$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Singleton Lifecycle Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the decision flow inside the `getInstance()` method for the **Double-Checked Locking** variant — the most commonly drawn diagram in KTU answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Client calls getInstance]) --> check1A{instance == null?}
    check1A -- No --> returnA([Return existing instance])
    check1A -- Yes --> lockA[Acquire class-level monitor lock]
    lockA --> check2A{instance == null? Second check}
    check2A -- No --> releaseA[Release lock]
    releaseA --> returnA
    check2A -- Yes --> createA[new Singleton constructor invoked]
    createA --> assignA[Assign reference to static field]
    assignA --> releaseA
```

## 4.2 Bill Pugh Singleton Class Architecture

The following diagram maps the structural relationship between the outer class, the static inner helper class, and the single instance — demonstrating the lazy class-loading mechanism.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class BillPughSingleton {
        -BillPughSingleton() private constructor
        +getInstance() static BillPughSingleton
    }
    class SingletonHelper {
        -INSTANCE static final BillPughSingleton
    }
    class ClientCode {
        +main method
    }
```
