```mermaid
    BillPughSingleton ..> SingletonHelper : triggers class loading on first getInstance call
    SingletonHelper --> BillPughSingleton : creates the sole INSTANCE
    ClientCode --> BillPughSingleton : calls getInstance to obtain reference
```

## 4.3 Singleton Violation Diagram (Multi-Instance Anti-Pattern)

This schematic visualizes the failure mode of the **Naive Lazy** variant when accessed concurrently by multiple threads, helping students explain the problem in exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    threadT1[Thread T1] --> checkN{instance null?}
    threadT2[Thread T2] --> checkN
    checkN -- Yes for both --> race1[Both threads pass null check]
    race1 --> new1[new Singleton by T1]
    race1 --> new2[new Singleton by T2]
    new1 --> heap1[Heap Object 1]
    new2 --> heap2[Heap Object 2]
    heap1 -.Overwrites.-> staticField[static instance field]
    heap2 -.Overwrites.-> staticField
    staticField --> finalRef[Final reference points to Object 2 only]
    heap1 --> orphan1[Object 1 is orphaned and eligible for GC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Even though two objects are constructed, only one reference survives in the `static` field; the other becomes garbage. From the perspective of *holding the only reference*, the singleton invariant is *accidentally* preserved — but transiently observable states during construction can break visibility guarantees for other static fields. This is why the pattern must be designed correctly from the start, not relied upon to "self-correct."

## 4.4 Use-Case Mapping — Singleton vs. Other Creational Patterns

A high-level decision-support diagram showing when Singleton is the right choice versus alternative creational patterns from the GoF catalog.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startU([Need to create an object]) --> q1U{How many instances are needed?}
    q1U -- Exactly one --> q2U{Global access point required?}
    q1U -- A few --> factory[Use Factory Method Pattern]
    q1U -- Many with shared state --> flyweight[Use Flyweight Pattern]
    q1U -- Clone from a prototype --> proto[Use Prototype Pattern]
    q1U -- Family of related --> absf[Use Abstract Factory Pattern]
    q1U -- One with construction logic --> builder[Use Builder Pattern]
    q2U -- Yes --> singleton[Use Singleton Method]
    q2U -- No but one --> singleTonAlt[Use Static Class Utility instead]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the Singleton design pattern. List any two real-world scenarios where it is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Singleton design pattern is a creational pattern that ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to that instance. **(1 mark)**

The three structural requirements are: **(1 mark)**
1. A `private` constructor to prevent external instantiation.
2. A `private static` reference holding the single instance.
3. A `public static` accessor method (e.g., `getInstance()`).

**Two real-world scenarios:** **(1 mark)**
- **Logging frameworks** — one shared logger writes to a single log file.
- **Database connection pool manager** — one pool governs all DB connections.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between Eager Initialization and Lazy Initialization in the Singleton pattern. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Eager Initialization | Lazy Initialization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Instance creation | At class loading | On first `getInstance()` call |
| Thread-safety | Inherent (JVM-guaranteed) | Requires explicit handling |
| Memory usage | Allocated even if unused | Allocated only when needed |

**Eager advantage:** Thread-safe by default with no extra code. **(1.5 marks)**
**Lazy advantage:** Memory-efficient — instance created only when actually required. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note to students:** KTU ESE follows a strict **Module Internal Choice** — both Question A and Question B below belong to Module 2 and test the Singleton Method. Solve *either* one as instructed on the answer booklet.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Singleton Design

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the Singleton design pattern with its intent and structure. Write the Java code for a thread-safe Singleton class using the **Double-Checked Locking** mechanism. Justify the use of the `volatile` keyword. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **Bill Pugh Singleton** implementation. Explain how it achieves thread-safety and lazy loading without using the `synchronized` keyword. List two advantages of Bill Pugh over DCL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Intent:** Ensure a class has exactly one instance and provide a global point of access. **(1 mark)**

**Structure:** Three components — private constructor, private static field, public static accessor. **(1 mark)**

**Code (DCL version):** **(3 marks)**

```
public class DCLSingleton {
    private static volatile DCLSingleton instance;

    private DCLSingleton() {}

    public static DCLSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            synchronized (DCLSingleton.class) {
                if (instance == null) {
                    instance = new DCLSingleton();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**Justification for `volatile`:** The line `instance = new DCLSingleton()` is not atomic — it involves memory allocation, object initialization, and reference assignment. Without `volatile`, the JVM may reorder these steps, causing a second thread to observe a non-null but *partially constructed* object. The `volatile` keyword inserts a memory barrier preventing this reordering. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Bill Pugh Implementation:** **(3 marks)**

```
public class BillPughSingleton {
    private BillPughSingleton() {}

    private static class SingletonHelper {
        private static final BillPughSingleton INSTANCE = new BillPughSingleton();
    }

    public static BillPughSingleton getInstance() {
        return SingletonHelper.INSTANCE;
    }
}
```

**Mechanism explanation:** **(2 marks)**
The inner class `SingletonHelper` is loaded by the JVM only when `getInstance()` is first called. JVM class loading is itself thread-safe (the JVM uses an internal class-init lock), so the `INSTANCE` field is initialized exactly once with no explicit synchronization. The final keyword on `INSTANCE` provides additional immutability guarantees.

**Two advantages over DCL:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Simpler code:** No `synchronized` block, no `volatile` keyword, fewer lines of code — hence fewer chances of developer error.
2. **Better performance:** No lock acquisition overhead on the hot path (no DCL second-check overhead either).
3. *(Optional third, if asked)* Lazy loading without the visibility hazards inherent in DCL.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Full-Length Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Question Paper]`
>
> **(a)** What is a Singleton design pattern? Explain the **Eager Initialization** method with a suitable example. Mention one drawback. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Synchronized Method** approach for implementing a thread-safe Singleton. Why is it considered inefficient in high-performance applications? Suggest a more efficient alternative and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A creational design pattern that restricts a class to a single instance and provides a global access point.

**Eager Initialization Code (3 marks):**

```
public class EagerSingleton {
    // Instance created at class loading time
    private static final EagerSingleton instance = new EagerSingleton();

    private EagerSingleton() {}

    public static EagerSingleton getInstance() {
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** The `static final` field is initialized by the JVM when the class is first loaded. The `final` modifier guarantees the reference cannot be reassigned. The constructor remains private, blocking external instantiation.

**Drawback (1 mark):** The instance is created even if the application never uses it, leading to wasted memory — especially problematic if the constructor performs expensive I/O or resource allocation.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Synchronized Method Approach (3 marks):**

```
public class SyncSingleton {
    private static SyncSingleton instance;

    private SyncSingleton() {}

    public static synchronized SyncSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new SyncSingleton();
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**Why Inefficient (2 marks):** Once the instance is created (which happens on the first call), every subsequent call to `getInstance()` still acquires and releases the monitor lock. The lock acquisition is a relatively expensive JVM operation. In applications with millions of calls per second, this overhead is significant — the lock provides no benefit after the first call but still incurs its cost.

**More Efficient Alternative (2 marks):** **Double-Checked Locking (DCL)** — the first null check happens *without* acquiring the lock. Only when `instance == null` does the thread enter the `synchronized` block. After the instance is created, all subsequent calls bypass the lock entirely, reducing overhead to a single null comparison per call.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `volatile` in DCL:** Writing DCL code without the `volatile` modifier is a guaranteed **-2 mark** deduction. Examiners specifically test for this keyword.
> 2. **Returning a clone:** Some students write a Singleton that returns `clone()` of the instance — this violates the pattern entirely.
> 3. **Public constructor:** Declaring the constructor as `public` instead of `private` immediately disqualifies the pattern.
> 4. **Confusing Singleton with Static Class:** A Singleton can implement interfaces, be passed as a parameter, and be lazily initialized — a `static` utility class cannot. Examiners often pose a comparison question; know the distinction.
> 5. **Not mentioning `java.lang.Runtime`:** When asked for a real-world JDK example, always cite `Runtime.getRuntime()`. Omitting it loses easy impression marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Category:** Creational Design Pattern (Gang of Four).
- **Core Intent:** Exactly **one instance** + **global access point**.
- **Three Pillars:** Private constructor, private static field, public static accessor.
- **Four Canonical Variants:** Eager, Synchronized, DCL, Bill Pugh — in increasing order of sophistication.
- **`volatile` is mandatory in DCL** — without it, instruction reordering can expose partially constructed objects.
- **Bill Pugh is the recommended modern approach** — leverages JVM class-loader guarantees, no explicit synchronization needed.
- **JDK Example:** `java.lang.Runtime.getRuntime()` is an Eager Singleton.
- **Use Cases:** Logging, DB connection pools, configuration managers, caches, device drivers.
- **Anti-Patterns to Avoid:** Public constructor, multiple `getInstance()` paths, returning `new` objects inside accessor, using `clone()` or `readResolve()` incorrectly.
- **Singleton vs Static Utility Class:** Singleton supports interfaces, inheritance, and lazy init; static class does not.
- **Performance Hierarchy (fastest to slowest in steady state):** Bill Pugh $\approx$ Eager $>$ DCL hot-path $>$ Synchronized.
- **Common KTU keywords to memorize for 14-mark answers:** "creational," "GoF," "thread-safety," "class-loader guarantee," "memory barrier," "instruction reordering," "double-checked locking idiom."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototype Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prototype Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prototype Model (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> The **Prototype Model** is an iterative, incremental, and evolutionary software development life cycle (SDLC) paradigm in which a **working but incomplete model** (a *prototype*) of the final software system is rapidly built, demonstrated to the end-user/stakeholder, refined through continuous feedback, and progressively enhanced until it evolves into the acceptable final product or is discarded.

In KTU terminology, this belongs to the family of **Iterative and Incremental Development Models**. The central tenet of the prototype approach is *user involvement* — uncertainty in the requirements is reduced by letting the user *see, touch, and react* to a tangible artifact rather than abstract specifications.

### 1.2 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Prototype** | A preliminary, partial, or experimental model of the proposed system built to validate requirements and design decisions. |
| **Throwaway Prototyping** | A prototype built quickly, used only to clarify requirements, and then discarded once the actual system is built. |
| **Evolutionary Prototyping** | A prototype that is gradually refined and evolved into the final system. |
| **End-User / Customer** | The stakeholder who evaluates the prototype and provides feedback driving iteration. |
| **Horizontal Prototype** | A broad but shallow mock-up covering all layers/functionalities superficially. |
| **Vertical Prototype** | A deep, narrow working slice of a single functionality. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tailor & the Client**
> Imagine you ask a tailor to stitch a wedding suit. Instead of describing the design in words for hours, the tailor first stitches a *rough sample* in cheap cloth. You try it on, the tailor observes the fit, gathers feedback ("sleeves too long, collar too tight"), and after 2-3 such iterations, the final premium suit is delivered. The cheap sample is the **Prototype**; the iterative fitting-and-feedback loop is the **Prototype Model**.

A software prototype works identically — we build a *quick-and-dirty version* of the system (e.g., UI mockups, partial logic), let users test it, gather deficiencies, and refine until the user signs off.

### 1.4 Why Prototype? — The Underlying Problem It Solves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Many software projects fail because **requirements are ambiguous or misunderstood** at project start. Traditional Waterfall assumes requirements are *frozen* early. When users cannot visualize the system from text-based SRS documents, the Prototype Model breaks this ambiguity by **"showing rather than telling."**

Mathematically, the **defect amplification curve** (from Brooks, *The Mythical Man-Month*) shows that a requirement defect fixed at the requirements stage costs **1×**, while the same defect fixed after deployment can cost **100× to 200×**. Prototyping drastically reduces requirement-stage ambiguity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Phases of the Prototype Model

The Prototype Model is executed through the following structured, iterative sequence:

1. **Requirements Identification & Gathering** — The developer collects the *broadly known* requirements from the customer. Complete and detailed requirements are **not** expected at this stage.
2. **Initial Prototype Construction** — A quick, working model is built using available tools, partial code, UI wireframes, or third-party components. Speed and clarity are prioritized over performance and robustness.
3. **User Evaluation & Feedback** — The prototype is handed to the end-user. The user "plays" with it and supplies concrete comments on what is missing, what is wrong, and what is right.
4. **Refinement & Iteration** — The developer analyzes feedback, refines the prototype (or rebuilds parts of it), and re-presents it to the user. This loop continues.
5. **Production-Ready System Development** — Once requirements are *now* well-understood, the actual system is engineered — either by **evolving** the prototype or by **discarding** it and rebuilding from scratch using stable requirements.

### 2.2 Classification of Prototypes (KTU Board-Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **four types of prototypes**. Memorize the matrix below thoroughly.

| Prototype Type | Goal | Final Fate | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Throwaway (Rapid) Prototype** | Clarify unclear requirements | Discarded after learning | "Learn and throw" |
| **Evolutionary Prototype** | Progressively deliver working software | Evolves into final system | "Grow the system" |
| **Incremental Prototype** | Deliver final system in functional slices | Merged into final product | "Module-by-module" |
| **Extreme Prototype** | Used mainly for **web applications** — static → dynamic → complete | Becomes the system | "3-stage web proto" |

### 2.3 The Extreme Prototyping Lifecycle (Web-Centric)

> [!NOTE]
> Extreme Prototyping is a 3-phase method commonly used in web engineering and is a high-value KTU topic.

- **Phase 1 — Static Prototype:** A pure HTML/CSS mock-up of all screens with hard-coded data. Focus is on layout and navigation.
- **Phase 2 — Dynamic Prototype:** Real interactivity is added. Forms post data, but the data layer is simulated using mock services.
- **Phase 3 — Complete Prototype:** The simulated services are replaced with real databases, authentication, and business logic. The system is now production-ready.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix (Board-Favorite)

| Attribute | Waterfall | Prototype | RAD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Requirement Stability** | Requires stable, frozen requirements | Tolerates unstable/vague requirements | Requires moderate stability |
| **User Involvement** | Only at start and end | Continuous & intensive | Continuous |
| **Iteration Cycles** | None (linear) | Multiple (loop-based) | Multiple (component-based) |
| **Documentation** | Heavy, formal | Light, informal | Moderate |
| **Risk of Scope Creep** | Low | **High** | Moderate |
| **Best Suited Domain** | Well-understood, regulated projects | Web apps, AI/ML UX, novel systems | Modular, time-critical business apps |
| **Development Time** | Long | Moderate | Short (60-90 days) |

### 2.5 Mathematical Estimation Aid (COCOMO-Inspired Heuristic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Prototype-to-Final Cost Ratio** is a useful KTU exam heuristic.

$$
C_{\text{final}} \approx k \cdot C_{\text{prototype}}
$$

Where:

- $C_{\text{final}}$ is the estimated cost of the final system.
- $C_{\text{prototype}}$ is the cost of the throwaway prototype.
- $k$ is a project complexity multiplier: $k \in [3, 10]$.

**Interpretation:** A throwaway prototype typically consumes **10%–30%** of the final system cost but eliminates **40%–60%** of requirement-stage ambiguities — yielding massive downstream savings.

### 2.6 When to Choose the Prototype Model (Engineering Judgment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Prototype Model is *not* universally applicable. Use it when:

- Requirements are **unclear, vague, or unstable**.
- The user interface (UI/UX) is the dominant risk.
- The system has heavy **human-computer interaction** (HCI) elements.
- The development team lacks prior experience in the application domain.
- New, exploratory technologies (AI, AR/VR, IoT) are being used where no template exists.
- The customer demands *visible progress* before committing full budget.

### 2.7 Advantages & Disadvantages (Top Board Question)

**Advantages**
- **Early Detection of Missing / Misunderstood Requirements.**
- **Better User Involvement and Satisfaction** — users "own" the system.
- **Reduced Risk of Failure** through continuous validation.
- **Identifies missing functionality** that textual SRS cannot capture.
- **Useful in training users** before the real system arrives.
- **Improves Communication** between developers and non-technical stakeholders.

**Disadvantages**
- **Insufficient documentation** due to fast iterations.
- **Tendency toward "prototype-as-final-system"** by poor managers, even when unsuitable.
- **User Confusion** — users may mistake the prototype for the final product.
- **Developer Mindset** — quick code may be inefficient, poorly structured, or technically infeasible.
- **Scope Creep** — repeated feedback may expand requirements indefinitely.
- **Not suitable for embedded, safety-critical, or mission-critical systems** (where prototypes may be unsafe).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Flow-Control Logic of the Prototype Loop

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Explain the working of the Prototype Model with a flow diagram and pseudocode."* Below is the **analytical derivation** of the iteration logic.

The Prototype Model is governed by the following decision-theoretic recursion. Let:
- $R$ = Set of identified requirements.
- $P_k$ = Prototype at iteration $k$.
- $F_k$ = Feedback from user after iteration $k$.
- $A(R)$ = Acceptance test on requirements.

$$
P_0 = \emptyset \quad \text{(no prototype initially)}
$$

$$
P_{k+1} = \text{Refine}(P_k, F_k) \quad \text{for } k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n-1
$$

$$
\text{STOP if } A(R) = \text{Accepted} \quad \lor \quad k = n_{\max}
$$

The recursion **terminates** when one of two conditions is met:

1. **Acceptance condition:** User signs off, requirements are frozen.
2. **Bound condition:** Maximum iteration cap $n_{\max}$ is reached (typically 3–5 iterations in KTU exam scenarios).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Estimation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A throwaway prototype for a banking kiosk interface costs ₹ 2,50,000 to build. Given the project complexity multiplier $k = 6.5$ and prototype-cost ratio $r = 0.18$ of the final system, estimate the cost of the **final system** and verify the **cost of requirements ambiguity saved**.

**Step 1: Verify $C_{\text{prototype}}$ as a fraction of $C_{\text{final}}$.**

$$
r = \frac{C_{\text{prototype}}}{C_{\text{final}}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{final}} = \frac{C_{\text{prototype}}}{r} = \frac{250000}{0.18} = 1388888.89 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the multiplier heuristic $C_{\text{final}} \approx k \cdot C_{\text{prototype}}$.**

$$
C_{\text{final}} \approx 6.5 \times 250000 = 1625000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3: Reconcile both estimates via the geometric mean (engineering convention).**

$$
C_{\text{final}}^{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{C_{\text{final}} \cdot k \cdot C_{\text{prototype}}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{final}}^{\text{mean}} = \sqrt{1625000 \times 250000} = \sqrt{4.0625 \times 10^{11}} \approx 637456.50 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 4: Estimate the "Cost of Ambiguity Saved" (CoAS).**

Assume the defect-amplification factor $A = 50$ (post-deployment cost multiplier) and that the prototype detected $p = 0.45$ (45%) of latent requirement defects.

$$
\text{CoAS} = A \cdot p \cdot C_{\text{prototype}} = 50 \times 0.45 \times 250000 = 5625000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Spending ₹ 2,50,000 on a prototype saves ₹ 56,25,000 in potential post-deployment rework — a **22.5× return on investment (ROI)**. This is the *core economic argument* for prototyping and a frequent 14-marker question in KTU.

### 3.3 Decision Matrix — Choosing Between Throwaway vs. Evolutionary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation shows how to **algorithmically decide** which prototype type to use.

Let:
- $\rho$ = Requirement stability index, $\rho \in [0, 1]$.
- $\sigma$ = Technical risk score, $\sigma \in [0, 1]$.
- $\tau$ = Time-to-market pressure, $\tau \in [0, 1]$.

$$
\text{Choose Throwaway} \iff \rho < 0.4 \quad \land \quad \sigma > 0.7
$$

$$
\text{Choose Evolutionary} \iff \rho \geq 0.4 \quad \land \quad \tau > 0.6
$$

$$
\text{Choose Incremental} \iff \rho \geq 0.6 \quad \land \quad \sigma \leq 0.5
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **High technical risk + low requirement clarity** → Throwaway (learn, then build clean).
- **Moderate requirements + urgent delivery** → Evolutionary.
- **Stable requirements + low technical risk** → Incremental (build modules in slices).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart of the Prototype Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Identify Basic Requirements"] --> B["Develop Initial Prototype P0"]
    B --> C["Demonstrate Prototype to User"]
    C --> D["User Reviews and Provides Feedback"]
    D --> E{"User Accepts Refined Requirements?"}
    E -- No --> F["Refine Prototype using Feedback"]
    F --> G["Iteration Count Check: k < nmax?"]
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> H["Escalate to Project Manager / Re-scope"]
    H --> A
    E -- Yes --> I["Build Final Production System"]
    I --> J["Test, Deploy, and Maintain"]
    J --> K["End"]
```

### 4.2 Sub-Architecture: Extreme Prototyping for Web Apps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Phase 1: Static HTML/CSS Mockups"] --> B["Phase 2: Dynamic Forms with Mock Services"]
    B --> C["Phase 3: Real Database and Business Logic"]
    C --> D["Production-Ready Web Application"]
    A --> A1["Login Page UI"]
    A --> A2["Dashboard Layout"]
    A --> A3["Report Skeleton"]
    B --> B1["Form Validation"]
    B --> B2["Session Simulation"]
    B --> B3["JSON Mock APIs"]
    C --> C1["SQL/NoSQL DB"]
    C --> C2["Authentication Service"]
    C --> C3["REST API Layer"]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Waterfall vs. Prototype

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WF["Waterfall Model - Linear"]
        W1["Requirement"] --> W2["Design"] --> W3["Implementation"] --> W4["Testing"] --> W5["Maintenance"]
    end
    subgraph PM["Prototype Model - Cyclic"]
        P1["Basic Requirement"] --> P2["Quick Prototype"] --> P3["User Feedback"]
        P3 -- Refine --> P2
        P3 -- Approved --> P4["Full System Build"] --> P5["Deployment"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Feedback-Driven Iteration Loop (Analytical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Requirement R0"] --> P1["Prototype Build P1"]
    P1 --> U1["User Evaluation U1"]
    U1 --> F1["Feedback Set F1"]
    F1 -- Delta greater than 0 --> P2["Prototype Build P2"]
    P2 --> U2["User Evaluation U2"]
    U2 --> F2["Feedback Set F2"]
    F2 -- Delta equals 0 --> S["Sign-off and Final Build"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note for KTU Board Answers:** Always label each iteration explicitly ($P_1, P_2, P_3 \dots$), as the examiner specifically looks for evidence of *iteration counting* in your model diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define the Prototype Model. List any two situations where it is most suitable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Prototype Model is an iterative SDLC approach in which a preliminary working model of the software is built, demonstrated to the user, refined through feedback, and progressively enhanced until it is accepted.

**Two suitable situations (1.5 marks each):**
1. When **user requirements are unclear or unstable**, and the customer cannot visualize the system from textual specifications.
2. When developing **user-interface-intensive applications** such as e-commerce portals, kiosks, or mobile apps, where interaction design is the dominant risk.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Throwaway Prototyping and Evolutionary Prototyping.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Throwaway Prototype | Evolutionary Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To clarify requirements only | To gradually become the final system |
| **Final Fate** | Discarded after learning | Continues to evolve |
| **Code Quality** | Quick and dirty; ignored later | Production-quality from start |
| **When Preferred** | High technical risk, unclear requirements | Moderate-to-clear requirements, time pressure |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
> **Explain the Prototype Model in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and the situations in which it is most appropriate. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
The Prototype Model is an iterative SDLC model where a working model (prototype) of the system is built early, evaluated by the user, refined through feedback cycles, and finally evolved into the production system.

**2. Block Diagram & Working (3 marks):**

The process follows the loop:
*Identify Basic Requirements → Develop Initial Prototype → User Review → Feedback → Refine Prototype → (loop) → Final System Build → Deployment.*

Each iteration tightens the requirement set, and the loop terminates when the user accepts the refined system or the maximum iteration count is reached.

**3. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Early detection of missing requirements.
- Continuous user involvement → higher satisfaction.
- Reduced development risk through early validation.

**4. Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Inadequate documentation.
- Risk of scope creep.
- Users may mistake the prototype for the final product.

**5. Suitable Situations (0.5 marks):**
- Unclear/vague requirements, UI-heavy systems, novel/exploratory technologies.

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
> **Compare the Prototype Model with the Waterfall Model across six parameters. Which one is preferable for a startup building a brand-new AI-based fitness mobile app? Justify. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Six-Parameter Comparison Table (4.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Waterfall | Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Requirement Stability** | Requires frozen, well-defined requirements | Tolerates evolving/vague requirements |
| **User Involvement** | Only at start and end | Continuous, iterative |
| **Risk Handling** | Late-stage risk detection | Early risk detection |
| **Documentation** | Heavy and formal | Light and informal |
| **Iteration** | None (strictly linear) | Multiple, feedback-driven |
| **Time-to-Market** | Long | Moderate (shorter via early MVP) |

**Startup AI Fitness App – Recommendation (2.5 marks):**
The **Prototype Model** is preferable. Justification:
1. **Requirements evolve rapidly** in AI-driven apps as user data reveals behavioural patterns — Waterfall's frozen SRS will fail.
2. **UI/UX is critical** — users must see and "feel" the fitness interface (dashboards, recommendations) before sign-off.
3. **Time-to-market pressure** is extreme; an MVP (Minimum Viable Product) prototype lets the startup acquire early users.
4. **ML model accuracy** needs continuous user feedback to improve — the prototype loop enables this naturally.
5. The startup's small team benefits from rapid iterations rather than 200-page SRS documents.

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> **Explain the different types of prototypes with examples. Describe Extreme Prototyping in detail with a diagram. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Types of Prototypes (4 marks):**
- **Throwaway (Rapid):** Built to clarify vague requirements; discarded once requirements are clear. *Example: A sketch-based UI mock-up for a new banking app.*
- **Evolutionary:** Continually refined until it becomes the final system. *Example: Iterative releases of an operating system (e.g., Ubuntu LTS versions).*
- **Incremental:** Final system delivered in functional slices. *Example: Payroll module first, then HR module in an enterprise ERP.*
- **Extreme:** Web-specific 3-phase method (Static → Dynamic → Complete). *Example: A new e-commerce website.*

**2. Extreme Prototyping Detailed (3 marks):**
- **Phase 1 — Static Prototype:** HTML/CSS-only screens of all pages with hard-coded content. Purpose: finalize navigation, layout, branding.
- **Phase 2 — Dynamic Prototype:** Forms, validations, and interactive elements are added. The data layer uses **mock services** (e.g., JSON placeholders) to simulate real responses.
- **Phase 3 — Complete Prototype:** The mock services are replaced with real databases, REST APIs, authentication, and business logic. The system is now production-ready.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for the schematic.)*

> **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> **A software company builds a throwaway prototype for a hospital management system at a cost of ₹ 4,00,000. If the prototype captures 50% of requirement defects at a stage where the post-deployment defect-fix cost is 60× the prototype cost, calculate the cost of ambiguity saved and comment on the ROI. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $C_{\text{prototype}} = 4,00,000$ INR
- Defect detection rate $p = 0.50$
- Defect amplification factor $A = 60$

**Step 1: Apply the CoAS Formula (3 marks):**

$$
\text{CoAS} = A \cdot p \cdot C_{\text{prototype}}
$$

$$
\text{CoAS} = 60 \times 0.50 \times 4,00,000 = 1,20,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2: Compute ROI (3 marks):**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{CoAS} - C_{\text{prototype}}}{C_{\text{prototype}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{1,20,00,000 - 4,00,000}{4,00,000} \times 100\% = 2900\%
$$

**Step 3: Engineering Comment (1 mark):**
The hospital management system gains a **2900% ROI** by investing just ₹ 4,00,000 in prototyping. The investment prevents ₹ 1.2 Crore in potential post-deployment rework, validating the prototype method for safety-critical, complex domains like healthcare.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Prototype Model Answers:**
> 1. **Conflating "Prototype" with "Beta version."** A prototype is *not* a beta; it is a learning artifact, not a release candidate.
> 2. **Forgetting the iteration count.** Always mention the **loop count cap (3-5 iterations)** explicitly. Examiners deduct marks if you imply infinite loops.
> 3. **Missing the "Discard vs. Evolve" decision.** When asked about types, you **must** explicitly state whether the prototype is *discarded* or *evolved* into the final system.
> 4. **No diagram = guaranteed 2-mark loss.** A flow diagram is **mandatory** in any 7-mark or 14-mark question on SDLC models.
> 5. **Ignoring safety-critical contexts.** Students often write "Prototype is best" universally. Wrong — it is unsuitable for **embedded, medical-device, or avionics** systems.
> 6. **Conflating RAD with Prototype.** RAD has **60–90 day strict time-boxes** with strong user involvement; Prototype is more flexible. Distinguish them clearly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Prototype Method:**

- **Definition:** Iterative SDLC where a working but partial model is built, refined, and finalized through user feedback.
- **Core Principle:** *"Show, don't tell."* Visualize requirements before coding the final system.
- **Four Types:** Throwaway (discarded), Evolutionary (becomes the system), Incremental (slice-based), Extreme (web-specific 3-phase).
- **Three Phases of Extreme Prototyping:** Static HTML → Dynamic Mock Services → Real Production System.
- **Suitable For:** Unclear requirements, UI-heavy apps, novel tech (AI/AR/VR/IoT), startups needing MVPs.
- **Unsuitable For:** Safety-critical, embedded, mission-critical, or heavily regulated systems.
- **Key Advantages:** Early defect detection, user ownership, reduced risk, better communication.
- **Key Disadvantages:** Poor documentation, scope creep, prototype mistaken for final product.
- **Iteration Cap:** Typically **3 to 5** feedback cycles.
- **Cost Heuristic:** $C_{\text{final}} \approx k \cdot C_{\text{prototype}}$ with $k \in [3, 10]$.
- **Defect Amplification:** Requirement defects caught late cost **50–200×** more than at the design stage.
- **CoAS Formula:** $\text{CoAS} = A \cdot p \cdot C_{\text{prototype}}$ — a high-yield KTU numerical.
- **Comparison Anchor:** Waterfall is *linear*; Prototype is *cyclic*; RAD is *component-based and time-boxed*.
- **Diagram Must-Have:** Flow diagram with explicit **iteration loop**, **user feedback node**, and **decision diamond** for accept/reject.
- **Frequent 3-Mark Questions:** Definitions, types comparison, suitable situations, RAD vs. Prototype.
- **Frequent 14-Mark Questions:** Detailed working + diagram + advantages/disadvantages + situational justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Builder Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Builder Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Builder Pattern (GoF Creational Pattern):**
> The Builder Method is a *creational design pattern* that **separates the construction of a complex object from its representation**, allowing the same construction process to create different representations. It constructs an object **step-by-step** under the control of a *Director* client, encapsulating the logic of how the object's parts are assembled.

The Builder Pattern is one of the classic **Gang of Four (GoF)** design patterns, formally catalogued by *Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, and John Vlissides* in their seminal 1994 work on object-oriented reusable software. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, it is studied under **Module 2 – Design Patterns**, specifically within the *Creational Patterns* family.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this pattern is taught in KTU:**
> KTU examiners frequently test whether a student understands the *intent* of a pattern (its problem statement) versus its *implementation* (its class structure). For the Builder, the intent is to handle **telescoping constructor anti-patterns** and **complex object assembly**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a Custom Computer (PC)**
> Imagine walking into a computer store to buy a custom-built PC. You do not receive a finished computer in one shot. Instead:
> 1. You tell the **Sales Executive (Director)** what you want — a "Gaming PC" or "Office PC."
> 2. The Executive hands your requirements to a **Technician (Builder)**, who assembles parts step-by-step — motherboard → CPU → RAM → GPU → Storage → Power Supply → Cooling.
> 3. Each *step* is a small, manageable operation, but the **final assembled object** is a complete, complex computer.
> 4. The customer never needs to know *how* the parts are wired; they only specify the *recipe* (configuration).
> 
> The **Builder Pattern** works exactly like this. The **Director** invokes builder methods in a specific order, and the **Builder** knows how to assemble each piece, returning a fully-constructed object at the end.

**Key takeaway:** The Builder pattern is *not* about creating objects in a single call (like Factory), but about **orchestrating a multi-step construction process** where the final product can vary based on the *sequence and type* of building steps performed.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Product**: The complex object that is being constructed (e.g., the `Computer`).
- **Builder (Abstract Interface)**: Declares construction steps (e.g., `buildCPU()`, `buildRAM()`).
- **ConcreteBuilder**: Implements the abstract builder, assembles parts, and tracks the in-progress product.
- **Director**: Invokes builder methods in a specific order to produce a particular configuration.
- **Client**: The end-user that creates a Director, configures it with a ConcreteBuilder, and requests the build.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguishing Boundary:** Unlike the **Abstract Factory** (which returns a *family of related products* in one shot), the Builder pattern is focused on **assembling a single complex product** through a sequence of steps. This is the most common differentiator asked in KTU viva and 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Builder pattern operates through a tightly defined collaboration between four primary actors. Below is the structural logic that the KTU examiner expects you to write in a 7-mark or 14-mark answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Philosophy:** *Decompose the construction of a complex object into discrete, ordered steps, and delegate the assembly responsibility to a dedicated Builder object rather than embedding it in the Client.*

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

- **Step 1 — Client Instantiates a ConcreteBuilder**
  The Client creates a specific builder object (e.g., `GamingPCBuilder`, `OfficePCBuilder`). This builder will hold the **in-progress product** (often called `product` or `result` in UML).

- **Step 2 — Client Creates a Director and Configures It**
  The Client creates a `Director` instance and **injects** the ConcreteBuilder into it. The Director is essentially a controller that knows the *recipe* — the correct order in which build steps must execute.

- **Step 3 — Director Drives the Construction Process**
  The Client invokes a single method on the Director (commonly named `construct()` or `build()`). The Director then iterates through its pre-defined recipe:
  - `builder.buildMotherboard()`
  - `builder.buildCPU()`
  - `builder.buildRAM()`
  - `builder.buildStorage()`
  - `builder.buildGPU()` (optional, only for Gaming variant)
  - `builder.buildCooling()`

- **Step 4 — Builder Accumulates State**
  Each `build*()` method modifies the internal product reference held by the builder. The product is **not yet returned** to the client at this stage.

- **Step 5 — Director Returns the Finished Product**
  After all steps complete, the Director calls `builder.getResult()` (or `getProduct()`). The fully-assembled product is handed back to the Client, fully constructed and ready to use.

- **Step 6 — Why This Works (The "Why" Behind the "How")**
  The Director can produce **different representations** of the same product (e.g., gaming vs. office PC) by simply swapping the ConcreteBuilder instance. No conditional logic, no telescoping constructors, no broken encapsulation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Comparison Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Builder Pattern** | **Abstract Factory** | **Factory Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Intent** | Construct complex object step-by-step | Produce *family* of related objects | Produce *single* object via subclass |
| **Construction Style** | Multi-step, sequential assembly | One-shot, parallel creation | One-shot, single object |
| **Number of Methods Called** | Multiple (`build*()` calls) | Multiple (one per family member) | One (`create()` or `factoryMethod()`) |
| **Product Complexity** | High (composite, many parts) | Medium (related family) | Low (single product) |
| **Director Role** | Yes, orchestrates steps | No, Client calls directly | No, subclass decides |
| **Final Product Returned Via** | `getResult()` / `getProduct()` | Direct method return | Direct method return |
| **Telescoping Constructor Solved?** | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | **High** (14-mark favorite) | Medium | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** When asked to *differentiate* design patterns in a 3-mark or 7-mark question, examiners award marks specifically for stating the **intent** of each pattern in one clean sentence. Memorize the intent column above.

## 2.3 Mathematical Representation of Construction

While the Builder pattern is not a numerical formula, the KTU 2024 scheme rewards a formal, notation-driven treatment. We can model the construction process as a **compositional function**:

$$ P = \text{build}_n \circ \text{build}_{n-1} \circ \dots \circ \text{build}_2 \circ \text{build}_1 (\emptyset) $$

Where:

- $P$ = final constructed Product
- $\text{build}_i$ = the $i$-th construction step method
- $\emptyset$ = the initial empty state of the product
- $\circ$ = functional composition operator

This shows that the **final product is the cumulative result of applying $n$ ordered transformations** to an empty starting state — which is precisely the *Why* the Builder pattern exists: to make $P$ the deterministic result of a *reproducible* sequence of steps.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Builder Pattern is Used in Industry:**
> - **Java's `StringBuilder` and `StringBuffer`**: Append operations chain to build a string.
> - **Java's `Stream.Builder`**: Adds elements one at a time, builds the stream at the end.
> - **Apache `HttpClientBuilder`**: Constructs HTTP clients with custom timeouts, SSL, proxies.
> - **Lombok's `@Builder` annotation**: Auto-generates builder code in Java projects.
> - **SQL Query Builders** (e.g., JOOQ, jOOQ): `select().from().where().orderBy().build()`.
> - **Android `AlertDialog.Builder`**: Builds complex dialogs step-by-step.
> - **`.NET`'s `StringBuilder` and `UriBuilder`**: Constructs complex URIs incrementally.

The pattern's **engineering utility** lies in making object construction *declarative*, *readable*, and *immutable-friendly* — all critical for enterprise-grade codebases.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: UML Class Structure of the Builder Pattern

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to draw the **complete UML class diagram** for design patterns. Below is the formal derivation of the class structure, step by step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal UML Notation Legend:**
> - `<<interface>>` or `«interface»` = Abstract type
> - `+` = public visibility
> - `-` = private visibility
> - Italicized names = abstract methods
> - Hollow triangle arrow = inheritance / realization

### Step 1: Identify the Four Canonical Participants

The GoF definition lists exactly four participants. Let's formalize each as a class.

**Participant 1 — `Builder` (Abstract Interface)**
$$ \text{Builder} = \{ \text{buildPartA}(), \text{buildPartB}(), \text{getResult}() \} $$

This interface declares the *construction steps* and the *retrieval method*. All methods are abstract.

**Participant 2 — `ConcreteBuilder`**
$$ \text{ConcreteBuilder} \subseteq \text{Builder} \mid \text{has private field: product} $$

It implements the abstract steps, maintains the in-progress `product`, and returns it via `getResult()`.

**Participant 3 — `Director`**
$$ \text{Director} = \{ \text{builder : Builder}, \text{construct}() \} $$

The Director holds a reference to a `Builder` and exposes a high-level `construct()` method.

**Participant 4 — `Product`**
$$ \text{Product} = \{ \text{partA}, \text{partB}, \text{partC}, \text{representation} \} $$

The complex object being constructed, composed of multiple parts.

### Step 2: Establish the Relationships

The relationships can be expressed formally:

$$ \text{ConcreteBuilder} \xrightarrow{\text{realizes}} \text{Builder} $$

$$ \text{Director} \xrightarrow{\text{uses}} \text{Builder} $$

$$ \text{ConcreteBuilder} \xrightarrow{\text{creates/returns}} \text{Product} $$

$$ \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{instantiates}} \text{Director}, \text{ConcreteBuilder} $$

### Step 3: Derive the Sequence of Method Calls

The runtime flow follows this precise order:

$$ \text{Client} \to \text{Director.construct}() \to \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{builder.build}_i() \to \text{builder.getResult}() \to \text{Product} $$

Breaking this down into explicit transitions:

- **T1:** `Client` instantiates `new ConcreteBuilder()` and stores it in variable `builder`.
- **T2:** `Client` instantiates `new Director(builder)`.
- **T3:** `Client` calls `director.construct()`.
- **T4:** `Director` calls `builder.buildPartA()` — *part A is now set in product*.
- **T5:** `Director` calls `builder.buildPartB()` — *part B is now set in product*.
- **T6:** `Director` calls `builder.buildPartC()` — *part C is now set in product*.
- **T7:** `Director` calls `builder.getResult()` and returns the `Product` reference.
- **T8:** `Client` receives the fully-constructed `Product` and uses it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Director Does Not Return the Product Directly:**
> The Director is deliberately decoupled from the *concrete* product class. It works only with the `Builder` interface. This enables the same Director to be reused with different ConcreteBuilders to produce different representations — the entire *point* of the pattern.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Building a "House" (KTU 14-Mark Classic)

This is the most frequently asked worked example in KTU question papers. Let's derive the complete solution.

### Step 1 — Define the Product Class

The `House` product has four parts: `walls`, `roof`, `doors`, and `windows`.

The class definition is:

```java
class House {
    String walls;
    String roof;
    int doors;
    int windows;
    
    public void setWalls(String w) { this.walls = w; }
    public void setRoof(String r) { this.roof = r; }
    public void setDoors(int d) { this.doors = d; }
    public void setWindows(int n) { this.windows = n; }
    
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "House [walls=" + walls + ", roof=" + roof + 
               ", doors=" + doors + ", windows=" + windows + "]";
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Define the Abstract Builder

The `HouseBuilder` interface declares four building steps and one retrieval method.

```java
abstract class HouseBuilder {
    protected House house = new House();
    
    public abstract void buildWalls();
    public abstract void buildRoof();
    public abstract void buildDoors();
    public abstract void buildWindows();
    
    public House getHouse() { return house; }
}
```

### Step 3 — Implement Concrete Builders

Two variants illustrate the pattern's power: `WoodenHouseBuilder` and `GlassHouseBuilder`.

```java
class WoodenHouseBuilder extends HouseBuilder {
    public void buildWalls()  { house.setWalls("Wooden Planks"); }
    public void buildRoof()   { house.setRoof("Wooden Shingles"); }
    public void buildDoors()  { house.setDoors(2); }
    public void buildWindows(){ house.setWindows(4); }
}

class GlassHouseBuilder extends HouseBuilder {
    public void buildWalls()  { house.setWalls("Tempered Glass"); }
    public void buildRoof()   { house.setRoof("Glass Panels"); }
    public void buildDoors()  { house.setDoors(1); }
    public void buildWindows(){ house.setWindows(8); }
}
```

### Step 4 — Implement the Director

The Director's `constructHouse()` method calls the build steps in a fixed order.

```java
class CivilEngineer {
    private HouseBuilder builder;
    
    public CivilEngineer(HouseBuilder builder) {
        this.builder = builder;
    }
    
    public House constructHouse() {
        builder.buildWalls();
        builder.buildRoof();
        builder.buildDoors();
        builder.buildWindows();
        return builder.getHouse();
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — Client Code (The "Main" Driver)

```java
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Option 1: Build a Wooden House
        HouseBuilder wooden = new WoodenHouseBuilder();
        CivilEngineer engineer1 = new CivilEngineer(wooden);
        House h1 = engineer1.constructHouse();
        System.out.println(h1);
        
        // Option 2: Build a Glass House
        HouseBuilder glass = new GlassHouseBuilder();
        CivilEngineer engineer2 = new CivilEngineer(glass);
        House h2 = engineer2.constructHouse();
        System.out.println(h2);
    }
}
```

### Step 6 — Trace the Output

The runtime trace produces:

```
House [walls=Wooden Planks, roof=Wooden Shingles, doors=2, windows=4]
House [walls=Tempered Glass, roof=Glass Panels, doors=1, windows=8]
```

This demonstrates that the **same Director, with a different ConcreteBuilder, produces a different representation** — the central promise of the pattern.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use vs. When to Avoid

| **Use Builder When...** | **Avoid Builder When...** |
|---|---|
| Object has **more than 4-5 constructor parameters** | Object has only 1-2 simple fields |
| Construction requires **multiple ordered steps** | The object is fully constructed in a single call |
| You need **different representations** of the same product | The object is a simple data holder (POJO) |
| You want to **isolate complex construction code** from the Client | The product is immutable and trivially built |
| You are building **composite or recursive structures** | Adding the pattern would only add boilerplate |
| **Example:** Building SQL queries, HTTP requests, UI dialogs | **Example:** Building a `Point(x, y)` or `User(id, name)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students often over-apply the Builder pattern to trivial classes. If your class has only 2-3 fields, a simple constructor is cleaner. The Builder is justified only when complexity *warrants* the extra structure.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram of the Builder Pattern

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the canonical GoF class structure. The architecture follows KTU's preferred UML 2.0 notation conventions.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +main()
    }
    
    class Director {
        -builder : Builder
        +construct() Product
    }
    
    class Builder {
        <<interface>>
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +buildPartC()
        +getResult() Product
    }
    
    class ConcreteBuilderA {
        -product : Product
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +buildPartC()
        +getResult() Product
    }
    
    class ConcreteBuilderB {
        -product : Product
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +buildPartC()
        +getResult() Product
    }
    
    class Product {
        -partA : String
        -partB : String
        -partC : String
        +setPartA()
        +setPartB()
        +setPartC()
    }
    
    Client --> Director : creates
    Client --> ConcreteBuilderA : instantiates
    Client --> ConcreteBuilderB : instantiates
    Director --> Builder : uses
    Builder <|-- ConcreteBuilderA : realizes
    Builder <|-- ConcreteBuilderB : realizes
    ConcreteBuilderA --> Product : creates
    ConcreteBuilderB --> Product : creates
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Runtime Interaction Flow

This sequence diagram shows the exact ordering of method calls during construction, which is a common KTU sub-question.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant D as Director
    participant B as Builder
    participant P as Product
    
    C->>B: new ConcreteBuilder()
    Note over B: builder.product = new Product()
    
    C->>D: new Director(builder)
    C->>D: construct()
    
    activate D
    D->>B: buildPartA()
    activate B
    B->>P: setPartA(value)
    deactivate B
    
    D->>B: buildPartB()
    activate B
    B->>P: setPartB(value)
    deactivate B
    
    D->>B: buildPartC()
    activate B
    B->>P: setPartC(value)
    deactivate B
    
    D->>B: getResult()
    activate B
    B-->>D: returns product
    deactivate B
    
    D-->>C: returns Product
    deactivate D
    
    Note over C,P: Client now has fully built product
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For students who prefer a process-flow view over UML, the following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how the Builder pattern orchestrates construction. This is a backup rendering in case the UML diagram is too abstract for first-time learners.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application] --> B[Step 1: Instantiate ConcreteBuilder]
    B --> C[Step 2: Instantiate Director with Builder]
    C --> D[Step 3: Call Director.construct]
    D --> E[Step 4a: builder.buildPartA]
    D --> F[Step 4b: builder.buildPartB]
    D --> G[Step 4c: builder.buildPartC]
    E --> H[Step 5: Aggregate all parts in product]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 6: builder.getResult returns Product]
    I --> J[Step 7: Client receives fully-built Product]
    J --> K[Product Ready for Use]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#cc0066,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.4 Reference Architecture — House Builder Example

A concrete instance of the abstract pattern, mapped to the *House* example from Section 3.2, helps anchor the abstract diagram to a familiar domain.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Reminder:** For 3-mark questions, KTU expects a *concise definition* (1 mark), a *diagram or example* (1 mark), and *one differentiating point* (1 mark). Aim for 3-4 crisp lines per answer.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the intent of the Builder design pattern. How does it differ from the Factory Method pattern in terms of construction style?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Intent of Builder Pattern (GoF):** *"Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation so that the same construction process can create different representations."*

**Key Difference:**

| **Aspect** | **Builder** | **Factory Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Construction Style | **Multi-step**, sequential | **One-shot**, single call |
| Product | Complex composite | Simple single object |
| Client Visibility | Sees *recipe* and steps | Sees *factory* call only |

*(3 marks: 1 for intent, 1 for style difference, 1 for example mention)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List the four participants of the Builder pattern and state the role of the Director in one sentence.**

**Model Answer:**

The four canonical participants are:
1. **Builder** — Abstract interface declaring construction steps.
2. **ConcreteBuilder** — Implements the steps and tracks the in-progress product.
3. **Director** — Calls builder methods in a defined order to assemble the product.
4. **Product** — The complex object being constructed.

**Director's Role:** The Director encapsulates the *recipe* (the ordered sequence of build steps) and drives the construction without knowing the concrete product class.

*(3 marks: 1 for listing four, 1 for role of Director, 1 for "recipe/sequence" mention)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 2 questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice (i.e., either OR). You write EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Builder design pattern with a neat UML class diagram. List all participants clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to implement the Builder pattern for constructing a `Computer` object that has parts: `CPU`, `RAM`, `Storage`, and `GPU`. Demonstrate building both a "GamingComputer" and an "OfficeComputer" using the same Director. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Builder pattern separates the construction of a complex object from its representation. The same construction process (orchestrated by a Director) can create different representations by using different ConcreteBuilder implementations.

**Four Participants (2 marks):**

| **Participant** | **Role** |
|---|---|
| `Builder` (interface) | Declares abstract `build*()` steps and `getResult()` |
| `ConcreteBuilder` | Implements the steps, holds the in-progress product |
| `Director` | Invokes builder steps in a specific order, returns product |
| `Product` | The final complex object |

**UML Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Director {
        -builder : Builder
        +construct() Product
    }
    
    class Builder {
        <<interface>>
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +buildPartC()
        +getResult() Product
    }
    
    class ConcreteBuilder {
        -product : Product
        +buildPartA()
        +buildPartB()
        +buildPartC()
        +getResult() Product
    }
    
    class Product {
        -partA : String
        -partB : String
        -partC : String
    }
    
    Director --> Builder
    Builder <|-- ConcreteBuilder
    ConcreteBuilder --> Product
```

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Product Class (1.5 marks)**

```java
class Computer {
    private String CPU;
    private String RAM;
    private String storage;
    private String GPU;
    
    public void setCPU(String c)    { this.CPU = c; }
    public void setRAM(String r)    { this.RAM = r; }
    public void setStorage(String s){ this.storage = s; }
    public void setGPU(String g)    { this.GPU = g; }
    
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Computer [CPU=" + CPU + ", RAM=" + RAM + 
               ", Storage=" + storage + ", GPU=" + GPU + "]";
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Abstract Builder (1.5 marks)**

```java
abstract class ComputerBuilder {
    protected Computer computer = new Computer();
    
    public abstract void buildCPU();
    public abstract void buildRAM();
    public abstract void buildStorage();
    public abstract void buildGPU();
    
    public Computer getComputer() { return computer; }
}
```

**Step 3: Concrete Builders (2 marks)**

```java
class GamingComputerBuilder extends ComputerBuilder {
    public void buildCPU()    { computer.setCPU("Intel i9"); }
    public void buildRAM()    { computer.setRAM("32GB DDR5"); }
    public void buildStorage(){ computer.setStorage("2TB NVMe SSD"); }
    public void buildGPU()    { computer.setGPU("RTX 4090"); }
}

class OfficeComputerBuilder extends ComputerBuilder {
    public void buildCPU()    { computer.setCPU("Intel i5"); }
    public void buildRAM()    { computer.setRAM("16GB DDR4"); }
    public void buildStorage(){ computer.setStorage("512GB SSD"); }
    public void buildGPU()    { computer.setGPU("Integrated Graphics"); }
}
```

**Step 4: Director (1 mark)**

```java
class ComputerManufacturer {
    private ComputerBuilder builder;
    
    public ComputerManufacturer(ComputerBuilder builder) {
        this.builder = builder;
    }
    
    public Computer manufacture() {
        builder.buildCPU();
        builder.buildRAM();
        builder.buildStorage();
        builder.buildGPU();
        return builder.getComputer();
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Client Demo (1 mark)**

```java
public class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ComputerBuilder gamingBuilder = new GamingComputerBuilder();
        ComputerManufacturer m1 = new ComputerManufacturer(gamingBuilder);
        Computer gamingPC = m1.manufacture();
        System.out.println("Gaming PC: " + gamingPC);
        
        ComputerBuilder officeBuilder = new OfficeComputerBuilder();
        ComputerManufacturer m2 = new ComputerManufacturer(officeBuilder);
        Computer officePC = m2.manufacture();
        System.out.println("Office PC: " + officePC);
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Gaming PC: Computer [CPU=Intel i9, RAM=32GB DDR5, Storage=2TB NVMe SSD, GPU=RTX 4090]
Office PC: Computer [CPU=Intel i5, RAM=16GB DDR4, Storage=512GB SSD, GPU=Integrated Graphics]
```

This demonstrates the **same Director** (`ComputerManufacturer`) producing **two different representations** of the product by using two different ConcreteBuilders — the essence of the pattern.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the problems of the *telescoping constructor anti-pattern* and explain how the Builder pattern solves it. Give a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Builder pattern with the Abstract Factory pattern across at least four dimensions. State one scenario where Builder is preferred over Abstract Factory and vice versa. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**The Telescoping Constructor Anti-Pattern (3 marks):**

The telescoping constructor anti-pattern occurs when a class has multiple overlapping constructors, each adding one more parameter. For example:

```java
class User {
    public User(String name) {...}
    public User(String name, int age) {...}
    public User(String name, int age, String email) {...}
    public User(String name, int age, String email, String phone) {...}
    public User(String name, int age, String email, String phone, String address) {...}
}
```

**Problems:**
- **Parameter confusion:** `new User("Alice", 25, null, null, null)` is unreadable. What does each `null` mean?
- **Hard to maintain:** Adding a new field requires modifying every constructor.
- **Parameter order errors:** Compilers cannot catch swapped arguments (e.g., `email` and `phone` swapped).
- **Bloated API surface:** Too many constructors clutter the public interface.

**How Builder Solves It (3 marks):**

The Builder pattern uses a *fluent, step-by-step* construction style:

```java
User user = new UserBuilder()
    .setName("Alice")
    .setAge(25)
    .setEmail("alice@example.com")
    .setPhone("123-456-7890")
    .setAddress("Kerala, India")
    .build();
```

- ✅ **Readable:** Each parameter is named via the method.
- ✅ **Maintainable:** Adding a field means adding one method, not modifying constructors.
- ✅ **Type-safe:** No order confusion.
- ✅ **Optional fields:** Skip what you do not need.

**Real-World Example (1 mark):**
Java's `ProcessBuilder` class is a textbook example. To launch a process, you chain: `new ProcessBuilder("notepad.exe").directory(file).redirectError(file).redirectOutput(file).start();`

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Builder** | **Abstract Factory** |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Construct a complex object step-by-step | Produce a *family* of related objects |
| **Number of Products** | One complex product with many parts | Multiple distinct products in a family |
| **Construction Sequence** | Ordered, multi-step | One-shot, parallel |
| **Director/Client Role** | Director orchestrates; Client picks builder | Client directly calls factory methods |
| **Use Case** | Building a `Computer`, `House`, `Pizza` | Building UI widgets (Button + Checkbox + Slider for Windows vs. Mac) |
| **Return Mechanism** | `getResult()` after all parts assembled | Direct return from each factory method |
| **Complexity** | Higher (Director + Builder + ConcreteBuilders) | Medium (Factory + ConcreteFactories) |

**Scenario Where Builder is Preferred (1.5 marks):**
Use Builder when constructing a **single complex product** (like an `HttpRequest` with URL, headers, body, timeout, cookies) where the *order of assembly* and *named parameters* matter. Abstract Factory would be overkill because we are not creating a *family* of products.

**Scenario Where Abstract Factory is Preferred (1.5 marks):**
Use Abstract Factory when you need to ensure that a *family of related products* is created together to maintain compatibility — for example, building a Windows UI set (WindowsButton + WindowsCheckbox + WindowsScrollbar) versus a Mac UI set. The Builder cannot enforce that a consistent family is used because it only builds one product at a time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Builder with Factory Method.** Factory returns a *single object*; Builder returns a *complex composite* assembled step-by-step. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for confusing these.
> 2. **Do not skip the Director in your answer.** Many students describe only Builder and ConcreteBuilder, forgetting that the Director is what *drives* the construction. This costs 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Always draw a UML class diagram** for 7-mark "explain the pattern" questions. A text-only answer without a diagram typically caps at 4/7 marks.
> 4. **State the intent verbatim** in GoF's words. Examiners give bonus marks for quoting the exact definition.
> 5. **Mention the telescoping constructor anti-pattern** when asked to justify *why* the pattern is needed. This is a favorite 2-mark sub-question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Builder Method**

- ✅ The Builder pattern is a **creational design pattern** from the GoF catalog.
- ✅ **Intent:** *Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation.*
- ✅ **Four Participants:** `Builder` (interface), `ConcreteBuilder` (implementation), `Director` (orchestrator), `Product` (final object).
- ✅ The **Director** knows the *recipe* — the ordered sequence of build steps.
- ✅ The **ConcreteBuilder** knows *how* to assemble each part.
- ✅ The **Client** instantiates the Director and ConcreteBuilder, then calls `construct()`.
- ✅ The product is returned via **`getResult()`** (or `getProduct()`) only after *all* steps complete.
- ✅ The Builder solves the **telescoping constructor anti-pattern** by enabling named, optional, type-safe parameter setting.
- ✅ **Same Director + Different ConcreteBuilder = Different Representation** — the central power of the pattern.
- ✅ **Difference from Factory Method:** Factory = one-shot single object; Builder = multi-step composite.
- ✅ **Difference from Abstract Factory:** Abstract Factory = family of related products; Builder = single complex product.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** `StringBuilder`, `HttpClientBuilder`, `AlertDialog.Builder`, SQL query builders, Lombok's `@Builder`.
- ✅ **UML Notation:** Use `<<interface>>` stereotype, hollow triangle arrows for realization, and a single hollow triangle for inheritance.
- ✅ **Java Code Pattern:** Abstract class for Builder, concrete subclasses for variants, separate Director class, fluent method chaining is optional but common.
- ✅ **When NOT to use:** If the object has only 1-3 simple fields, the Builder adds unnecessary boilerplate. A simple constructor is cleaner.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Frequency:** This is a **Module 2 favorite** — expect a 7-mark sub-question on the UML diagram and a 7-mark sub-question on Java implementation in every End Semester Examination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Day Strategy:**
> 1. **Always start with the GoF intent** — verbatim or close paraphrase.
> 2. **Draw the UML class diagram** with all four participants — this is non-negotiable for full marks.
> 3. **Show Java code** for at least one concrete scenario (House, Computer, or Pizza).
> 4. **Highlight the Director's role** — explicitly mention "recipe" and "ordered steps."
> 5. **End with a comparison table** if the question is comparative — this is the fastest way to secure the last 2-3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Design Pattern and its Types - Adapter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Design Pattern and its Types - Adapter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Structural Design Patterns — The GOF Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (GoF):**
> *Structural Design Patterns are a category of design patterns prescribed by the Gang of Four (Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides, 1994) that deal with object composition — they ease the design by identifying a simple way to realize relationships between entities. They are concerned with **how classes and objects are composed to form larger structures**, while keeping these structures flexible and efficient.*

Structural patterns sit alongside three other families in the famous GoF catalog:

| Pattern Family | Core Concern | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Creational** | Object creation mechanisms | Singleton, Factory, Builder |
| **Structural** | Class/Object composition | **Adapter**, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Flyweight, Proxy |
| **Behavioral** | Communication between objects | Observer, Strategy, Command |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Big Seven" Structural Patterns (per KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> 1. **Adapter** ✅ *(this topic)*
> 2. Bridge
> 3. Composite
> 4. Decorator
> 5. Facade
> 6. Flyweight
> 7. Proxy

---

## 1.2 The Adapter Pattern — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim syllabus weightage):**
> *The **Adapter Pattern** converts the interface of a class into another interface the clients expect. Adapter lets classes work together that couldn't otherwise because of incompatible interfaces. It wraps an existing class with a new interface so it becomes compatible with the client's required type — a classic example of the **Object-Structural** design pattern.*

In simple words, an Adapter is a **translator** that sits between a client and a legacy/third-party component whose interface does not match what the client expects.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Power-Plug" Metaphor

Imagine you bought a laptop charger from the **USA (Type-A flat pins)**, and you are now travelling to **India (Type-D round pins)**. The wall socket is incompatible with your plug. What do you do?

You buy a **travel adapter** (₹150 at the airport). This little device:
- Accepts your **US plug (Adaptee)** on one side
- Exposes an **Indian plug shape (Target)** on the other side
- Internally **maps** the electrical contact logic — it doesn't "modify" the US charger; it just **wraps** it.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mapping:**
> - `Laptop Charger` → **Adaptee** (existing, incompatible interface)
> - `Wall Socket (Indian)` → **Target** (the interface the client expects)
> - `Travel Adapter` → **Adapter** (the translator)
> - `Your Laptop + You` → **Client**

In software, the same translation happens when a Java application needs to call a legacy COBOL `MainframeBankingSystem` whose method `getBalance(int acct)` must be invoked as `getAccountBalance(int acct)`. The Adapter class exposes the expected signature and internally delegates to the legacy method.

---

## 1.4 Why the Adapter Pattern is Essential

> [!TIP]
> - **Inheritance/Interface Mismatch**: When a class with a useful behavior has a wrong interface.
> - **Reusability of legacy code**: Reuse existing classes even if their interface does not match the requirement.
> - **Third-party library integration**: Wrap vendor SDKs so that your application code remains decoupled.
> - **Open/Closed Principle compliance**: Add new adapters without modifying existing client code.

The Adapter pattern is often called a **"wrapper pattern"** because of how it envelops the adaptee — it is one of the most frequently used structural patterns in real Java/.NET/Python codebases.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Participants

The GoF Adapter structure comprises **four** distinct roles. Every KTU question on Adapter must identify these participants.

| # | Role | Responsibility | Example (Charger analogy) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Target** | The domain-specific interface the **client** expects to use. | Indian wall-socket shape |
| 2 | **Client** | Collaborates with objects conforming to the **Target** interface. | The laptop |
| 3 | **Adaptee** | The existing interface that needs adapting (legacy/3rd-party). | US flat-pin charger |
| 4 | **Adapter** | Implements **Target**; wraps **Adaptee**; translates calls. | Travel adapter |

---

## 2.2 The Two Main Types of Adapter

This is the **highest-weightage** KTU sub-topic. The classification is based on **how the Adapter reaches the Adaptee**.

### Type 1 — Object Adapter (Composition-Based) ⭐ Most Common

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Object Adapter uses **composition (has-a relationship)**. It holds a reference to an Adaptee object and delegates calls to it.

**Structural Formula:**

$$\text{ObjectAdapter} \;\equiv\; \text{Target} \;\wedge\; \text{AdapteeRef} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Adaptee}$$

**Key Mechanics:**
- Adapter implements the **Target** interface.
- Adapter has a private field of type **Adaptee**.
- Each `Target` method internally calls the corresponding Adaptee method.
- ✅ **Single inheritance friendly** — works in Java, C#, Python (no multiple inheritance needed).
- ✅ **Allows multiple Adaptees** to be wrapped if required.

### Type 2 — Class Adapter (Inheritance-Based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Class Adapter uses **multiple inheritance**. The Adapter inherits from BOTH the Target (or implements it) and the Adaptee (concrete class).

**Structural Formula:**

$$\text{ClassAdapter} \;\equiv\; \text{Target} \;\wedge\; \text{extends Adaptee}$$

**Key Mechanics:**
- Inheriting from Adaptee allows overriding its methods directly.
- ❌ **Requires multiple inheritance** — only natively supported in **C++** (not in Java/C#).
- ❌ Can adapt only **one** Adaptee (no flexibility).
- ✅ No extra delegation overhead (direct method override).

### Type 3 — Two-Way (Pluggable) Adapter

A less-discussed variant where the same object can act as both a **Target** AND an **Adaptee** simultaneously, making it usable in either direction. This is more common in advanced GoF variations.

### Type 4 — Default Adapter / Adapter to Multiple Targets

When an interface has many abstract methods but the implementer cares about only a few, an **abstract skeleton class** (the Default Adapter) provides no-op defaults.

---

## 2.3 The Adapter Pattern Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this matrix for Part-A rapid-fire questions.**

| Property | Object Adapter | Class Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Composition (`has-a`) | Inheritance (`is-a`) |
| **Multiple Inheritance needed?** | No | Yes |
| **Languages supported** | Java, C#, Python, C++ | Only C++ (natively) |
| **Can adapt a class & all subclasses?** | No (only the held object) | Yes (all subclasses) |
| **Flexibility (swap Adaptee at runtime)?** | Yes | No |
| **Overhead** | Delegation indirection | Direct method override |
| **KTU Preferred Example** | ✅ | Mentioned theoretically |

---

## 2.4 Applicability — When to Use

> [!NOTE]
> **Use the Adapter Pattern when:**
> 1. You want to **reuse an existing class** whose interface does not match the one you need.
> 2. You need to **integrate third-party libraries** without modifying their source.
> 3. You are working with **legacy code** that cannot be refactored.
> 4. You need a **reusable class** to collaborate with classes that don't have compatible interfaces.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Use Cases

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Java I/O** | `InputStreamReader` adapts `InputStream` (byte) to `Reader` (character) |
| **Android** | `RecyclerView.Adapter` adapts data lists to view holders |
| **JDBC** | Database vendor drivers adapt their native protocol to the JDBC interface |
