| **Spring MVC** | `HandlerAdapter` adapts different controller types to a uniform dispatch interface |
| **Enterprise** | Adapting SOAP/XML web services to REST/JSON clients |

---

## 2.6 Consequence Analysis (GoF-Style)

**Positive Consequences:**
- ✅ Decouples client from adaptee — **Open/Closed Principle**.
- ✅ Improves **reusability** of pre-existing components.
- ✅ Introduces a clean separation of concerns.

**Negative Consequences:**
- ❌ Adds an extra layer of indirection (Object Adapter).
- ❌ Class Adapter is not flexible (locked at compile time).
- ❌ Over-adaptation may indicate deeper design smell (consider refactoring instead).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Design Process & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Step Design Procedure (Class Adapter — C++ Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU textbook classic):**
*You have a `LegacyRectangle` class with method `display(int x1, int y1, int x2, int y2)`. Your modern graphics application uses a `Shape` abstract class with method `draw(int x, int y, int w, int h)`. Design an Adapter.*

**Step 1 — Identify the Target interface.**

Define the abstract type the client code expects:

```cpp
class Shape {
public:
    virtual void draw(int x, int y, int w, int h) = 0;
};
```

**Step 2 — Identify the Adaptee class.**

This is the pre-existing, incompatible class:

```cpp
class LegacyRectangle {
public:
    void display(int x1, int y1, int x2, int y2) {
        // legacy draw routine
    }
};
```

**Step 3 — Construct the Adapter using multiple inheritance.**

The Adapter must be a `Shape` AND extend `LegacyRectangle`:

```cpp
class RectangleAdapter : public Shape, public LegacyRectangle {
public:
    void draw(int x, int y, int w, int h) override {
        // Step 4: Convert the (x, y, w, h) representation
        int x1 = x;
        int y1 = y;
        int x2 = x + w;   // derive opposite corner
        int y2 = y + h;
        // Step 5: Delegate to the Adaptee
        display(x1, y1, x2, y2);
    }
};
```

**Step 4 — Perform the interface translation logic.**

Here, the coordinate `(x, y, w, h)` is mapped to `(x1, y1, x2, y2)` — a classic **width/height → opposite-corner** conversion.

**Step 5 — Client uses the Target interface uniformly:**

```cpp
Shape* s = new RectangleAdapter();
s->draw(10, 20, 100, 50);   // client unaware of LegacyRectangle
```

---

## 3.2 Equivalent Object Adapter (Java Style)

Since Java does not allow multiple inheritance, we use **composition**:

```java
interface Shape {
    void draw(int x, int y, int w, int h);
}

class LegacyRectangle {
    public void display(int x1, int y1, int x2, int y2) {
        System.out.println("LegacyRectangle drawn");
    }
}

class RectangleAdapter implements Shape {
    private final LegacyRectangle adaptee;          // composition

    public RectangleAdapter(LegacyRectangle adaptee) {
        this.adaptee = adaptee;                     // dependency injection
    }

    @Override
    public void draw(int x, int y, int w, int h) {
        int x1 = x, y1 = y, x2 = x + w, y2 = y + h; // coordinate mapping
        adaptee.display(x1, y1, x2, y2);            // delegation
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analytical Insight:** Both versions produce identical external behavior. The Object Adapter is **runtime-pluggable** — you can swap `LegacyRectangle` for any other shape-bearing class implementing the legacy method. The Class Adapter is **compile-time locked** but has zero indirection cost.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Mapping Function

If we model the Adaptee method as $f: A \rightarrow R$ and the Target method as $g: T \rightarrow R$, the Adapter implements a **bijection** $m: T \rightarrow A$ such that:

$$g(t) \;\equiv\; f(m(t)) \quad \forall \, t \in T$$

For the rectangle example:

$$m(x, y, w, h) = (x,\; y,\; x+w,\; y+h)$$

The **draw** operation $g$ is then defined as:

$$g(x, y, w, h) = f\bigl(m(x, y, w, h)\bigr) = f(x,\; y,\; x+w,\; y+h)$$

This formalizes Adapter as a **function-composition** operation: $g = f \circ m$.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Examiner's Favourite)

| Evaluation Dimension | Class Adapter | Object Adapter | Pluggable Adapter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pattern Category** | Class-Structural | Object-Structural | Object-Structural |
| **Relationship** | Inherits Adaptee | Holds Adaptee reference | Holds Adaptee reference |
| **Reusability of Adaptee subclasses** | High (inherits all) | Low (only the held instance) | Low |
| **Runtime Adaptee substitution** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Language support** | C++ only | All OOP languages | All OOP languages |
| **Lines of code (typical)** | Less | More | More |
| **Maintenance cost** | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **KTU mark weightage** | 4 marks | 8 marks | 2 marks |

---

## 3.5 Pattern Distinction — Adapter vs Decorator vs Proxy

This is a **classic KTU trick question**. Memorize:

| Feature | Adapter | Decorator | Proxy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Convert interface | Add behavior | Control access |
| **Relationship** | Adapts to new interface | Wraps to add features | Wraps to control lifecycle |
| **Interface** | **Changes** the interface | **Keeps** the interface | **Keeps** the interface |
| **Composition** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **When used** | Pre-existing incompatible class | Dynamic feature addition | Lazy loading, security, remote |

> [!WARNING]
> **The differentiator**: Adapter **changes** the interface; Decorator and Proxy **preserve** it. If the client can call the wrapper exactly like the wrapped object, it is *not* an Adapter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram — Object Adapter (Composition)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +doWork() void
    }
    class ITarget {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }
    class Adapter {
        -adaptee : Adaptee
        +request() void
    }
    class Adaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
    }
    Client --> ITarget : uses
    ITarget <|.. Adapter : implements
    Adapter o-- Adaptee : holds reference
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Client** depends only on the abstract `ITarget` (DIP — Dependency Inversion Principle).
- The **Adapter** `implements ITarget` and **has-a** `Adaptee`.
- Arrow direction **Adapter o-- Adaptee** indicates composition (the Adaptee is held, not inherited).

---

## 4.2 UML Class Diagram — Class Adapter (Multiple Inheritance, C++)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Client {
        +doWork() void
    }
    class Target {
        <<interface>>
        +request() void
    }
    class Adapter {
        +request() void
    }
    class Adaptee {
        +specificRequest() void
    }
    Client --> Target : uses
    Target <|.. Adapter : implements
    Adaptee <|-- Adapter : inherits
```

**Interpretation:**
- `Adapter` has **two** parents: `Target` and `Adaptee`.
- In C++, this is expressed as `class Adapter : public Target, public Adaptee`.
- The Adapter simply **overrides** `specificRequest()` to make it satisfy the `Target::request()` contract.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Object Adapter Runtime Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Adapter as RectangleAdapter
    participant Adaptee as LegacyRectangle
    Client->>Adapter: draw(10, 20, 100, 50)
    activate Adapter
    Adapter->>Adapter: convert (x,y,w,h) to (x1,y1,x2,y2)
    Adapter->>Adaptee: display(10, 20, 110, 70)
    activate Adaptee
    Adaptee-->>Adapter: legacyDraw complete
    deactivate Adaptee
    Adapter-->>Client: return (no value)
    deactivate Adapter
```

**Reading the flow:**
1. Client calls `draw()` on the Adapter (Target interface).
2. Adapter performs **coordinate mapping** internally.
3. Adapter delegates the converted call to `display()` on the Adaptee.
4. Adaptee executes its legacy implementation.
5. Result is unwound back to the Client, which remains oblivious of `LegacyRectangle`.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram shows how the Adapter sits as a **bridge layer** in a layered system:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CLIENT_LAYER["Client Layer"]
        A1["Application Module"]
    end
    subgraph ADAPTER_LAYER["Adapter Layer - Structural Pattern"]
        B1["Target Interface ITarget"]
        B2["Concrete Adapter ObjectAdapter"]
    end
    subgraph ADAPTEE_LAYER["Adaptee Layer - Legacy/3rd Party"]
        C1["Adaptee Class Adaptee"]
        C2["Third Party Library"]
    end
    A1 -->|depends on| B1
    B1 -.->|implemented by| B2
    B2 -->|wraps via composition| C1
    B2 -->|wraps via composition| C2
```

**Engineering Insight:**
- The Adapter layer acts as an **anti-corruption layer (DDD terminology)**: it prevents the legacy interface vocabulary from leaking into the client domain.
- If the legacy class is replaced, only the Adapter is rewritten — the client remains untouched.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2023]`
**Define the Adapter Pattern. Identify its two main types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Adapter Pattern** is a structural design pattern that converts the interface of an existing class (Adaptee) into another interface (Target) that the client expects, enabling incompatible classes to work together. *(1 mark)*
>
> The two main types are: *(2 marks)*
> 1. **Object Adapter** — uses **composition** (has-a) to wrap the Adaptee.
> 2. **Class Adapter** — uses **multiple inheritance** to inherit both Target and Adaptee.
>
> Object Adapter is the more commonly used form as it works in single-inheritance languages like Java and C#, while Class Adapter is feasible only in C++.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Object Adapter and Class Adapter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Object Adapter | Class Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Composition (has-a) | Inheritance (is-a) |
| Multiple inheritance required | No | Yes (C++ only) |
| Runtime Adaptee swap | Possible | Not possible |

> *(1 mark per point — any three valid differences acceptable)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the GoF Adapter design pattern with its intent and applicability. List the four main participants in its structure. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a class diagram for an Object Adapter that adapts a `LegacyPrinter` class (with method `printOldFormat(String text)`) so that a client expecting `NewPrinter` interface (with method `printDocument(String text)`) can use it. Write the Java code. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Intent (1 mark):**
Convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect. The pattern lets classes work together that otherwise couldn't due to incompatible interfaces.

**Applicability (2 marks):**
- Reusing an existing class whose interface does not match the one required.
- Integrating third-party libraries without source modification.
- Working with legacy code that cannot be refactored.

**Four Participants (4 marks — 1 each):**

| Participant | Role |
|---|---|
| **Target** | Defines the domain-specific interface the Client uses. |
| **Client** | Collaborates with objects conforming to Target. |
| **Adaptee** | The existing interface that needs adapting. |
| **Adapter** | Implements Target; wraps Adaptee; translates calls. |

> **Valuation Tip:** Always draw the Target as an `<<interface>>` and the Adaptee as the existing concrete class. The Adapter must connect both — a missing arrow costs 1 mark.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class INewPrinter {
        <<interface>>
        +printDocument(String text) void
    }
    class Client {
        +run() void
    }
    class PrinterAdapter {
        -legacy : LegacyPrinter
        +PrinterAdapter(LegacyPrinter p)
        +printDocument(String text) void
    }
    class LegacyPrinter {
        +printOldFormat(String text) void
    }
    Client --> INewPrinter
    INewPrinter <|.. PrinterAdapter
    PrinterAdapter o-- LegacyPrinter
```

**Java Code (4 marks):**

```java
// Target
interface INewPrinter {
    void printDocument(String text);
}

// Adaptee (legacy)
class LegacyPrinter {
    public void printOldFormat(String text) {
        System.out.println("[LEGACY] " + text);
    }
}

// Object Adapter
class PrinterAdapter implements INewPrinter {
    private final LegacyPrinter legacy;

    public PrinterAdapter(LegacyPrinter legacy) {
        this.legacy = legacy;
    }

    @Override
    public void printDocument(String text) {
        // Translation logic (interface mapping)
        String normalized = text == null ? "" : text.trim().toUpperCase();
        legacy.printOldFormat(normalized);   // delegation
    }
}

// Client
class Client {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        INewPrinter p = new PrinterAdapter(new LegacyPrinter());
        p.printDocument("hello world");
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:** `[LEGACY] HELLO WORLD`

---

### Question B — 14 Marks  `[KTU University Exam - May 2022, Dec 2024 Expected]`

**(a)** With a suitable real-world analogy, explain the Adapter Pattern. Why is it classified as a structural pattern? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the Class Adapter and Object Adapter in terms of mechanism, flexibility, language support, and consequences. Write the C++ code for a Class Adapter that adapts a `XMLData` class (with method `getXMLData()`) to a `IData` interface (with method `getData()`). *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Real-world Analogy (3 marks):**
Consider a **laptop charger from the USA** that has flat pins (Type-A), and you are plugging it into an **Indian wall socket** that has round-pin receptacles (Type-D). You buy a **travel adapter** — it accepts the US plug (Adaptee) on one end and exposes the Indian plug shape (Target) on the other. The travel adapter is the **Adapter** — it does not change the charger; it merely translates the interface. Similarly, in software, an Adapter class wraps a legacy component and exposes the modern interface expected by the client.

**Why Structural (2 marks):**
Adapter is classified as a **structural** pattern because it deals with **how classes and objects are composed** to form larger structures. Specifically, it composes an Adaptee inside (or with) an Adapter object/class to bridge interface mismatches — this composition is the essence of structural patterns.

**Key Mapping (2 marks):**

| Analogy | Software |
|---|---|
| US charger | Adaptee |
| Indian socket | Target |
| Travel adapter | Adapter |
| Laptop | Client |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Class Adapter | Object Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Multiple inheritance | Composition |
| Flexibility | Low (compile-time locked) | High (runtime swappable) |
| Language support | C++ only | All OOP |
| Can override Adaptee behavior? | Yes (subclass access) | No (delegation only) |
| Indirection overhead | None | One extra call |

**C++ Class Adapter Code (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

// Adaptee
class XMLData {
public:
    void getXMLData() {
        cout << "<data>XML Payload</data>" << endl;
    }
};

// Target
class IData {
public:
    virtual void getData() = 0;
};

// Class Adapter - uses multiple inheritance
class XMLDataAdapter : public IData, public XMLData {
public:
    void getData() override {
        cout << "[Adapter] Forwarding to XML legacy method..." << endl;
        getXMLData();   // direct inheritance access
    }
};

// Client
int main() {
    IData* d = new XMLDataAdapter();
    d->getData();
    return 0;
}
```

> **Sample Output:**
> ```
> [Adapter] Forwarding to XML legacy method...
> <data>XML Payload</data>
> ```

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Adapter Pattern questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Adapter with Decorator/Proxy** — Adapter CHANGES the interface; the other two PRESERVE it. Examiners set one-mark trick questions on this. *(−1 mark if confused)*
> 2. **Forgetting the four participants** — Examiner expects **Target, Client, Adaptee, Adapter** explicitly named. Just drawing arrows is not enough. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Writing C++ multiple inheritance in Java code** — Java forbids it. Use composition. *(−3 marks if attempted)*
> 4. **Not showing the translation logic** — In code, students often write `legacy.printOldFormat(text)` without any transformation. The whole point of Adapter is the **interface translation** — show a mapping (e.g., `(x,y,w,h) → (x1,y1,x2,y2)`). *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Missing `<<interface>>` stereotype on Target** in UML — KTU examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting the Client class in the diagram** — Always include the Client to show the "Client → Target" dependency arrow (proves DIP).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ Adapter is a **structural** pattern under GoF — it deals with **object/class composition**.
- ✅ Intent: *Convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect.*
- ✅ **Four participants**: Target, Client, Adaptee, Adapter.
- ✅ **Object Adapter** = composition (`has-a`) → most common, language-agnostic.
- ✅ **Class Adapter** = multiple inheritance → only in **C++**, more efficient, less flexible.
- ✅ Adapter **changes** the interface; Decorator & Proxy **preserve** it. **(Most-tested one-liner)**
- ✅ Real-world mapping: US charger = Adaptee, Indian socket = Target, travel plug = Adapter, laptop = Client.
- ✅ Canonical Java example: `InputStreamReader` adapts `InputStream` → `Reader`.
- ✅ The Adapter implements the **Target** interface; the **Client** only ever sees the Target.
- ✅ The Adapter pattern respects the **Open/Closed Principle** — open for extension (new adapters), closed for modification (client unchanged).
- ✅ Object Adapter supports **runtime substitutability** of the Adaptee; Class Adapter does not.
- ✅ For full marks, your answer must contain: **(i)** definition, **(ii)** participants, **(iii)** class diagram, **(iv)** code, **(v)** a sentence on consequences.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bridge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

The **Bridge Design Pattern** is a **structural design pattern** (one of the 23 Gang-of-Four patterns) whose **intent** is to:

> *"Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently."*
> — Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (GoF, 1994)

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Bridge pattern achieves this decoupling by **delegating** the implementation work to a separate object (the *Implementor*) referenced through composition, rather than inheriting implementation behavior. The reference connecting the two hierarchies is metaphorically called the **"bridge."**

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight — OECST723 Module 2 (Design Patterns)**
> The Bridge pattern falls under **Structural Patterns**. KTU expects students to:
> 1. State the **intent** of the pattern.
> 2. Draw the **UML Structure Diagram** with all four participants.
> 3. **Apply** the pattern to a real-world scenario in the exam.
> 4. **Compare** it with similar patterns (Adapter, Strategy, Abstract Factory).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Remote Control

Imagine a **Universal Remote Control** (Abstraction) and a **Television** (Implementation):

- A *Samsung Remote* can operate a *Sony TV*, an *LG TV*, or a *Panasonic TV* — provided they all conform to a standard command interface (HDMI-CEC / IR protocol).
- The remote does **not** know which TV brand sits at the other end. It only sends standardized commands (`powerOn()`, `setVolume()`, `changeChannel()`).
- The TV does **not** know which remote is being used. It simply receives standardized commands.
- You can introduce a **new remote** (say, a touch-screen remote) **without changing** any TV.
- You can introduce a **new TV brand** **without breaking** any remote.

| Dimension in the Analogy | Real-World Entity | Pattern Role |
|---|---|---|
| Remote Control hierarchy | BasicRemote, SmartRemote, TouchRemote | **Abstraction / RefinedAbstraction** |
| TV hierarchy | SonyTV, LGTV, PanasonicTV | **Implementor / ConcreteImplementor** |
| The IR/HDMI-CEC link | The "bridge" reference | **Composition relationship** |

> [!IMPORTANT] The "bridge" is **NOT** a class — it is a **reference (HAS-A relationship)** held by the Abstraction that points to an Implementor object. This is the essence of **composition over inheritance**.

## 1.3 The Core Problem the Bridge Solves

Consider a drawing application with:
- **3 Shapes**: `Circle`, `Square`, `Triangle`
- **3 Renderers**: `OpenGL`, `DirectX`, `Vulkan`

**Without Bridge (naive inheritance):** You must create $3 \times 3 = 9$ classes: `CircleOpenGL`, `CircleDirectX`, `CircleVulkan`, `SquareOpenGL`, ... and so on. Adding **one** new shape OR **one** new renderer means **3 more** classes.

**With Bridge:** You create $3 + 3 = 6$ classes. Adding **one** new shape OR **one** new renderer means **only 1 more** class.

This is the **Cartesian Product Problem**, and the Bridge pattern collapses the multiplicative growth into additive growth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 When to Apply the Bridge Pattern (Applicability Criteria)

The Bridge pattern is the **right** choice when **any** of the following conditions hold:

1. **Independent Extension in Two Dimensions:** You want to avoid a permanent binding between an abstraction and its implementation, and both should be extensible by subclassing independently.
2. **Static Inheritance is Inflexible:** A class hierarchy using inheritance couples abstraction and implementation permanently; the Bridge pattern lets you switch implementations at runtime.
3. **Implementation Hiding:** Changes in the implementation should have **zero impact** on client code.
4. **Sharing Implementations:** You want to share an implementation among multiple objects, and this fact should be hidden from the client.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Decision Rule:** If your design has a **Cartesian product** of subclasses (Shape × Renderer, Device × Remote, Account × Database), the Bridge pattern is the textbook answer.

## 2.2 The Four Participants (GoF Canonical Roles)

| # | Participant | Role | KTU-Expected Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Abstraction** | Declares the high-level interface that clients use. Holds a reference to an `Implementor`. | "Defines the abstract control layer" |
| 2 | **RefinedAbstraction** | Extends `Abstraction` with domain-specific behavior. | "Adds new refined operations on top of the base abstraction" |
| 3 | **Implementor** | Declares the interface for the **implementation** classes. Does **NOT** have to match the Abstraction's interface exactly. | "Defines the low-level implementation contract" |
| 4 | **ConcreteImplementor** | Implements the `Implementor` interface for a specific platform/technology. | "Provides the concrete platform-specific implementation" |

## 2.3 Collaboration & Runtime Behavior

1. The **client** creates a `ConcreteImplementor` object (e.g., `OpenGLAPI`).
2. The client creates a `RefinedAbstraction` (e.g., `Circle`) and **passes the Implementor into it** (constructor injection or setter).
3. When the client calls `circle.draw()`, the Abstraction **forwards** the call to `implementor.drawCircle(...)`.
4. The Abstraction never knows which `ConcreteImplementor` is being used; the Implementor never knows which `RefinedAbstraction` is calling it.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Consequences Trade-off Table

| Aspect | Benefit (+) or Cost (−) | KTU-Examiner's One-Line |
|---|---|---|
| Decoupling | **+** Interface and implementation are fully decoupled | "Reduces compile-time dependencies" |
| Extensibility | **+** Both hierarchies vary independently | "Open/Closed Principle is satisfied" |
| Hiding Details | **+** Implementation details hidden from clients | "Clients see only the Abstraction" |
| Runtime Binding | **+** Implementor can be switched at runtime | "Strategy-like flexibility for structure" |
| Extra Indirection | **−** One extra level of indirection | "Slight performance and complexity overhead" |
| Initial Design Cost | **−** Harder to apply retroactively | "Best designed **upfront**, not patched later" |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use Case | Abstraction | Implementor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GUI Toolkits** | Window toolkit running on multiple OS | `Window` | `WindowImpl` (X11, Win32, Wayland) |
| **JDBC Drivers** | Java application connecting to any DB | `java.sql.Connection` | `MySQLDriver`, `OracleDriver` |
| **Device Drivers** | Generic printer API | `Printer` | `HPPrinter`, `CanonPrinter` |
| **Game Engines** | Game object abstraction | `GameEntity` | `DirectXRenderer`, `OpenGLRenderer` |
| **Banking Software** | Account type | `Account` | `DatabasePersistence`, `FilePersistence` |

> [!NOTE] The **JDBC** architecture in Java is a **classic industry example** of the Bridge pattern — `DriverManager` (Abstraction) is bridged to vendor-specific `Driver` implementations (ConcreteImplementors).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Design Analysis & Worked Example

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Bridge Design Methodology

> **Methodology:** Use this 5-step procedure to apply the Bridge pattern in any KTU exam problem.

### Step 1: Identify the Two Independent Dimensions

Ask: *"Does my class hierarchy have two (or more) orthogonal axes of variation?"*

For our example:
- **Dimension A — Shapes:** Circle, Square, Triangle (geometric abstraction)
- **Dimension B — Renderers:** OpenGL, DirectX, Vulkan (platform implementation)

If the answer is "yes," proceed to Step 2.

### Step 2: Define the Implementor Interface

The Implementor interface should expose **primitive operations** that the Abstraction will compose.

```java
// Step 2: Implementor interface
public interface DrawingAPI {
    void drawCircle(double x, double y, double radius);
    void drawSquare(double x, double y, double side);
    void drawTriangle(double x, double y, double base, double height);
}
```

### Step 3: Create Concrete Implementors

One ConcreteImplementor per platform/technology.

```java
// Step 3a: ConcreteImplementor 1
public class OpenGLAPI implements DrawingAPI {
    @Override public void drawCircle(double x, double y, double r) {
        System.out.println("OpenGL: Drawing circle at (" + x + "," + y + "), r=" + r);
    }
    // ... similar for drawSquare, drawTriangle
}

// Step 3b: ConcreteImplementor 2
public class DirectXAPI implements DrawingAPI {
    @Override public void drawCircle(double x, double y, double r) {
        System.out.println("DirectX: Drawing circle at (" + x + "," + y + "), r=" + r);
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Define the Abstraction

The Abstraction **holds a reference** to the Implementor (the actual "bridge") and defines higher-level operations.

```java
// Step 4: Abstraction
public abstract class Shape {
    protected DrawingAPI drawingAPI;   // <<< THIS IS THE BRIDGE
    
    protected Shape(DrawingAPI drawingAPI) {
        this.drawingAPI = drawingAPI;
    }
    
    public abstract void draw();          // high-level operation
    public abstract void resizeByFactor(double factor); // refined operation
}
```

### Step 5: Create Refined Abstractions

Each RefinedAbstraction maps its own behavior onto Implementor primitives.

```java
// Step 5a: RefinedAbstraction 1
public class Circle extends Shape {
    private double x, y, radius;
    
    public Circle(double x, double y, double r, DrawingAPI api) {
        super(api);
        this.x = x; this.y = y; this.radius = r;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        drawingAPI.drawCircle(x, y, radius);   // <-- forwarding
    }
    
    @Override
    public void resizeByFactor(double factor) {
        radius *= factor;
    }
}
```

### Step 6 (Client Wiring): Compose at Runtime

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        DrawingAPI api1 = new OpenGLAPI();
        Shape circle = new Circle(1.0, 2.0, 3.0, api1);
        circle.draw();   // "OpenGL: Drawing circle at (1.0,2.0), r=3.0"
        
        // Runtime swap: same Circle, different renderer
        DrawingAPI api2 = new DirectXAPI();
        Shape circle2 = new Circle(1.0, 2.0, 3.0, api2);
        circle2.draw();  // "DirectX: Drawing circle at (1.0,2.0), r=3.0"
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Quantitative Justification of Bridge (Mathematical Comparison)

Let $N$ = number of abstractions (e.g., shapes) and $M$ = number of implementations (e.g., renderers).

**Number of classes without Bridge (naive inheritance):**
$$C_{\text{naive}} = N \times M$$

**Number of classes with Bridge:**
$$C_{\text{bridge}} = N + M$$

**Class-count reduction ratio:**
$$R = \frac{C_{\text{naive}}}{C_{\text{bridge}}} = \frac{N \times M}{N + M}$$

For $N = 5$ and $M = 4$:
$$C_{\text{naive}} = 5 \times 4 = 20$$
$$C_{\text{bridge}} = 5 + 4 = 9$$
$$R = \frac{20}{9} \approx 2.22 \text{ (i.e., 55\% fewer classes)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU examiners love this quantitative justification. Memorize the formula $N \times M$ vs $N + M$ — it has appeared in previous university exams as a sub-question.

## 3.3 Worked Comparison: Bridge vs Adapter vs Strategy

| Question | Bridge | Adapter | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What does it decouple?** | Abstraction ↔ Implementation | Client ↔ Adaptee (legacy class) | Context ↔ Algorithm |
| **When is it designed?** | Upfront (during initial design) | Retrofitted (post hoc, for legacy code) | Upfront (when algorithms vary) |
| **Does the client know the Implementor?** | No | No | No |
| **Hierarchy split?** | Abstraction hierarchy + Implementor hierarchy | Single client + single Adaptee | Context + interchangeable Algorithm objects |
| **Intent (one line)** | Vary abstraction and implementation independently | Convert one interface to another | Swap algorithms at runtime |

> [!WARNING] **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently confuse **Bridge** with **Adapter**. The single-sentence discriminator is: **"Bridge is designed upfront to let BOTH sides vary; Adapter is retrofitted to make EXISTING incompatible classes work together."**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical GoF Class Diagram (Mermaid)

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names. Labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Abstraction {
        -implementor : Implementor
        +operation() void
    }
    class RefinedAbstraction {
        +refinedOperation() void
    }
    class Implementor {
        <<interface>>
        +operationImpl() void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorA {
        +operationImpl() void
    }
    class ConcreteImplementorB {
        +operationImpl() void
    }

    Abstraction <|-- RefinedAbstraction : extends
    Abstraction o-- Implementor : bridge reference
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorA : implements
    Implementor <|.. ConcreteImplementorB : implements
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Exam:**
- The **filled triangle / open triangle** (extends) shows the **Abstraction hierarchy** on the left.
- The **dashed triangle with hollow head** (implements) shows the **Implementor hierarchy** on the right.
- The **hollow diamond** (aggregation) at `Abstraction o-- Implementor` **is the actual "bridge"** — composition of one object inside another.

## 4.2 Applied Class Diagram: Shape × DrawingAPI

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Shape {
        <<abstract>>
        #drawingAPI : DrawingAPI
        +draw() void
        +resizeByFactor(factor double) void
    }
    class Circle {
        -x : double
        -y : double
        -radius : double
        +draw() void
        +resizeByFactor(factor double) void
    }
    class Square {
        -x : double
        -y : double
        -side : double
        +draw() void
        +resizeByFactor(factor double) void
    }
    class DrawingAPI {
        <<interface>>
        +drawCircle(x double, y double, r double) void
        +drawSquare(x double, y double, s double) void
    }
    class OpenGLAPI {
        +drawCircle(x double, y double, r double) void
        +drawSquare(x double, y double, s double) void
    }
    class DirectXAPI {
        +drawCircle(x double, y double, r double) void
        +drawSquare(x double, y double, s double) void
    }

    Shape <|-- Circle : extends
    Shape <|-- Square : extends
    Shape o-- DrawingAPI : bridge
    DrawingAPI <|.. OpenGLAPI : implements
    DrawingAPI <|.. DirectXAPI : implements
```

## 4.3 Runtime Sequence Diagram (Object Interaction)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant CircleObj as Circle
    participant OpenGLImpl as OpenGLAPI
    participant DirectXImpl as DirectXAPI

    Client->>OpenGLImpl: new OpenGLAPI()
    Client->>CircleObj: new Circle(1, 2, 3, openGLImpl)
    Client->>CircleObj: draw()
    CircleObj->>OpenGLImpl: drawCircle(1, 2, 3)
    OpenGLImpl-->>CircleObj: rendered
    CircleObj-->>Client: done

    Note over Client,DirectXImpl: Runtime swap of implementor

    Client->>DirectXImpl: new DirectXAPI()
    Client->>CircleObj: drawWith(newApi)
    CircleObj->>DirectXImpl: drawCircle(1, 2, 3)
    DirectXImpl-->>CircleObj: rendered
    CircleObj-->>Client: done
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Deployment View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientLayer["CLIENT LAYER Application Code"]
    AbstractionLayer["ABSTRACTION LAYER Shape hierarchy Circle, Square, Triangle"]
    BridgeLink["BRIDGE HAS A reference to Implementor"]
    ImplementorLayer["IMPLEMENTOR LAYER DrawingAPI interface"]
    ConcreteLayer1["CONCRETE IMPL A OpenGL API"]
    ConcreteLayer2["CONCRETE IMPL B DirectX API"]
    ConcreteLayer3["CONCRETE IMPL C Vulkan API"]

    ClientLayer --> AbstractionLayer
    AbstractionLayer -->|holds reference| BridgeLink
    BridgeLink --> ImplementorLayer
    ImplementorLayer --> ConcreteLayer1
    ImplementorLayer --> ConcreteLayer2
    ImplementorLayer --> ConcreteLayer3
```

**Key Insight from the Block Diagram:** The two hierarchies (Abstraction Layer and Implementor Layer) are **physically and logically independent**. The Bridge (middle block) is the **only coupling** between them — a single composition link.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define the Bridge design pattern. State its intent and mention the four key participants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Bridge** is a structural design pattern whose **intent** is to *"decouple an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently."* It achieves this by composing the abstraction with a reference to a separate implementor object, rather than using inheritance to bind them.
>
> The **four key participants** are:
> 1. **Abstraction** — defines the high-level interface and holds the implementor reference.
> 2. **RefinedAbstraction** — extends the abstraction with refined behavior.
> 3. **Implementor** — declares the interface for implementation classes.
> 4. **ConcreteImplementor** — implements the Implementor interface for a specific platform.
>
> *(1 mark: definition + intent; 1 mark: Abstraction & RefinedAbstraction; 1 mark: Implementor & ConcreteImplementor)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the Bridge pattern and the Adapter pattern in 5 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Bridge | Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| Intent | Decouple abstraction and implementation so both can vary independently | Convert the interface of an existing class into another interface clients expect |
| Design time | Designed **upfront** during initial system design | Retrofitted **post hoc** to integrate legacy/third-party classes |
| Hierarchy | Two parallel hierarchies (Abstraction × Implementor) | Single class adapting a single Adaptee |
| Extensibility | Both sides can be extended via subclassing | Adaptee side is usually fixed (legacy) |

*(Allocate ~1 mark per key differentiator.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **intent, structure (with UML diagram), and participants** of the Bridge design pattern. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Intent (1 mark):** Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently. It uses composition (the "bridge") instead of inheritance.

2. **UML Class Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class AbstractionA {
        -implA : ImplementorA
        +operationA() void
    }
    class RefinedA1 {
        +refinedOpA() void
    }
    class ImplementorA {
        <<interface>>
        +operationImplA() void
    }
    class ConcImplA1 {
        +operationImplA() void
    }
    class ConcImplA2 {
        +operationImplA() void
    }
    AbstractionA <|-- RefinedA1
    AbstractionA o-- ImplementorA
    ImplementorA <|.. ConcImplA1
    ImplementorA <|.. ConcImplA2
```

3. **Participants (2 marks):**
   - **Abstraction** — high-level control interface, holds the Implementor reference.
   - **RefinedAbstraction** — extends the abstraction with specialized behavior.
   - **Implementor** — defines the low-level operations (does not have to match Abstraction's interface).
   - **ConcreteImplementor** — provides the concrete implementation of the Implementor interface.

---

**(b)** Apply the Bridge pattern to design a **Remote Control — TV** system. The system must support **BasicRemote** and **AdvancedRemote** as abstractions, and **SamsungTV** and **LGTV** as implementations. Draw the UML diagram and write the Java interface definitions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Identify the dimensions (1 mark):** Remotes (BasicRemote, AdvancedRemote) and TV brands (SamsungTV, LGTV). Two independent dimensions — Bridge is appropriate.

2. **Implementor interface (2 marks):**
   ```java
   public interface TV {
       void on();
       void off();
       void tuneChannel(int channel);
       void setVolume(int level);
   }
   ```

3. **ConcreteImplementors (1 mark):**
   ```java
   public class SamsungTV implements TV {
       public void on() { System.out.println("Samsung TV ON"); }
       public void off() { System.out.println("Samsung TV OFF"); }
       public void tuneChannel(int c) { System.out.println("Samsung ch: " + c); }
       public void setVolume(int v) { System.out.println("Samsung vol: " + v); }
   }
   public class LGTV implements TV {
       // similar implementation, but with "LG" prefix
   }
   ```

4. **Abstraction + RefinedAbstraction (2 marks):**
   ```java
   public abstract class RemoteControl {
       protected TV tv;   // <<< THE BRIDGE
       protected RemoteControl(TV tv) { this.tv = tv; }
       public abstract void power();
       public abstract void channelUp();
   }
   public class BasicRemote extends RemoteControl {
       public BasicRemote(TV tv) { super(tv); }
       public void power() { tv.on(); }
       public void channelUp() { /* delegate to tv */ }
   }
   public class AdvancedRemote extends RemoteControl {
       // adds mute(), inputSelect(), etc.
   }
   ```

5. **UML diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class RemoteControl {
        <<abstract>>
        #tv : TV
        +power() void
        +channelUp() void
    }
    class BasicRemote {
        +power() void
        +channelUp() void
    }
    class AdvancedRemote {
        +power() void
        +channelUp() void
        +mute() void
    }
    class TV {
        <<interface>>
        +on() void
        +off() void
        +tuneChannel(c int) void
    }
    class SamsungTV {
        +on() void
        +off() void
        +tuneChannel(c int) void
    }
    class LGTV {
        +on() void
        +off() void
        +tuneChannel(c int) void
    }
    RemoteControl <|-- BasicRemote
    RemoteControl <|-- AdvancedRemote
    RemoteControl o-- TV
    TV <|.. SamsungTV
    TV <|.. LGTV
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of the Bridge pattern**. Compare it with the **Strategy pattern** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Bridge Structure (4 marks):** Two parallel class hierarchies connected by composition.
   - **Abstraction hierarchy** (left): `Abstraction → RefinedAbstraction`
   - **Implementor hierarchy** (right): `Implementor → ConcreteImplementorA, ConcreteImplementorB`
   - **Connection:** `Abstraction` holds a reference to `Implementor` (the "bridge" — aggregation with hollow diamond).

   *(Same GoF class diagram as in 4.1 above.)*

2. **Bridge vs Strategy Table (3 marks):**

   | Aspect | Bridge | Strategy |
   |---|---|---|
   | Intent | Decouple abstraction from implementation | Encapsulate interchangeable algorithms |
   | Object role | Implementor defines **structure of objects** | Algorithm defines **behavioral steps** |
   | Variation in | Both hierarchies can vary | Only the algorithm varies |
   | Composition link | Abstraction HAS-A Implementor | Context HAS-A Strategy |
   | When applied | Upfront design with two dimensions | When you need to swap algorithms at runtime |

---

**(b)** Discuss the **applicability, consequences, and a real-world scenario** where the Bridge pattern is the most appropriate choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Applicability (3 marks):**
   - When you want to avoid a permanent binding between an abstraction and its implementation.
   - When both the abstraction and the implementation should be extensible by subclassing independently.
   - When changes in the implementation should not impact client code.
   - When the class hierarchy grows as a Cartesian product (e.g., $N$ shapes × $M$ renderers = $N \times M$ classes).

2. **Consequences (2 marks):**
   - **+** Decoupling of interface and implementation.
   - **+** Improved extensibility — both hierarchies can be extended independently (Open/Closed Principle).
   - **+** Implementation details are hidden from clients.
   - **+** An object can change its implementation at runtime.
   - **−** Increased complexity due to extra level of indirection.
   - **−** Best designed upfront; harder to retrofit after the fact.

3. **Real-World Scenario (2 marks):**
   **Scenario: A Cross-Platform Drawing Application.**
   - **Abstraction dimension:** Shapes (`Circle`, `Square`, `Triangle`).
   - **Implementation dimension:** Rendering backends (`OpenGL`, `DirectX`, `Vulkan`).
   - **Why Bridge?** Adding a new shape (e.g., `Pentagon`) should not require re-coding the rendering backends, and adding a new backend (e.g., `Metal` for macOS) should not require re-coding the shapes. Bridge pattern separates these two independent axes of variation, keeping the design linearly extensible.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong arrow type in UML:** Drawing **inheritance** between Abstraction and Implementor (a `----|>` arrow) instead of **aggregation/composition** (`o--` or `*--`). The Bridge is **HAS-A**, not **IS-A** — losing **1 to 2 marks** if wrong.
> 2. **Confusing participants:** Calling the Implementor the "Concrete Class" or omitting the Implementor interface — losing **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up with Adapter:** Failing to mention that Bridge is designed **upfront**, not retrofitted — losing **1 mark** in comparison questions.
> 4. **No runtime binding explanation:** Bridge enables **runtime** swapping of implementors. Forgetting this loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the formula:** In quantitative questions, the formula $N \times M$ vs $N + M$ is worth **1 mark** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Print this section. Read it 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Full name:** Bridge Design Pattern (Gang-of-Four, Structural category).
- ✅ **Intent (memorize verbatim):** *"Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so that the two can vary independently."*
- ✅ **Four participants (in order):** `Abstraction` → `RefinedAbstraction` (left hierarchy); `Implementor` → `ConcreteImplementor` (right hierarchy).
- ✅ **The "bridge" is NOT a class** — it is a **composition reference** from Abstraction to Implementor.
- ✅ **Design principle:** **Composition over Inheritance**.
- ✅ **Math:** $N \times M$ classes without Bridge vs $N + M$ with Bridge.
- ✅ **Apply Bridge when:** Cartesian product explosion; need runtime implementation swap; both hierarchies should be extensible independently.
- ✅ **Do NOT apply Bridge when:** Only one dimension of variation exists (use Strategy instead); integrating a single legacy class (use Adapter instead).
- ✅ **Key benefit:** Both Abstraction and Implementor hierarchies can evolve **without touching each other** (Open/Closed Principle).
- ✅ **Real-world examples to quote in exams:** JDBC drivers, GUI toolkits (Swing/AWT on X11/Win32), cross-platform drawing apps, remote control – TV, device drivers.
- ✅ **Single-sentence discriminator vs Adapter:** Bridge is **upfront, two parallel hierarchies, both vary**; Adapter is **retrofitted, single Adaptee, make compatible**.
- ✅ **Single-sentence discriminator vs Strategy:** Bridge varies **structure and implementation**; Strategy varies **only the algorithm**.
- ✅ **UML tip:** The aggregation diamond (`o--`) between Abstraction and Implementor is the **visual signature** of the Bridge pattern — examiners check this first.
- ✅ **Common implementation language in KTU exam:** Java (occasionally C++ or Python). Use `interface` keyword for Implementor and `abstract class` for Abstraction.
- ✅ **Constructor injection** is the most common way to wire the bridge (`Shape(DrawingAPI api)`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proxy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Proxy # 🏛️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Proxy Design Pattern** is a **Structural Design Pattern** (one of the Gang-of-Four / GoF patterns) that provides a **surrogate** or **placeholder** for another object in order to **control access** to it. The proxy object has the same interface as the real (subject) object, making it transparently substitutable from the client's perspective, while internally it adds cross-cutting concerns such as lazy initialization, access control, caching, logging, or remote marshalling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim intent):** > *"Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it."* > — Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides (GoF), adapted for KTU SE Module 2. The proxy is **NOT** a copy or a replica of the real object. It is a **lightweight handle** that defers the creation or invocation of the actual resource-intensive object until it is genuinely required. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Debit Card = Proxy" Intuition Imagine you walk into a luxury hotel restaurant. Instead of handing you the entire vault of cash to pay for your meal, the hotel gives you a **debit card** linked to your bank account. | Real-World Element | Proxy Pattern Equivalent | |---|---| | Your **debit card** | The **Proxy object** | | Your **bank account (real money)** | The **RealSubject object** | | The **POS machine / swipe terminal** | The **Client code** | | **PIN verification** before withdrawal | **Access control** check inside the proxy | - The waiter (client) doesn't directly access your bank vault. They use the card (proxy). - The card enforces rules: insufficient balance → reject; wrong PIN → reject. - The card is **interface-compatible** with cash — it can be "swiped" wherever cash is accepted, but with added security and control. This is **exactly** what the Proxy pattern does: it stands in front of a real object, exposes the **same interface**, and adds **control, security, or optimization** transparently. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary) > [!NOTE] > **Terms you MUST memorize for the KTU ESE:** - **Subject** → The common interface implemented by both the RealSubject and the Proxy. The client programs against `Subject`. - **RealSubject** → The actual, original, resource-heavy object that does the real work. - **Proxy** → The surrogate that holds a reference to the RealSubject and controls access to it. - **Client** → The code that requests a service; it only knows about `Subject`, not whether it's getting the real object or the proxy. - **Protection Proxy** → Controls access based on access rights. - **Remote Proxy** → Represents an object that lives in a different address space (e.g., a different JVM / server). - **Virtual Proxy** → Defers expensive object creation (lazy loading). - **Cache Proxy** → Returns previously computed results to avoid recomputation. - **Smart Proxy** → Adds housekeeping like reference counting, logging, locking. > [!TIP] > KTU examiners LOVE the phrase *"transparent to the client"*. Always mention this in your answers. --- ## 1.4 Why Proxy? — The Engineering Motivation | Problem in Software | Proxy-Based Solution | |---|---| | Loading a **10 MB image** at app start slows boot time | **Virtual Proxy** loads it on first `display()` call | | A naïve user should not call `deleteUser()` on admin DB | **Protection Proxy** checks role before forwarding | | Calling a server in Germany from Kerala adds **latency** | **Remote Proxy** marshals + caches the network call | | Re-running `fib(40)` 1000 times wastes CPU | **Cache Proxy** stores and reuses prior results | | Need logging on every method call for audit | **Smart Proxy** wraps every call with a log statement | The single most important engineering idea: **decoupling the *act of requesting* a service from the *act of providing* it.** # 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Canonical Participants in Proxy The GoF pattern prescribes **four participants** interacting through well-defined responsibilities. Mastering these roles is the single most important step to scoring full marks in a KTU 14-mark question. ### 2.1.1 Participant 1 — `Subject` (The Abstraction) - Declares the **common interface** shared by `RealSubject` and `Proxy`. - In Java/C++ this is an **abstract class** or **interface**. - In UML it is rendered as an `<>` stereotype. - The client variable is **always typed as `Subject`**, never as the concrete class. ### 2.1.2 Participant 2 — `RealSubject` (The Heavyweight) - The **original object** that performs the real business logic. - Typically expensive in one or more dimensions: - Memory footprint (e.g., large image buffers) - Construction time (e.g., database connections) - Network latency (e.g., remote services) - Security sensitivity (e.g., admin operations) - Implements the `Subject` interface so it can be transparently used. ### 2.1.3 Participant 3 — `Proxy` (The Gatekeeper) - Holds a **reference** (`has-a` relationship) to a `RealSubject`. - Implements the **same `Subject` interface** as `RealSubject`. - **Forwards** requests to `RealSubject` after (optionally) applying: - Pre-processing (authentication, logging, caching lookup) - Post-processing (result caching, metrics, cleanup) - May decide **never** to instantiate `RealSubject` (lazy proxy). ### 2.1.4 Participant 4 — `Client` (The Requester) - Programs against the `Subject` abstraction. - **Unaware** of whether it is holding a `Proxy` or a `RealSubject`. - The proxy's transparency is what makes the pattern non-invasive. --- ## 2.2 The Operational Logic — A Step-by-Step Trace When the client invokes a method on a `Subject` reference, the following decision tree executes inside the proxy: ```text STEP 1 → Client invokes subject.request() STEP 2 → JVM resolves subject to a Proxy instance (polymorphism) STEP 3 → Proxy.performPreHook() [e.g., check access, log, look in cache] STEP 4 → IF cache hit → return cached value (skip RealSubject) STEP 5 → IF access denied → throw SecurityException STEP 6 → IF RealSubject not yet created → instantiate it now (lazy) STEP 7 → result = realSubject.request() STEP 8 → Proxy.performPostHook() [e.g., store in cache, log exit] STEP 9 → return result to Client ``` > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind the Pattern** > The Proxy pattern obeys the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)** and the **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**. The `RealSubject` focuses on *business logic*; the `Proxy` focuses on *cross-cutting infrastructure concerns*. Each class has one reason to change. --- ## 2.3 The Five Variants of Proxy (High-Yield for KTU) | # | Variant | Core Purpose | Real-World Software Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Virtual Proxy** | Lazy initialization of heavy objects | Lazy image loading in document viewers | | 2 | **Protection Proxy** | Access control / authorization | Role-based access in admin panels | | 3 | **Remote Proxy** | Local representative of remote object | Stub in Java RMI, gRPC client stub | | 4 | **Cache Proxy** | Memoization of results | HTTP response cache, query cache | | 5 | **Smart Proxy** | Add housekeeping (logging, locking, refcount) | AOP-style method interception | > [!TIP] > KTU frequently asks: *"Differentiate between Proxy and Adapter."* The **Adapter** changes the interface, while the **Proxy** keeps the **same** interface. --- ## 2.4 Proxy vs. Other Structural Patterns (Comparison Matrix) | Criterion | Proxy | Decorator | Adapter | Facade | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Intent** | Control access | Add behaviour | Convert interface | Simplify interface | | **Interface to client** | Same as RealSubject | Same as Component | Different | Simplified | | **Number of wrapped objects** | One | One or more | One | Many subsystem objects | | **Construction time** | Often lazy | Always eager | N/A | Eager | | **Reference holder?** | Yes (often) | Yes (chained) | Yes | Yes (collection) | | **Typical use** | Security, lazy load | Adding scrollbars to a view | Legacy integration | Unified subsystem API | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **definitive summary table** — memorize it before the ESE. | # | Concept | Symbolic / Structural Form | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Interface conformance | $I_{Proxy} \equiv I_{RealSubject}$ | Both implement the same `Subject` | | 2 | Reference relation | $P \rightarrow R$ | Proxy holds a pointer to RealSubject | | 3 | Client indirection | $C \rightarrow I_{Subject}$ | Client holds only the abstraction | | 4 | Lazy instantiation | $R = \emptyset \text{ initially}; \; R \leftarrow \text{new } R \text{ on first call}$ | RealSubject built on demand | | 5 | Cache lookup | $\text{if } key \in M_{cache} \rightarrow \text{return } M_{cache}[key]$ | Avoids recomputation | | 6 | Access check | $\text{if } \text{role}(C) \notin ACL \rightarrow \text{throw}$ | Prevents unauthorized call | | 7 | Transparency property | $\forall m \in M_{Subject}, \; \text{Proxy}.m \approx \text{RealSubject}.m$ | Client cannot tell them apart | | 8 | Time-complexity (cache proxy) | $T(n) = O(1)$ on cache hit, $O(f(n))$ on miss | Constant-time repeated reads | | 9 | Space complexity | $S_{proxy} = S_{ref} + S_{cache}$ | Memory grows with cached entries | | 10 | Participants count | $N = 4$ | Subject, RealSubject, Proxy, Client | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The Proxy pattern is the **architectural backbone** of the following technologies: - **Java RMI** → `Stub` objects are Remote Proxies. - **Spring AOP** → `ProxyFactoryBean` creates dynamic proxies at runtime. - **Hibernate** → Lazy-loading associations return proxy objects that fetch data only on first access. - **Web/CDN caching** → Reverse proxies (Nginx, Varnish, Cloudflare) are Cache Proxies. - **Virtual file systems / ORM** → Lazy collections are Virtual Proxies. - **Distributed systems (gRPC, Thrift)** → Generated client stubs are Remote Proxies. > [!TIP] > A 14-mark KTU question often ends with: *"Give two real-world examples."* Use **Hibernate lazy loading** and **Spring AOP** — these are the most commonly credited answers. # 🔍 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 UML Class Diagram — Exhaustive Construction The most KTU-rewarded artifact for a 14-mark "Explain Proxy" question is a **fully labelled UML class diagram**. We will derive it step-by-step, the way an examiner expects to see it written in an answer script. ### Step 1 — Identify the abstraction We begin with a `Subject` interface. It declares the operations the client may request. ```text ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ <> │ │ Subject │ ├──────────────────────────────┤ │ + request(): void │ └──────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Step 2 — Identify the real implementer `RealSubject` is the heavyweight class. It implements `Subject` and contains the genuine business logic. ```text ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ RealSubject │ ├──────────────────────────────┤ │ + request(): void │ ← heavy work │ - loadFromDisk(): void │ │ - decrypt(): void │ └──────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Step 3 — Identify the surrogate `Proxy` also implements `Subject` and holds a reference to a `RealSubject`. ```text ┌──────────────────────────────┐ │ Proxy │ ├──────────────────────────────┤ │ - realSubject: RealSubject │ ← pointer │ + request(): void │ ← forwards │ - checkAccess(): boolean │ ← pre-hook │ - logAccess(): void │ ← post-hook └──────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### Step 4 — Establish the relationships Three relationships are mandatory: $$ \text{Proxy} \xrightarrow{\text{implements}} \text{Subject} $$ $$ \text{RealSubject} \xrightarrow{\text{implements}} \text{Subject} $$ $$ \text{Proxy} \xrightarrow{\text{has-a (association)}} \text{RealSubject} $$ ### Step 5 — Final unified class diagram ```mermaid classDiagram class Subject { <> + request() void } class RealSubject { + request() void - loadFromDisk() void } class Proxy { - realSubject: RealSubject + request() void - checkAccess() boolean - logAccess() void } class Client { + main() void } Subject <|.. RealSubject : implements Subject <|.. Proxy : implements Proxy --> RealSubject : holds reference Client --> Subject : uses ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU answer scripts, always draw the **`<>` stereotype**, the **inheritance arrows with hollow triangles**, and label the **association** between `Proxy` and `RealSubject`. Each is worth marks. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — A Protection Proxy for an Admin Operation This example walks through the most common KTU scenario: a proxy that blocks unauthorized callers from invoking a sensitive operation. ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement > *"Design a protection proxy that allows only users with the role `ADMIN` to call `deleteUser()` on a `UserService`."* ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Define the abstraction** $$ \text{interface } UserService \{ \; + \text{deleteUser}(id: String): void \;\} $$ **Step 2 — Build the real subject (the actual database delete operation)** $$ \text{class } UserServiceImpl : UserService \{ \; \text{implements } deleteUser \rightarrow \text{issues } SQL \; DELETE \;\} $$ **Step 3 — Build the proxy with embedded access control** $$ \text{class } UserServiceProxy : UserService \{ $$ $$ \quad \text{private realService} = \text{new } UserServiceImpl(); $$ $$ \quad \text{private currentRole} = \text{getCallerRole()}; $$ $$ \quad \text{deleteUser}(id) \{ $$ $$ \qquad \text{if } (\text{currentRole} \neq \text{"ADMIN"}) \; \text{throw new } SecurityException(\text{"Forbidden"}); $$ $$ \qquad \text{else} \; \text{realService.deleteUser}(id); $$ $$ \quad \} $$ $$ \} $$ **Step 4 — Client-side invocation (the client doesn't know it's a proxy)** $$ \text{UserService svc} = \text{new } UserServiceProxy(); $$ $$ \text{svc.deleteUser}(\text{"u123"}); $$ The variable `svc` is typed as the **abstraction** `UserService`. At runtime, polymorphism dispatches `deleteUser` to the proxy, which enforces the rule. If the role is `GUEST`, the proxy **never** calls the real subject — the database is never touched. The protection is enforced at the **architectural** layer, not inside business code. ### 3.2.3 Trace Table — KTU-Style Walkthrough | Step | Caller | Operation | Role | Proxy Action | RealService Called? | Outcome | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Alice | `deleteUser("u1")` | ADMIN | Pass check | ✅ Yes | User deleted | | 2 | Bob | `deleteUser("u2")` | GUEST | Block | ❌ No | `SecurityException` | | 3 | Carol | `deleteUser("u3")` | MANAGER | Block | ❌ No | `SecurityException` | | 4 | Alice | `deleteUser("u1")` | ADMIN (again) | Pass + log | ✅ Yes | Audit log written | This kind of **stepwise trace table** consistently earns full method marks in KTU evaluations. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — A Virtual Proxy (Lazy Image Loader) **Step 1 —** Client calls `image.display()`. The variable holds a `ProxyImage`, not a `RealImage`. **Step 2 —** Inside `ProxyImage.display()`: $$ \text{if } (\text{realImage} = \text{null}) \; \text{realImage} = \text{new } RealImage(\text{filename}); $$ **Step 3 —** `realImage.display()` executes the expensive `loadFromDisk()` + decode operation. **Step 4 —** On the **second** call, the `null` check fails, so no disk I/O occurs. The proxy saves $\approx 100\text{ ms}$ per subsequent display. This is the canonical example used in the GoF book. KTU examiners recognize it instantly and award higher marks if you can phrase it as: *"The proxy defers the full cost of construction to first use."* --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Proxy | Symptom in Design | Diagnosis | Recommended Proxy Variant | |---|---|---| | Object constructor is slow and may not be needed | Lazy loading missing | **Virtual Proxy** | | Sensitive operation callable from anywhere | No authorization layer | **Protection Proxy** | | Local code calling remote service directly | Tight coupling to network | **Remote Proxy** | | Repeated identical expensive queries | No memoization | **Cache Proxy** | | Need cross-cutting logging/metrics | Boilerplate polluting business code | **Smart Proxy** | --- ## 3.5 Pseudocode Trace — Cache Proxy We derive the cache proxy behaviour formally to make the algorithm unambiguous for KTU answer scripts. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{function } \text{get}(key): \\ &\quad \text{if } key \in M_{cache}: \\ &\qquad \text{return } M_{cache}[key] \quad \text{[cache hit, } O(1)\text{]} \\ &\quad \text{else:} \\ &\qquad \text{value} \leftarrow \text{realService.compute}(key) \quad \text{[cache miss, } O(f(n))\text{]} \\ &\qquad M_{cache}[key] \leftarrow \text{value} \\ &\qquad \text{return value} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** - The first invocation has cost $O(f(n))$, where $f$ is the cost of `realService.compute`. - All subsequent invocations with the same `key` have cost $O(1)$. - The trade-off is **space for time**: memory $S_{cache}$ grows linearly with the number of unique keys, but average-case latency collapses to constant time. > [!TIP] > A 14-mark question may end with: *"Discuss the trade-off."* Use this **space-vs-time** framing — it is the textbook-correct answer and guarantees the method marks. # 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sequence Diagram — Runtime Object Interaction The following Mermaid sequence diagram captures the **exact message flow** between Client, Proxy, and RealSubject when a cache-miss occurs. This diagram is what a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark descriptive answer. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Client as Client participant Proxy as Proxy participant Cache as CacheStore participant Real as RealSubject Client->>Proxy: request() Proxy->>Cache: contains(key)? Cache-->>Proxy: false (miss) Proxy->>Real: instantiate() if null Real-->>Proxy: realSubject reference Proxy->>Real: request() Real-->>Proxy: result Proxy->>Cache: store(key, result) Proxy-->>Client: return result Note over Client,Real: Second call - cache hit Client->>Proxy: request() Proxy->>Cache: contains(key)? Cache-->>Proxy: true Proxy-->>Client: return cached result ``` > [!NOTE] > The `autonumber` directive adds step numbers, which mirrors KTU answer-script step numbering and earns method marks. --- ## 4.2 Component-Level Architecture — Block Diagram View The following block schematic shows how the proxy sits between the client layer and the real resource layer, and how the variants attach to it. This is the **block-level functional architecture** the KTU rubric rewards when students cannot draw every physical detail. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ClientLayer["CLIENT LAYER"] C["Client Code
typed as Subject"] end subgraph ProxyLayer["PROXY LAYER"] P["Proxy Object
implements Subject"] P --> H1["Pre-Processing Hook
auth, logging, cache-lookup"] P --> H2["Post-Processing Hook
cache-store, metrics, cleanup"] end subgraph RealLayer["REAL SUBJECT LAYER"] R["RealSubject
implements Subject
expensive / sensitive / remote"] end C --> P P --> R P -. variant .-> V1["Virtual Proxy
lazy init"] P -. variant .-> V2["Protection Proxy
access control"] P -. variant .-> V3["Remote Proxy
network stub"] P -. variant .-> V4["Cache Proxy
memoization"] P -. variant .-> V5["Smart Proxy
housekeeping"] ``` > [!TIP] > The **dotted lines** indicate *variants* (semantic roles) of the same `Proxy` class. KTU examiners accept this notation; it conveys that the variants are *configurations* of the same structural idea. --- ## 4.3 Request-Processing State Machine This state diagram formalizes the proxy's decision logic as a finite-state machine, which is a high-credit visualization in KTU evaluations. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> AuthCheck: request received AuthCheck --> Denied: role invalid AuthCheck --> CacheLookup: role valid CacheLookup --> ReturnCached: hit CacheLookup --> LazyInit: miss LazyInit --> Forward: already created LazyInit --> Instantiate: null Instantiate --> Forward Forward --> PostProcess: real result PostProcess --> ReturnToClient: store in cache, log ReturnCached --> ReturnToClient Denied --> [*]: SecurityException ReturnToClient --> Idle ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Read this state machine like a story:** > 1. The proxy starts **Idle**. > 2. A request triggers an **AuthCheck**. > 3. If denied → terminal state. > 4. If allowed → check the **cache**. > 5. Cache hit → return immediately. Cache miss → lazily instantiate and forward. > 6. After the real subject responds, **post-process** (log, cache-store, metrics), then return to idle. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q1.** Define the Proxy design pattern. Mention any two of its variants. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Proxy design pattern is a **structural pattern** that provides a **surrogate or placeholder** for another object to **control access** to it. The proxy implements the **same interface** as the real object, making it transparently substitutable from the client's perspective. *(2 marks)* > > Two variants: *(1 mark)* > 1. **Virtual Proxy** – defers expensive object creation until first use. > 2. **Protection Proxy** – enforces access control based on caller rights. > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2.** Differentiate between **Adapter** and **Proxy** design patterns. > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Criterion | Adapter | Proxy | > |---|---|---| > | **Intent** | Converts one interface to another that the client expects | Provides a surrogate with the **same** interface to control access | > | **Interface change** | Yes — bridges incompatible interfaces | No — interface is identical to the RealSubject | > | **Primary use** | Legacy code integration | Lazy loading, access control, remote calls, caching | > > *(1 mark per correct row, 3 rows = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] > > **(a)** Explain the **Proxy design pattern** with a neat UML class diagram. List the participants and describe the role of each. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** With a suitable example, illustrate how a **Virtual Proxy** is used to implement **lazy loading** of a large image object. *(7 marks)* ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Explanation + UML Class Diagram *(7 marks)* **Definition (2 marks):** The Proxy pattern is a structural GoF pattern that provides a surrogate for another object to control access. The proxy and the real object share a **common interface (`Subject`)**, ensuring transparency to the client. **Four participants (3 marks):** 1. **Subject** — the common interface that both `Proxy` and `RealSubject` implement. 2. **RealSubject** — the original, resource-intensive object that performs the real work. 3. **Proxy** — holds a reference to `RealSubject`, implements `Subject`, and adds control logic (pre/post hooks). 4. **Client** — programs against the `Subject` abstraction; unaware of the proxy. **UML Class Diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid classDiagram class Subject { <> + request() void } class RealSubject { + request() void } class Proxy { - realSubject: RealSubject + request() void - checkAccess() boolean - logAccess() void } class Client { + main() void } Subject <|.. RealSubject : implements Subject <|.. Proxy : implements Proxy --> RealSubject : holds reference Client --> Subject : uses ``` #### Part (b) — Virtual Proxy Lazy-Loading Example *(7 marks)* **Scenario (1 mark):** A document viewer must display high-resolution images. Loading all images eagerly at startup would slow the application. **Subject interface (1 mark):** $$ \text{interface } Image \{ \; + \text{display}(): \text{void} \;\} $$ **RealSubject (1.5 marks):** $$ \text{class } RealImage : Image \{ \; \text{private filename; constructor loads from disk + decodes; display() paints to screen} \;\} $$ **Proxy (2.5 marks):** $$ \text{class } ProxyImage : Image \{ $$ $$ \quad \text{private realImage: RealImage} = \text{null}; $$ $$ \quad \text{ProxyImage}(\text{filename}) \{ \text{this.filename} = \text{filename}; \} $$ $$ \quad \text{display}() \{ $$ $$ \qquad \text{if } (\text{realImage} = \text{null}) \; \text{realImage} = \text{new } RealImage(\text{filename}); $$ $$ \qquad \text{realImage.display}(); $$ $$ \quad \} $$ $$ \} $$ **Client usage (1 mark):** $$ \text{Image img} = \text{new ProxyImage}(\text{"photo.jpg"}); $$ $$ \text{img.display}(); \quad \text{[triggers actual load here — first call only]} $$ The `RealImage` is constructed **only on the first `display()` call**, deferring the heavy I/O cost. --- > ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] > > **(a)** Discuss the **different types of Proxy** patterns with real-world software examples for each. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Compare and contrast **Proxy** with **Decorator** pattern. In what scenario would you choose one over the other? *(7 marks)* ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Types of Proxy *(7 marks)* | # | Type | Description (1.2 marks each) | Real-World Example (0.2 marks each) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Virtual Proxy** | Defers creation of expensive objects until first access. | Hibernate lazy-loading of `@OneToMany` collections | | 2 | **Protection Proxy** | Filters access based on caller identity/role. | Spring Security method-level `@PreAuthorize` | | 3 | **Remote Proxy** | Local representative of an object in a different address space. | Java RMI **stub**; gRPC client stub | | 4 | **Cache Proxy** | Stores previously computed results to avoid recomputation. | Redis client facade; CDN edge caches | | 5 | **Smart Proxy** | Adds housekeeping (logging, locking, ref-count). | Spring AOP `@Around` advice; `Proxy.newProxyInstance` | #### Part (b) — Proxy vs Decorator *(7 marks)* | Dimension (1 mark each × 5 = 5 marks) | Proxy | Decorator | |---|---|---| | **Primary intent** | Control access to an object | Add new behaviour to an object | | **Object construction** | Often **lazy** (deferred) | Always **eager** (wraps existing object) | | **Number of objects** | Usually wraps **one** real subject | Can chain **multiple** decorators | | **Client's awareness** | Proxy is **transparent** | Client **explicitly** wraps and chains | | **Typical trigger** | Constructed by a factory/framework | Composed by the client code | **Scenario selection (2 marks):** - **Choose Proxy** when you need to **control HOW or WHEN** the real object is accessed (lazy creation, security, remote marshalling, caching). The wrapping is **invisible** to the client. - **Choose Decorator** when you need to **ADD responsibilities** dynamically (e.g., adding scrollbars, borders, or compression to a UI component). The chaining is **explicit** and open-ended. > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing Proxy with Decorator** — both are *structural wrappers*, but Proxy *controls* while Decorator *extends*. If your answer uses phrases like *"adds new features"* for Proxy, you will lose at least 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the `<>` stereotype** in the UML class diagram — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark. > 3. **Drawing an arrow from Client to RealSubject** — this breaks the indirection principle. The client must point to `Subject`, not to `RealSubject`. > 4. **Missing the four participants** — `Subject`, `RealSubject`, `Proxy`, `Client` must all appear and be **named and defined**. > 5. **Omitting a worked example** — for a 14-mark question, a textual or pseudocode example is mandatory to secure the last 2–3 method marks. > 6. **Writing the answer as a list with no diagram** — KTU mandates a UML class diagram; absence usually means a cap of 8/14 marks. --- ## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before ESE** - ✅ Proxy is a **Structural** GoF pattern that **controls access** to another object via a **surrogate**. - ✅ Four mandatory participants: **`Subject`** (interface), **`RealSubject`** (heavy), **`Proxy`** (gatekeeper), **`Client`** (consumer). - ✅ Both `Proxy` and `RealSubject` **must implement the same `Subject` interface** — this is what makes the proxy *transparent*. - ✅ The proxy holds a **has-a** reference (composition) to the `RealSubject`, **not is-a** inheritance. - ✅ The **client variable is typed as `Subject`**, never as `Proxy` or `RealSubject` directly. - ✅ **Five variants** to memorize: Virtual, Protection, Remote, Cache, Smart Proxy. - ✅ **Virtual Proxy** = lazy init. **Protection Proxy** = access control. **Remote Proxy** = local stub for remote object. **Cache Proxy** = memoization. **Smart Proxy** = housekeeping. - ✅ **Trade-off equation** — Cache proxy trades $S_{cache}$ (memory) for $\bar{T}(n) = O(1)$ (latency). - ✅ **Real-world anchors** (cite at least one): Hibernate lazy loading, Spring AOP, Java RMI stubs, gRPC stubs, Nginx reverse proxy. - ✅ **Proxy vs Decorator** — Proxy *controls*, Decorator *extends*. Proxy is often *lazy*, Decorator is always *eager*. - ✅ **Proxy vs Adapter** — Proxy *keeps* the interface, Adapter *changes* the interface. - ✅ **UML must include** `<>` stereotype, hollow-triangle inheritance arrows, and the `Proxy → RealSubject` association with multiplicity `1`. - ✅ **Lazy initialization formula** in pseudocode: `if (realSubject == null) realSubject = new RealSubject();` - ✅ **Sequence diagram must show** cache-lookup → lazy-instantiate → forward → cache-store flow (autonumber steps for marks). - ✅ Always state the **transparency property**: the client cannot distinguish the proxy from the real subject. - ✅ **Mention SRP & OCP** — Proxy separates cross-cutting concerns from business logic; this single sentence often earns the "design principle" mark. - ✅ **Avoid these words in the wrong places**: do NOT say "Proxy *adds* behaviour" (that's Decorator) or "Proxy *changes* the interface" (that's Adapter).

topics.title: Composite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Composite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Composite Design Pattern

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Pattern (Gang-of-Four, Structural Category):**
> *"Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Composite lets clients treat individual objects (leaves) and compositions of objects (composites) uniformly."*

The Composite Design Pattern is a **Structural Design Pattern** used in object-oriented software design when the core domain model can be represented as a **tree (or recursive hierarchical) structure** in which individual objects (*leaves*) and groups of objects (*composites*) must be manipulated through a **single, uniform client interface**. The pattern promotes **recursive composition**, enabling client code to remain agnostic of whether it is working with a primitive element or a complex subtree.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Company Organization Chart

Imagine an IT company like Infosys:

- A **Manager** has a name, salary, and a set of direct reports.
- A **Developer** is an individual with a name and salary — no reports.
- A **Director** is also a "manager," but his direct reports are themselves managers.

When the CEO wants to compute the *total salary expense* of the organization, the CEO does **not** care whether the operation is being applied to a leaf developer (a person with no subordinates) or to a composite director (a person whose subordinates themselves have subordinates). The CEO simply calls a uniform operation: `getTotalSalary()`. The pattern internally **recursively delegates** the call down the hierarchy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Composite pattern is essential whenever the domain exhibits a *recursive aggregation* relationship — a "contains-a" relationship that may consist of primitives or other containers of the same type.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Examiner Visibility)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Component** | The abstract interface or class that declares the common operations for both leaves and composites. |
| **Leaf** | A primitive object with no children; implements Component directly. |
| **Composite** | A container object that stores child Components and delegates operations to them. |
