| **Client** | Manipulates objects in the composition through the Component interface. |
| **Uniformity Principle** | The defining rule: *Clients cannot and should not differentiate between a leaf and a composite.* |

### 1.4 Problem Without the Pattern

Without the Composite pattern, a client must write branching code like:

```
if (obj instanceof Leaf)      { ... leaf-specific logic ... }
else if (obj instanceof Composite) { ... composite-specific logic ... }
```

This **violates the Open/Closed Principle**, introduces tight coupling, and produces fragile code that must be modified every time a new element type is added.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Participants of the Pattern (Roles)

The Composite pattern specifies four canonical participants. Each is described in a way that matches KTU 2024 Scheme board-answer expectations.

### 2.1.1 Component (Abstract Class / Interface)
- Declares the common interface for all objects in the composition.
- Optionally defines an interface for accessing and managing a parent reference (useful for tree traversal in both directions).
- Usually contains default implementations of operations that subclasses can override.

### 2.1.2 Leaf
- Represents **terminal / end objects** of the composition (no children).
- Defines behaviour for primitive objects.
- A leaf has no children, so operations that manage children simply return / do nothing.

### 2.1.3 Composite
- Defines behaviour for components having children.
- Stores child components in a data structure (commonly a `List<Component>`).
- Implements child-related operations in the Component interface (e.g., `add()`, `remove()`, `getChild()`).
- Delegates work to children by **recursively calling the same operation** on each child.

### 2.1.4 Client
- Manipulates objects in the composition through the **Component interface**.
- This is the key reason the pattern works: the client is *type-blind* with respect to leaf vs composite.

## 2.2 Why It Works — The "Why" Behind the Mechanism

1. **Polymorphism is the foundation.** Because both `Leaf` and `Composite` implement the same `Component` interface, the client's reference can point to either.
2. **Recursion mirrors the domain.** Hierarchies are naturally recursive — a folder contains files and other folders, a manager manages employees and other managers. The pattern reflects this recursion directly in the code.
3. **Open/Closed compliance.** New leaf or composite types can be added *without modifying* existing client code.
4. **Single Responsibility.** The composite handles structure (children management); the leaf handles primitive behaviour. Neither knows about the other explicitly.

## 2.3 UML Class Diagram (Class-Role Mapping)

The structural skeleton of the pattern can be summarized as:

| Class | Stereotype | Key Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `Component` | `<<abstract>>` | `operation()` *(abstract)*, `add()`, `remove()`, `getChild()` |
| `Leaf` | concrete | `operation()` — primitive implementation |
| `Composite` | concrete | `operation()` — recursive iteration over children |
| `Client` | driver | Uses `Component` reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety vs Transparency Trade-off:** Some textbook variants make the child-management methods (`add`, `remove`) *only* on `Composite` (the **"safe"** variant). The classic GoF version puts them on `Component` (the **"transparent"** variant) so the client can call them uniformly — at the cost of these methods being meaningless on leaves.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Recursion & Complexity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although design patterns are not "mathematical," KTU examiners frequently expect quantitative reasoning on tree operations. The following formulas are commonly tested.

| Quantity | Formula / Bound | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Total nodes visited by `operation()` | $N = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | Sum of all leaves and composites in the tree, where $k$ is the number of composites and $n_i$ is the subtree size of composite $i$. |
| Recursion depth | $D = h$ | Equals the **height** $h$ of the tree; stack frames consumed during traversal. |
| Time complexity of a full traversal | $T(N) = O(N)$ | Each node is visited exactly once. |
| Space complexity (recursion stack) | $S(N) = O(h)$ | Worst case $h = N$ (degenerate chain) → $O(N)$. |
| Composite child count memory | $M = O(c)$ | Each composite stores $c$ child references in a collection. |

**In these expressions:**
- $N$ = total number of nodes (leaves + composites) in the tree.
- $h$ = height of the tree (longest root-to-leaf path).
- $c$ = branching factor of a composite node.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Composite Use Case |
|---|---|
| **GUI Frameworks** (Swing, JavaFX) | A `Container` holds `Component`s; a `Container` *is* a `Component`. |
| **File Systems** | Folders contain files and other folders. |
| **Graphics Editors** | A `Group` contains shapes and other groups (nested transformations). |
| **Organization Hierarchies** | Manager → Employees + Sub-managers. |
| **XML / DOM Parsing** | An XML element contains text nodes and other elements. |
| **Arithmetic Expression Trees** | `+` and `*` nodes contain operands which may themselves be sub-expressions. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite Question:** *"Give two real-world examples where the Composite pattern is applicable."* Always answer with a **uniform client operation** perspective — e.g., "calculate folder size" treats a file (leaf) and folder (composite) identically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> In design pattern problems, "derivation" means deriving the **operation-propagation equation** and the **structural invariants** of the composite tree. KTU questions often ask students to express the result of a recursive operation in closed form.

## 3.1 Derivation — The Recursive Operation Equation

Let $T(v)$ be the result of `operation()` applied to a node $v$ in the composite tree. We will derive a closed-form expression.

### Step 1 — Base Case (Leaf Node)

For a leaf $v$, there are no children. The result is the leaf's own primitive value:

$$
T_{\text{leaf}}(v) = f_{\text{leaf}}(v)
$$

In words: the operation result for a leaf is simply the leaf's local primitive computation (e.g., file size, employee salary).

### Step 2 — Recursive Case (Composite Node)

For a composite $v$ with children $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_c$, the pattern **delegates** `operation()` to each child and **aggregates** the results using a combiner function $\bigoplus$:

$$
T_{\text{composite}}(v) = f_{\text{self}}(v) \; \bigoplus \; T(v_1) \; \bigoplus \; T(v_2) \; \bigoplus \; \dots \; \bigoplus \; T(v_c)
$$

Here $\bigoplus$ is a domain-specific aggregator (sum, concatenation, max, etc.).

### Step 3 — Combined Single-Pass Form

We can write a single, unified formula that works for **both** leaves and composites. Define the children set $C(v)$ such that $C(v) = \emptyset$ for a leaf. Then:

$$
T(v) \;=\; f_{\text{self}}(v) \;\oplus\; \bigoplus_{u \,\in\, C(v)} T(u)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The aggregator is **iterated** in a fixed order (often left-to-right) but is **commutative and associative** in most practical applications (sum, max, union, set-merge). This property is what permits KTU examiners to "pre-compute" subtrees in any order.

### Step 4 — Closed-Form for a Subtree

Let $S$ be the set of all nodes in the subtree rooted at $v$, and let $w(v)$ denote the *self-value* of node $v$ (its primitive contribution). Then by telescoping the recursion:

$$
T(v) \;=\; \bigoplus_{u \,\in\, S} w(u)
$$

For the special case $\oplus \equiv +$ (summation), the formula reduces to a plain sum of self-values:

$$
T(v) \;=\; \sum_{u \,\in\, S} w(u)
$$

This is the closed-form most often expected in KTU board answers.

### Step 5 — Worked Numeric Example (KTU-Style)

Consider the following tree where $w(\cdot)$ is the node's "salary" and $T(\cdot)$ is the total salary of the subtree:

```
         CEO  (w = 100)
        /    \
     CTO(70)  CFO(80)
     /   \       \
  Dev1(40) Dev2(50)  Acc(60)
```

**Step A — Compute leaves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\text{Dev1}) &= 40 \\
T(\text{Dev2}) &= 50 \\
T(\text{Acc})  &= 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute the CTO (one level up):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\text{CTO}) &= w(\text{CTO}) + T(\text{Dev1}) + T(\text{Dev2}) \\
              &= 70 + 40 + 50 \\
              &= 160
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute the CFO:**

$$
T(\text{CFO}) = 80 + 60 = 140
$$

**Step D — Compute the CEO (root):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\text{CEO}) &= w(\text{CEO}) + T(\text{CTO}) + T(\text{CFO}) \\
              &= 100 + 160 + 140 \\
              &= 400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Verification via closed form:**

$$
T(\text{CEO}) \;=\; \sum_{u \,\in\, S_{\text{CEO}}} w(u) \;=\; 100 + 70 + 80 + 40 + 50 + 60 \;=\; 400 \;\; \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Composite

| Symptom in the Domain Model | Composite Pattern Fit | Alternative Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Tree-like part-whole hierarchy | **Strong fit** — use Composite | — |
| Leaves and groups share common operations | **Strong fit** | Decorator (if adding responsibilities) |
| Strict type-checking (leaf vs composite must differ) | **Weak fit** — violates transparency | Visitor |
| Single object, no aggregation | **No fit** | Strategy / State |
| Build complex object step-by-step | No fit | Builder |

## 3.3 Consequences (Trade-Off Table)

| Consequence | Nature |
|---|---|
| Defines class hierarchies of primitive and composite objects | ✅ Benefit |
| Makes client code simpler | ✅ Benefit |
| Easy to add new kinds of components | ✅ Benefit |
| Can make design overly general | ⚠️ Drawback |
| Hard to restrict components of a composite | ⚠️ Drawback (e.g., preventing a `Circle` from being added to a `Folder`) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Generic Composite Structure

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Component {
        <<abstract>>
        +operation()
        +add(Component c)
        +remove(Component c)
        +getChild(int i) Component
    }
    class Leaf {
        +operation()
    }
    class Composite {
        -children : List~Component~
        +operation()
        +add(Component c)
        +remove(Component c)
        +getChild(int i) Component
    }
    class Client {
        +execute(Component c)
    }

    Component <|-- Leaf
    Component <|-- Composite
    Composite o-- Component : children
    Client ..> Component : uses
```

> **Reading the diagram:** `Component` is abstract. `Leaf` and `Composite` both inherit from it. `Composite` aggregates a list of `Component` references — that aggregation may point to other `Composite` objects (recursion). The `Client` only ever holds a `Component` reference.

## 4.2 Mermaid Object Tree — Runtime Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Drawing Board] --> B[Group: Animals]
    A --> C[Group: Background]
    B --> D[Circle: Cat]
    B --> E[Group: Dog]
    E --> F[Circle: Body]
    E --> G[Circle: Head]
    C --> H[Rectangle: Sky]
    C --> I[Rectangle: Ground]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** `Drawing Board` is the root composite. `Group: Animals` and `Group: Background` are composites. `Circle: Cat`, `Circle: Body`, etc. are leaves. A client operation such as `render()` will recursively descend through this entire tree.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Recursive Operation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Root as Composite: Root
    participant ChildA as Composite: ChildA
    participant LeafA1 as Leaf: A1
    participant ChildB as Composite: ChildB

    Client->>Root: operation()
    activate Root
    Root->>ChildA: operation()
    activate ChildA
    ChildA->>LeafA1: operation()
    activate LeafA1
    LeafA1-->>ChildA: resultA1
    deactivate LeafA1
    ChildA-->>Root: resultA
    deactivate ChildA
    Root->>ChildB: operation()
    activate ChildB
    ChildB-->>Root: resultB
    deactivate ChildB
    Root-->>Client: aggregatedResult
    deactivate Root
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This shows the **post-order** traversal that the pattern executes. The leaf returns its primitive value; composites aggregate child results before returning. The client sees only one `operation()` call regardless of internal structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Composite design pattern. State any two real-world examples where it is applicable.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern, 3 marks):**

The Composite Design Pattern is a *structural* pattern that composes objects into **tree structures** representing **part-whole hierarchies**, allowing clients to treat individual objects and groups of objects **uniformly** through a common interface. *(1 mark for definition)*

**Real-world examples:** *(½ mark each)*

1. **File System:** A `Folder` (composite) contains `File`s (leaves) and other `Folder`s. An operation like `getSize()` works uniformly on both.
2. **Organization Chart:** A `Manager` (composite) contains `Employee`s and other `Manager`s. The operation `getTotalSalary()` works uniformly.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the four participants of the Composite pattern and state the role of the `Composite` class.**

**Model Answer:**

The four participants are: *(½ mark each)*

1. **Component** — the abstract interface common to all objects in the composition.
2. **Leaf** — the primitive object with no children.
3. **Composite** — the container that holds child components and delegates operations to them.
4. **Client** — manipulates objects via the `Component` interface.

**Role of `Composite` class:** It stores child `Component` objects in a collection (e.g., `List<Component>`), implements child-management operations (`add`, `remove`, `getChild`), and in its `operation()` method, **recursively iterates** over its children, calling `operation()` on each, and **aggregates** the results. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1**

#### (a) Draw the UML class diagram of the Composite pattern. Explain the responsibilities of each participant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Component {
        <<interface>>
        +operation()
        +add(Component c)
        +remove(Component c)
        +getChild(int i) Component
    }
    class Leaf {
        +operation()
    }
    class Composite {
        -children : List~Component~
        +operation()
        +add(Component c)
        +remove(Component c)
        +getChild(int i) Component
    }

    Component <|.. Leaf
    Component <|.. Composite
    Composite "1" o-- "*" Component : contains
```

**Responsibilities:** *(1 mark each, plus 1 mark for neat diagram)*

- **Component:** Declares the common interface (e.g., `operation()`) and optionally a default implementation of child-management methods. It is the type the client uses.
- **Leaf:** Represents the end objects. Implements `operation()` with primitive behaviour. Has no children, so `add`/`remove` are typically no-ops.
- **Composite:** Stores a collection of child `Component`s. Implements `add`/`remove`/`getChild` to manage children. Its `operation()` recursively calls `operation()` on each child and aggregates the results.
- **Client (implicit):** Treats all objects uniformly through the `Component` interface; is unaware of leaf vs composite.

#### (b) Design a Composite structure for a "Drawing Application" where a drawing contains shapes (Circle, Rectangle) and groups of shapes. Show the structure diagram and write the algorithmic logic for a `draw()` operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Domain Mapping:**

| Domain Object | Pattern Role |
|---|---|
| `Graphic` (abstract) | `Component` |
| `Circle`, `Rectangle` | `Leaf` |
| `Picture` (group) | `Composite` |

**Structure Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Picture: Scene] --> C1[Circle: Sun]
    P --> PG[Picture: Sky]
    PG --> C2[Circle: Cloud1]
    PG --> C3[Circle: Cloud2]
    P --> R1[Rectangle: Ground]
```

**Algorithmic Logic for `draw()` (in pseudocode):**

```
CLASS Graphic            // Component
    ABSTRACT METHOD draw(canvas)

CLASS Circle EXTENDS Graphic     // Leaf
    PROPERTY centerX, centerY, radius
    METHOD draw(canvas)
        canvas.renderCircle(centerX, centerY, radius)

CLASS Rectangle EXTENDS Graphic  // Leaf
    PROPERTY x, y, width, height
    METHOD draw(canvas)
        canvas.renderRectangle(x, y, width, height)

CLASS Picture EXTENDS Graphic    // Composite
    PROPERTY children : LIST<Graphic> = []
    METHOD add(g : Graphic)
        children.append(g)
    METHOD remove(g : Graphic)
        children.remove(g)
    METHOD draw(canvas)
        FOR EACH g IN children
            g.draw(canvas)         // RECURSIVE CALL — uniform!
```

**Key Point:** The client invokes `picture.draw(canvas)` once; recursion handles every nested group. *(Mark allocation: 2 for diagram, 3 for structure, 2 for recursion explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

#### (a) Explain the concept of "uniformity" in the Composite pattern. Discuss the transparency vs safety trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Uniformity:** The Composite pattern's defining feature is that **client code cannot distinguish between a leaf and a composite**. A reference of type `Component` may, at runtime, point to either, and the client invokes the *same* set of methods (e.g., `operation()`, `add()`, `getChild()`) without conditional type checks. This uniformity is what makes the pattern elegant and extensible. *(2 marks)*

**Transparency Variant (Classic GoF):** All child-management methods (`add`, `remove`, `getChild`) are declared on the **Component** interface. The client can call them on any node. The advantage is **uniform API**; the disadvantage is that **leaves inherit meaningless methods** — calling `leaf.add(...)` is either a runtime error or a silent no-op. *(2½ marks)*

**Safety Variant:** Child-management methods are declared **only on the Composite** subclass. The client cannot accidentally call `add` on a leaf because the type system forbids it. The disadvantage is that **the client must use a `Composite` reference** to add/remove children, which partially breaks uniformity. *(2½ marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "the Composite pattern is uniform" without explaining **why uniformity matters** (polymorphic client code, Open/Closed Principle). Always tie your answer back to *what the uniformity achieves in design*.

#### (b) Consider a company organization with the following structure. Apply the Composite pattern to compute the **total salary** of the organization. Show the class design and the recursive computation. **(7 marks)**

**Organization Structure:**

```
                CEO
              /     \
           CTO      CFO
          /   \       \
       Dev1  Dev2   Accountant
```

**Salaries:** CEO = 100, CTO = 70, CFO = 80, Dev1 = 40, Dev2 = 50, Accountant = 60.

**Model Solution:**

**Class Design:**

```
CLASS Employee          // Component
    PROTECTED name : String
    PROTECTED salary : double
    ABSTRACT METHOD getTotalSalary() : double
    METHOD getName() : String
        RETURN name

CLASS IndividualEmployee EXTENDS Employee   // Leaf
    CONSTRUCTOR(name, salary)
        super.name = name; super.salary = salary
    METHOD getTotalSalary() : double
        RETURN salary                  // base case

CLASS Manager EXTENDS Employee           // Composite
    PRIVATE subordinates : LIST<Employee> = []
    METHOD addSubordinate(e : Employee)
        subordinates.add(e)
    METHOD getTotalSalary() : double
        total = salary                  // self
        FOR EACH e IN subordinates
            total = total + e.getTotalSalary()  // RECURSION
        RETURN total
```

**Recursive Computation (Tracing):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\text{Dev1}) &= 40 \\
T(\text{Dev2}) &= 50 \\
T(\text{CTO})   &= 70 + 40 + 50 = 160 \\
T(\text{Acct}) &= 60 \\
T(\text{CFO})   &= 80 + 60 = 140 \\
T(\text{CEO})   &= 100 + 160 + 140 = 400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total salary of the organization = **400 units**. *(Mark allocation: 2 for class design, 2 for recursion logic, 2 for trace, 1 for final answer)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Composite Pattern**

- ✅ Composite is a **Structural** design pattern from the **Gang-of-Four (GoF)** catalog.
- ✅ Its sole intent is to build **part-whole / tree hierarchies** where leaves and groups share a **common interface**.
- ✅ Four participants to memorize: **Component, Leaf, Composite, Client**.
- ✅ The `Composite` class stores children in a **collection (e.g., `List<Component>`)** — *aggregation*, not inheritance.
- ✅ Recursion is the engine: `operation()` on a composite calls `operation()` on each child and **aggregates** the results.
- ✅ Closed-form for summed operations: $T(v) = \sum_{u \in S_v} w(u)$.
- ✅ Time complexity of a full traversal = $O(N)$; space (recursion stack) = $O(h)$, worst-case $O(N)$ for a chain.
- ✅ **Transparency** = uniform API but meaningless leaf methods; **Safety** = type-safe but breaks uniformity.
- ✅ Classic examples: **File systems, GUI containers, organization charts, XML DOM, expression trees**.
- ✅ Top KTU board keywords to use in answers: *part-whole hierarchy*, *uniform treatment*, *recursive composition*, *polymorphic client*, *single responsibility*.
- ✅ Common mistakes to avoid:
  - Confusing Composite with Decorator (both are structural but Composite builds *trees*, Decorator adds *behaviour*).
  - Forgetting that the **client holds a `Component` reference**, not a `Leaf` or `Composite` reference.
  - Failing to mention **recursion** explicitly when explaining `Composite.operation()`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decorator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decorator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Decorator Pattern** is a **structural design pattern** (one of the Gang of Four — GoF patterns) that dynamically attaches additional responsibilities (behaviors) to an individual object at runtime, while providing a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality. It does this by *wrapping* the original object inside a special wrapper object that shares the same interface.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **GoF Canonical Definition (Gamma et al., 1994):**
> "Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality."

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Tag:** This topic is mapped to **CO2** (Apply structural design patterns to model real-world software architectures) under Module 2: *Structural Design Patterns*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **getting dressed for winter**:

1. You start with a base — your **body / inner clothing** (`ConcreteComponent`).
2. You wrap a **shirt** over it.
3. Then you wrap a **sweater** over the shirt.
4. Then a **jacket** over the sweater.
5. Finally, a **raincoat** for waterproofing.

Each layer is a **decorator**. Each layer:
- Covers what is beneath it.
- **Adds** a new feature (warmth, waterproofing, style).
- Does **not** alter the base body.
- Can be **added or removed dynamically** (you decide today's outfit at 7 AM, not at birth).

Now translate this to software: the base object is your core business object, and decorators are middleware layers (logging, encryption, compression, caching) — added on the fly, at runtime, without modifying the base class.

> [!TIP] > **One-liner Memory Hook:** "Decorators are **wrappers that add behavior**, layer by layer, like onions and winter clothing."

## 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Component** | The abstract interface common to both the original object and its decorators. |
| **ConcreteComponent** | The base object to which responsibilities are added. |
| **Decorator** | Abstract class that holds a reference to a `Component` and conforms to its interface. |
| **ConcreteDecorator** | A specific wrapper that adds new behavior before/after delegating to the wrapped object. |
| **Delegation** | The mechanism by which a decorator forwards calls to the wrapped object. |
| **Open/Closed Principle (OCP)** | The OOP principle that decorators uphold — open for extension, closed for modification. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" — Problem Statement

Suppose you are building a `Window` GUI class. Later, requirements arrive:

- Add a **scrollbar**.
- Add a **black border**.
- Add a **drop shadow**.

**Naïve Solution (subclassing explosion):**

$$N_{classes} \;=\; 2^{n}$$

where $n$ is the number of optional features. For just $n=3$ features, you need $2^{3}=8$ subclasses (`Window`, `WindowWithScrollbar`, `WindowWithBorder`, `WindowWithScrollbarAndBorder`, ...). For $n=10$, you get **1024 classes** — combinatorially explosive and unmaintainable.

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Decorator** with **Inheritance**. Decorator is preferred when features are *combinatorial* and *runtime-selectable*, not fixed at compile-time.

## 2.2 The "How" — The 4-Part Recipe

1. **Define a `Component` interface** — the common contract.
2. **Create a `ConcreteComponent`** — the base class implementing the interface.
3. **Create an abstract `Decorator`** — also implements `Component`; holds a reference to a `Component`.
4. **Create `ConcreteDecorator` subclasses** — each adds one specific feature, then calls the wrapped object's method via delegation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Element | Type | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Component` | Interface / Abstract Class | Common contract for wrappers and wrapped objects | `Shape` |
| `ConcreteComponent` | Concrete Class | The original object being decorated | `Circle` |
| `Decorator` | Abstract Class | Holds reference to a `Component`; implements/extends `Component` | `ShapeDecorator` |
| `ConcreteDecorator` | Concrete Class | Adds specific feature, delegates base call | `RedShapeDecorator` |

### Key Design Equations

The **number of classes** required to support $n$ independent features:

$$C_{decorator} \;=\; 1 \;+\; 1 \;+\; 1 \;+\; n \;=\; n + 3$$

Compare with the **subclassing explosion**:

$$C_{inheritance} \;=\; 2^{n}$$

### Boundary Conditions

- **Limit 1:** Decorators **must** share the same interface as the object they wrap (Liskov Substitution Principle).
- **Limit 2:** The order of decoration **matters** — e.g., `Encrypt(Compress(file))` ≠ `Compress(Encrypt(file))`.
- **Limit 3:** Decorators are **transparent** to the client — the client does not know it is dealing with a decorator.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Decorator |
|---|---|
| **Java I/O Streams** | `BufferedReader` decorates `FileReader`; `DataInputStream` decorates `InputStream`. |
| **Web Frameworks (Django, Express)** | Middleware functions wrap request/response handlers (logging, auth, CORS). |
| **GUI Toolkits (Java Swing, JavaFX)** | Border, scroll, shadow decorators on visual components. |
| **Distributed Systems** | `Retry` and `CircuitBreaker` decorators wrap RPC clients. |
| **Logging Frameworks** | MDC context decorators wrap log handlers. |
| **Cryptography Stacks** | Cipher chains — `AES(Compressed(Serialized(payload)))`. |

> [!NOTE] > **Industry Insight:** Java's `java.io` package is the **canonical textbook example** of Decorator. Every `FilterInputStream` subclass is a decorator. This is a guaranteed interview/KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Construction

Since this is a **structural design pattern** topic, the "derivation" here is the **step-by-step construction** of a working Decorator model, showing every transition and decision explicitly.

## 3.1 Worked Example: A `Shape` Decorator

**Scenario:** You have a `Shape` interface. You want to draw a `Circle` or `Rectangle`, and optionally decorate it with a **red border**.

### Step 1 — Define the `Component` Interface

```java
public interface Shape {
    void draw();
}
```

**Logic:** This is the common contract. Any class — base or decorator — must implement `draw()`.

### Step 2 — Implement the `ConcreteComponent`

```java
public class Circle implements Shape {
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }
}

public class Rectangle implements Shape {
    @Override
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Rectangle");
    }
}
```

**Logic:** These are the **base objects**. They are not aware of any decoration.

### Step 3 — Create the Abstract `Decorator`

```java
public abstract class ShapeDecorator implements Shape {
    protected Shape decoratedShape;   // HAS-A relationship (composition)

    public ShapeDecorator(Shape decoratedShape) {
        this.decoratedShape = decoratedShape;
    }

    public void draw() {
        decoratedShape.draw();   // Delegation
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The decorator:
- **Implements** `Shape` (so it *is-a* `Shape` from the client's view).
- **Holds** a `Shape` reference (so it *has-a* `Shape` it can wrap).
- **Delegates** the call to the wrapped object.

### Step 4 — Create the `ConcreteDecorator`

```java
public class RedShapeDecorator extends ShapeDecorator {
    public RedShapeDecorator(Shape decoratedShape) {
        super(decoratedShape);
    }

    @Override
    public void draw() {
        decoratedShape.draw();            // Delegate first
        setRedBorder(decoratedShape);     // Add behavior
    }

    private void setRedBorder(Shape shape) {
        System.out.println("Border Color: Red");
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The concrete decorator **adds** behavior *before* or *after* delegating. This is the heart of the pattern.

### Step 5 — Client Code (Usage)

```java
public class DecoratorPatternDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape circle = new Circle();
        Shape redCircle = new RedShapeDecorator(new Circle());
        Shape redRectangle = new RedShapeDecorator(new Rectangle());

        System.out.println("Normal Circle:");
        circle.draw();

        System.out.println("\nRed Circle:");
        redCircle.draw();

        System.out.println("\nRed Rectangle:");
        redRectangle.draw();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Normal Circle:
Drawing a Circle

Red Circle:
Drawing a Circle
Border Color: Red

Red Rectangle:
Drawing a Rectangle
Border Color: Red
```

## 3.2 Multi-Decorator Stacking (Layered Decoration)

You can stack decorators **infinitely**:

$$\text{Shape} \;=\; \text{DottedShapeDecorator}\bigl(\text{RedShapeDecorator}\bigl(\text{Circle}\bigr)\bigr)$$

The structural equation for a stack of $k$ decorators wrapping a component $C$:

$$D_{stack} \;=\; D_{k} \circ D_{k-1} \circ \cdots \circ D_{1} \circ C$$

where $\circ$ denotes **wrapping composition**. Each $D_i$ is an independent, reusable wrapper.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw or describe the **call chain** when a client invokes a method on the outermost decorator. The call flows **inward** (outermost → innermost), and the responses flow **outward** (innermost → outermost), with each decorator injecting its own behavior at the right moment.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Subclassing (Inheritance) | Decorator (Composition) |
|---|---|---|
| **Binding Time** | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| **Number of Classes for $n$ features** | $2^{n}$ (combinatorial) | $n+3$ (linear) |
| **Flexibility** | Low — features fixed at compile-time | High — features added/removed at runtime |
| **OCP Compliance** | Violated (must modify base class) | Fully compliant |
| **Object Identity** | Different subclass instances | Same object, wrapped differently |
| **Stackability** | Hard (cross-product hierarchy) | Native — decorators wrap decorators |
| **Debugging Complexity** | Low | Higher — call stack traversal is deeper |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UML Class Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class comp1 {
        <<interface>>
        +operation()
    }
    class concComp1 {
        +operation()
    }
    class decor1 {
        #component comp1
        +operation()
    }
    class concDecorA {
        +operation()
        +addedState
        +addedBehavior()
    }
    class concDecorB {
        +operation()
        +addedBehavior()
    }

    comp1 <|.. concComp1
    comp1 <|.. decor1
    decor1 <|-- concDecorA
    decor1 <|-- concDecorB
    decor1 o-- comp1 : wraps
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `comp1` (Component) is the interface.
- `concComp1` (ConcreteComponent) **implements** it.
- `decor1` (Decorator) **implements** it and **holds a reference** to a `comp1` (composition/diamond).
- `concDecorA` and `concDecorB` (ConcreteDecorators) **extend** `decor1`.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    client1[Client Code] --> call1[Invokes operation on outermost decorator]
    call1 --> decorA[Concrete Decorator A]
    decorA --> decorB[Concrete Decorator B]
    decorB --> decorC[Concrete Decorator C]
    decorC --> comp1[Concrete Component base operation]
    comp1 --> resp3[Response flows outward]
    resp3 --> decorC2[Decorator C adds behavior]
    decorC2 --> decorB2[Decorator B adds behavior]
    decorB2 --> decorA2[Decorator A adds behavior]
    decorA2 --> client1
```

**Reading the flow:** A single client call **cascades inward** through the decorator chain to the base component, then the responses **cascade outward**, with each decorator layer adding its own pre- or post-behavior.

## 4.3 Object Diagram — A Concrete Instantiation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    client1[Client] --> obj1[RedShapeDecorator]
    obj1 --> obj2[DottedShapeDecorator]
    obj2 --> obj3[Circle concrete component]
    obj1 -.adds.-> b1[Red border behavior]
    obj2 -.adds.-> b2[Dotted stroke behavior]
    obj3 -.base.-> b3[Circle draw behavior]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the Decorator design pattern. List any two real-world applications where it is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Decorator pattern** is a structural design pattern that allows behavior to be added to individual objects dynamically, without affecting the behavior of other objects of the same class, by wrapping them inside decorator objects. *(1 mark)*

It adheres to the **Open/Closed Principle** by extending functionality through composition rather than inheritance. *(1 mark)*

**Two real-world applications:** *(1 mark)*
1. **Java I/O streams** — `BufferedReader` decorates `FileReader` to add buffering.
2. **Web middleware** — Express.js / Django middleware wrap request handlers for logging, authentication, or CORS.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between the Decorator pattern and inheritance-based extension. State one disadvantage of the Decorator pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inheritance | Decorator |
|---|---|---|
| Binding time | Compile-time | Runtime |
| Number of classes | $2^{n}$ for $n$ features | $n + 3$ |
| Flexibility | Low | High |

*(2 marks for the comparison)*

**Disadvantage:** Decorator patterns produce many small classes, which can increase **debugging complexity** as the call chain becomes long and harder to trace. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **intent**, **structure**, and **participants** of the Decorator design pattern with a neat UML class diagram. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Intent:** To attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality. *(1 mark)*

**Structure:** A `Component` interface is implemented by both `ConcreteComponent` and `Decorator`. The `Decorator` abstract class holds a reference to a `Component` and forwards requests to it. Concrete decorators subclass `Decorator` and add behavior before/after delegating. *(3 marks)*

**Participants:** *(3 marks)*
- **Component** — common interface (e.g., `Shape`).
- **ConcreteComponent** — base class (e.g., `Circle`).
- **Decorator** — abstract wrapper holding a `Component` reference.
- **ConcreteDecorator** — adds specific behavior (e.g., `RedShapeDecorator`).

*(UML diagram: 1 mark — see Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

**Q.A (b)** Design a Decorator pattern for a `Coffee` ordering system where a `SimpleCoffee` (base cost ₹50) can be decorated with `Milk` (+₹20) and `Sugar` (+₹10). Show the output for a `Milk + Sugar + SimpleCoffee` order. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

```java
// Component
interface Coffee {
    double getCost();
    String getDescription();
}

// ConcreteComponent
class SimpleCoffee implements Coffee {
    public double getCost() { return 50; }
    public String getDescription() { return "Simple Coffee"; }
}

// Decorator
abstract class CoffeeDecorator implements Coffee {
    protected Coffee decoratedCoffee;
    public CoffeeDecorator(Coffee c) { this.decoratedCoffee = c; }
    public double getCost() { return decoratedCoffee.getCost(); }
    public String getDescription() { return decoratedCoffee.getDescription(); }
}

// ConcreteDecorator 1
class MilkDecorator extends CoffeeDecorator {
    public MilkDecorator(Coffee c) { super(c); }
    public double getCost() { return super.getCost() + 20; }
    public String getDescription() { return super.getDescription() + ", Milk"; }
}

// ConcreteDecorator 2
class SugarDecorator extends CoffeeDecorator {
    public SugarDecorator(Coffee c) { super(c); }
    public double getCost() { return super.getCost() + 10; }
    public String getDescription() { return super.getDescription() + ", Sugar"; }
}
```

**Client Code and Output:** *(3 marks)*

```java
public class CoffeeShop {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Coffee coffee = new SugarDecorator(
                           new MilkDecorator(
                               new SimpleCoffee()));
        System.out.println(coffee.getDescription()
                           + " = Rs." + coffee.getCost());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Simple Coffee, Milk, Sugar = Rs.80
```

**Cost Computation Trace:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{Total} \;=\; 50 \;+\; 20 \;+\; 10 \;=\; 80
$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q.B (a)** With reference to Java I/O streams, explain how the **Decorator pattern** is used in `java.io`. Identify the role of `FilterInputStream`, `BufferedInputStream`, and `DataInputStream`. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

In `java.io`, the abstract class `InputStream` plays the role of the **Component**. Specific streams like `FileInputStream` are **ConcreteComponents**. *(2 marks)*

The abstract class `FilterInputStream` plays the role of the **Decorator** — it holds a reference to an `InputStream` and delegates `read()` calls to it. *(2 marks)*

- `BufferedInputStream` is a **ConcreteDecorator** that adds buffering (caching blocks of data) to improve I/O performance. *(1 mark)*
- `DataInputStream` is a **ConcreteDecorator** that adds the ability to read primitive Java data types (`readInt()`, `readDouble()`) from the underlying byte stream. *(1 mark)*

**Example stacking:** *(1 mark)*

```java
DataInputStream dis = new DataInputStream(
    new BufferedInputStream(
        new FileInputStream("data.bin")));
```

This is **two layers of decoration** over a `FileInputStream` ConcreteComponent.

---

**Q.B (b)** Compare the **Decorator pattern** with the **Adapter pattern** in terms of intent, structure, and applicability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Decorator | Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| **Intent** | Add new behavior to an object dynamically. | Convert the interface of an existing class into another interface the client expects. |
| **Structure** | Wraps object with the **same** interface. | Wraps object with a **different** interface. |
| **Purpose** | Enhances functionality. | Bridges incompatible interfaces. |
| **Number of Wrappers** | Often multiple, stacked. | Usually a single wrapper. |
| **Client Awareness** | Client is unaware of decoration. | Client uses the adapter as if it were the target. |
| **OCP Compliance** | Fully compliant. | Partially — depends on adapter type. |
| **Use Case** | `BufferedReader(FileReader)` | `InputStreamReader(InputStream)` |

*(6 marks for comparison; 1 mark for a one-line distinguishing statement: "Decorator keeps the interface identical and adds behavior; Adapter changes the interface for compatibility.")*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Decorator with Adapter or Proxy** — Decorator **adds** behavior; Adapter **converts** interface; Proxy **controls access**. *(Lose 1–2 marks for vague definitions.)*
> 2. **Forgetting the HAS-A composition arrow in UML** — the decorator must hold a *reference* to the Component, not inherit from a concrete class. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Not showing the call-chain in execution** — Examiners expect to see the call going inward and behavior added outward. *(Lose 2 marks.)*
> 4. **Misspelling "delegation" as "delegacy" or "inheritance"** — these are different OOP concepts.
> 5. **Not mentioning Open/Closed Principle** — this is the *why* behind Decorator's existence.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Decorator = Structural Pattern** (GoF) that **adds behavior dynamically** via **wrapping**.
- [ ] **Four participants:** `Component`, `ConcreteComponent`, `Decorator`, `ConcreteDecorator`.
- [ ] **Decorator implements the Component interface AND holds a Component reference** (IS-A + HAS-A).
- [ ] **Delegation** is the core mechanism — the decorator forwards calls to the wrapped object.
- [ ] **Open/Closed Principle** is the design philosophy.
- [ ] **Class count formula:** $n + 3$ for $n$ features (vs. $2^{n}$ with inheritance).
- [ ] **Stackable:** Decorators wrap decorators — `D3(D2(D1(C)))`.
- [ ] **Order matters:** `Encrypt(Compress(x))` ≠ `Compress(Encrypt(x))`.
- [ ] **Java I/O** is the canonical example — `FilterInputStream` is the abstract Decorator.
- [ ] **Advantages:** flexible, OCP-compliant, runtime-configurable, no class explosion.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** many small classes, debugging harder, identical interface required.
- [ ] **Common confusion points:** Adapter (interface change), Proxy (access control), Composite (tree structure) — none of these *add* behavior the way Decorator does.
- [ ] **UML must show:** interface realization (dashed triangle) + composition (filled diamond) from Decorator to Component.
- [ ] **Client code is identical** whether calling a base or decorated object — that's the power of polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Façade
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Façade

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Façade Design Pattern** is a **structural design pattern** (one of the Gang-of-Four / GoF patterns) that provides a **unified, simplified, higher-level interface** to a set of interfaces in a complex subsystem. The Façade defines a single entry-point that makes the subsystem easier to use by hiding the intricate internal collaborations between dozens of subsystem classes behind one clean API.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *"Provide a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. Façade defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use."* — GoF Catalogue, used verbatim in KTU Module 2 (Structural Patterns).

The word **"Façade"** literally means the *front face* of a building — the part that the public sees. In the same way, the Façade class is the **front face of a subsystem** that the client sees and interacts with.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **5-star hotel front-desk concierge**:

- A guest (the *Client*) wants: a table at a restaurant, a cab to the airport, and a spa appointment.
- Instead of calling the restaurant, the cab company, and the spa directly (3 separate subsystem calls), the guest makes **ONE call to the concierge**.
- The **Concierge (Façade)** internally talks to all those departments (subsystem classes), coordinates them, and returns a single result to the guest.
- The guest does **not need to know** how the internal coordination works.

**Another analogy:** The **"Start" button on a car's dashboard**. One press starts the engine, checks oil, turns on lights, and engages climate control — but the driver just presses ONE button (the Façade) instead of operating 10 separate switches.

**Engineering Analogy:** When you boot your computer, you press the **power button** — internally, the BIOS, bootloader, kernel, drivers, and services all fire up in a complex sequence. The power button is the **Façade** for the entire boot subsystem.

### 1.3 Why Façade? — The Core Problem It Solves

> [!NOTE]
> **The Symptom (Before Façade):**
> - A client class becomes tightly coupled to **many** subsystem classes.
> - Business logic for *how* to coordinate the subsystem is **leaked into the client**.
> - Reusing the subsystem requires copy-pasting the same coordination code in every client.
> - The subsystem is hard to learn, test, and maintain.

**The Cure (After Façade):**
> - Client depends on **only one class** — the Façade.
> - All coordination logic lives inside the Façade.
> - Subsystem remains accessible (Façade does **not forbid** direct access; it just provides a *simpler* path).

### 1.4 KTU High-Yield Keywords (Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning in Façade Context |
|---|---|
| **Subsystem** | The set of related classes that together deliver a complex behaviour. |
| **Façade** | The single class that exposes a simplified API. |
| **Client** | The code/user that calls the Façade. |
| **Loose Coupling** | Client no longer needs to know subsystem internals. |
| **Layer of Abstraction** | Façade sits between the Client layer and Subsystem layer. |
| **Optional Additional Façade** | A subsystem can have more than one Façade (e.g., a "Beginner" Façade and an "Advanced" Façade). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Participants (The Four Actors)

A Façade pattern implementation has **three logical participants** (GoF lists them as four, but `Client` is the consumer, not part of the pattern structure):

1. **Façade** (Core Class)
   - Knows which subsystem classes are responsible for a request.
   - Delegates client requests to appropriate subsystem objects.
   - Performs minimal coordination logic (sequencing, retries, defaults).

2. **Subsystem Classes** (Workers)
   - Implement the actual functionality.
   - Have **no knowledge of the Façade** (they don't hold a reference to it).
   - They can still be used directly if a power-user needs fine-grained control.

3. **Client** (Caller)
   - Uses the Façade instead of the subsystem directly.
   - Sends requests to the Façade only.

4. *(Optional)* **Additional Façade** — to provide specialized views of the subsystem.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (How a Façade Call Flows)

- **Step 1 — Client Invocation:** The client calls `facade.operation()` (e.g., `homeTheater.watchMovie("Inception")`).
- **Step 2 — Façade Receives Request:** The Façade method is entered; the Façade does **not** contain the actual business logic.
- **Step 3 — Internal Coordination:** The Façade calls a *sequence* of subsystem methods in the correct order (e.g., `dvd.on() → projector.on() → amp.on() → lights.dim()`).
- **Step 4 — Subsystem Execution:** Each subsystem class performs its piece of work.
- **Step 5 — Result Aggregation:** The Façade may return a value to the client (or return `void`).
- **Step 6 — Optional Reverse Path:** For some operations (e.g., `watchMovie` end → `endMovie()`), the Façade cleans up by calling shutdown methods in *reverse order*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When you draw a sequence diagram for a Façade on the exam, always show **one arrow from Client to Façade**, then **multiple arrows from Façade to Subsystem objects**. Never show arrows from Client directly into the subsystem — that defeats the whole point.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Decision Sheet

| # | Design Question | Façade's Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | When should I use a Façade? | When a subsystem is **complex** (many classes) and clients need a **simple** view. |
| 2 | Does Façade hide the subsystem completely? | **No.** It just provides a simpler alternative path; direct access remains possible. |
| 3 | Is Façade a creational pattern? | **No**, it is a **structural** pattern (deals with class/object composition). |
| 4 | Can a subsystem have multiple Façades? | **Yes** — one general-purpose, others for specialized workflows. |
| 5 | Does the Façade know the subsystem? | **Yes**, it knows *which* classes to call. |
| 6 | Does the subsystem know the Façade? | **No**, it is **independent** (one-way coupling). |
| 7 | Does Façade promote tight or loose coupling? | **Loose** coupling between Client and Subsystem. |
| 8 | Façade vs. Adapter? | Façade = **simplifies** many classes into one. Adapter = makes **one** class fit an expected interface. |
| 9 | Façade vs. Mediator? | Façade = **one-way** (Client → Façade → Subsystem). Mediator = **multi-way** (colleagues ↔ Mediator ↔ colleagues). |
| 10 | Façade vs. Proxy? | Façade = no extra indirection, just simplification. Proxy = **same interface** as the real object, controls access. |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utilities

- **Compilers:** A `Compiler` Façade exposes `compile(sourceFile)` while internally invoking the Lexer, Parser, Optimizer, and Code-Generator.
- **JDBC / Database Libraries:** `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` form a subsystem; framework APIs (e.g., Spring's `JdbcTemplate`) act as a Façade.
- **Web Frameworks:** `Express.js`, `Spring Boot` controllers are Façades over HTTP server, routing, JSON serialization, etc.
- **Operating Systems:** The `System` class in Java is a Façade over `System.in`, `System.out`, `System.exit`, etc.
- **Home Theatre Systems:** The classic GoF textbook example (Projector + Amplifier + DVD + Lights + Popcorn).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to remember:** The Façade reduces the **public API surface** of a subsystem for *typical* clients. A typical GoF Façade cuts a subsystem of **N classes** down to **1 entry point** for the common case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Designing a Façade — Exhaustive Walk-Through (Home Theatre Example)

> This is the **canonical KTU-board-style problem**. Work through it exactly as you would in the exam.

**Step 1 — Identify the Subsystem and its classes**

We have a home-theatre system with five hardware devices:

$$
S = \{ \text{Amplifier},\ \text{DVDPlayer},\ \text{Projector},\ \text{TheatreLights},\ \text{PopcornPopper} \}
$$

So the subsystem size is $\vert S \vert = 5$.

**Step 2 — Identify the high-level use cases the Client wants**

$$
U = \{ \text{watchMovie},\ \text{endMovie},\ \text{listenToRadio},\ \text{shutdown} \}
$$

So the Façade needs $\vert U \vert = 4$ public methods.

**Step 3 — Coupling Reduction Analysis (Before vs After Façade)**

**Before Façade** — The client must hold references to *all five* subsystem objects and orchestrate them:

$$
C_{\text{before}} = \sum_{u \in U} \text{(number of subsystem calls inside } u \text{)}
$$

$$
C_{\text{before}} = 5 + 4 + 3 + 5 = 17
$$

The client makes **17 direct subsystem calls** across the four use cases.

**After Façade** — The client holds **one** reference (the Façade) and calls **one** method per use case:

$$
C_{\text{after}} = \vert U \vert = 4
$$

**Coupling Reduction Ratio** $\rho$ is defined as:

$$
\rho = \frac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}} = \frac{17 - 4}{17} = \frac{13}{17} \approx 0.7647
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho \approx 76.47\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Façade **eliminates roughly 76 % of the client's coupling** to the subsystem. This is a typical KTU-style quantitative justification you can drop in an exam answer for full marks.

**Step 4 — Internal coordination sequence for `watchMovie(movie)`**

The Façade internally fires this ordered call chain (a *Façade call graph*):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Façade.watchMovie}(m) \rightarrow\ & \text{PopcornPopper.on()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{PopcornPopper.pop()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{TheatreLights.dim(10\%)} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Projector.on()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Projector.wideScreenMode()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Amplifier.on()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Amplifier.setVolume(5)} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Amplifier.setSurroundSound()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{DVDPlayer.on()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{DVDPlayer.play}(m)
\end{aligned}
$$

Total: **10 subsystem method invocations** behind **1 Façade call**.

**Step 5 — Reverse sequence for `endMovie()`**

A good Façade also provides a clean *teardown*. The order is reversed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Façade.endMovie}() \rightarrow\ & \text{DVDPlayer.stop()} \rightarrow \text{DVDPlayer.off()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Amplifier.off()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{Projector.off()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{TheatreLights.on()} \\
\rightarrow\ & \text{PopcornPopper.off()}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Strengths vs Weaknesses (Common KTU 2-Mark Sub-Question)

| Dimension | Strength ✅ | Weakness ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Reduces client–subsystem coupling. | Subsystem still has many internal dependencies. |
| **Encapsulation** | Hides ordering, defaults, and sequencing details. | Does not truly *encapsulate* — direct access is still possible. |
| **Reusability** | Common workflows are reusable across clients. | Custom workflows may still need direct subsystem access. |
| **Testability** | Easy to test client against a mock Façade. | The Façade itself becomes a god-object if it grows too much. |
| **Performance** | No indirection overhead. | None significant. |
| **Maintainability** | Changes to subsystem order are localized. | Adding many Façade methods leads to a bloated class. |

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Façade vs Adapter vs Mediator vs Proxy (14-Mark Favourite)

| Pattern | Intent | Direction | Number of Classes Hidden | KTU 1-Line Memory Aid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Façade** | Simplify a complex subsystem | One-way (Client → Façade → Subsystem) | Many → 1 | "**One door to many rooms**." |
| **Adapter** | Convert one interface to another | One-way (Client → Adapter → Adaptee) | 1 | "**Plug converter**." |
| **Mediator** | Centralize colleague-to-colleague comms | Multi-way (Colleagues ↔ Mediator) | N → 1 | "**Air-traffic controller**." |
| **Proxy** | Control access to a single object | One-way (Client → Proxy → Real) | 1 | "**Stand-in bodyguard**." |
| **Decorator** | Add behaviour dynamically | One-way, recursive | 1 (wrapped) | "**Onion layers**." |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Façade Class Diagram (UML in Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Client {
        +main()
    }
    class HomeTheatreFacade {
        -amp : Amplifier
        -dvd : DVDPlayer
        -projector : Projector
        -lights : TheatreLights
        -popper : PopcornPopper
        +watchMovie(String)
        +endMovie()
        +listenToRadio(double)
        +shutdown()
    }
    class Amplifier {
        +on()
        +off()
        +setVolume(int)
        +setSurroundSound()
    }
    class DVDPlayer {
        +on()
        +off()
        +play(String)
        +stop()
    }
    class Projector {
        +on()
        +off()
        +wideScreenMode()
    }
    class TheatreLights {
        +on()
        +dim(int)
    }
    class PopcornPopper {
        +on()
        +off()
        +pop()
    }
    Client --> HomeTheatreFacade : delegates to
    HomeTheatreFacade --> Amplifier : controls
    HomeTheatreFacade --> DVDPlayer : controls
    HomeTheatreFacade --> Projector : controls
    HomeTheatreFacade --> TheatreLights : controls
    HomeTheatreFacade --> PopcornPopper : controls
```

### 4.2 Façade Sequence Diagram — `watchMovie("Inception")`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client
    participant F as HomeTheatreFacade
    participant P as PopcornPopper
    participant L as TheatreLights
    participant Pr as Projector
    participant A as Amplifier
    participant D as DVDPlayer
    C->>F: watchMovie("Inception")
    F->>P: on()
    F->>P: pop()
    F->>L: dim(10)
    F->>Pr: on()
    F->>Pr: wideScreenMode()
    F->>A: on()
    F->>A: setVolume(5)
    F->>A: setSurroundSound()
    F->>D: on()
    F->>D: play("Inception")
    F-->>C: Movie playing
```

### 4.3 Layered Architecture Block Diagram (Where Façade Fits)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Presentation Layer (UI / API Endpoints)"]
        UI["Client Application"]
    end
    subgraph L2["Façade Layer (Simplified Entry Point)"]
        F["HomeTheatreFacade\nwatchMovie() / endMovie()"]
    end
    subgraph L3["Subsystem Layer (Complex Internal APIs)"]
        S1["Amplifier"]
        S2["DVDPlayer"]
        S3["Projector"]
        S4["TheatreLights"]
        S5["PopcornPopper"]
    end
    UI -- one call --> F
    F -- many calls --> S1
    F -- many calls --> S2
    F -- many calls --> S3
    F -- many calls --> S4
    F -- many calls --> S5
    UI -. optional direct access .-> S1
    UI -. optional direct access .-> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrows from `Client` to `S1`/`S2` are the **optional advanced path**. Façade does **not** forbid direct subsystem use — it just discourages it for typical use cases. This is the single most-asked KTU theoretical point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define the Façade design pattern. List any two situations where you would prefer to use it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Façade pattern** is a *structural design pattern* that provides a **unified, simplified interface** to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. It hides the complexity of the subsystem behind a single higher-level class (the Façade), so the client depends on one class instead of many. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Two situations where Façade is preferred:** *(1 mark)*
> 1. When the subsystem is **complex** with many interdependent classes and the client only needs a subset of its functionality.
> 2. When there are **many dependencies** between clients and implementation classes, and you want to decouple the client from the subsystem to promote **layered architecture / loose coupling**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected QP]**
**Differentiate between Façade and Adapter design patterns in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Façade | Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simplifies a **complex subsystem** (many classes → 1 interface). | Converts the interface of **a single class** into another expected interface. |
| 2 | Used to **reduce coupling** and provide a clean layered view. | Used to make **incompatible interfaces work together** (legacy code reuse). |
| 3 | Optional — direct subsystem access is still possible. | Mandatory — clients always go through the Adapter. |

*(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for the final concluding sentence.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question has internal choice — the student answers **either OR**. Both options below carry full marks.

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model QP, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **intent, structure (with diagram), and participants** of the Façade design pattern. Illustrate with a real-world example of your choice.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Write down the **consequences (benefits and drawbacks)** of using the Façade pattern. Mention how it relates to the **Law of Demeter** (Principle of Least Knowledge).

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Intent, Structure, Participants** *(7 marks)*

**Intent:** The Façade pattern provides a **unified interface** to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. It defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem **easier to use**. *(1 mark)*

**Structure (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):** *(3 marks for diagram)*

**Participants:** *(3 marks)*
- **Façade** — Knows which subsystem classes handle a request; delegates work to them. E.g., `HomeTheatreFacade`.
- **Subsystem Classes** — Implement the actual functionality; have no knowledge of the Façade. E.g., `Amplifier`, `DVDPlayer`, `Projector`, `TheatreLights`, `PopcornPopper`.
- **Client** — Uses the Façade instead of the subsystem directly.

**Real-world example:** **Online Shopping Cart Checkout.** The `CheckoutFacade` exposes `checkout(cart, address, paymentInfo)`. Internally it talks to `InventoryService`, `PaymentGateway`, `ShippingService`, `InvoiceService`, and `EmailNotifier`. The user clicks one button; the system coordinates 5 subsystems. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Consequences and Law of Demeter** *(7 marks)*

**Benefits:** *(3 marks)*
1. **Shields clients from subsystem components**, reducing coupling and the number of objects the client must deal with.
2. Promotes **weak coupling** between the subsystem and its clients (subsystem classes don't know about the Façade).
3. **Layered architecture** support — Façade becomes the boundary between application logic and subsystem code.
4. Easy to test and refactor — clients can be unit-tested with a mock Façade.

**Drawbacks:** *(2 marks)*
1. A Façade can grow into a **god-object** coupled to every subsystem class.
2. It does not prevent advanced clients from accessing subsystem classes directly, which can lead to inconsistency.
3. Adding new workflow variants may require modifying the Façade (violates OCP if not designed well).

**Relation to Law of Demeter (Principle of Least Knowledge):** *(2 marks)*

> The Law of Demeter (LoD) states: *a method should only talk to its immediate friends, not to strangers.* A Façade is a **direct application of LoD** — instead of a client navigating through subsystem objects (`a.getB().getC().doX()`), the client calls a method on the Façade, and the Façade handles the chain internally. This reduces *train-wreck* code (`a.getB().getC()`) and makes the system easier to maintain.

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model QP, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* A banking application has subsystems for **Account Management, Loan Processing, Credit Check, and Notification**. Design a Façade class `BankServiceFacade` that provides unified methods `openAccount()`, `applyForLoan()`, and `getCreditScore()`. Draw the **class diagram** and explain.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare and contrast **Façade vs Mediator** and **Façade vs Proxy**. State one scenario each where Façade is preferred over them.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) BankServiceFacade Design** *(7 marks)*

**Class Diagram:** *(3 marks)*

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class BankClient {
        +main()
    }
    class BankServiceFacade {
        -accMgr : AccountManager
        -loanProc : LoanProcessor
        -credit : CreditCheckService
        -notif : NotificationService
        +openAccount(String, double)
        +applyForLoan(int, double)
        +getCreditScore(int)
    }
    class AccountManager {
        +createAccount()
        +validateKYC()
    }
    class LoanProcessor {
        +submitApplication()
        +checkEligibility()
    }
    class CreditCheckService {
        +fetchScore(int)
    }
    class NotificationService {
        +sendSMS()
        +sendEmail()
    }
    BankClient --> BankServiceFacade
    BankServiceFacade --> AccountManager
    BankServiceFacade --> LoanProcessor
    BankServiceFacade --> CreditCheckService
    BankServiceFacade --> NotificationService
```

**Explanation:** *(4 marks)*
- The `BankServiceFacade` consolidates four subsystem services behind **three** public methods.
- `openAccount(name, balance)` internally calls `AccountManager.createAccount()` → `AccountManager.validateKYC()` → `NotificationService.sendEmail("Welcome")`.
- `applyForLoan(custId, amount)` calls `CreditCheckService.fetchScore(custId)` → `LoanProcessor.checkEligibility()` → `LoanProcessor.submitApplication()` → `NotificationService.sendSMS()`.
- `getCreditScore(custId)` calls `CreditCheckService.fetchScore(custId)` and returns the value.
- The Client (UI or web app) does **not** know any of the four subsystem classes — a textbook Façade win.

---

**(b) Façade vs Mediator vs Proxy** *(7 marks)*

**Façade vs Mediator:** *(3.5 marks)*

| Aspect | Façade | Mediator |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | **One-way** (Client → Façade → Subsystem). | **Multi-way** (Colleague A ↔ Mediator ↔ Colleague B). |
| Goal | Simplify a subsystem. | Coordinate many-to-many interactions. |
| Awareness | Subsystem has no idea Façade exists. | Colleagues *do* know and reference the Mediator. |

**Façade preferred when:** Subsystem has a single "consumer face" — e.g., `Compiler` Façade vs the Mediator that would be needed if Lexer, Parser, and Optimizer all needed to talk to each other.

**Façade vs Proxy:** *(3.5 marks)*

| Aspect | Façade | Proxy |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Simplify usage of a complex subsystem. | Control access to a single real object. |
| Interface | May expose a **brand-new** simpler interface. | Usually exposes the **same** interface as the real object. |
| Lazy / Security | Not its concern. | Often used for lazy init, security checks, remote stubs. |

**Façade preferred when:** You want to give clients a cleaner API to a cluster of classes (e.g., `HomeTheatreFacade`), but you do not need to control access, cache, or virtualize a single object — those are Proxy's job.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Façade with Adapter.** Many students write "Façade converts interface" — that is **Adapter**. Façade's job is to **simplify**, not convert. *(−2 marks typical)*
> 2. **Saying "Façade hides the subsystem".** The GoF explicitly states Façade does *not* hide the subsystem; it only *simplifies access*. Subsystem classes remain publicly accessible. Examiners love to test this nuance. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Drawing the wrong coupling direction.** Always show arrows **Client → Façade → Subsystem**. Do *not* show Façade → Client or Subsystem → Façade. *(−2 marks on diagram question)*
> 4. **Forgetting the Law of Demeter link.** When asked about consequences, dropping a one-liner on LoD usually fetches the extra 1–2 marks that separate a 12 from a 14.
> 5. **Writing Java code when the question asks for a *diagram*.** Read the verb: "Draw / Illustrate" → diagram; "Implement / Code" → code. Mismatching loses 4–5 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Façade is a STRUCTURAL pattern** (along with Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Flyweight, Proxy).
- ✅ **Intent:** Provide a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. *Simplify, not hide.*
- ✅ **Three participants:** Façade, Subsystem Classes, Client.
- ✅ **One-way coupling:** Façade → Subsystem. Subsystem has **no knowledge** of Façade.
- ✅ **Law of Demeter** is the underlying OO principle.
- ✅ **Multiple Façades per subsystem** are allowed (Beginner / Advanced views).
- ✅ **Façade ≠ Adapter:** Adapter fixes *incompatible* interfaces (1 class); Façade simplifies *many* classes.
- ✅ **Façade ≠ Mediator:** Façade is one-way, Mediator is multi-way and *colleagues know* it.
- ✅ **Façade ≠ Proxy:** Proxy keeps the same interface and controls access; Façade reshapes the interface for simplicity.
- ✅ **Canonical example:** Home Theatre system; Compiler; Banking `BankServiceFacade`; `Compiler.compile()`.
- ✅ **Quantitative metric to quote in exams:** Coupling-reduction ratio $\rho = (C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}) / C_{\text{before}}$ — typically 70–80 % in textbook cases.
- ✅ **Exam-winning diagram:** Show Client → Façade (one arrow) and Façade → Subsystem (many arrows). Mention that direct Client → Subsystem is *optional* via dashed arrow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flyweight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flyweight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Flyweight Pattern** is a *structural design pattern* (one of the 23 Gang-of-Four / GoF patterns) whose primary intent is to **minimize memory usage and computational expense by sharing as much data as possible with similar objects**. It achieves this by extracting the *intrinsic* (shared, stateless) portion of an object's state and storing it in a shared flyweight object, while passing the *extrinsic* (context-specific, unique) state to the object on demand via parameters.

Formally stated in KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:

> *"Use sharing to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently."* — Gang of Four

The pattern divides object state into two categories:

- **Intrinsic State** — Stored *inside* the flyweight. It is **independent of the object's context**, hence **shareable** and **immutable**.
- **Extrinsic State** — Stored *outside* the flyweight. It **depends on the flyweight's context**, hence **unique per object** and **passed in by the client** when an operation is invoked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Flyweight pattern is a high-weightage topic in Module 2 (Software Design Patterns). Examiners routinely ask for the UML class diagram, identification of intrinsic vs. extrinsic state, and Java/C++ code snippets. Mastering this pattern is mandatory.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Library Card System

Imagine a large public library serving **10 lakh readers**.

Instead of giving each reader a thick, personalized book repository containing every possible book, the library operates on a far smarter model:

- The **books themselves** are **shared (intrinsic state)** — every reader who borrows *Wings of Fire* reads the same book.
- The **library card** held by each reader contains the **borrower's personal context** (name, ID, borrow date, due date) — this is the **extrinsic state**.

The library doesn't manufacture a new book for every reader. It **shares a single physical book copy**, and the per-reader details (extrinsic info) are maintained by the lending system. If 1,000 readers want the same book, the library still stocks only one book.

The **Flyweight pattern applies the exact same logic to software objects**:
- One shared object (flyweight) ↔ one book copy.
- The client supplies the unique context ↔ the library card and borrow details.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realization:** The object count problem in software is not "how do we create more objects" but rather "how do we **share** the common parts while still allowing individuality through external data."

### 1.3 Why Does This Pattern Exist? — The Problem It Solves

Consider a graphical editor that must render a forest with **1 million trees**. If every tree object stores:
- its species name (string, ~30 bytes),
- texture bitmap reference (~2 KB),
- mesh data (~5 KB),
- 3D coordinates, color tint, scale, etc.

Then memory consumption becomes catastrophic:

$$\text{Memory}_{\text{naive}} = N \times (\text{Intrinsic Size} + \text{Extrinsic Size})$$

$$\text{Memory}_{\text{naive}} = 10^6 \times (7.03 \text{ KB}) \approx 7.03 \text{ GB}$$

The Flyweight pattern reduces this to:

$$\text{Memory}_{\text{flyweight}} = K \times \text{Intrinsic Size} + N \times \text{Extrinsic Size}$$

where **K ≪ N** (only a few species exist, but millions of trees). This is called the **memory optimization ratio**.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Valuation Insight):** Examiners reward students who explicitly identify the *Object Count Problem* and quantify memory savings using the above formulas. Always state K and N clearly in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Participants / Structure of the Pattern

The Flyweight pattern has **five canonical participants** (per the GoF classification). KTU questions frequently test identification of these participants from a UML diagram.

| # | Participant | Role | Responsibility |
|---|-------------|------|----------------|
| 1 | **Flyweight (Interface/Abstract Class)** | Contract | Declares the interface through which flyweights receive extrinsic state and act upon it. |
| 2 | **ConcreteFlyweight** | Implementor | Implements the `Flyweight` interface. Stores **intrinsic state** only. Must be **shareable**. |
| 3 | **UnsharedConcreteFlyweight** | Optional variant | A non-shared concrete flyweight; useful when composite structures need both shared & unique nodes. |
| 4 | **FlyweightFactory** | Manager | Creates and manages flyweight objects. Ensures shareable flyweights are shared properly. Uses a **cache** (e.g., HashMap) keyed by intrinsic state. |
| 5 | **Client** | Consumer | Maintains a reference to flyweights; computes or stores **extrinsic state** and passes it to the flyweight on each call. |

### 2.2 Logical Operation Steps

The pattern operates in a well-defined sequence. The KTU valuation scheme awards full marks only when all of these steps are explicitly stated:

1. **State Partitioning** — The designer partitions the object's state into *intrinsic* (shared) and *extrinsic* (unique) components.
2. **Removal of Extrinsic State** — The intrinsic-only version of the class is created (`ConcreteFlyweight`). The class no longer holds any per-instance context fields.
3. **Factory Construction** — A `FlyweightFactory` is built that maintains a **pool/cache** of flyweight instances, typically using a hash-based key derived from the intrinsic state.
4. **Client-Side Context Storage** — The client (or a separate context class) stores the extrinsic state as plain data.
5. **Request Dispatch** — Whenever the client needs an operation, it **requests a flyweight from the factory** and **passes the extrinsic state as a parameter** to the flyweight's method.
6. **Lifecycle Management** — The factory ensures no duplicate flyweights are created for the same intrinsic state. Optionally, a **reference-counting cleanup** may evict unused flyweights.

### 2.3 Applicability — When Should You Use Flyweight?

Use Flyweight when **ALL** of the following are true:

- An application uses a **large number of objects**.
- **Storage cost is high** because of the sheer object count.
- **Most object state can be made extrinsic** (i.e., transferable outside the object).
- Once extrinsic state is removed, **many distinct objects can be replaced by relatively few shared objects**.
- The application **does not depend on object identity** — two equivalent objects are considered the same.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error (KTU):** Students often confuse Flyweight with **Singleton**. **Singleton ensures ONE instance of a class globally**, while **Flyweight allows MANY instances but shared intelligently by intrinsic key**. They are orthogonal concepts.

### 2.4 Consequences (Trade-offs)

| Advantage | Disadvantage |
|-----------|--------------|
| **Massive memory savings** when many similar objects exist. | **Computational overhead** is shifted to computation time — extrinsic state must be recomputed or passed on every call. |
| **Reduction in total object count** to manageable levels. | **Increased code complexity** — clients must distinguish intrinsic vs. extrinsic state. |
| **Centralized state management** via the factory cache. | **Slower runtime performance** if extrinsic state transfer becomes a bottleneck. |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| Naive memory cost | $M_{\text{naive}} = N \times (S_i + S_e)$ | $N$ objects, each with intrinsic size $S_i$ and extrinsic size $S_e$. |
| Flyweight memory cost | $M_{\text{fly}} = K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e$ | $K$ unique intrinsic states shared across $N$ objects. |
| Memory savings ratio | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e}{N \cdot (S_i + S_e)}$ | Fractional reduction in memory. |
| Simplified savings (when $S_e \to 0$) | $\eta \approx 1 - \dfrac{K}{N}$ | The intrinsic share dominates the savings. |
| Cache lookup (factory) | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ average (HashMap) | Factory operations are constant-time on average. |
| Space complexity of factory | $\mathcal{O}(K)$ | Factory stores one entry per unique intrinsic state. |
| Total object count after pattern | $K + N_{\text{unshared}}$ | Shared + unshared components. |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and set notations use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve table integrity, e.g., $\vert S_i \vert$ for size magnitude.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Flyweight pattern is heavily used in:

- **Compilers** — Sharing literal constants in the symbol table (every occurrence of `42` may map to the same `Integer` flyweight).
- **Text Editors** — Glyphs (characters) are shared; only positional/contextual data is extrinsic.
- **Game Engines** — Tree, grass, particle, and tile sprites are flyweights.
- **Database Connection Pools** — Reusing pre-built connections.
- **GUI Frameworks** — Common icons, fonts, brushes (e.g., Windows GDI brush pool).
- **Big Data / String Interning** — Java's `String` constant pool, Python's small integer cache `[-5, 256]`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Memory Optimization for a Forest

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):**

> A graphical application renders a forest.
