Each tree object has the following fields: species name (40 bytes), texture (2 KB), mesh data (5 KB), and three extrinsic fields — x-coordinate, y-coordinate, and color tint (12 bytes total). The forest contains **1,000,000 trees** of **only 12 unique species**.
>
> (a) Calculate the **memory cost** using a naive (no-pattern) approach.
> (b) Calculate the **memory cost** after applying the Flyweight pattern.
> (c) Compute the **memory savings ratio** $\eta$.

**Given:**

$$
N = 10^6 \text{ trees}, \quad K = 12 \text{ unique species}
$$

$$
S_i = 40 \text{ B} + 2048 \text{ B} + 5120 \text{ B} = 7208 \text{ B}
$$

$$
S_e = 12 \text{ B}
$$

#### Part (a) — Naive Memory Cost

$$
M_{\text{naive}} = N \times (S_i + S_e)
$$

$$
M_{\text{naive}} = 10^6 \times (7208 + 12)
$$

$$
M_{\text{naive}} = 10^6 \times 7220 \text{ B}
$$

$$
M_{\text{naive}} = 7.22 \times 10^9 \text{ B} = 7.22 \text{ GB}
$$

#### Part (b) — Flyweight Memory Cost

$$
M_{\text{fly}} = K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e
$$

$$
M_{\text{fly}} = 12 \times 7208 + 10^6 \times 12
$$

$$
M_{\text{fly}} = 86496 \text{ B} + 12 \times 10^6 \text{ B}
$$

$$
M_{\text{fly}} = 86496 + 12000000 = 12086496 \text{ B} \approx 12.09 \text{ MB}
$$

#### Part (c) — Memory Savings Ratio

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{M_{\text{fly}}}{M_{\text{naive}}}
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{12086496}{7220000000}
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - 0.001674 \approx 0.9983
$$

$$
\eta \approx 99.83\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The Flyweight pattern reduces memory consumption by **~99.83%**, taking the forest from **7.22 GB** down to **~12 MB** — a 600× reduction.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

Use this matrix to determine if Flyweight is the right choice for a given problem (commonly asked as a 7-mark question):

| Condition | Weight | If True → Strong Flyweight Candidate |
|-----------|--------|--------------------------------------|
| Object count $N$ is very high ($> 10^4$) | **High** | Memory pressure is severe. |
| Number of unique intrinsic states $K$ is small ($K \ll N$) | **High** | High sharing factor = high savings. |
| Intrinsic state size $S_i$ dominates extrinsic $S_e$ | **High** | Eliminates the bulk of the data. |
| Object identity is unimportant | **Medium** | Two equivalent objects can be treated as one. |
| Extrinsic state is easy to compute/pass | **Medium** | Avoids CPU bottleneck from state transfer. |
| Application is read-mostly (no mutation of intrinsic state) | **Medium** | Ensures thread-safety and shareability. |

### 3.3 Detailed Implementation Walkthrough (Java)

A KTU-favored question format is "Write a Java/C++ program demonstrating the Flyweight pattern." Below is a complete, evaluator-friendly implementation:

**Step 1 — Flyweight Interface**

```java
public interface TreeFlyweight {
    void render(int x, int y, String colorTint);   // extrinsic passed in
}
```

**Step 2 — Concrete Flyweight**

```java
public class ConcreteTree implements TreeFlyweight {
    private final String species;     // intrinsic
    private final byte[] texture;     // intrinsic
    private final byte[] mesh;        // intrinsic

    public ConcreteTree(String species, byte[] texture, byte[] mesh) {
        this.species = species;
        this.texture = texture;
        this.mesh    = mesh;
    }

    @Override
    public void render(int x, int y, String colorTint) {
        // x, y, colorTint are EXTRINSIC — passed in, not stored
        System.out.println("Drawing " + species + " at (" + x + "," + y + ") tint=" + colorTint);
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Flyweight Factory**

```java
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;

public class TreeFactory {
    private static final Map<String, TreeFlyweight> cache = new HashMap<>();

    public static TreeFlyweight getTree(String species) {
        if (!cache.containsKey(species)) {
            // Expensive intrinsic data created ONCE
            byte[] texture = new byte[2048];
            byte[] mesh    = new byte[5120];
            cache.put(species, new ConcreteTree(species, texture, mesh));
        }
        return cache.get(species);
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Client**

```java
public class ForestClient {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String[] speciesList = {"Oak", "Pine", "Maple"};
        for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) {
            String s = speciesList[i % 3];           // only 3 flyweights
            int x = i % 1920, y = i % 1080;          // extrinsic
            String tint = "rgb(120,200,80)";         // extrinsic
            TreeFactory.getTree(s).render(x, y, tint);
        }
    }
}
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Boosting Tip:** In your answer script, always draw a small **UML class diagram** beside the code listing. The factory pattern is universally tested in this combination.

### 3.4 Reference UML Class Diagram (Textual Representation)

```
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
| <<interface>>   |        | FlyweightFactory    |
|   Flyweight     |<>------| - cache: HashMap    |
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
| + operation(ex) |        | + getFlyweight(key) |
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
         ^                              ^
         |                              |
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
| ConcreteFlyweight|       |       Client        |
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
| - intrinsicState|        | - extrinsicState    |
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
| + operation(ex) |        | + doWork()          |
+-----------------+        +---------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Object Interaction Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application] --> B[Step 1: Maintain Extrinsic State]
    B --> C[Step 2: Request Flyweight from Factory]
    C --> D[Step 3: Factory Checks Internal Cache]
    D --> E{Is Flyweight Already Cached?}
    E --> F[Yes: Return Existing Shared Instance]
    E --> G[No: Create New Concrete Flyweight]
    G --> H[Step 4: Store New Flyweight in Cache]
    H --> I[Step 5: Return New Flyweight]
    F --> J[Step 6: Client Invokes operation extrinsic]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 7: Flyweight Renders Computes Using Intrinsic Extrinsic]
    K --> L[Step 8: Result Returned to Client]
    L --> M[Client Continues Loop Over Millions of Objects]
```

### 4.2 Memory Layout Comparison (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SubNaive["Naive Object Layout (N = 1,000,000)"]
        direction TB
        N1[Tree 1: 7.2 KB Total]
        N2[Tree 2: 7.2 KB Total]
        N3[Tree 3: 7.2 KB Total]
        NDots[... up to 1,000,000 trees ...]
        NN[Tree N: 7.2 KB Total]
    end

    subgraph SubFly["Flyweight Layout (K = 12)"]
        direction TB
        F1[Flyweight Oak: 7.2 KB Intrinsic]
        F2[Flyweight Pine: 7.2 KB Intrinsic]
        F3[Flyweight Maple: 7.2 KB Intrinsic]
        FDots[... 12 species total ...]
        C1[Client Context: 12 bytes x 1,000,000 = 12 MB]
    end
```

### 4.3 Class Relationship Architecture

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Flyweight {
        <<interface>>
        +operation(extrinsicState)
    }
    class ConcreteFlyweight {
        -intrinsicState
        +operation(extrinsicState)
    }
    class UnsharedConcreteFlyweight {
        -allState
        +operation(extrinsicState)
    }
    class FlyweightFactory {
        -cacheMap
        +getFlyweight(key)
        +getUnshared(key)
    }
    class Client {
        -extrinsicState
        +doWork()
    }

    Flyweight <|.. ConcreteFlyweight
    Flyweight <|.. UnsharedConcreteFlyweight
    FlyweightFactory --> ConcreteFlyweight : creates and caches
    FlyweightFactory --> UnsharedConcreteFlyweight : creates optionally
    Client --> FlyweightFactory : requests
    Client --> Flyweight : invokes with extrinsic
```

### 4.4 Sequence of Operations

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Factory
    participant FlyweightObj as ConcreteFlyweight
    Client->>Client: 1. Generate extrinsic context
    Client->>Factory: 2. getFlyweight(species)
    alt Cache miss
        Factory->>FlyweightObj: 3a. new ConcreteTree(species)
        Factory->>Factory: 4a. cache.put(species, instance)
    else Cache hit
        Factory->>Factory: 3b. lookup in cache
    end
    Factory-->>Client: 5. return flyweight reference
    Client->>FlyweightObj: 6. operation(x, y, color)
    FlyweightObj->>FlyweightObj: 7. combine intrinsic + extrinsic
    FlyweightObj-->>Client: 8. render output
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Flyweight pattern. Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic state with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Flyweight pattern** is a structural design pattern that uses object sharing to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently by minimizing memory consumption.

| Aspect | Intrinsic State | Extrinsic State |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Storage location** | Inside the flyweight object | Outside, in the client |
| **Shareability** | Shareable across instances | Unique per instance |
| **Mutability** | Immutable | Variable |
| **Example** | Species name, texture, mesh | x-coordinate, y-coordinate, color tint of a tree |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for example.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three participants of the Flyweight pattern and state their responsibilities.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Flyweight (Interface)** — Declares the interface through which flyweights can receive and act on extrinsic state *(1 mark)*.
2. **ConcreteFlyweight** — Implements the Flyweight interface and stores only the intrinsic (shareable) state *(1 mark)*.
3. **FlyweightFactory** — Creates, manages, and shares flyweight instances via an internal cache, ensuring no duplicates for the same intrinsic state *(1 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the Flyweight design pattern with its intent. Draw the UML class diagram showing all participants and their relationships. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a word processor that displays characters. Suppose there are **100,000 character objects** but only **96 unique glyphs** (ASCII printable characters). Each character has intrinsic data of **2 KB** (glyph bitmap) and extrinsic data of **8 bytes** (font, size, position). Calculate:
- (i) The memory used **without** the Flyweight pattern.
- (ii) The memory used **with** the Flyweight pattern.
- (iii) The percentage memory savings. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation & UML (7 marks):**

**Intent (1 mark):** Use sharing to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently.

**Explanation (3 marks):** The Flyweight pattern reduces memory by separating intrinsic state (shared) from extrinsic state (unique). The factory maintains a cache so that requests for the same intrinsic key return the same shared instance. The client holds the extrinsic state and passes it into the flyweight's operations.

**UML Class Diagram (3 marks):**

```
                +-------------------+
                |   <<interface>>   |
                |    Flyweight      |
                +-------------------+
                | +operation(ext)   |
                +-------------------+
                          ^
                          |
                +-------------------+
                | ConcreteFlyweight |
                +-------------------+
                | -intrinsicState   |
                +-------------------+
                | +operation(ext)   |
                +-------------------+

  +-------------------+         +-------------------+
  | FlyweightFactory  |         |      Client       |
  +-------------------+         +-------------------+
  | -cache: HashMap   |         | -extrinsicState   |
  +-------------------+         +-------------------+
  | +getFlyweight(k)  |         | +doWork()         |
  +-------------------+         +-------------------+
           |                              |
           v                              v
    (creates & caches)            (requests & uses)
```

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 100{,}000$, $K = 96$, $S_i = 2 \text{ KB} = 2048 \text{ B}$, $S_e = 8 \text{ B}$.

**(i) Without Flyweight (2 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{naive}} = N \times (S_i + S_e) = 100000 \times (2048 + 8)
$$

$$
M_{\text{naive}} = 100000 \times 2056 = 205600000 \text{ B} \approx 205.6 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) With Flyweight (2 marks):**

$$
M_{\text{fly}} = K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e = 96 \times 2048 + 100000 \times 8
$$

$$
M_{\text{fly}} = 196608 + 800000 = 996608 \text{ B} \approx 996.6 \text{ KB}
$$

**(iii) Savings Percentage (3 marks):**

$$
\eta = 1 - \dfrac{996608}{205600000} = 1 - 0.004847
$$

$$
\eta \approx 0.99515 \approx 99.52\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The Flyweight pattern reduces memory from **~205.6 MB** to **~1 MB**, achieving a **99.52% memory reduction** — a **~206× compression**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Flyweight** and **Singleton** design patterns. State at least four distinguishing points. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the Flyweight pattern for rendering trees in a forest. Identify clearly which fields are intrinsic and which are extrinsic. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Flyweight | Singleton |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Intent** | Share state across many similar objects | Ensure one global instance per class |
| **Number of instances** | Multiple (one per unique intrinsic state) | Exactly one per class loader |
| **Key mechanism** | Factory cache + state partitioning | Private constructor + static reference |
| **Object identity** | Equivalence is by intrinsic state | Single global identity |
| **Memory goal** | Reduce memory for *large object populations* | Control access to a *single shared resource* |
| **State mutability** | Intrinsic state is immutable | State may be mutable (e.g., global config) |
| **Client role** | Holds extrinsic state | Does not pass external context |
| **Example** | Tree species flyweight in a forest | Logger, configuration manager |

*(Allocate 1.5–2 marks per major point; 4 well-explained points = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Java Implementation (7 marks):**

*(See Section 3.3 above for the complete reference implementation.)*

**Key identification (1 mark):**
- **Intrinsic:** `species`, `texture`, `mesh` (in `ConcreteTree`).
- **Extrinsic:** `x`, `y`, `colorTint` (parameters to `render(...)`).
- **Factory role:** `TreeFactory.getTree(...)` (1 mark).
- **Client role:** `ForestClient.main(...)` maintains extrinsic state and calls factory (1 mark).
- **Code compiles and runs** (3 marks for correctness, indentation, comments).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Flyweight with Prototype or Singleton** — clearly state the difference in 1–2 lines.
> 2. **Forgetting to make intrinsic state `final`/`immutable`** — KTU examiners explicitly test immutability.
> 3. **Drawing UML without multiplicity or arrowheads** — Use open triangle for inheritance and dashed arrows for dependencies.
> 4. **Failing to show extrinsic state in the diagram** — Always include an `extrinsicState` parameter on the operation method.
> 5. **Skipping numerical units** in memory problems — always state *bytes / KB / MB* explicitly.
> 6. **Not addressing the factory's role** — Examiners expect explicit mention of caching, key-based lookup, and the `if (cache.containsKey(...))` logic.
> 7. **Writing code without comments** — Even 1–2 inline comments explaining intrinsic vs. extrinsic earns bonus perception marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print & Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Category:** Structural Design Pattern (GoF).
- ✅ **Intent:** Use sharing to support large numbers of fine-grained objects efficiently.
- ✅ **Key Mechanism:** Partition object state into *intrinsic* (shared) and *extrinsic* (context-specific).
- ✅ **Core Participants (5):** `Flyweight`, `ConcreteFlyweight`, `UnsharedConcreteFlyweight`, `FlyweightFactory`, `Client`.
- ✅ **Factory uses caching** (HashMap) keyed by intrinsic state — ensures sharing.
- ✅ **Intrinsic state must be IMMUTABLE** — this guarantees thread-safety and correctness when shared.
- ✅ **Extrinsic state is passed as method parameters**, not stored inside the flyweight.
- ✅ **Memory Formula:** $M_{\text{fly}} = K \cdot S_i + N \cdot S_e$.
- ✅ **Savings Formula:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{M_{\text{fly}}}{M_{\text{naive}}}$.
- ✅ **NOT a Singleton** — Flyweight allows *multiple* instances, one per unique intrinsic key.
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** String pools (Java), glyph caches in editors, sprite sharing in games, GDI brushes in Windows.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Memory savings come at the cost of *increased CPU usage* for extrinsic state transfer.
- ✅ **When NOT to use:** Small object counts, mutable intrinsic state, or when object identity is critical.
- ✅ **UML Tip:** Always mark intrinsic state with `-` (private final) and show extrinsic state as a method parameter.
- ✅ **Code Tip:** Use `containsKey` check before insertion in the factory; mark cache as `private static final`.
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** *"Share the common, pass the unique."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behavioral Design Pattern
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Behavioral Design Pattern

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioral Design Patterns** are a category of design patterns in software engineering (specifically within the **Gang of Four (GoF) classification**) that are concerned with **algorithms, the assignment of responsibilities between objects, and the communication patterns between them**. They characterize complex control flow that is difficult to follow at runtime, and shift the focus away from the flow of control to concentrate on **how objects are interconnected and how they communicate**.

According to the **GoF (Gang of Four) Catalog** (Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides - 1994), there are exactly **11 (Eleven)** Behavioral Design Patterns. Their primary purpose is to **decouple the sender of a request from its receiver**, thereby promoting **loose coupling**, **flexibility**, and **dynamic responsibility assignment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Examiners frequently test (a) pattern classification, (b) intent, (c) participants, and (d) applicability of these 11 patterns. Memorize all 11 names verbatim.

### 1.2 The 11 Canonical Behavioral Patterns (GoF Catalog)

| # | Pattern | One-Line Intent |
|---|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Chain of Responsibility (CoR)** | Pass a request along a chain of handlers. |
| 2 | **Command** | Encapsulate a request as an object. |
| 3 | **Interpreter** | Define a grammar and an interpreter for it. |
| 4 | **Iterator** | Access elements of an aggregate sequentially. |
| 5 | **Mediator** | Centralize complex communication between objects. |
| 6 | **Memento** | Capture and externalize an object's internal state. |
| 7 | **Observer** | Notify dependents automatically of state changes. |
| 8 | **State** | Alter behavior when internal state changes. |
| 9 | **Strategy** | Define a family of interchangeable algorithms. |
| 10 | **Template Method** | Defer exact steps of an algorithm to subclasses. |
| 11 | **Visitor** | Add new operations to objects without modifying them. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a busy international airport. Hundreds of pilots (objects) want to land, take off, or change altitude (requests). Instead of every pilot shouting instructions to every other pilot (tight coupling, chaos), all communication flows through the **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Tower**. The tower (the **Mediator**) decides who can do what and when. Pilots only listen to the tower, not to each other.

Similarly, in software:
- The **Tower** is the **Mediator** (centralizing communication).
- The **Pilots** are the **Colleagues** (decoupled objects).
- The **landing request** flowing through the system is the **Command/Message** object.
- A pilot watching the weather channel and adjusting flight plans = the **Observer** pattern.
- The plane's behavior changing from "Takeoff mode" to "Cruise mode" to "Landing mode" = the **State** pattern.

### 1.4 Why Are Behavioral Patterns Important in KTU?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Outcomes Addressed (KTU 2024):**
> - **CO2:** Apply design patterns to solve recurring software design problems.
> - **CO3:** Evaluate the trade-offs between competing design solutions.
> Behavioral patterns are the most heavily weighted topic in Module 2, typically worth **14 marks** in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of the 11 Behavioral Patterns

Behavioral patterns can be sub-classified by **WHAT they manage**:

> **Category A — Responsibility / Communication Patterns** (Who handles the request?)
> - **Chain of Responsibility**, **Mediator**, **Command**

> **Category B — State / Behavior Encapsulation Patterns** (How does behavior change?)
> - **State**, **Strategy**, **Template Method**

> **Category C — Object Traversal / Decoupling Patterns** (How do we navigate or extend?)
> - **Iterator**, **Visitor**, **Interpreter**

> **Category D — Notification / Snapshot Patterns** (How do we react or remember?)
> - **Observer**, **Memento**

---

### 2.2 Operational Deep-Dive: The "Big Five" High-Yield Patterns

#### 2.2.1 Strategy Pattern

**Intent:** Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable. Strategy lets the algorithm vary independently from clients that use it.

**Participants:**
1. **Strategy** — interface common to all supported algorithms.
2. **ConcreteStrategyA / ConcreteStrategyB** — implement specific algorithms.
3. **Context** — maintains a reference to a Strategy object; may define an interface for Strategy to access its data.

**Key Insight:** Eliminates conditional statements (no `if-else` chains for algorithm selection).

#### 2.2.2 Observer Pattern

**Intent:** Define a **one-to-many** dependency between objects so that when one object (the **Subject**) changes state, all its dependents (**Observers**) are notified automatically.

**Participants:**
1. **Subject** — knows its observers; provides attach/detach interface.
2. **Observer** — interface for objects that should be notified.
3. **ConcreteSubject** — stores state of interest; sends notification.
4. **ConcreteObserver** — maintains a reference to ConcreteSubject; implements the update interface.

**Key Insight:** Foundation of the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** and event-driven GUIs (e.g., Java Swing `ActionListener`, JavaScript `addEventListener`).

#### 2.2.3 Command Pattern

**Intent:** Encapsulate a request as an object, thereby allowing parameterization of clients with different requests, queue or log requests, and support **undo/redo** operations.

**Participants:** `Command`, `ConcreteCommand`, `Invoker`, `Receiver`, `Client`.

**Key Insight:** Decouples the object that invokes the operation from the one that knows how to perform it.

#### 2.2.4 State Pattern

**Intent:** Allow an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes. The object will appear to **change its class**.

**Participants:** `Context`, `State` (interface), `ConcreteStateA`, `ConcreteStateB`, ...

**Key Insight:** Eliminates massive `switch` statements by delegating state-specific behavior to dedicated state classes.

#### 2.2.5 Mediator Pattern

**Intent:** Define an object that encapsulates how a set of objects interact. Mediator promotes **loose coupling** by keeping objects from referring to each other explicitly.

**Participants:** `Mediator`, `ConcreteMediator`, `Colleague` classes.

**Key Insight:** The "Air Traffic Tower" pattern. Often contrasted with **Facade** (Facade is unidirectional; Mediator is bidirectional).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Pattern Name | Core Problem Solved | Key Participants | When to Apply (KTU Trigger Phrases) |
|--------------|--------------------|------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Chain of Responsibility** | Multiple handlers for a request | Handler, ConcreteHandler | "Pass request along chain", "Multiple objects may handle" |
| **Command** | Decouple invoker from receiver | Command, Invoker, Receiver | "Undo/Redo", "Queue requests", "Macro recording" |
| **Interpreter** | Implement a custom language | AbstractExpression, TerminalExpression | "Grammar/parsing of language", "Domain-specific language" |
| **Iterator** | Sequential access without exposing structure | Iterator, Aggregate, ConcreteIterator | "Traverse collection", "Hide internal representation" |
| **Mediator** | Reduce many-to-many object coupling | Mediator, Colleague | "Centralize communication", "Many-to-many complexity" |
| **Memento** | Save/restore object state | Originator, Memento, Caretaker | "Undo", "Snapshot", "Rollback" |
| **Observer** | One-to-many state change notification | Subject, Observer, ConcreteObserver | "Event listeners", "Publish-Subscribe", "MVC" |
| **State** | Behavior depends on state | Context, State, ConcreteState | "Finite state machine", "Replace switch statements" |
| **Strategy** | Select algorithm at runtime | Strategy, ConcreteStrategy, Context | "Family of algorithms", "Replace conditionals" |
| **Template Method** | Skeleton with customizable steps | AbstractClass, ConcreteClass | "Framework hooks", "Inversion of Control" |
| **Visitor** | Add operations without modifying classes | Visitor, ConcreteVisitor, Element, ObjectStructure | "Operations on object structure", "Compiler AST" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Pattern names in KTU papers are often phrased as descriptions. Memorize the **trigger phrases** in the right column above — they directly map to exam questions.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these patterns show up in production systems:**
> - **Java Spring Framework:** Uses **Observer** (`ApplicationEvent`), **Template Method** (`JdbcTemplate`), **Strategy** (`PlatformTransactionManager`).
> - **Compilers (GCC, LLVM):** Built on **Visitor** (AST traversal), **Interpreter** (expression evaluation).
> - **GUI Toolkits (JavaFX, React, Angular):** Built on **Observer**, **Mediator**, **Command**.
> - **Version Control (Git internals):** Uses **Memento** for snapshots, **Command** for `git checkout`.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Strategy Pattern (Full Java Implementation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A navigation app needs to compute a route using one of three strategies — *Driving*, *Walking*, or *Public Transit*. The choice must be made at runtime.

#### Step 1: Identify the **Context**

The `NavigationApp` (the Context) needs to compute a route. The exact algorithm (driving/walking/transit) is what varies.

#### Step 2: Define the **Strategy Interface**

```java
// Step 2: Strategy interface
public interface RouteStrategy {
    String buildRoute(String origin, String destination);
}
```

**Logic:** Any algorithm that can build a route must implement this contract. The interface is the **abstraction** layer.

#### Step 3: Implement **Concrete Strategies**

```java
public class DrivingStrategy implements RouteStrategy {
    @Override
    public String buildRoute(String origin, String destination) {
        return "Driving route from " + origin + " to " + destination
               + " via NH-66 (45 km, ~1 hr)";
    }
}

public class WalkingStrategy implements RouteStrategy {
    @Override
    public String buildRoute(String origin, String destination) {
        return "Walking route from " + origin + " to " + destination
               + " via footpath (5 km, ~1 hr)";
    }
}

public class PublicTransitStrategy implements RouteStrategy {
    @Override
    public String buildRoute(String origin, String destination) {
        return "Transit route: Bus 7 -> Metro Red Line"
               + " from " + origin + " to " + destination;
    }
}
```

**Logic:** Each strategy encapsulates **one** algorithm. They are interchangeable because they all honor the `RouteStrategy` contract.

#### Step 4: Define the **Context**

```java
public class NavigationApp {
    private RouteStrategy strategy;   // composition, not inheritance

    public void setStrategy(RouteStrategy strategy) {
        this.strategy = strategy;     // injected at runtime
    }

    public String navigate(String origin, String destination) {
        // Delegates to the strategy object
        return strategy.buildRoute(origin, destination);
    }
}
```

**Logic:** The Context **HAS-A** Strategy (composition). The algorithm is selected by client code (e.g., a UI button click) and **delegated** to the strategy object.

#### Step 5: Client Code (Runtime Selection)

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NavigationApp app = new NavigationApp();

        // User picks "Driving" from the UI
        app.setStrategy(new DrivingStrategy());
        System.out.println(app.navigate("Kochi", "Trivandrum"));

        // User changes mind to "Public Transit"
        app.setStrategy(new PublicTransitStrategy());
        System.out.println(app.navigate("Kochi", "Trivandrum"));
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Driving route from Kochi to Trivandrum via NH-66 (45 km, ~1 hr)
Transit route: Bus 7 -> Metro Red Line from Kochi to Trivandrum
```

#### Step 6: Analytical Conclusion (Why is this a Strategy Pattern?)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **if-else conditional** that would normally be inside `navigate()` has been **pushed out** to the client. The Context remains **closed for modification but open for extension** — this is the **Open/Closed Principle** of SOLID. Adding a new `BicycleStrategy` requires no change to `NavigationApp`.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Observer Pattern (Java Implementation)

#### Step 1: Define the **Observer Interface**

```java
public interface Observer {
    void update(String news);    // called by Subject
}
```

#### Step 2: Define the **Subject Interface**

```java
public interface Subject {
    void attach(Observer o);     // subscribe
    void detach(Observer o);     // unsubscribe
    void notifyObservers();      // broadcast
}
```

#### Step 3: Implement **ConcreteSubject**

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class NewsAgency implements Subject {
    private List<Observer> observers = new ArrayList<>();
    private String latestNews;

    public void setNews(String news) {
        this.latestNews = news;
        notifyObservers();           // automatic push
    }

    @Override public void attach(Observer o) { observers.add(o); }
    @Override public void detach(Observer o) { observers.remove(o); }

    @Override
    public void notifyObservers() {
        for (Observer o : observers) {
            o.update(latestNews);    // pull/push
        }
    }
}
```

#### Step 4: Implement **ConcreteObserver**

```java
public class NewsChannel implements Observer {
    private String name;
    public NewsChannel(String name) { this.name = name; }

    @Override
    public void update(String news) {
        System.out.println(name + " received: " + news);
    }
}
```

#### Step 5: Client Demo

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NewsAgency agency = new NewsAgency();
        Observer cnn = new NewsChannel("CNN");
        Observer bbc = new NewsChannel("BBC");
        agency.attach(cnn);
        agency.attach(bbc);
        agency.setNews("KTU results announced!");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
CNN received: KTU results announced!
BBC received: KTU results announced!
```

**Analysis:** `setNews()` triggers an automatic fan-out to all subscribers. The Subject **does not know** what the Observers do with the news — perfect decoupling.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pattern Selection Decision Tree

> [!TIP]
> **Use this decision tree for KTU exam design questions:**

```
START: What is your design problem?
│
├── Multiple objects might handle a request, OR
│   the handler is unknown in advance
│   └──> CHAIN OF RESPONSIBILITY
│
├── Need to parameterize objects with an action,
│   queue requests, or support UNDO
│   └──> COMMAND
│
├── Object behavior should change when its
│   INTERNAL STATE changes (FSM)
│   └──> STATE
│
├── Need a family of interchangeable ALGORITHMS
│   selected at runtime
│   └──> STRATEGY
│
├── Need to notify an UNKNOWN number of
│   dependents of state changes
│   └──> OBSERVER
│
├── Many-to-many object interaction is becoming
│   spaghetti
│   └──> MEDIATOR
│
├── Need to traverse a collection WITHOUT
│   exposing its internal structure
│   └──> ITERATOR
│
├── Need to add NEW OPERATIONS to existing classes
│   WITHOUT modifying them
│   └──> VISITOR
│
├── Need to define a SKELETON algorithm with
│   customizable steps in subclasses
│   └──> TEMPLATE METHOD
│
├── Need to SAVE and RESTORE object state
│   (undo, snapshot, rollback)
│   └──> MEMENTO
│
└── Need to implement a CUSTOM LANGUAGE or
    grammar
    └──> INTERPRETER
```

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Strategy Pattern

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Context {
        -strategy
        +setStrategy(s)
        +executeAlgorithm()
    }
    class Strategy {
        <<interface>>
        +algorithmInterface()
    }
    class ConcreteStrategyA {
        +algorithmInterface()
    }
    class ConcreteStrategyB {
        +algorithmInterface()
    }
    class ConcreteStrategyC {
        +algorithmInterface()
    }
    Context --> Strategy : holds reference
    Strategy <|.. ConcreteStrategyA
    Strategy <|.. ConcreteStrategyB
    Strategy <|.. ConcreteStrategyC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Observer Pattern

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant Subject
    participant Observer1
    participant Observer2
    Client->>Subject: attach(Observer1)
    Client->>Subject: attach(Observer2)
    Client->>Subject: setState(NEW_VALUE)
    Subject->>Subject: state changed
    Subject->>Observer1: update(NEW_VALUE)
    Subject->>Observer2: update(NEW_VALUE)
    Observer1-->>Client: render UI
    Observer2-->>Client: render UI
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Class Diagram — State Pattern

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Context {
        -state
        +request()
        +setState()
    }
    class State {
        <<interface>>
        +handle()
    }
    class ConcreteStateA {
        +handle()
    }
    class ConcreteStateB {
        +handle()
    }
    class ConcreteStateC {
        +handle()
    }
    Context --> State : current
    State <|.. ConcreteStateA
    State <|.. ConcreteStateB
    State <|.. ConcreteStateC
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mediator Pattern (Air Traffic Tower)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Colleagues
        A[Pilot Alpha]
        B[Pilot Bravo]
        C[Pilot Charlie]
        D[Pilot Delta]
    end
    M((Air Traffic Tower\nMEDIATOR))
    A -->|request landing| M
    B -->|request takeoff| M
    C -->|report position| M
    D -->|altitude change| M
    M -->|grant clearance| A
    M -->|hold pattern| B
    M -->|descent order| C
    M -->|climb order| D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** The arrows are **bidirectional** through the Mediator, and there are **no arrows directly between Pilots**. This is the visual signature of the Mediator pattern.

### 4.5 Mermaid Class Diagram — Command Pattern (with Undo Support)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Invoker {
        -command
        +setCommand()
        +executeCommand()
        +undoCommand()
    }
    class Command {
        <<interface>>
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class ConcreteCommand {
        -receiver
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class Receiver {
        +action()
    }
    Invoker --> Command
    Command <|.. ConcreteCommand
    ConcreteCommand --> Receiver
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Chain of Responsibility

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    participant H1 as Handler 1
    participant H2 as Handler 2
    participant H3 as Handler 3
    Client->>H1: sendRequest(REQ_100)
    H1->>H1: canHandle(100)? NO
    H1->>H2: forwardRequest(REQ_100)
    H2->>H2: canHandle(100)? NO
    H2->>H3: forwardRequest(REQ_100)
    H3->>H3: canHandle(100)? YES
    H3-->>Client: processRequest(REQ_100)
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Strategy design pattern. List any two situations where it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Strategy** design pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. It allows the algorithm to vary independently from the clients that use it.

**Two Applicability Situations (any two of the following):**
1. When many related classes differ only in their behavior — Strategy provides a way to configure a class with one of many behaviors.
2. When you need different variants of an algorithm — e.g., different sorting algorithms (QuickSort, MergeSort, HeapSort) selected at runtime.
3. When an algorithm uses data that the client should not know about — Strategy avoids exposing complex, algorithm-specific data structures.
4. When a class defines many behaviors via conditional statements — Strategy replaces conditional branching with delegation to strategy objects.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Mediator and Facade design patterns.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mediator | Facade |
|--------|----------|--------|
| **Intent** | Centralize communication between many objects | Provide a unified interface to a subsystem |
| **Direction** | Bidirectional (Colleagues ↔ Mediator) | Unidirectional (Client → Facade → Subsystems) |
| **Coupling** | Colleagues know only the Mediator | Subsystems remain independent of Facade |
| **Use Case** | Many-to-many object interaction | Simplifying a complex subsystem |
| **GoF Type** | Behavioral | Structural |

> **Key takeaway:** A Mediator is a **behavioral** pattern that lets objects **talk to each other through a hub**, while Facade is a **structural** pattern that simply **hides complexity** behind a clean interface.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ➤ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**Q. (a) Explain the Observer design pattern with a neat UML class diagram. Describe its participants, applicability, and consequences. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
> The **Observer** pattern defines a **one-to-many dependency** between objects such that when one object (the **Subject**) changes state, all of its dependents (**Observers**) are notified and updated automatically.

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Subject {
        <<interface>>
        +attach(Observer)
        +detach(Observer)
        +notify()
    }
    class Observer {
        <<interface>>
        +update()
    }
    class ConcreteSubject {
        -state
        +getState()
        +setState()
        +notify()
    }
    class ConcreteObserver {
        -subjectRef
        +update()
    }
    Subject <|.. ConcreteSubject
    Observer <|.. ConcreteObserver
    ConcreteSubject --> Observer : notifies
    ConcreteObserver --> ConcreteSubject : observes
```

**Participants:**
1. **Subject (Interface):** Provides `attach()`, `detach()`, and `notifyObservers()` methods.
2. **Observer (Interface):** Defines the `update()` method called when state changes.
3. **ConcreteSubject:** Stores the actual state; calls `notifyObservers()` on state change.
4. **ConcreteObserver:** Holds a reference to the ConcreteSubject; implements `update()` to react.

**Applicability (when to use):**
1. When an abstraction has two aspects, one dependent on the other — e.g., Model-View separation.
2. When a change to one object requires changing others, and you don't know how many objects need to change.
3. When an object should notify other objects without making assumptions about who these objects are.

**Consequences:**
- **Pros:** Abstract coupling between Subject and Observer; support for broadcast communication; dynamic registration of observers.
- **Cons:** Unexpected updates; memory leaks if observers aren't detached properly; cascading update overhead.

---

**Q. (b) Design and implement a Command pattern to support UNDO/REDO operations for a Text Editor that supports INSERT and DELETE operations. Provide the class structure and a sample Java implementation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Class Structure:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Editor {
        -text
        +insert(s, pos)
        +delete(pos, len)
    }
    class Command {
        <<interface>>
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class InsertCommand {
        -editor
        -text
        -pos
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class DeleteCommand {
        -editor
        -pos
        -len
        -deletedText
        +execute()
        +undo()
    }
    class Invoker {
        -history
        -redoStack
        +executeCommand(c)
        +undo()
        +redo()
    }
    Command <|.. InsertCommand
    Command <|.. DeleteCommand
    InsertCommand --> Editor
    DeleteCommand --> Editor
    Invoker --> Command
```

**Java Implementation:**

```java
// Receiver
class Editor {
    private StringBuilder text = new StringBuilder();
    public void insert(String s, int pos) { text.insert(pos, s); }
    public String delete(int pos, int len) {
        String removed = text.substring(pos, pos + len);
        text.delete(pos, pos + len);
        return removed;
    }
    public String getText() { return text.toString(); }
}

// Command interface
interface Command {
    void execute();
    void undo();
}

// Concrete Commands
class InsertCommand implements Command {
    private Editor editor;
    private String text;
    private int pos;
    public InsertCommand(Editor e, String t, int p) {
        this.editor = e; this.text = t; this.pos = p;
    }
    public void execute() { editor.insert(text, pos); }
    public void undo()   { editor.delete(pos, text.length()); }
}

class DeleteCommand implements Command {
    private Editor editor;
    private int pos, len;
    private String deleted;
    public DeleteCommand(Editor e, int p, int l) {
        this.editor = e; this.pos = p; this.len = l;
    }
    public void execute() { deleted = editor.delete(pos, len); }
    public void undo()    { editor.insert(deleted, pos); }
}

// Invoker with UNDO/REDO stacks
import java.util.Stack;
class Invoker {
    private Stack<Command> undoStack = new Stack<>();
    private Stack<Command> redoStack = new Stack<>();

    public void executeCommand(Command c) {
        c.execute();
        undoStack.push(c);
        redoStack.clear();          // new action invalidates redo
    }
    public void undo() {
        if (!undoStack.isEmpty()) {
            Command c = undoStack.pop();
            c.undo();
            redoStack.push(c);
        }
    }
    public void redo() {
        if (!redoStack.isEmpty()) {
            Command c = redoStack.pop();
            c.execute();
            undoStack.push(c);
        }
    }
}
```

**Client Test:**
```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Editor editor = new Editor();
        Invoker invoker = new Invoker();
        invoker.executeCommand(new InsertCommand(editor, "Hello ", 0));
        invoker.executeCommand(new InsertCommand(editor, "World", 6));
        System.out.println(editor.getText());   // Hello World
        invoker.undo();
        System.out.println(editor.getText());   // Hello 
        invoker.redo();
        System.out.println(editor.getText());   // Hello World
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **store the deleted text in a private field** inside `DeleteCommand` (line: `private String deleted;`). Without this, **UNDO cannot restore the original text**, and you lose 2 marks. The deleted content must be **remembered by the Command object itself**, not by querying the receiver again.

---

### ➤ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**Q. (a) Explain the State design pattern. How does it differ from the Strategy pattern? Illustrate with an example of a TCP connection that transitions through LISTENING, ESTABLISHED, and CLOSED states. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**State Pattern Definition:**
> The **State** pattern allows an object to **alter its behavior when its internal state changes**. The object will appear to change its class. It encapsulates state-specific behavior into separate state classes and delegates to the current state object.

**Comparison: State vs. Strategy**

| Aspect | State | Strategy |
|--------|-------|----------|
| **Intent** | Behavior changes with **internal state** | Algorithm is **selected by client** at runtime |
| **Transitions** | States know about each other (self-transitions) | Strategies are independent of each other |
| **Who decides** | The **Context** + **State** objects decide transitions | The **Client** decides which strategy to inject |
| **Typical Use** | Finite State Machines (TCP, Vending Machine) | Algorithm families (Sorting, Compression) |

**Java Implementation — TCP Connection:**

```java
// State interface
interface TCPState {
    void open(TCPConnection c);
    void close(TCPConnection c);
    void acknowledge(TCPConnection c);
}

// Context
class TCPConnection {
    private TCPState state;
    public TCPConnection() { state = new Listening(); }
    public void setState(TCPState s) { this.state = s; }
    public void open()        { state.open(this); }
    public void close()       { state.close(this); }
    public void acknowledge() { state.acknowledge(this); }
}

// Concrete States
class Listening implements TCPState {
    public void open(TCPConnection c)        { System.out.println("Established"); c.setState(new Established()); }
    public void close(TCPConnection c)       { System.out.println("Closed"); c.setState(new Closed()); }
    public void acknowledge(TCPConnection c) { System.out.println("ACK ignored in LISTENING"); }
}

class Established implements TCPState {
    public void open(TCPConnection c)        { System.out.println("Already open"); }
    public void close(TCPConnection c)       { System.out.println("Closing..."); c.setState(new Closed()); }
    public void acknowledge(TCPConnection c) { System.out.println("ACK received"); }
}

class Closed implements TCPState {
    public void open(TCPConnection c)        { System.out.println("Reopening -> Listening"); c.setState(new Listening()); }
    public void close(TCPConnection c)       { System.out.println("Already closed"); }
    public void acknowledge(TCPConnection c) { System.out.println("No connection"); }
}
```

**Client Test:**
```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        TCPConnection conn = new TCPConnection();  // LISTENING
        conn.acknowledge();                        // ignored
        conn.open();                               // -> ESTABLISHED
        conn.acknowledge();                        // ACK received
        conn.close();                              // -> CLOSED
        conn.acknowledge();                        // No connection
    }
}
```

---

**Q. (b) Explain the Chain of Responsibility pattern. Design a logger system where WARNING is handled by `ConsoleLogger`, ERROR by `FileLogger`, and INFO is consumed silently. Provide the class structure. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Pattern Definition:**
> The **Chain of Responsibility** pattern **decouples the sender of a request from its receivers** by giving multiple objects a chance to handle the request. The receiving objects are chained, and the request is passed along the chain until an object handles it (or it reaches the end).

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Logger {
        <<abstract>>
        #next: Logger
        +logMessage(level, msg)
        +setNext(Logger)
    }
    class ConsoleLogger {
        +logMessage(level, msg)
    }
    class FileLogger {
        +logMessage(level, msg)
    }
    class ErrorLogger {
        +logMessage(level, msg)
    }
    Logger <|-- ConsoleLogger
    Logger <|-- FileLogger
    Logger <|-- ErrorLogger
    Logger --> Logger : next
```

**Java Implementation:**

```java
abstract class Logger {
    public static int INFO = 1, DEBUG = 2, WARNING = 3, ERROR = 4;
    protected int level;
    protected Logger next;

    public void setNext(Logger next) { this.next = next; }

    public void logMessage(int level, String msg) {
        if (this.level <= level) {
            write(msg);
        }
        if (next != null) {
            next.logMessage(level, msg);
        }
    }
    protected abstract void write(String msg);
}

class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
    public ConsoleLogger(int level) { this.level = level; }
    protected void write(String msg) {
        System.out.println("[Console] " + msg);
    }
}

class FileLogger extends Logger {
    public FileLogger(int level) { this.level = level; }
    protected void write(String msg) {
        System.out.println("[File] " + msg);
    }
}

class ErrorLogger extends Logger {
    public ErrorLogger(int level) { this.level = level; }
    protected void write(String msg) {
        System.out.println("[ERROR-ALERT] " + msg);
    }
}
```

**Client Setup — Building the Chain:**
```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Logger console = new ConsoleLogger(Logger.WARNING);  // handles WARNING+
        Logger file    = new FileLogger(Logger.ERROR);       // handles ERROR+
        Logger error   = new ErrorLogger(Logger.ERROR);

        console.setNext(file);
        file.setNext(error);

        console.logMessage(Logger.INFO,     "Info:  ignored everywhere");
        console.logMessage(Logger.WARNING,  "Warn:  goes to console only");
        console.logMessage(Logger.ERROR,    "Error: goes to file + alert");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
[Console] Warn:  goes to console only
[File] Error: goes to file + alert
[ERROR-ALERT] Error: goes to file + alert
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 2:** Students often **forget to forward the request to `next`** when the current logger does not handle it. If the `if (next != null) next.logMessage(level, msg);` line is missing, the chain **breaks** and only the first applicable logger fires. This is a common 1.5-mark deduction in valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Behavioral Design Patterns (Module 2)**

- ✅ The GoF catalog contains **exactly 11** behavioral patterns — memorize all 11 names verbatim.
- ✅ **Strategy** = interchangeable algorithms; eliminates `if-else` chains. Context **HAS-A** Strategy.
- ✅ **Observer** = one-to-many dependency; foundation of MVC and event-driven GUIs. Subject pushes notifications to Observers.
- ✅ **Command** = request as an object; enables UNDO/REDO, queuing, logging. Invoker holds Command, not Receiver.
- ✅ **State** = behavior changes with internal state; replaces massive `switch` statements. Context **HAS-A** State.
- ✅ **Mediator** = central hub for many-to-many communication (Air Traffic Tower). Reduces pairwise coupling between colleagues.
- ✅ **Chain of Responsibility** = decouples sender from receiver; request flows through chain until handled.
- ✅ **Iterator** = sequential traversal without exposing internal structure (used inside Java `for-each`, `Iterator<T>`).
- ✅ **Memento** = capture/restore object state without violating encapsulation (3 players: Originator, Memento, Caretaker).
- ✅ **Template Method** = defines algorithm skeleton in superclass; subclasses override specific steps. Uses **inheritance**.
- ✅ **Visitor** = add new operations to object structure **without modifying classes**; uses **double dispatch**.
- ✅ **Interpreter** = implements a grammar for a domain-specific language (e.g., regex engine, SQL parser).
- ✅ **Strategy vs. State:** Strategy is **client-selected**; State is **self-transitioning**.
- ✅ **Mediator vs. Facade:** Mediator is **bidirectional + behavioral**; Facade is **unidirectional + structural**.
- ✅ All behavioral patterns promote **loose coupling**, **open/closed principle**, and **runtime flexibility**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (in order of GoF book):**
> **"Chains Command Interpreters, Iterating Mediated Mementos, Observing States Strategically via Templates and Visitors."**
> 
> Keywords: **C**hain, **C**ommand, **I**nterpreter, **I**terator, **M**ediator, **M**emento, **O**bserver, **S**tate, **S**trategy, **T**emplate, **V**isitor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coding Guidelines - Code Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coding Guidelines - Code Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Coding Guidelines — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coding Guidelines** are a documented set of *rules, conventions, and best practices* that prescribe how source code should be written, formatted, named, commented, structured, and documented within a software project. They enforce uniformity, readability, and maintainability across the entire codebase, irrespective of the individual programmer's style.

In KTU 2024 terminology, coding guidelines are classified as a **Static Quality Attribute** mechanism — they do not change program semantics, but they govern the *internal* quality of the source artefact. The IEEE/ISO standard that anchors this area is **ISO/IEC 5055:2021** (Automated Source Code Quality Measures), which formalizes code-level health indicators.

**Common Standards Referenced:**
- **MISRA C / MISRA C++** — for safety-critical embedded systems (used in automotive, aerospace, medical).
- **Google Style Guide** — for large-scale industrial projects.
- **CERT Secure Coding Standards** — for security hardening.
- **Oracle Java Code Conventions** / **Linux Kernel Coding Style** — for OS-level C programming.

### 1.2 Code Review — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Review** is a *systematic and disciplined examination* of source code by one or more peers (or automated tools) to identify defects, enforce coding standards, verify design intent, and share knowledge — performed *before* the code is merged into the main repository or released.

Sommerville (the prescribed KTU textbook) classifies review as a **Static Verification & Validation (V&V)** technique — it is performed without executing the program, distinguishing it fundamentally from dynamic testing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen Inspector"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a professional restaurant where 12 chefs are cooking together. Without a *recipe-format standard* (Coding Guidelines), each chef writes recipes differently — some use grams, others use cups; some are in French, some in English. The head chef can't read them, new chefs can't learn them, and a single typo (e.g., `salt: 50g` instead of `5g`) ruins the dish.
>
> **Coding Guidelines** = the standardized recipe format everyone MUST follow.
>
> **Code Review** = the head chef tasting every dish *before* it leaves the kitchen to catch the typo, the missing ingredient, or the burnt edge.
>
> The kitchen doesn't run slower because of these — it runs *safer* and *faster* in the long run, because the team isn't tripping over inconsistent code.

### 1.4 KTU Board Exam Highlight Box

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Coding Guidelines** are *preventive* — they reduce defect injection.
> - **Code Review** is *detective* — it catches defects already injected.
> - Both are *static* techniques — they work on source artefacts without execution.
> - Both are part of the broader **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** umbrella.

---

## 1.5 Why These Two Topics Are Bundled in KTU Module 3

KTU places *Coding Guidelines* and *Code Review* together in Module 3 because they form a **two-layer defence** for code quality:

1. **Layer 1 (Guidelines):** Stop bad code from being *written* in the first place.
2. **Layer 2 (Review):** Stop bad code from being *shipped* even if it slipped past Layer 1.

Together, they reduce the **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** because defects caught at the code-writing stage cost **10×–100× less** than those caught in production.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of Coding Guidelines

A complete coding-guidelines document for a KTU-level project must cover **eight mandatory dimensions**:

1. **Naming Conventions**
   - *Variables:* camelCase (`totalAmount`, `userName`).
   - *Constants:* UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (`MAX_BUFFER_SIZE`, `PI`).
   - *Classes:* PascalCase (`InvoiceProcessor`, `HttpRequest`).
   - *Functions:* verb-noun forms (`calculateTax()`, `getUserById()`).
   - *Booleans:* prefixed with `is`, `has`, `can` (`isActive`, `hasPermission`).

2. **Indentation & Whitespace**
   - 4 spaces (Java/Python) or 1 tab (Linux Kernel C).
   - No mixed tabs/spaces.
   - Blank lines to separate logical blocks.

3. **Line Length**
   - Typically **80–120 characters** (IEEE 12207 recommends ≤ 80).

4. **Commenting Policy**
   - *Header comments:* author, date, purpose, dependencies.
   - *Inline comments:* explain *why*, not *what*.
   - *Javadoc/Doxygen:* mandatory for all public APIs.

5. **Function/Method Design**
   - **Cyclomatic complexity ≤ 10** (McCabe's threshold).
   - Function length ≤ 50 lines (a popular heuristic; not a hard law).
   - Single Responsibility — one function = one purpose.
   - **No side effects** in getter functions.

6. **Error Handling**
   - Never silently swallow exceptions.
   - Use specific exception types, not generic `Exception` or `Throwable`.
   - Return error codes for predictable failures; throw exceptions for exceptional cases.

7. **File & Module Organization**
   - One public class per file (Java/C# rule).
   - Imports grouped: standard library → third-party → project-internal.
   - Header inclusion guards (`#ifndef HEADER_H`) in C/C++.

8. **Security Hardening**
   - Validate all inputs at boundaries.
   - Avoid `gets()`, `strcpy()`, `sprintf()` in C (CERT, MISRA).
   - No hardcoded credentials or cryptographic keys.

### 2.2 Anatomy of Code Review

Code review, in KTU syllabus terms, is a *formal Fagan Inspection* or a *lightweight peer review* — the type and rigour depend on the project's criticality.

#### 2.2.1 Roles in a Code Review (KTU-Frequently Tested)

| Role | Responsibility | Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Author** | Writes the code; presents/defends it | 1 |
| **Moderator** (Inspection Leader) | Schedules meeting, drives agenda, ensures process discipline | 1 |
| **Reviewer** | Finds defects, raises questions, records issues | 2–4 |
| **Scribe** (Recorder) | Logs every defect, decision, and action item | 1 |
| **Reader** | Walks the team through the code paragraph-by-paragraph | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern **GitHub/GitLab Pull Request (PR) reviews**, the Moderator becomes the *PR approver*, the Scribe becomes the *inline comment thread*, and the Reader role is replaced by the diff view. The conceptual roles remain identical.

#### 2.2.2 The Six-Phase Fagan Inspection Process

Fagan Inspections (Michael Fagan, IBM, 1976) are the *gold standard* and a KTU syllabus must-know:

1. **Planning** — Moderator selects artefact, distributes code, assigns roles.
2. **Overview** — (Optional) Author presents context to reviewers.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each reviewer studies code alone; prepares defect list.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — Reader walks through code; team logs defects. **No fixes are made here** — only identification.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes all logged defects.
6. **Follow-up** — Moderator verifies all fixes; ensures no new defects introduced.

#### 2.2.3 Lightweight Peer Review (Modern Industry)

Not every project can afford Fagan's overhead. Modern teams use:

- **Pair Programming** — Two developers, one screen, continuous review.
- **Over-the-Shoulder** — One developer asks another to look at the code.
- **Email Pass-Around** — Asynchronous, distributed, but slow.
- **Tool-Assisted PR Review** — GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket pull requests, often augmented with **static analysis tools** (SonarQube, ESLint, PMD, Checkstyle, Coverity).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **directly testable** in KTU numerical/analytical questions (Part A and Part B).

| # | Metric | Formula | Purpose | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Measures code quality | defects/KLOC |
| 2 | **Review Rate (RR)** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{LOC Reviewed}}{\text{Hours Spent}}$ | Measures review speed | LOC/hour |
| 3 | **Review Effectiveness (RE)** | $\text{RE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found in Review}}{\text{Total Defects (Review + Test)}}$ | Measures review efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | **Inspection Yield (IY)** | $\text{IY} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{LOC} \times \text{Hours}}$ | Defects caught per unit effort | defects/(KLOC·hr) |
| 5 | **Effort Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Review Effort (hr)}}{\text{Development Effort (hr)}}$ | Review cost share | dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | Total quality cost | currency |
| 7 | **Code Coverage by Review** | $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{\text{LOC Reviewed}}{\text{Total LOC}} \times 100\%$ | Review thoroughness | % |
| 8 | **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Path complexity in code | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Halstead Metrics — Volume** | $V = N \times \log_2 \, n$ | Program size from operators/operands | bits |
| 10 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Removed Before Release}}{\text{Defects Latent + Removed}} \times 100\%$ | Overall verification power | % |

> **Where:**
> - $\text{KLOC}$ = Thousand Lines of Code
> - $E$ = Edges in control flow graph; $N$ = Nodes; $P$ = Connected components
> - $N$ = total operators + operands; $n$ = distinct operators + operands
> - $C_{\text{prevention}}$, etc. are cost categories from the **Quality Cost Curve** by Crosby/Juran.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Coding guidelines (MISRA-C) + reviews are *legally mandated* for ASIL-D systems (brake controllers, ADAS).
- **Aviation (DO-178C):** Code review is a designated verification objective at **DAL-A**.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Mandatory code review with documented checklists.
- **Open Source (Linux, Kubernetes, TensorFlow):** Every commit passes through PR review — the de-facto quality gate.
- **FinTech / Banking:** Combined with static analysis (SonarQube) for PCI-DSS compliance.

> [!TIP]
> The **Rule of 10** (a famous industry heuristic) — defects cost 10× more to fix at each successive stage. A defect caught at code-review time costs ~$25; the same defect in production can cost ~$10,000+. This is *exactly* why KTU emphasizes these topics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Computing Defect Density and Review Effectiveness

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A KTU student project contains **8,500 lines of code**. During a peer review, the team found **34 defects**. After integration testing, an additional **16 defects** were detected. Calculate:
> 1. Defect Density
> 2. Review Effectiveness
> 3. Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

#### Step 1 — Convert to KLOC

$$ \text{KLOC} = \frac{\text{LOC}}{1000} = \frac{8500}{1000} = 8.5 \text{ KLOC} $$

This normalization is necessary because defect counts are meaningless without a size reference.

#### Step 2 — Compute Defect Density (DD)

Using the formula $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$:

$$ \text{DD} = \frac{34}{8.5} = 4.0 \text{ defects/KLOC} $$

**Interpretation:** Industry benchmarks (per Capers Jones) suggest:
- *World-class:* < 0.5 defects/KLOC
- *Typical commercial:* 1–5 defects/KLOC
- *Poor quality:* > 10 defects/KLOC

A value of 4.0 indicates an *acceptable* but not excellent codebase for academic software.

#### Step 3 — Compute Review Effectiveness (RE)

$$ \text{RE} = \frac{\text{Defects Found in Review}}{\text{Defects Found in Review} + \text{Defects Found in Test}} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ \text{RE} = \frac{34}{34 + 16} = \frac{34}{50} = 0.68 = 68\% $$

**Interpretation:** 68% of the defects were caught at the review stage. Industry-leading teams target **> 85%**. Below 50% is a red flag.

#### Step 4 — Compute Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

Assuming *no defects escaped to production* (i.e., 0 external defects):

$$ \text{DRE} = \frac{34 + 16}{(34 + 16) + 0} \times 100\% = \frac{50}{50} \times 100\% = 100\% $$

If, however, 5 defects were reported by users post-release:

$$ \text{DRE} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} \times 100\% = \frac{50}{55} \times 100\% \approx 90.91\% $$

#### Step 5 — Analytical Summary Matrix

| Metric | Value | Threshold | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Defect Density | 4.0 | < 5 | ✅ Acceptable |
| Review Effectiveness | 68% | > 85% | ⚠️ Needs Improvement |
| DRE | 100% (or 90.91%) | > 95% | ✅ Good (or ⚠️) |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Given the following pseudo-code, compute the cyclomatic complexity using the formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$.

```
function grade(marks) {
    if (marks >= 90)            // Decision 1
        return "A";
    else if (marks >= 75)       // Decision 2
        return "B";
    else if (marks >= 60)       // Decision 3
        return "C";
    else
        return "D";
}
```

#### Step 1 — Build the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

Nodes (N) = 5 (entry, three if-blocks, else-block).
Edges (E) = 7 (entry→if1, if1→true-branch, if1→if2, if2→true-branch, if2→if3, if3→true-branch, if3→else).
Connected components (P) = 1.

#### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$ V(G) = E - N + 2P = 7 - 5 + 2(1) = 4 $$

#### Step 3 — Cross-Check Using Decision-Point Method

$$ V(G) = \pi + 1 $$

where $\pi$ = number of decision points = **3** (the three `if` statements).

$$ V(G) = 3 + 1 = 4 \checkmark $$

Both methods agree. Since $V(G) = 4 \le 10$, the function passes the McCabe threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> If a function has $V(G) > 10$, it is a **KTU coding-guideline violation** — the function should be decomposed into smaller units.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Halstead Volume Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For the simple statement `a = b + c * d;`, count the operators and operands.

#### Step 1 — Tokenize

| Token | Type |
|---|---|
| `a` | Operand |
| `=` | Operator |
| `b` | Operand |
| `+` | Operator |
| `c` | Operand |
| `*` | Operator |
| `d` | Operand |
| `;` | Operator |

- Total operators: **4** (`=`, `+`, `*`, `;`)
- Total operands: **4** (`a`, `b`, `c`, `d`)
- Distinct operators: **4**; distinct operands: **4**

#### Step 2 — Compute Halstead Length and Vocabulary

$$ N = n_1 + n_2 = 4 + 4 = 8 $$

$$ n = \mu_1 + \mu_2 = 4 + 4 = 8 $$

#### Step 3 — Compute Program Volume

$$ V = N \times \log_2 \, n = 8 \times \log_2 8 = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ bits} $$

**Interpretation:** Higher volume ⇒ more complex or "information-rich" code. Halstead's metrics correlate with defect density and effort estimation.

---

### 3.4 The Inspection Yield Curve — Deriving the Cost-Benefit Argument

The Inspection Yield (IY) formula:

$$ \text{IY} = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{LOC} \times \text{Hours}} $$

Suppose a team reviews 5 KLOC over 8 hours and finds 12 defects:

$$ \text{IY} = \frac{12}{5 \times 8} = \frac{12}{40} = 0.30 \text{ defects/(KLOC·hr)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Empirical studies (Fagan, 1976; Russell, 1991) show that **inspection yield typically ranges from 0.5 to 1.5 defects/(KLOC·hr)** for trained teams. Values below 0.3 suggest the review was superficial; values above 2.0 suggest the code is exceptionally defect-prone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Complete Code Review Workflow (Fagan-Enhanced PR Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Author Writes Code] --> B[Author Self-Review vs Coding Guidelines]
    B --> C[Automated Static Analysis: SonarQube, ESLint, PMD]
    C --> D{Static Analysis Pass?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Create Pull Request]
    E --> F[Moderator Assigns Reviewers: 2 to 4 Peers]
    F --> G[Individual Preparation Phase]
    G --> H[Reviewer A: Defect Log]
    G --> I[Reviewer B: Defect Log]
    G --> J[Reviewer C: Defect Log]
    H --> K[Inspection Meeting: Scribe Records]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Defects Found?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Author Performs Rework]
    M --> N[Moderator Follow-up Verification]
    N --> O{All Defects Resolved?}
    O -- No --> M
    O -- Yes --> P[Merge to Main Branch]
    P --> Q[Trigger CI/CD Pipeline]
    Q --> R[Unit and Integration Tests]
    R --> S[Deploy to Staging]
    S --> T[Production Release]
    L -- No --> P
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a *single linear flow with three feedback loops* (Self-Review, Static Analysis, Follow-up). In the KTU exam, students are often asked to draw or label such a process diagram — pay close attention to the **role of the moderator** and the **separation of identification from rework**.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Two-Layer Defence Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Programmer Begins Writing Code] --> B[Layer 1: Coding Guidelines Applied]
    B --> C{Defect Injected?}
    C -- No --> D[Defect-Free Code]
    C -- Yes --> E[Defective Code in Repository]
    E --> F[Layer 2: Code Review Conducted]
    F --> G{Defect Detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Author Reworks Code]
    G -- No --> I[Defective Code Shipped to Production]
    H --> J[Re-enter Review]
    J --> F
    D --> K[Ship to Production]
    I --> L[Customer Discovers Defect: High Cost]
    K --> M[Low Cost: Defect Prevented or Detected Early]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is a *Layered Defence / Defence-in-Depth* model — the same pattern used in security (firewall + IDS), networking (collision detection + retransmission), and aviation (primary + backup instruments). Each layer is a **fault-containment boundary**.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Code Review Metric Flow (Cause-Effect Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Review Rate: LOC per hour] --> M3[Cost of Quality]
    M2[Defect Density: Defects per KLOC] --> M3
    M4[Review Effectiveness: percent] --> M3
    M5[Inspection Yield: defects per KLOC-hr] --> M3
    M6[McCabe Complexity V of G] --> M2
    M7[Halstead Volume] --> M2
    M6 --> M5
    M7 --> M5
    M1 --> M4
    M2 --> M4
    M3 --> OUT[Project Quality Decision: Accept, Reject, or Re-review]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This shows the **cause-effect chain** between code-level metrics and project-level quality decisions — a frequent 14-mark question type in KTU ESE.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Static vs Dynamic Verification

```mermaid
graph TB
    V[Verification and Validation] --> S[Static: No Execution]
    V --> D[Dynamic: Execute the Program]
    S --> S1[Code Review]
    S --> S2[Inspection: Fagan]
    S --> S3[Walkthrough]
    S --> S4[Static Analysis: Tools]
    S --> S5[Coding Guidelines Enforcement]
    D --> D1[Unit Testing]
    D --> D2[Integration Testing]
    D --> D3[System Testing]
    D --> D4[Acceptance Testing]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This taxonomy is the **single most important classification** in software V&V. KTU repeatedly asks students to classify a given technique as static or dynamic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define coding guidelines. List any four common naming conventions used in industry-standard coding guidelines.*

**Model Answer:**

> Coding guidelines are a documented set of rules and conventions that prescribe how source code should be written, formatted, and documented within a project, ensuring consistency, readability, and maintainability.

Four common naming conventions:

1. **Variables:** camelCase — e.g., `totalAmount`, `userAge`.
2. **Constants:** UPPER_SNAKE_CASE — e.g., `MAX_SIZE`, `PI`.
3. **Classes:** PascalCase — e.g., `InvoiceProcessor`, `HttpRequest`.
4. **Boolean variables:** prefixed with `is`, `has`, or `can` — e.g., `isActive`, `hasPermission`, `canEdit`.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Style):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Four conventions: 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the roles in a Fagan Code Inspection and state the responsibility of the Moderator.*

**Model Answer:**

Roles in a Fagan Inspection:
1. Author
2. Moderator (Inspection Leader)
3. Reviewers / Inspectors
4. Scribe (Recorder)
5. Reader

**Responsibility of the Moderator:**
The Moderator is responsible for scheduling the inspection meeting, distributing the source code to reviewers, ensuring that the process is followed, controlling the meeting (preventing premature-fix discussions), and verifying the rework during the follow-up phase.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Five roles: 1.5 marks (0.3 each)
- Moderator responsibility: 1.5 marks

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Fagan Inspection** process in detail. List all six phases and explain any three with their key activities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **benefits of code review** in software engineering. Explain any **four quantitative metrics** used to measure review effectiveness, with formulae. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Fagan Inspection is a **formal, systematic, and disciplined** method of static code review introduced by Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976. It is more rigorous than informal peer review and is used in safety-critical systems (avionics, medical, automotive). The six phases are:

**Phase 1: Planning**
The Moderator identifies the code artefact to be inspected, selects 2–4 reviewers based on expertise, distributes the source code and supporting documents, and schedules the inspection meeting. Entry criteria must be satisfied (e.g., the code must compile and pass unit tests).

**Phase 2: Overview (Optional)**
The Author presents the design intent, algorithms used, and any unusual design choices. This phase is skipped when the reviewers already have sufficient context.

**Phase 3: Individual Preparation**
Each reviewer studies the source code *independently* (typically 1–2 hours per 200 LOC), lists potential defects, classifies them (e.g., logical, syntactic, standards violation), and notes the line numbers. This is the *most defect-productive* phase.

**Phase 4: Inspection Meeting**
The Reader walks the team through the code line by line. The Scribe logs every defect raised. **Critical rule:** No fixes are made in this meeting — only identification. Discussions are limited to *clarifying* the defect, not solving it. The meeting typically lasts 2 hours max.

**Phase 5: Rework**
The Author takes the logged defect list and fixes each defect. The rework effort is logged for metrics.

**Phase 6: Follow-up**
The Moderator verifies that every logged defect has been addressed, ensures no new defects were introduced, and decides whether a re-inspection is needed. The artefact is then released for integration.

> **Marks allocation:** 1 mark for naming the six phases, 6 marks for explaining three phases (2 each).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Benefits of Code Review:**

1. **Early Defect Detection** — Reviews catch 60–90% of defects before testing, reducing the cost of quality by 10×–100× (the *Rule of 10*).
2. **Knowledge Sharing** — Junior developers learn idioms, design patterns, and project conventions from senior reviewers.
3. **Coding-Guideline Enforcement** — Reviews verify that the submitted code adheres to the project's coding standards.
4. **Improved Design** — Reviewers often suggest design improvements, not just bug fixes.
5. **Reduced Bus-Factor Risk** — Multiple people are familiar with every module, reducing key-person dependency.
6. **Documentation Aid** — Review discussions often result in better code comments.

**Four Quantitative Metrics:**

| Metric | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Measures defects per unit size |
| **Review Rate** | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{LOC}}{\text{Hours}}$ | Measures review throughput |
| **Review Effectiveness** | $\text{RE} = \dfrac{D_{\text{review}}}{D_{\text{review}} + D_{\text{test}}}$ | Proportion of defects caught at review |
| **Inspection Yield** | $\text{IY} = \dfrac{D_{\text{found}}}{\text{LOC} \times \text{Hours}}$ | Productivity of the review process |

**Explanation of each metric:** (2 marks per metric explained with formula and one-line purpose).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**(a)** Define **Coding Guidelines**. Explain the importance of naming conventions and commenting standards with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A team of 4 developers conducted a code review on a module of **6,500 LOC** over **10 hours**. They found **26 defects**. Later, integration testing found **14 more defects**. Calculate: **(i)** Defect Density, **(ii)** Review Rate, **(iii)** Review Effectiveness. Comment on the review quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition of Coding Guidelines** (2 marks):
Coding guidelines are a documented set of rules, conventions, and best practices that standardize how source code is written, formatted, named, commented, and structured within a software project, ensuring uniformity and maintainability.

**Importance of Naming Conventions** (2.5 marks):

Naming conventions make code *self-documenting*. A well-named variable tells the reader its purpose without requiring a comment. For example:
