- `int totalAmount` (good) vs `int ta` (bad) — the first is descriptive, the second requires the reader to chase the definition.
- `bool isLoggedIn` (good) vs `bool flag` (bad) — the first conveys a clear boolean meaning.

Consistent naming also enables automated tooling — IDE refactoring, grep-based searching, and code-quality scanners (e.g., SonarQube) all depend on predictable naming.

**Importance of Commenting Standards** (2.5 marks):

Comments explain *why*, not *what*. For example:

```c
// BAD COMMENT: This loop iterates over the array
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { ... }

// GOOD COMMENT: Use binary search because the array is sorted at this point
// (verified by pre-condition in caller)
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { ... }
```

A documenting comment header on every public API (e.g., Javadoc, Doxygen) reduces the time new developers spend understanding interfaces. Comments must be **kept in sync with the code** — outdated comments are worse than no comments.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- LOC = 6,500
- Review hours = 10
- Defects in review = 26
- Defects in integration test = 14

**Step 1 — Defect Density (i):**

$$ \text{DD} = \frac{26}{6500/1000} = \frac{26}{6.5} = 4.0 \text{ defects/KLOC} $$

**Step 2 — Review Rate (ii):**

$$ \text{RR} = \frac{6500}{10} = 650 \text{ LOC/hour} $$

**Step 3 — Review Effectiveness (iii):**

$$ \text{RE} = \frac{26}{26 + 14} = \frac{26}{40} = 0.65 = 65\% $$

**Comment on Review Quality:**

- *Defect Density of 4.0* is **acceptable** for academic software (industry benchmark: < 5).
- *Review Rate of 650 LOC/hr* is **reasonable** — empirical studies suggest 150–500 LOC/hr for thorough reviews; 650 is on the high side, suggesting the review may have been rushed.
- *Review Effectiveness of 65%* is **below the industry target of 85%**, meaning 35% of defects escaped to testing.

**Recommendation:** Increase individual preparation time, use automated static analysis as a pre-filter, and reduce the size of review units (review smaller diffs, not 6,500 LOC at once).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Fagan Inspection with Walkthrough.** A Fagan Inspection is *formal* with defined roles; a walkthrough is *informal* and led by the author. Examiners *will* deduct a mark for this mix-up.
>
> 2. **Wrong units in Defect Density.** Writing "4.0 defects per LOC" instead of "4.0 defects per KLOC" is a common error — always normalize by KLOC, not LOC.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to comment on metrics.** A 14-mark question with calculation *also* requires interpretation. Writing only the formula and answer (with no comment on quality) loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Mixing static and dynamic techniques.** Code review is **static** (no execution). Unit testing is **dynamic** (requires execution). Examiners penalize this classification error heavily.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Scribe's role in Fagan Inspection.** Many students list only Author, Moderator, Reviewer — forgetting the **Scribe/Recorder**, which is a *mandatory* role in Fagan's original definition.
>
> 6. **Using "inspect" vs "review" interchangeably.** A *code review* is a lightweight, often-informal check. An *inspection* is a Fagan-style, formal, metrics-driven event. Don't blur the terms in the answer script.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist (print this for last-minute review):**

- [x] **Coding Guidelines** = preventive, static, style-enforcing.
- [x] **Code Review** = detective, static, defect-finding.
- [x] Both are part of **Static V&V** — no program execution involved.
- [x] **MISRA-C** and **CERT** are the most cited coding standards for safety-critical C.
- [x] **Naming conventions:** camelCase (vars), PascalCase (classes), UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (constants), `is/has/can` (booleans).
- [x] **McCabe's Complexity** $V(G) = E - N + 2P = \pi + 1$. Threshold: **≤ 10**.
- [x] **Fagan Inspection** has **6 phases**: Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up.
- [x] **5 Roles:** Author, Moderator, Reader, Reviewer, Scribe.
- [x] **No fixes in the inspection meeting** — only identification. This is the most-tested rule.
- [x] **Defect Density** = Defects ÷ KLOC. Industry target: < 5.
- [x] **Review Effectiveness** = Defects in review ÷ Total defects. Industry target: > 85%.
- [x] **Rule of 10:** Defects cost 10× more at each stage from coding → unit test → integration → system → production.
- [x] Modern reviews use **GitHub/GitLab PRs + SonarQube/ESLint** as the de-facto standard.
- [x] **Code Review vs Pair Programming:** Review is *asynchronous and pull-based*; Pair Programming is *synchronous and continuous*.
- [x] **Three lightweight review types:** Over-the-shoulder, Email pass-around, PR review (industry standard).
- [x] **Halstead Volume** = $N \log_2 n$ — measures program size from token statistics.
- [x] **Code review also enforces coding guidelines** — the two topics form a single quality loop.

> **Last Word:** If you remember the **Fagan 6 phases**, the **5 roles**, the **8 dimensions of coding guidelines**, the **Defect Density and Review Effectiveness formulas**, and the **Rule of 10**, you will cover 90% of the marks KTU expects on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction: Static Verification in Software Engineering

In the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), verifying that the code we write actually does what it is supposed to do is critical. Broadly, this verification is done in two ways:
- **Dynamic Testing** — executing the code with sample inputs.
- **Static Verification** — analyzing the code *without* executing it.

**Code Walkthroughs** and **Code Inspections** are the two most important forms of *formal, structured static analysis* (also called **peer reviews** or **Fagan Inspections** in their most formal variant). They are listed explicitly under the **Software Testing Strategies** module of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A **Code Walkthrough** is an *informal-to-semiformal* group review technique in which the author of the code leads a small team of peers through the source code line-by-line, narrating the logic, while the team asks questions and surfaces potential defects, logic errors, and standards violations.
>
> A **Code Inspection** is a *highly formal, structured, multi-step* group review technique (originated by Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976) in which a trained moderator guides a team through a detailed, line-by-line examination of the source code using predefined checklists, rules, and roles to identify defects, violations of standards, and other problems — without the author narrating.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Manuscript Review"**
>
> Imagine a novelist has just finished the first draft of a chapter. Before publishing, the author wants feedback. Two possible scenarios:
>
> - **Code Walkthrough ≈ "Reading Aloud to Friends"** — The author *reads the chapter aloud* to two or three friends, narrating their intent: "Here, the detective enters the room; here, he finds the clue." The friends ask questions like "But why didn't the guard see him?" The author is *defending their own work*.
>
> - **Code Inspection ≈ "Submission to a Professional Editor with a Style Guide"** — The manuscript is handed to a trained editor who uses a formal *checklist* (grammar, plot consistency, character name consistency, pacing rules) and a documented process (planning → individual reading → team meeting → rework → follow-up) to *systematically find defects*. The author does **not** narrate.
>
> The walkthrough is collaborative and author-led. The inspection is formal, checklist-driven, and moderator-led.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Terms

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Defect** | Any flaw in code (logic, syntax, standards, performance). |
| **Reviewer / Inspector** | A peer who examines the code. |
| **Moderator** | The leader of an inspection. |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Person who logs all defects found. |
| **Author** | The developer who wrote the code under review. |
| **Reader** | Walks the team through the code in walkthroughs. |
| **Entry/Exit Criteria** | Conditions that must be met before/after the review. |
| **Checklist** | Predefined list of items to look for in inspection. |
| **Fagan Inspection** | The most famous, formal type of code inspection. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Code Walkthrough — Deep Dive

A walkthrough is the lighter, more casual of the two review techniques. Its purpose is **knowledge transfer** and **early defect detection** in a low-pressure environment.

### Characteristics
- **Led by the author** (not a moderator).
- The author **narrates** the logic, line by line, often using a printed copy.
- Reviewers ask questions — "what if?", "why not?", "is this boundary safe?".
- Focus is on **logic, clarity, and intent**, not strict standards compliance.
- **No formal checklist** is mandatory; comments are free-flowing.
- Often used for **junior developers' code** or for **complex algorithms** that benefit from group reasoning.

### Typical Roles in a Walkthrough
- **Author / Presenter** — narrates the code.
- **Reader** — sometimes the author, sometimes separate; paraphrases code aloud.
- **Reviewers** — typically 2 to 5 peers (testers, senior devs, architects).
- **Recorder** (optional) — documents issues raised.

### Process Flow
1. Author distributes source code to participants a day or two before.
2. Participants skim the code individually.
3. In the meeting, the author narrates logic.
4. Reviewers raise questions, suggest improvements.
5. Author records action items and fixes them later.

### Strengths & Limitations

> [!TIP]
> **Strengths**
> - Cheap, fast, and easy to schedule.
> - Excellent for **knowledge sharing** across the team.
> - Effective at catching **logic, design, and requirement issues**.
> - Builds team ownership of the code.
>
> **Limitations**
> - Author bias — the author may unconsciously skip over "obvious" parts.
> - Inconsistent coverage — depends on reviewer experience.
> - No formal metrics collected.
> - Tends to be social rather than systematic.

## 2.2 Code Inspection — Deep Dive

Code inspection is the most rigorous, formal static analysis technique. Michael Fagan of IBM is credited with formalizing it in **1976**, and his process is so influential it is often called the **Fagan Inspection**.

### Characteristics
- **Led by a trained Moderator** (not the author).
- Follows a **strict, multi-phase process** with explicit **entry and exit criteria** for each phase.
- Uses a **written checklist** of common defect types (e.g., uninitialized variables, off-by-one errors, missing error handling, SQL injection, memory leaks).
- The author is a **passive participant** in the inspection meeting — they answer questions but do not narrate.
- The output is a **formal defect log** and **metrics** (e.g., inspection rate, defect density).

### Fagan's Six-Phase Inspection Process
1. **Planning** — Moderator forms the team, distributes materials, schedules the meeting.
2. **Overview** *(optional)* — Author presents the design/intent to the team so they have context.
3. **Individual Preparation** — Each inspector studies the code **alone** and marks defects using the checklist.
4. **Inspection Meeting** — The reader (not the author) walks through the code, paraphrasing it. The scribe logs defects. **No fixes are proposed in the meeting** — only defects are logged.
5. **Rework** — Author fixes the logged defects.
6. **Follow-up** — Moderator verifies that all defects were addressed and that no new defects were introduced.

### Strengths & Limitations

> [!TIP]
> **Strengths**
> - Highest defect-detection rate of any static technique (industry studies show it can find **60–90% of defects**).
> - Produces **measurable metrics** (defects per KLOC, inspection rate).
> - Checklist ensures **consistent coverage** across reviews.
> - Catches **standards violations, security flaws, and style issues** systematically.
> - Scales well to large codebases.
>
> **Limitations**
> - Time-consuming and expensive to set up.
> - Requires **trained moderators and participants**.
> - Can become bureaucratic if over-applied.
> - Author may feel defensive (psychological pressure).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

This table is **critical** for KTU exams — it is a frequently-asked 14-mark question format.

| Parameter | Code Walkthrough | Code Inspection |
|---|---|---|
| **Formality** | Informal to semiformal | Highly formal |
| **Led by** | Author | Trained Moderator |
| **Checklist used?** | Optional / informal | Mandatory, written |
| **Roles** | Author-led, free flowing | Defined: Moderator, Reader, Scribe, Author, Reviewer |
| **Phases** | Usually one meeting | **6 phases** (Planning, Overview, Prep, Meeting, Rework, Follow-up) |
| **Author participation** | Narrates the code | Passive; answers questions only |
| **Reader role** | Often the author | Separate person, paraphrases code |
| **Defect logging** | Informal notes | Formal defect log + metrics |
| **Entry/Exit criteria** | Not formalized | Strictly defined per phase |
| **Typical duration** | 30 min – 2 hrs | Several hours to a day |
| **Cost** | Low | Higher |
| **Defect detection rate** | Moderate (20–40%) | High (60–90%) |
| **Best for** | Knowledge transfer, early code | Safety-critical, standards-heavy code |
| **Originator** | General practice | Michael Fagan, IBM, 1976 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "compare" or "differentiate", you must mention **at least 6 points** to score full marks. Lead with who leads the review and the use of a checklist.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Avionics & Medical Devices** — DO-178C and IEC 62304 *mandate* code inspections for safety-critical software. Walkthroughs alone are insufficient.
- **Open Source & Agile** — Modern equivalents of walkthroughs are **pair programming** and **pull-request reviews** on GitHub. Modern equivalents of inspections are **formal security audits** (e.g., OWASP code reviews).
- **Cybersecurity** — Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools like SonarQube, Checkmarx, and Coverity *automate* the checklist-driven logic of inspections.
- **ISO 9001 & CMMI** — Require documented peer review evidence; inspections satisfy this directly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough of the Inspection Process

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** of the Fagan Inspection. Each step includes the *why* behind it, which KTU examiners reward.

### Step 1 — Planning
- **Who:** Moderator.
- **What:** Selects inspection team (typically 3–5 people: moderator, reader, scribe, author, plus 1–2 inspectors). Distributes the source code, design documents, and the appropriate **checklist**.
- **Why:** Ensures the right expertise is in the room and that everyone has a clear scope. The checklist ensures *consistent defect detection* across inspections.

### Step 2 — Overview *(Optional but recommended for complex code)*
- **Who:** Author.
- **What:** Briefly presents the purpose of the code, the design decisions, and the interfaces with other modules.
- **Why:** Gives inspectors *context*. Without it, they may flag valid code as suspicious simply because they don't understand the intent.

### Step 3 — Individual Preparation
- **Who:** Each inspector, working **alone** (typically 1–2 hours per 100–200 lines of code).
- **What:** Inspector reads the code, marks defects against the checklist, and lists questions.
- **Why:** This is the **most defect-rich phase**. Studies (Fagan's original IBM data) show that individual preparation finds **the majority of defects** — the team meeting is mainly for confirmation and consensus.

### Step 4 — Inspection Meeting
- **Who:** Moderator leads; Reader paraphrases the code; Scribe logs defects; Author answers questions.
- **What:**
  1. Reader walks the team through the code, **paraphrasing** it in plain language (e.g., "this loop iterates from 1 to n, and on each iteration we increment counter c…").
  2. When a defect is identified, the scribe logs it with: **defect ID, line number, defect type, severity**.
  3. The team **does NOT propose fixes** in the meeting. The moderator enforces this.
- **Why:** Paraphrasing forces a clear, line-by-line reading, exposing hidden assumptions. Banning fix discussions keeps the meeting short and focused on *finding* defects, not solving them.
- **Time limit:** Typically **≤ 2 hours** to maintain focus (Fagan's data shows defect-detection efficiency drops sharply after that).

### Step 5 — Rework
- **Who:** Author.
- **What:** Author addresses every logged defect, either by fixing it or by documenting why the code is correct.
- **Why:** Ensures accountability and resolution.

### Step 6 — Follow-up
- **Who:** Moderator.
- **What:** Re-inspects the changed code to verify fixes and check that no new defects were introduced.
- **Why:** Closes the loop. Without follow-up, inspections degrade into suggestion boxes that are ignored.

## 3.2 Defect-Detection Effectiveness — Fagan's Model

Fagan's original IBM research established an empirical relationship between **inspection rate** and **defect detection efficiency**.

Let:
- $L$ = Lines of code inspected.
- $T_p$ = Total preparation time in person-hours.
- $D$ = Defects found.
- $K$ = Known defects in the code (ground truth).

Inspection rate is defined as:

$$
R = \frac{L}{T_p} \quad \text{[lines per person-hour]}
$$

Defect density is:

$$
\rho = \frac{D}{L / 1000} \quad \text{[defects per KLOC]}
$$

Fagan's empirical finding: defect detection efficiency peaks when:

$$
R \approx 150 \text{ lines per person-hour}
$$

Below this rate (too slow), inspectors lose focus. Above it (rushing), they miss defects. This is why **time-boxing the meeting at 2 hours** and limiting individual prep are essential.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Choose Which

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Junior developer's first Java module | **Walkthrough** | Knowledge transfer, low stakes. |
| Safety-critical avionics control loop | **Inspection** | Regulatory mandate (DO-178C). |
| Quick check of a 50-line utility script | **Walkthrough** | Overhead of inspection not justified. |
| Module handling credit-card data | **Inspection + Checklist** | Security checklist is mandatory. |
| New team member on existing module | **Walkthrough** | Author can explain intent & history. |
| Pre-release certification audit | **Inspection** | Need formal defect log for auditors. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Code Walkthrough — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Author finishes coding] --> B[Distribute source code to 2-5 peers]
    B --> C[Reviewers skim code individually]
    C --> D[Meeting: Author narrates logic line-by-line]
    D --> E[Reviewers ask questions and raise issues]
    E --> F[Author records notes and action items]
    F --> G[Author fixes code after meeting]
    G --> H[End of Walkthrough]
```

## 4.2 Code Inspection (Fagan) — 6-Phase Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Planning - Moderator forms team and distributes checklist] --> P2[Phase 2: Overview - Author presents context]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Individual Preparation - Each inspector works alone]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Inspection Meeting - Reader paraphrases, Scribe logs defects]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Rework - Author fixes logged defects]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Follow-up - Moderator verifies fixes and no new defects]
    P6 --> P7[Inspection Complete - Metrics recorded]
```

## 4.3 Roles & Information Flow in a Fagan Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MOD[Moderator] -->|leads| TEAM[Inspection Team]
    TEAM --> READER[Reader]
    TEAM --> SCRIBE[Scribe]
    TEAM --> INSP1[Inspector 1]
    TEAM --> INSP2[Inspector 2]
    AUTHOR[Author] -->|passive - answers questions| TEAM
    READER -->|paraphrases code aloud| TEAM
    SCRIBE -->|logs defects| LOG[Defect Log]
    INSP1 -->|uses checklist| LOG
    INSP2 -->|uses checklist| LOG
    LOG -->|post-meeting| AUTHOR
    AUTHOR -->|fixes| REWORK[Reworked Code]
    REWORK -->|verification| MOD
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Static Review System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Source Code Module] --> IN[Input Stage: Planning and Material Distribution]
    IN --> PREP[Preparation Stage: Individual Inspector Analysis with Checklist]
    PREP --> MEET[Meeting Stage: Reader Paraphrase, Scribe Log Defects]
    MEET --> DEC[Decision Gate: Are defects found?]
    DEC -->|Yes| REW[Rework Stage: Author Fixes Defects]
    REW --> VER[Follow-up Verification by Moderator]
    VER --> DEC
    DEC -->|No| OUT[Output: Approved Code plus Defect Metrics]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Code Inspection. List any four roles in a Fagan Inspection.
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Code Inspection is a formal, systematic, group static-analysis technique used to identify defects in source code by following a structured multi-phase process led by a trained moderator using predefined checklists, **without executing the code**.

Four roles in a Fagan Inspection are:
1. **Moderator** – leads the inspection and enforces process discipline.
2. **Reader** – paraphrases the code aloud during the meeting.
3. **Scribe / Recorder** – logs every defect identified with line number and type.
4. **Author** – the developer who wrote the code; remains passive during the meeting and performs rework afterward.
*(Other valid roles: Inspector, Reviewer.)*

---

### Q2. What is the main difference between a Code Walkthrough and a Code Inspection?
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The main difference lies in **formality and leadership**:
- A **Code Walkthrough** is *informal*, **led by the author**, who narrates the code line-by-line to a small group of peers. There is no formal checklist or strict process.
- A **Code Inspection** is *highly formal*, **led by a trained moderator** (not the author), follows a strict 6-phase process (Planning, Overview, Individual Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, Follow-up), and uses a **mandatory written checklist** to systematically detect defects.

In short: **Walkthrough = author-led, informal; Inspection = moderator-led, formal, checklist-driven.**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** Explain the six phases of a Fagan Code Inspection in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**Q.A (b)** Differentiate between Code Walkthrough and Code Inspection on at least eight parameters. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Six Phases of Fagan Inspection

The Fagan Code Inspection, introduced by **Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976**, is a highly formal static analysis technique consisting of **six well-defined phases**, each with explicit entry and exit criteria:

**1. Planning:**
The **Moderator** identifies the inspection scope, selects the inspection team (typically 3–5 members including Moderator, Reader, Scribe, Author, and 1–2 Inspectors), distributes the source code, supporting design documents, and the appropriate **checklist**. The moderator also schedules the inspection meeting. *Purpose:* ensure right expertise and clear scope.

**2. Overview (Optional):**
The **Author** presents the purpose of the code, design decisions, and how the module interfaces with the rest of the system. *Purpose:* give inspectors enough context to interpret the code correctly.

**3. Individual Preparation:**
Each **Inspector** studies the code **independently**, typically for 1–2 hours per 100–200 lines, and marks defects using the checklist. *Purpose:* this is the most defect-rich phase; studies show individual preparation uncovers the majority of defects.

**4. Inspection Meeting:**
The **Reader** walks the team through the code, **paraphrasing** each statement in plain language. As defects are identified, the **Scribe** logs them with ID, line number, type, and severity. The author answers questions but does not narrate. **No fixes are proposed** in this meeting. The meeting is strictly time-boxed (≤ 2 hours). *Purpose:* confirm and consolidate defects efficiently.

**5. Rework:**
The **Author** addresses each logged defect — by either fixing it or documenting why the code is correct. *Purpose:* ensure accountability.

**6. Follow-up:**
The **Moderator** re-inspects the modified code to verify that every defect was correctly addressed and that no new defects were introduced. *Purpose:* close the loop and ensure quality.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Walkthrough vs. Inspection (8+ Parameters)

| # | Parameter | Code Walkthrough | Code Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Formality** | Informal / semiformal | Highly formal |
| 2 | **Led by** | Author | Trained Moderator |
| 3 | **Checklist** | Optional / informal | Mandatory, written |
| 4 | **Process phases** | Typically a single meeting | Six structured phases |
| 5 | **Author's role in meeting** | Narrates the code | Passive; answers questions only |
| 6 | **Defect logging** | Informal notes | Formal defect log with metrics |
| 7 | **Entry/Exit criteria** | Not formalized | Strictly defined per phase |
| 8 | **Defect detection rate** | Moderate (≈20–40%) | High (≈60–90%) |
| 9 | **Cost & time** | Low | Higher |
| 10 | **Best suited for** | Knowledge transfer, junior devs, small code | Safety-critical, standards-heavy, large code |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B (a)** What is a Code Walkthrough? Describe its characteristics, typical roles, and process. **[7 Marks]**

**Q.B (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Fagan Inspection process. State four advantages and two limitations of code inspection. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Code Walkthrough

**Definition:** A Code Walkthrough is a semiformal group review technique in which the **author of the code leads a small team of 2–5 peers** through the source code, narrating its logic line-by-line, while reviewers ask questions, raise concerns, and identify potential defects, logic errors, and standards violations.

**Characteristics:**
- Author-led (not moderator-led).
- Code is **not executed** — purely static.
- Narration is line-by-line, with explanation of intent.
- Reviewers focus on **logic, design, and clarity**.
- Light documentation; informal notes only.
- Often used for **knowledge transfer** and **early defect detection** in low-stakes code.

**Typical Roles:**
- **Author / Presenter** – narrates the code.
- **Reviewers** – 2 to 5 peers (testers, senior devs, architects).
- **Recorder / Scribe** (optional) – notes issues.
- **Reader** (sometimes separate from author) – paraphrases code aloud.

**Process:**
1. Author finishes writing the code.
2. Source is distributed to reviewers 1–2 days in advance.
3. Reviewers skim the code individually.
4. In the meeting, author narrates the code logic.
5. Reviewers ask "what if?" questions and raise defects.
6. Author records action items and fixes the code after the meeting.

---

### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Diagram, Advantages, Limitations

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes — the 6-phase Fagan Inspection flowchart.)*

The Fagan Inspection proceeds through **Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up**, with strict entry/exit criteria and a moderator-led process using checklists.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **High defect-detection rate** — 60–90% of defects are found before execution, saving debugging cost.
2. **Measurable metrics** — defect density, inspection rate, and rework time can be tracked over projects.
3. **Consistent coverage** — the checklist ensures uniform defect detection across reviews.
4. **Knowledge transfer** — inspectors learn the codebase and coding standards.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Time-consuming and costly** to set up and run; requires trained moderators.
2. **Author defensiveness** — the formal nature can create psychological pressure on developers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up who leads the review.** Many students write "moderator leads walkthrough" — this is wrong. **Walkthrough = author-led; Inspection = moderator-led.**
> 2. **Omitting the checklist.** If you forget to mention that inspections use a *mandatory written checklist*, you lose at least 1 mark in any comparison question.
> 3. **Skipping the Overview / Follow-up phases.** Students often list only 4 phases. Always include all **6 phases** in order.
> 4. **Saying defects are "fixed" in the inspection meeting.** This is strictly forbidden in Fagan's process. The meeting only **logs** defects; the author fixes them in the **Rework** phase.
> 5. **Confusing walkthrough/inspection with dynamic testing.** They are **static** techniques — no execution of code.
> 6. **Forgetting the originator.** Mentioning **Michael Fagan, IBM, 1976** shows depth and earns a brownie mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Walkthrough vs Inspection** — both are **static** (no code execution), but walkthrough is **author-led, informal, no mandatory checklist**; inspection is **moderator-led, formal, checklist-driven, 6 phases**.
- **6 Phases of Fagan Inspection:** **Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up.**
- **Key roles in Inspection:** **Moderator, Reader, Scribe, Author, Inspector.**
- **Meeting rules:** Reader paraphrases; Scribe logs; Author is passive; **no fixes proposed in the meeting.**
- **Inspection meeting** is strictly time-boxed (≤ 2 hours); **inspection rate** ≈ 150 lines/person-hour for optimal defect detection.
- **Defect detection rate:** Walkthrough ~20–40%, Inspection ~60–90%.
- **Originator:** Michael Fagan, IBM, 1976.
- **Modern equivalents:** Walkthrough ≈ Pull-request review / pair programming; Inspection ≈ formal security audit / SAST tools (SonarQube).
- **Best for inspections:** Safety-critical (DO-178C), security-sensitive, and standards-heavy code.
- **Best for walkthroughs:** Knowledge transfer, junior developer code, small utility scripts.
- **Always mention** the **checklist** in any inspection-related answer — it is the single most exam-relevant detail.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Debugging and its Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Debugging and its Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Debugging** is the systematic, methodical process of locating, analyzing, and removing the **root cause** of a software defect (bug) from a computer program. It is a *post-execution* analytical activity that begins **after** a failure has been observed during software testing.

In the formal KTU 2024 Software Engineering vocabulary, debugging is positioned as a **corrective maintenance** and **verification** activity. It is **not** synonymous with testing. Testing proves the *existence* of failure; debugging proves the *location* of the fault and removes it.

**Associated Standard Definitions (KTU Glossary):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Error (Mistake)** | A human action that produces an incorrect result. |
| **Defect (Bug/Fault)** | A flaw in a software system that may cause it to behave incorrectly. |
| **Failure** | The observable manifestation of a defect during execution. |
| **Debugging** | The process of finding the *defect* that caused the *failure*. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Detecting a Water Leak in a House:**
> Imagine a house where the floor of the living room is wet. **Testing** is when you walk through the house and *discover* the wet floor (symptom/failure). **Debugging** is the process of:
> 1. Tracing the plumbing blueprint (code reading),
> 2. Following the pipe from the wet spot back toward the source (backtracking),
> 3. Listening for hissing sounds at various joints (instrumentation),
> 4. Cutting small inspection windows to find the cracked joint (breakpoints),
> 5. Finally, replacing the broken pipe section (fixing the bug).
>
> The wet floor is the *failure*. The cracked pipe is the *defect*. Walking through the house is *testing*. The whole investigation is *debugging*.

## 1.3 Why Debugging is a Distinct Engineering Discipline

- **Determinism Challenges:** Software is largely deterministic, yet bugs arise from complex state interactions, timing, and edge cases.
- **Cognitive Load:** Modern programs have millions of lines of code; the *search space* for a bug is enormous.
- **The Debugging Paradox:** The very act of debugging requires running the program, but running the program often hides the bug (Heisenbug effect).
- **Economic Impact:** Industry data shows that debugging can consume up to **50%** of the total development time of a software system.

## 1.4 The Heisenbug Phenomenon (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!WARNING]
> A **Heisenbug** is a bug that *changes its behavior* or *disappears* when you try to study it (named after Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle). It usually happens because the act of adding `print` statements or attaching a debugger alters the timing, memory layout, or compiler optimization, masking the original defect. The opposite — a bug that *only* appears when a debugger is attached — is called a **Bohr bug**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Debugging Process (KTU Module Standard)

The debugging workflow is universally modeled as a **5-stage process**. This is a high-yield diagram-topic for KTU 14-mark questions.

### Step 1 — Reproduce the Defect
- Create the **smallest possible, repeatable** test case that reliably triggers the failure.
- Identify the input conditions, environment, and execution path.

### Step 2 — Isolate the Source
- Narrow the suspect region of code using binary search-like mental partitioning.
- Apply a hypothesis-driven approach: *form a theory, test it, refine it*.

### Step 3 — Identify the Cause
- Once isolated, study the local logic, data flow, and control flow to find the precise line of code responsible.

### Step 4 — Design and Apply the Fix
- Modify the code to remove the defect.
- **Crucial Rule:** Ensure the fix does not introduce *regression* (new bugs in previously working code).

### Step 5 — Verify and Validate
- Re-run the failing test case to confirm the fix.
- Re-run the **regression test suite** to ensure no existing functionality broke.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Conceptual Metrics)

While debugging is not a purely mathematical subject, KTU examiners love quantitative metrics. The following table summarizes the **industry-standard formulas** used to quantify debugging effort and effectiveness.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time To Debug (MTTD)** | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{d,i}}{n}$ | Average time spent locating a defect across $n$ incidents. | Hours |
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defects per thousand lines of code; lower is better. | Defects / KLOC |
| **Debugging Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{d}}{N_{d} + N_{r}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of defects fixed correctly ($N_d$) to total attempts. | Percent (%) |
| **Bug Introduction Rate** | $B = \dfrac{\text{Bugs introduced}}{\text{Function Points}}$ | Forward-looking quality predictor. | Bugs/FP |
| **Cost Ratio (Debug : Code)** | $C_{ratio} = \dfrac{C_{debug}}{C_{code}}$ | Typical mature value is approximately **2 : 1**. | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value or conditional separators in mathematical text use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $x \vert y$) — never the bare pipe character `|`, which would break markdown rendering.

## 2.3 Categories of Debugging Methods (The Core KTU Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups debugging methods into three major families:

### Family A — Manual / Intuition-Driven Methods

> [!NOTE]
> These rely entirely on human cognitive reasoning, without (or with minimal) tool support.

1. **Brute Force Method**
   - Add `print`/`log` statements liberally throughout the code.
   - Run the program; collect massive output; visually scan for anomalies.
   - **Pros:** Simple, requires no tools. **Cons:** Time-consuming, output overload, can cause Heisenbugs.

2. **Backtracking Method**
   - Starting from the *symptom* (failure point), walk **backwards** through the source code.
   - At each step, manually verify whether the state could have led to the failure.
   - **Best for:** Small, localized programs. **Fails** in large systems with many call paths.

3. **Cause Elimination Method**
   - List all possible causes of the failure (hypotheses).
   - Run targeted tests to **eliminate** causes one by one.
   - Related to **bisection** (binary search of the code history/code region).

### Family B — Analytical / Reasoning-Based Methods

4. **Induction Method (Bottom-Up)**
   - Collect *all* data about the bug: inputs, outputs, error messages, logs.
   - From the data, induce (infer) a general rule or theory of the defect.
   - Formula: $\text{Data} \longrightarrow \text{Pattern} \longrightarrow \text{Hypothesis} \longrightarrow \text{Theorem}$.

5. **Deduction Method (Top-Down)**
   - Start from a *general hypothesis* (e.g., "the array is overflowing").
   - Deduce specific sub-hypotheses and test each one.
   - Formula: $\text{General Theory} \longrightarrow \text{Sub-hypotheses} \longrightarrow \text{Contradiction} \longrightarrow \text{Root Cause}$.

### Family C — Automated / Tool-Driven Methods

6. **Interactive Debuggers (IDEs)**
   - Tools like **GDB**, **LLDB**, **WinDbg**, **Eclipse Debugger**, **IntelliJ Debugger**, **VS Code Debugger**.
   - Provide features: **breakpoints**, **step-over**, **step-into**, **step-out**, **watchpoints**, **variable inspection**, **call stack tracing**.

7. **Automated Debugging Tools (Delta Debugging, Slicing)**
   - **Program Slicing:** Automatically extract the subset of statements that affect a variable's value at a specific point.
   - **Delta Debugging:** Automate the test-case minimization process to find the *minimal failing input*.
   - **Symbolic Execution:** Explore all execution paths systematically using symbolic inputs (e.g., KLEE, angr).

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix of Debugging Methods

| Method | Skill Required | Tool Support | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force | Low | None / Logs | Quick triage | Inefficient at scale |
| Backtracking | Medium | None | Small programs | Path explosion |
| Cause Elimination | High | None | Hypotheses-rich | Slow elimination |
| Induction | High | None | Data-rich failures | Requires data collection |
| Deduction | Very High | None | Theory-driven | Hard to formulate |
| Interactive Debugger | Medium | IDE/GDB | Most production use | Heisenbug risk |
| Program Slicing | High | Specialized tools | Large codebases | Tool cost |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Debugging flight-control firmware where a Heisenbug could be fatal — *symbolic execution* is standard.
- **Finance:** Algorithmic trading systems use *record-and-replay* debugging to reproduce a billion-dollar trade glitch.
- **Web/Cloud:** Distributed systems use *distributed tracing* (OpenTelemetry) for debugging cross-service failures.
- **AI/ML:** Debugging involves tracing tensor shapes and gradient flows, not just variables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Debugging Cost vs Time (The Classical Curve)

A frequently asked 14-mark KTU question asks you to derive the relationship between debugging effort and defect discovery rate. The classical model is the **Software Reliability Growth Model (SRGM)** — specifically the **Goel-Okumoto (Exponential) Model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a step-by-step derivation of the expected number of remaining defects as a function of debugging time.

### Given
- $\mu(t)$ = expected cumulative number of defects detected by time $t$.
- $a$ = total expected number of defects in the system (asymptote).
- $b$ = defect detection rate constant (per unit time).

### Assumption (Goel-Okumoto)
The rate of defect detection is proportional to the *remaining* (undetected) defects:

$$\frac{d\mu(t)}{dt} = b \cdot (a - \mu(t))$$

### Step 1 — Recognize it is a first-order linear ODE.
This is a standard separable differential equation.

### Step 2 — Separate variables.
Rearrange so that all $\mu$ terms are on the left, $t$ on the right:

$$\frac{d\mu(t)}{a - \mu(t)} = b \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate both sides.
Apply the initial condition $\mu(0) = 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{\mu(t)} \frac{dx}{a - x} = \int_{0}^{t} b \, d\tau$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.
The left integral is $-\ln(a - \mu)$ evaluated from $0$ to $\mu(t)$. The right integral is $b \tau$ from $0$ to $t$:

$$-\ln(a - \mu(t)) + \ln(a) = b \cdot t$$

### Step 5 — Simplify using logarithm laws.
Combine the two logs on the left:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{a}{a - \mu(t)}\right) = b \cdot t$$

### Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides to remove the log.

$$\frac{a}{a - \mu(t)} = e^{b t}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the remaining defects.
Let $N_{rem}(t) = a - \mu(t)$ be the number of defects *still hidden* at time $t$:

$$N_{rem}(t) = a \cdot e^{-b t}$$

### Step 8 — Final Equation (The KTU High-Yield Result).

$$\boxed{\,N_{rem}(t) = a \cdot e^{-b t}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This equation tells us that debugging effort $t$ follows an **exponential decay** of remaining bugs. Early debugging yields rapid bug discovery; later debugging yields diminishing returns. This is why KTU examiners emphasize *when* to stop debugging and ship.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Debugging Method

A common KTU question: *"Given a scenario, which debugging method would you apply?"* The matrix below provides the examiner-grade answer.

| Scenario Description | Symptoms | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small C program, one logic error, single function | Wrong output for specific input | **Backtracking** | Small code base, easy to trace backwards |
| 50,000-line enterprise system, intermittent crash | Random SIGSEGV, no pattern | **Interactive Debugger + Core Dump** | Need breakpoints & call-stack inspection |
| Multithreaded race condition, no repro | Bug appears 1 in 1000 runs | **Instrumentation + Deduction** | Must form hypothesis on thread interleaving |
| Legacy code with no documentation | Cannot understand code | **Brute Force (print/log) + Induction** | No mental model, must gather data first |
| Performance regression after a refactor | Slow execution, profiler shows hotspot | **Profiling + Program Slicing** | Slice code to the hot path |
| Distributed microservice failure | Cascade error across services | **Distributed Tracing (OpenTelemetry)** | Cross-service correlation required |
| Security vulnerability (buffer overflow) | Crash on large input | **Symbolic Execution (e.g., KLEE)** | Need to find worst-case input automatically |

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost of Debugging per Phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **1-10-100 Rule** (or **Boehm's Rule**) and is a guaranteed 7-mark KTU sub-question.

Let $C_1$ be the cost to fix a defect found in the requirements phase. Then:

- Requirements phase fix: $C_1$
- Design phase fix: $C_1 \times 10$
- Coding phase fix: $C_1 \times 10^2 = 100 \, C_1$
- Post-release fix: $C_1 \times 10^3 = 1000 \, C_1$

### Mathematical Form
The cost grows geometrically with the phase index $k$ (where $k=0$ is requirements):

$$C_{fix}(k) = C_1 \cdot 10^{k}$$

### Worked Example
Suppose a defect costs **₹500** to fix during requirements. What is the cost of the same defect if it escapes to production ($k=3$)?

$$C_{fix}(3) = 500 \cdot 10^{3} = \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often write "10 times" or "100 times" without showing the geometric index. The KTU answer key requires the explicit formula $C_{fix}(k) = C_1 \cdot 10^{k}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Debugging Process Flow (KTU Standard Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Failure] --> B[Reproduce Defect Reliably]
    B --> C[Collect Diagnostic Data]
    C --> D{Form Hypothesis}
    D -->|Confirmed| E[Locate Exact Source Code Line]
    D -->|Rejected| C
    E --> F[Design the Fix]
    F --> G[Apply Code Patch]
    G --> H[Run Unit Test for Bug]
    H --> I{Test Passes?}
    I -->|No| D
    I -->|Yes| J[Run Full Regression Suite]
    J --> K{All Tests Pass?}
    K -->|No| L[Investigate Regression]
    L --> F
    K -->|Yes| M[Close Defect Ticket]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop `C → D → C` represents the **iterative hypothesize-test cycle**, the cognitive core of debugging. Notice the two quality gates: a passing unit test (gate 1) and a clean regression run (gate 2).

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Debugging Methods (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Debugging Methods]
    Root --> M1[Manual Methods]
    Root --> M2[Analytical Methods]
    Root --> M3[Tool-Based Methods]
    M1 --> M1A[Brute Force]
    M1 --> M1B[Backtracking]
    M1 --> M1C[Cause Elimination]
    M2 --> M2A[Induction]
    M2 --> M2B[Deduction]
    M3 --> M3A[Interactive Debugger]
    M3 --> M3B[Program Slicing]
    M3 --> M3C[Symbolic Execution]
    M3 --> M3D[Delta Debugging]
```

## 4.3 The Debugging Stack (Architecture View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Source Code] --> L2[Compiler / Build System]
    L2 --> L3[Binary Executable]
    L3 --> L4[Runtime Environment]
    L4 --> L5[Failure Manifestation]
    L5 --> L6[Debugging Tool Layer]
    L6 --> L6A[IDE Debugger]
    L6 --> L6B[Log Analyzer]
    L6 --> L6C[Core Dump Analyzer]
    L6 --> L6D[Symbolic Executor]
    L6 --> L7[Engineer Analysis]
    L7 --> L8[Source Code Patch]
    L8 -.->|Iterate| L1
```

## 4.4 Hypothesis-Driven Debugging Cycle (Induction vs Deduction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Induction["INDUCTION (Bottom-Up)"]
        I1[Collect All Symptoms] --> I2[Find Patterns in Data]
        I2 --> I3[Infer General Theory]
    end
    subgraph Deduction["DEDUCTION (Top-Down)"]
        D1[Form General Hypothesis] --> D2[Derive Sub-Hypotheses]
        D2 --> D3[Test Each Sub-Hypothesis]
    end
    Induction --> Result[Root Cause Identified]
    Deduction --> Result
```

## 4.5 Common Debugger Breakpoint Strategy Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Run
    Run --> BreakpointHit: Execution Reaches BP
    BreakpointHit --> Inspect: Examine Variables
    Inspect --> StepOver: Resume Line-by-Line
    StepOver --> Inspect
    Inspect --> StepInto: Enter Function Call
    StepInto --> Inspect
    Inspect --> StepOut: Exit Function
    StepOut --> Inspect
    Inspect --> Continue: Run to Next BP
    Continue --> Run
    Continue --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define the term **debugging**. How is it different from software testing?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Debugging** is the systematic process of locating, analyzing, and removing the root cause of a software defect (bug) from a computer program. It is a corrective activity that begins *after* a failure is observed.

**Difference from Testing:**

| Aspect | Testing | Debugging |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Show that defects exist | Locate and remove the defect |
| Activity type | Verification (preventive) | Correction (reactive) |
| Initiation | Before failure | After a known failure |
| Tools | Test runners, harnesses | Debuggers, log analyzers |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the difference table — KTU key standard.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *What is a **Heisenbug**? Why is it difficult to debug?*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
A **Heisenbug** is a software bug that *changes its behavior or disappears* when an attempt is made to study or debug it. The term is a play on Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle.

**Why it is difficult:**
1. The act of inserting debug code (`print`, `log`) alters timing, memory layout, or compiler optimization, hiding the original fault.
2. Often occurs in concurrent, time-dependent, or memory-layout-sensitive code (race conditions, uninitialized memory).
3. May require specialized tools like record-and-replay debuggers to reproduce reliably.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the reasoning — KTU key standard.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Questions (ESE Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU's 2024 End Semester Examination, each Module contains **one full 14-mark question with internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q7A]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the systematic process of debugging in detail, with a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the following debugging methods: *Brute Force*, *Backtracking*, *Induction*, and *Deduction*. Mention one engineering scenario where each is best suited. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
The systematic debugging process consists of **5 well-defined stages**:

1. **Reproduce the Defect (1 mark):** Create the smallest reliable test case. Without reproducibility, debugging is impossible.

2. **Isolate the Source (1.5 marks):** Use binary search on the code (e.g., comment out halves) to narrow down the suspect region. Apply a *cause-elimination* sub-process.

3. **Identify the Cause (1.5 marks):** Examine local logic, data flow, and control flow. Form a hypothesis, test it, and refine it.

4. **Design and Apply the Fix (1.5 marks):** Patch the code. Critical rule — the fix must **not introduce regressions**. Always write a regression test *before* the fix.

5. **Verify and Validate (1.5 marks):** Re-run the failing test (must pass) and the full regression suite (must still pass). Update documentation if necessary.

*(Include the flowchart from Section 4.1 — the KTU examiner expects a labeled diagram worth at least 1.5 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Method | Core Logic | Best-Suited Engineering Scenario | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brute Force** | Add many `print`/`log` statements, scan output for anomalies | Initial triage of an unfamiliar codebase | Inefficient at scale |
| **Backtracking** | Trace execution **backwards** from the failure point | Small C program with a single faulty loop | Path explosion in large code |
| **Induction** | Collect all data → find patterns → infer theory | Data-rich failure with full logs available | Requires good logging |
| **Deduction** | Start from general theory → derive sub-hypotheses → eliminate | Complex systems where a known theory exists | Hard to formulate the initial theory |

*(Award 1 mark for each method's core logic, 0.5 mark for scenario, 0.5 mark for limitation. Total 4 marks for the table. Add 3 marks for a well-written concluding paragraph emphasizing when to choose which method.)*

---

### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q7B]**
>
> **(a)** Define debugging. Explain the **Induction** and **Deduction** methods of debugging with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** What is a debugger? Explain the various features of an interactive debugger (such as GDB or any IDE debugger) with their purpose. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Debugging is the systematic process of locating and removing the root cause of a defect in a software program.

**Induction Method (3 marks):**
- *Philosophy:* Bottom-up reasoning.
- *Steps:*
  1. Gather all available data about the failure (input, output, logs, environment).
  2. Identify *patterns* in the data (e.g., "fails only when input contains 'X'").
  3. From the pattern, induce a *general theory* (e.g., "the function mishandles unicode characters").
  4. Test the theory and refine.
- *Example:* A web app returns HTTP 500 only when the username contains a space. Induction: collect logs, find a pattern, infer that the input validator does not handle spaces.

**Deduction Method (3 marks):**
- *Philosophy:* Top-down reasoning.
- *Steps:*
  1. Begin with a *general hypothesis* (e.g., "the database connection is leaking").
  2. Deduce specific *sub-hypotheses* (e.g., "the connection is not closed in the error path").
  3. Test each sub-hypothesis. Eliminate the wrong ones.
  4. The last surviving hypothesis is the root cause.
- *Example:* Program slows down over time. General theory: resource leak. Sub-hypotheses: file handles? DB connections? Memory? Each is tested in turn.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

A **debugger** (1 mark) is a software tool that allows a developer to *control* the execution of a program, *inspect* its state at chosen moments, and *modify* it to understand or fix defects.

**Features of an Interactive Debugger (6 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Feature | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Breakpoint** | Pauses execution at a specified line/function. |
| **Step Over** | Executes the current line; if it is a function call, executes it fully without entering. |
| **Step Into** | Enters the called function, line by line. |
| **Step Out** | Completes the current function and pauses at the caller's next line. |
| **Watch / Inspect** | Monitors the value of a variable or expression throughout execution. |
| **Call Stack View** | Shows the chain of function calls leading to the current line. |

**Bonus (optional):** *Watchpoint* (breaks when a variable's value changes) and *Conditional Breakpoint* (breaks only when a user-defined condition is true).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing testing and debugging** in the opening definition. Loss: **2 marks** in Q7A(a) and similar questions.
> 2. **Writing a generic essay** on "the importance of debugging" without the specific sub-parts. KTU keys *demand* the table-comparison format.
> 3. **Omitting the diagram** (flowchart) in 7-mark "explain in detail" questions. The KTU 2024 scheme allocates **1.5 to 2 marks** for the diagram alone.
> 4. **Mixing up induction and deduction.** A common error: writing induction where the question asks for deduction. Memorize: **Induction = Data → Theory; Deduction = Theory → Data**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the Heisenbug effect** in debugger-related questions. Examiners consider this a hallmark of depth.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Code Debugging and its Methods**

- [x] **Debugging ≠ Testing.** Testing finds failures; debugging finds the *defect* behind them.
- [x] The **5-stage debugging process**: Reproduce → Isolate → Identify → Fix → Verify.
- [x] **Brute Force** = mass print/logging. Simple but inefficient.
- [x] **Backtracking** = walk backwards from the symptom. Best for small programs.
- [x] **Cause Elimination** = list all possible causes, eliminate one by one.
- [x] **Induction** = *Data → Pattern → Theory* (Bottom-up).
- [x] **Deduction** = *Theory → Sub-hypotheses → Test* (Top-down).
- [x] **Heisenbug** = a bug that disappears when you try to study it.
- [x] **Bohr bug** = a bug that appears *only* when a debugger is attached.
- [x] **Interactive Debugger** features: Breakpoint, Step Over, Step Into, Step Out, Watch, Call Stack.
- [x] **Program Slicing** = automated subset extraction of code affecting a variable.
- [x] **Symbolic Execution** = exploring all paths using symbolic inputs (tools: KLEE, angr).
- [x] **Delta Debugging** = automated minimization of failing test cases.
- [x] **SRGM formula** (Goel-Okumoto): $N_{rem}(t) = a \cdot e^{-b t}$. Defects decay exponentially with debugging time.
- [x] **1-10-100 Rule**: $C_{fix}(k) = C_1 \cdot 10^{k}$ — cost grows 10× per phase.
- [x] **Defect Density**: $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$.
- [x] Always include a **flowchart or table** in 7-mark "explain" questions — they are the high-scoring differentiator.
- [x] Always end the answer with the **regression-test verification** step — examiners reward completeness.
- [x] Memorize at least **two named tools** (GDB, KLEE, WinDbg, Eclipse Debugger, IntelliJ) for full marks in tool-based questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing - Unit Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing - Unit Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Unit Testing** is the lowest level of software testing in which individual *units* (or *components*) of a software application are tested in isolation from the rest of the system. A **unit** is the smallest testable part of any software — typically a single function, method, procedure, module, class, or object.

According to the **IEEE Standard 829-2008** and the KTU 2024 OECST723 syllabus, unit testing is performed during the *validation* stage of software development, usually by the developer who wrote the code, to verify that each unit performs as designed and conforms to its specification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"Unit testing is a software testing method by which individual units of source code are tested to determine whether they are fit for use. It is typically automated and forms the foundation of the testing pyramid."*

### Key Terminology Box

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Unit** | Smallest testable piece of code (function/method/class) |
| **SUT** | System Under Test — the code unit being tested |
| **Test Case** | A set of conditions/variables used to determine correctness |
| **Test Fixture** | Fixed state used as a baseline for running tests |
| **Test Stub** | A dummy module that provides canned responses |
| **Test Driver** | A main routine that calls the unit under test |
| **Test Suite** | A collection of test cases for a unit/module |
| **Assertion** | A boolean expression that must hold true for test to pass |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Car Factory Assembly Line"

Imagine a **massive car manufacturing plant**. Before the entire car is assembled, every individual component is rigorously tested:
- The **engine** is tested on a standalone test bench.
- The **brake pads** are tested for friction coefficient.
- The **steering wheel** is tested for rotational torque.
- The **air conditioner compressor** is tested in a climate-controlled chamber.

Each component is tested **in isolation** — *without* being mounted on the car. Only the components that pass these isolated tests are then integrated to form the complete vehicle.

> **In software terms, the "engine" or "brake pad" is your UNIT, the "test bench" is your TEST HARNESS, and the "test results" are your ASSERTIONS.**

If a car fails the brake test on the assembly line, you don't disassemble the whole car to find the problem — you already know it's the brake. Similarly, if a unit test fails, the developer knows *exactly* which function or method has the bug, making debugging **fast, localized, and cheap**.

## 1.3 Why Unit Testing Matters — The Cost of Bugs

> [!NOTE]
> **Boehm's Curve (KTU Frequently Asked):** The cost of fixing a defect grows exponentially as it propagates through the software lifecycle. A bug found during **unit testing** may cost **\$1** to fix, but the same bug found after deployment can cost **\$10,000+**. This is the central economic argument for thorough unit testing.

| Phase When Bug is Found | Relative Cost to Fix |
|-------------------------|---------------------|
| Unit Testing | 1× (baseline) |
| Integration Testing | 10× |
| System Testing | 100× |
| Post-Release | 1000×+ |

## 1.4 Properties of a "Good" Unit Test (FIRST Principles)

The **FIRST** acronym is universally expected in KTU answer scripts:

- **F — Fast**: Unit tests must run in milliseconds, not minutes.
- **I — Independent/Isolated**: Tests must not depend on each other or on external state.
- **R — Repeatable**: Same input must produce same result every time.
- **S — Self-Validating**: Tests must automatically report pass/fail via assertions (no manual inspection).
- **T — Timely/Thorough**: Tests should be written *alongside* the code (TDD), not after.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend Question:** Examiners frequently ask *"List the characteristics of a good unit test"* — the FIRST acronym is the expected answer framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Testing in the Software Testing Lifecycle (V-Model Context)

The KTU 2024 syllabus places unit testing at the **bottom of the V-Model**, corresponding directly to the *lowest-level design* (module/component design). The testing pyramid is:

```
            ╱╲
           ╱  ╲         ← Acceptance Testing (Manual, E2E)
          ╱────╲
         ╱      ╲       ← System Testing
        ╱────────╲
       ╱          ╲     ← Integration Testing
      ╱────────────╲
     ╱              ╲   ← Unit Testing (AUTOMATED, FAST, MANY)
    ╱────────────────╲
```

**The Testing Pyramid Rule:** More unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewer system tests, fewest acceptance tests. Each layer aggregates upwards.

## 2.2 Types of Unit Testing

### 2.2.1 Structural (White-Box) Unit Testing

Tests the **internal structure**, code paths, and control flow of the unit.

| Technique | Description | KTU Relevance |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Statement Coverage** | Every executable statement executed at least once | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| **Branch Coverage** | Every branch (true/false) of each control structure executed | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| **Condition Coverage** | Every boolean sub-expression evaluated both true and false | ⭐⭐ High |
| **Path Coverage** | Every independent execution path traversed | ⭐⭐ High |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity Testing** | Number of linearly independent paths | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| **Mutation Testing** | Inject faults and check if tests detect them | ⭐⭐ Moderate |

### 2.2.2 Functional (Black-Box) Unit Testing

Tests the **input-output behavior** of the unit, treating it as a black box.

| Technique | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Equivalence Partitioning** | Divide inputs into equivalence classes |
| **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** | Test at the edges of equivalence classes |
| **Error Guessing** | Use experience to guess likely failure points |
| **Decision Table Testing** | Tabulate inputs and corresponding outputs |

## 2.3 xUnit — The Universal Unit Testing Framework Family

**xUnit** is the collective name for the family of unit testing frameworks that share the architecture designed by **Kent Beck** (SUnit for Smalltalk, 1998). The "x" denotes the language.

| Framework | Language | Origin |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **JUnit** | Java | Kent Beck & Erich Gamma (2000) |
| **NUnit** | C#/.NET | Initially port of JUnit |
| **pytest / unittest** | Python | Python community |
| **Google Test (gTest)** | C++ | Google |
| **Jest / Mocha** | JavaScript | Community |
| **Kotlin Test** | Kotlin | JetBrains |
| **XCTest** | Swift/Obj-C | Apple |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Special Note:** For the OECST723 course, **JUnit** is the canonical example. Be prepared to draw/write the *test lifecycle annotations*: `@Test`, `@Before`, `@After`, `@BeforeClass`, `@AfterClass`, `@Ignore`.

### 2.3.1 The xUnit Test Lifecycle (Architecture)

```
┌──────────────────────┐
│   Test Suite Setup   │  @BeforeClass / @AfterClass
└──────────┬───────────┘
           │
           ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│   Test Fixture Set   │  @Before  ──┐  (runs for EACH test)
└──────────┬───────────┘             │
           ▼                         │
┌──────────────────────┐             │
│   @Test Method       │ ◄───────────┘
│   (SUT Invocation +  │
│    Assertions)       │
└──────────┬───────────┘
           │
           ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Test Fixture Tear   │  @After    ──┐
└──────────┬───────────┘             │
           │                         │
           ▼                         │
       Repeat for next @Test ◄───────┘
           │
           ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Test Suite Teardown │  @AfterClass
└──────────────────────┘
```

## 2.4 Test-Driven Development (TDD) — The Red-Green-Refactor Cycle

**TDD** is a development methodology where unit tests are written *before* the production code. The mantra is **"Red → Green → Refactor"**.

| Phase | Description | Time Spent (Typical) |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| 🔴 **Red** | Write a failing test for the next bit of functionality | 1–2 min |
| 🟢 **Green** | Write the *minimum* code to make the test pass | 2–5 min |
| 🔵 **Refactor** | Clean up code while keeping tests green | 2–5 min |

This cycle is repeated every few minutes throughout the development process.

## 2.5 Stubs, Drivers, and Mocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorites:** "Differentiate between stubs, drivers, and mocks" appears in nearly every previous KTU question paper.

| Tool | Purpose | Direction | Returns |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Driver** | Calls the unit under test | Top-down integration helper | Real output |
| **Stub** | Replaces a *lower-level* module | Top-down integration helper | Canned/hardcoded response |
| **Mock** | A *smart* stub that also verifies expectations | Both directions | Canned + tracks calls |
| **Fake** | Working implementation unsuitable for production (e.g., in-memory DB) | Both | Real but simplified |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Coverage Metrics & Complexity

| # | Metric / Formula | Description | Range |
|---|------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Statement Coverage (SC)** $\;=\;\dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}}\times 100\%$ | Percentage of statements run | $[0, 100\%]$ |
| 2 | **Branch Coverage (BC)** $\;=\;\dfrac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}}\times 100\%$ | Percentage of decision outcomes | $[0, 100\%]$ |
| 3 | **Path Coverage (PC)** $\;=\;\dfrac{\text{Independent Paths Tested}}{\text{Total Independent Paths}}\times 100\%$ | All linearly independent paths | $[0, 100\%]$ |
| 4 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** $\;V(G)\;=\;e\;-\;n\;+\;2p$ | Graph-theoretic complexity (nodes *n*, edges *e*, connected components *p*) | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 1}$ |
| 5 | **V(G) via Decision Points** $\;V(G)\;=\;1\;+\;\sum \text{decision points}$ | Practical count (each `if`, `case`, `for`, `while`, `&&`, `\|\|`) | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 1}$ |
| 6 | **Mutation Score (MS)** $\;=\;\dfrac{\text{Killed Mutants}}{\text{Total Mutants}}\times 100\%$ | Effectiveness of test suite in detecting faults | $[0, 100\%]$ |
| 7 | **Defect Density (DD)** $\;=\;\dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{KLOC (Thousands of Lines)}}$ | Defects per KLOC | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Continuous Integration (CI):** Unit tests are the gate that every code commit must pass (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI).
- **Regression Safety:** When refactoring, unit tests act as a safety net, instantly flagging any broken behavior.
- **Living Documentation:** Well-named unit tests serve as executable specifications of how the code is supposed to behave.
- **Refactoring Enablement:** Without unit tests, refactoring is terrifying. With them, it is routine and safe.
- **Industry Adoption:** Tech giants like Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Amazon enforce strict unit-test coverage policies (e.g., Google requires 100% coverage on critical-path code).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cyclomatic Complexity Calculation

**Problem (KTU Standard Pattern):** Given the following C/Java function, compute the **Cyclomatic Complexity** and the **minimum number of test cases** required for complete path coverage.

```java
public int computeGrade(int marks) {
    int grade = 0;                              // statement 1
    if (marks >= 90) {                          // decision 1
        grade = 5;
    } else if (marks >= 75) {                   // decision 2
        grade = 4;
    } else if (marks >= 60) {                   // decision 3
        if (marks >= 50 && marks < 60) {         // decision 4 (compound)
            grade = 3;
        } else {
            grade = 2;
        }
    } else {
        grade = 1;
    }
    return grade;                               // statement n
}
```

### Step 1 — Identify Decision Points

A *decision point* is any control structure that can cause a branch.

| # | Construct | Type | Decision Point? |
|---|-----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | `if (marks >= 90)` | Branch | ✅ Yes (+1) |
| 2 | `else if (marks >= 75)` | Branch | ✅ Yes (+1) |
| 3 | `else if (marks >= 60)` | Branch | ✅ Yes (+1) |
| 4 | `marks >= 50 && marks < 60` | Compound | ✅ Yes (+1 for `&&` split into two sub-decisions) |
| 5 | `else` (inside inner if) | Branch | ✅ Yes (+1) |
| 6 | `else` (outer) | Branch | ✅ Yes (+1) |

> **Total decision points = 6**

### Step 2 — Apply the V(G) Formula

$$
V(G) \;=\; 1 \;+\; \sum \text{(decision points)} \;=\; 1 \;+\; 6 \;=\; 7
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Cyclomatic Complexity } V(G) = 7\;}
$$

This means:
- The function has **7 linearly independent paths**.
- A minimum of **7 unit test cases** is required to achieve **100% path coverage** (in theory).
- A function with $V(G) > 10$ is considered **high-risk and difficult to test** — refactoring is recommended.

### Step 4 — Verification via Flow Graph Method

Construct the control flow graph and count:
- **Nodes (n)** = 8 (entry, exit, plus 6 logical blocks)
- **Edges (e)** = 13
- **Connected components (p)** = 1

$$
V(G) \;=\; e \;-\; n \;+\; 2p \;=\; 13 \;-\; 8 \;+\; 2(1) \;=\; 7 \;\;\checkmark
$$

Both methods agree — excellent cross-verification.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Coverage Metric Calculation

**Problem:** A module has **120 executable statements** and **30 decision branches**. A test suite executes **96 statements** and traverses **24 branches**. Compute Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, and Path Coverage (given 8 independent paths and 5 tested).

### Step 1 — Statement Coverage

$$
SC \;=\; \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{96}{120} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;SC \;=\; 80\%\;}
$$

### Step 2 — Branch Coverage

$$
BC \;=\; \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{24}{30} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;BC \;=\; 80\%\;}
$$

### Step 3 — Path Coverage

$$
PC \;=\; \frac{\text{Paths Tested}}{\text{Total Independent Paths}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{5}{8} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;PC \;=\; 62.5\%\;}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Mutation Score Calculation

**Problem:** A mutation testing tool generated **40 mutants** of a `calculateTax()` function. The test suite *killed* **36 mutants** and *survived* **4**.

$$
MS \;=\; \frac{\text{Killed Mutants}}{\text{Total Mutants}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{36}{40} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;MS \;=\; 90\%\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 4 surviving mutants indicate the test suite is **insufficient** — there are 4 logical fault patterns it cannot detect. New tests must be added to kill these mutants.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Boundary Value Analysis for a Unit

**Problem:** A function `validateAge(int age)` must accept ages in the range **[18, 60]**. Apply BVA to derive unit test cases.

### Step 1 — Identify Boundaries
- Lower boundary = 18
- Upper boundary = 60
- Below lower = 17
- Above upper = 61

### Step 2 — BVA Test Cases

| Test ID | Input (age) | Expected Output | Category |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|----------|
| TC-01 | 17 | INVALID | Just below lower |
| TC-02 | 18 | VALID | Lower boundary |
| TC-03 | 19 | VALID | Just above lower |
| TC-04 | 59 | VALID | Just below upper |
| TC-05 | 60 | VALID | Upper boundary |
| TC-06 | 61 | INVALID | Just above upper |
| TC-07 | -5 | INVALID | Robustness (negative) |
| TC-08 | 200 | INVALID | Robustness (very high) |
| TC-09 | 0 | INVALID | Robustness (zero) |

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Minimum BVA test cases} = 7 \;\; (\text{or } 9 \text{ for robustness})\;}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to Use What?"

| Testing Technique | Best For | KTU Marks Weightage | Coverage Level |
|-------------------|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| Statement Coverage | Quick sanity test | ⭐⭐ | Lowest |
| Branch Coverage | Standard requirement | ⭐⭐⭐ | Better |
| Condition Coverage | Complex boolean expressions | ⭐⭐ | Decent |
| Path Coverage | Critical financial/safety code | ⭐⭐⭐ | Highest |
| Mutation Testing | Test suite *quality* evaluation | ⭐⭐ | Meta-evaluation |
| Boundary Value Analysis | Numeric range inputs | ⭐⭐⭐ | Excellent for ranges |
| Equivalence Partitioning | Reducing redundant test cases | ⭐⭐ | Efficient |

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Equivalence Partitioning for `login(username, password)`

**Problem:** A login function accepts:
- `username`: alphanumeric, length 6–20
- `password`: any character, length 8–30

### Step 1 — Identify Partitions

| Parameter | Invalid Partition 1 | Invalid Partition 2 | Valid Partition | Invalid Partition 3 |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| `username` | length < 6 | length > 20 | length ∈ [6, 20] AND alphanumeric | non-alphanumeric |
| `password` | length < 8 | length > 30 | length ∈ [8, 30] | — |

### Step 2 — One Representative Test Per Partition

- **Valid:** `("user123", "pass1234")` → login success
- **Invalid Username (short):** `("usr", "pass1234")` → error
- **Invalid Username (long):** `("x".repeat(21), "pass1234")` → error
- **Invalid Password (short):** `("user123", "pwd")` → error
- **Invalid Password (long):** `("user123", "x".repeat(31))` → error

This is the **canonical KTU pattern** for equivalence partitioning problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Unit Testing Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Unit to Test] --> B[Identify Unit Boundary]
    B --> C[Choose Test Strategy: White-Box or Black-Box]
    C --> D[Write Test Cases using BVA or Equivalence]
    D --> E[Create Test Fixture: setUp Method]
    E --> F[Invoke System Under Test SUT]
    F --> G{Assertion Holds?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mark Test as PASS Green]
    G -- No --> I[Mark Test as FAIL Red]
    I --> J[Log Failure: Stack Trace and Inputs]
    J --> K[Developer Fixes the Bug]
    K --> L[Re-run All Tests in Suite]
    L --> F
    H --> M{More Test Cases?}
    M -- Yes --> D
    M -- No --> N[Generate Coverage Report]
    N --> O{Coverage Threshold Met?}
    O -- No --> P[Add More Test Cases]
    P --> D
    O -- Yes --> Q[Commit Code to Repository]
    Q --> R[End: Unit Approved for Integration]
```

## 4.2 The xUnit Framework — Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Developer Writes Test Class] --> B[Test Runner]
    B --> C[Reflection Engine: Discovers Test Methods]
    C --> D[Lifecycle Manager]
    D --> E[BeforeClass Hook]
    E --> F[Before Hook per Test]
    F --> G[Invoke Test Method]
    G --> H{Assertion Pass?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Record PASS in Test Report]
    H -- No --> J[Record FAIL with Exception]
    I --> K[After Hook per Test]
    J --> K
    K --> L{More Tests?}
    L -- Yes --> F
    L -- No --> M[AfterClass Hook]
    M --> N[Generate Test Report XML or HTML]
    N --> O[CI Pipeline Consumes Report]
```

## 4.3 The Test Pyramid — Visual Hierarchy of Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[E2E / UI Tests: Few, Slow, Brittle] --> B[System Tests]
    B --> C[Integration Tests]
    C --> D[Component Tests]
    D --> E[Service Tests]
    E --> F[Unit Tests: Many, Fast, Stable]
    
    style A fill:#FFB6B6
    style B fill:#FFD9A8
    style C fill:#FFEDA8
    style D fill:#D4F1B8
    style E fill:#B8E6D4
    style F fill:#A8D8EA
```

> **Visual Cue:** The pyramid is widest at the **unit test** base, indicating that unit tests should be the **largest in number** and **fastest in execution**.

## 4.4 TDD Red-Green-Refactor Cycle — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Red: Write failing test
    Red --> Green: Write minimum code
    Green --> Refactor: Clean code, keep tests green
    Refactor --> Red: Write next failing test
    Green --> [*]: All requirements covered
    Refactor --> [*]: All requirements covered
```

## 4.5 Stub vs Driver vs Mock — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TopDownIntegration[Top-Down Integration]
        A[Main Module SUT] --> B[STUB: Simulates lower module]
    end
    
    subgraph BottomUpIntegration[Bottom-Up Integration]
        C[Lower Module SUT] --> D[DRIVER: Calls the SUT]
    end
    
    subgraph MockBased[Mock-Based Testing]
        E[Module SUT] <--> F[MOCK: Verifies interactions]
    end
    
    style B fill:#FFE4B5
    style D fill:#B0E0E6
    style F fill:#98FB98
```

## 4.6 JUnit Test Lifecycle — Sequence Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant J as JUnit Runner
    participant F as Test Fixture
    participant S as System Under Test
    
    J->>F: @BeforeClass (one-time setup)
    loop For each @Test method
        J->>F: @Before (per-test setup)
        J->>S: invoke test method
        S-->>J: return result
        J->>J: evaluate assertion
        J->>F: @After (per-test teardown)
    end
    J->>F: @AfterClass (one-time cleanup)
    J->>J: compile report
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Unit Testing. List any four characteristics of a good unit test. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (Word count target: ~80 words):**
