topics.title: System Testing and its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Testing and its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Testing** is a level of software testing that evaluates the **complete and integrated software system** as a whole. It is a black-box testing technique performed on a fully integrated system to verify that the system meets its specified **functional, performance, security, and reliability requirements**. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, system testing is conducted in an environment that closely resembles the production environment and forms a critical phase of the **Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** System Testing is positioned between *Integration Testing* and *Acceptance Testing* in the V-Model. The objective is to validate the system against the Software Requirement Specification (SRS) document.

The **IEEE Standard 829** defines a system test as a test conducted on an integrated system to evaluate the system's compliance with its specified requirements. It verifies both **functional** and **non-functional** requirements simultaneously on the integrated product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **chief engineer responsible for delivering a new commercial passenger aircraft**. Before it is allowed to carry a single paying passenger, you must test the *entire aircraft* — not just the engines, not just the landing gear, not just the avionics. You must test:

- Whether all engines work **together** under full load.
- Whether the **cabin pressure**, **air-conditioning**, **navigation**, and **entertainment systems** all function when flying at 35,000 feet.
- Whether the aircraft can **land safely** in heavy rain with crosswinds.
- Whether it can **survive a lightning strike** and continue operating.

This is **System Testing**. Instead of testing individual components in isolation, we test the **complete, integrated software product** in an environment that mimics the real world. The "aircraft" here is the software application — every module has been built, every module has been integrated, and now we want to ensure that when a real user "flies" the system, everything works end-to-end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** If **Unit Testing** checks a single brick, and **Integration Testing** checks a wall built from those bricks, then **System Testing** checks the **entire building** — including elevators, plumbing, fire alarms, and electrical wiring — all working together as a complete, livable structure.

### 1.3 Why System Testing is Essential

| Reason | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Validates End-to-End Flow** | Confirms complete business workflows function correctly across all integrated modules. |
| **Verifies Non-Functional Requirements** | Checks performance, security, usability, and reliability under realistic conditions. |
| **Mimics Real-World Environment** | Tests are conducted in a production-like setting with actual hardware, software, and network configurations. |
| **Identifies Interface Defects** | Detects mismatches in data formats, timing, and protocols between integrated components. |
| **Validates Against SRS** | Confirms the system fulfills every requirement documented in the Software Requirement Specification. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Goals and Objectives of System Testing

The primary goals of system testing, as described in standard software engineering textbooks and aligned with the KTU syllabus, are:

1. **Verification of Functional Requirements** — Ensure every function described in the SRS works as intended.
2. **Validation of Non-Functional Requirements** — Confirm performance benchmarks, security constraints, and usability standards are met.
3. **Risk Mitigation** — Identify and reduce the probability of failures in production.
4. **Compliance Verification** — Ensure the system adheres to industry standards, legal regulations, and contractual obligations.
5. **End-to-End Workflow Validation** — Verify complete business processes from initiation to closure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Perspective:** When asked to describe system testing, students must emphasize that it tests the **integrated system** against **specified requirements** in a **production-like environment**. Missing any one of these three keywords can cost marks.

### 2.2 Types of System Testing — Comprehensive Classification

System testing is broadly divided into two categories: **Functional Testing** and **Non-Functional Testing**. Each category contains several specific test types.

#### 2.2.1 Functional System Testing Types

**1. Smoke Testing**
- A preliminary test conducted to check whether the basic, critical functionalities of the system are operational.
- Acts as a **gatekeeper** — if smoke testing fails, the build is rejected and returned to the development team.
- The name originates from hardware testing: plug in a device, turn it on, and see if it produces smoke (i.e., fails immediately).

**2. Sanity Testing**
- A narrow, focused test executed to verify that a specific functionality or bug fix works as expected.
- Performed **after receiving a new build** with minor changes to ensure the fix is working and no new defects have been introduced.

**3. Regression Testing**
- Re-execution of previously passed test cases to ensure that recent code changes have **not broken existing functionality**.
- Critical when bugs are fixed, new features are added, or the system is migrated to a new environment.
- Usually automated using tools like **Selenium, QTP/UFT, or TestComplete**.

**4. User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**
- Conducted by the **end-user or client** to validate the system against business requirements.
- Two subtypes: **Alpha Testing** (performed at the developer's site by internal users) and **Beta Testing** (performed at the customer's site by a limited set of real users).

#### 2.2.2 Non-Functional System Testing Types

**5. Performance Testing**
- Evaluates the system's **speed, response time, and stability** under a workload.
- Subtypes include:
  - **Load Testing** — Tests system behavior under expected normal and peak load conditions.
  - **Stress Testing** — Tests system behavior beyond normal operational capacity to determine the breaking point.
  - **Volume Testing** — Tests the system with a large volume of data in the database.
  - **Endurance Testing (Soak Testing)** — Tests system behavior under sustained load over an extended period.

**6. Security Testing**
- Identifies vulnerabilities, threats, and risks in the system to prevent unauthorized access, data breaches, and malicious attacks.
- Includes penetration testing, vulnerability scanning, SQL injection testing, and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) testing.

**7. Usability Testing**
- Evaluates the system's **user-friendliness, learnability, and overall user experience**.
- Conducted with real users under realistic conditions.

**8. Compatibility Testing**
- Verifies that the system works correctly across different **browsers, operating systems, devices, hardware platforms, and network environments**.

**9. Recovery Testing**
- Tests the system's ability to **recover from crashes, hardware failures, and network interruptions** within a specified time period.

**10. Installation Testing**
- Validates the system's **installation, uninstallation, and upgrade processes** on target environments.

**11. Compliance Testing (Regulatory Testing)**
- Ensures the system adheres to **industry standards, government regulations, and legal requirements** (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While system testing does not heavily rely on mathematical formulas, certain **metrics and ratios** are essential for KTU board examinations.

| Metric / Concept | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Defect Density** | $D_d = \dfrac{N_d}{S}$ | Number of defects divided by software size | Defects per KLOC |
| **Test Coverage** | $T_c = \dfrac{T_e}{T_t} \times 100$ | Executed test cases divided by total test cases | Percentage (%) |
| **Requirement Coverage** | $R_c = \dfrac{R_t}{R_{total}} \times 100$ | Tested requirements divided by total requirements | Percentage (%) |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{found}}{D_{found} + D_{escaped}} \times 100$ | Defects found before release divided by total defects | Percentage (%) |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N}$ | Average operational time between failures | Hours / Cycles |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum r_i}{N}$ | Average time taken to repair a failure | Hours / Minutes |
| **System Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$ | Uptime as a percentage of total time | Percentage (%) |
| **Reliability Growth** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of no failure in time interval $t$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> Where:
> - $N_d$ = Number of defects
> - $S$ = Software size in KLOC (Kilo Lines of Code)
> - $T_e$ = Test cases executed, $T_t$ = Total test cases
> - $R_t$ = Requirements tested, $R_{total}$ = Total requirements
> - $D_{found}$ = Defects found during testing, $D_{escaped}$ = Defects found after release
> - $t_i$ = Time before $i$-th failure, $r_i$ = Time to repair $i$-th failure
> - $N$ = Total number of failures observed
> - $\lambda$ = Failure rate (failures per unit time)

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

System testing has direct applications across engineering domains:
- **Aerospace:** Validating flight control software under simulated turbulence, extreme temperatures, and hardware faults.
- **Automotive:** Ensuring ECU (Engine Control Unit) software responds correctly under varying road conditions.
- **Healthcare:** Verifying that patient monitoring systems generate accurate alerts and maintain data integrity.
- **Banking:** Validating transaction processing under concurrent user load to prevent system crashes during peak hours.
- **IoT Systems:** Testing integrated sensor-actuator networks for latency, interoperability, and security.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 System Testing Process — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Breakdown

The system testing process is a structured sequence of activities. Below is the exhaustive derivation of each phase:

#### Step 1: Test Planning
- **Input:** Software Requirement Specification (SRS), System Design Document, Project Plan.
- **Activities:** Define test objectives, scope, test strategy, resource allocation, schedule, and risk assessment.
- **Output:** **System Test Plan document** (as per IEEE 829 standard).

#### Step 2: Test Case Design
- **Input:** System Test Plan, SRS, Use Case documents.
- **Activities:** Write detailed test cases, test scripts, and prepare test data. Identify test conditions from requirements.
- **Output:** **System Test Cases document**, **Test Data**, and **Test Scripts**.

#### Step 3: Test Environment Setup
- **Input:** System Test Plan, hardware/software requirements.
- **Activities:** Configure the production-like environment — install the application, set up servers, databases, network configurations, and test tools.
- **Output:** **Test Environment Readiness Report**.

#### Step 4: Test Execution
- **Input:** Approved test cases, configured test environment, test data.
- **Activities:** Execute test cases, log results (Pass/Fail), capture screenshots, and record actual vs. expected outcomes.
- **Output:** **Test Execution Log**, **Defect Reports** for failed cases.

#### Step 5: Defect Reporting and Tracking
- **Input:** Failed test cases.
- **Activities:** Log defects in a tracking tool (e.g., JIRA, Bugzilla), assign severity and priority, track to closure.
- **Output:** **Defect Log**, **Defect Status Reports**.

#### Step 6: Regression Testing
- **Input:** Fixed defects, updated builds.
- **Activities:** Re-execute previously passed test cases to ensure no regression. Perform full or partial regression based on the scope of changes.
- **Output:** **Regression Test Report**.

#### Step 7: Test Closure
- **Input:** All executed test cases, defect reports, test metrics.
- **Activities:** Evaluate test completion criteria, document lessons learned, archive test artifacts.
- **Output:** **System Test Summary Report (Test Closure Report)**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria for System Testing Types

The following matrix helps determine **which system test type to apply** based on the project context and risk profile.

| Test Type | Primary Objective | When to Apply | Common Tools |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Smoke Testing** | Verify build stability | After every new build | Manual / Selenium |
| **Regression Testing** | Ensure no broken functionality | After bug fixes or new features | Selenium, QTP |
| **Load Testing** | Validate performance under expected load | Before production deployment | JMeter, LoadRunner |
| **Stress Testing** | Determine breaking point | When scalability is critical | JMeter, Gatling |
| **Security Testing** | Identify vulnerabilities | Always, especially for web apps | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite |
| **Usability Testing** | Assess user experience | For consumer-facing products | UserTesting, Lookback |
| **Compatibility Testing** | Ensure cross-platform support | For multi-platform products | BrowserStack, Sauce Labs |
| **Recovery Testing** | Validate fail-over mechanisms | For mission-critical systems | Chaos Monkey, Gremlin |
| **Installation Testing** | Verify install/uninstall process | Before product release | Manual / Custom scripts |
| **UAT (Acceptance)** | Validate business requirements | Final phase before go-live | Manual with end-users |

### 3.3 Derivation: Calculating System Availability

The system availability metric is crucial for non-functional system testing. Below is the complete derivation:

$$
A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100
$$

Since $Uptime$ is conceptually related to $MTTF$ and $Downtime$ to $MTTR$:

$$
A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100
$$

**Worked Example:**
A system has an **MTTF of 900 hours** and an **MTTR of 100 hours**. Calculate the system availability.

$$
A = \dfrac{900}{900 + 100} \times 100
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{900}{1000} \times 100
$$

$$
A = 90\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system is operational and available for use **90% of the time** during any given period. This metric is critical for Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

### 3.4 Derivation: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

$$
DRE = \dfrac{D_{found}}{D_{found} + D_{escaped}} \times 100
$$

**Worked Example:**
During system testing, **85 defects** were found. After release, the customer reported **15 additional defects** that escaped testing. Calculate the DRE.

$$
DRE = \dfrac{85}{85 + 15} \times 100
$$

$$
DRE = \dfrac{85}{100} \times 100
$$

$$
DRE = 85\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system testing phase successfully removed **85% of all defects** before the product reached the customer. A DRE value above **85%** is generally considered good industry practice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Testing Position in the V-Model

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **position of system testing in the V-Model of software development** and its correspondence to the requirements phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeReq["Requirements Specification"]
    nodeHLD["High Level Design"]
    nodeLLD["Low Level Design"]
    nodeCode["Implementation Coding"]
    nodeUnit["Unit Testing"]
    nodeInteg["Integration Testing"]
    nodeSys["System Testing"]
    nodeAccept["Acceptance Testing"]
    nodeOp["Operation and Maintenance"]

    nodeReq --> nodeHLD
    nodeHLD --> nodeLLD
    nodeLLD --> nodeCode
    nodeCode --> nodeUnit
    nodeUnit --> nodeInteg
    nodeInteg --> nodeSys
    nodeSys --> nodeAccept
    nodeAccept --> nodeOp

    nodeReq -.-> nodeAccept
    nodeHLD -.-> nodeSys
    nodeLLD -.-> nodeInteg
    nodeCode -.-> nodeUnit
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The downward arrow on the left represents the **development phases** (decomposition), while the upward arrow on the right represents the **testing phases** (integration and validation). System Testing corresponds directly to the **High-Level Design** phase, validating that the overall system architecture meets the specified requirements.

### 4.2 Classification of System Testing Types

The following flowchart provides a **block-level functional architecture** classifying all major types of system testing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["System Testing"]
    nodeStart --> nodeFunc["Functional Testing"]
    nodeStart --> nodeNonFunc["Non-Functional Testing"]

    nodeFunc --> nodeSmoke["Smoke Testing"]
    nodeFunc --> nodeSanity["Sanity Testing"]
    nodeFunc --> nodeReg["Regression Testing"]
    nodeFunc --> nodeUAT["User Acceptance Testing"]

    nodeNonFunc --> nodePerf["Performance Testing"]
    nodeNonFunc --> nodeSec["Security Testing"]
    nodeNonFunc --> nodeUsab["Usability Testing"]
    nodeNonFunc --> nodeComp["Compatibility Testing"]
    nodeNonFunc --> nodeRec["Recovery Testing"]
    nodeNonFunc --> nodeInst["Installation Testing"]

    nodePerf --> nodeLoad["Load Testing"]
    nodePerf --> nodeStress["Stress Testing"]
    nodePerf --> nodeVol["Volume Testing"]
    nodePerf --> nodeEndure["Endurance Testing"]

    nodeUAT --> nodeAlpha["Alpha Testing"]
    nodeUAT --> nodeBeta["Beta Testing"]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** System testing branches into **Functional** and **Non-Functional** categories. Each category is further decomposed into specific test types. This hierarchical structure is a **high-yield answer** for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions asking to "Explain the different types of system testing."

### 4.3 System Testing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["Test Planning"]
    nodeP2["Test Case Design"]
    nodeP3["Test Environment Setup"]
    nodeP4["Test Execution"]
    nodeP5["Defect Reporting"]
    nodeP6["Regression Testing"]
    nodeP7["Test Closure"]

    nodeP1 --> nodeP2
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6
    nodeP6 --> nodeP4
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The process flows linearly from planning to closure, with a **feedback loop** from regression testing back to test execution — representing the iterative nature of fixing defects and re-testing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define System Testing. List any four types of system testing.**

**Model Answer:**
**System Testing** is a level of software testing performed on a **complete, integrated system** to evaluate its compliance with specified **functional and non-functional requirements**. It is a black-box testing technique conducted in an environment that closely resembles the production environment.

The four types of system testing are:
1. **Smoke Testing** — Verifies the basic critical functionalities of a new build.
2. **Regression Testing** — Ensures that code changes have not broken existing functionality.
3. **Performance Testing** — Evaluates the system's speed, response time, and stability under workload.
4. **Security Testing** — Identifies vulnerabilities to protect against unauthorized access and threats.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Smoke Testing and Sanity Testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Smoke Testing | Sanity Testing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Broad and shallow — covers critical end-to-end functionalities | Narrow and deep — focuses on specific modules or bug fixes |
| **Objective** | Verify build stability before further testing | Verify the rationality of a specific fix or change |
| **Performed by** | Testers or QA team | Testers or developers |
| **When executed** | On initial, new builds | After receiving a build with minor changes or fixes |
| **Documentation** | Can be scripted or unscripted | Usually scripted |
| **Subset of** | Acceptance Testing | Regression Testing |

> Smoke testing is a **gatekeeper** that decides whether the build is testable, while sanity testing **verifies the logic** of a specific module after a change.

---

### Part B: Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and may contain an internal choice. The structure is typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain in detail the different types of system testing with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

System testing encompasses a wide range of test types, classified broadly into **Functional** and **Non-Functional** testing.

**1. Functional System Testing Types:**

- **Smoke Testing:** A preliminary test to check if the build is stable. *Example:* After deploying an e-commerce application, verify that the homepage loads, the login page works, and a product can be added to the cart.
- **Sanity Testing:** A focused test on a specific functionality after a bug fix. *Example:* After fixing the discount calculation bug, verify that the corrected discount is applied at checkout.
- **Regression Testing:** Re-runs existing test cases after changes. *Example:* After adding a new payment gateway (UPI), re-test credit card, debit card, and net banking flows to ensure they still work.
- **User Acceptance Testing (UAT):** Validates the system against business requirements. *Example:* The client tests whether the monthly financial report generated by the ERP system matches the expected format and data.

**2. Non-Functional System Testing Types:**

- **Performance Testing (Load/Stress/Volume/Endurance):** Evaluates system responsiveness. *Example:* Simulating 10,000 concurrent users on a ticket booking website during a movie release.
- **Security Testing:** Identifies vulnerabilities. *Example:* Attempting SQL injection on a login form to gain unauthorized database access.
- **Usability Testing:** Assesses user-friendliness. *Example:* Observing first-time users complete a registration process and noting where they face difficulty.
- **Compatibility Testing:** Verifies cross-platform support. *Example:* Testing a web application on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge across Windows, macOS, and Linux.
- **Recovery Testing:** Validates system recovery after failures. *Example:* Disconnecting the database server mid-transaction and verifying that the application reconnects and resumes operations within 30 seconds.
- **Installation Testing:** Verifies the installation process. *Example:* Testing the smooth installation, uninstallation, and upgrade of a desktop application on a fresh Windows 11 machine.

> **Conclusion:** A comprehensive system testing strategy must include both functional and non-functional test types to ensure the software is correct, reliable, secure, and user-friendly.

---

**Q3(b). [Alternative 14-Mark Choice]**
**Describe the system testing process. Explain the various test metrics used to evaluate the effectiveness of system testing.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) System Testing Process:**

The system testing process consists of the following sequential phases:

1. **Test Planning:** Define objectives, scope, resources, schedule, and deliverables. Output: *System Test Plan*.
2. **Test Case Design:** Write detailed test cases, prepare test data and scripts. Output: *System Test Cases and Test Data*.
3. **Test Environment Setup:** Configure hardware, software, networks, and tools to mirror production. Output: *Test Environment Readiness Report*.
4. **Test Execution:** Run test cases, log pass/fail status, and capture evidence. Output: *Test Execution Logs*.
5. **Defect Reporting and Tracking:** Log defects with severity and priority; track to closure. Output: *Defect Log*.
6. **Regression Testing:** Re-execute failed test cases after fixes; run full regression suite. Output: *Regression Report*.
7. **Test Closure:** Evaluate completion criteria, archive artifacts, document lessons learned. Output: *Test Summary Report*.

**(ii) Test Metrics for Evaluating System Testing Effectiveness:**

The key metrics are:

1. **Defect Density:** Measures the number of defects per unit size of software.

$$
D_d = \dfrac{N_d}{S}
$$

A low defect density indicates high-quality code.

2. **Test Coverage:** Percentage of test cases actually executed out of the total planned.

$$
T_c = \dfrac{T_e}{T_t} \times 100
$$

3. **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE):** Measures the percentage of defects found before release.

$$
DRE = \dfrac{D_{found}}{D_{found} + D_{escaped}} \times 100
$$

4. **System Availability:** Measures the percentage of time the system is operational.

$$
A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100
$$

5. **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Average operational time between consecutive failures.

6. **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Average time required to fix a failure.

> **Conclusion:** The system testing process is iterative and metric-driven. By tracking these metrics, project managers can quantitatively assess testing effectiveness, identify weak areas, and make data-driven decisions about release readiness.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing System Testing with Integration Testing:** System testing tests the *complete integrated system* against the *SRS*, while integration testing tests *interactions between modules*. Many students write them interchangeably — this is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> 2. **Omitting "Non-Functional" Types:** When asked to "list types of system testing," many students list only functional types (smoke, regression, UAT) and forget non-functional types (performance, security, usability). Always mention both categories.
> 3. **Skipping Examples:** KTU examiners award additional marks for *suitable examples*. Always pair a test type with a real-world or application-level example.
> 4. **Forgetting the Process Flow:** If the question asks about the system testing *process*, you must describe all seven phases (Planning → Closure). A partial answer loses up to 5 marks.
> 5. **Not Mentioning IEEE 829:** Citing the *IEEE 829 standard* for test documentation (Test Plan, Test Case, Test Log, Test Summary Report) demonstrates syllabus-level depth and earns brownie points.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for System Testing and Its Types:**

- **Definition:** System testing is the testing of the **complete, integrated system** against the **SRS** in a **production-like environment**.
- **Position:** Lies between **Integration Testing** and **Acceptance Testing** in the V-Model.
- **Type of Testing:** Primarily a **black-box** testing technique.
- **Functional Types:** Smoke, Sanity, Regression, User Acceptance (Alpha/Beta).
- **Non-Functional Types:** Performance (Load/Stress/Volume/Endurance), Security, Usability, Compatibility, Recovery, Installation, Compliance.
- **Smoke vs. Sanity:** Smoke is **broad and shallow** (build stability); Sanity is **narrow and deep** (specific fix validation).
- **Regression Testing:** Must be performed **after every code change** to catch unintended side effects.
- **Process Flow:** Test Planning → Test Case Design → Environment Setup → Test Execution → Defect Reporting → Regression Testing → Test Closure.
- **Key Metrics to Remember:**
  - Defect Density = $N_d / S$
  - Test Coverage = $(T_e / T_t) \times 100$
  - DRE = $(D_{found} / (D_{found} + D_{escaped})) \times 100$
  - Availability = $(MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR)) \times 100$
- **Standards:** **IEEE 829** for test documentation; **ISO/IEC/IEEE 25010** for software product quality.
- **Who Performs It:** Typically an **independent QA team** (not the development team) to ensure objectivity.
- **Entry Criteria:** Complete integration testing, approved test plan, test environment ready.
- **Exit Criteria:** All planned test cases executed, no critical defects open, test summary report approved.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Testing and White Box Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing and White Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Black-Box Testing — The "Unknown Box" Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Black-Box Testing** is a software testing technique in which the tester examines the functionality of an application **without peering into its internal structures, implementation details, or internal paths**. Test cases are designed solely based on the **software requirements specification (SRS)**, the **design documents**, and the **observable input–output behavior** of the system.

The term *black box* literally means an opaque container — you cannot see what is happening inside. You only know what you push in (input) and what comes out (output).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Black-Box Testing is also referred to as **Behavioral Testing**, **Functional Testing**, **Specification-Based Testing**, or **Closed-Box Testing**.

#### Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Mystery Diner 🍽️
Imagine you walk into a restaurant and order a dish. You are a **black-box tester**. You do not enter the kitchen. You do not know the recipe, the chef's technique, or how the stove is wired. You only verify:
- Did the dish arrive (output produced)?
- Does it taste correct (matches expected output)?
- Was it served on time (performance boundary)?

If the dish arrives cold when it should be hot, the system has failed — even though the kitchen process is invisible to you.

#### Core Characteristics of Black-Box Testing

- **Focus:** *What* the system does, not *how* it does it.
- **Knowledge Required:** Only the **requirements document**.
- **Performed by:** Independent testers, QA engineers, end-users (UAT), or auditors.
- **Applicable at:** All testing levels — Unit, Integration, System, and Acceptance.
- **Defects Caught:** Missing functions, incorrect outputs, interface errors, performance issues, initialization errors, data structure misuse, and external database access errors.

---

### 1.2 White-Box Testing — The "Transparent Glass" Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **White-Box Testing** is a software testing technique in which the tester has **full visibility of the internal architecture, source code, control flow, data flow, and implementation logic** of the application. Test cases are derived by examining the program's **internal structure**, ensuring that every statement, branch, path, and condition executes correctly.

White-box testing is also called **Structural Testing**, **Open-Box Testing**, **Clear-Box Testing**, **Glass-Box Testing**, or **Code-Based Testing**.

#### Real-World Analogy: The Car Mechanic 🔧
Now imagine you are a car mechanic inspecting an engine. The hood is open. You see the pistons, the wiring, the fuel lines. You can test:
- Does each piston fire (statement coverage)?
- Does every branch of the fuel system engage (branch coverage)?
- Are all error-handling conditions triggered correctly (condition coverage)?

You are not just looking at the car's external behavior; you are probing the **internal mechanics**.

#### Core Characteristics of White-Box Testing

- **Focus:** *How* the system implements functionality through code paths.
- **Knowledge Required:** Source code, programming language, internal architecture.
- **Performed by:** Developers, white-box testers, or specialized QA engineers.
- **Applicable at:** Primarily **Unit Testing** and **Integration Testing**.
- **Defects Caught:** Logical errors, code path defects, infinite loops, uninitialized variables, dead code, unreachable branches, and security vulnerabilities.

---

### 1.3 Black-Box vs. White-Box — The High-Yield Contrast Table

| Parameter | **Black-Box Testing** | **White-Box Testing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Also Called** | Functional / Behavioral / Closed-Box | Structural / Open-Box / Glass-Box |
| **Knowledge Needed** | Requirements only | Source code + architecture |
| **Performed By** | Testers, End-users | Developers |
| **Level of Testing** | System, Acceptance, Integration | Unit, Integration |
| **Basis of Test Design** | SRS, Specifications | Code, Internal Logic |
| **Programming Skill** | Not required | Mandatory |
| **Coverage Metric** | Functional coverage | Statement, Branch, Path coverage |
| **Time Consumption** | Lower | Higher |
| **Detects** | Missing/wrong functionality | Coding errors, dead code |
| **Automation Ease** | Moderate (record-playback tools) | High (unit testing frameworks) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

> [!NOTE]
> **Grey-Box Testing** is the hybrid blend — a tester with **partial knowledge** of internals (e.g., access to high-level database schemas or API contracts but not source code). It is a common KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Black-Box Testing Techniques (Specification-Based)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following **five canonical techniques**. Each is examined in detail.

#### 2.1.1 Equivalence Partitioning (EP)

- The input domain is divided into **disjoint partitions** (equivalence classes) such that any single representative value from a class is assumed to produce the same output behavior.
- **Goal:** Reduce the total number of test cases while still achieving broad coverage.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Test at least one value from each equivalence class.

**Types of Equivalence Classes:**
- **Valid Class:** Inputs that are accepted by the system.
- **Invalid Class:** Inputs that are rejected (e.g., negative ages, alphabetic characters in numeric fields).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Worked Example — Age Field Validation (Valid range: 18–60):**
> Partition 1: age < 18 (invalid) → test with **10**
> Partition 2: 18 ≤ age ≤ 60 (valid) → test with **35**
> Partition 3: age > 60 (invalid) → test with **75**
> Only 3 test cases needed instead of dozens.

---

#### 2.1.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

- Concentrates test cases at the **edges (boundaries)** of equivalence classes, where defects are statistically most common.
- The principle: *Most software errors cluster at the boundaries of input ranges.*

**Standard BVA Test Points:**
- **Min-** (one below minimum)
- **Min** (minimum valid value)
- **Min+** (one above minimum)
- **Nominal** (a typical middle value)
- **Max-** (one below maximum)
- **Max** (maximum valid value)
- **Max+** (one above maximum)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> For range **18–60** (5 boundary values per side):
> Test: 17, 18, 19, 35 (nominal), 59, 60, 61 — a total of 7 carefully chosen test cases.

---

#### 2.1.3 Cause-Effect Graphing

- A systematic technique that translates **requirements** (causes) and **effects** (outputs) into a **Boolean graph**, which is then converted into a **decision table**.
- Uses Boolean operators (**AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR**) to model logical relationships.
- Helps identify combinations of inputs that produce specific outputs, reducing redundancy.

**Steps:**
1. Identify **causes** (input conditions) and **effects** (output conditions).
2. Draw a **cause-effect graph** linking them with Boolean operators.
3. Convert graph → **decision table** → **test cases**.

---

#### 2.1.4 Error Guessing

- Relies on the **tester's experience and intuition** to anticipate error-prone areas.
- No formal rules — purely heuristic.
- Common guesses: division by zero, empty fields, null inputs, leap years, special characters, very large numbers.

---

#### 2.1.5 State Transition Testing

- Used when the system exhibits **finite state behavior** — outputs depend on both inputs *and* the current state.
- Models the system as a **state diagram** with states, transitions, events, and actions.
- Tests verify that valid transitions occur and invalid transitions are handled.

---

### 2.2 White-Box Testing Techniques (Structure-Based)

#### 2.2.1 Statement Coverage (Line Coverage)

- Ensures that **every executable statement** in the source code has been executed at least once.
- **Formula:**

$$\text{Statement Coverage} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Number of Statements}} \right) \times 100\%$$

- **Weakest** form of coverage. Cannot guarantee branch correctness.

---

#### 2.2.2 Branch (Decision) Coverage

- Ensures that **every possible branch** (true/false outcome of each decision) has been executed.
- **Formula:**

$$\text{Branch Coverage} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Number of Branches}} \right) \times 100\%$$

- Stronger than statement coverage. **100% branch coverage ≈ 100% statement coverage**, but not always vice versa.

---

#### 2.2.3 Condition Coverage

- Ensures that **each Boolean sub-expression** (atomic condition) evaluates to both true and false.
- Independent of the overall decision outcome.
- Example: For `if (A && B)`, condition coverage needs `A=true, B=true`, `A=false, B=false` (or mixed).

---

#### 2.2.4 Path Coverage

- Ensures that **every independent path** from the program's entry to its exit point is executed.
- Computed using **Cyclomatic Complexity** (developed by Thomas J. McCabe in 1976).
- **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity Formula:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph
- $N$ = number of nodes in the control flow graph
- $P$ = number of connected components (usually **1** for a single program)

**Alternative Forms:**

$$V(G) = \pi + 1$$

Where $\pi$ = number of predicate nodes (decision points).

$$V(G) = R + 1$$

Where $R$ = number of regions (closed areas) in the planar flow graph.

- The value of $V(G)$ also equals the **minimum number of independent paths** that must be tested for full path coverage.

---

#### 2.2.5 Independent Path (Basis Path) Testing

- Identifies a **basis set** of linearly independent paths through the code.
- These paths form the **minimum test set** required to exercise every statement and branch at least once.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula or Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Statement Coverage | $\dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Branch Coverage | $\dfrac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (graph form) | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Dimensionless integer |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (predicate form) | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | Dimensionless integer |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (region form) | $V(G) = R + 1$ | Dimensionless integer |
| Minimum Independent Paths | $= V(G)$ | Integer |
| Risk Classification (McCabe) | $V \leq 10$ = Low Risk; $V \geq 30$ = High Risk | Threshold-based |
| BVA Points per Boundary | 3 (min-1, min, min+1) on each side | Total 6 + nominal |
| Equivalence Classes Goal | Minimize cases, maximize coverage | Heuristic |
| Black-Box Levels | Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance | Hierarchical |
| White-Box Primary Level | Unit + Integration | Lower-level focus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **McCabe Risk Rule:** If $V(G) \geq 30$, the module is considered **untestable in isolation** and must be refactored. KTU examiners frequently test this threshold.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications

- **Black-Box Testing** powers **UAT (User Acceptance Testing)** in banking, healthcare, and aviation industries where end-user validation is critical.
- **White-Box Testing** is foundational in **safety-critical systems** (aerospace, medical devices, automotive) where code paths must be fully exercised to comply with **DO-178C**, **IEC 62304**, and **ISO 26262** standards.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** is integrated into **CI/CD pipelines** via tools like **SonarQube**, **JaCoCo**, and **Cobertura** to automatically reject overly complex modules.
- The two techniques are **complementary, not competing** — KTU 2024 emphasizes a **hybrid testing strategy** for industrial-grade software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Cyclomatic Complexity Using the Predicate Method

**Given Source Code Snippet:**

```
1: int classify(int x, int y) {
2:     if (x > 0 && y > 0) {       // Predicate Node A
3:         printf("Quadrant I");
4:     } else if (x < 0 && y > 0) { // Predicate Node B
5:         printf("Quadrant II");
6:     } else {                      // Implicit Predicate C (else)
7:         printf("Other");
8:     }
9:     return 0;
10: }
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Count the number of **predicate (decision) nodes** $\pi$.

- Line 2: `x > 0 && y > 0` → Node A (1)
- Line 4: `x < 0 && y > 0` → Node B (1)
- Line 6: Implicit `else` (combined with Node A and B as final false branch) — we count A and B as binary decisions, with their collective false path covered.

For a clean analysis using McCabe's convention:
- Each compound Boolean condition is counted as **one predicate node** (not counting sub-expressions separately).
- Two predicate nodes: A and B.
- $\pi = 2$

**Step 2:** Apply the **predicate-node formula**.

$$V(G) = \pi + 1$$

$$V(G) = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

- The program has **3 independent paths** that must be tested for full path coverage.
- **Path 1:** A = true → "Quadrant I"
- **Path 2:** A = false, B = true → "Quadrant II"
- **Path 3:** A = false, B = false → "Other"

**Step 4:** Verify using the **graph method**.

Construct a flow graph:
- Nodes: 1 (start), 2 (decision A), 3 (action Q1), 4 (decision B), 5 (action Q2), 6 (action Other), 7 (return/end)
- Edges: 1→2, 2→3, 2→4, 3→7, 4→5, 4→6, 5→7, 6→7
- $N = 7$, $E = 8$, $P = 1$

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

$$V(G) = 8 - 7 + 2(1)$$

$$V(G) = 1 + 2 = 3$$

✅ Both methods yield **V(G) = 3** — results verified.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Statement and Branch Coverage

**Given Test Case:** A single test with `x = 5, y = 5` is executed.

**Step 1:** Identify total executable statements.

- Line 1 (declaration)
- Line 2 (decision — predicate statement)
- Line 3 (printf)
- Line 4 (decision — predicate statement)
- Line 5 (printf)
- Line 7 (printf)
- Line 9 (return)

Total executable statements = **7**.

**Step 2:** Identify total branches.

- Predicate A has 2 branches (true, false).
- Predicate B has 2 branches (true, false).
- Total branches = **4**.

**Step 3:** Track execution with `x = 5, y = 5`.

- Line 1: executed.
- Line 2 (A=true): executed → true branch taken.
- Line 3: executed.
- Line 9: executed.
- Statements executed = **4 out of 7**.

**Step 4:** Compute statement coverage.

$$\text{Statement Coverage} = \left( \frac{4}{7} \right) \times 100\% \approx 57.14\%$$

**Step 5:** Compute branch coverage.

- A: true branch executed ✅; false branch not executed ❌.
- B: true branch not executed ❌; false branch not executed ❌.
- Branches executed = **1 out of 4**.

$$\text{Branch Coverage} = \left( \frac{1}{4} \right) \times 100\% = 25\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** A single test case achieves only **57% statement coverage** and **25% branch coverage**. This illustrates why **multiple test cases** are essential — the KTU 2024 scheme often tests such calculations.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Equivalence Partitioning + Boundary Value Analysis

**Requirement:** A program accepts a **CGPA input** in the range **[0.0, 10.0]**, inclusive, to determine scholarship eligibility.

**Step 1:** Identify equivalence classes.

| Class | Range | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | CGPA < 0.0 | Invalid |
| C2 | 0.0 ≤ CGPA ≤ 10.0 | Valid |
| C3 | CGPA > 10.0 | Invalid |

**Step 2:** Apply Boundary Value Analysis — select 7 test cases.

| Test ID | Input (CGPA) | Class | Expected Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | -0.1 | C1 (Min-) | Error: "CGPA cannot be negative" |
| T2 | 0.0 | C2 (Min) | Accepted — No scholarship |
| T3 | 0.1 | C2 (Min+) | Accepted — No scholarship |
| T4 | 7.5 | C2 (Nominal) | Accepted — Partial scholarship |
| T5 | 9.9 | C2 (Max-) | Accepted — Full scholarship |
| T6 | 10.0 | C2 (Max) | Accepted — Full scholarship |
| T7 | 10.1 | C3 (Max+) | Error: "CGPA cannot exceed 10.0" |

**Step 3:** Verify statement coverage qualitatively.

If the validation code is a single `if-else` block, all **7 test cases** would yield **100% branch coverage**.

---

### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Testing a login form | Equivalence Partitioning + BVA | Many input combinations; boundaries matter |
| Validating date logic (e.g., leap year) | Boundary Value Analysis | Edge cases at month/year boundaries |
| Testing state machine of an ATM | State Transition Testing | Behavior depends on prior state |
| Testing a complex nested `if-else` module | Cyclomatic Complexity + Path Testing | Need to identify all independent paths |
| Finding missing functions in SRS | Black-Box Functional Testing | No code visibility required |
| Security vulnerability scanning | White-Box (e.g., static analysis) | Need to inspect code paths |
| Verifying end-user business flow | Black-Box UAT | User perspective is critical |
| Unit testing in TDD | White-Box Statement/Branch Coverage | Developer-driven, code-aware |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Black-Box Testing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Testing Phase] --> B0[Read SRS Document]
    B0 --> C0[Identify Input Domain]
    C0 --> D0[Select Black-Box Technique]
    D0 --> D1[Equivalence Partitioning]
    D0 --> D2[Boundary Value Analysis]
    D0 --> D3[Cause-Effect Graphing]
    D0 --> D4[State Transition Testing]
    D0 --> D5[Error Guessing]
    D1 --> E0[Generate Test Cases]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    D4 --> E0
    D5 --> E0
    E0 --> F0[Execute Tests on System Under Test]
    F0 --> G0[Compare Actual vs Expected Output]
    G0 --> H0{Output Matches?}
    H0 --> I0[Yes: Mark Pass]
    H0 --> J0[No: Log Defect]
    J0 --> K0[Report to Dev Team]
    I0 --> L0[End]
    K0 --> L0[End]
```

---

### 4.2 White-Box Testing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start White-Box Phase] --> P1[Access Source Code]
    P1 --> P2[Build Control Flow Graph CFG]
    P2 --> P3[Identify Nodes and Edges]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Cyclomatic Complexity V G]
    P4 --> P5[Identify Independent Basis Paths]
    P5 --> P6[Design Test Cases per Path]
    P6 --> P7[Execute Test Cases]
    P7 --> P8[Measure Statement Coverage]
    P8 --> P9[Measure Branch Coverage]
    P9 --> P10[Measure Path Coverage]
    P10 --> P11{Coverage Target Met}
    P11 --> P12[Yes: Mark Pass]
    P11 --> P13[No: Add More Test Cases]
    P13 --> P6
    P12 --> P14[End]
```

---

### 4.3 Black-Box vs. White-Box — Visual Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BB[BLACK BOX TESTING]
        BB1[Tester] --> BB2[Provides Inputs]
        BB2 --> BB3[System Under Test]
        BB3 --> BB4[Observed Output]
        BB4 --> BB5[Compare with Expected]
        BB5 --> BB6[Test Verdict]
    end
    subgraph WB[WHITE BOX TESTING]
        WB1[Tester Developer] --> WB2[Reads Source Code]
        WB2 --> WB3[Analyzes Control Flow]
        WB3 --> WB4[Derives Test Cases from Logic]
        WB4 --> WB5[Executes with Code Instrumentation]
        WB5 --> WB6[Code Coverage Report]
    end
    BB3 -.->|Internal Code Hidden| BB1
    WB2 -.->|Full Code Visibility| WB1
```

---

### 4.4 Control Flow Graph of Sample Code (For Complexity Derivation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Start: classify function] --> N2{x > 0 and y > 0}
    N2 -->|True| N3[Print Quadrant I]
    N2 -->|False| N4{x < 0 and y > 0}
    N4 -->|True| N5[Print Quadrant II]
    N4 -->|False| N6[Print Other]
    N3 --> N7[Return 0]
    N5 --> N7
    N6 --> N7
    N7 --> N8[End]
```

**Metrics from this CFG:**
- Nodes $N = 8$
- Edges $E = 9$
- Predicate nodes $\pi = 2$ (N2 and N4)
- Regions $R = 3$

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P = 9 - 8 + 2 = 3$$

$$V(G) = \pi + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

$$V(G) = R + 1 = 3 + 0 = 3$$

All three McCabe formulations yield **V(G) = 3** independent paths. ✅
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Black-Box and White-Box testing. List any two techniques used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Black-Box Testing | White-Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of internals | Not required | Required |
| Focus | Functional behavior | Internal structure |
| Performed by | Testers | Developers |
| Techniques | (i) Equivalence Partitioning, (ii) Boundary Value Analysis, (iii) Cause-Effect Graphing, (iv) State Transition Testing, (v) Error Guessing | (i) Statement Coverage, (ii) Branch Coverage, (iii) Condition Coverage, (iv) Path Coverage, (v) Cyclomatic Complexity-based testing |

> **Any two techniques from each list carry full marks.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is Cyclomatic Complexity? Write McCabe's formula and state its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Cyclomatic Complexity, developed by **Thomas J. McCabe (1976)**, is a software metric that quantifies the **number of linearly independent paths** through a program's source code. It indicates the minimum number of test cases required to achieve full path coverage.

**McCabe's Formula (graph-based):**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components.

**Significance:**
1. Determines the **minimum number of test cases** for path coverage.
2. Acts as a **complexity indicator** — high $V(G)$ means harder-to-maintain code.
3. Serves as a **risk indicator** — $V \leq 10$ is low risk; $V \geq 30$ is high risk and demands refactoring.
4. Guides **basis path testing** by identifying independent paths.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]**
> **Module:** Module 3 — Software Testing
> **Max Marks:** 14 | **Cognitive Levels:** Apply (L3), Analyze (L4)

---

### ✦ QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the following Black-Box Testing techniques with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**
   (i) Equivalence Partitioning
   (ii) Boundary Value Analysis

**(b) Compute Statement Coverage and Branch Coverage for the following pseudo-code using the given test case. (7 Marks)**

```
1: int grade(int marks) {
2:     if (marks >= 90)
3:         return 'A';
4:     else if (marks >= 75)
5:         return 'B';
6:     else if (marks >= 50)
7:         return 'C';
8:     else
9:         return 'F';
10: }
```

**Test Case:** `marks = 85`

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### (a) (i) Equivalence Partitioning — 3.5 Marks

Equivalence Partitioning (EP) is a black-box testing technique that divides the input domain of a software module into **disjoint groups (partitions)** of data from which test cases can be derived. The underlying assumption is that if one test case in a partition passes, all other values in that partition will also pass — thus reducing the **total number of test cases** while maintaining **broad coverage**.

**Key Concepts:**
- **Valid Partition:** Set of inputs that the system accepts as correct.
- **Invalid Partition:** Set of inputs that the system should reject (e.g., out-of-range, wrong type).
- A **single representative value** is selected from each partition for testing.

**Example — Password Field (Required: 8–16 characters, alphanumeric only):**

| Partition | Range | Representative Test Value |
|---|---|---|
| Invalid (Too short) | length < 8 | `abc` (3 chars) |
| Valid | 8 ≤ length ≤ 16 | `abcd1234` (8 chars) |
| Invalid (Too long) | length > 16 | `abcdefghijklmnopq` (17 chars) |
| Invalid (Special chars) | Contains symbols | `abcd@123` |

**Only 4 test cases** cover the input space effectively.

#### (a) (ii) Boundary Value Analysis — 3.5 Marks

BVA focuses on testing the **boundary (edge) values** of equivalence partitions, because empirical studies show that most software defects occur at input boundaries rather than at the center of the input range.

**Standard BVA Strategy (per boundary):** Test the boundary, just below it, and just above it.

**Example — Age Field (Valid range: 18–60):**

| Test ID | Input (Age) | Boundary Type | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 17 | Min – 1 (invalid) | "Age too low" |
| T2 | 18 | Min (valid) | "Accepted" |
| T3 | 19 | Min + 1 (valid) | "Accepted" |
| T4 | 35 | Nominal (valid) | "Accepted" |
| T5 | 59 | Max – 1 (valid) | "Accepted" |
| T6 | 60 | Max (valid) | "Accepted" |
| T7 | 61 | Max + 1 (invalid) | "Age too high" |

**7 carefully chosen test cases** ensure boundary robustness.

---

#### (b) Coverage Computation — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Identify executable statements.

- Line 1: `int grade(int marks)` (declaration — sometimes counted)
- Line 2: `if (marks >= 90)` (predicate)
- Line 3: `return 'A';`
- Line 4: `else if (marks >= 75)` (predicate)
- Line 5: `return 'B';`
- Line 6: `else if (marks >= 50)` (predicate)
- Line 7: `return 'C';`
- Line 8: `else` (implicit predicate)
- Line 9: `return 'F';`

Total executable statements = **9**.

**Step 2:** Identify branches.

- Predicate 1 (Line 2): True branch (Line 3) | False branch (Line 4)
- Predicate 2 (Line 4): True branch (Line 5) | False branch (Line 6)
- Predicate 3 (Line 6): True branch (Line 7) | False branch (Line 9)

Total branches = **6**.

**Step 3:** Trace execution with `marks = 85`.

- Line 1: executed ✅
- Line 2: `85 >= 90` → **False** → false branch taken
- Line 4: `85 >= 75` → **True** → true branch taken
- Line 5: executed ✅
- Return statement reached.

**Statements executed:** Lines 1, 2, 4, 5 = **4 statements**.

**Step 4:** Compute Statement Coverage.

$$\text{Statement Coverage} = \left( \frac{4}{9} \right) \times 100\% \approx 44.44\%$$

**Step 5:** Compute Branch Coverage.

- Predicate 1: True branch ❌ (not executed), False branch ✅
- Predicate 2: True branch ✅, False branch ❌
- Predicate 3: True branch ❌, False branch ❌

**Branches executed = 2 out of 6.**

$$\text{Branch Coverage} = \left( \frac{2}{6} \right) \times 100\% \approx 33.33\%$$

> **Final Answer:** Statement Coverage = **44.44%**, Branch Coverage = **33.33%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common mistake is counting the `else` keyword as a separate statement or forgetting to count the declaration line. Always count every executable line, including `if` and `else if` predicate lines. Miscounting even one line can shift the answer by ~10%.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a) Explain White-Box Testing in detail. Describe Statement, Branch, and Path Coverage techniques with examples. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Draw the Control Flow Graph (CFG) for the following program and compute Cyclomatic Complexity using all three McCabe's formulas. List the independent paths. (7 Marks)**

```
1: void check(int n) {
2:     if (n > 0) {
3:         if (n % 2 == 0)
4:             printf("Positive Even");
5:         else
6:             printf("Positive Odd");
7:     } else {
8:         printf("Non-Positive");
9:     }
10: }
```

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### (a) White-Box Testing Detailed Explanation — 7 Marks

White-Box Testing (also called **Structural Testing** or **Glass-Box Testing**) is a testing methodology in which the tester possesses **complete knowledge of the internal structure, source code, and implementation logic** of the software under test. It is primarily performed by **developers during unit testing** and is rooted in examining the program's **control flow, data flow, and code structure**.

**1. Statement Coverage (Line Coverage):**
- Ensures that **every executable statement** in the source code is executed at least once during testing.
- **Formula:**

$$\text{Statement Coverage} = \left( \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \right) \times 100\%$$

- **Example:** For the code `if (a>0) b=1; else b=2;`, the test `a=5` executes the `b=1` statement; the test `a=-1` executes the `b=2` statement. Together, 100% statement coverage is achieved.
- **Limitation:** Does not guarantee that all decision outcomes (branches) are tested.

**2. Branch (Decision) Coverage:**
- Ensures that **each branch** of every decision point is executed at least once.
- **Formula:**

$$\text{Branch Coverage} = \left( \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \right) \times 100\%$$

- **Example:** For `if (a>0) b=1; else b=2;`, the test `a=5` exercises the true branch; the test `a=-1` exercises the false branch. Together, 100% branch coverage.
- Stronger than statement coverage. **100% branch coverage often implies 100% statement coverage.**

**3. Path Coverage:**
- Ensures that **every possible execution path** from entry to exit is tested.
- Computed using **Cyclomatic Complexity** — the number of independent paths equals $V(G)$.
- **Example:** For nested `if-else` with two predicates, $V(G) = 3$ → at least 3 test cases required.
- **Strongest** coverage, but combinatorially expensive.

> **Comparison:** Statement ≤ Branch ≤ Path Coverage (in terms of strength).

---

#### (b) CFG Construction and Cyclomatic Complexity — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Draw the Control Flow Graph.

The CFG contains the following nodes:
- N1: Entry of `check` function (Line 1)
- N2: Predicate `n > 0` (Line 2)
- N3: Predicate `n % 2 == 0` (Line 3)
- N4: `printf("Positive Even")` (Line 4)
- N5: `printf("Positive Odd")` (Line 6)
- N6: `printf("Non-Positive")` (Line 8)
- N7: Exit of function (Line 10)

**Edges:**
- N1 → N2
- N2 → N3 (true branch)
- N2 → N6 (false branch)
- N3 → N4 (true branch)
- N3 → N5 (false branch)
- N4 → N7
- N5 → N7
- N6 → N7

**Step 2:** Count metrics.

- Nodes: $N = 7$
- Edges: $E = 8$
- Connected components: $P = 1$
- Predicate nodes: $\pi = 2$ (N2 and N3)
- Regions: $R = 3$

**Step 3:** Apply all three McCabe formulas.

**Formula 1 — Graph-based:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

$$V(G) = 8 - 7 + 2(1)$$

$$V(G) = 1 + 2 = 3$$

**Formula 2 — Predicate-based:**

$$V(G) = \pi + 1$$

$$V(G) = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Formula 3 — Region-based:**

$$V(G) = R + 1$$

$$V(G) = 3 + 0 = 3$$

**All three formulas yield $V(G) = 3$** — verified consistent result. ✅

**Step 4:** List the three independent paths.

| Path | Sequence | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Path 1 | N1 → N2 → N3 → N4 → N7 | n > 0 and n is even → "Positive Even" |
| Path 2 | N1 → N2 → N3 → N5 → N7 | n > 0 and n is odd → "Positive Odd" |
| Path 3 | N1 → N2 → N6 → N7 | n ≤ 0 → "Non-Positive" |

**Conclusion:** A minimum of **3 test cases** is required for **100% path coverage**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget the factor of **2P** in the graph-based formula (using $E - N + 2$ instead of $E - N + 2P$) or miscount the number of nodes/edges when constructing the CFG. Always **double-check the edge count** — for nested `if-else` structures, the count can be tricky.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- ✅ **Black-Box Testing** = Functional, Behavioral, Closed-Box. Based on SRS. No code knowledge.
- ✅ **White-Box Testing** = Structural, Open-Box, Glass-Box. Based on source code. Developer-driven.
- ✅ **Five Black-Box Techniques:** EP, BVA, Cause-Effect Graphing, Error Guessing, State Transition Testing.
- ✅ **Five White-Box Techniques:** Statement, Branch, Condition, Path, Basis Path Coverage.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** is the **single most important metric** in Module 3 — master all three McCabe formulas.
- ✅ McCabe's Risk Rule: $V(G) \leq 10$ → safe; $V(G) \geq 30$ → high risk, refactor required.
- ✅ Cyclomatic Complexity = **Minimum number of independent test cases** needed.
- ✅ **Grey-Box Testing** = hybrid (partial knowledge). Always mention it in viva when asked for the third type.
- ✅ Equivalence Partitioning reduces test cases by selecting **one representative per class**.
- ✅ BVA targets **boundary edges** — minimum, just below, just above.
- ✅ Statement Coverage is the **weakest**; Path Coverage is the **strongest** form of white-box testing.
- ✅ **Hybrid strategy** (combining both techniques) is industry best practice.
- ✅ Always show **step-by-step numerical evaluation** in KTU answers — partial marks are awarded for methodology.
- ✅ When asked to compute coverage, **state the formula first**, then the numerator, denominator, and final percentage.

> 🎯 **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** If a 14-mark question asks for "explain with example" — always include a **worked numerical example** (e.g., a sample program with cyclomatic complexity computed). Examiners award bonus marks for concrete examples and proper diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regression Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Regression Testing** is a type of software testing technique that verifies whether a software system still meets its specified requirements and continues to function correctly after any modification, enhancement, or change in the source code. It ensures that previously validated functionalities remain unaffected by newly introduced changes, including bug fixes, new feature additions, performance optimizations, or environmental configuration updates.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Software Engineering curriculum**, regression testing is classified under the broader umbrella of **Maintenance Testing** and **Dynamic Testing Strategies**, and is a critical activity in iterative and incremental development models such as Agile, Scrum, and the Rational Unified Process (RUP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Anchor:**
> - **SUT (System Under Test):** The software application or module currently being re-validated.
> - **Test Suite:** A collection of test cases used to verify regression behavior.
> - **Baseline Tests:** Pre-existing test cases that established the known-good behavior of the system.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **well-balanced old wooden bookshelf** standing in your house. For years, it has been holding your books perfectly without falling over. One day, you decide to add a new **decorative plank** to the top of the bookshelf, or you move the bookshelf to a different corner of the room, or you tighten some loose screws.

Now, before you trust it again, you will naturally:
1. **Place the original books back** on it (re-test old behavior),
2. **Add the new decoration** and check it doesn't topple the shelf (test new feature),
3. **Shake the shelf slightly** to make sure nothing has come loose (validate integration).

This is exactly what **Regression Testing** does in software engineering. Every time developers "touch" the code (add a feature, fix a bug, or refactor), there is a risk that the **previously working parts may break**. Regression testing acts as the **safety net** that re-runs old, validated tests to ensure that the **old behavior is still intact** after the change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** In civil engineering, when a load-bearing wall is reinforced, engineers re-test the structural integrity of the entire building — not just the new wall. Regression testing follows the same philosophy of *holistic re-validation after localized change*.

## 1.3 Why Regression Testing is Necessary (The "Why")

| Trigger Event | Risk Introduced | Why Regression Testing Helps |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bug Fix** | New code paths may interact with old code | Confirms the fix didn't break adjacent logic |
| **New Feature** | Integration points with old modules | Validates backward compatibility |
| **Performance Optimization** | Refactored algorithms may alter output | Ensures functional equivalence post-refactor |
| **Environment Change** | Porting to new OS/Hardware/Database | Validates behavior under new platform |
| **Configuration Update** | Library/dependency version upgrades | Detects breaking changes in APIs |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Empirical Industry Statistic:** Industry studies (notably NIST and IBM research) indicate that **more than 50% of the defects uncovered during the software maintenance phase** are *regressions* — defects that were either re-introduced or caused by prior fixes. This makes regression testing a **mandatory high-priority activity** in any mature Software Quality Assurance (SQA) process.

## 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Software Testing Taxonomy

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** classification under Module 3 (Software Testing Strategies), regression testing sits at the intersection of:

- **Testing by Process:** → Dynamic Testing (execution-based, not static review).
- **Testing by Granularity:** → Can be applied at Unit, Integration, System, and Acceptance levels.
- **Testing by Intent:** → A *Defect-Containment* and *Defect-Discovery* technique.

This positioning justifies its **universal applicability** across all phases of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Theory: How Regression Testing Works

Regression testing is not a single activity — it is a **structured process** that follows a deterministic sequence of operations. The theoretical workflow is enumerated below in **eight logical stages**:

1. **Change Identification & Impact Analysis:**
   The first logical step is to identify *what* changed in the software. This is done by analyzing the **Change Request (CR)**, **Version Control Diff Logs** (e.g., Git commits), and **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**.

2. **Regression Test Suite Curation:**
   Based on the impact analysis, a curated subset (or the entirety) of the existing test suite is selected. Three primary selection strategies exist:
   - **Retest All:** Re-execute 100% of the existing test cases.
   - **Regression Test Selection:** Run only those test cases that are *affected* by the change.
   - **Test Case Prioritization:** Re-order test cases so that high-priority (critical-path) cases run first.

3. **Test Case Prioritization & Scheduling:**
   Prioritization can be done by **code coverage**, **requirement priority**, or **fault-exposure history**. This is critical when test execution time is bounded (e.g., nightly CI/CD builds).

4. **Test Environment Setup:**
   The **SUT** is deployed in an isolated, controlled environment that mirrors production. Test data must be deterministic and version-controlled.

5. **Test Execution:**
   The selected regression tests are executed. Results are categorized as **Pass, Fail, Blocked, or Skipped**. The system is observed for *side-effect defects* — defects that are not the originally targeted feature but emerge as collateral damage.

6. **Defect Reporting & Triage:**
   Any failure is logged with full diagnostic context (stack trace, inputs, environment state). The defect is then triaged (prioritized) for developer resolution.

7. **Re-Validation (Re-Test the Fix):**
   After a developer resolves a regression defect, the specific failing test case is re-run, and the **complete affected subset** is re-executed to confirm no new regressions were introduced by the fix.

8. **Test Suite Maintenance & Update:**
   New test cases covering the changed functionality are added to the regression suite, and obsolete test cases are retired. This maintains the suite's *relevance index* over the project lifecycle.

> [!NOTE] > **Key Conceptual Distinction:** Regression Testing is *not* the same as **Re-Testing**. *Re-testing* verifies that a *specific identified defect* has been fixed. *Regression testing* is a *broader sweep* to confirm that *no other functionality* has broken as a result of the change.

## 2.2 Types of Regression Testing (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Description | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unit Regression** | Re-tests individual functions or methods after local code changes. | During developer-level TDD/Unit testing cycles. |
| **Partial Regression** | Re-tests a specific module whose internal logic has been modified, while keeping its interfaces with other modules stable. | After localized bug fixes within a module. |
| **Complete Regression** | Re-tests the entire system end-to-end after a major release, refactor, or platform migration. | Before major milestones, releases, or UAT hand-off. |
| **Progressive Regression** | Used when new test cases are designed for new functionality; the test suite evolves alongside the application. | In iterative/Agile development with continuously growing features. |
| **Selective Regression** | A pre-determined subset of the test suite is executed, typically chosen via a selection tool/algorithm that analyzes the impact graph. | When test execution time is constrained (CI/CD pipelines). |
| **Corrective Regression** | Re-testing when there are no changes in the application's specifications or code; the existing test cases can be reused as-is. | Minor cosmetic changes or version-control housekeeping. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Since Regression Testing involves computational metrics for test selection, prioritization, and effectiveness, the following KTU-relevant formulas are tabulated:

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Definitions | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test Case Effectiveness (TCE)** | $TCE = \dfrac{N_{defects\ found}}{N_{tests\ executed}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{defects\ found}$ = defects detected; $N_{tests\ executed}$ = total tests run | Quantifies regression suite quality |
| **Code Coverage (CC)** | $CC = \dfrac{L_{executed}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $L_{executed}$ = lines executed; $L_{total}$ = total lines in module | Determines coverage of regression run |
| **Requirement Coverage (RC)** | $RC = \dfrac{R_{tested}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $R_{tested}$ = requirements covered by tests; $R_{total}$ = total requirements | Measures requirement-trace completeness |
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D_{total}}{KLOC}$ | $D_{total}$ = total defects; $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code | Module-level quality indicator |
| **Prioritization Score (PS)** | $PS = w_1 \cdot P_{req} + w_2 \cdot P_{fault} + w_3 \cdot P_{cov}$ | $P_{req}$ = requirement priority; $P_{fault}$ = historical fault density; $P_{cov}$ = code coverage gain; $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = weights | Used in greedy test prioritization algorithms |
| **Regression Risk Index (RRI)** | $RRI = C \times P \times I$ | $C$ = Change complexity; $P$ = Probability of failure; $I$ = Impact severity | Decides if regression is needed & how extensive |
| **Test Suite Reduction Ratio (TSRR)** | $TSRR = 1 - \dfrac{\vert T_{reduced} \vert}{\vert T_{original} \vert}$ | $\vert T_{reduced} \vert$ = tests in reduced suite; $\vert T_{original} \vert$ = tests in original suite | Measures efficiency of selection algorithms |
| **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum t_{detect}}{N_{defects}}$ | $t_{detect}$ = time to detect a defect; $N_{defects}$ = number of defects | CI/CD pipeline efficiency metric |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In real-world DevOps pipelines, the **Regression Risk Index (RRI)** and **Test Suite Reduction Ratio (TSRR)** are used in tandem. A high RRI justifies a *complete regression run*; a low RRI allows *selective regression* — saving CI compute hours and accelerating delivery.

## 2.4 Test Case Prioritization Techniques (Deep Dive)

Test Case Prioritization (TCP) is the **process of scheduling test cases in an order that maximizes early defect detection**. The most cited KTU-relevant approaches are:

### a) Greedy Algorithm (Total Fault Coverage)
- **Input:** A set of test cases $T = \{t_1, t_2, ..., t_n\}$ and a fault matrix $M$ where $M_{ij} = 1$ if test $t_i$ covers fault $f_j$.
- **Output:** An ordered sequence $T'$ such that the **Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD)** metric is maximized.

$$APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} TFi - \frac{n}{2}}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$$

where $TFi$ is the position of the first test that detects fault $i$, $n$ is the number of tests, and $m$ is the total number of faults. A **higher APFD** indicates a more effective prioritization.

### b) Coverage-Based Prioritization
Uses **code coverage** as the ordering heuristic. Tests are sorted in descending order of the code branches, statements, or paths they cover.

### c) History-Based Prioritization
Uses **past defect data** to rank tests. Test cases that have historically detected the most defects are run first.

### d) Requirements-Based Prioritization
Test cases linked to **high-priority requirements** (e.g., safety-critical, revenue-impacting) are prioritized.

## 2.5 Regression Test Selection (RTS) Algorithms

A formal RTS algorithm works as follows:

1. **Static Analysis:** Parse the code and identify all modified functions/modules since the last baseline.
2. **Dependency Graph Construction:** Build a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = modules and $E$ = call/dependency edges.
3. **Transitive Closure Computation:** Starting from the modified nodes $V_{mod} \subseteq V$, compute the **transitive closure** $V_{affected} = Reach(V_{mod})$.
4. **Test-to-Module Mapping:** For each test case $t_i$, identify the set of modules it covers: $C(t_i) \subseteq V$.
5. **Selection Filter:** Include $t_i$ in the regression suite if and only if $C(t_i) \cap V_{affected} \neq \emptyset$.

> [!NOTE] > **Practical Implementation:** This is the underlying theory behind commercial tools like **JIRA + Zephyr**, **IBM Rational Test Workbench**, and the open-source **DejaGnu** framework used in GNU/Linux projects.
