Functional capacity grows to maintain user satisfaction. This justifies the dominance of **perfective maintenance** in mature systems. **Justification:** Explains the empirical "50% perfective" distribution.

**Law 7 — Declining Quality (1 mark):**
Quality degrades unless actively maintained. This justifies **corrective maintenance** as a non-zero recurring cost. **Justification:** No system is ever "done."

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Lehman"s Laws, taken together, prove that **maintenance is an inevitable, regulated, and bounded activity** for any large-scale software system. The four maintenance types map directly onto the forces described by the laws.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]`

**"Maintenance consumes 60%–80% of the total lifecycle cost of a typical software product. (a) Estimate the annual maintenance cost for a system developed at ₹50 lakh, given a maintenance-to-development ratio M = 0.6 and a growth exponent α = 0.15. (b) Use a Maintenance Effort equation to estimate the effort and schedule for a 10 KLOC project, with A = 3.0, B = 1.10, product of effort multipliers = 1.20, and standard COCOMO II constants."** *(14 marks, CO4, BL5 — Evaluate)*

#### **Part (a)** — Annual maintenance cost estimation. *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $C_{dev} = ₹50$ lakh.
- $M = 0.6$.
- $\alpha = 0.15$.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
C_{annual}(t) = C_{dev} \cdot M \cdot t^{\alpha}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation (5 marks):**

**Year 1:**

$$
C(1) = 50 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1^{0.15} = 30.00 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Year 2:**

$$
C(2) = 50 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 2^{0.15} = 30 \cdot 1.108 = 33.24 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Year 3:**

$$
C(3) = 50 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 3^{0.15} = 30 \cdot 1.168 = 35.05 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Year 4:**

$$
C(4) = 50 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 4^{0.15} = 30 \cdot 1.214 = 36.42 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Year 5:**

$$
C(5) = 50 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 5^{0.15} = 30 \cdot 1.252 = 37.57 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Cumulative cost over 5 years (1 mark):**

$$
C_{cum} = 30.00 + 33.24 + 35.05 + 36.42 + 37.57 = 172.28 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The cumulative maintenance cost over 5 years is **₹172.28 lakh** — more than 3.4× the development cost. This empirically validates the statement that *"maintenance consumes 60%–80% of the lifecycle cost."*

---

#### **Part (b)** — COCOMO II maintenance effort and schedule estimation. *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $KLOC = 10$.
- $A = 3.0$.
- $B = 1.10$.
- $\prod EM_{i} = 1.20$.

**Step 1 — Maintenance Effort Formula (1 mark):**

$$
E_{M} = A \cdot \text{KLOC}^{B} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} EM_{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $10^{1.10}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
10^{1.10} = 10^{1.0} \cdot 10^{0.10} = 10 \cdot 1.2589 = 12.589
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute $E_{M}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_{M} = 3.0 \cdot 12.589 \cdot 1.20 = 45.32 \text{ person-months}
$$

**Step 4 — Schedule Formula (1 mark):**

$$
T_{M} = C \cdot E_{M}^{D}
$$

where $C = 3.67$ and $D = 0.28 + 0.2 \cdot (B - 0.91)$.

**Step 5 — Compute D (1 mark):**

$$
D = 0.28 + 0.2 \cdot (1.10 - 0.91) = 0.28 + 0.2 \cdot 0.19 = 0.28 + 0.038 = 0.318
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $T_{M}$ (1 mark):**

$$
T_{M} = 3.67 \cdot 45.32^{0.318} = 3.67 \cdot 3.738 = 13.72 \text{ months}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The maintenance project requires approximately **45 person-months** of effort over a schedule of **13.7 months** — a realistic estimate for a moderate-sized legacy modernisation.

---

## 5.3 🚨 KTU EXAMINER"S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students 2–3 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Perfective with Adaptive Maintenance.** Many students write them as the same thing. Examiner will deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw a diagram.** A 14-mark question without a diagram usually caps at **10/14**. Always draw at least the 4-type taxonomy.
> 3. **Plugging wrong values into COCOMO II.** The default calibration constant is $A = 2.94$, not $A = 3.0$. If the question specifies a value, use that; if not, state the default.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Maintenance effort is in **person-months (PM)**, schedule in **months**, cost in the unit used in the question.
> 5. **Skipping Lehman"s Laws.** A 14-mark question on maintenance types is *expected* to mention at least 3–4 of Lehman"s Laws in the conclusion.
> 6. **Not linking theory to real-world examples.** Examiners award **+0.5 to +1 bonus marks** for genuine, named real-world examples (e.g., "Windows 10 EOL → Adaptive Maintenance").

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section once, 30 minutes before the exam. It covers 90% of the marks.**

- **Definition (IEEE 1219):** Modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, adapt to changes, improve performance, or add features.
- **Four Types (CAPP order):** **C**orrective → **A**daptive → **P**erfective → **P**reventive.
- **Corrective** = *fixing defects* (reactive, post-failure).
- **Adaptive** = *adapting to environment* (reactive to external change).
- **Perfective** = *adding/improving features* (proactive, user-driven).
- **Preventive** = *preempting future defects* (proactive, refactor/docs).
- **Maintenance is 60%–80%** of total software lifecycle cost.
- **Lientz & Swanson 1980** distribution: Perfective 50%, Adaptive 25%, Corrective 20%, Preventive 5%.
- **Lehman"s 8 Laws** govern software evolution — mention at least 4 (Continuing Change, Increasing Complexity, Declining Quality, Conservation of Familiarity).
- **COCOMO II Maintenance Effort:** $E_{M} = A \cdot \text{KLOC}^{B} \cdot \prod EM_{i}$.
- **Schedule:** $T_{M} = C \cdot E_{M}^{D}$ with $C = 3.67$, $D = 0.28 + 0.2 \cdot (B - 0.91)$.
- **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **Maintenance Process:** Request → Classification → Analysis → Design → Implementation → Test → Release.
- **Maintenance Cost Growth:** $C(t) = C_{dev} \cdot M \cdot t^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha \in [0.10, 0.30]$.
- **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers — at least the 4-type taxonomy and the 7-step process.
- **Use real-world examples** to earn bonus marks (Y2K bug = corrective; GST slab change = adaptive; dark mode = perfective; ES6 migration = preventive).
- **Standards to mention by name (without fabricating references):** IEEE Std 1219-1998, ISO/IEC 14764, COCOMO II (Boehm et al., 2000), Lehman"s Laws (1974–1996).
- **Maintainability Index (Halstead-derived)** gives a quick numerical score for code health.
- **Closing principle:** Maintenance is not a *post-script* to development — it **is** the software"s *lifelong journey*.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Notes — Software Maintenance and its Types.**
> *Compiled as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education framework, with COs mapped and Bloom"s Levels (BL) tagged throughout.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boehm's Maintenance Models (Both Legacy and Non-Legacy)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boehm's Maintenance Models (Both Legacy and Non-Legacy)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boehm's Maintenance Models** are a set of cost-estimation frameworks proposed by **Dr. Barry W. Boehm** (TRW/UCLA, 1981–1983) to predict and control the effort, cost, and risk involved in the *post-delivery* evolution of a software system. KTU 2024 Scheme groups them into two distinct sub-models:

1. **Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model (Original Model, 1983)** – Used to estimate the maintenance effort of *new-generation* software (where the source code, design, and environment are well understood and accessible).
2. **Boehm's Legacy Maintenance Model (Re-engineering Spiral Model, 1990s)** – Used to estimate the effort of *re-engineering* or *maintaining* legacy systems (old, undocumented, obsolete systems that must be salvaged).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Highlight:** The question paper may ask you to *compare* the two models or to *estimate maintenance effort* using the Non-Legacy formula. Always mention **ACT (Annual Change Traffic)** in your answer — it is the single most important variable in the Non-Legacy model.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you bought a **brand-new Maruti car in 2020** (Non-Legacy) versus a **vintage 1985 Ambassador** (Legacy):

- For the **new car**, you have the service manual, the wiring diagram, the spare parts catalogue, and trained mechanics everywhere. You can **predict** exactly how much each annual service will cost. → This is **Boehm's Non-Legacy Model**.
- For the **vintage Ambassador**, the manual is missing, parts are custom-fabricated, the design is obsolete, and the mechanic is a specialist who must first *understand* the engine before touching it. You can only estimate via a **spiral of trial-and-error understanding → re-design → re-implement**. → This is **Boehm's Legacy (Re-engineering) Model**.

### 1.3 Why Maintenance Models Matter (KTU Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry fact (Booch, 2004):** Approximately **60–80 %** of the total lifecycle cost of any software system is spent in **maintenance**, not development. Hence predicting this cost *before* deployment is critical for project planning — this is precisely the problem Boehm's models solve.

| Term | Meaning | KTU Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **ACT** | Annual Change Traffic (% of source code changed per year) | ACT |
| **AAM** | Annual Adaptive Maintenance fraction | AAM |
| **AAAF** | Adaptive Adjustment Factor | AAAF |
| **SDL** | Source Delivered Lines | KDSI / SLOC |
| **EM** | Maintenance Effort (Person-Months) | EM |
| **Legacy System** | Old, obsolete software lacking modern documentation | – |
| **Re-engineering** | Restructuring or rewriting legacy code to make it maintainable | – |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Maintenance Cost Driver Categories (Boehm, 1983)

Boehm's Non-Legacy Model classifies *all* the factors that influence maintenance effort into **5 categories**. KTU frequently asks you to list these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for exams: "PC-EAS" → Person, Computer, Environment, Application, Software**

1. **Personnel Factors (P)** — *Who* maintains it?
   - Software engineering capability
   - Application experience
   - Hardware/SW environment experience
   - Language experience
   - Personnel continuity
   - Programmer capability

2. **Software Product Factors (S)** — *What* is being maintained?
   - Reliability
   - Database size
   - Product complexity
   - Required software reliability
   - Documentation match to life-cycle needs

3. **Computer Environment Factors (C)** — *On what* does it run?
   - Hardware constraints
   - Virtual machine volatility
   - Turnaround time
   - Computer performance (response time, throughput)

4. **Application Environment (A)** — *Where* is it deployed?
   - Adaptive maintenance fraction
   - Database migration
   - Input/Output device volatility
   - Processing complexity

5. **Project Factors (Project)** — *How* is it managed?
   - Use of modern programming practices
   - Use of software tools
   - Multisite development
   - Customer interface stability

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working of the Non-Legacy Model

**Step 1:** Determine the **size** of the software being maintained.
- Measured in **SDL** (Source Delivered Lines) or **KDSI** (Thousands of Delivered Source Instructions).

**Step 2:** Determine the **Adaptive Maintenance Fraction (AAM)**.
- The fraction of total maintenance that is *adaptive* (driven by environmental change: OS upgrade, hardware change, regulation).
- Typical value: **0.20 to 0.50** (i.e. 20–50 % of all maintenance is adaptive).

**Step 3:** Determine the **Annual Change Traffic (ACT)**.
- Percentage of source code actually modified per year.
- Typical value: **5 % to 25 %** for stable systems; can be >50 % for unstable systems.

**Step 4:** Apply the **Annual Maintenance Effort Formula** (see Section 3 for derivation).

**Step 5:** Multiply by the **Maintenance Adjustment Factor (FA)** — a multiplier that combines the 5 categories above to refine the estimate.

### 2.3 The Legacy (Re-engineering) Model — Spiral Approach

Boehm adapted his **Spiral Model** specifically for legacy systems. Instead of predicting *maintenance cost*, it iterates through *understanding → re-design → re-implementation*.

The **6 activities** of Boehm's Legacy Re-engineering Model:

| # | Activity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Original System Analysis** | Identify the *business* value of the legacy system. |
| 2 | **Software Requirements Re-design** | Capture *implicit* requirements hidden in old code. |
| 3 | **Re-architecting / Re-design** | Build a new architecture while preserving data. |
| 4 | **Source Code Restructuring** | Convert unstructured code (e.g. COBOL, FORTRAN) to structured code. |
| 5 | **Data Restructuring** | Migrate flat-file or hierarchical DBs to relational/object DBs. |
| 6 | **Reverse & Forward Engineering** | Round-trip the design and code to ensure consistency. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Compare Boehm's Legacy vs Non-Legacy models," write:
> - Non-Legacy → *predictive* (one-shot formula).
> - Legacy → *evolutionary* (spiral iteration).
> - Non-Legacy → assumes documentation exists.
> - Legacy → assumes documentation is *missing or obsolete* and must be recovered.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use `\vert` for absolute value)

| Symbol | Formula / Meaning | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $EM$ | Maintenance Effort = $\frac{ACT \cdot SDL}{SDT} \cdot AAM$ | Person-Months |
| $ACT$ | Annual Change Traffic (fraction of code changed/year) | 0.05 – 0.50 |
| $SDL$ | Source Delivered Lines | 1000 – 100000 |
| $SDT$ | Software Development Time | Months |
| $AAM$ | Adaptive Maintenance Fraction | 0.20 – 0.50 |
| $AAAF$ | Annual Adaptive Adjustment Factor | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| $EM_{\text{final}}$ | $EM \cdot FA$ (FA = Maintenance Adjustment Factor) | Person-Months |
| $\vert$ACT$\vert$ | Magnitude of change traffic | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are **effort-based** (output in person-months) and **annualized** (computed on a per-year basis). This is the *expected KTU answer format* for a 14-mark problem.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Bidding & Tendering:** Govt. of India / Indian defence projects use Boehm-style models to predict 5-year maintenance costs before awarding a contract.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement) Pricing:** Software vendors quote annual maintenance as **15–25 % of license cost** — derived from Boehm's ACT.
- **Modern Agile / DevOps:** Although DevOps uses continuous delivery, Boehm's ACT is still used to compute **technical debt** in tools like SonarQube.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Boehm Non-Legacy Maintenance Effort Formula

**Goal:** Derive the master maintenance-effort equation
$$EM = ACT \times \frac{SDL}{SDT} \times AAM$$

#### Step 1 — Express the *Developed* Effort Baseline

Boehm's basic software engineering cost equation (from his **COCOMO** model) gives the *development* effort as

$$ED = a \cdot (KDSI)^{b} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} EM_i$$

where $EM_i$ are the effort multipliers. For a *first-cut* maintenance model, Boehm linearised this into a **per-KDSI** development effort:

$$ED_{\text{per-line}} = \frac{ED}{KDSI}$$

#### Step 2 — Convert Per-Line Effort to Per-Change Effort

Only a fraction of the code changes per year. That fraction is the **Annual Change Traffic (ACT)**. Hence the number of lines actually modified per year is

$$L_{\text{changed}} = ACT \times KDSI$$

If a developer can process $SDT$ units of code in a month, the **time to process the changed lines** is

$$T_{\text{change}} = \frac{L_{\text{changed}}}{KDSI / SDT} = ACT \times SDT$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Adaptive Maintenance Fraction

Not all changes are perfective/corrective. A fraction $AAM$ of the maintenance budget is *adaptive* (driven by environment changes). So the **adaptive maintenance effort** becomes

$$EM_{\text{adaptive}} = AAM \times T_{\text{change}}$$

#### Step 4 — Final Consolidated Formula

Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$EM = AAM \times ACT \times SDT$$

If we want the **absolute person-months** (not the multiplier) we use SDL instead of SDT:

$$\boxed{EM = ACT \times \frac{SDL}{SDT} \times AAM}$$

#### Step 5 — Adjusted Effort with Cost Drivers

Multiplying by the composite Maintenance Adjustment Factor $FA$ (which encodes the 5 categories of Section 2.1):

$$EM_{\text{adjusted}} = EM \times FA = \left(ACT \times \frac{SDL}{SDT} \times AAM\right) \times FA$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Clue:** $FA > 1$ when the project is *complex* (e.g. low personnel experience, obsolete hardware). $FA = 1$ for an *ideal* project. $FA < 1$ is theoretically possible for highly automated systems but is rare in practice.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A software system has 20 000 delivered source lines, was developed in 12 months, has an Annual Change Traffic of 15 %, an Adaptive Maintenance fraction of 40 %, and a Maintenance Adjustment Factor of 1.2. Compute the *annual maintenance effort*.

#### Solution

**Given data**

$$SDL = 20\,000 \text{ lines}, \quad SDT = 12 \text{ months}, \quad ACT = 0.15, \quad AAM = 0.40, \quad FA = 1.2$$

**Substitute into the master formula**

$$EM = ACT \times \frac{SDL}{SDT} \times AAM$$

**Compute the per-year development-equivalent effort**

$$\frac{SDL}{SDT} = \frac{20\,000}{12} = 1666.67 \text{ lines/month}$$

**Multiply by ACT**

$$ACT \times \frac{SDL}{SDT} = 0.15 \times 1666.67 = 250.0 \text{ line-equivalents}$$

**Multiply by AAM**

$$EM = 0.40 \times 250.0 = 100.0 \text{ person-months}$$

**Apply adjustment factor**

$$EM_{\text{adjusted}} = 100.0 \times 1.2 = 120.0 \text{ person-months}$$

**Final Answer (Boxed for exam)**

$$\boxed{EM_{\text{adjusted}} = 120 \text{ person-months / year}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** The organisation must budget **120 person-months of effort per year** to maintain this system. If a developer costs ₹80 000 / month, the annual maintenance budget is **₹96 lakhs**.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Legacy vs Non-Legacy

| Parameter | Non-Legacy Model (1983) | Legacy Model (Spiral) |
|---|---|---|
| **Year proposed** | 1983 | 1990 (extension) |
| **Input** | ACT, AAM, SDL, SDT | Business value, code health index |
| **Output** | Single effort number (person-months) | Iterative risk-list of re-engineering steps |
| **Documentation assumed** | Complete and up-to-date | Missing or obsolete — must be recovered |
| **Method** | Analytic / closed-form | Evolutionary / spiral |
| **Process type** | One-shot estimation | Multi-cycle (Analysis → Re-design → Re-impl) |
| **Best for** | New generation, well-documented systems | Old COBOL/Fortran, mainframe, undocumented systems |
| **Risk handling** | Implicit (via FA) | Explicit (risk-driven spiral) |
| **Tooling** | Spreadsheet | CASE tools, reverse-engineering tools |
| **Cost driver focus** | 5 categories of cost drivers | 6 re-engineering activities |
| **Effort granularity** | Annual | Per-spiral-cycle |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Non-Legacy Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Software System SDL] --> B[Compute ACT Annual Change Traffic]
    C[Project History SDT] --> D[Compute per-line dev effort]
    B --> E[Compute Annual Change Effort]
    D --> E
    F[Adaptive Maintenance Fraction AAM] --> G[Apply AAM to Annual Change]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Base Maintenance Effort EM]
    I[Five Cost Driver Categories] --> J[Compute Maintenance Adjustment Factor FA]
    J --> K[Adjusted Maintenance Effort EM x FA]
    H --> K
    K --> L[Annual Maintenance Budget]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart of Boehm's Legacy Re-engineering Spiral

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Identify Legacy System] --> S1[Original System Analysis]
    S1 --> S2[Software Requirements Re-design]
    S2 --> S3[Re-architecting Re-design]
    S3 --> S4[Source Code Restructuring]
    S4 --> S5[Data Restructuring]
    S5 --> S6[Reverse and Forward Engineering]
    S6 --> Q{All Business Goals Met?}
    Q -- No --> S1
    Q -- Yes --> S7[Deploy Re-engineered System]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — 5 Cost Driver Categories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Personnel
        P1[Capability]
        P2[Experience]
        P3[Continuity]
    end
    subgraph SoftwareProduct
        S1[Reliability]
        S2[Complexity]
        S3[Documentation]
    end
    subgraph ComputerEnvironment
        C1[Hardware]
        C2[Turnaround]
        C3[Volatility]
    end
    subgraph ApplicationEnvironment
        A1[AAM Fraction]
        A2[DB Migration]
        A3[IO Volatility]
    end
    subgraph ProjectFactors
        Pr1[Tools]
        Pr2[Practices]
        Pr3[Multisite]
    end
    Personnel --> FA[Maintenance Adjustment Factor FA]
    SoftwareProduct --> FA
    ComputerEnvironment --> FA
    ApplicationEnvironment --> FA
    ProjectFactors --> FA
    FA --> EM[Final Maintenance Effort]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All mermaid nodes use alphanumeric IDs (e.g. `P1`, `S2`) and clean uppercase text labels — strictly compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE diagram safeguard rules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **Annual Change Traffic (ACT)** in the context of Boehm's maintenance model. State its typical range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** ACT is the *fraction of the source code that is modified, added or deleted per year* during the maintenance phase.
- **(1 mark)** It is expressed as a percentage or decimal of the total Source Delivered Lines (SDL).
- **(1 mark)** Typical value lies between **5 % and 50 %** for commercial systems; can exceed 50 % in unstable or poorly-designed systems.

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List the **five categories of maintenance cost drivers** identified by Boehm.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **(0.6)** Personnel factors
2. **(0.6)** Software product factors
3. **(0.6)** Computer (hardware/software) environment factors
4. **(0.6)** Application environment factors
5. **(0.6)** Project factors

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often *forget* to convert ACT from a **percentage** to a **decimal** (e.g. 15 % → 0.15) — this alone leads to an answer 100× wrong.
> - Students often *omit* the Maintenance Adjustment Factor $FA$ when it is explicitly given.
> - In Legacy-model questions, students write generic *SDLC phases* instead of Boehm's **6 specific re-engineering activities** — answers are marked down.
> - Always **box your final numerical answer** to secure the last 1–2 marks reserved for presentation.

#### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Boehm's Non-Legacy Maintenance Model** in detail. Derive the maintenance effort formula
$$EM = ACT \times \frac{SDL}{SDT} \times AAM$$

**(b) [7 marks]** A system has $SDL = 50\,000$ lines, $SDT = 18$ months, $ACT = 0.20$, $AAM = 0.35$, and $FA = 1.15$. Calculate the *adjusted annual maintenance effort* and comment on the result.

**Model Solution**

**(a) [7 marks] — Explanation + Derivation**
- **(1.5 marks)** Define maintenance and the *need* for a cost model — reference to 60–80 % of lifecycle cost being post-deployment.
- **(1.5 marks)** List the **5 cost driver categories** of Boehm (Personnel, Software Product, Computer, Application, Project) with one example each.
- **(2 marks)** State the master formula and explain each variable (ACT, SDL, SDT, AAM, FA).
- **(2 marks)** Show the step-by-step derivation:
    1. Effort per line: $ED_{per-line} = ED / KDSI$
    2. Lines changed per year: $L = ACT \times KDSI$
    3. Time spent on changes: $T = ACT \times SDT$
    4. Adaptive portion: $EM = AAM \times ACT \times SDT$
    5. Final adjusted form: $EM \times FA$

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution**

Given:
$$SDL = 50\,000, \quad SDT = 18, \quad ACT = 0.20, \quad AAM = 0.35, \quad FA = 1.15$$

- **(1.5 marks)** Compute per-month line throughput: $SDL/SDT = 50\,000 / 18 = 2777.78$ lines/month.
- **(1.5 marks)** Apply ACT: $0.20 \times 2777.78 = 555.56$ line-equivalents.
- **(1.5 marks)** Apply AAM: $0.35 \times 555.56 = 194.44$ person-months.
- **(1.5 marks)** Apply FA: $194.44 \times 1.15 = 223.6$ person-months.
- **(1 mark)** Comment: *The system demands 223.6 person-months of maintenance effort per year. If developer cost = ₹80 000/month, annual budget ≈ ₹1.79 crore.*
- **(0.5 mark)** **Box the final answer**:
$$\boxed{EM_{\text{adjusted}} \approx 223.6 \text{ person-months / year}}$$

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Boehm's Legacy Maintenance (Re-engineering Spiral) Model**. List its six key activities and explain any three in detail.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare Boehm's Legacy and Non-Legacy Maintenance Models on at least **six** parameters.

**Model Solution**

**(a) [7 marks] — Legacy Model**
- **(1.5 marks)** Define *legacy system* — old, obsolete, undocumented, business-critical.
- **(1 mark)** State that Boehm adapted the **spiral model** for legacy maintenance.
- **(1.5 marks)** List the **6 activities** (System Analysis, Req. Re-design, Re-architecting, Code Restructuring, Data Restructuring, Reverse/Forward Engineering).
- **(3 marks)** Explain *any three* in detail (1 mark each):
    - *Re-architecting:* Decompose monolithic code into modular components while preserving external behaviour.
    - *Code Restructuring:* Convert unstructured or obsolete code (e.g. COBOL) into structured, modular form.
    - *Data Restructuring:* Migrate flat-files or hierarchical DBs into modern relational/Object DBs.

**(b) [7 marks] — Comparison Table**
- Construct a 6-row × 3-column table with parameters: (Input, Output, Documentation, Method, Best For, Risk Handling).
- **(1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks)** + **(1 mark)** for overall conclusion: *"Non-Legacy = predictive one-shot; Legacy = evolutionary spiral."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Author:** Dr. Barry W. Boehm (1981/1983/1990).
- **Two models:** Non-Legacy (predictive, 1983) and Legacy (spiral, 1990).
- **Master Formula:** $EM = ACT \times \dfrac{SDL}{SDT} \times AAM$ and $EM_{\text{adj}} = EM \times FA$.
- **ACT** = Annual Change Traffic, typical 5–50 %.
- **AAM** = Adaptive Maintenance Fraction, typical 20–50 %.
- **FA** = Maintenance Adjustment Factor, usually 1.0–2.0.
- **5 cost driver categories:** Personnel, Software Product, Computer, Application, Project (mnemonic **PC-EAS**).
- **6 Legacy re-engineering activities:** Analysis, Req. Re-design, Re-architect, Code Restructure, Data Restructure, Reverse/Forward Engineering.
- **Legacy** = *evolutionary* / iterative; **Non-Legacy** = *predictive* / closed-form.
- **Unit check:** SDL in lines, SDT in months, $EM$ in person-months.
- **Conversion tip:** Always convert ACT **% → decimal** (15 % → 0.15) before substitution.
- **Industrial use:** Quoted annual maintenance is **15–25 % of license cost** — derived from ACT.
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting the FA multiplier or using a non-Boehm SDLC answer in a Legacy question.
- **Exam mnemonic for cost drivers:** **"P SCAP"** → **P**ersonnel, **S**oftware-product, **C**omputer, **A**pplication, **P**roject.
- **Final-answer presentation rule:** Always **box** numerical answers and **underline** key terms in definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk and its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk and its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk** in Software Engineering is formally defined as the possibility of suffering a **loss or harm** due to uncertainty in software project activities, deliverables, processes, or environment. According to the **IEEE Standard 1540-2001** and standard risk management literature (Pressman, Sommerville, KTU 2024 Module 4), Risk is mathematically expressed as a function of **two key parameters**: the **Probability of an undesirable event** and the **Consequence (Impact/Cost)** of that event.

Formally:

$$
Risk = f(P, C)
$$

Where:
- $P$ = **Probability** of the adverse event occurring (ranging from 0.0 to 1.0)
- $C$ = **Consequence / Cost / Impact** magnitude if the event occurs (in person-hours, money, schedule, or quality units)

The most widely accepted scalar metric is **Risk Exposure (RE)**:

$$
RE = P \times C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A risk that has a **0% probability** is not a risk — it is a certainty or impossibility. A risk with **100% probability** is no longer a risk; it has become a problem. Risk inherently lives in the **grey zone of uncertainty** (0 < P < 1).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy: Driving a Car in Kerala Monsoon Rains ☔🚗**

Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Munnar during the monsoon. You face several risks:

| Risk Element | Monsoon Driving Example | Software Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Probability (P)** | 60% chance of landslides on the ghat section | 40% chance the new API integration will fail |
| **Consequence (C)** | Severe injury, ₹5 lakh vehicle damage | 3-month delay, ₹20 lakh revenue loss |
| **Risk Exposure (P × C)** | 0.6 × 5L = ₹3 Lakh exposure | 0.4 × 20L = ₹8 Lakh exposure |
| **Mitigation** | Travel at dawn, carry first-aid, inform family | Buy insurance, do prototype testing, code reviews |

Just as a wise driver does not avoid the journey but **anticipates, prepares, and reduces exposure**, a competent software project manager does the same with project risks.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Risk Definition:** **"U × I"** — **U**ncertainty multiplied by **I**mpact. If either factor is zero, there is no meaningful risk.

---

## 1.3 Why "Risk" is a First-Class Citizen in KTU Module 4

The KTU 2024 Scheme places Risk Management as a **core competency** because industry data (Standish Group CHAOS Report) consistently shows that **the top three causes of software project failure are directly risk-related**:

1. **Incomplete requirements** (Technical + Project risk)
2. **Lack of user involvement** (Business risk)
3. **Unrealistic schedules and budgets** (Project + Financial risk)

Mastering risk identification and classification is therefore **not optional** — it is the difference between a successful KTU project viva, a 14-mark university answer, and a doomed real-world IT career.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition: The Anatomy of Software Risk

Every software risk can be broken down along **three analytical axes**:

### Axis 1 — Nature of the Risk Source
- **Project Risks** — threaten the **project plan** (schedule, budget, resources)
- **Technical Risks** — threaten the **quality and architecture** of the software
- **Business Risks** — threaten the **viability of the product** in the market
- **Product Risks** — threaten the **developed product's** functionality or performance
- **Process Risks** — threaten the **process used** to develop the product (e.g., poor testing methodology)

### Axis 2 — Degree of Knowledge
- **Known Risks** — Identified through careful project analysis; their probability and impact **can be predicted** (e.g., "team lacks React experience")
- **Unknown Risks** — Cannot be identified in advance; their probability and impact **cannot be predicted** (these are the true "Black Swan" events — COVID-19, sudden vendor shutdown)

### Axis 3 — Time/Project Lifecycle Stage
- **Anticipated Risks** — Expected at known phases (requirements freeze, integration test phase)
- **Residual Risks** — Risks that **remain** even after mitigation strategies are applied

---

## 2.2 The Reactive vs Proactive Risk Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask the difference between these two strategies. Memorize this distinction.

| Dimension | Reactive Risk Management | Proactive Risk Management |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophy** | "Fix the problem **after** it happens" | "Prevent the problem **before** it happens" |
| **Cost** | High (firefighting is expensive) | Low (prevention is cheap) |
| **KTU Score Weight** | Mentioned but **low marks** | **High-yield topic (7+ marks)** |
| **Industry Practice** | Small teams, startups | Mature organizations, ISO 31000 compliant |
| **Trigger** | Crisis event | Risk register review meeting |

**Proactive Risk Management** follows a **5-step formal cycle** (RMMM — Risk Management Mitigation, Monitoring, and Management as per Pressman, Chapter 16 of KTU Module 4):

1. **Risk Identification** — What can go wrong?
2. **Risk Analysis** — What is the probability and impact?
3. **Risk Planning** — How do we mitigate it?
4. **Risk Monitoring** — Are our assumptions still valid?
5. **Risk Control** — Execute the contingency plan

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Use **LaTeX `\vert`** (not the pipe symbol `|`) in all formulas below to avoid markdown table corruption.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | KTU Typical Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Exposure (RE)** | $RE = P \times C$ | Ranking and prioritization of risks | Person-hours or ₹ or % |
| 2 | **Risk Magnitude** | $RM = P \times I$ | Quick classification into Low/Medium/High | Dimensionless score (0–100) |
| 3 | **Risk Reduction Leverage (RRL)** | $RRL = \dfrac{RE_{before} - RE_{after}}{Cost\ of\ Risk\ Reduction}$ | Cost-benefit of mitigation strategy | Ratio (higher is better) |
| 4 | **Aggregate Project Risk** | $R_{project} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i$ | Total exposure of the entire project | Person-hours or ₹ |
| 5 | **Residual Risk** | $R_{residual} = R_{initial} - R_{mitigated}$ | Risk remaining after applying controls | Same as initial |
| 6 | **Risk Probability Index** | $RPI = \dfrac{N_{realized}}{N_{total}}$ | Empirical calibration from past projects | Decimal (0 to 1) |
| 7 | **Probability Impact Grid Score** | $PIG = P_{score} \times I_{score}$ | Where $P_{score}, I_{score} \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ | Score 1 to 25 |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Risk Quantification

1. **Insurance & Contract Drafting** — SLA (Service Level Agreement) penalties are derived from $P \times C$ calculations.
2. **Agile Sprint Planning** — Story point allocation uses "known risk" reserves (typically **15–20%** buffer).
3. **Safety-Critical Systems** — Aerospace (DO-178C) and Medical (IEC 62304) demand **formal hazard analysis** using these exact formulas.
4. **Cybersecurity** — CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) scores use a **probability × impact** model identical to $RE$.
5. **Project Investment Decisions** — NPV (Net Present Value) of a software project is computed **after** subtracting aggregate risk exposure $R_{project}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Trick:** Whenever you compute Risk Exposure, **always state the units**. Writing "Risk = 0.3 × ₹5,00,000 = ₹1,50,000" earns more marks than just "0.3 × 5 = 1.5".

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Classification Matrix of Software Risk Types

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4) explicitly mandates mastery of the following risk taxonomy. Each type is **derived** with explanation, example, and analytical metric.

---

### 3.1.1 **Project Risks** (Plan-Centric Risks)

**Definition:** Risks that threaten the **project plan**, meaning the schedule, budget, work allocation, or resource availability.

**Why they exist:** Poor estimation, scope creep, personnel turnover, unrealistic deadlines, inadequate tooling.

**How they manifest:**
- Schedule slippage
- Budget overrun
- Resource unavailability (e.g., key developer quits)
- Scope inflation

**Typical Examples:**

$$
\text{Project Risk Examples} = \{\text{Staff turnover},\ \text{Vendor delay},\ \text{Scope creep},\ \text{Regulatory change}\}
$$

---

### 3.1.2 **Technical Risks** (Product-Architecture Centric)

**Definition:** Risks that threaten the **quality, design, implementation, or integration** of the software product.

**Why they exist:** Adoption of new/unfamiliar technology, complex algorithms, performance bottlenecks, integration with legacy systems.

**How they manifest:**
- Performance failure
- Security vulnerability
- Maintainability decay
- Architecture mismatch

**Formal Pattern:**

$$
\text{Technical Risk} = f(\text{Technology Maturity},\ \text{Team Skill},\ \text{Complexity})
$$

**Typical Examples:**
- "We have never built a distributed microservices system before"
- "Real-time response < 50ms is required but unproven"

---

### 3.1.3 **Business Risks** (Market-Commercial Centric)

**Definition:** Risks that threaten the **commercial viability or strategic viability** of the product being built.

**Why they exist:** Market shifts, competitor moves, changing customer needs, budget cuts, strategic re-prioritization by management.

**How they manifest:**
- Product becomes obsolete before launch
- Customer cancels contract
- Company is acquired and project is killed
- ROI (Return on Investment) becomes negative

**Typical Examples:**

$$
\text{Business Risk Examples} = \{\text{Market competition},\ \text{Customer churn},\ \text{Regulatory shift},\ \text{Cancel build}\}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 **Product Risks** (Built-Software Centric)

**Definition:** Risks that threaten the **delivered software product's** ability to meet user needs, performance, and reliability expectations.

**Why they exist:** Missing requirements, design defects, late-found bugs, performance issues, poor UX.

**How they manifest:**
- Critical bug in production
- Failure under load
- Low user adoption
- Poor usability scores

**Analytical Insight:** The Pressman Risk Triangle (from KTU Module 4 reference) links **product risk ↔ project risk ↔ business risk** as mutually reinforcing:

$$
R_{total} = R_{project} \oplus R_{technical} \oplus R_{business} \oplus R_{product}
$$

The $\oplus$ symbol denotes **superposition** (they compound, not just add).

---

### 3.1.5 **Known vs. Unknown Risks** (Knowledge Axis)

| Attribute | Known Risk | Unknown Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Identifiable in advance?** | Yes | No |
| **Can compute RE?** | Yes, $RE = P \times C$ | No — requires expert judgment |
| **Mitigation strategy** | Concrete action plan | Contingency reserves, insurance |
| **KTU Example** | "Team lacks React experience" | "Pandemic disrupts global supply chain" |
| **Reactive approach** | Direct response | Crisis management activation |

---

### 3.1.6 **Process Risks** (Methodology-Centric)

**Definition:** Risks arising from the **process** used to develop, test, and deliver the software.

**Why they exist:** Inadequate SDLC choice, missing reviews, weak change management, no version control.

**Typical Examples:**
- "No formal code review process in place"
- "Test cases are written ad-hoc, not from requirements"

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Risk Exposure Calculation

**Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
A software project has identified **3 risks** with the following data:

| Risk ID | Description | Probability (P) | Cost Impact (C) in ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | Senior developer quits mid-project | 0.30 | 5,00,000 |
| R2 | New payment gateway API fails integration | 0.20 | 8,00,000 |
| R3 | Customer changes requirements late | 0.50 | 3,00,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute individual Risk Exposures.**

$$
RE_1 = P_1 \times C_1 = 0.30 \times 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
RE_1 = 1{,}50{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RE_2 = P_2 \times C_2 = 0.20 \times 8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
RE_2 = 1{,}60{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RE_3 = P_3 \times C_3 = 0.50 \times 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
RE_3 = 1{,}50{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Aggregate Project Risk Exposure (assuming risks are independent).**

$$
R_{project} = RE_1 + RE_2 + RE_3
$$

$$
R_{project} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
R_{project} = 4{,}60{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Risk Reduction Leverage (RRL).**

Suppose the company invests ₹50,000 in a **comprehensive code review process** that reduces R2's probability from 0.20 to 0.05.

$$
RE_{2,\ after} = 0.05 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RRL = \dfrac{RE_{before} - RE_{after}}{Cost\ of\ Mitigation}
$$

$$
RRL = \dfrac{1{,}60{,}000 - 40{,}000}{50{,}000}
$$

$$
RRL = \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{50{,}000} = 2.4
$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹1 spent on code review returns **₹2.40 in risk reduction**. Since $RRL > 1$, the mitigation strategy is **economically justified**.

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Rule:** Accept the mitigation if $RRL > 1$. Reject if $RRL \le 1$. This is a **favourite KTU examiner question** for 7-mark subparts.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The Master Risk Type Summary Table

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 4. You can reproduce this in your exam to score high marks on any "types of risk" question.

| Risk Type | What It Threatens | Primary Source | KTU Textbook Example | Key Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Project Risk** | Plan, schedule, budget | Estimation errors, staff turnover | "Schedule may slip by 6 weeks" | Buffer time, backup staff |
| **Technical Risk** | Design, performance, quality | New tech, complex algorithms | "Unproven AI/ML model in production" | Prototyping, POCs, training |
| **Business Risk** | Market viability, ROI | Competition, strategy change | "Competitor launches same feature" | Market analysis, pivoting |
| **Product Risk** | Delivered software, user value | Missing req, design defects | "Critical bug in checkout flow" | Beta testing, user feedback |
| **Process Risk** | SDLC methodology, quality gates | No reviews, poor testing | "No formal test plan exists" | Adopt CMMI, ISO 9001 |
| **Known Risk** | Identifiable deliverables | Project analysis | "Team lacks React skills" | Training, hiring, mentoring |
| **Unknown Risk** | The unknown itself | External shocks | "Sudden geopolitical disruption" | Contingency reserves, insurance |
| **Anticipated Risk** | Known lifecycle phase | Project lifecycle knowledge | "Integration testing usually reveals bugs" | Pre-allocate buffer weeks |
| **Residual Risk** | Remnant after mitigation | Imperfect controls | "5% integration failure still possible" | Acceptance, monitoring |

---

## 3.4 Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS) — The Hierarchical View

A **Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS)** is a hierarchical decomposition of risks, similar to a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) but for risks. The top-down derivation is:

$$
RBS_{root} \rightarrow \{R_{project},\ R_{technical},\ R_{business},\ R_{product},\ R_{process}\}
$$

$$
R_{project} \rightarrow \{Schedule,\ Budget,\ Resource,\ Scope\}
$$

$$
R_{technical} \rightarrow \{Architecture,\ Performance,\ Security,\ Integration\}
$$

$$
R_{business} \rightarrow \{Market,\ Strategic,\ Financial,\ Customer\}
$$

Each leaf node is then assigned a **Probability (P)** and **Cost (C)** value to compute its individual $RE = P \times C$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Risk Classification Taxonomy (Hierarchical Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["SOFTWARE RISK UNIVERSE"] --> B1["PROJECT RISK"]
    A0 --> B2["TECHNICAL RISK"]
    A0 --> B3["BUSINESS RISK"]
    A0 --> B4["PRODUCT RISK"]
    A0 --> B5["PROCESS RISK"]

    B1 --> C1A["SCHEDULE SLIP"]
    B1 --> C1B["BUDGET OVERRUN"]
    B1 --> C1C["STAFF TURNOVER"]
    B1 --> C1D["SCOPE CREEP"]

    B2 --> C2A["ARCHITECTURE DEFECT"]
    B2 --> C2B["PERFORMANCE FAILURE"]
    B2 --> C2C["SECURITY VULN"]
    B2 --> C2D["INTEGRATION FAIL"]

    B3 --> C3A["MARKET SHIFT"]
    B3 --> C3B["COMPETITOR MOVE"]
    B3 --> C3C["STRATEGY CHANGE"]
    B3 --> C3D["REGULATORY CHANGE"]

    B4 --> C4A["CRITICAL BUG"]
    B4 --> C4B["UX FAILURE"]
    B4 --> C4C["RELIABILITY DROP"]
    B4 --> C4D["MISSED REQUIREMENT"]

    B5 --> C5A["WEAK REVIEWS"]
    B5 --> C5B["NO TEST PLAN"]
    B5 --> C5C["POOR CHANGE MGMT"]
    B5 --> C5D["NO VERSION CONTROL"]
```

---

## 4.2 Risk Management Process Flow (Proactive Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST1["STEP 1 IDENTIFY RISKS"] --> ST2["STEP 2 ANALYZE P AND C"]
    ST2 --> ST3["STEP 3 PLAN MITIGATION"]
    ST3 --> ST4["STEP 4 MONITOR TRIGGERS"]
    ST4 --> ST5["STEP 5 CONTROL EXECUTE"]
    ST5 --> ST1

    ST1 -. FEEDBACK LOOP .-> ST5
    ST2 -. UPDATE RISK REGISTER .-> ST3
    ST3 -. ASSIGN OWNERS .-> ST4
    ST4 -. TRIGGER CONTINGENCY .-> ST5
```

---

## 4.3 Risk Exposure Decision Matrix (Probability x Impact Grid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["INPUT RISK EVENT"] --> M1["PROBABILITY 0 TO 1"]
    M0 --> M2["IMPACT COST IN INR"]
    M1 --> M3["RE EQUALS P TIMES C"]
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4{"RE GREATER THAN THRESHOLD"}
    M4 -->|"YES"| M5["RED HIGH PRIORITY IMMEDIATE ACTION"]
    M4 -->|"NO"| M6{"RE MODERATE"}
    M6 -->|"YES"| M7["YELLOW MONITOR AND MITIGATE"]
    M6 -->|"NO"| M8["GREEN ACCEPT AND LOG"]
```

---

## 4.4 Known vs Unknown Risk Spectrum Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K0["RISK KNOWLEDGE AXIS"] --> K1["KNOWN RISKS"]
    K0 --> K2["DISCOVERABLE RISKS"]
    K0 --> K3["UNKNOWN RISKS"]

    K1 --> K1A["PROBABILITY COMPUTABLE"]
    K1 --> K1B["RE EQUALS P TIMES C"]
    K1 --> K1C["CONCRETE MITIGATION"]

    K2 --> K2A["IDENTIFIABLE BUT UNCERTAIN"]
    K2 --> K2B["EXPERT JUDGMENT NEEDED"]
    K2 --> K2C["CONTINGENCY PLANNING"]

    K3 --> K3A["NOT PREDICTABLE"]
    K3 --> K3B["BLACK SWAN EVENTS"]
    K3 --> K3C["INSURANCE AND RESERVE"]
```

---

## 4.5 Reactive vs Proactive Risk Management Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["RISK MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES"] --> L1["REACTIVE APPROACH"]
    L0 --> L2["PROACTIVE APPROACH"]

    L1 --> L1A["WAIT FOR RISK EVENT"]
    L1A --> L1B["CRISIS MODE FIREFIGHTING"]
    L1B --> L1C["POSTMORTEM LEARNING"]

    L2 --> L2A["FORECAST RISK IN ADVANCE"]
    L2A --> L2B["RMMM RISK MITIGATION MONITORING MGMT"]
    L2B --> L2C["CONTINUOUS RISK REGISTER"]
    L2C --> L2A
```
