# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Define **software risk**. Explain the two parameters used to compute **Risk Exposure (RE)** with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Software risk is the possibility of suffering loss due to uncertainty in software project activities. It is quantified by two parameters:

**Parameter 1 — Probability (P):** The likelihood that an adverse event will occur, expressed as a decimal between 0 and 1. *Example:* There is a 0.3 probability that a critical developer quits mid-project.

**Parameter 2 — Cost / Impact (C):** The magnitude of damage if the event occurs, measured in money, time, or quality units. *Example:* If a key developer quits, it costs ₹5,00,000 in recruitment and project delay.

**Risk Exposure Formula:**

$$
RE = P \times C
$$

For the above example, $RE = 0.3 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000\ \text{INR}$. This ₹1,50,000 figure helps the project manager **prioritize** which risks need immediate mitigation.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Differentiate between **Known Risks** and **Unknown Risks**. Give one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Known Risk | Unknown Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Risks that can be **identified and predicted** through project analysis | Risks that **cannot be identified** in advance |
| **Probability/Impact** | Both are **computable** ($RE = P \times C$ possible) | Cannot be reliably computed |
| **Mitigation** | Concrete action plan and ownership | Contingency reserves, insurance, agility |
| **Example 1** | "Team lacks React.js experience" | COVID-19 pandemic disrupting global IT supply chain |
| **Example 2** | "Payment gateway API has integration issues" | Sudden geopolitical war affecting cloud regions |

Known risks live in the **"known unknowns"** zone (we know we don't know), while unknown risks are the **"unknown unknowns"** (we don't know what we don't know).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **different types of software risks** (Project, Technical, Business, Product) with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A software project identifies the following risks:
> - **R1:** Database server crash — Probability = 0.10, Impact = ₹10,00,000
> - **R2:** Requirement change — Probability = 0.40, Impact = ₹4,00,000
> - **R3:** Third-party API failure — Probability = 0.25, Impact = ₹6,00,000
>
> Calculate the **Risk Exposure for each risk** and the **Aggregate Project Risk Exposure**. State which risk should be mitigated first and justify. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Types of Software Risks **[7 marks]**

**1. Project Risks** — These threaten the **project plan**, schedule, budget, or resources. *Examples:* Staff turnover, schedule slip, scope creep, budget overrun, vendor delays.

**2. Technical Risks** — These threaten the **technical quality, design, and architecture** of the software. *Examples:* Use of unproven new technology, performance failure, complex algorithm, integration issues, security vulnerabilities.

**3. Business Risks** — These threaten the **market viability or commercial success** of the product. *Examples:* Competitor launches similar product, market shift, customer cancellation, regulatory change, company acquisition.

**4. Product Risks** — These threaten the **delivered software's** ability to meet user requirements and expectations. *Examples:* Critical bug, UX failure, missing requirements, reliability drop, performance degradation.

**5. Process Risks** — These threaten the **SDLC process** used to develop the product. *Examples:* No code reviews, inadequate testing, weak change management, no version control.

> **Pro Tip (extra 1 mark):** End with a one-line statement: *"Effective software engineering practice requires identifying risks across all these categories and applying proactive mitigation."*

#### Part (b) — Risk Exposure Calculation **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Risk Exposure for R1:**

$$
RE_1 = P_1 \times C_1 = 0.10 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 2 — Risk Exposure for R2:**

$$
RE_2 = P_2 \times C_2 = 0.40 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 3 — Risk Exposure for R3:**

$$
RE_3 = P_3 \times C_3 = 0.25 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate Project Risk Exposure:**

$$
R_{project} = RE_1 + RE_2 + RE_3
$$

$$
R_{project} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
R_{project} = 4{,}10{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

**Step 5 — Prioritization Decision:**

| Risk ID | Risk Exposure | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| R2 | ₹1,60,000 | **1st (Highest)** |
| R3 | ₹1,50,000 | 2nd |
| R1 | ₹1,00,000 | 3rd |

**Justification:** R2 (Requirement Change) has the **highest Risk Exposure (₹1,60,000)** and must be **mitigated first** through techniques such as: (i) freezing requirements early, (ii) using formal change control board (CCB), (iii) signed MoUs with clients, and (iv) adopting agile user story acceptance criteria.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> Students often **write only the formula and final answer** without showing all intermediate steps. The KTU 2024 scheme **awards step-marks** (1.5 marks per correct sub-step). Always write "$RE_1 = 0.10 \times 10L = 1L$" **on its own line** with the unit. Skipping the unit (₹, INR) costs **0.5 marks** per risk.

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Define **Risk Exposure**. Explain the **Risk Management Process** with its key steps. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A project has two mitigation strategies:
> - **Strategy A:** Costs ₹80,000 and reduces a risk from $P = 0.5,\ C = ₹6,00,000$ to $P = 0.2$
> - **Strategy B:** Costs ₹50,000 and reduces a risk from $P = 0.4,\ C = ₹8,00,000$ to $P = 0.1$
>
> Compute the **Risk Reduction Leverage (RRL)** for both and recommend which is more cost-effective. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Risk Exposure & Process **[7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** Risk Exposure (RE) is the **expected loss** from a risk, computed as $RE = P \times C$, where P is probability and C is the cost/impact. It provides a **single numeric value** to rank and prioritize risks.

**Risk Management Process (5 marks) — Five Steps:**

1. **Risk Identification** — Catalog all possible risks using techniques like brainstorming, checklists, Delphi method, WBS decomposition, and RBS (Risk Breakdown Structure).
2. **Risk Analysis** — Quantify each identified risk's $P$ and $C$, then compute $RE = P \times C$. Categorize as Low/Medium/High using Probability-Impact grids.
3. **Risk Planning (Mitigation)** — For each high-priority risk, design a mitigation strategy: avoid, transfer (insurance), mitigate (reduce), or accept.
4. **Risk Monitoring** — Continuously track risk triggers and re-evaluate $P$ and $C$ values as the project progresses.
5. **Risk Control** — Activate contingency plans when monitored triggers cross pre-defined thresholds.

> **Diagram Bonus (extra 1 mark):** Draw a simple cyclic flowchart showing the five steps with feedback loops.

#### Part (b) — RRL Calculation **[7 marks]**

**Strategy A:**

$$
RE_{A,\ before} = 0.5 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RE_{A,\ after} = 0.2 \times 6{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
\Delta RE_A = 3{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RRL_A = \dfrac{\Delta RE_A}{Cost_A} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{80{,}000} = 2.25
$$

**Strategy B:**

$$
RE_{B,\ before} = 0.4 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}20{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RE_{B,\ after} = 0.1 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 80{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
\Delta RE_B = 3{,}20{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 2{,}40{,}000\ \text{INR}
$$

$$
RRL_B = \dfrac{\Delta RE_B}{Cost_B} = \dfrac{2{,}40{,}000}{50{,}000} = 4.80
$$

**Decision:**

| Strategy | Cost | RRL | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | ₹80,000 | 2.25 | Acceptable ($RRL > 1$) |
| B | ₹50,000 | **4.80** | **More cost-effective (RRL > 1)** |

**Recommendation:** **Strategy B is more cost-effective** because its Risk Reduction Leverage is **4.80** vs Strategy A's **2.25**. Every ₹1 spent on Strategy B recovers **₹4.80** in risk reduction, which is **2.13× more efficient** than Strategy A.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. Students frequently **forget the decision rule** ($RRL > 1$ ⇒ accept, $RRL \le 1$ ⇒ reject). Stating this rule explicitly **earns the final 1 mark** for "judgment/justification".
> 2. Some students compute the **simple cost difference** (₹80,000 - ₹50,000 = ₹30,000) instead of RRL. This is **wrong**; RRL is a **ratio**, not a difference.
> 3. Do not confuse **RE after** with **RE before**. The formula is $RRL = (RE_{before} - RE_{after}) / Cost$. Reversing them yields a **negative RRL**, which is mathematically incorrect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision gold — read this section 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

✅ **Definition Mastery**
- Risk = Uncertainty × Impact
- Risk Exposure $RE = P \times C$
- Risk Reduction Leverage $RRL = (RE_{before} - RE_{after}) / Cost_{mitigation}$

✅ **Five Core Risk Types (must-memorize)**
1. **Project Risk** — threatens the plan/schedule/budget
2. **Technical Risk** — threatens design/architecture/quality
3. **Business Risk** — threatens market/commercial viability
4. **Product Risk** — threatens the delivered software
5. **Process Risk** — threatens the SDLC methodology

✅ **Two Knowledge Axes**
- **Known Risks** → $RE$ computable → concrete mitigation
- **Unknown Risks** → $RE$ not computable → contingency reserves

✅ **Two Strategic Paradigms**
- **Reactive** = "fix it after it breaks" (low marks in KTU)
- **Proactive** = "prevent it before it breaks" (high-yield, 7+ marks)

✅ **Five-Step Risk Management Cycle (RMMM)**
- Identify → Analyze → Plan → Monitor → Control (with feedback loops)

✅ **Numerical Units Are Mandatory**
- Always append **INR / ₹ / person-hours** to every $RE$ value
- Always state the **decision rule** ($RRL > 1$ ⇒ accept)

✅ **Common KTU Examiner Traps**
- Writing formulas without **units** = -0.5 marks per risk
- Skipping the **"which to mitigate first"** justification = -1 mark
- Confusing **probability** with **impact** in the $RE$ formula = 0/7 marks
- Forgetting the **reactive vs proactive** comparison when asked about "risk management"

✅ **High-Frequency KTU 14-Mark Template**
1. Define risk and $RE$ formula (2 marks)
2. List 4–5 risk types with examples (4 marks)
3. Compute $RE$ for given data (4 marks)
4. Recommend mitigation using RRL or prioritization (2 marks)
5. Conclude with proactive risk management statement (2 marks)

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Identify, Quantify, Mitigate, Monitor — in that order. Always show units. Always justify the decision."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Monitoring and Management Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Risk Monitoring and Management Model # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Risk Monitoring and Management Model (RMMM)** is a systematic, closed-loop engineering process used in software project management to identify, analyze, plan, track, control, and communicate software risks throughout the entire project life cycle. According to **Pressman's Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach**, the Risk Management Process consists of six distinct, but interlinked, activities: 1. **Risk Identification (RID)** — Cataloging potential risks. 2. **Risk Analysis / Risk Estimation (REA)** — Assessing probability and impact. 3. **Risk Prioritization (RPR)** — Ranking risks by exposure. 4. **Risk Management / Planning (RMP)** — Defining mitigation, monitoring, and contingency strategies. 5. **Risk Monitoring (RMT)** — Continuous tracking of risk status, indicators, and triggers. 6. **Risk Resolution (RRE)** — Executing contingency plans when risks materialize. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Standard):** > *Risk Monitoring and Management is the formal, iterative process of identifying, classifying, prioritizing, planning mitigation strategies for, and continuously tracking risks that may compromise the schedule, budget, quality, or scope of a software project.* The output of this process is a **Risk Information Sheet (RIS)** / **Risk Data Sheet** that acts as the single source of truth for the project's risk register. ## 1.2 The Risk Itself — Formal Definition A **software risk** is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a **positive or negative effect** on at least one project objective (cost, schedule, quality, scope). Mathematically, the fundamental risk equation used in KTU evaluation is: $$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Probability}, \text{Impact})$$ Where: - **Probability (P)** = Likelihood of the risk event occurring (0.0 → 1.0). - **Impact (I)** = Severity of consequence if the risk occurs (often rated on a 1–5 or 1–10 scale). The composite metric — **Risk Exposure (RE)** — is computed as: $$RE = P \times I$$ > [!NOTE] > **Risk Exposure (RE)** is also called the **Risk Impact Factor** in some texts. It is the single most important quantitative metric in KTU board questions on this topic. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ship Captain's Logbook" Imagine you are the **captain of a cargo ship** crossing the Indian Ocean from Kochi to Singapore: | Ship-Management Term | Software Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Weather forecast reports | **Risk Identification** — looking for storms ahead | | Storm severity scale (1–10) | **Risk Analysis** — measuring how bad the storm is | | Priority boarding of cargo | **Risk Prioritization** — protecting the most valuable goods first | | Action plan if storm hits | **Risk Management / Mitigation** — preparing a backup plan | | Daily weather radio check | **Risk Monitoring** — continuously tracking weather | | Actual reroute to a safe harbor | **Risk Resolution / Contingency** — executing the backup plan | Just as a captain does not ignore cyclones, a software project manager does not ignore risks. **Risk Monitoring and Management** is the "weather watch" of project management — systematic, documented, and continuous. ## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024 > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **14-mark topic** in Module 4 of OECST723. It maps to **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Apply software engineering principles and techniques for software project planning, estimation, and risk management."* Expect direct questions on: > - RMMM Framework > - Risk Table / Risk Data Sheet > - Boehm's Top 10 Risk List > - Risk Exposure formula derivation and computation > - Difference between Reactive vs. Proactive Risk Strategies ]<]minimax[>[ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six-Stage Risk Management Model (Pressman's RMMM) The RMMM model is essentially a **closed-loop control system** for project uncertainty. Below is the analytical breakdown of each stage: ### Stage 1 — Risk Identification (RID) - **Goal:** Generate a comprehensive list of all possible risks. - **How:** Use **taxonomy-based** or **checklist-based** identification. - **Product risks:** requirements ambiguity, performance, reliability. - **Process risks:** schedule slippage, budget overrun. - **Technology risks:** obsolete platform, unproven DBMS. - **People risks:** staff turnover, skill gaps. - **Business risks:** market shift, competitor launch, funding cut. - **External risks:** regulatory change, vendor bankruptcy. - **Tools:** Brainstorming, Delphi technique, expert interviews, checklist. ### Stage 2 — Risk Analysis / Risk Estimation (REA) - **Goal:** Quantify the *probability* of each risk and the *impact* on the project. - **Approach:** - **Quantitative:** Use historical data and statistical models → outputs numeric $P$ and $I$. - **Qualitative:** Use expert judgement and ranking matrices → outputs low/medium/high buckets. - **Output:** The **Risk Information Sheet (RIS)** containing both qualitative and quantitative attributes. ### Stage 3 — Risk Prioritization (RPR) - **Goal:** Rank risks so that high-exposure items get addressed first. - **Why:** Project managers have finite time and resources — they must apply the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**. ### Stage 4 — Risk Management / Planning (RMP) — The Core of RMMM Each prioritized risk gets a **dedicated Risk Management Plan** with three components (this is the **RMMM "3M"** in Pressman): 1. **Mitigation (M₁):** Steps to *avoid* the risk or *reduce* its probability/impact. 2. **Monitoring (M₂):** Indicators, triggers, and tracking mechanisms to detect risk occurrence early. 3. **Management (M₃):** A **contingency plan** (Plan-B) to execute *if* the risk actually materializes. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **"3M = Mitigation, Monitoring, Management"** — the heart of Pressman's RMMM framework. This is the most frequently asked 7-mark sub-question. ### Stage 5 — Risk Monitoring (RMT) - **Goal:** Track the *state* of each identified risk over time. - **Key activities:** - Periodically review the **Risk Information Sheet**. - Compute updated Risk Exposure: $RE_{new} = P_{new} \times I_{new}$. - Track **trigger conditions** (early warning signals). - Maintain a **Risk Burndown Chart** analogous to Agile burndown. - **Outputs:** Updated risk register, status reports, escalation requests. ### Stage 6 — Risk Resolution (RRE) - **Goal:** Execute the **contingency plan** when the trigger condition fires. - **Result:** Either the risk is contained, accepted, transferred, or terminated. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Risk | $R = f(P, I)$ | Qualitative or Quantitative | Composite function of probability and impact | | 2 | Risk Exposure (RE) | $RE = P \times I$ | $0 \leq RE \leq 1$ (if P, I in [0,1]); or 1–100 | Used to rank risks | | 3 | Aggregate Risk Exposure (REI) | $REI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times I_i)$ | Sum of all identified risks | Top-down project-level exposure | | 4 | Boehm Risk Priority | Priority $= \text{Probability} \times \text{Loss}$ (0.1 to 1.0) | Scale 0.1 – 1.0 | Boehm's 1989 model | | 5 | Re-exposure | $RE_{new} = P_{new} \times I_{new}$ | Updated value post mitigation | Used in monitoring stage | | 6 | Risk Reduction | $\Delta RE = RE_{old} - RE_{new}$ | Positive value = improvement | Effectiveness of mitigation | | 7 | Mitigation Effectiveness | $M_{eff} = 1 - \frac{RE_{post}}{RE_{pre}}$ | 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%) | KTU 14-mark derivation question | | 8 | Probability Scale (KTU standard) | 0.1 (low), 0.3 (moderate), 0.5 (high), 0.7 (very high), 0.9 (critical) | Categorical | Match KTU board marking | | 9 | Impact Scale (KTU standard) | 1 (negligible) to 5 (catastrophic) | Categorical 1–5 | Or 1–10 | | 10 | Risk Category (Boehm) | Top 10 risk list | Categories: Personnel, Schedule, Requirements, etc. | Pressman Ch. 5 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Marking Pitfall:** When computing Risk Exposure, students often multiply the *subjective* category (Low/Med/High) directly. **Always convert categories to numeric values** first (e.g., Low = 0.1, Med = 0.4, High = 0.7). The board deducts 2 marks for unconverted units. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **NASA Software Projects:** RMMM is mandatory for all Class-A flight software. - **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Functional safety risk management = automotive RMMM. - **Banking / FinTech:** Regulatory risk + cybersecurity risk managed via RMMM. - **Healthcare (IEC 62304):** Medical device software uses RMMM for patient-safety risk. ## 2.4 Boehm's Top 10 Software Risk Items (Frequently Asked) Per **Pressman (Ch. 5)**, the most cited risk taxonomy is Barry Boehm's Top 10: 1. Personnel shortfalls 2. Unrealistic schedules and budgets 3. Developing the wrong software functions 4. Developing the wrong user interface 5. Gold plating (continued feature creep beyond requirements) 6. Continuing stream of requirement changes 7. Shortfalls in externally furnished components 8. Shortfalls in externally performed tasks 9. Real-time performance shortfalls 10. Straining computer-science capabilities ## 2.5 The Risk Information Sheet (RIS) — Structure The **Risk Data Sheet / Risk Information Sheet** is a one-page document per risk with these fields (must be memorized for KTU): | Field | Description | |---|---| | **Risk ID** | Unique identifier (e.g., R-001) | | **Date raised** | When the risk was first identified | | **Description** | Short summary of the risk event | | **Category** | Type (Product / Process / People / Tech) | | **Probability (P)** | Numeric value or category | | **Impact (I)** | Numeric value or category | | **Risk Exposure (RE)** | Computed P × I | | **Mitigation Plan** | Steps to reduce P or I | | **Monitoring Indicators** | Early warning signals / triggers | | **Contingency Plan** | Plan-B if risk materializes | | **Owner** | Person responsible for the risk | | **Status** | Active / Mitigated / Occurred / Closed | ]<]minimax[>[ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Risk Exposure from First Principles > **Problem (KTU 14-mark style):** A software project identifies three risks R1, R2, R3 with the following categorical data: > - R1: P = 0.5 (High probability), I = 4 (Major impact) > - R2: P = 0.3 (Moderate probability), I = 2 (Minor impact) > - R3: P = 0.7 (Very High probability), I = 5 (Catastrophic impact) > > Compute (a) the Risk Exposure of each, (b) the total Risk Exposure Index (REI) of the project, (c) prioritize the risks. ### Step 1 — Define the Risk Exposure Function The basic risk function is given by: $$R = f(P, I)$$ For independent risks, the simplest valid form is the **product model**: $$RE = P \times I$$ This is the linear independence assumption — the expected loss per unit occurrence, expressed as the product of likelihood and consequence severity. ### Step 2 — Convert Categorical to Numeric (if needed) In the problem, probability and impact are already numeric, so direct multiplication applies. ### Step 3 — Compute RE for R1 $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{R1} &= P_1 \times I_1 \\ &= 0.5 \times 4 \\ &= 2.0 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute RE for R2 $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{R2} &= P_2 \times I_2 \\ &= 0.3 \times 2 \\ &= 0.6 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute RE for R3 $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{R3} &= P_3 \times I_3 \\ &= 0.7 \times 5 \\ &= 3.5 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Compute Aggregate Risk Exposure Index (REI) The REI is the sum of all individual Risk Exposures, since the risks are assumed **independent** events: $$ \begin{aligned} REI &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times I_i \\ &= RE_{R1} + RE_{R2} + RE_{R3} \\ &= 2.0 + 0.6 + 3.5 \\ &= 6.1 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Prioritize the Risks Sorting by $RE$ in descending order: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{1st Priority: } & R3 \;(RE = 3.5) \\ \text{2nd Priority: } & R1 \;(RE = 2.0) \\ \text{3rd Priority: } & R2 \;(RE = 0.6) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Interpretation The project manager should allocate **mitigation budget and monitoring effort** in the order R3 → R1 → R2. R3 is the dominant contributor to REI (≈ 57% of total), making it the **"vital few"** in the Pareto sense. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mitigation Effectiveness (Re-Risk Exposure After Mitigation) > **Problem:** After applying a mitigation plan, R3's probability drops from 0.7 to 0.4 and impact drops from 5 to 3. Compute the reduction in REI. ### Step 1 — Compute New RE for R3 $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{R3}^{new} &= P_3^{new} \times I_3^{new} \\ &= 0.4 \times 3 \\ &= 1.2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Old RE for R3 (already known) $$ RE_{R3}^{old} = 0.7 \times 5 = 3.5 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Reduction in RE for R3 $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta RE_{R3} &= RE_{R3}^{old} - RE_{R3}^{new} \\ &= 3.5 - 1.2 \\ &= 2.3 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute New Aggregate REI $$ \begin{aligned} REI^{new} &= RE_{R1} + RE_{R2} + RE_{R3}^{new} \\ &= 2.0 + 0.6 + 1.2 \\ &= 3.8 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Mitigation Effectiveness $$ \begin{aligned} M_{eff} &= 1 - \frac{REI^{new}}{REI^{old}} \\ &= 1 - \frac{3.8}{6.1} \\ &= 1 - 0.6230 \\ &= 0.3770 \end{aligned} $$ So the mitigation plan reduced the project's aggregate risk by **≈ 37.7%**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Categorical-to-Numeric Conversion Matrix (KTU Standard) For KTU board questions where categorical values are given: | Category | Probability Value | Impact Value | |---|---|---| | Very Low | 0.1 | 1 | | Low | 0.3 | 2 | | Moderate | 0.5 | 3 | | High | 0.7 | 4 | | Very High | 0.9 | 5 | > **Step 1:** Identify the categorical pair. > **Step 2:** Convert each to its numeric equivalent using the matrix above. > **Step 3:** Apply the formula $RE = P \times I$. This conversion is **mandatory** before any calculation in KTU valuation. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reactive vs. Proactive Risk Strategies | Parameter | **Reactive Strategy** | **Proactive Strategy (RMMM)** | |---|---|---| | **When risks are addressed** | After they occur | Before they occur | | **Time cost** | High (firefighting) | Lower (planning) | | **Budget impact** | High (last-minute fixes) | Predictable (pre-allocated) | | **Approach** | Ad-hoc, team-dependent | Formal, process-driven | | **Tools** | None formal | Risk Information Sheet, RMMM, Delphi, FMEA | | **KTU recommendation** | ❌ Not recommended | ✅ Industry best practice | | **Triggered by** | Crisis event | Identified trigger condition | | **Documentation** | Sparse | Comprehensive (Risk Register) | > [!NOTE] > KTU 14-mark questions often include a "compare and contrast" sub-question. Memorize the table above and adapt it to the question's context. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Risk Types and Examples | Risk Category | Example | Probability | Impact | Typical Mitigation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Product Size** | Function point underestimation | 0.5 | 4 | Use multiple estimation models (FP, COCOMO II) | | **Business Impact** | Loss of customer trust | 0.3 | 5 | Agile demos, frequent releases | | **Customer** | Unclear requirements | 0.7 | 4 | Joint Application Development (JAD) sessions | | **Process** | Inadequate testing | 0.5 | 4 | TDD, code reviews | | **Technology** | New unproven DBMS | 0.6 | 4 | Prototyping, PoC in early iteration | | **People** | Key developer resigns | 0.3 | 5 | Knowledge transfer, pair programming | | **Estimation** | Schedule optimism | 0.7 | 3 | Three-point estimation, buffers | | **Tools** | IDE crashes frequently | 0.2 | 2 | Use stable LTS versions | | **External** | Vendor supply failure | 0.1 | 5 | Dual sourcing, SLA contracts | | **Schedule** | Parallel team conflicts | 0.5 | 3 | Gantt chart, dependency tracking | ]<]minimax[>[ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 RMMM Closed-Loop Control Diagram (Pressman Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Stage 1: Risk Identification RID"] --> B["Stage 2: Risk Analysis REA"] B --> C["Stage 3: Risk Prioritization RPR"] C --> D["Stage 4: Risk Management Planning RMP"] D --> E["Stage 5: Risk Monitoring RMT"] E --> F{"Did the risk occur?"} F -- Yes --> G["Stage 6: Risk Resolution RRE"] G --> H["Update Risk Information Sheet"] F -- No --> E H --> E E -. Continuous Feedback Loop .-> A ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The RMMM model is a **closed-loop control system**. The horizontal arrows (A → B → C → D → E → F) represent the **forward flow** of risk processing. The decision diamond (F) branches into Resolution (G) on risk occurrence, with the result feeding back into the Risk Information Sheet (H), which then re-enters Monitoring (E). The dotted arrow from E back to A represents **continuous re-identification** of new risks throughout the project life cycle. ## 4.2 Risk Data Sheet (Risk Information Sheet) Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Project Kickoff"] --> P2["Identify Risks via
Brainstorming and Checklist"] P2 --> P3["Populate Risk
Information Sheet RIS"] P3 --> P4["Compute RE equals P times I"] P4 --> P5["Sort by Descending RE"] P5 --> P6{"RE above Threshold?"} P6 -- Yes --> P7["Assign Owner and
Build 3M Plan"] P6 -- No --> P8["Add to Watchlist"] P7 --> P9["Weekly Risk Review"] P8 --> P9 P9 --> P10{"Trigger Condition
Met?"} P10 -- Yes --> P11["Activate Contingency Plan"] P10 -- No --> P9 P11 --> P12["Post Incident Review"] P12 --> P9 ``` ## 4.3 Boehm's Top 10 Risk Categories — Hierarchical Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["Boehm Top 10
Software Risk Categories"] Root --> R1["1. Personnel
Shortfalls"] Root --> R2["2. Unrealistic
Schedules and Budgets"] Root --> R3["3. Wrong Software
Functions"] Root --> R4["4. Wrong User
Interface"] Root --> R5["5. Gold Plating"] Root --> R6["6. Stream of
Requirement Changes"] Root --> R7["7. Shortfalls in
External Components"] Root --> R8["8. Shortfalls in
External Tasks"] Root --> R9["9. Real Time
Performance Gaps"] Root --> R10["10. Straining CS
Capabilities"] ``` ## 4.4 Risk Monitoring Frequency Cadence ```mermaid gantt title Risk Monitoring Cadence Across Project Life Cycle dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD axisFormat %b section Inception Initial Risk Workshop :milestone, m1, 2025-01-15, 1d section Elaboration Weekly Risk Review :active, r1, 2025-02-01, 60d Update RIS :r2, 2025-02-08, 7d section Construction Daily Standup Risks :r3, 2025-04-01, 90d Sprint Retrospective :r4, 2025-04-15, 14d section Transition Release Risk Audit :milestone, m2, 2025-07-01, 1d ``` > **Interpretation:** Risk monitoring frequency should **intensify** as the project moves from Inception → Transition. The cadence tightens from "one-time" (Inception) to "daily" (Construction) to "audit" (Transition). ## 4.5 Proactive vs. Reactive Risk Strategy Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Risk Identified] --> B{Strategy Type} B -- Proactive RMMM --> C[Build 3M Plan
Mitigation Monitoring Management] C --> D[Track Trigger Conditions] D --> E{Trigger Fires?} E -- No --> D E -- Yes --> F[Execute Contingency Plan] B -- Reactive --> G[Wait for Risk to Occur] G --> H[Crisis Meeting] H --> I[Damage Control] I --> J[Post Mortem] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The **Proactive branch** (left side) handles risks *before* they materialize via the 3M plan, while the **Reactive branch** (right side) is the "firefighting" mode that RMMM seeks to avoid. ]<]minimax[>[ # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "Risk Exposure" in the context of software project management. How is it computed?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Risk Exposure (RE) is a quantitative measure of the overall impact of a risk on a software project, expressed as the product of the probability of occurrence and the severity of impact. $$RE = P \times I$$ Where **P** is the probability (0.0 to 1.0) and **I** is the impact (typically 1 to 5 on KTU scale). A higher RE value indicates a risk that demands greater managerial attention. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three (3) components of Pressman's Risk Management Plan (RMMM 3M).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three components of the RMMM plan are: 1. **Mitigation** — Steps to reduce probability or impact of the risk. (1 mark) 2. **Monitoring** — Indicators, triggers, and tracking mechanisms to detect risk early. (1 mark) 3. **Management (Contingency)** — A Plan-B to execute if the risk materializes. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4]` **Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Risk Monitoring and Management Model (RMMM)** as proposed by Pressman. Discuss each stage in detail. (7 marks) **Q. (b)** A software project for a banking system has identified the following five risks: | Risk ID | Description | Category | Probability | Impact | |---|---|---|---|---| | R1 | Vendor DBMS failure | Technology | 0.4 | 4 | | R2 | Key developer resignation | People | 0.2 | 5 | | R3 | Regulatory change (RBI norms) | External/Business | 0.6 | 3 | | R4 | Late delivery of third-party API | Process | 0.5 | 4 | | R5 | Gold-plating feature creep | Product | 0.3 | 2 | For each risk, compute the **Risk Exposure (RE)**, the **aggregate Risk Exposure Index (REI)**, and **prioritize** the risks. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** The RMMM model has **six stages** as shown in the diagram below: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RID: Risk Identification"] --> B["REA: Risk Analysis"] B --> C["RPR: Risk Prioritization"] C --> D["RMP: Risk Management Planning"] D --> E["RMT: Risk Monitoring"] E --> F{"Risk Occurred?"} F -- Yes --> G["RRE: Risk Resolution"] G --> H["Update Risk Info Sheet"] F -- No --> E H --> E ``` **Stage 1 — Risk Identification (RID):** Generate a comprehensive list of potential risks using techniques like brainstorming, Delphi, expert interviews, and historical checklists. Output: a raw risk catalog. (1 mark) **Stage 2 — Risk Analysis (REA):** Each risk is characterized by its **probability (P)** and **impact (I)**. Use either **qualitative** (Low/Medium/High) or **quantitative** (numeric 0–1 and 0–5) scales. Output: a **Risk Information Sheet (RIS)**. (1 mark) **Stage 3 — Risk Prioritization (RPR):** Risks are ranked using $RE = P \times I$ to focus attention on the "vital few". Output: ordered risk list. (1 mark) **Stage 4 — Risk Management Planning (RMP):** Each prioritized risk gets a **3M plan** (Mitigation, Monitoring, Management). Output: a formal **Risk Mitigation, Monitoring and Management Plan**. (2 marks) **Stage 5 — Risk Monitoring (RMT):** Continuously track risk status, re-compute RE, and check for **trigger conditions**. Output: updated risk register, escalation reports. (1 mark) **Stage 6 — Risk Resolution (RRE):** Execute the **contingency plan** when the risk triggers. Conduct a **post-mortem** to update the risk library. (1 mark) #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Compute RE for each risk using $RE = P \times I$:** $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{R1} &= 0.4 \times 4 = 1.6 \\ RE_{R2} &= 0.2 \times 5 = 1.0 \\ RE_{R3} &= 0.6 \times 3 = 1.8 \\ RE_{R4} &= 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0 \\ RE_{R5} &= 0.3 \times 2 = 0.6 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute aggregate REI:** $$ \begin{aligned} REI &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} P_i \times I_i \\ &= 1.6 + 1.0 + 1.8 + 2.0 + 0.6 \\ &= 7.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Prioritize the risks (descending RE):** | Rank | Risk ID | RE | % of REI | Recommended Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | R4 | 2.0 | 28.57% | Dual-vendor API, integration buffer | | 2 | R3 | 1.8 | 25.71% | Compliance team, regulatory watcher | | 3 | R1 | 1.6 | 22.86% | PoC for DBMS, SLA with vendor | | 4 | R2 | 1.0 | 14.29% | Cross-training, KT documentation | | 5 | R5 | 0.6 | 8.57% | Strict scope freeze, change control board | (7 marks — 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for computation, 2 marks for REI, 1 mark for prioritization logic, 1 mark for the action column) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 — Alternative Choice]` **Q. (a)** Explain **Boehm's Top 10 Software Risk Items** with a brief description of each. Discuss how these are useful in proactive risk management. (7 marks) **Q. (b)** A project identifies a critical risk with P = 0.7 and I = 5. After applying a mitigation plan, the probability reduces to 0.3 and the impact reduces to 2. Compute: (i) Original RE, (ii) New RE, (iii) Reduction in RE ($\Delta RE$), (iv) Mitigation effectiveness as a percentage. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** Boehm's Top 10 Software Risk Items (with brief description): 1. **Personnel shortfalls** — Inadequate or under-skilled staff, hiring delays, key-person dependency. (0.5 mark) 2. **Unrealistic schedules and budgets** — Estimation optimism, padding removed, deadline pressure. (0.5 mark) 3. **Developing the wrong software functions** — Incomplete or misinterpreted requirements. (0.5 mark) 4. **Developing the wrong user interface** — UI that does not match user mental models. (0.5 mark) 5. **Gold plating** — Continued feature additions beyond agreed scope. (0.5 mark) 6. **Continuing stream of requirement changes** — Volatile requirements causing rework. (0.5 mark) 7. **Shortfalls in externally furnished components** — COTS/GOTS components that fail to meet specs. (0.5 mark) 8. **Shortfalls in externally performed tasks** — Subcontractor delays or quality issues. (0.5 mark) 9. **Real-time performance shortfalls** — Latency/throughput violations. (0.5 mark) 10. **Straining computer-science capabilities** — Pushing algorithmic or technical limits. (0.5 mark) **Utility in proactive risk management (1.5 marks):** - Acts as a **checklist** for risk identification workshops. - Categorizes risks into **Product, Process, People, Technology, External** buckets, easing analysis. - Provides a **shared vocabulary** between developers, managers, and customers. - Enables benchmarking against **historical project data** to set realistic $P$ and $I$ values. - Drives the construction of **Risk Information Sheets** for each identified top-10 risk. #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Original RE:** $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{old} &= P_{old} \times I_{old} \\ &= 0.7 \times 5 \\ &= 3.5 \end{aligned} $$ (2 marks) **Step 2 — New RE (after mitigation):** $$ \begin{aligned} RE_{new} &= P_{new} \times I_{new} \\ &= 0.3 \times 2 \\ &= 0.6 \end{aligned} $$ (2 marks) **Step 3 — Reduction in RE ($\Delta RE$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta RE &= RE_{old} - RE_{new} \\ &= 3.5 - 0.6 \\ &= 2.9 \end{aligned} $$ (1 mark) **Step 4 — Mitigation effectiveness as a percentage:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{eff} &= \left(1 - \frac{RE_{new}}{RE_{old}}\right) \times 100\% \\ &= \left(1 - \frac{0.6}{3.5}\right) \times 100\% \\ &= (1 - 0.1714) \times 100\% \\ &= 82.86\% \end{aligned} $$ (2 marks) **Conclusion:** The mitigation plan was highly effective, reducing the risk exposure by **2.9 units (≈ 83% effectiveness)**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting categorical-to-numeric conversion:** If the question says "P = High, I = Major", students must convert to **0.7** and **4** (or as per the KTU scale) before computing RE. Marks lost: **2/7**. > 2. **Confusing Mitigation, Monitoring, and Management:** Students often swap Monitoring (tracking) with Mitigation (prevention) in 3M explanations. Marks lost: **1–2/7**. > 3. **Missing the closed-loop arrow** in the RMMM diagram: The "feedback from Resolution → Monitoring" arrow is mandatory. Omitting it costs **1 mark**. > 4. **Forgetting to prioritize** after computing RE: A common omission that costs **1 mark** in 7-mark sub-parts. > 5. **Not showing formula substitution step-by-step:** KTU board examiners require each arithmetic step explicitly; skipping the substitution loses **1–2 marks**. > 6. **Writing only "Boehm's Top 10"** without explaining how they map to the RMMM process: Always tie back to the proactive RMMM loop. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - ✅ **Definition of Risk:** Uncertain event with positive or negative impact on project objectives. - ✅ **RMMM = 6 stages:** **RID → REA → RPR → RMP → RMT → RRE** (Pressman model). - ✅ **Core formula:** $RE = P \times I$ (Risk Exposure = Probability × Impact). - ✅ **Aggregate metric:** $REI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times I_i$ (independent risks). - ✅ **Mitigation Effectiveness:** $M_{eff} = 1 - \frac{RE_{new}}{RE_{old}}$ (express as 0–1 or 0–100%). - ✅ **3M Plan Components:** **Mitigation** (avoid/reduce), **Monitoring** (track), **Management** (contingency). - ✅ **Risk Information Sheet (RIS):** One-page document per risk with ID, P, I, RE, 3M plan, owner, status. - ✅ **Boehm's Top 10:** Personnel, Schedule, Wrong functions, Wrong UI, Gold plating, Requirement changes, External component shortfall, External task shortfall, Real-time performance, CS capability strain. - ✅ **Proactive vs. Reactive:** Proactive (RMMM) > Reactive (firefighting) — industry best practice. - ✅ **Re-identification is continuous** — dotted feedback loop in diagram. - ✅ **Categorical-to-numeric conversion** is mandatory before any RE calculation. - ✅ **Prioritization is mandatory** — output a ranked table with action column. - ✅ **Trigger conditions** are the bridge between Monitoring and Resolution stages. - ✅ **For 14-mark questions:** Always include diagram + formula + computation + interpretation. - ✅ **Common exam traps:** Confusing $RE$ with $REI$; omitting the feedback loop; mixing up 3M definitions. - ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"RID REA RPR RMP RMT RRE = 3M Loop"** = the entire RMMM framework in one line. - ✅ **Real-world link:** NASA, ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical) all use RMMM-style processes. - ✅ **Pareto rule:** 80% of project risk comes from 20% of the identified risks — focus on top-ranked items. ]<]minimax[>[

topics.title: Software Project Management - Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Project Management - Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Software Project Management Planning?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> *Software Project Management Planning* is the disciplined process of defining the **scope**, **objectives**, **deliverables**, **resources**, **schedule**, **cost**, **quality**, and **risk** of a software project, and producing a **Software Project Management Plan (SPMP)** that guides the entire Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). It establishes a roadmap so that the right product is built, in the right way, at the right time, within budget.

In KTU's 2024 NEP-aligned framework, planning is treated as a *cognitive* and *managerial* activity that directly satisfies:

- **CO4**: *Apply software engineering principles, tools, and techniques for project planning and management.*
- Bloom's Levels: *Apply (L3)*, *Analyze (L4)*, *Evaluate (L5)*.

---

## 1.2 The Big Picture: 7 P's of Software Project Planning

| S.No | **P** | Meaning | Real-World Sense |
|------|------|------|------|
| 1 | **Product** | The software to be built | The *house* you will construct |
| 2 | **Process** | The SDLC model used | The *blueprint* of construction |
| 3 | **Project** | All activities, resources | The *construction project* itself |
| 4 | **People** | Team & stakeholders | The *workers, engineers, clients* |
| 5 | **Performance** | Quality & reliability measures | How *strong & efficient* the house is |
| 6 | **Productivity** | Output per unit input | How *fast* workers build per day |
| 7 | **Prediction** | Estimates & forecasts | How *accurately* we guess cost/time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Almost every 14-mark question in Module 4 expects students to map a scenario to these 7 P's. Memorize the table above — it is a *favourite* of question setters.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Planning a Wedding vs. Planning Software

Think of **planning a wedding** (real-world analogy):

- **Scope:** Invite 300 guests, 2-day event → equivalent to *Feature list* in software.
- **Budget:** ₹10 lakhs → equivalent to *Cost estimation* (COCOMO).
- **Timeline:** 6 months of prep → equivalent to *Schedule estimation*.
- **Vendors:** Caterers, decorators, DJ → equivalent to *Staffing & resources*.
- **Risks:** Rain, vendor cancellation → equivalent to *Risk management*.
- **Quality:** Delicious food, good music → equivalent to *Quality planning*.

> [!TIP]
> Just as a wedding without planning turns chaotic, software without planning overruns budget by **50–200%** (Standish Group CHAOS report). Planning converts *chaos* into a *controlled engineering process*.

---

## 1.4 Hierarchical View of Project Planning Levels

Project planning is not a single document — it is a **hierarchy of decisions**:

1. **Strategic Planning** — *Why* build? (feasibility, business case) — *months*.
2. **Tactical Planning** — *What* & *How*? (requirements, architecture) — *weeks*.
3. **Operational Planning** — *When & Who*? (tasks, schedules, Gantt) — *days/hours*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SPMP (Software Project Management Plan)** is the *single, formal document* consolidating all three. It is governed by IEEE Standard **830-1998 / 1058-2018**.

---

## 1.5 Inputs to the Planning Process

The planning function consumes the following inputs (from earlier modules):

- Problem statement / Vision document
- Requirement Specification (SRS)
- Feasibility Study Report
- Organizational policies (standards, templates)
- Historical data (from past projects in the same domain)
- Available resource pool (HR, hardware, licenses)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequent 3-mark question asks: *"List any four inputs to software project planning."* Memorize at least 4–5.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 6 Core Activities of Project Planning (Decomposed)

A software project plan is built in the following logical chain. Each step *feeds* the next.

1. **Scope Definition & WBS Creation**
   - *Why:* To decompose the project into manageable *work packages*.
   - *How:* A **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** is a hierarchical decomposition — Project → Phases → Tasks → Sub-tasks → Work packages.
   - *Analogy:* A wedding planner breaks "Wedding" into "Ceremony", "Reception", "Catering", each into sub-tasks.

2. **Size Estimation**
   - *Why:* Cost/Time depend on *size*.
   - *How:* Two main families → **Lines of Code (LOC)** and **Function Points (FP)**.
   - *Analogy:* Estimating the size of a house by counting bricks (LOC) or counting rooms (FP).

3. **Effort Estimation**
   - *Why:* How many *person-months* of work?
   - *How:* **COCOMO, COCOMO II, Use-Case Points, Expert Judgment, Delphi, Analogy.**
   - *Analogy:* Estimating how many *labourers* will build the house.

4. **Schedule Estimation**
   - *Why:* Convert effort into *calendar time* and *milestones*.
   - *How:* **PERT, CPM, Gantt Charts, Staffing profiles.**
   - *Analogy:* Deciding the *construction sequence* (foundation → walls → roof).

5. **Cost/Budget Estimation**
   - *Why:* Total money to be sanctioned.
   - *How:* Cost = Effort × Rate per person-month + overhead + tools.
   - *Analogy:* Wedding budget = vendors + venue + food + decoration.

6. **Risk & Quality Planning**
   - *Why:* Anticipate failure modes and quality goals.
   - *How:* **Risk register, RMMM (Risk Mitigation, Monitoring, Management), Quality metrics.**
   - *Analogy:* Wedding insurance + vendor backup plan.

---

## 2.2 Size Estimation — The Two Schools

### A) Lines of Code (LOC) / KLOC

- **Definition:** Physical or logical source lines of code.
- *Metric:* **1 KLOC = 1000 Lines of Code.**
- *Pros:* Simple, language-aware.
- *Cons:* Depends on language, programmer style — *unreliable early in the project*.

### B) Function Points (FP) — Albrecht's Method (1979)

A **technology-independent** measure based on the user's view of functionality.

**Step-by-step FP calculation:**

1. Identify **5 FP components**: External Inputs (EI), External Outputs (EO), External Inquiries (EQ), Internal Logical Files (ILF), External Interface Files (EIF).
2. Classify each as **Low / Average / High** complexity.
3. Look up weights in the standard FP table.
4. Compute the **Unadjusted FP (UFP)**.
5. Compute the **Technical Complexity Factor (TCF)** using 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs).

The formula is:

$$
TCF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum_{i=1}^{14} F_i
$$

where $F_i$ is the influence rating of the $i^{th}$ GSC (range 0–5).

$$
FP = UFP \times TCF
$$

---

## 2.3 Effort Estimation — The COCOMO Model Suite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COCOMO = COnstructive COst MOdel** (Boehm, 1981). It is the *single most important* estimation model for KTU Module 4.

### 2.3.1 Basic COCOMO

Three project classes:

| Project Type | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ | Description |
|--------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| **Organic** | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 | Small teams, familiar environment |
| **Semi-Detached** | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 | Medium-size, mixed experience |
| **Embedded** | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 | Tight constraints, real-time |

The governing equations:

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^{b} \quad \text{[Effort in Person-Months (PM)]}
$$

$$
D = c \times (E)^{d} \quad \text{[Development Time in Months]}
$$

$$
P = \frac{E}{D} \quad \text{[Average Team Size, People]}
$$

### 2.3.2 Intermediate COCOMO

Adds **15 Effort Multipliers (EMs)** for cost drivers (product, hardware, personnel, project attributes).

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^{b} \times EAF
$$

where $EAF = \prod_{i=1}^{15} EM_i$ and each $EM_i \in \{Very\ Low,\ Low,\ Nominal,\ High,\ Very\ High,\ Extra\ High\}$.

### 2.3.3 Detailed COCOMO

Each phase (requirements, design, code, test, etc.) is estimated independently, then summed.

### 2.3.4 COCOMO II (1997, Boehm et al.)

Modern, incremental, spiral-friendly. The form is:

$$
E = A \times (Size)^{B} \times \prod_{i=1}^{17} EM_i
$$

where $A = 2.94$ is the calibration constant, $B$ ranges 1.1–1.24 (scale factors), $Size$ can be in FP, LOC, or Use-Case points, and 17 EMs replace the older 15.

---

## 2.4 Schedule & Time Estimation

### 2.4.1 Gantt Chart

- Horizontal bar chart: tasks vs. calendar time.
- Shows *start*, *end*, *duration*, *dependencies*, *milestones*, *critical path*.
- Invented by **Henry Gantt (1910s)**.

### 2.4.2 PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)

- Three-point estimate: **Optimistic (O), Most Likely (M), Pessimistic (P)**.
- Expected duration:

$$
t_e = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
$$

- Variance per activity:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^{2}
$$

### 2.4.3 CPM (Critical Path Method)

- Find the **longest path** through the network — the *Critical Path*.
- Critical Path = **minimum** time required.
- Activities on it have **zero slack/float**.

---

## 2.5 The Putnam Estimation Model (North-American)

Effort follows a **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
E = \left( \frac{L^{3}}{C_k^{3} \cdot t_d^{4}} \right)^{1/3}
$$

where $L$ = lines of code, $C_k$ = technology constant, $t_d$ = development time.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print or memorize this table** — these are the equations that appear in **Part B (14-mark) numerical problems**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Function Point | $FP = UFP \times TCF$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | TCF | $TCF = 0.65 + 0.01 \cdot \sum F_i$ | $F_i \in [0,5]$ |
| 3 | COCOMO Basic Effort | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^{b}$ | Person-Months (PM) |
| 4 | COCOMO Basic Time | $D = c \cdot E^{d}$ | Months |
| 5 | Team Size | $P = E / D$ | Persons |
| 6 | COCOMO Intermediate | $E = a \cdot (KLOC)^{b} \cdot EAF$ | PM, $EAF$ is dimensionless |
| 7 | COCOMO II | $E = 2.94 \cdot (Size)^{B} \cdot \prod EM_i$ | modern |
| 8 | PERT Expected Time | $t_e = (O + 4M + P)/6$ | months/days |
| 9 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^{2} = ((P - O)/6)^{2}$ | — |
| 10 | Productivity | $PR = Size / E$ | $LOC/PM$ or $FP/PM$ |
| 11 | Cost | $Cost = E \times Rate_{PM}$ | currency |
| 12 | Putnam Model | $E = (L^{3}/(C_k^{3} t_d^{4}))^{1/3}$ | PM |
| 13 | Cost-Per-Defect | $CPDef = Cost / Defects$ | ₹/defect |
| 14 | Schedule Risk | $SR = \sigma_{proj} / t_e$ | dimensionless |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **COCOMO** is used in **defense projects (DRDO, ISRO)** for large embedded systems.
- **Function Points** are mandated by **ISBSG (International Software Benchmarking Standards Group)** for benchmarking.
- **PERT/CPM** are foundational to all **Agile/Scrum sprint planning** today.
- The **7 P's** appear verbatim in **PMI/PMBOK** curriculum — students writing KTU exams can quote PMBOK to score extra marks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Problem 1 (Basic COCOMO — Organic)**
> A software project is estimated at **8 KLOC** and is of *organic* nature. Compute the **effort, development time, and average team size**.

### Solution — Step 1: Identify the project class

For *organic* projects:

$$
a = 2.4, \quad b = 1.05, \quad c = 2.5, \quad d = 0.38
$$

### Step 2: Compute the effort (PM)

$$
E = a \times (KLOC)^{b} = 2.4 \times (8)^{1.05}
$$

Compute $8^{1.05}$:

$$
8^{1.05} = 8 \times 8^{0.05}
$$

We know $8^{0.05} = e^{0.05 \ln 8} = e^{0.05 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.10397} \approx 1.1095$.

Thus:

$$
8^{1.05} \approx 8 \times 1.1095 \approx 8.876
$$

Therefore:

$$
E = 2.4 \times 8.876 \approx 21.30 \text{ Person-Months (PM)}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the development time (months)

$$
D = c \times E^{d} = 2.5 \times (21.30)^{0.38}
$$

Compute $21.30^{0.38}$:

$$
21.30^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln 21.30} = e^{0.38 \times 3.0587} = e^{1.1623} \approx 3.197
$$

Therefore:

$$
D = 2.5 \times 3.197 \approx 7.99 \approx 8.0 \text{ Months}
$$

### Step 4: Average team size

$$
P = \frac{E}{D} = \frac{21.30}{8.0} \approx 2.66 \approx 3 \text{ People}
$$

### Final Answer

> **Effort = 21.30 PM, Development Time = 8.0 months, Team Size = 3 persons.**

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Function Point Estimation)**
> A system has: **10 External Inputs (Average)**, **6 External Outputs (High)**, **4 External Inquiries (Low)**, **3 Internal Logical Files (Average)**, **2 External Interface Files (Low)**. The sum of 14 GSC influence ratings is **40**. Compute the **Function Points**.

### Step 1: Compute Unadjusted Function Points (UFP)

Standard FP weight table (Albrecht 1979):

| Function Type | Low | Average | High |
|---------------|-----|---------|------|
| External Inputs (EI) | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| External Outputs (EO) | 4 | 5 | 7 |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 3 | 4 | 6 |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 7 | 10 | 15 |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 5 | 7 | 10 |

Apply weights:

- EI Average: $10 \times 4 = 40$
- EO High: $6 \times 7 = 42$
- EQ Low: $4 \times 3 = 12$
- ILF Average: $3 \times 10 = 30$
- EIF Low: $2 \times 5 = 10$

Summing:

$$
UFP = 40 + 42 + 12 + 30 + 10 = 134
$$

### Step 2: Compute Technical Complexity Factor (TCF)

$$
TCF = 0.65 + 0.01 \times \sum F_i = 0.65 + 0.01 \times 40 = 0.65 + 0.40 = 1.05
$$

### Step 3: Compute FP

$$
FP = UFP \times TCF = 134 \times 1.05 = 140.7
$$

### Final Answer

> **Function Points ≈ 141.**

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Problem 3 (PERT Network)**
> A project has 5 activities with the following three-time estimates (O, M, P) in days: A(2, 4, 6), B(3, 6, 9), C(1, 5, 9), D(4, 7, 10), E(2, 4, 6). Compute the **expected duration of each activity** and the **variance**.

### Step-by-step PERT Calculation

For each activity, apply $t_e = (O + 4M + P)/6$ and $\sigma^2 = ((P - O)/6)^2$.

**Activity A:** O=2, M=4, P=6

$$
t_e^{A} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.00
$$

$$
\sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^{2} = 0.444
$$

**Activity B:** O=3, M=6, P=9

$$
t_e^{B} = \frac{3 + 24 + 9}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6.00
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2 = \left(\frac{6}{6}\right)^{2} = 1.000
$$

**Activity C:** O=1, M=5, P=9

$$
t_e^{C} = \frac{1 + 20 + 9}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5.00
$$

$$
\sigma_C^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^{2} = 1.778
$$

**Activity D:** O=4, M=7, P=10

$$
t_e^{D} = \frac{4 + 28 + 10}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7.00
$$

$$
\sigma_D^2 = \left(\frac{6}{6}\right)^{2} = 1.000
$$

**Activity E:** O=2, M=4, P=6

$$
t_e^{E} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = 4.00
$$

$$
\sigma_E^2 = 0.444
$$

### Tabulated Result

| Activity | O | M | P | $t_e$ (days) | $\sigma^2$ |
|---------|---|---|---|------------|----------|
| A | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4.00 | 0.444 |
| B | 3 | 6 | 9 | 6.00 | 1.000 |
| C | 1 | 5 | 9 | 5.00 | 1.778 |
| D | 4 | 7 | 10 | 7.00 | 1.000 |
| E | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4.00 | 0.444 |

---

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What Estimator?

| Situation | Best Estimator | Why |
|-----------|---------------|-----|
| Small project, in-house team, familiar domain | Basic COCOMO Organic | Calibrated for small teams |
| Large project, embedded real-time | Embedded COCOMO | High $b$ captures constraint complexity |
| Long-life enterprise app, 5+ years | COCOMO II | Handles evolution, re-use, language change |
| Early stage, no design yet | Function Points | Technology-independent |
| Modern Agile sprint | Story Points + PERT for sprint risk | Lightweight |
| Distributed offshore team | COCOMO II with EAF | EAF handles communication overhead |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU:** If a question says *"estimate effort and time"*, the expected default answer is **Basic COCOMO with the three-class table (Organic/Semi-Detached/Embedded)**. If a question says *"use Intermediate COCOMO"*, you must mention **EAF and the 15 cost drivers**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Software Project Planning Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Project Inception]) --> req[Requirement Analysis from SRS]
    req --> scope[Define Scope and Objectives]
    scope --> wbs[Build Work Breakdown Structure WBS]
    wbs --> size[Size Estimation: LOC or FP]
    size --> effort[Effort Estimation: COCOMO or Delphi]
    effort --> sched[Schedule Estimation: PERT or CPM]
    sched --> cost[Cost and Budget Estimation]
    cost --> staff[Staffing and Team Structure]
    staff --> risk[Risk Identification and RMMM Plan]
    risk --> qual[Quality Planning: Metrics and Reviews]
    qual --> spmp[Compile Software Project Management Plan SPMP]
    spmp --> baseline[Establish Baselines for Control]
    baseline --> monitor([Execution, Monitoring and Control])
    monitor --> feedback[Feedback Loop to Plan]
    feedback --> spmp
```

> [!NOTE]
> *Block-Level Functional Flow:* the planning process is **cyclic**, not linear — the feedback loop from monitoring to SPMP is what makes the plan a *living document* throughout the SDLC.

---

## 4.2 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) — Hierarchical Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p[Project: ATM Software System] --> p1[Phase 1: Requirements]
    p --> p2[Phase 2: Design]
    p --> p3[Phase 3: Implementation]
    p --> p4[Phase 4: Testing]
    p --> p5[Phase 5: Deployment]

    p1 --> p1a[Task 1.1: Elicit User Needs]
    p1 --> p1b[Task 1.2: Prepare SRS]
    p1 --> p1c[Task 1.3: Review and Approve]

    p2 --> p2a[Task 2.1: HLD Architecture]
    p2 --> p2b[Task 2.2: Database Schema]
    p2 --> p2c[Task 2.3: UI Mockups]

    p3 --> p3a[Task 3.1: Backend Coding]
    p3 --> p3b[Task 3.2: Frontend Coding]
    p3 --> p3c[Task 3.3: Integration]

    p4 --> p4a[Task 4.1: Unit Testing]
    p4 --> p4b[Task 4.2: System Testing]
    p4 --> p4c[Task 4.3: UAT]

    p5 --> p5a[Task 5.1: Pilot at One Branch]
    p5 --> p5b[Task 5.2: Full Rollout]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** WBS questions typically give a *project* and ask the student to draw the WBS. The most common mistake is to **stop at 2 levels**. Always decompose to *work packages* (level 3 or 4) for full marks.

---

## 4.3 PERT / CPM Network Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode([Start]) --> actA[Activity A: t=4]
    startNode --> actB[Activity B: t=6]
    actA --> merge1((Node X))
    actB --> merge1
    merge1 --> actC[Activity C: t=5]
    merge1 --> actD[Activity D: t=7]
    actC --> merge2((Node Y))
    actD --> merge2
    merge2 --> actE[Activity E: t=4]
    actE --> endNode([End])

    style merge1 fill:#fff,stroke:#000
    style merge2 fill:#fff,stroke:#000
    style actD fill:#ffe,stroke:#900
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the schematic above, the **critical path is Start → B → D → E → End** (the highlighted path) because its total duration ($6 + 7 + 4 = 17$ days) is the *longest* of all paths. The other path (A → C → E) gives $4 + 5 + 4 = 13$ days, hence the *slack/float* on that path is $17 - 13 = 4$ days.

---

## 4.4 COCOMO II Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in[Inputs: Size in KLOC or FP] --> scale[Scale Factors: 5 drivers B]
    in --> em[Effort Multipliers: 17 cost drivers]
    in --> phase[Phase Distribution across Life Cycle]
    scale --> form[Formula: E = 2.94 x Size^B x EM product]
    em --> form
    phase --> form
    form --> effortOut[Effort in PM]
    form --> schedOut[Schedule in Months]
    form --> costOut[Cost Estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **COcomposed Cost Output:** COCOMO II produces *effort*, *schedule*, and *cost* simultaneously from a single formula evaluation — making it a unified estimation engine rather than three separate calculations.
