ES_H = EF_G = 23
$$

$$
EF_H = ES_H + D_H = 23 + 2 = 25
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Duration** = $EF_H = \mathbf{25 \; days}$.

---

### 3.1.4 Part (b continued) — Backward Pass (Computing LS and LF)

The backward pass rule is:

$$
LS_i = LF_i - D_i
$$

and

$$
LF_i = \min_{\text{all successors } j \text{ of } i} \big(LS_j\big)
$$

We start with $LF_H = EF_H = 25$ (project must end on day 25).

For activity **H**:

$$
LF_H = 25
$$

$$
LS_H = LF_H - D_H = 25 - 2 = 23
$$

For activity **G** (successor = H):

$$
LF_G = LS_H = 23
$$

$$
LS_G = LF_G - D_G = 23 - 3 = 20
$$

For activity **F** (successor = G):

$$
LF_F = LS_G = 20
$$

$$
LS_F = LF_F - D_F = 20 - 4 = 16
$$

For activity **E** (successor = F):

$$
LF_E = LS_F = 16
$$

$$
LS_E = LF_E - D_E = 16 - 7 = 9
$$

For activity **D** (successor = F):

$$
LF_D = LS_F = 16
$$

$$
LS_D = LF_D - D_D = 16 - 6 = 10
$$

For activity **C** (successor = E):

$$
LF_C = LS_E = 9
$$

$$
LS_C = LF_C - D_C = 9 - 3 = 6
$$

For activity **B** (successors = D, E):

$$
LF_B = \min(LS_D,\; LS_E) = \min(10,\; 9) = 9
$$

$$
LS_B = LF_B - D_B = 9 - 5 = 4
$$

For activity **A** (successors = B, C):

$$
LF_A = \min(LS_B,\; LS_C) = \min(4,\; 6) = 4
$$

$$
LS_A = LF_A - D_A = 4 - 4 = 0
$$

---

### 3.1.5 Part (b continued) — Slack Table and Critical Path

The slack (total float) for every activity is:

$$
\text{Slack}_i = LS_i - ES_i
$$

| Activity | Duration $D_i$ | $ES_i$ | $EF_i$ | $LS_i$ | $LF_i$ | Slack $LS_i-ES_i$ | Critical? |
|:--------:|:--------------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-----------------:|:---------:|
| **A** | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | **Yes ★** |
| **B** | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | 0 | **Yes ★** |
| **C** | 3 | 4 | 7 | 6 | 9 | 2 | No |
| **D** | 6 | 9 | 15 | 10 | 16 | 1 | No |
| **E** | 7 | 9 | 16 | 9 | 16 | 0 | **Yes ★** |
| **F** | 4 | 16 | 20 | 16 | 20 | 0 | **Yes ★** |
| **G** | 3 | 20 | 23 | 20 | 23 | 0 | **Yes ★** |
| **H** | 2 | 23 | 25 | 23 | 25 | 0 | **Yes ★** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path** = path joining all activities with Slack = 0.
>
> $$\text{Critical Path} = \mathbf{A \;\rightarrow\; B \;\rightarrow\; E \;\rightarrow\; F \;\rightarrow\; G \;\rightarrow\; H}$$
>
> **Project Duration** = $\mathbf{25 \; days}$ (sum of durations of critical activities: $4+5+7+4+3+2 = 25$ ✓).

---

### 3.1.6 Part (c) — Gantt Chart Construction

The Gantt chart plots each activity as a bar from $ES_i$ to $EF_i$ on a horizontal time axis (Day 0 to Day 25). The critical activities are shown as **solid dark bars**; the non-critical activities are **hatched/light bars**. A milestone diamond (◆) marks the project end at Day 25.

```
                          Day: 0    5    10   15   20   25
                                |    |    |    |    |    |
  Activity A  [Critical]  ████                         (Day 0–4)
  Activity B  [Critical]       █████                   (Day 4–9)
  Activity C  [Non-Crit]       ▓▓▓                     (Day 4–7, slack 2)
  Activity D  [Non-Crit]            ▓▓▓▓▓▓             (Day 9–15, slack 1)
  Activity E  [Critical]       ███████                 (Day 9–16)
  Activity F  [Critical]                ████           (Day 16–20)
  Activity G  [Critical]                    ███        (Day 20–23)
  Activity H  [Critical]                        ██▄ ◆  (Day 23–25, end milestone)
  ------------------------------------------------------------------->
                                                          Project End ◆
```

**Reading the chart:**
- The **top horizontal axis** is the time (in days).
- The **leftmost column** is the activity list.
- The **dark solid bars** are critical activities.
- The **hatched/light bars** are non-critical (slack > 0).
- The **diamond (◆)** is the project-end milestone.

---

### 3.1.7 Part (d) — Impact of a 2-day Delay in Activity E

Originally $D_E = 7$ days, $ES_E = 9$, $EF_E = 16$.

With a 2-day delay: $D_E^{\text{new}} = 7 + 2 = 9$ days.

New $EF_E = ES_E + D_E^{\text{new}} = 9 + 9 = 18$ days.

Re-running the forward pass (only activities downstream of E are affected):

| Activity | Old $EF$ | New $EF$ |
|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| E | 16 | **18** |
| F (predecessor E gives max(15, 18) = 18) | 20 | **22** |
| G (predecessor F) | 23 | **25** |
| H (predecessor G) | 25 | **27** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **New Project Completion Date = Day 27** (instead of Day 25).
> **Project delay = 2 days.**
> Since E lies on the critical path, *any* delay to a critical activity **directly delays the project by the same amount** — this is a *very* important KTU concept.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Scheduling using Gantt Chart — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Gantt-Chart Scheduling Pipeline

The figure below shows how a Gantt Chart is *constructed from upstream engineering artefacts* and *consumed by downstream tracking activities*. Each block is a real KTU syllabus item; arrows indicate data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Project Charter and Scope Statement]) --> wbsNode[WBS Work Breakdown Structure]
    wbsNode --> actList[Activity List with Estimated Durations]
    actList --> seqNode[Activity Sequencing AOA or AON Network]
    seqNode --> cpmNode[CPM Forward and Backward Pass Engine]
    cpmNode --> esEfBox[ES EF LS LF and Slack Computation]
    esEfBox --> critPathBox[Critical Path Identification]
    critPathBox --> ganttNode[Gantt Chart Generator UI]
    ganttNode --> milestoneBox[Milestone Overlay and Dependency Arrows]
    ganttNode --> resourceBox[Resource Load Allocation Overlay]
    ganttNode --> progressBox[Progress Tracking Shaded Sub Bars]
    ganttNode --> reportBox[Weekly Status Report and SPI CPI]
    progressBox --> eacBox[EAC Estimate at Completion Updates]
    reportBox --> eacBox
    eacBox --> feedbackA[Feedback to Project Manager]
    feedbackA --> ganttNode
    milestoneBox --> endH([Schedule Baseline Frozen])
    resourceBox --> endH
    reportBox --> endH
```

**Block-by-Block Reading (KTU-friendly):**

1. **Project Charter & Scope Statement** — the *seed* document containing objectives, deliverables, and boundaries.
2. **WBS** — decomposes deliverables into *work packages* (the rows of the Gantt chart).
3. **Activity List** — adds the *time* dimension (durations from COCOMO, FP, Delphi, or expert judgement).
4. **Sequencing (AOA/AON)** — adds the *logic* dimension (which activity precedes which).
5. **CPM Engine** — runs the *forward pass* and *backward pass* algorithmically to compute ES/EF/LS/LF/Slack.
6. **Critical Path Identification** — selects activities with Slack = 0.
7. **Gantt Chart Generator** — paints the bars onto the calendar timeline.
8. **Milestone Overlay** — places zero-duration diamond markers (◆) on key deliverables.
9. **Resource Allocation Overlay** — places each bar on the corresponding human/team calendar.
10. **Progress Tracking (Shaded Sub-bar)** — overlays an "actual-done" shaded region on top of the planned bar.
11. **Weekly Status Report** — calculates **SPI** and **CPI** from Earned Value.
12. **EAC (Estimate at Completion)** — revises the forecasted total project cost & schedule.
13. **Feedback Loop** — the updated Gantt chart becomes input to the *next* iteration (re-planning if variance > threshold).

## 4.2 Gantt-Chart vs Network-Diagram vs Milestone-Chart Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph comparisonBlock[Three Project Scheduling Visual Tools]
        ganttTool[Gantt Chart Bar Style]:::ganttStyle
        networkTool[Network Diagram AOA or AON]:::netStyle
        milestoneTool[Milestone Chart Diamond Markers]:::mileStyle
    end
    ganttTool -- Shows WHEN --> whenAxis[Time and Duration]
    ganttTool -- Shows PROGRESS --> progAxis[Actual vs Planned]
    networkTool -- Shows LOGIC --> logicAxis[Dependencies and Critical Path]
    networkTool -- Shows SLACK --> slackAxis[Float and Risk]
    milestoneTool -- Shows EVENTS --> eventAxis[Key Deliverables and Reviews]
    whenAxis --> sharedOut[Project Schedule]
    progAxis --> sharedOut
    logicAxis --> sharedOut
    slackAxis --> sharedOut
    eventAxis --> sharedOut
    classDef ganttStyle fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef netStyle fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#003060,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mileStyle fill:#C0F0C0,stroke:#006020,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Gantt Chart** is best for *when* and *progress* questions.
- The **Network Diagram (AOA/AON)** is best for *logic* and *critical-path* questions.
- The **Milestone Chart** is best for *event* / *deliverable* questions.
- All three contribute to the **single project schedule** at the centre.

## 4.3 The Dependency-Relationship Subgraph (FS / SS / FF / SF)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fsBlock[FS Finish to Start]:::fsStyle --> codeEx[Code must finish before Test starts]
    ssBlock[SS Start to Start]:::ssStyle --> crEx[Code and Code Review can start together review lags by 1 day]
    ffBlock[FF Finish to Finish]:::ffStyle --> utEx[Unit Test and Integration Test must finish together]
    sfBlock[SF Start to Finish]:::sfStyle --> msEx[Rare example night shift maintenance starts when day shift finishes]
    classDef fsStyle fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#803000,color:#000000
    classDef ssStyle fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#003060,color:#000000
    classDef ffStyle fill:#C0FFC0,stroke:#006020,color:#000000
    classDef sfStyle fill:#FFC0C0,stroke:#600000,color:#000000
```

This subgraph visually anchors the four dependency types to their canonical software-engineering examples — a frequent KTU short-answer target.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Always state the dependency type assumed** (FS is the default; explicitly mention "Finish-to-Start is assumed" to gain easy 1 mark).
> 2. **In forward pass, do not forget the MAX over multiple predecessors.** A common mark-loss is to write only one predecessor's EF and ignore the others.
> 3. **Slack = 0 is the ONLY definition of a critical activity.** Writing "minimum duration" or "most important" is wrong and costs 1 mark.
> 4. **In the Gantt chart, mark critical activities distinctly** (solid dark fill) — examiners reward this with 1 mark.
> 5. **Project Duration = EF of the terminal activity**, *not* the sum of all durations.
> 6. **If a non-critical activity slips, the project may not slip** — calculate first; do not assume. Conversely, **any slip in a critical activity slips the project by the same amount**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a Gantt chart. List any four components of a Gantt chart used in software project scheduling.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Board-Key Pattern):**

A Gantt chart is a **horizontal bar-chart** type of project-scheduling tool that represents the **start and finish dates** of the various **terminal elements (work packages)** of a project against a **calibrated time axis** *(1 mark)*. It was developed by **Henry L. Gantt** in **1917** *(½ mark)*.

The four components are: *(2 marks — ½ mark each)*

1. **Activity / Task Bar** — a horizontal bar representing a unit of work, whose length equals its duration.
2. **Time Axis (Calendar)** — the horizontal scale (days, weeks, sprints) against which bars are drawn.
3. **Milestone** — a zero-duration event symbol (◆) marking a key deliverable.
4. **Dependency Arrow** — a connector showing the logical relationship (FS/SS/FF/SF) between activities.

*(Other acceptable components: Progress Indicator, Resource Tag, Summary Bar.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Finish-to-Start (FS) and Start-to-Start (SS) dependencies with a software-engineering example.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Finish-to-Start (FS) | Start-to-Start (SS) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Rule** | Successor can start *only after* the predecessor *finishes*. | Successor can start *as soon as* the predecessor *starts* (optionally with a lag). |
| **Equation** | $ES_j = EF_i$ | $ES_j = ES_i + \text{lag}$ |
| **Software Example** | "Coding" must finish before "Testing" can start. | "Coding" and "Code Review" can start together, with review lagging by 1 day. |
| **Default?** | Yes — assumed if no relation is mentioned. | No — must be specified. |

*(2 marks for the table; 1 mark for the examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> A software project consists of the following 7 activities. Draw the **AON network**, find the **critical path and project duration** using CPM, and **draw the Gantt chart**. State assumptions clearly. *(14 marks)*

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessor(s) |
|:--------:|:---------------:|:--------------:|
| A | 6 | — |
| B | 4 | A |
| C | 3 | A |
| D | 5 | B |
| E | 7 | B, C |
| F | 4 | D, E |
| G | 6 | F |

#### (a) AON Network & Forward-Backward Pass (7 marks)

**AON Network (textual):** `A → B`, `A → C`, `B → D`, `B → E`, `C → E`, `D → F`, `E → F`, `F → G`.

**Forward pass — ES and EF:**

For activity **A** (no predecessor):

$$
ES_A = 0
$$

$$
EF_A = 0 + 6 = 6
$$

For activity **B** (predecessor = A):

$$
ES_B = EF_A = 6
$$

$$
EF_B = 6 + 4 = 10
$$

For activity **C** (predecessor = A):

$$
ES_C = EF_A = 6
$$

$$
EF_C = 6 + 3 = 9
$$

For activity **D** (predecessor = B):

$$
ES_D = EF_B = 10
$$

$$
EF_D = 10 + 5 = 15
$$

For activity **E** (predecessors = B, C):

$$
ES_E = \max(EF_B,\; EF_C) = \max(10,\; 9) = 10
$$

$$
EF_E = 10 + 7 = 17
$$

For activity **F** (predecessors = D, E):

$$
ES_F = \max(EF_D,\; EF_E) = \max(15,\; 17) = 17
$$

$$
EF_F = 17 + 4 = 21
$$

For activity **G** (predecessor = F):

$$
ES_G = EF_F = 21
$$

$$
EF_G = 21 + 6 = 27
$$

**Backward pass — LS and LF** (start with $LF_G = 27$):

$$
LF_G = 27,\quad LS_G = 27 - 6 = 21
$$

$$
LF_F = LS_G = 21,\quad LS_F = 21 - 4 = 17
$$

$$
LF_E = LS_F = 17,\quad LS_E = 17 - 7 = 10
$$

$$
LF_D = LS_F = 17,\quad LS_D = 17 - 5 = 12
$$

$$
LF_C = LS_E = 10,\quad LS_C = 10 - 3 = 7
$$

$$
LF_B = \min(LS_D,\; LS_E) = \min(12,\; 10) = 10
$$

$$
LS_B = 10 - 4 = 6
$$

$$
LF_A = \min(LS_B,\; LS_C) = \min(6,\; 7) = 6
$$

$$
LS_A = 6 - 6 = 0
$$

**Slack table:**

| Activity | $D_i$ | $ES_i$ | $EF_i$ | $LS_i$ | $LF_i$ | Slack | Critical? |
|:--------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-----:|:---------:|
| A | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | **Yes** |
| B | 4 | 6 | 10 | 6 | 10 | 0 | **Yes** |
| C | 3 | 6 | 9 | 7 | 10 | 1 | No |
| D | 5 | 10 | 15 | 12 | 17 | 2 | No |
| E | 7 | 10 | 17 | 10 | 17 | 0 | **Yes** |
| F | 4 | 17 | 21 | 17 | 21 | 0 | **Yes** |
| G | 6 | 21 | 27 | 21 | 27 | 0 | **Yes** |

#### (b) Gantt Chart (7 marks)

```
                              Day: 0    5    10   15   20   25   30
                                    |    |    |    |    |    |    |
  Activity A  [Critical]   ██████                           (Day 0–6)
  Activity B  [Critical]         ████                       (Day 6–10)
  Activity C  [Non-Crit]         ▓▓▓                        (Day 6–9, slack 1)
  Activity D  [Non-Crit]              ▓▓▓▓▓                 (Day 10–15, slack 2)
  Activity E  [Critical]         ███████                    (Day 10–17)
  Activity F  [Critical]                  ████              (Day 17–21)
  Activity G  [Critical]                      ██████ ◆      (Day 21–27, end milestone)
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------->
                                                              Project End
```

> **Critical Path:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G$
> **Project Duration:** $\mathbf{27 \; days}$ (verified: $6+4+7+4+6 = 27$ ✓).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

> For a software project, the following activities with their durations and dependencies are given.
>
> **(a)** Construct the AON network and compute the **critical path and minimum project duration** using CPM. Show the forward and backward pass in a tabular form. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Draw the Gantt chart. If activity **D** is delayed by **3 days**, determine the **new project completion time**. *(7 marks)*

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessor(s) |
|:--------:|:---------------:|:--------------:|
| A | 5 | — |
| B | 6 | A |
| C | 4 | A |
| D | 3 | B |
| E | 8 | B, C |
| F | 5 | D, E |
| G | 4 | F |

#### (a) CPM Tabulation (7 marks)

**Forward pass:**

$$
ES_A = 0,\quad EF_A = 5
$$

$$
ES_B = 5,\quad EF_B = 11
$$

$$
ES_C = 5,\quad EF_C = 9
$$

$$
ES_D = EF_B = 11,\quad EF_D = 14
$$

$$
ES_E = \max(EF_B,\; EF_C) = \max(11,\; 9) = 11,\quad EF_E = 19
$$

$$
ES_F = \max(EF_D,\; EF_E) = \max(14,\; 19) = 19,\quad EF_F = 24
$$

$$
ES_G = EF_F = 24,\quad EF_G = 28
$$

**Backward pass** (start $LF_G = 28$):

$$
LS_G = 28 - 4 = 24
$$

$$
LF_F = LS_G = 24,\quad LS_F = 24 - 5 = 19
$$

$$
LF_E = LS_F = 19,\quad LS_E = 19 - 8 = 11
$$

$$
LF_D = LS_F = 19,\quad LS_D = 19 - 3 = 16
$$

$$
LF_C = LS_E = 11,\quad LS_C = 11 - 4 = 7
$$

$$
LF_B = \min(LS_D,\; LS_E) = \min(16,\; 11) = 11,\quad LS_B = 11 - 6 = 5
$$

$$
LF_A = \min(LS_B,\; LS_C) = \min(5,\; 7) = 5,\quad LS_A = 5 - 5 = 0
$$

**Slack summary table:**

| Activity | $D_i$ | $ES_i$ | $EF_i$ | $LS_i$ | $LF_i$ | Slack | Critical? |
|:--------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:-----:|:---------:|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | **Yes** |
| B | 6 | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | 0 | **Yes** |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 11 | 2 | No |
| D | 3 | 11 | 14 | 16 | 19 | 5 | No |
| E | 8 | 11 | 19 | 11 | 19 | 0 | **Yes** |
| F | 5 | 19 | 24 | 19 | 24 | 0 | **Yes** |
| G | 4 | 24 | 28 | 24 | 28 | 0 | **Yes** |

**Critical Path:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G$
**Project Duration:** $\mathbf{28 \; days}$ (verified: $5+6+8+5+4 = 28$ ✓)

#### (b) Gantt Chart & 3-Day Delay Analysis (7 marks)

**Gantt Chart:**

```
                              Day: 0    5    10   15   20   25   30
                                    |    |    |    |    |    |    |
  Activity A  [Critical]   █████                                (Day 0–5)
  Activity B  [Critical]         ██████                         (Day 5–11)
  Activity C  [Non-Crit]         ▓▓▓▓                          (Day 5–9, slack 2)
  Activity D  [Non-Crit]              ▓▓▓                       (Day 11–14, slack 5)
  Activity E  [Critical]         ████████                      (Day 11–19)
  Activity F  [Critical]                  █████                (Day 19–24)
  Activity G  [Critical]                      ████ ◆           (Day 24–28, end milestone)
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------->
                                                            Project End
```

**Effect of 3-day delay in activity D:**

Activity D has a slack of **5 days** (it is *not* on the critical path). A delay of 3 days is **less than the available slack of 5 days**, so the project duration is **unchanged**.

$$
\text{New } EF_D = 14 + 3 = 17
$$

Since $EF_D = 17 < ES_E = 11$ is false, but the relevant comparison is:

$$
\text{New } EF_D = 17 \;\leq\; LF_D = 19
$$

The condition holds, so activity D's finish absorbs the delay within its float, and the project still finishes on **Day 28**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU teaching point:** This is the *only* scenario where the project *does not* slip despite an activity delay — a 3-mark conceptual question that is regularly asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mile Revision List — Glance Through Twice Before the Exam.**

- [x] A **Gantt chart** is a **horizontal bar chart** invented by **Henry L. Gantt** in **1917**, used for **project scheduling**.
- [x] Gantt chart is **time-oriented**, **activity-oriented**, and **graphical** — it does **not** by itself show the critical path (need CPM for that).
- [x] **7 components** to memorise: Activity bar, Time axis, Milestone, Dependency arrow, Progress indicator, Resource tag, Summary bar.
- [x] **4 dependency types**: FS (most common), SS, FF, SF (rare).
- [x] **Critical activity** = activity with **Slack = 0** (and *only* this definition).
- [x] Forward-pass formulas: $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$; $ES_j = \max(EF_i)$ over predecessors.
- [x] Backward-pass formulas: $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$; $LF_i = \min(LS_j)$ over successors.
- [x] **Project Duration** = $EF$ of the **terminal activity** in the AON network.
- [x] **Any delay in a critical activity → project delay of the same amount** (no absorption by float).
- [x] **Delay in a non-critical activity ≤ its slack → no project slip**; **delay > slack → project slips by (delay − slack)** days.
- [x] **Gantt chart vs Network diagram:** Gantt = *when*, *progress*; Network = *logic*, *critical path*, *slack*.
- [x] **5 advantages** + **2 limitations** must be memorised in standard form.
- [x] **Types of Gantt charts:** Bar, Linked, Load, Progress, Master/Summary.
- [x] **Progress overlay** in a Gantt chart uses **Earned Value (EV)** techniques → calculates **SPI** and **CPI**.
- [x] **Mermaid / ASCII Gantt chart** in exam: draw critical activities as **solid dark bars**, non-critical as **hatched/light bars**, and mark the project-end with a **diamond ◆**.
- [x] Always **state the dependency assumption** ("Finish-to-Start assumed unless otherwise stated") at the start of any CPM/Gantt solution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Configuration Management and its Phases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Configuration Management and its Phases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Configuration Management (SCM)** is the discipline of tracking and controlling changes in the software. It is a software engineering process that encompasses the techniques, tools, and policies used to manage the evolution of a software system's configuration, ensuring integrity, traceability, and consistency throughout the software development lifecycle (SDLC) and across all its versions and releases.

According to the **IEEE Standard 828-2012 (Software Configuration Management Plans)**, SCM is the process of identifying and defining the configuration items in a system, controlling the release and change of these items throughout the system lifecycle, recording and reporting the status of configuration items and change requests, and verifying the completeness and correctness of the items.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing a **college project report** with your team of 4 members. You have:
- The initial draft (`v1.0`)
- After your mentor's corrections (`v1.1`)
- After formatting fixes (`v1.2`)
- The final submission (`v2.0_FINAL`)

Now, imagine 10 different people editing the document simultaneously, with different versions saved on different laptops, and someone accidentally deletes a critical chapter. **Disaster, right?**

> [!NOTE]
> **SCM is the "smart librarian" of your software.** It tracks every change, every version, who made it, when, and why. It ensures that everyone is working on the right version, prevents unauthorized changes, and lets you roll back to any previous stable state if something breaks. Without SCM, large software projects would collapse into chaos — this is why nearly **100% of professional software projects** (as of 2024) use some form of SCM (e.g., Git, GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Subversion).

### 1.3 Why SCM is Critical in KTU Context

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech curriculum**, SCM is classified under the **Engineering/Managerial track** of software engineering. Examiners consistently test:
- Definitions of configuration items (CI) and baselines
- The **6 phases of SCM** (Planning, Identification, Control, Status Accounting, Auditing, Release Management)
- Difference between **version control** and **change control**
- Tools used in SCM

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Anchor:** Remember the acronym **"PIC-SAR"** for SCM phases: **P**lanning, **I**dentification, **C**ontrol, **S**tatus Accounting, **A**uditing, **R**elease Management. This is a favorite KTU exam question.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Configuration Item (CI)** | A single artifact or document that is placed under SCM control | Source code files, design docs, test cases |
| **Baseline** | A formally approved snapshot of CIs at a specific point in time | `v1.0_Approved_For_Testing` |
| **Version** | A specific revision of a CI over time | `app.py v1.0`, `app.py v1.1` |
| **Repository** | Central storage location for all CIs | GitHub repo, SVN server |
| **Change Request (CR)** | A formal proposal to modify a baseline | Bug report #412 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of SCM

#### 2.1.1 The Core Need: Managing Change

Software systems evolve. Every change — whether a bug fix, new feature, or platform upgrade — introduces **risk**:
- Who changed this file?
- Why was it changed?
- Did the change break other features?
- Can we roll back if it does?

SCM answers all these questions systematically.

#### 2.1.2 The Four Pillars of SCM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **four functional pillars** of SCM, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:
> 1. **Configuration Identification** — What to control?
> 2. **Configuration Control** — How to manage changes?
> 3. **Configuration Status Accounting** — What is the current state?
> 4. **Configuration Auditing** — Is the product correct and complete?

### 2.2 The 6 Phases of SCM — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Module 4 places significant weightage on enumerating and explaining the phases of SCM. The canonical 6-phase model is the most frequently asked topic.

#### **Phase 1: Configuration Planning (P)**

- **Goal:** Establish the SCM policy, procedures, tools, and schedule for the project.
- **Output:** A formal **Software Configuration Management Plan (SCMP)** document.
- **Activities:**
  - Define SCM tools (Git, SVN, ClearCase, Mercurial)
  - Define roles (Configuration Manager, Change Control Board)
  - Schedule baselines and reviews
  - Define branching and merging strategy

> **Real-world utility:** In industry, the SCMP is part of the project's quality plan, audited at CMMI Level 2+ assessments.

#### **Phase 2: Configuration Identification (I)**

- **Goal:** Identify the CIs that need to be placed under SCM.
- **Output:** A **Configuration Item list** with unique identifiers.
- **Activities:**
  - Select CIs: source code, design models, test data, requirements specs, build scripts
  - Assign unique IDs (e.g., `MOD-CALC-001`)
  - Define baselines
  - Establish traceability links between CIs

**Types of CIs:**
- **Software CIs:** Source code, executable files, libraries
- **Document CIs:** SRS, SDD, STD (Test Documents)
- **Data CIs:** Test datasets, configuration files, environment variables

#### **Phase 3: Configuration Control (C)**

- **Goal:** Control changes to baselines after they are approved.
- **Output:** Approved change requests, updated baselines.
- **Activities:**
  - Process Change Requests (CRs) via a **Change Control Board (CCB)**
  - Evaluate impact, cost, and risk
  - Approve, defer, or reject changes
  - Implement approved changes

> **The Change Control Workflow:**
> ```
> Submit CR → Review by CCB → Impact Analysis → Approve/Reject → Implement → Re-baseline
> ```

#### **Phase 4: Configuration Status Accounting (S)**

- **Goal:** Record and report the status of CIs and changes.
- **Output:** Status reports, audit logs, version history.
- **Activities:**
  - Track the history of each CI
  - Report pending CRs, approved changes
  - Maintain a **Configuration Status Log**

> **Example:** Git's `git log` command is a direct implementation of status accounting.

#### **Phase 5: Configuration Auditing (A)**

- **Goal:** Verify that the software product matches its specifications and that the SCM process is being followed.
- **Two Types:**
  - **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA):** Does the software perform as per SRS?
  - **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA):** Does the build match the approved baseline?

#### **Phase 6: Release Management (R)**

- **Goal:** Package, distribute, and track software releases.
- **Activities:**
  - Build management (continuous integration)
  - Version tagging (`v1.0.0`, `v1.1.0`)
  - Distribution to customers/staging servers
  - Post-release monitoring

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Element** | **Description** | **Standard** |
|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **IEEE 828** | Standard for SCM Plans | Configuration plan structure |
| **IEEE 1042** | Standard for SCM | Guiding principles |
| **Baseline Formula** | $B_i = \{CI_1, CI_2, \ldots, CI_n\}_{\text{approved at } t_i}$ | Snapshot at time $t_i$ |
| **Version Increment** | $V_{n+1} = V_n + \Delta C$ | New version after change $\Delta C$ |
| **Change Request (CR) Lifecycle** | $\text{Submit} \rightarrow \text{Review} \rightarrow \text{Approve} \rightarrow \text{Implement} \rightarrow \text{Verify}$ | State machine |
| **Branching Model** | $\text{main} \rightarrow \text{develop} \rightarrow \text{feature/branch}$ | Git Flow |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** When writing mathematical relations involving sets in your answer sheet, avoid the vertical pipe `|`. Instead use $\vert$ or $\mid$. Example: Use $C \mid I \in B$, not $C \vert I \in B$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SCM is the **backbone** of:
- **DevOps pipelines** (CI/CD)
- **Open source collaboration** (Linux Kernel uses Git)
- **Aerospace & Defense** (DO-178C compliance requires rigorous SCM)
- **Medical Device Software** (FDA mandates SCM under IEC 62304)
- **Banking & Finance** (SOX compliance requires auditable change trails)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Flow Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The SCM Phases — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Process

> [!NOTE]
> Since SCM is a **process-oriented** topic (not a purely mathematical one), the "derivation" here is a detailed state-by-state process walk-through that an examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

#### Step 1: Configuration Planning

The process begins by answering: **"What is the SCM strategy for this project?"**

$$\text{SCMP} = \{P_{\text{policy}}, T_{\text{tools}}, R_{\text{roles}}, S_{\text{schedule}}\}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{policy}}$ = Configuration management policy document
- $T_{\text{tools}}$ = Selected tools (e.g., Git + Jenkins + JIRA)
- $R_{\text{roles}}$ = Defined roles (CM, CCB, Dev Lead)
- $S_{\text{schedule}}$ = Timeline of baselines and reviews

#### Step 2: Configuration Identification

Identify CIs and assign metadata. Each CI is defined as:

$$CI_i = \{ID_i, \text{Name}_i, \text{Type}_i, \text{Owner}_i, \text{Version}_i\}$$

**Example derivation for a Student Management System:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI_1 &= \{\text{SRS-001, SRS, Document, BA, v1.0}\} \\
CI_2 &= \{\text{SRC-001, main.py, Source, Dev1, v1.0}\} \\
CI_3 &= \{\text{TST-001, test_main.py, Test, QA, v1.0}\} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Baseline Establishment

A baseline is the **official starting point** for further development.

$$B_0 = \{CI_1(v1.0), CI_2(v1.0), CI_3(v1.0)\} \quad \text{(Baseline at project start)}$$

**Common Baselines in Software Engineering:**
- **Requirements Baseline:** Approved SRS
- **Design Baseline:** Approved SDD
- **Implementation Baseline:** Approved source code at milestone
- **Release Baseline:** Approved executable for customer delivery

#### Step 4: Change Control Flow

A change request (CR) flows through a **state machine**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_0 &= \text{Submitted} \\
\text{State}_1 &= \text{Under Review (CCB)} \\
\text{State}_2 &= \text{Approved} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Rejected} \\
\text{State}_3 &= \text{Implemented} \\
\text{State}_4 &= \text{Verified} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Rollback to } B_i \\
\text{State}_5 &= \text{Closed (New Baseline } B_{i+1} \text{ created)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mathematical Representation of a New Baseline:**

$$B_{i+1} = B_i \cup \Delta C_{\text{approved}} \setminus C_{\text{deprecated}}$$

Where:
- $B_{i+1}$ = New baseline
- $B_i$ = Previous baseline
- $\Delta C_{\text{approved}}$ = Set of approved changes
- $C_{\text{deprecated}}$ = Items removed or replaced

#### Step 5: Status Accounting

A status report at time $t$ is a snapshot:

$$SA(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Status}(CI_i, t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \{\text{Version}_i, \text{Location}_i, \text{ChangeCount}_i\}$$

For example, in Git, this is implemented by:

```bash
git log --all --oneline
```

#### Step 6: Configuration Auditing

The audit verifies two things:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCA} &: \text{Software} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{SRS Specification} \\
\text{PCA} &: \text{Build Artifacts} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Approved Baseline } B_i
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Release Management

A release is tagged and distributed:

$$\text{Release}_j = B_{\text{release}} + \text{Release Notes}_j + \text{Build}_j + \text{Distribution Channel}_j$$

Version numbering follows **Semantic Versioning (SemVer)**:

$$V = \text{MAJOR}.\text{MINOR}.\text{PATCH}$$

- **MAJOR** changes: Incompatible API changes
- **MINOR** changes: Backward-compatible new features
- **PATCH** changes: Backward-compatible bug fixes

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SCM Phases vs. Activities vs. Outputs

| **Phase** | **Key Question** | **Activities** | **Outputs / Deliverables** |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|----------------------------|
| Planning | What is the SCM strategy? | Define tools, roles, schedule | SCMP document |
| Identification | What do we control? | Select CIs, assign IDs, define baselines | CI list, baseline definitions |
| Control | How do we manage changes? | CR processing, CCB reviews, change implementation | Approved CRs, updated baselines |
| Status Accounting | What is the state? | Track versions, log changes, generate reports | Status reports, audit logs |
| Auditing | Are we correct? | FCA, PCA, compliance checks | Audit reports, non-conformance lists |
| Release Management | How do we deliver? | Build, package, distribute, monitor | Release packages, version tags |

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis: Version Control vs. Change Control

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU question asks: *"Differentiate between version control and change control."* This is a guaranteed 5-7 mark question.

| **Aspect** | **Version Control** | **Change Control** |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Purpose** | Track and manage multiple versions of a CI | Manage the process of requesting, evaluating, and approving changes |
| **Scope** | Single CI (e.g., one source file) | System-wide impact |
| **Trigger** | Developer commits a code change | User/customer reports an issue or requests a feature |
| **Tool Examples** | Git, SVN, Mercurial | JIRA, Bugzilla, ServiceNow |
| **Process** | Automatic (commit, branch, merge) | Manual, formal (CR + CCB approval) |
| **Frequency** | Very high (multiple times/day per dev) | Low (weeks or months) |
| **Owner** | Developers | Change Control Board (CCB) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level SCM Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Phase 1: Configuration Planning]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Configuration Identification]
    C --> D[Baseline Established B0]
    D --> E{Change Request Raised?}
    E -->|No| H[Phase 5: Configuration Auditing]
    E -->|Yes| F[Phase 3: Configuration Control]
    F --> G[CCB Review and Approval]
    G -->|Approved| I[Implement Change]
    I --> J[New Baseline B1]
    J --> K[Phase 4: Status Accounting]
    G -->|Rejected| K
    K --> H
    H --> L{Conformance?}
    L -->|No| M[Corrective Actions]
    M --> F
    L -->|Yes| N[Phase 6: Release Management]
    N --> O[Software Release to Customer]
    O --> P[Post Release Monitoring]
```

### 4.2 Change Control Workflow (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Submitted
    Submitted --> UnderReview: CR Logged
    UnderReview --> Approved: CCB Approves
    UnderReview --> Rejected: CCB Rejects
    Approved --> Implemented: Developer Applies Change
    Implemented --> Verified: QA Testing
    Verified --> Closed: New Baseline Created
    Verified --> Reopened: Defect Found
    Reopened --> UnderReview
    Rejected --> [*]
    Closed --> [*]
```

### 4.3 SCM Phases Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANNING[Phase 1 Planning]
        P1[SCMP Document]
        P2[Tools Selection]
        P3[Role Assignment]
    end

    subgraph IDENTIFICATION[Phase 2 Identification]
        I1[CI Selection]
        I2[Unique ID Assignment]
        I3[Baseline Definition]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Phase 3 Control]
        C1[Change Request]
        C2[CCB Review]
        C3[Change Implementation]
    end

    subgraph STATUS[Phase 4 Status Accounting]
        S1[Version History]
        S2[Status Reports]
        S3[Audit Logs]
    end

    subgraph AUDIT[Phase 5 Auditing]
        AU1[Functional Audit]
        AU2[Physical Audit]
    end

    subgraph RELEASE[Phase 6 Release Management]
        R1[Build and Package]
        R2[Version Tagging]
        R3[Distribution]
    end

    PLANNING --> IDENTIFICATION
    IDENTIFICATION --> CONTROL
    CONTROL --> STATUS
    STATUS --> AUDIT
    AUDIT --> RELEASE
    AUDIT -.->|Non Conformance| CONTROL
```

### 4.4 Configuration Item (CI) Tree Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS[Software System] --> REQ[Requirements Document CI]
    SYS --> DES[Design Document CI]
    SYS --> SRC[Source Code CI]
    SYS --> TST[Test Cases CI]
    SYS --> BLD[Build Scripts CI]
    SYS --> DOC[User Manuals CI]

    SRC --> S1[Module A Code]
    SRC --> S2[Module B Code]
    SRC --> S3[Module C Code]

    TST --> T1[Unit Tests]
    TST --> T2[Integration Tests]
    TST --> T3[System Tests]
```

### 4.5 Tools Landscape in SCM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCM Tools] --> B[Version Control]
    A --> C[Build and CI]
    A --> D[Issue Tracking]
    A --> E[Release Management]

    B --> B1[Git]
    B --> B2[Subversion SVN]
    B --> B3[Mercurial]

    C --> C1[Jenkins]
    C --> C2[GitHub Actions]
    C --> C3[GitLab CI]

    D --> D1[JIRA]
    D --> D2[Bugzilla]
    D --> D3[Redmine]

    E --> E1[Docker]
    E --> E2[Kubernetes]
    E --> E3[Ansible]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]**

**Define Software Configuration Management. List any four functions of SCM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Software Configuration Management is the discipline of tracking and controlling changes in the software. It encompasses the techniques, tools, and procedures used to manage the evolution of a software system's configuration, ensuring integrity, traceability, and consistency throughout its lifecycle.

**Four Functions of SCM (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Configuration Identification** — Selecting and identifying the configuration items.
2. **Configuration Control** — Managing changes to baselines via Change Control Boards.
3. **Configuration Status Accounting** — Recording and reporting the status of CIs.
4. **Configuration Auditing** — Verifying conformance to specifications and standards.

*(Alternative 4th function: Release Management)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**What is a Configuration Item (CI)? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A Configuration Item is any artifact (document, source file, library, test case, etc.) that is placed under configuration control. Each CI has a unique identifier, owner, version, and change history.

**Examples (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
1. The Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document.
2. A source code file, e.g., `LoginModule.java` in a banking application.
3. *(Optional third example: Test data, build script, design diagram)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the different phases of Software Configuration Management in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** Differentiate between version control and change control with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A. (a) — 7 Marks**

Software Configuration Management (SCM) consists of **six phases**, as follows:

**1. Configuration Planning (1 Mark):**
This is the first phase where the SCM strategy is defined. It involves creating a Software Configuration Management Plan (SCMP), selecting SCM tools (Git, SVN), assigning roles (Configuration Manager, CCB members), and scheduling baseline reviews. The SCMP becomes part of the project's overall project plan.

**2. Configuration Identification (1 Mark):**
In this phase, the configuration items (CIs) are identified. Examples include source code files, design documents, test cases, and requirement specifications. Each CI is assigned a unique identifier, owner, and version. Baselines are also defined at this stage.

**3. Configuration Control (1.5 Marks):**
This phase manages changes to approved baselines. All change requests (CRs) are submitted, reviewed by a Change Control Board (CCB), evaluated for impact, and either approved, deferred, or rejected. Only approved changes are implemented, and a new baseline is created. This prevents unauthorized or uncontrolled modifications.

**4. Configuration Status Accounting (1 Mark):**
This phase records and reports the status of all CIs and change requests. Status reports include version history, pending CRs, and the latest baseline. In modern tools like Git, this is automated via commit history and pull request logs.

**5. Configuration Auditing (1.5 Marks):**
Auditing verifies the integrity and correctness of the software product. There are two types:
- **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA):** Ensures the software performs as per the SRS.
- **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA):** Ensures the built artifacts match the approved baseline.

**6. Release Management (1 Mark):**
The final phase involves building, packaging, versioning, and distributing the software. Tools like Jenkins, Docker, and Kubernetes are commonly used. Versioning typically follows Semantic Versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A. (b) — 7 Marks**

| **Aspect** | **Version Control** | **Change Control** |
|------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition (1 Mark each)** | Tracks multiple versions/revisions of a single CI over time | Manages the formal process of requesting, evaluating, and approving changes to baselines |
| **Scope (1 Mark)** | Single artifact (e.g., one file or module) | System-wide, affecting multiple CIs |
| **Examples (1 Mark each)** | A developer committing changes to `app.py` multiple times in Git; switching between v1.0 and v1.1 of an SRS document | A customer raising a CR for a new feature, reviewed by CCB, approved, and implemented in v2.0 |
| **Frequency (1 Mark)** | Very high (multiple times per developer per day) | Low (weekly or monthly) |
| **Tools** | Git, SVN, Mercurial, CVS | JIRA, Bugzilla, Change Request forms |
| **Trigger** | Developer-driven, routine | Customer/stakeholder-driven, formal |
| **Automation** | Mostly automated | Mostly manual/formal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loses 2-3 marks):** Students often confuse version control with backup systems. Version control tracks *changes* with author, timestamp, and rationale — not just file copies. Always mention **who, what, when, why** in your version control definition.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Supplementary Exam 2023, Module 4]**

**Q.B. (a)** What is a baseline in SCM? Explain the different types of baselines used in software projects. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** Discuss the role of Configuration Status Accounting and Configuration Auditing in ensuring software quality. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B. (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A baseline is a formally approved and fixed snapshot of one or more configuration items at a specific point in time. Once baselined, the CIs can only be changed through a formal change control process. Mathematically, a baseline is a frozen set of CI versions:

$$B_i = \{CI_1(v_n), CI_2(v_m), \ldots, CI_k(v_p)\} \quad \text{at time } t_i$$

**Types of Baselines (5 Marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Requirements Baseline:** Created when the Software Requirements Specification (SRS) is formally approved. All subsequent development is based on this baseline.

2. **Design Baseline:** Established when the Software Design Description (SDD) is approved. It includes architectural and detailed design artifacts.

3. **Implementation Baseline:** Created after a coding milestone is achieved. It includes all approved source code, libraries, and unit tests.

4. **Test Baseline:** Established when the test plan, test cases, and test data are approved. Used for regression testing in future releases.

5. **Release Baseline (Product Baseline):** The final approved baseline for customer release. Includes executable, release notes, user manuals, and installation scripts.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B. (b) — 7 Marks**

**Configuration Status Accounting (CSA) (3.5 Marks):**

CSA is the SCM phase that **records and reports** the state of every CI and change request. It provides visibility into:
- Current versions of all CIs
- Pending change requests
- Approved changes
- Baseline history

In modern DevOps environments, CSA is automated. For example:
- **Git** tracks commit history, branches, and tags.
- **JIRA** tracks the status of bugs and feature requests.
- **CI/CD dashboards** (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions) show the status of builds and deployments.

**CSA answers the question: "What is the current state of the project?"**

**Configuration Auditing (3.5 Marks):**

Configuration Auditing is the phase that **verifies the integrity and correctness** of the software and the SCM process. There are two types:
- **Functional Configuration Audit (FCA):** Confirms the software performs all functions as specified in the SRS.
- **Physical Configuration Audit (PCA):** Confirms the build artifacts (executables, libraries, docs) exactly match the approved baseline — no extra, no missing files.

Auditing is critical for **regulatory compliance** in domains like medical devices (FDA), aerospace (DO-178C), and finance (SOX).

**CSA answers "What is the state?" — Auditing answers "Is the state correct?"**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loses 2 Marks):** Students often write a generic definition of auditing and forget to distinguish between FCA and PCA. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **Always write both types with one-line examples.**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Module 4 SCM questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CI with a baseline.** A CI is an *item*; a baseline is a *snapshot of items*. (Lose 1-2 marks)
> 2. **Listing only 4 phases instead of 6.** The full 6-phase model (PIC-SAR) is expected. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting FCA vs PCA** in the auditing phase. (Lose 2 marks)
> 4. **Writing vague answers** like "SCM manages code" without specifying *what* it manages and *how*. Examiners want **keywords**: baselines, versions, change control, CCB, traceability. (Lose 1-2 marks)
> 5. **Not giving examples.** A 7-mark question without examples is graded as a 5-mark answer.
> 6. **Mixing up version control and change control.** They are complementary, not the same.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **SCM = tracking + controlling changes** in software (IEEE 828 definition).
- ✅ **Six phases (PIC-SAR):** Planning, Identification, Control, Status Accounting, Auditing, Release Management.
- ✅ **Configuration Item (CI):** Any artifact under SCM control — code, docs, tests, data.
- ✅ **Baseline:** Approved frozen snapshot of CIs. Types: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Test, Release.
- ✅ **Change Control Board (CCB):** Body that reviews and approves change requests.
- ✅ **Version Control:** Tracks revisions of a CI (Git, SVN). **Change Control:** Manages the formal change process (JIRA).
- ✅ **Two types of audits:** FCA (functional conformance) and PCA (physical build matches baseline).
- ✅ **Status Accounting:** Records and reports CI state. **Auditing:** Verifies state correctness.
- ✅ **SemVer:** MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., v2.1.3).
- ✅ **Tools:** Git (VCS), Jenkins (CI), JIRA (Issue Tracking), Docker (Containerization).
- ✅ **Standards:** IEEE 828 (SCMP), IEEE 1042 (SCM principles), CMMI Level 2 (configuration management).
- ✅ **Real-world usage:** Linux kernel (Git), Android (Gerrit), Banking (SOX compliance), Aerospace (DO-178C).

> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition (2 marks)**
> 2. **Phases/Components with headings (8 marks)**
> 3. **Diagram or table (2 marks)**
> 4. **Example or real-world tool (2 marks)**
>
> This ensures you cover all evaluation parameters of a KTU examiner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Quality Management - ISO 9000
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Quality Management - ISO 9000

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Software Quality Management (SQM) — Formal Definition

**Software Quality Management (SQM)** is a coordinated set of activities (planning, assurance, control) that direct and control an organization with regard to software quality. It is formally defined by the **ISO 9000** family as *"the totality of features and characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (aligned with IEEE Std 730 — Standard for Software Quality Assurance), SQM comprises three pillars:

| Pillar | Focus | KTU Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Planning** | Pre-project | Selecting applicable standards & metrics |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Process-oriented | Defect prevention via process maturity |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Product-oriented | Defect detection via reviews & testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **QA = Prevention** (build it right) — managerial, process-focused
> - **QC = Detection** (find defects) — technical, product-focused
>
> KTU examiners frequently allot **3-mark questions** contrasting these two.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a restaurant as a software company:

- **Quality Planning** = Deciding the menu, choosing suppliers, writing recipes *before* service starts.
- **Quality Assurance** = Training chefs to wash hands, follow hygiene rules (HACCP), maintain consistent recipes — the **process** prevents bad food.
- **Quality Control** = The head chef tasting each dish *before* it leaves the kitchen — **detecting** bad dishes before customers receive them.

If the kitchen only does QC (tasting), bad food still gets made. If it only does QA (training), sometimes a chef slips up and no one catches it. **Mature kitchens do both — this is SQM.**

---

## 1.3 ISO 9000 — The Global Quality Standard Family

**ISO 9000** is a series of international standards developed by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** in **Geneva, Switzerland** that establish requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**.

### The ISO 9000 Family (Memorize This Table)

| Standard | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 9000** | Fundamentals & vocabulary | Defines 8 QMS principles |
| **ISO 9001** | Requirements (auditable) | The **only** certifiable standard |
| **ISO 9004** | Guidelines for performance improvement | Beyond 9001 — "how to win" |
| **ISO 19011** | Auditing QMS | How audits are conducted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note for KTU:**
> - **ISO 9001:1987** — first edition
> - **ISO 9001:2000** — process-based approach (currently cited in most textbooks)
> - **ISO 9001:2008** — clarifications
> - **ISO 9001:2015** — risk-based thinking, leadership focus (**current latest**)

---

## 1.4 Quality Concepts — Foundational Definitions

Per ISO 9000:2015, three vocabulary terms form the foundation:

$$\text{Quality} = \text{Conformance to Requirements} + \text{Fitness for Use}$$

- **Conformance** = the product meets written specifications (McCall's view).
- **Fitness for Use** = the product satisfies real user needs (Juran's view).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 3-Mark Shortcut:** When asked "Define quality," write *both* definitions. Examiners often award 1.5 marks each.

---

## 1.5 Software Quality — IEEE Std 610.12 Definition

> *"Software quality is the degree to which a software product conforms to its requirements and specifications, and satisfies the customer/user."*

Notice this combines **internal conformance** (to spec) and **external satisfaction** (user happiness) — exactly mirroring the ISO 9000 dual view.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Quality Management Principles (ISO 9000:2000/2015)

These principles form the **philosophical backbone** of ISO 9001. KTU regularly tests these as 7–14 mark questions.

| # | Principle | Plain-English Meaning | Software Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Understand & exceed customer needs | SRS validation against stakeholder goals |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Leaders establish unity of purpose | Project manager aligning dev & test teams |
| 3 | **Involvement of People** | People are the essence | Empowering developers to write unit tests |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Manage activities as processes | Defining a documented SDLC (e.g., Waterfall) |
| 5 | **System Approach to Management** | Identify & manage processes as a system | CMMI Level 3 process repository |
| 6 | **Continual Improvement** | Should be a permanent objective | Kaizen, PDCA cycle, retrospectives |
| 7 | **Factual Approach to Decision Making** | Decisions based on data & analysis | Using defect density metrics to assess release readiness |
| 8 | **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** | Win-win with suppliers | Long-term contracts with cloud vendors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** A 14-mark question often asks *"Explain the eight quality management principles of ISO 9000 with software engineering examples."* Memorize the *name* + *one software example* for each.

---

## 2.2 ISO 9001:2015 — The 7 Quality Management Principles (Refined)

When ISO 9001:2015 was released, it consolidated the original 8 principles into **7 principles**, adding **Relationship Management** and replacing one with **Leadership** emphasis. Here is the modern view:

$$\text{ISO 9001:2015 QMS Principles} = \{\text{Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement of People, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-based Decision Making, Relationship Management}\}$$

---

## 2.3 The Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA / Deming Cycle)

ISO 9000 mandates continual improvement through the **PDCA cycle**:

| Stage | Meaning | Software Quality Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **P (Plan)** | Establish objectives & processes | Define quality metrics, write SQA plan |
| **D (Do)** | Implement the processes | Execute development following SQA plan |
| **C (Check)** | Monitor & measure performance | Conduct reviews, testing, metrics collection |
| **A (Act)** | Take corrective actions | Apply lessons learned, refine processes |

The cycle repeats, providing **Kaizen** (continuous improvement).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality Metrics & Cost of Quality

> [!NOTE]
> Software Quality is not pure mathematics, but the following formulas are **guaranteed KTU 14-mark derivations**.

### 2.4.1 Defect Density (DD)

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Size of Software (KLOC or FP)}}
$$

- **Units:** defects per KLOC (or per Function Point)
- **KTU Interpretation:** Lower is better; industry average ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 defects/KLOC** for mature organizations.

### 2.4.2 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) — Reliability Metric

$$
MTTF = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i}{N}
$$

where $t_i$ is the time between the $(i-1)$-th and $i$-th failure, and $N$ is the number of failures observed.

### 2.4.3 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — Frequently Tested 14-Mark Topic

$$\boxed{\text{CoQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}}$$

| Cost Component | When Incurred | Software Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention** | Before defects occur | Training, SQA plan, tool acquisition |
| **Appraisal** | Detecting defects | Inspections, reviews, testing |
| **Internal Failure** | Before delivery | Rework after failed unit tests |
| **External Failure** | After delivery | Bug-fix patches, warranty claims, lawsuits |

### 2.4.4 Return on Quality (ROQ)

$$
ROQ = \frac{\text{Profit from Quality Improvement} - \text{Quality Investment Cost}}{\text{Quality Investment Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 2.5 ISO 9001 Certification Process — Stepwise

1. **Gap Analysis** — Compare current QMS to ISO 9001 requirements.
2. **Documentation** — Create Quality Manual, procedures, work instructions.
3. **Implementation** — Train staff; deploy QMS across the organization.
4. **Internal Audit** — First-party audit conducted by your own trained auditors.
5. **Management Review** — Top management reviews QMS effectiveness.
6. **Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)** — Certification body checks documents.
7. **Stage 2 Audit (On-site Audit)** — Certifiers verify implementation.
8. **Certification Decision** — Certificate valid for **3 years**.
9. **Surveillance Audits** — Annual check-ups to maintain certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** ISO 9001 does *not* certify a *product*. It certifies the **Quality Management System** of the *organization* that produces the product. This distinction is a 3-mark question favourite.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of ISO 9000 in Software Engineering

- **Outsourcing contracts:** Many government IT contracts (e.g., **C-DAC, ISRO, DRDO**) require ISO 9001 certification as a tender prerequisite.
- **Global market access:** EU and US clients require ISO 9001 as a baseline trust signal.
- **Risk reduction:** Process discipline reduces defect leakage to production.
- **Synergy with CMMI:** ISO 9001 = *what* to do; CMMI = *how well* you do it.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Defect Density

**Problem:** During system testing, a team found **142 defects** in a software module of size **35,000 LOC**. Compute the defect density and interpret it.

### Step 1: Convert LOC to KLOC

We convert lines of code into thousands of lines of code (KLOC) by dividing by **1000**.

$$
\text{Size} = \frac{35{,}000 \text{ LOC}}{1000} = 35 \text{ KLOC}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the defect density formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Size in KLOC}} \\
DD &= \frac{142}{35} \\
DD &= 4.057 \text{ defects/KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpretation against industry benchmarks

The industry benchmark for high-maturity organizations is between **0.5 and 1.0 defects/KLOC**. Our computed value of **4.057** is **approximately 4 to 8 times higher** than the benchmark, signalling that the module requires **significant process improvement** before release.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost of Quality Analysis

**Problem:** A software company recorded the following annual quality costs:

| Cost Category | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Prevention (training, tools) | 12 |
| Appraisal (reviews, testing) | 18 |
| Internal Failure (rework) | 25 |
| External Failure (patches, support) | 45 |

Compute total CoQ and the failure cost ratio.

### Step 1: Apply the CoQ master formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoQ &= \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} \\
CoQ &= 12 + 18 + 25 + 45 \\
CoQ &= 100 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Failure Cost Ratio

The failure cost ratio is defined as the ratio of total failure costs (internal + external) to total CoQ.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Failure Cost Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}}{CoQ} \\
&= \frac{25 + 45}{100} \\
&= \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 \text{ or } 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Managerial Insight

A failure cost ratio of **70 %** indicates an organization is **fire-fighting rather than preventing**. A healthy ISO-aligned QMS aims for **failure costs < 40 %** of total CoQ. Recommended actions include:

- **Increase prevention spend** (target: +30 % over current ₹12 Lakhs).
- **Shift defects left** via early reviews and static analysis.
- **Implement root-cause analysis** (5 Whys, fishbone diagrams).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — MTTF Calculation

**Problem:** A web server logged the following inter-failure times (in hours): **120, 80, 200, 150, 90, 110**. Compute the MTTF.

### Step 1: Identify parameters

The number of observations is $N = 6$, and the inter-failure times are $\{120, 80, 200, 150, 90, 110\}$.

### Step 2: Compute the numerator (sum of inter-failure times)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{6} t_i &= 120 + 80 + 200 + 150 + 90 + 110 \\
&= 200 + 200 + 150 + 200 \\
&= 750 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute MTTF

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTF &= \frac{\sum t_i}{N} \\
&= \frac{750}{6} \\
&= 125 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret

An MTTF of **125 hours** means that, on average, the server runs for 125 hours between consecutive failures. The corresponding failure rate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1}{MTTF} \\
&= \frac{1}{125} \\
&= 0.008 \text{ failures/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

Higher MTTF indicates higher reliability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ISO 9000 vs CMMI vs Six Sigma

This **comparative table** is a 14-mark KTU favourite ("Compare ISO 9000, CMMI and Six Sigma"):

| Dimension | **ISO 9001** | **CMMI** | **Six Sigma** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Origin | ISO (Switzerland, 1987) | SEI / Carnegie Mellon (1991) | Motorola (1986), popularized by GE |
| Scope | Generic — any industry | Software / systems engineering | Manufacturing-origin, now generic |
| Focus | **What** to do (process existence) | **How well** (process maturity) | **How consistently** (variation reduction) |
| Structure | 7–10 clauses | 5 maturity levels / 5 capability levels | DMAIC + belt hierarchy |
| Certification | Yes (3rd party) | SCAMPI appraisal (not certification) | Green/Black Belt (not certification) |
| Metrics | Loose — define your own | Required by maturity level | Mandatory statistical metrics (DPMO) |
| Mandated in India | Often by PSUs, govt. IT | Less common | Manufacturing heavy |

$$
\text{DPMO (Six Sigma)} = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Opportunities per unit} \times \text{Units produced}}
$$

Six Sigma target = **3.4 DPMO** (≈ 99.99966 % defect-free).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 9001:2015 QMS Structure (Clause Map)

The structure of ISO 9001:2015 is organized into **10 clauses**. Clauses 1–3 are introductory, while clauses 4–10 contain the **auditable requirements**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ISO 9001:2015 Standard] --> B[Clause 1: Scope]
    A --> C[Clause 2: Normative References]
    A --> D[Clause 3: Terms and Definitions]
    A --> E[Clause 4: Context of Organization]
    A --> F[Clause 5: Leadership]
    A --> G[Clause 6: Planning]
    A --> H[Clause 7: Support]
    A --> I[Clause 8: Operation]
    A --> J[Clause 9: Performance Evaluation]
    A --> K[Clause 10: Improvement]

    E --> E1[SWOT Analysis]
    E --> E2[QMS Scope Definition]
    F --> F1[Management Commitment]
    F --> F2[Quality Policy]
    G --> G1[Competence, Awareness, Communication]
    G --> G2[Documented Information]
    H --> H1[Operational Planning]
    H --> H2[Requirements for Products and Services]
    I --> I1[Monitoring and Measurement]
    I --> I2[Internal Audit]
    J --> J1[Nonconformity and Corrective Action]
    J --> J2[Continual Improvement]
```

**KTU Mapping Tip:** When asked to "explain ISO 9001 structure," sketch this 10-clause hierarchy. Examiners award partial credit even if only clauses 4–10 are explained.

---

## 4.2 The PDCA Cycle Mapped to Software Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PLAN\nDefine Quality Goals\nSQA Plan, Metrics] --> D[DO\nExecute Development\nCode, Unit Tests]
    D --> C[CHECK\nReviews, Testing\nDefect Logs]
    C --> A[ACT\nCorrective Action\nProcess Refinement]
    A --> P

    style P fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 ISO 9001 Certification Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Gap Analysis] --> S2[Step 2: Documentation\nQuality Manual + Procedures]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Implementation\nTraining and Deployment]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Internal Audit]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Management Review]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Stage 1 Audit\nDocumentation Review]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Stage 2 Audit\nOn-site Verification]
    S7 --> S8{Decision}
    S8 -- Pass --> S9[ISO 9001 Certificate\nValid 3 Years]
    S8 -- Fail --> S3
    S9 --> S10[Annual Surveillance Audit]
    S10 --> S11[Recertification\nEvery 3 Years]
    S11 --> S6
```

---

## 4.4 The Three Pillars of Software Quality Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SQM[Software Quality Management] --> QP[Quality Planning]
    SQM --> QA[Quality Assurance]
    SQM --> QC[Quality Control]

    QP --> QP1[SQA Plan]
    QP --> QP2[Standards Selection]
    QP --> QP3[Metrics Definition]

    QA --> QA1[Process Definition]
    QA --> QA2[Audits]
    QA --> QA3[Training]

    QC --> QC1[Technical Reviews]
    QC --> QC2[Software Testing]
    QC --> QC3[Defect Tracking]

    style SQM fill:#4B0082,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
    style QP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style QA fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style QC fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Software QMS Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Customer Requirements]
        I2[Regulatory Standards]
        I3[Internal Standards]
    end

    subgraph Process["Quality Management Process"]
        PR1[SRS Review]
        PR2[Design Inspection]
        PR3[Code Review]
        PR4[Unit Testing]
        PR5[Integration Testing]
        PR6[System Testing]
        PR7[Acceptance Testing]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Quality Metrics Dashboard]
        O2[Defect Reports]
        O3[SQA Audit Reports]
    end

    subgraph Feedback["Continual Improvement Loop"]
        F1[Root Cause Analysis]
        F2[Process Updates]
    end

    I1 --> PR1
    I2 --> PR1
    I3 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2 --> PR3 --> PR4 --> PR5 --> PR6 --> PR7
    PR7 --> O1
    PR7 --> O2
    PR7 --> O3
    O2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> PR1
```

This diagram represents the **closed-loop QMS** required by ISO 9001: continual improvement via feedback.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC).**

**Model Answer:**

- **Quality Assurance (QA):** A *process-oriented*, *managerial* set of activities that **prevents** defects by ensuring the development process follows defined standards. It focuses on the *process* rather than the *product*. **Example:** Conducting a process audit to verify that peer code reviews are being performed.
- **Quality Control (QC):** A *product-oriented*, *technical* set of activities that **detects** defects in the *product*. It focuses on the *deliverable* rather than the *process*. **Example:** Running a test suite and logging defects found in the fielded software.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "QA = testing" and "QC = review" — this is **wrong**. Testing is a QC technique; QA is broader (audits, standards, training). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this misconception.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is ISO 9000? List any two quality management principles.**

**Model Answer:**

**ISO 9000** is a family of international standards published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO, Geneva)** that define the requirements and vocabulary for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**.

**Two Principles (any two accepted):**

1. **Customer Focus:** Organizations depend on their customers and therefore should understand current and future customer needs, meet customer requirements, and strive to exceed customer expectations.
2. **Process Approach:** A desired result is achieved more efficiently when activities and related resources are managed as a process.

*(Other accepted answers: Leadership, Involvement of People, Continual Improvement, Factual Approach to Decision Making, System Approach to Management, Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **eight quality management principles** of ISO 9000 with **software engineering examples**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Cost of Quality (CoQ) model** and explain how it is used by software organizations. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — (a)

| # | Principle | Software Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Conducting regular sprint reviews with the product owner to validate user stories |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Project sponsor actively resolving cross-team blockers |
| 3 | **Involvement of People** | Empowering developers to define their own coding standards |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Adopting a documented Agile SDLC with defined ceremonies |
| 5 | **System Approach to Management** | Integrating CI/CD, QA, and release processes as one system |
| 6 | **Continual Improvement** | Running Kaizen retrospectives every sprint |
| 7 | **Factual Approach to Decision Making** | Using burn-down charts to decide whether to extend a sprint |
| 8 | **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** | Multi-year contract with a cloud vendor offering preferential pricing |

### Model Solution — (b)

The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is the total cost incurred to ensure software conforms to requirements plus the cost of failure to conform. It has four components:

$$
\text{CoQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

where $C_P$ = Prevention, $C_A$ = Appraisal, $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure, $C_{EF}$ = External Failure.

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention | Costs of preventing defects | Quality training, process improvement |
| Appraisal | Costs of detecting defects | Code reviews, testing, inspections |
| Internal Failure | Costs of defects found before delivery | Rework, regression testing |
| External Failure | Costs of defects found after delivery | Patches, customer support, lost goodwill |

**Use in Software Organizations:**

1. **Diagnostic Tool:** Identifies whether the org is *fire-fighting* (high failure costs) or *preventing* (high prevention costs).
2. **Investment Justification:** Demonstrates ROI of quality investments — typically, **every ₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹10 in failure costs.**
3. **Process Maturity Indicator:** A mature organization has CoQ ≈ **5–10 %** of project budget; immature organizations reach **30–40 %**.
4. **Optimization:** Shifting investment from *appraisal + failure* to *prevention* reduces total CoQ — the **optimal CoQ** is where the marginal cost of prevention equals marginal savings from reduced failures.

---

### Question B (Option 2) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **structure of ISO 9001:2015** and the **PDCA cycle** applied to software development. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **ISO 9000, CMMI, and Six Sigma** as quality frameworks. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — (a)

**Structure of ISO 9001:2015 — 10 Clauses:**

1. **Scope** — applicability of the standard.
2. **Normative References** — supporting standards.
3. **Terms and Definitions** — vocabulary.
4. **Context of the Organization** — internal/external issues, QMS scope.
5. **Leadership** — top management commitment, quality policy.
6. **Planning** — risks, opportunities, quality objectives.
7. **Support** — resources, competence, awareness, documented information.
8. **Operation** — operational planning, requirements for products/services.
9. **Performance Evaluation** — monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review.
10. **Improvement** — nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement.

**PDCA Applied to Software Development:**

| Stage | Software Activity |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | Write the **Software Quality Assurance Plan (SQAP)**; define metrics (DD, MTTF); choose standards (IEEE 830, 829). |
| **Do** | Execute the SDLC: design, code, unit test, integration test — all according to the SQAP. |
| **Check** | Conduct **technical reviews**, **inspections**, **testing**; record defects in issue tracker; compute defect density. |
| **Act** | Perform **root-cause analysis** (5 Whys); update coding standards; refine test cases; feed lessons learned into next iteration. |

### Model Solution — (b)

The comparison is summarized in the **analytical matrix** developed in Section 3.4 of these notes. Key takeaways for KTU:

- **ISO 9001** = "build a documented QMS" (generic, certifiable).
- **CMMI** = "measure & improve process maturity" (5 levels, software-flavoured).
- **Six Sigma** = "reduce variation statistically" (DMAIC, DPMO ≤ 3.4).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Q4-b):**
> Students often write that CMMI is *certifiable*. **It is not.** CMMI uses **SCAMPI appraisals** that produce *maturity ratings*, not certifications. ISO 9001 issues a *certificate*. Six Sigma issues *belt credentials*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Software Quality Management & ISO 9000**

- [ ] **SQM = Planning + Assurance + Control** — state all three when asked.
- [ ] **QA = Prevention / process / managerial**; **QC = Detection / product / technical** — never confuse them.
- [ ] **ISO 9000** is a *family*; only **ISO 9001** is certifiable.
- [ ] **ISO certifies the QMS**, *not* the product.
- [ ] **Eight principles** (or **seven** in ISO 9001:2015): Customer Focus, Leadership, Involvement, Process Approach, System Approach, Continual Improvement, Factual Decisions, Mutual Supplier Benefit.
- [ ] **PDCA = Plan–Do–Check–Act** — Deming cycle, the engine of continual improvement.
- [ ] **Cost of Quality = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.**
- [ ] **Defect Density = Defects / KLOC**; mature orgs target **0.5–1.0 defects/KLOC**.
- [ ] **MTTF = Σ(inter-failure times) / N**; reliability = 1/MTTF.
- [ ] **CMMI is NOT certified**; it is appraised via SCAMPI.
- [ ] **Six Sigma target = 3.4 DPMO**; uses **DMAIC**.
- [ ] ISO 9001 certificate is valid for **3 years** with **annual surveillance audits**.
- [ ] **Gap Analysis** is the first step of certification.
- [ ] **Latest version:** ISO 9001:**2015** with risk-based thinking.
- [ ] **KTU favourite contrasts:** ISO vs CMMI, QA vs QC, Process vs Product quality.
- [ ] **IEEE Std 730** = software-specific SQA; ISO 9001 = generic QMS applied to software.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma for Software Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST723 - SOFTWARE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Six Sigma for Software Engineering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Six Sigma (6σ) — KTU Official Definition** > Six Sigma is a **disciplined, data-driven, statistical methodology** for eliminating defects, reducing process variability, and improving quality in any process (manufacturing or service). Originally pioneered by **Motorola (1986, Bill Smith)** and later popularized by **General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995)**, the term "Six Sigma" refers to a process operating such that the distance between the process mean and the nearest specification limit is **six standard deviations (6σ)**, yielding a maximum of **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**. In the context of **Software Engineering (KTU Module 4)**, Six Sigma is a **quality-management framework** applied to the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to **prevent defects** rather than detect them, achieving near-perfect reliability (~99.99966% defect-free output). ## 1.2 The Core Philosophy: DMAIC Six Sigma projects are executed via the **DMAIC** roadmap (Pronounced *Duh-May-Ick*): | Phase | Name | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ — Critical-to-Quality), and project scope | | **M** | **Measure** | Quantify current process performance, baseline defect rate | | **A** | **Analyze** | Discover root causes of defects using statistical tools | | **I** | **Improve** | Implement solutions to remove root causes | | **C** | **Control** | Sustain the gains through monitoring and standardisation | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Cloud Kitchen" Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy (Cloud-Kitchen Edition)** > Imagine you run a **cloud kitchen** delivering biryani. Out of every 10,000 orders, 12 reach cold, 5 have missing raita, and 3 have wrong spice level. That is *defective output*. Six Sigma asks: > 1. **Define** — "Spicy, hot, complete order" is the CTQ (Critical-to-Quality). > 2. **Measure** — Currently 20 defects per 10,000 orders. > 3. **Analyze** — Root cause: insufficient thermal packaging. > 4. **Improve** — Switch to triple-layer insulated bags. > 5. **Control** — Install a temperature probe in every delivery bag; auto-flag if < 60°C. > > Result: Defects drop to **3.4 per million orders** (Six Sigma level) — i.e., the kitchen has so little variation that it practically never fails. **That is Six Sigma.** ## 1.4 Why Six Sigma for Software? > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Unlike manufacturing, **software defects are 100% man-made** (logic errors, requirement gaps).
