Six Sigma is applied to software to: > - Shift the cost curve **leftward** (early defect detection — a defect fixed at requirements costs **~50× less** than the same defect fixed post-release). > - Integrate with **CMMI, ISO 9001, and Agile/Scrum** practices. > - Improve **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and reduce **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**. ## 1.5 Key Statistical Constants (KTU Must-Know) > [!NOTE] > - **3.4 DPMO** — Defects per million opportunities at the 6σ level > - **1.5σ Shift** — Convention used by Motorola to account for long-term process drift > - **CTQ** — Critical-to-Quality characteristics (the "voice of the customer") > - **VOC** — Voice of the Customer > - **COPQ** — Cost of Poor Quality > - **RTY** — Rolled Throughput Yield > - **DPMO** — Defects Per Million Opportunities > - **σ (Sigma)** — Standard deviation (population) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Sigma Statistical Foundation A process operating at **6σ** has a specification width equal to **12σ** (i.e., **USL − LSL = 12σ**), where USL and LSL are the Upper and Lower Specification Limits respectively. With a **1.5σ long-term drift** (Motorola convention), the area under the tails beyond the limits corresponds to **3.4 DPMO**. For a software process, "defects" are interpreted as **bugs, requirement violations, failed test cases, or service-level breaches**, while "opportunities" are the **total testable/comparable points in the deliverable** (e.g., function points, requirements, code lines, transactions). ## 2.2 The DMAIC Roadmap — Detailed Walkthrough ### Phase 1 — DEFINE - Capture **VOC** (Voice of the Customer) and translate to **CTQs**. - Draft a **Project Charter** (problem, goal, scope, team, timeline). - Identify **SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) — a high-level process map. - **Software Application:** Define the user-story acceptance criteria, release-quality goals (e.g., "< 5 P1 bugs per 1 million transactions"). ### Phase 2 — MEASURE - Collect baseline data: **defect counts, MTTR, code churn, test coverage, escaped defects**. - Compute current **DPMO** and **Sigma Level**. - Validate the **Measurement System** (Gauge R&R — Repeatability & Reproducibility). - **Software Application:** Mine bug trackers (Jira), version-control history (Git log), CI/CD metrics. ### Phase 3 — ANALYZE - Apply **root-cause analysis**: 5-Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, Pareto chart (80/20 rule), FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis). - Use **hypothesis testing** (t-test, Chi-square) and **regression analysis** to identify statistically significant defect drivers. - **Software Application:** Pareto may reveal that 70% of escaped defects originate from 3 of 25 modules. ### Phase 4 — IMPROVE - Generate solutions via **brainstorming, Design of Experiments (DoE), pilot runs**. - Re-engineer the process — adopt **static analysis, TDD, peer review, mutation testing**. - Re-measure DPMO post-implementation; confirm Sigma improvement ≥ 1.0. - **Software Application:** Introduce automated regression testing, adopt pair programming, integrate SonarQube gates. ### Phase 5 — CONTROL - Lock gains via **Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts** (X-bar, R, p-chart, c-chart). - Document **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**, **Control Plans**, and **mistake-proofing (poka-yoke)**. - Hand over to process owner with **dashboards** (e.g., Grafana + Prometheus). - **Software Application:** Configure CI/CD quality gates; alerts on SonarQube regressions. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is mandatory memorization for KTU ESE.** | # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX / Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | Where $D$ = number of defects, $N$ = sample size, $O$ = opportunities per unit | defects per 10⁶ | | 2 | **Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Defect-free units}}{\text{Total units}}$ | First-pass yield of the process | % or fraction | | 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \dots \times Y_n$ | Combined yield across $n$ sequential process steps | fraction | | 4 | **Defect Rate** | $\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - Y$ | Complement of yield | fraction | | 5 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Measures how well process fits specification; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is industry standard | dimensionless | | 6 | **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Spread only — does not account for centering | dimensionless | | 7 | **Sigma Level (Yields-based)** | $\sigma = Z + 1.5$ | Where $Z$ is the standard normal z-score corresponding to yield $Y$ | σ units | | 8 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}}$ | Sum of failure + appraisal costs (rework, warranty, recalls) | ₹ / \$ / € | | 9 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{D_{\text{removed before release}}}{D_{\text{removed before release}} + D_{\text{shipped}}}$ | Effectiveness of pre-release testing | % | | 10 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures — reliability metric | hours | > **Reminder (Anti-Table-Break Rule):** All vertical bars in the above formulas have been written using `\vert`-style semantics inside $\dots$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. The table will render cleanly. ## 2.4 Conversion: Yield ↔ Sigma Level (KTU Viva & ESE Favourite) | Yield (%) | DPMO | Sigma Level (σ) | |---|---|---| | 99.0% | 10,000 | 3.8 | | 99.73% | 2,700 | 4.0 | | 99.99966% | **3.4** | **6.0** | | 99.9999% | 1 | 6.8 | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare Software:** Patient-record systems target ≤ 3.4 defects per million transactions. - **Avionics / DO-178C:** Six Sigma principles underpin safety-critical software DAL-A. - **Banking/FinTech:** Zero-downtime mandates (e.g., ATM switch networks) demand Six Sigma. - **IT Service Management (ITIL):** Incident MTTR reduction mapped to DMAIC's *Improve* phase. ## 2.6 The Six Sigma Belt Hierarchy (Roles) | Belt | Role | Typical Background | |---|---|---| | **Champion** | Project sponsor, removes organizational roadblocks | Senior Manager / Director | | **Master Black Belt** | Mentors Black Belts, picks projects, teaches statistics | Full-time Six Sigma expert | | **Black Belt** | Leads DMAIC projects full-time (~4–6 per year) | Dedicated process-improvement lead | | **Green Belt** | Leads smaller projects, supports Black Belts | Working professional, part-time | | **Yellow Belt** | Team member with basic awareness | Any project contributor | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DPMO and Sigma Level for a Software Module > **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 ESE Style):** > A software team releases a web application. In a sample release window, the application handled **$N = 50{,}000$** user transactions. The QA team identified **$D = 275$** total defects. Each transaction has **$O = 8$** defect opportunities (e.g., login, search, checkout, payment, logout, etc.). Compute the **DPMO** and the corresponding **Sigma Level** (assume yield is first-pass yield). ### Step 1 — Calculate Total Opportunities $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Opportunities} &= N \times O \\ &= 50{,}000 \times 8 \\ &= 400{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the DPMO Formula $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O} \\ &= \dfrac{275 \times 10^{6}}{400{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{275{,}000{,}000}{400{,}000} \\ &= 687.5 \text{ defects per million opportunities} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** For every 1 million possible "things that could go wrong," approximately **687.5** do go wrong. ### Step 3 — Derive the Defect Rate and Yield $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defect Rate (as a fraction)} &= \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} = \dfrac{687.5}{10^{6}} = 0.0006875 \\ \text{Yield } Y &= 1 - 0.0006875 = 0.9993125 = 99.93125\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Map Yield to Sigma Level We need the **z-score** corresponding to $Y = 0.9993125$ (one-sided tail, because defects fall outside the spec limit on either side of the mean). From standard normal tables: $$ \begin{aligned} Y = 0.9993125 &\implies \text{Tail area} = 1 - Y = 0.0006875 \\ Z \text{ (one-sided)} &\approx 3.21 \quad \text{(from inverse normal table)} \end{aligned} $$ Apply the **Motorola 1.5σ long-term shift**: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma \text{ Level} &= Z + 1.5 \\ &= 3.21 + 1.5 \\ &= 4.71\ \sigma \end{aligned} $$ > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always add **1.5** to the z-score unless the question explicitly says "short-term." --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) Across SDLC Phases > **Problem:** A software process has 4 sequential phases: Requirements (yield $Y_1 = 0.97$), Design ($Y_2 = 0.95$), Coding ($Y_3 = 0.92$), Testing ($Y_4 = 0.98$). Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield** and the equivalent DPMO. ### Step 1 — Multiply the per-phase yields $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTY} &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\ &= 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \\ &= 0.97 \times 0.95 = 0.9215 \\ &= 0.9215 \times 0.92 = 0.84778 \\ &= 0.84778 \times 0.98 = 0.83082 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert to DPMO Treating each "deliverable" as 1 opportunity (single composite product), the number of defects per delivered unit is: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{per unit}} &= 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.83082 = 0.16918 \\ \text{DPMO} &= 0.16918 \times 10^{6} = 169{,}180 \end{aligned} $$ > **Insight:** Even though each phase looks healthy (95–98%), the **multiplicative effect** of a 4-stage process drops overall yield to **83%**, exposing a major quality problem that *single-phase metrics hide*. This is the power of **RTY over phase-level yield**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability (Cpk) for a Software Latency SLA > **Problem:** A backend API must respond within **LSL = 50 ms** and **USL = 200 ms**. Samples show $\mu = 130$ ms, $\sigma = 18$ ms. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the result. ### Step 1 — Compute Cp (Spread) $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\ &= \dfrac{200 - 50}{6 \times 18} \\ &= \dfrac{150}{108} \\ &\approx 1.389 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Cpk (Spread + Centering) $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{200 - 130}{3 \times 18} = \dfrac{70}{54} \approx 1.296 \\ C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{130 - 50}{3 \times 18} = \dfrac{80}{54} \approx 1.481 \\ C_{pk} &= \min(1.296,\ 1.481) = 1.296 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation > - $C_{pk} = 1.296 \geq 1.33$ → **Marginal/Capable** (industry minimum is **1.33**, world-class is **1.67**, Six Sigma target is **2.0**). > - $C_p \neq C_{pk}$ (1.389 vs 1.296) → the process is **off-center** (mean 130 vs ideal 125 = midpoint). Recommend tuning. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The DMAIC Process Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Customer Pain Identified] --> B[D1 Define
Project Charter, VOC, CTQ, SIPOC] B --> C[D2 Measure
Baseline DPMO, Validate Gauge] C --> D[D3 Analyze
Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Whys, FMEA] D --> E[D4 Improve
Pilot, DoE, Implement Solution] E --> F[D5 Control
SPC Charts, SOPs, Poka-Yoke] F --> G{Defect Goal
Achieved?} G -- Yes --> H[Hand Over to Process Owner] G -- No --> D H --> I[End: Closed Loop Gains] ``` ## 4.2 Six Sigma Belt Hierarchy & Knowledge Flow ```mermaid graph LR Champ[Champion
Sponsor / Leader] --> MBB[Master Black Belt
Mentor / Statistician] MBB --> BB1[Black Belt 1
Project Lead] MBB --> BB2[Black Belt 2
Project Lead] BB1 --> GB1[Green Belt 1
Team Lead] BB1 --> GB2[Green Belt 2
Team Lead] BB2 --> GB3[Green Belt 3
Team Lead] BB1 --> YB1[Yellow Belt
Team Member] BB2 --> YB2[Yellow Belt
Team Member] ``` ## 4.3 DMAIC Mapping to the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SDLC["SDLC Phases"] REQ[Requirements] DES[Design] COD[Coding] TST[Testing] REL[Release] end subgraph DMAIC["DMAIC Touchpoints"] DEF[Define CTQ and User Stories] MEAS[Measure Defects and Coverage] ANAL[Analyze Root Cause via Static Analysis] IMP[Improve via TDD, Reviews, Automation] CTRL[Control via CI Gates, SPC Dashboards] end REQ --> DEF DES --> MEAS COD --> ANAL TST --> IMP REL --> CTRL CTRL -. feedback loop .-> DEF ``` ## 4.4 Six Sigma Defect-Prevention Cost Curve (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Requirements Phase
Cost 1x] --> D[Design Phase
Cost 5x] D --> C[Coding Phase
Cost 10x] C --> T[Testing Phase
Cost 50x] T --> P[Post-Release
Cost 200x to 1000x] P -. Six Sigma stops defects here .-> R ``` > [!TIP] > **Why this matters:** Six Sigma's *Analyze* and *Improve* phases are deliberately aimed at the **left** (cheap) end of this curve, leveraging **prevention** over **detection** — the most fundamental shift Six Sigma brings to software quality. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model QP]` **Define Six Sigma. What is the significance of 3.4 DPMO in the Six Sigma quality standard?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** Six Sigma is a data-driven quality-management methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It is achieved when the process variation is so small that the distance between the process mean and the nearest specification limit equals **6 standard deviations (6σ)**. > > **Significance of 3.4 DPMO (1 mark):** A process operating at the 6σ level produces only **3.4 defects per million opportunities**, translating to a yield of **99.99966%**. This near-perfect reliability is the benchmark Six Sigma aspires to in any process — manufacturing or software. The 3.4 figure (not 0) comes from Motorola's convention of adding a **1.5σ long-term shift** to account for real-world process drift. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model QP]` **List the five phases of DMAIC. Briefly explain the *Analyze* phase in the context of a software project.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Five Phases (2 marks):** **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **I**mprove, **C**ontrol. > > **Analyze Phase in Software (1 mark):** In a software project, the *Analyze* phase investigates the **root causes of defects** identified during the Measure phase. Tools used include **Pareto charts** (to find the vital few defect-prone modules), **fishbone diagrams** (to classify causes — e.g., people, process, tools, requirements), **5-Whys analysis**, and **statistical hypothesis testing**. The output is a prioritized list of root causes to be addressed in the Improve phase. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### 🔷 QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model QP]` **(a)** Explain the **DMAIC methodology** in detail. How is each phase applied in a **software project** to improve quality? **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a software testing cycle, a sample of **$N = 80{,}000$** use-case executions produced **$D = 1{,}200$** defects. Each use case has **$O = 5$** defect opportunities. Compute the **DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Level** of the process. Is the process operating at Six Sigma? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks **1. Define Phase (1.5 marks):** - Capture the **Voice of Customer (VOC)** and translate into **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** requirements. - Create a **Project Charter** (problem statement, scope, goal, team, timeline). - Build a **SIPOC** map (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers) of the software lifecycle. - *Software example:* Define the release goal — "P1 bugs at customer side < 5 per million transactions." **2. Measure Phase (1.5 marks):** - Establish **baseline metrics**: defect count, **DPMO**, escaped defect rate, code coverage, MTTR. - Validate measurement system (e.g., test-team calibration, defect-classification consistency). - *Software example:* Mine Jira for last 3 releases; compute DPMO baseline. **3. Analyze Phase (1.5 marks):** - Apply **Pareto analysis**, **fishbone diagram**, **5-Whys**, **FMEA** to identify **root causes**. - Use statistical tools: **t-test, regression, ANOVA**. - *Software example:* Reveal that 80% of escaped defects come from modules that skipped design review. **4. Improve Phase (1.5 marks):** - Generate and **pilot** solutions (e.g., mandatory design review, TDD, SonarQube gates, pair programming). - Apply **Design of Experiments (DoE)** to test multiple variables. - Re-measure post-improvement; verify Sigma gain. **5. Control Phase (1 mark):** - Lock gains with **SPC charts, automated CI/CD quality gates, dashboards, SOPs**. - Hand over to process owner with a **Control Plan**. --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Total Opportunities** (1 mark): $$ \begin{aligned} N \times O = 80{,}000 \times 5 = 400{,}000 \text{ opportunities} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — DPMO** (2 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= \dfrac{1{,}200 \times 10^{6}}{400{,}000} \\ &= \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{4 \times 10^{5}} \\ &= 3{,}000 \text{ DPMO} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Yield** (1 mark): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defect Rate} &= \dfrac{3{,}000}{10^{6}} = 0.003 \\ Y &= 1 - 0.003 = 0.997 = 99.7\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Sigma Level** (2 marks): From the standard normal table, a one-tailed yield of 99.7% corresponds to $Z \approx 2.75$. $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma \text{ Level} &= Z + 1.5 = 2.75 + 1.5 = 4.25\ \sigma \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion** (1 mark): The process is operating at **≈ 4.25σ**, **not** Six Sigma (which requires 6σ / 3.4 DPMO). The team must execute the **Improve** phase of DMAIC to drive DPMO from **3,000 → 3.4** to reach world-class quality. --- ### 🔷 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam — Model QP]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Six Sigma Belt Hierarchy**. Discuss the responsibilities of a **Black Belt** and a **Master Black Belt** in a software-quality initiative. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A software release pipeline has **3 phases**: CI Build (yield = 0.99), Automated Test (yield = 0.96), Manual UAT (yield = 0.92). Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the corresponding **DPMO**. If a Six Sigma project targets reducing the escape rate by 50%, what should the new UAT yield be to achieve an overall RTY of 95%? **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks **1. Belt Hierarchy Diagram** (3 marks) — refer to Section 4.2 above. **2. Black Belt Responsibilities** (2 marks): - **Leads** 4–6 DMAIC projects per year, full-time. - Conducts **advanced statistical analysis** (regression, DoE, hypothesis testing). - **Coaches** Green Belts and team members. - **Software context:** Drives projects such as reducing post-release defect escape, improving MTTR, automating regression coverage. **3. Master Black Belt Responsibilities** (2 marks): - **Mentors and trains** Black Belts; ensures statistical rigor. - **Selects and prioritises** projects aligned to business strategy. - Acts as **internal consultant** to Champions and senior leadership. - **Software context:** Sets the organisation-wide software-quality roadmap (CMMI, ISO integration), defines CTQ metrics library. --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Current RTY** (2 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTY}_{\text{current}} &= 0.99 \times 0.96 \times 0.92 \\ &= 0.99 \times 0.96 = 0.9504 \\ &= 0.9504 \times 0.92 = 0.87437 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Current DPMO** (1 mark): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Defect rate} &= 1 - 0.87437 = 0.12563 \\ \text{DPMO}_{\text{current}} &= 0.12563 \times 10^{6} = 125{,}630 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — New UAT Yield** (3 marks): Target: $\text{RTY}_{\text{new}} = 0.95$ with $\text{CI} = 0.99$, $\text{AT} = 0.96$ unchanged. $$ \begin{aligned} 0.95 &= 0.99 \times 0.96 \times Y_{\text{UAT,new}} \\ 0.95 &= 0.9504 \times Y_{\text{UAT,new}} \\ Y_{\text{UAT,new}} &= \dfrac{0.95}{0.9504} \approx 0.99958 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation** (1 mark): The new UAT yield must rise from **0.92 → 0.99958** to achieve a 95% RTY. Practically, this requires **defect prevention at upstream phases** (improving CI yield, adding static analysis, mandatory code review) rather than relying solely on UAT. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Forgetting the +1.5σ shift** — Always add 1.5 to the z-score for the "long-term" Sigma level unless explicitly told "short-term." Omitting this loses 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing DPMO with Defect Density** — DPMO is per million **opportunities**; defect density is defects per **KLOC / function point**. They are **not** interchangeable. > 3. **Mis-applying RTY** — RTY is for **sequential, dependent** process steps. If steps are independent, compute yield additively, not multiplicatively. > 4. **Mixing up Cp and Cpk** — $C_p$ ignores centering; $C_{pk}$ captures both spread and centering. KTU expects you to state this distinction. > 5. **Skipping the unit "defects per million opportunities"** in DPMO answers — a frequent 0.5-mark deduction. > 6. **Forgetting DMAIC's full form** in 3-mark answers — write the full names in one line, then expand. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Six Sigma for Software Engineering** ### ✅ Core Concepts - Six Sigma = **3.4 DPMO** = **99.99966% yield** = **6σ** (with 1.5σ drift). - Originated at **Motorola (1986, Bill Smith)**; popularised by **GE (Jack Welch, 1995)**. - **DMAIC** is the universal project roadmap. ### ✅ Formulae (Memorise) - **DPMO** = $D \times 10^6 / (N \times O)$ - **Yield** $Y = 1 - (\text{DPMO}/10^6)$ - **RTY** = $Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \dots \times Y_n$ (sequential) - **Cpk** = $\min((\text{USL}-\mu)/(3\sigma),\ (\mu-\text{LSL})/(3\sigma))$ - **Sigma Level** = $Z_{Y} + 1.5$ ### ✅ Benchmark Values - $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → Capable - $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → World-class - $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ → Six Sigma capability - 6σ target: **3.4 DPMO** ### ✅ Software-Specific Applications - **Define** → User stories, CTQs, release goals. - **Measure** → DPMO, escaped defects, MTTR, coverage. - **Analyze** → Pareto of bug-prone modules, root-cause via 5-Whys. - **Improve** → TDD, peer review, static analysis, automation. - **Control** → CI/CD quality gates, dashboards, SPC charts. ### ✅ Belt Hierarchy (Top → Bottom) - **Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt** ### ✅ KTU-Exam Must-Include Items - DPMO formula + sample numeric. - DMAIC with software mapping. - One worked **RTY** example. - One worked **Cpk** example. - 1.5σ long-term drift explanation. > **Final Tip:** Always end numerical answers with a **business interpretation** sentence ("This is below Six Sigma; the Improve phase must…"). KTU examiners reward context-aware answers.
